# MAD ACTS, MAD SPEECH, AND MAD PEOPLE IN LATE IMPERIAL CHINESE LAW AND MEDICINE

Fabien Simonis

A DISSERTATION

PRESENTED TO THE FACULTY

OF PRINCETON UNIVERSITY

IN CANDIDACY FOR THE DEGREE

OF DOCTOR OF PHILOSOPHY

RECOMMENDED FOR ACCEPTANCE

## PDF第3页

## Abstract

This dissertation attempts to explain how Chinese officials, physicians, and ordinary families construed and handled mad speech, crazy behavior, and insane people from roughly 1000 CE to the end of the Qing dynasty (1644-1911). I treat “madness” not as an entity, but as an ascribed characteristic of people, acts, and words.
Part 1 analyzes how physicians from Song (960-1279) to Qing explained and treated kuang or diankuang (“mania,” “insanity”), a chiefly behavioral illness. Far from splitting mind from body, most doctors assumed that psycho-behavioral pathology supervened upon organic imbalances; they thus treated mad patients with drugs designed to correct these imbalances. As I trace how doctors came to attribute insanity to the action of inner Fire and mucus (instead of invasion by Wind or demons), I explore several key issues in Chinese medical history: what doctrinal innovations Jin-Yuan physicians (1115-1368) proposed, why the most sophisticated Ming (1368-1644) medical writers were syncretists (zhezhong), why the “Danxi corpus”—a body of works attributed to Zhu Zhenheng (1282-1358)—dominated Ming medical thinking, and how profoundly intertextual the Chinese medical tradition was. I also study how doctors interpreted possession symptoms and postpartum insanity.
Part 2 examines Qing judicial and popular practices surrounding “madness.” Legal fengbing (“madness illness”) and medical kuang functioned differently because they emerged from (and were deployed within) fields of practices whose categories and concerns differed. The way legal officials handled mad acts was shaped by specific institutional concerns and procedures. In the 1660s, the new crime “killing because of madness” likened mad homicides to accidental killings. Because this crime was not punished physically, legislators soon ordered that mad people be preventively confined. Although the script by which Qing law understood mad killings hardly changed, mad homicides were sentenced increasingly severely starting in the 1750s, especially when victims were senior relatives. Yet few mad killers were executed. By contrast, ostensibly mad men whose written fantasies harnessed imperial symbols were often sentenced to death for subversion. This fear that “mad words” could threaten the symbolic fabric of the eighteenth-century Qing state sheds new light on the old topic of “literary trials” (wenziyu).

## PDF第12页

## Conventions

Abbreviations: all abbreviations found in the footnotes are explained in Bibliography A, where they appear in alphabetical order.

Alphabetical order: in the two bibliographies, the glossary (Appendix 1), and the list of

materia medica (Appendix 2), entries in Chinese are presented in the alphabetical order

of their first character. Lan Zai would therefore appear before Lang Waipo.

Chinese names: I refer to most people by their surname (xing姓) followed by their personal name (ming名). Other names (zi字, bao號) are listed in Bibliography A under the entries for these people.

Cross-references in the form “3.4” refer to chapter and section numbers in this dissertation—in this case, the fourth section of Chapter 3. The titles and locations of these sections can be found in the detailed table of contents that starts on page vi.

Dates (1): When I mention a range of dates, “1117-20” means all the years from 1117 to 1120, whereas “1117/20” refers to sometime between 1117 and 1120, inclusively. In this I follow Nathan Sivin’s usage.

Dates (2): for the sake of readability, I converted all Chinese dates into their equivalent in the Julian or Gregorian calendar and indicated the original dates in abbreviated form in parentheses. QL40.3.20, for example, refers to the  $ 20^{th} $ day of the  $ 3^{rd} $ month of the  $ 40^{th} $ year of the Qianlong (QL) reign. The abbreviations for Qing reign names are as follows:

Drug names: I refer to medicinal plants and minerals by their English names. The Chinese equivalents of these names can be found in Appendix 1, a glossary. More detailed information on these pharmaceutical ingredients—including their binomial Latin name and the literal meaning of their Chinese name—is tabulated in Appendix 2.

Fascicle numbers: in traditional Chinese bibliographies, fascicles (juan 卷) in works of two or three fascicles are usually referred to as “top fascicle” (卷上), “bottom fascicle” (卷下), and, if relevant, “middle fascicle” (卷中). I convert these into numbers. “Top fascicle” becomes juan 1 and “bottom fascicle” either juan 2 or juan 3 depending on whether there is a middle fascicle, which I always refer to as juan 2.

Glossary: a bilingual glossary of useful terms appears in Appendix 1 (pp. 707-741).

Laws: I cite Qing legal texts by their number in Huang Jingjia's 黃靜嘉 1970 edition of Xue

Yunsheng's 薛允升 Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 (1905). Appendix 7 lists all the laws cited.

Official histories: in the footnotes, references to official histories are to juan numbers followed by page numbers in the standard Zhonghua shuju editions.

## PDF第14页

Punctuation: Before the twentieth century, Chinese texts were almost never punctuated.

This is how I cite them, though I separate distinct clauses with spaces.

References to primary sources: my main goal when citing primary documents is to help the reader find the information cited. I refer to archival collections by abbreviations that can be found in Bibliography A, where each entry also explains what the rest of the citation format means. When I cite a medical book, I always indicate the name of the section from which I quote so that the reader can locate the relevant passage in any edition of that book.

Romanization: I adopt pinyin Romanization throughout. Even when I cite from a book that uses another method, I convert to pinyin without mentioning it. My only infraction to pinyin is to remove the “umlaut” in the syllables liè and niè, because this removal causes no ambiguity.

## Acknowledgments

This dissertation was long in the making and it took me through all sorts of emotions, from pleasure to angst, and from dismay at how much was left to do to the annual thrill of being “almost done.” But at least in this case, hell was not other people.
My most heartfelt thanks go to my advisor Susan Naquin, whose encouragements made me confident but never complacent. Her intolerance for murky thinking and writing—combined with her forbearance for many other shortcomings—helped immensely. Her tireless help in commenting on drafts even under the toughest circumstances not only enhanced this dissertation but also sharpened my skills as a historian. I can imagine few people being so dependably munificent.
I am grateful to Benjamin Elman, who, in the few months since he accepted to serve on my committee, has posed many stimulating questions and pointed to several exciting directions in which I could (and will) develop my reflections. I also thank Willard Peterson for taking time to comment on the long first draft of this manuscript and for making suggestions to improve it. And I am obliged to Charlotte Furth for agreeing to serve as my outside reader on such short notice despite the length of this dissertation.
All my professors at Princeton helped in various ways. At the beginning of this project, Ruth Rogaski reminded me that “madness in China” could not be treated like “Tintin in China,” and that it was always more fruitful to study interactions between people than interplays between abstract concepts. Buzzy Teiser gave me a brief lesson in writing that I am still digesting, and displayed extraordinary patience when I had to put other

## PDF第19页

# Mad acts, mad words, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law

I have a two-phrase saying: “At first you’re crazy. Past the immortals’ peak, you’re a divine immortal.” $ ^{1} $

我有兩句俗話 初頭是瘋癲 過了仙嶺是神仙

Lin Shiyuan 林時元, 1763

Lin Shiyuan was captured after he threw a roof tile to the governor of Fujian. Two slips of red paper attached to the tile were covered with “crazy and nonsensical words that are difficult to understand” (狂謬難解之詞). When the three highest officials in the province interrogated Lin to assess whether these were treasonous words in disguise, he spoke of dragon mothers and dream revelations, and claimed to be “the prime teacher-worthy” (第一師賢). The saying that Lin cited to prove he was not insane may have swayed listeners in other circumstances, but Lin’s relatives and neighbors insisted he was mad. $ ^{2} $ When asked what illness he suffered from, he replied: “In the third month of last year, I had a bit of a dizziness illness; they all said I was crazy and they invited a Taoist master to treat

## PDF第20页

me; I'm actually not crazy at all!" (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 他們都說我瘋癲 叫道

士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋.) The governor of Fujian sent men to investigate whether

Taoist master Wenyin 溫銀 had indeed treated Lin Shiyuan (he had) and whether he was

practicing “evil arts” 邪術 (he was not). Finally satisfied that Lin was crazy, the governor

sentenced him to immediate decapitation for “blithely circulating devious words, writing

placards, and rousing and confusing people’s hearts” (妄布邪言 書寫張貼 煽惑人心).³

Lin Shiyuan's case illustrates how difficult it is to discuss madness in historical contexts. Everyone around Lin seemed to understand and handle him differently. As recourse to a Taoist priest exemplifies, craziness was not always interpreted medically. Lin's adage, which emphasized the contextuality of madness, also clashed with the blatancy of insanity to those who witnessed it—for his relatives and neighbors, Lin was simply crazy. But Lin's relatives did not treat him as dangerous. But how, then, are we to explain why the governor of Fujian sentenced Lin to death even as he concluded that Lin was insane?

In this dissertation I explain the diverse and changing ways in which physicians, judicial officials, and ordinary people construed and handled the people, acts, and words that they labeled “mad.” One of my aims will be to highlight the disunity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion into an intelligible research object. In the rest of this introduction, I assess the historiography of insanity in China, I clarify the nature of my own approach to “madness,” and I justify why I shall focus on the fourteenth to nineteenth centuries, with particular attention to the Qing period (1644-1911).

## PDF第21页

### 1.1 Historiography: insights and oversights

Madness has just begun to attract the attention of historians of China. Vivien Ng's Madness in Late Imperial China (1990), until recently the only book on this topic, examined the way mad people were handled in Qing society and how their treatment changed over time. Ng was the first scholar to dissect a number of legal cases in which madness played a role, but the book's main thesis—that madness was transformed "from an illness to a form of criminal deviance" in the eighteenth century—is unpersuasive.

Ng’s reasoning rests on two Qing developments: 1) in the 1730s, the government ordered families to confine their mad relatives even when they had committed no crime; 2) in the eighteenth century, legislators meted out increasingly severe sentences upon those who killed someone in a fit of madness. Ng argued that these measures, which she attributed to a more-assumed-than-argued Manchu obsession with social control, represented a sea change in Chinese attitudes toward madness. But the “from illness to deviance” thesis is largely an artifact of the way Ng staged her documents. $ ^{4} $ When Ng claims that legal views replaced medical ones, it is in fact her analytical gaze that leaps from medical to legal documents, never to look back. As I shall show in chapters 11 to 15, medical, legal, religious, and popular views often overlapped, and they kept interacting in concrete social contexts throughout Qing times. Even Qing law consistently treated insanity as an illness.

Our understanding of madness in late imperial times still largely depends on research concerning Qing law. $ ^{5} $ We now understand the general sequence by which legislation evolved, but scholars have failed to see that laws and policies concerning mad people evolved

## PDF第22页

according to specific legal procedures, categories, and concerns. They have also tended to take madness as a ready-made target for the new laws, or to posit a uniform and unchanging referent: “lunatics” (Bünger 1950), “the mentally ill” (Nakamura 1973d and Hao 2002), “insane persons” (MacCormack 1992), and “insanity” and “the insane” (Ng 1990). Martha Li Chiu hinted at more complexity when she claimed that Qing law responded to “a particular kind of human crisis defined very early in classical medical texts under the label of kuang 狂” (Chiu 1981: 75), but she still suggested that the legal institution responded to a pre-existing object—albeit a culturally determined one—outside of itself.

I will show that the Qing legal field reacted neither to a condition (“madness”) nor to a kind of person (“the mad”), but first to individual acts of killing that officials attributed to insanity. “Killing because of madness” was a category of legal practice, not a label imposed upon a pre-existing reality. Through legislative and judicial practices, this penal denomination generated an official view of madness that differed from medical conceptions of kuang. $ ^{6} $

Until recently, there had been no serious historical work on whatever may be called madness in Chinese medicine. The many studies that tried to identify precursors to modern psychiatry did not take the original Chinese categories seriously. $ ^{7} $ Chinese historians have mostly focused on new discoveries, on categories that have survived into current “traditional Chinese medicine,” and on views that they consider compatible with those of modern science. Their research typically praises the “achievements” (成就) and “contributions” (贡献) of ancient physicians to the development of “Chinese psychiatry,” as if ancient Chinese

## PDF第23页

medicine had been working out its destiny unbeknownst of its practitioners. $ ^{8} $ Because symptomatic descriptions are more likely to give Chinese medicine a place in a “grand titration” (Needham 1969) of medical discoveries than obsolete etiologies and the drug-based treatments that went with them, this scholarly literature focuses primarily on the way ancient treatises described madness.

But “traditional Chinese psychiatry” did not exist. It never spoke of itself, was not recognized by any institution, and was not manifested in any specialized text. If we collect observations and techniques from various texts and treat them as “precursors” of something to come, we reduce the activities of Chinese medical writers to having hovered around—and ultimately failed to find—something they were not even looking for. Apart from the difficulty of claiming that people “contributed” to solving problems they never posed, this approach identifies superficial analogies with psychiatry without constructing a framework for the comparison. The loose array of analogies we end up with only makes sense within the by-default frames of modern psychiatry or modern “traditional Chinese medicine,” neither of which existed until the twentieth century. I shall discuss more fruitful ways of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatry in Chapter 3.

In recent Western scholarship, dian-kuang-xian 癲狂癎 has replaced “mental illness” as a focus of analysis, a typology that echoes recent Chinese compilations in which dian, kuang, and xian also form an inseparable trio. $ ^{9} $ This approach suitably avoids the dead end of “traditional Chinese psychiatry,” but it rests on two fragile assumptions: 1) that dian-kuang-

## PDF第24页

xian was a natural unit valid for all of Chinese history, and 2) that dian, kuang, and xian were "types of madness." Instead of saying that xian, a convulsive disorder, was a form of madness, why not claim (perhaps even more plausibly) that "madness was a form of seizure"? But even this would be missing the point: these three illnesses were not sub-types of a higher-order category like "madness." We will understand what made them stick together only when we stop considering their companionship as natural. As I will show throughout this dissertation, continuity in medical terminology helped to hide dramatic changes between ancient and later texts, most notably in the meaning of dian.

On medical approaches to madness, two recent works deserve particular attention: Angelika Messner's Medizinische Diskurse zu Irresein in China (2000) and Chen Hsiu-fen's "Medicine, Society, and the Making of Madness in Imperial China" (2003). Both cited abundant medical documents and explained them in some detail. Despite many perceptive comments, however, Messner made few connections between different medical understandings of madness, and thus misleadingly implied that Qing physicians were largely unaware of their predecessors or rival contemporaries. She also did not specify which views were dominant at any time and did not try to explain the changes that took place before the very late Qing, when translations of European psychiatric texts started to appear in China. $ ^{10} $ Messner's decision to cite most sources from an anthology of passages on "dian-kuang-xian" probably made it harder to recreate these larger pictures.

Chen Hsiu-fen's dissertation covered a longer span than Messner's book (from the second century BCE to about 1600) and was more attentive to change. Notably, Chen explained changes in the classification of dian, kuang, and xian, and mentioned the important

## PDF第25页

shift from Wind to mucus in the etiology of these disorders. Despite the title of her work, however, she made few connections between medical discourses and the society that produced them. Like Messner, she also placed more emphasis on classification and doctrine than on therapeutic practices, and she presented little critical reflection on what she meant by "madness," in spite of a long introduction devoted to this problem.

Martha Li Chiu's dissertation "Mind, Body, and Illness in a Chinese Medical Tradition" (1986) stands out as more reflexive. It highlighted the diversity of conceptions of illness found in the Grand Basis (Taisu 太素) version of the Inner Canon, and designed an original approach in order to define a Chinese medical object comparable to "mental illness": the cluster of mostly "mental" symptoms by which the Canon described kuang. By distinguishing "physical" from "mental" symptoms (rather than illnesses), however, Chiu reasserted at a lower level the very divide she attempted to overcome. In Chapter 3, I shall propose an alternative way of characterizing medical kuang as a coherent research object.

### 1.2 Michel Foucault and madness

Although madness is not inherently a “Foucaultian” topic and Foucault never wrote on Chinese history, his works can facilitate reflections on how to turn “madness” into an object of historical inquiry. Foucault’s preface to the first edition (1961)—titled Folie et déraison—of History of Madness expressed the impossibility of ever retrieving “madness itself” (Foucault 2006 [1961]: xxxii), because describing it with the tools of reason would mean discussing it with the very language that had already silenced it. $ ^{11} $ Instead, the book tried to

## PDF第26页

trace the history of “the historical ensemble – notions, institutions, judicial and police measures, scientific concepts – which hold captive a madness whose wild state can never in itself be reconstituted.” $ ^{12} $ Foucault sought between these fields the kinds of displacements, isomorphisms, and reversals that his friend Georges Dumézil had found between the mythologies of different Indo-European societies. $ ^{13} $ This approach is perhaps what gave History of Madness its “extremely strong conceptual architecture” (Gros 1997: 5). Foucault argued, for instance, that the treatment of madness in horizons as diverse as philosophy, institutional gestures, and psychiatry took the same general form of exclusion and silencing. $ ^{14} $

Though many scholars maintain that Foucault “snatched madness away from medical imperialism to restitute it to us as a historical and ethnographic object” (Charuty 1985: 8), History of Madness did not thoroughly historicize insanity. Even as he brought to light the referential complexity of “madness,” Foucault also tried to show that different fields were ultimately targeting the same entity under different names. By claiming, for instance, that the “great confinement” targeted the heralds of unreason (the mad, but also sodomites, sorcerers, etc.), he tried to correlate the physical removal of these people with Descartes’s exclusion of madness from the cogito. It is as though Foucault had conjured up “unreason” (a term that did not exist in the seventeenth century) to serve as counterpart to Reason, debates on whose nature were then busying philosophers. $ ^{15} $ The term “experiences

## PDF第27页

of madness” (expériences de la folie) that appeared so often in Foucault’s book also seemed to imply a common object to all these experiences.

“Madness” in History of Madness thus meant at least three things: a) the “notions, institutions, juridical and police measures, and scientific concepts” that created historically shifting experiences of madness; b) the Ding an sich (“madness itself”)—carefully black-boxed by Foucault—that tied this ensemble together; and c) (crucially for Foucault’s Kantian critique of subject-centered philosophies) the entity the silencing of which was the transcendental condition of possibility of all the “experiences” discussed in the book. This tension is probably part of what still makes Foucault’s book uniquely attractive.

I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for phenomenology, madness exists but it is not a thing, whereas we should say on the contrary that madness does not exist, but that it is not nothing for that reason (mais qu’elle n’est pas rien pour autant).”¹⁸ Words have referents even if these are not natural objects. One of my aims will be to highlight this artificial unity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion

## PDF第28页

into an intelligible research object. Throughout, I shall retain Foucault's sensitivity to how specific historical objects emerge, and to how discourses take roots in social practices.

### 1.3 Substances and their referents: “what is madness?” and what is “madness”?

Meaning and referentiality are immensely difficult philosophical issues that I will not attempt to solve. But deflecting metaphysical questions is a pre-condition for understanding how referents emerge historically—that is, how practices attach words to specific realities.

Nouns are the Trojan horses of ontology. “A substantive misleads one into looking for a substance. One populates the world with ethereal beings, the shadowy companions of the substantive. One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20} $

I shall therefore treat “what is madness?” not as a metaphysical but as a methodological question. For my purposes, “mad” will be what counted as mad in specific historical contexts, and “madness” will be shorthand for “acts, words, or people that were sometimes designated by a Chinese word that translates as ‘mad’ in English.” I speak of

## PDF第29页

“acts, words, or people” because documents rarely problematized madness as a whole, and because making such a distinction sidesteps the distracting question of what madness is.

“Madness” did not precede the representations that people made of “it” (remember the longhand). And what held together demonic possession, postpartum confusion, and furious fits occasioned by high fevers was not a common ontological substrate, but notions that Qing people found intelligibly related: mainly kuang 狂, feng 瘋, and dian 顛／癲. None of these terms was exclusive to any field. Ritual specialists used them; so did doctors and legislators. And most texts used these words to label similar symptoms.

What makes comparisons possible is that Chinese people—experts and commoners alike—identified as mad the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we usually include in an intuitive category of “madness.” $ ^{21} $ The legal and medical practices I study often relied on this self-evidence, on people’s intuition that some acts naturally deserved to be called kuang, dian, or feng, three words that usually meant “mad” or “acting madly” in the same vague sense as the English words I just translated them by.

I will still strive to understand what each of these terms meant, but I will not do so in the abstract. We could apply to “madness” what Ludwig Wittgenstein said about “time”: “If I know how I am to use the word ‘time’, if I understand it in the most diverse contexts, then I know quite precisely ‘what time is’, and no formulation can make this clearer to me” (Baker et al. 2003: 487). As I will show, the referents, connotations, and performative force of kuang, dian, and feng emerged through historical practices and depended on the practical contexts and the network of notions within which these words were deployed.

What was unique to fields like Qing law or medicine was less the statements they made about mad acts, mad words, and mad people than the scripts in which they made these

## PDF第30页

statements meaningful and consequential. A “script” is a scenario according to which a process—e.g., the course of an illness or a crime—was understood or expected to unfold. These scripts were templates for describing reality, but also interpretive frameworks that guided people’s interventions in fluid processes. Physicians deployed such scripts when they treated patients, judicial officials when they sentenced crimes.

To avoid making generalizations that will predictably not apply to all people of all periods, I use the notion of “prototype.” $ ^{22} $ Most definitions are prototypical in that they mark out the most typical members of a given category (“tables,” “burrowing animals”). Categories contain more than prototypes, but their members gradually radiate away from the prototypical hub. A three-legged chair is thus a less prototypical chair than a four-legged one, the platypus a less prototypical mammal than the elephant.

Since prototypes also apply to the way we interpret events, I will call “prototypical scripts” the recurring scenarios by which experts explained how illnesses or crimes took place. The script “internal depletion occurs → Wind intrudes → illness ensues,” for instance, was omnipresent in medical treatises from the seventh and eighth centuries (see 3.3). New scripts emphasizing the pathology of mucus gradually replaced Wind-based ones and came to dominate medical practice from the fourteenth century onwards.

Prototypical definitions and scenarios were not the only ones available, and they were neither unchanging nor indivisible, but we find them in enough places to suspect that they shaped the way a large number of physicians or officials reflected on insanity. Non-prototypical explanations need not be seen as “exceptions” (since prototypes are effects of practice, not rules), but as different ways of construing things. As we shall see, doctors often purposely proposed atypical explanations in order to distance themselves from a prototype

## PDF第31页

they and their patients or readers were aware of. A hunt for prototypes will therefore help to identify what currents were dominant in a given field of practice.

Without pretending to touch “the Real,” my approach pays close attention to the complex array of phenomena that hide behind the encompassing notion of “madness.” It recognizes that madness was often self-evident, yet highly dependent on cultural explanations (as in the case of Lin Shiyuan with which this chapter started); that insanity is deeply grounded in situation, yet can be compared across cultures (through cross-culturally recognizable acts); and that Chinese law and medicine handled madness in conflicting, yet mutually intelligible ways. My method also allows me to consider medical and legal objects on a par with less technical ones, simply because they shared referents. The following chapters will test how fruitful this method is for studying madness historically.

### 1.4 Medicine and law in Qing times: convergence and diversity

Even without psychiatry and specialized institutions like mental asylums, Qing people possessed a rich array of ways to discuss crazy behavior, mad words, and insane people. For the first time under the Qing, law treated homicides committed in a state of madness as a distinct kind of crime. New legislation on this issue stirred debates on how to control mad men and women, and led to frequent adaptations in the severity of their punishments. Qing physicians recorded how they treated mad patients with minerals, plants, and animal parts that they decocted according to the rich medical lore they had both inherited and enhanced. Several men who took themselves for gods or the emperor were meanwhile executed for sedition. Mad figures proliferated in novels and short stories, and we can even glean anecdotes on eminent people who spoke or acted strangely. Scattered

## PDF第32页

evidence also exists about how madness was handled in less literate social circles, often but not always through religious ritual.

In the interrelated ways in which they explained and handled madness, the three contemporaneous fields of homicide law, medicine, and trials for sedition form a manifold cultural formation, a network of discourses and practices that is most profitably studied as a whole if we want to appreciate what any of them meant. $ ^{23} $ To understand why Qing law discussed insanity and handled “mad criminals” the way it did, we need to compare it to the medical and religious approaches that were also current at the time. And we shall appreciate the nature of medical treatments all the better if we grasp the ways in which other fields handled mad behavior.

Since madness was never an important focus of medical reflections, changes in medical views of madness are difficult to study. Kuang was the object of no specialized literature, and no debate concerning it had any major impact on doctrinal developments. But since late imperial physicians were expected to treat all disorders—from furor to furuncles—the way they handled any illness usually depended on how they understood pathogenesis, vitality, and general therapeutic principles. I posit that changes in approaches to kuang can be explained only if we understand broader transformations in medical thinking. The study of a single disorder can thus help to elucidate broader issues in Chinese medical history.

But medical discourses and practices should not be studied with a periodization derived from legal history. I can analyze Qing legislation concerning madness without much insight into the laws of previous dynasties, but Qing medicine took roots in much deeper soils. Medical descriptions of insanity usually relied on a corpus that was compiled more than

## PDF第33页

1,700 years before the advent of the Qing (1644-1911). Qing practices also derived from the profound changes in medical thinking that took place from the eleventh to the fourteenth century and again in the sixteenth century. Because medical history poses such a singular challenge, this dissertation is divided into two asymmetric sections. Part One, which focuses on medicine, spans from the Warring States period (5 $ ^{th} $ century–221 BCE) to the Qing. Part Two concerns everything else than medicine, but concentrates on Qing history. Part Two is also where I compare medicine with other fields of practice more explicitly. Each large section starts with a prologue that highlights the issues I will try to tackle in it.

The chapters of Part One focus on the history of Chinese medical approaches to madness. Chapter 3 is a conceptual and historical introduction to the other medical chapters. I explain how kuang was described in the Yellow Emperor corpus (1 $ ^{st} $ century BCE – 3 $ ^{rd} $ century CE) and I map the large cluster of “Wind illnesses” in which kuang and dian were classified from roughly the third to the tenth century. This chapter reminds the reader that Chinese illness classifications were not guided by distinctions between the mental and the physical. In Chapter 4, I explain how Chinese physicians from the eleventh to the fourteenth century re-scripted kuang as an illness caused no longer by Wind, but by mucus and Fire. I emphasize the complexity of medical change, the role of individual thinkers in it, and the way new doctrines were transmitted and enhanced through concrete social networks. Chapter 5 explains how elite physicians adopted a synthetic approach to both illness and textual composition between 1360 and 1520. This chapter focuses on three interrelated issues: the diffusion of medical knowledge and techniques, the sway that “Danxi” approaches held over Ming medical thinkers, and the strong intertextuality of the Chinese medical tradition.

Chapter 6 examines debates concerning the proper treatment of madness between 1520 and 1650, when some doctors criticized aggressive therapies and proposed alternative

## PDF第34页

cures based on new views of human vitality. Chapter 7 confirms the wide currency of the mucus etiology of madness in Qing medicine, and explains a new transformation in the meaning of dian. It also highlights new trends in the medical understanding of madness from the late seventeenth to the late nineteenth century. Chapter 8 describes how physicians made sense of possession symptoms after 1600 and focuses on competition between different kinds of healers to treat these symptoms. Chapter 9 concerns the gendered ways in which doctors wrote about and treated the mad behavior of women from roughly 1550 to 1900. I also explain the etiologies and main treatments of “postpartum madness,” trying to draw the implications of discussing it side-by-side with kuang in a historical study of madness.

In these medical chapters I shall explain why the category of “Wind illnesses” fell apart between the twelfth and the fourteenth century, how mucus and Fire replaced Wind as the identified cause of a large number of disorders, what the concrete content of “Jin-Yuan medicine” was and how it evolved during the Ming dynasty, how a flexible conception of illness transformed the writing of medical treatises, by what channels a prominent doctrinal synthesis spread during the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries and dominated medical thinking for several centuries after that, and why Chinese medical doctrines can appear so integrative. These are all important issues in Chinese medical history.

Part Two starts with Chapter 11, which explains how the new crime of “killing because of madness” emerged in the 1660s and later bloomed into a set of original views concerning mad acts and mad people. This chapter focuses on the conceptions and practices that legal institutions generated from 1669 to 1762, when a simple monetary payment to the family of the victim was the mandated punishment for “killing because of madness.”

Chapter 12 explains why laws concerning homicides committed because of madness multiplied and became increasingly severe from the 1750s to the end of the Qing. Chapter

## PDF第35页

13 contrasts the legal handling of crimes committed by mad people and medical treatments of dian and kuang with the way commoners handled their crazy relatives and explained the origins of their madness. I emphasize the contextuality of the terminology used to refer to odd words and behavior.

Very few words have survived from the men and women who suffered from what the people around them called madness. I use the exceptions to write the next two chapters, which are concerned with speech, writing, and power. Chapter 14 uses judicial depositions and other surviving texts to document a few “madmen’s” outlook on their world, their grievances, and the nature of the fantasies that estranged them from their relatives and neighbors. This chapter is a short but essential counterpoint to the other ones, which all present insane people as silent objects of discourses and practices. Chapter 15 shows how some fantasies could prove deadly to those who presented them in writing to officials. In the trials that followed these offerings of texts, words that may have sounded crazy in other contexts were treated as highly subversive because they recycled imperial symbols or resembled the discourse of organized rebels. Chapter 16, an epilogue, reflects on the way meaning emerges from practices, and compares medical and legal views of language, illness, and behavior.

Even as I survey many fields and eras, I neglect many aspects of the rich topic of Chinese interpretations and uses of madness. But my investigations should ease research into these topics. Any study of the functions of mad speech in poetry and fiction should benefit from my findings. My explanations of how common people treated their mad relatives will also provide useful contrasts for studies of figures of madness in Buddhism (both Chan and Tantric: see Strickmann 1994, Shahar 1998, and parts of Faure 1994), in traditions of protest (Schneider 1980), and even in painting and calligraphy (see Hsiung 1984 and Cahill 1990). I

## PDF第36页

shall not inquire in depth into religious and ritual treatments of madness. Judith Boltz (1985) has done some related work for the Song and Michel Strickmann (2002) for earlier periods, and there must have been similar material for the Qing (as shown by Katz 2009: 105-7), but my knowledge of the Taoist and Buddhist literatures is too shallow to present findings comparable to those I made about law and medicine.

Unlike anthropologists, who can prompt information by posing questions, historians can only find data in texts that were created according to goals that differed from those of the modern investigator. Throughout, I examine how the texts I use emerged from concrete activities, and how these activities shaped the content of these documents.

## PDF第37页

Part One

# MEDICAL OBJECTS AND PRACTICES

Warring States to Qing

(300 BCE – 1911)

## PDF第38页

## 2 

Prologue to Part One

Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfer

Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In addition to painting a more complete image of medical views of madness, this broad

## PDF第39页

approach also deemphasizes the contrast between “elite medicine” and “popular practice” by showing that a large range of literate practitioners lived between these two abstract extremes.

My broad reading of medical texts will also help to show that although madness was rarely at the core of doctrinal controversies, medical discussions of insanity quickly absorbed new doctrinal developments. The seven chapters of Part One will thus narrate how conceptions of kuang and its related illnesses evolved along with the rest of medical precepts and practices.

Throughout, I use “illness” as a translation of the term bing 病 and as a label for the kinds of disorders that Chinese medicine discussed: clusters of clinically relevant symptoms that were grouped under one particular name. The problem in translating bing is not to determine whether that term really meant “illness,” “disease,” or “disorder,” but to find a rendering that neither clashes with the meaning of bing nor brings undue connotations into our analysis. To some extent, a translation is good because of what it avoids. Translating bing as “illness” will not presuppose that all bing defined in Chinese medical texts were “diseases,” a term that now seems to imply rootedness in biological processes. I also avoid “disorder” because it would make Chinese concepts of madness sound too close to those of the DSM (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders), the influential manual that calls most psychiatric derangements “disorders” (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder and the like). $ ^{4} $

## PDF第40页

There were effectively no “types of madness” in Chinese medicine, just one madness—kuang or diankuang—with many symptoms. Historians usually interpret dian as a form of madness distinct from kuang (see 1: 5n9), but I will argue that a clear symptomatic distinction between dian and kuang only crystallized in the nineteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Kuang referred to various disorders of perception, affect, language, and especially behavior. People were labeled kuang when they lost some or all social competence—a woman ran outside naked, a man became delirious or took himself for a god. Physicians may have focused on patent mad behavior because they were only called to treat men and women whom their relatives saw as patently mad. Most feelings of mental discomfort were probably not reported to doctors, and thus rarely appeared in medical documents. I will call the symptoms of kuang “psycho-behavioral.”

Although madness was defined socially, physicians usually interpreted insane behavior through categories that drew diagnosis away from the patient's daily context. Armed with notions such as "stasis," "depletion," or "Liver Fire," they reduced to organic imbalances problems that had probably developed from social and interpersonal events. Perhaps because of doctors' emphasis on the organic roots of mad behavior, their chief therapeutic goal was usually to stop disabling symptoms rather than to foster a patient's wellbeing. Even physicians who knew that madness had social roots rarely attempted to reinstate their patients into meaningful social relations. They may have judged this to be the family's

## PDF第41页

task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an

affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $

Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子).

The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. A formal analysis would show that this kind of “scheme transfer” (Bourdieu 1990: 250) was the main mode of change in medical understandings of madness throughout imperial history.

But it was of course people—physicians—who tried to explain madness by borrowing scripts from other illnesses or who drew on current conceptions of vitality to treat insane symptoms in new ways. They based their interpretations on clinical experience, observed symptoms, and deductions from familiar concepts, but the way in which they

## PDF第42页

couched these interpretations also owed much to the strong intertextuality of the Chinese medical tradition. Before they compiled a treatise or a formulary, medical writers had indeed read a large number of treatises and formularies. I will show in Chapter 5 that from the fourteenth century onwards, even the most educated physicians composed treatises by collating excerpts from other medical books. The composition of medical treatises by textual collage favored textual densification around certain issues and thus allowed certain scripts and approaches to be propagated more than others.

One major repository of medical arguments throughout imperial history was the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經), the earliest versions of which were compiled in the first century BCE on the basis of earlier materials. $ ^{8} $ Although the text of its main versions was stabilized in the eleventh century, $ ^{9} $ the parts of the Canon that doctors considered central to medical thinking changed drastically. Liu Wansu 劉完素 (ca. 1120-1200), for example, relied on a short passage from pian 74 of the Basic Questions to argue that most illnesses were caused by Fire and Heat (4.1). Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228), for his part, built his notion of “therapy by counter-affect” on the dynamics of Qi as described in pian 5 of the Basic Questions (4.2) whereas Li Gao 李昪 (1181-1251) found other excerpts to support his view that a man's vitality depended on the health of his stomach (4.3). When they eulogized “the Inner Canon,” writers were not referring to an invariant book, but to the precepts that their own interpretive tradition identified as central. The authority of the Canon was thus rarely questioned, but no doctor ever followed all of its precepts.

Where physicians displayed remarkable stability was in the way they described madness. Borrowing formulations from the Inner Canon, medical writers described the

## PDF第43页

symptoms of kuang with roughly the same words from the first century BCE to the late nineteenth century, probably assuming that readers would immediately recognize these words and what they referred to. Chapter 3, to which we now turn, begins by explaining how the two main versions of the Inner Canon described the symptoms of madness.

## PDF第44页

Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine

Concepts and early developments,  $ 3^{rd} $ century BCE – ca. 1150

This chapter provides conceptual and factual tools for understanding how medical treatments of insanity changed despite how stably Chinese doctors kept describing mad behavior. As I pointed out in Chapter 1, Chinese people called “mad” the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we intuitively include under the category of madness. Here I survey the way ancient medical texts described such symptoms and analyze how physicians explained and treated psycho-behavioral pathology before about 1150.

I start by examining the prototypical symptoms of kuang as defined in the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (first century BCE), a pivotal corpus from which medical writers drew their descriptions of madness for most of imperial history. I then criticize the way modern scholars have used "mental illness" as a foil against which to define Chinese approaches, or even as a reason to refuse comparisons between psychiatry and Chinese medicine. As I shall argue, Chinese medicine and modern psychiatry are comparable because both purport to explain and treat psycho-behavioral symptoms. As I explain next, Chinese doctors assumed that mental activities "supervened" upon organic processes. $ ^{1} $ This is the most critical feature of Chinese medical approaches to insanity. I then map the relations between kuang and proximate illnesses in a seventh-century treatise that attributed most psycho-behavioral pathologies to harmful Winds. The fourth section explains how dian (瑱, 顛, or 癲)—"seizure sickness"—came to refer to insanity by borrowing from non-medical usage.

## PDF第45页

The second half of this chapter traces how the growing prominence of mucus as a cause of illness starting in the eleventh century deeply altered the way in which Chinese physicians understood and handled madness. I first describe the origins of the notion of “mucus,” a pathological fluid that was not mentioned in the Inner Canon. I then review how the medical activism and publishing activities of the northern-Song government (976-1127) helped to create a pool of common references that facilitated medical debates and intertextual borrowings. For example, many Song doctors debated the claim made in a government-sponsored formulary that “Wind strokes” could be treated with vomit-inducing (“emetic”) drugs. Just around the same time (the turn of the twelfth century), these mucus-targeting emetics were starting to be used to treat madness.

### 3.1 The kuang 狂 core in the Yellow Emperor corpus

Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen in time" (Lo 2002: xxix).

The Inner Canon was the most important textual corpus in the history of Chinese medicine. For most of the two millennia that followed its first compilation (in the first century BCE), it served to doctors and medical writers as a repository of authoritative

## PDF第46页

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity.

One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier

Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸

經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when

eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang-

Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later

physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵

When this [vessel] is moved, one ails from: shivers and ailing from coldness; fondness for stretching; continual yawning, blackness in the center of the forehead; ailing from swelling; when severe, detestation for people and fire; when hearing the sound of wood, panicked alarm; panicky heart; the desire to close doors and windows and dwell in isolation; when the ailment is severe, the desire to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run. These constitute shin reversal. For these [ailments], the Yang-Brilliance vessel controls the treatment.

# Two passages from the Basic Questions appear to have been comments on the above.

Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $

“When the illness is serious, [one] discards clothes and runs about, mounts heights and sings. One may even not eat for several days. One will leap walls and ascend rooftops. One would not be able to climb [in such a way] ordinarily, yet when one is ill one unexpectedly can. Why is this?” Qibo said: “The four limbs are the root of all Yang [vessels]. When Yang is flourishing, the four limbs are replete, which makes one able to climb high.” The Yellow Emperor said: “Why does one shed clothes and run?” Qibo said: “When Heat is flourishing in the body, one sheds clothes and wants to run.” The Yellow Emperor: “Why does [the afflicted person] speak wildly, scold close or distant relatives indiscriminately, and sing?” Qibo: “Abundance of Yang makes one speak wildly, scold and revile without discriminating between close and distant relatives, and feel no desire to eat. Having no desire to eat, one runs wildly.”

<div style="text-align: center;">This passage cast aside physical ills and replaced them with behavioral symptoms: refusal to eat, indiscriminate scolding, and climbing walls and ascending roofs.</div>


For the rest of imperial history, medical treatises described kuang with formulaic language derived from chapters 30 and 49 of the Basic Questions, which were not even explicitly concerned with kuang. Only one passage rivaled them in textual dominance—pian 22 (“Dian and kuang” 癲狂篇) of the Divine Pivot, which described “five mental syndromes [that] form a recognizable cluster around the focal concern of kuang” (Chiu 1986: 303). $ ^{9} $

When kuang starts to arise, one is initially dejected and tends to be forgetful, angry, and fearful. This originates in anguish or hunger.... When kuang begins to manifest itself, [one] rests little and one does not feel hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd and wise, or as being highly ranked. One is prone to scolding and yelling, never ceasing day or night.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] a tendency to be frightened and to laugh, a fondness for songs and music, and wild activity without cease originate in great fear.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] disorderly vision and hearing and a tendency to call out are produced by insufficient Qi.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] abundant eating, a tendency to see ghosts and spirits, and a tendency to laugh [to oneself] with no external display originate in having greatly rejoiced at something. $ ^{10} $

狂 目妄見 耳妄聞 喜呼者 少氣

# The above were five possible manifestations of a multifaceted illness that demanded different therapeutic interventions depending on its symptoms and postulated origins.

Descriptions of madness from the Inner Canon were soon copied into shorter texts, such as the Canon of Problems (Nanjing 難經; a second-century synthesis of the Inner Canon), Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses (Shanghan zabing lun 傷寒雜病論; ca. 200 CE), Huangfu Mi's 皇甫謐 AB Canon of Acumoxa (Zhenjiu jiayijing 針灸甲乙經; 256/262), and Wang Xi's 王熙 Canon of the Pulse (Maijing 脈經; third century).¹¹ The most commonly cited in later imperial times was the Canon of Problems.¹² "How are the illnesses kuang and dian to be distinguished?" (狂癲之病，何以別之), inquired the 59th problem.

When kuang starts to manifest itself, one rarely rests and one does not feel hungry.

One considers oneself to be elevated or of superior virtue, to be shrewd and wise, or to be of high rank, thus acting in a conceited manner. One will laugh recklessly and enjoy singing and making music, moving recklessly without cease. When dian starts to manifest itself, one's thoughts are unhappy. One stares straight and falls backward or forward. The Yin and Yang in all three sections of the vessel [where the pulse is taken] will be flourishing. (My translation; cf. Unschuld 1986a: 527.)

The passage on kuang was cited almost verbatim from pian 22 of the Divine Pivot.

## PDF第50页

Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE) is the earliest extant work proposing to treat psycho-behavioral symptoms with drug-based remedies. $ ^{13} $ It generally attributed fits of madness (發狂)—discussed in a section on Yang-brilliance disorders—to excessive Heat. Zhang Ji was probably describing the ability of high fever to create dementia. The symptoms were ghostly visions (also mentioned in Divine Pivot 22 and the Canon of the Pulse), wild language (妄語; Basic Questions 30), and ceaseless wild movements (妄行不休: Divine Pivot 22 and Canon of Problems 59). For fits of madness, the Treatise proposed to “make Blood descend” (下血); it prescribed “Decoction of resistance” (抵當湯) to treat “as if manic” cases (如狂, less severe than fits). For serious disorders of the Yang-brilliance vessel, with madness or possession symptoms, the “Great Qi-supporting decoction” (大承氣湯) was recommended. These recipes were all purgatives designed to expel the inner Heat that was thought to cause the symptoms. The Great Qi-supporting decoction later became an important remedy for madness (see 4.1, 4.2).

Until the nineteenth century, physicians described the core symptoms of kuang with language borrowed from the Inner Canon and its shorter derivative works, adopting in passing the notion that madness mostly meant unusual behavior. I am not claiming that the real symptoms displayed by people identified as kuang did not change (we lack the data to tell), but that medical treatises kept defining kuang by the same prototypical symptoms for almost two millennia. The strong intertextuality of the Chinese medical tradition, which I explain in more detail in Chapter 5, helps to understand why these descriptions endured for so long.

## PDF第51页

I noted in Chapter 1 that Martha Li Chiu (1986) scoured the Inner Canon to find a Chinese medical object comparable to mental illness, and that she settled on kuang because it was mostly characterized by "mental symptoms." But by describing symptoms (rather than illnesses) as either mental or physical, she reproduced a problematic dichotomy at a lower level without uncovering the assumptions it hides. Below, I propose an alternative way of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatric ones. My goal is to foster our understanding of medical discourse on kuang by breaking free from the preconceptions that lurk beneath the notions of "mental" and "physical."

### 3.2 Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine

Comparisons: why not “mental illness”?

Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. From the era of “mad-doctoring” to the latest edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (or DSM; see APA 1994), behavioral symptoms have been at the center of psychiatric attention.

The preconception that the notion “mental illness” depends on a mind-body dualism has also hindered comparisons. There are indeed many distinct ways (symptomatic, etiological, and therapeutic) in which illnesses can be called “mental” without implying a dualism of substance: their symptoms may be defined cognitively (defects of reason, representation, or perception, which are widely held to be “mental” properties); they may be said to be caused

## PDF第52页

by mental processes (psychodynamics); or they may be considered treatable by action on mental processes (psychological or “moral” healing).

Since about 1980 in American psychiatry, disorders have only been “mental” at the level of symptoms. The DSM defines disorders by their manifestations, but it assumes that these clusters of symptoms are caused by discrete neuro-chemical processes that will eventually be understood. $ ^{15} $ This folding of mind on brain chemistry is not new: some even claim that psychiatry came of age when it started to trace mental and behavioral disorders to brain pathology in the nineteenth century. $ ^{16} $ Modern psychiatry’s discarding of psycho-dynamics has indeed rendered “the mind” almost irrelevant. $ ^{17} $ So much of psychiatry has focused on the brain (first on its purported “lesions,” now on its chemistry) that we must conclude psychiatric explanations do not depend on a dualism of body and mind.

There are of course differences between psychiatric approaches and Chinese medical understanding of psycho-behavioral pathology, and many reasons to expect Chinese medical objects to differ from psychiatric ones. One of Foucault's most fruitful insights was to show how institutional sites of observation (asylums, clinics, prisons) shaped the objects of disciplines like medicine, psychiatry, and the "human sciences" (2006 [1961], 1989 [1963], 1977 [1975]). In nineteenth-century Europe, asylums where "paralytic," "mad," epileptic,

## PDF第53页

and “idiotic” patients were regrouped provided nascent psychiatry with a core of clinical

cases on which to hone its conceptual instruments. $ ^{18} $ Chinese medical practices, on the other

hand, were not stabilized by institutional boundaries, and most Chinese treatises were textual

collages, not studies based on the examination or treatment of a group of segregated patients.

And for most of imperial history, there were no “types of madness,” only one disorder

(kuang or diankuang) characterized by a variety of pathological acts and affects. Whereas

nineteenth-century Western psychiatrists referred to different clusters of these symptoms by

different names, Chinese texts included many illness scenarios under very few appellations.

Despite the simplicity of this nosology and the marked differences between how psychiatrists and Chinese physicians produced knowledge, kuang and psychiatric illnesses remain comparable, since both have been concerned with psycho-behavioral symptoms. The disorders next to which kuang was discussed (e.g. xian 癎 seizures and dian 癎 “falling sickness”) also resembles the cluster of illnesses that early psychiatry targeted on the basis of the kinds of patients who were confined in asylum. And as we saw above in the section on the Inner Canon, kuang was usually described as being characterized by overt “behavioral dislocation” (Berrios 1985: 166), states of agitation manifested by naked running, strange talking, delusions of grandeur, and indiscriminate scolding. Kuang is therefore comparable with mania and other old psychiatric constructs that focused on the same symptoms.

If psychiatric disorders are mental merely at the level of symptoms, if these “mental” symptoms can be shown to be largely behavioral, and if we accept that psychiatry has not relied on mind-body dualism, then we have cleared the ground for meaningful comparisons.

Kuang was largely a behavioral disorder. Indeed, Chinese medicine shared “psychiatry’s time-

## PDF第54页

honored concern with alterations of social behavior" (Fabrega 1975: 1500). The next section explains how we can understand Chinese medical approaches to this kind of pathology.

## “Supervenience” and its consequences

To help make sense of Chinese understandings of psycho-behavioral disorders, let me borrow the concept of “supervenience” from modern philosophy of mind (see Kim 1993; Savellos and Yalcin 1995). For our purposes, we can simplify it as follows: process A is said to “supervene” upon process B when changes in process A (an idea or an emotion, for instance) are always accompanied by changes in process B (say, brain chemistry).

Most Chinese medical explanations of madness adopted a strong version of supervenience: not only were manic symptoms manifested in clinically detectable organic processes (excessive inner Fire, depletion of Cardiac Qi, etc.), they were also said to be caused by these processes and they were treated by drugs that acted directly on these processes. The difference with modern psychiatry is that whereas the latter identifies imbalances (in neurotransmitters, for example) that apply almost exclusively to psychiatric disorders, Chinese medicine claimed that Fire could cause any number of symptoms, including many we would consider purely physical (fevers, bleeding, ticklishness, etc.).

The supervenience between mental and physiological processes was inscribed in Chinese medical terminology. Most physicians thought that madness occurred because the Heart (xin 心) or its residing Spirit (shen 神) could not fulfill its functions. Translating xin as “heart-mind” highlights the uneasiness of rendering into English what was self-evident in Chinese: the junction of mind with a physical structure in a single word. $ ^{19} $ As a hinge between the “mental” and the “physical,” xin allowed Chinese physicians to trace psycho-

## PDF第55页

behavioral pathology to an imbalance in fluids, to mental processes, or to both at the same time without dissonance. We find the same double denotation in shen, which could refer to spiritual or vital functions, but also to the fluid that was thought to assure these functions.

Common expressions could accentuate either meaning of Heart or Spirit. In “the Spirit is confused” (神昏), shen referred to mental functions, whereas in “the Heart harbors the Spirit” (心藏神), Spirit meant a refined form of Qi (here described in spatial language). Shi xin 失心 is better rendered as “loss of mind” than as “loss of heart,” not only because “loss of heart” implies dejection rather than mindlessness in English, but also because what was “lost” according to the Chinese expression was not the heart as an organ but the functions it was thought to assure. However, when a medical text claimed that “failed Blood flushed the Heart” (败血冲心), the physical organ was unambiguously involved.

These two notions of Heart and Spirit provided an established semantic path and a ready-made conceptual schema for tracing psycho-behavioral pathology to physiological processes. They allowed seamless shifts between the mental and the physiological, two domains that English speakers are used to describing with distinct terms even when they do not embrace a mind-body dualism.

This being said, both ancient texts and late-imperial physicians could make clear distinctions between the body and mental activities. Pian 25 of the Basic Questions, for instance, explained the difference between Spirit and “physical form” (xing 形), or body. In a roughly contemporaneous text, we read that “in madness, the body is not damaged” (狂者形不虧).²⁰ An eighteenth-century physician said of a patient that “his body seemed calm, but his Heart was restless” (身似靜心多動), whereas a preface to a work of the same period

## PDF第56页

explained that “in past and present there have been two great disorders: those of the Heart [mind!] and those of the Body” (古今有大病二 其一在心 其一在身). $ ^{21} $

These distinctions between “Heart” and “Spirit” on the one hand and “embodied person” (shen 身) or “physical form” on the other were not used to divide disorders between “mental” and “physical.” All illnesses were grounded in organic imbalances. Diverse illnesses also communicated because they were supposedly treatable with the same remedies. Among the recipes Zhang Lu 張璐 (1617-1699) recommended for kuang in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通), we find a “Dragon-tooth soul-clarifying decoction” (龍齒清魂湯), whose recipe appeared in the section on “tremors” (顫震); the “Angelica Blood-livening decoction” (當歸活血湯), explained in “Blood accumulations” (蓄血); and another brew for which the reader was referred to the headache section. $ ^{22} $ No mental/physical division organized the social organization of therapy: late-imperial physicians were expected to treat all illnesses (except perhaps those of the eyes and skin). We are now ready to explain how kuang and related illnesses were explained in pre-twelfth century treatises and formularies.

### 3.3 Kuang and Wind illnesses

The Inner Canon and its derivative works attributed psycho-behavioral disorders to imbalances in the circulation tracts, insufficient Qi, emotional disturbances, or excessive Heat. The first major change (or addition) to this manifold etiology occurred in the first few centuries CE, when medical texts started to trace madness to onslaughts by Wind. How the pathological mechanisms of Wind described in the Inner Canon were first transferred to

## PDF第57页

madness is a little-understood process that I will not venture to explain. The evidence for this shift comes from “formularies” (方書), the collections of medical recipes that became an important medical genre after the third century CE. $ ^{23} $ Most extant pre-twelfth century formularies discussed kuang as a Wind illness. But before I turn to these explanations of madness, let me briefly explain the way Wind was thought to produce illness.

“The hundred illnesses all start in Wind”

In ancient times, Wind was often described (and probably experienced) as a dangerous intruder that could strike humans in their vulnerable spots. According to one common description, Wind was one of six kinds of Qi that were harmful when excessive, the other five being Cold (ban 寒), Summer Heat (shu 署), Dampness (shi 濕), Dryness (zào 燥), and Fire (buo 火). $ ^{24} $ Wind arguably became the most important of the six, as several passages in the Inner Canon called it “the beginning of the hundred illnesses” (百病之始). $ ^{25} $

Not all winds were considered harmful, though. To become injurious, they had to be “hollow” or “empty” (虚), that is, they had to blow from an unexpected direction. $ ^{26} $ Their proper direction was determined by expected cosmic rhythms, including the seasons. According to one influential scheme, a wind was called “empty” because it blew from an “empty palace” (see Yamada 1980 and Unschuld 1982). Empty winds were also called

## PDF第58页

“deviant” or “stray” (xie 邪), as opposed to “correct” or “straight” (zheng 正). $ ^{27} $ Divine Pivot

75 claimed that “correct Qi is correct Wind. It comes from one direction, neither over-replete nor empty. Deviant Qi is empty Wind that injures people violently. It strikes people deeply and it cannot leave on its own.” (正氣者 正風也 從一方來 非實風 又非虛風也 邪氣者 虛風之賊傷人也 其中人也深 不能自去) $ ^{28} $

Wind was thus an “alien invader” that “harmed by intrusion” (Kuriyama 1999: 251), but not even all harmful winds could cause illness. Only when a deviant wind encountered an internal “void” or “depletion” (xu, the term I translated as “empty” when discussing winds) could it enter the body through the skin (couli 腠理, a term that referred to both the texture of the skin and to its pores), “ride” (乘) on that internal void, and “take residence” (舍) or “settle intrusively” (客) in a specific location. $ ^{29} $ According to the Basic Questions, such pathogenic Qi could settle in individual vessels (pian 56, 63, 74), in organs (37, 74), between organs (39), in acupoints (31), at the top of the head (35), in the skin (56), etc.

Tracing the Symptoms of the Illnesses to their Origins (Zhubing yuanbou lun 諸病源候論; hereafter Treatise on Symptoms), which was presented to the Sui throne in 610 CE, was the earliest systematic Chinese presentation of etiology (Despeux and Obringer 1997: 21). Its hundreds of citations in Sun Simiao's Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold (Qianjin fang 千金方,

## PDF第59页

652/59) and Wang Tao's Arcane Essentials of the Outer Terrace (Waitai miyao 外臺秘要; 752) shows the extent of its influence in medical writing, though we have little idea how (or even whether) its precepts were applied in practice. $ ^{30} $

In its large section on Wind illnesses, the Treatise on Symptoms described disorders as occurring according to the following script, which had an air of inexorability: $ ^{31} $

## internal depletion occurs → Wind intrudes → illness ensues

The illnesses Wind triggered depended on where it settled. It bent the mouth by invading the face (1.3a-b; 風口喎候) or caused vertigo 眩 by entering the brain (風頭眩候; 2.9b).

Injurious gales encroached on the Heart (the result was fright 驚), the Liver (causing fear 恐; 1.15b-16a), or the Spleen and Stomach vessels (causing hemiplegia 風半身不隨; 1.9b-10a).

“Because they arose unexpectedly, spontaneously, irregularly, because they made harsh, abrupt shifts, winds became associated especially with the most dramatic, sudden afflictions – with strokes, epilepsy, madness” (Kuriyama 1999: 258), but they also caused less brusque disorders, such as numbness, paralysis, or stiffness of the limbs. If you add various quivers, itches, cramps, and vision problems, you may agree with Kuriyama (1999: 234) that “Where now we scarcely blame wind for any afflictions, Chinese doctors traditionally suspected its ravages in nearly all.”

## Mania and related disorders: a seventh-century constellation of illnesses

Like its successors Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold (652/59) and Arcane Essentials of the Outer Terrace (752), the Treatise on Symptoms (610) discussed kuang within a larger section on

## PDF第60页

Wind illnesses. As the Treatise's entry on “the symptoms of Wind kuang” (風狂候; 2.13ab) explained:

Kuang illness is made by the deviant [Qi] of Wind when it enters Yang. When the deviant [Qi] of Wind enters Blood, it disturbs the balance of repletion and depletion in a person’s Yin and Yang Qi. If the one is over-replete and the other depleted, Blood and Qi pile on each other. $ ^{32} $ If Qi piles on Yang, one becomes kuang. When kuang flares up, one wants to run and one sees oneself as lofty or worthy, or calls oneself a deity or a sage. Also: the Liver harbors the Cloud souls; sadness and grief stir the center and injure these souls. When the Cloud souls are injured, one becomes kuang, rash, and lacking in lucidity; one dares not face others, the tendons are tense, and one cannot raise the costal bones. If the bodily hair withers and complexion turns bluish, [the sick person] will die in the autumn. All [the above symptoms] are because Blood and Qi are depleted and are subject to Wind deviant [Qi], which makes Yin and Yang Qi pile on each other. This is why [this illness] is called Wind kuang.

狂病者由風邪入於陽所為也 風邪入血 使人陰陽二氣虛實不調 若一實一虛則令血氣相并 氣并於陽則為狂 發或欲走 或自高賢 稱神聖是也 又肝藏魂 悲哀動中則傷魂 魂傷則狂妄不精明 不敢正當人而攣筋兩脅骨不舉 毛瘁色天死於秋 皆由血氣虛受風邪致令陰陽氣相并所致 故名風狂

# Dian (風癲候; 2.11b-12a), an illness often paired with kuang, consisted mainly of seizures:

Wind dian [occurs] when deviant [Qi] enters the Yin vessels because Blood and Qi are depleted. When a person’s Blood and Qi are insufficient, the Heart is depleted: the essential Spirit scatters and the cloud and bone souls both circulate chaotically because they have been harmed by the deviant [Qi] of Wind. When that Qi enters

## PDF第61页

Yin [vessels], it causes dian illness. Or else, when one is frightened when in the womb, Essence and Qi pile on each other, making the child suffer dian fits. During such fits, [the sick person] falls to the ground, spits out saliva, and loses consciousness. The origin of dian can be traced to the deviant [Qi] of Wind.

風癲者由血氣虛 邪入於陰經故也 人有血氣少則心虛而精神離散 魂魄妄行 因為風邪所傷故 邪入於陰則為癲疾 又人在胎 其母卒太驚 精氣并居 令子發癲 其發則仆地吐涎沫 無所覺是也 原其癲病皆由風邪故也

Although the Treatise on Symptoms discussed dian and kuang next to each other, clearly it did not treat them as two "types of madness."

Figure 3.1 (next page) maps a constellation of illnesses that were related to dian and kuang by overlapping symptoms in the Treatise on Symptoms. It illustrates that dian was not strongly connected to kuang, and that kuang was not the only disorder that contained psycho-behavioral symptoms. It also shows that approaches based on dian-kuang-xian (1: 5-6) are either too broad (do we include seizures in a study of “madness”?), too narrow (as it would exclude important related disorders like xuan 眩), or simply badly defined. If our interest is in the symptoms of insanity, possession-induced mad behavior should surely be more central to our investigation than back-arching and vertigo. A more flexible approach will help to detect forms of coherence that fit under neither “madness” nor dian-kuang-xian.

Five Wind disorders cluster around  $ dian.\ Wind $ strokes (中風), convulsions (痙), vertigo (眩), back-arching (角弓反張), and mouth-tightening (囗噤). $ ^{34} $ The latter was also a symptom of “horse  $ dian $” and of one kind of Wind stroke. Convulsions, characterized by a tightened mouth and a stiff and arched back, were said to resemble fits of  $ xian.\ “Arching $

## PDF第62页

<div style="text-align: center;"><img src="imgs/img_in_image_box_268_272_1259_939.jpg" alt="Image" width="62%" /></div>


<div style="text-align: center;">Fig. 3.1: Constellation of illnesses surrounding dian and kuang in the Treatise on Symptoms (based on 1.1a-3a, 15b-10a, 1.15b-17a, 2.2a, 2.7b-17a, 23.1a-5b, 23.7a, 24.1a-5b, 24.7b-8a, 24.9b-10a, 28.8a, 37.1b-5b, 43.9b, 43.12a-15a, 45.6b-10a, 47.12a, and 48.1a-5a).</div>

## PDF第63页

backwards like a composite bow," which occurred when Wind "makes one's hips and back bend backwards" (令腰背反折), was listed among the symptoms of dian. Vertigo was described as "drawing up the anchor of the eyes" and said to turn into dian if not cured.

Disorders leading to the appearance of death also shared symptoms with  $ dian^{25} $ and related to madness because they were typified by hallucinations and odd behavior. The speechless victims of “corpse-like state” (尸; 23.7a) were riddled by a pervasive ill feeling. Among the possible symptoms were abdominal pains and confusion of mind. “Corpse contraversion” (尸厥; 23.2ab) was characterized by sudden loss of consciousness and the appearance of death. Like dian, it was said to “heal of its own” (自愈).
