It looked like dian seizures without the back arching and saliva-spitting, but it was said to occur for internal reasons (reversal of the flow of Yin Qi), not external ones like Wind invasion or demonic possession. Other manifestations were “stupefaction” (恍惚) and hallucinations (妄有所见).

In Figure 3.1, kuang is not tied to dian, but to disorders displaying symptoms that were commonly associated with kuang. “Wind fright heteropathy” (風驚邪; 1.15b-16a), manifested by alternating fright and joy, stupefaction, and loss of normality (其狀乍驚乍喜恍惚失常是也); “Wind deviant [Qi]” (風邪; 2.13b-14b), characterized by lack of consciousness, mad confusion and reckless words, as well as emotional instability (發則不自覺知狂惑妄言悲喜無度是也); and several demonic illnesses.

Many symptoms of possession disorders were akin to those of kuang. $ ^{36} $ “Stricken by evil” (中惡; 23.1a-2a) was caused by demonic Qi and characterized by sudden sharp pain in

## PDF第64页

the cardiac and abdominal regions, $ ^{37} $ as well as thoracic discomfort and mental confusion accompanied by the wish to die (其狀卒然心腹刺痛 悶亂欲死). One form led to the semblance of death. If demonic Qi was not fully expelled, it became “infestation” (zhū 注) manifested by periodic fits. Wu 忤 (23.3ab) “possession,” also caused by demonic Qi, was likened to “stricken by evil” (中惡之類). It was characterized by sharp cardiac and abdominal pains, a bloated stomach, inability to recognize people, and changes in the color of the flesh. A severe form made the struck person appear dead. It could turn into an episodic “infestation” disorder if not cured properly. Zhu 注 “infestations” were manifested by the long-term settlement (zhū 住) of external Qi in the body of the sick person. This external Qi could be demonic, as in the case of “evil strokes” and “wu possession.”

“Demonic evil” (鬼邪; 2.14b), was characterized by odd language, crying and running in a scared fashion, mad confusion, emotional turmoil, fear as if someone were coming to catch one, and either singing or refusal to speak. “Demonic captivation” (鬼魅; 2.17a): “those who have been captivated by demonic entities will be prone to sorrow and their hearts will be stirred up on their own” (凡人有為鬼物所魅則好悲而心自動), their minds will be confused as if they were drunk (或心亂如醉), they will speak madly and be frightened or scared (狂言驚怖), they will weep in sorrow facing walls (向壁悲啼), and some will have commerce with demons and spirits (或與鬼神交通). Intermittent feelings of cold and heat, abdominal bloating, and inability to eat were other characteristics.

The demonic explanation of mad acts has survived until today. Although the Wind etiology was never completely put aside, the new etiologies of Fire, mucus, and emotions

## PDF第65页

came to dominate later imperial medicine. Before I explain the beginning of this shift, let me explain how the relation between kuang and dian evolved as the meaning of dian started to change from an epileptiform disorder to a form of madness.

### 3.4 The dian-kuang dyad and the changing meanings of dian 瘡／顛／癲

In modern “traditional Chinese medicine,” kuang means mania and dian something like “withdrawal.” $ ^{38} $ Yet this association of dian with apathy only became widespread in the nineteenth century. In the early medical canons, dian may be better translated as “seizure sickness.” $ ^{39} $ Here I start to trace a double evolution: the shifts by which dian came to mean insanity, and the changing rationales behind the pairing of kuang with dian.

I mentioned above that the Divine Pivot (one version of the Inner Canon) contained a chapter called "Dian and kuang." That chapter, however, was split into two discrete sections and did not define the relationship between the two illnesses. $ ^{40} $ A work titled Keji wuzang kuangdian bingfang 客疾五藏狂颠病方, compiled in or before the first century BCE, confirms that dian and kuang could be paired despite differences in their typical symptoms. $ ^{41} $ The

## PDF第66页

second-century Canon of Problems bolstered their association, first by explaining how kuang and dian were to be discerned (problem 59), but especially by stating that “the doubling of Yang is kuang; the doubling of Yin is dian” (重陽者狂 重陰者癲; 20 $ ^{th} $ problem). This cryptic statement was abundantly cited in the medical literature on madness throughout imperial history. Since it did not describe a single symptom of either kuang or dian, it could continue to be adduced even after the meaning of dian had changed radically (see 5.5, 7.6).

The opposition of dian and kuang in Yin-Yang terms endured in part because Yin and Yang were flexible categories into which almost any material could be integrated. Zhang Ji's Essentials of the Golden Casket (early 3 $ ^{rd} $ century) interpreted the Yin-Yang frame as an etiological distinction: “Depletion of Yin Qi makes dian, depletion of Yang Qi makes kuang” (陰氣衰者為癲 陽氣衰者為狂). $ ^{42} $ The Canon of the Pulse (3 $ ^{rd} $ century) used the same language to distinguish pulse manifestations: “if Yin is appended to Yang: kuang; if Yang is appended to Yin: dian” (陰附陽則狂 陽附陰則癲). $ ^{43} $ In the Treatise on Symptoms (610), the Yin-Yang dyad became locational: “kuang illness is made by the deviant [Qi] of Wind when it enters Yang” (狂病者由風邪入於陽所為也); “Wind dian [occurs] when deviant [Qi] enters the Yin vessels because Blood and Qi are deficient” (風癲者由血氣虛 邪入於陰經故也).” $ ^{44} $ In all these works, dian referred to an illness manifested by seizures.

## PDF第67页

In 762, Wang Bing 王冰 completed his annotated version of the Basic Questions. $ ^{45} $ To a passage that read “mineral drugs: fits of dian; herbal drugs: fits of kuang” (石藥發填 芳草發狂), Wang added a comment explaining the two disorders mentioned: “Excessive joy is [makes?] dian, excessive anger is [makes?] kuang” (多喜為填 多怒為狂). $ ^{46} $ Wang did not specify whether joy and anger were manifestations or causes. Since I know of no other instance in the medical literature of the time where excessive joy was associated with dian, it is possible that Wang’s description derived from the frequent tying of kuang with anger and the perceived need to describe dian as the converse of kuang. Wang’s interpretation of dian was probably also based on broader changes in the meaning of dian.

The transformation of dian from a disorder characterized by seizures to a word meaning “madness” started outside medicine. The earliest instance I have traced of such a usage dates to the late fourth or early fifth century, in a passage from Zu Taizhi’s 祖台之 (fl. 376-401) $ ^{47} $ writings that discussed a man who had killed his wife in a “fit of dian illness” (癫疾發作). $ ^{48} $ The association of dian with violence and Zu’s subsequent remark about the lack of intention of such crimes suggests that he was discussing a fit of madness, not a seizure.

The Book of Wei (554 CE) mentioned that one Liu Yuan 柳遠 (ca. 500-539) was known as “crazy Liu” (Liu dian 柳顛), perhaps because he “was of coarse disposition and

## PDF第68页

knew no restraints” (性粗疏無拘檢). $ ^{49} $ Liu enjoyed playing the lute (琴), indulged in alcohol, and occasionally wrote texts and songs (時有文詠). $ ^{50} $ The nickname “crazy Liu” probably derived from one common meaning of dian 顛 (“upside-down”), which other sources confirm could be applied to a person’s character. The Book of Liang (636 CE) said of someone that his “heart [mind] was sometimes upside-down” (心有顛倒). $ ^{51} $ One of Yan Shigu’s 彥師古 (581-645) annotations to the Jijiu pian 急就篇 (first century BCE) claimed that “dian illness is when one’s natural dispositions are inverted and abnormal” 顛疾 性理顛倒失常. $ ^{52} $ The History of the Northern Dynasties (659 CE) called a man’s behavior “crazy and foolish” (顛顛癡癡). $ ^{53} $ The graph 癡 may thus have been a merger of 顛 with the old illness name 癡.

Official histories recount that calligrapher Zhang Xu 張旭 (mid 8 $ ^{th} $ century) was nicknamed “crazy Zhang” (張顛). $ ^{54} $ When drunk, he would scream and “run wildly” (狂走, a common symptom of madness in the medical literature from Han to Tang), and then grab his brush to write freely in grass style (草書), producing works of such beauty that it “seemed as though spirits had been helping him” (若有神助). $ ^{55} $ As with Liu Yuan, Zhang

## PDF第69页

Xu's madness was associated with drunkenness and manifested in his behavior. Dian connoted less an affliction than a useful eccentricity, the spontaneous creativity associated with the abandonment of constraints. This exalting portrayal resonated with the valuation of drunkenness and "craziness" in Tang poetic circles, with which Zhang Xu was associated through his friendship with Li Bai 李白. $ ^{56} $

Dian also appeared in the compound diankuang outside medical texts between the third and eight century. Two ordinances (ling 令) dated 737 (the last time the Tang Code was systematically overhauled) are good examples. The first one said that “those who are mad or fond of inebriation will not be allowed to serve as Imperial Guards” (諸癲狂酗酒 皆不得任侍衛之官).⁵⁷ A second ordinance turned mad people’s lack of ability into a reason for compassion: diankuang 癲狂 was one of the classes of “seriously disabled” (篤疾) people who would be granted clemency when they committed light crimes.⁵⁸ A story in the Old Book of Tang (945) concerning the “dian illness” (癲病) of Li Haode, the main symptom of which was the utterance of odd words that were taken to be seditious, is strong evidence that dian meant madness and could thus be used in that sense alongside kuang.⁵⁹

As early as the Tang dynasty, then, dian had become a close equivalent of kuang in non-medical usage, either a kind of eccentricity associated with drunkenness and creativity,

## PDF第70页

or one half of an undifferentiated diankuang compound that broadly and loosely referred to "madness." Wang Bing's association of dian and kuang with joy and anger in his annotations to the Basic Questions was probably based on these contemporary meanings.

In its dominant medical usage until at least the tenth century, however, dian continued to refer to a disorder characterized by seizures. Xu Sibo 徐嗣伯, who lived at the turn of the sixth century, had equated dian with xian, a pediatric illness that resembled epilepsy: “it is called dian for adults, xian for children” (大人曰癫 小兒曰癇).⁶⁰ Later works supported Xu’s reading. The Treatise on Symptoms (610) specified that xian was called dian when it affected people over ten years old,⁶¹ Sun Simiao 孫思邈 (mid 7ᵗʰ century) cited Xu’s explanation approvingly,⁶² and the Arcane Essentials of the Outer Terrace (762) put dian and xian in a general chapter on Wind and elaborated on xian in a section on childhood illnesses.⁶³ Song (960-1279) writings continued to speak of xian as a pediatric illness and of dian as its analog for adults.⁶⁴ Because in most of these books dian and xian were both manifested by epileptiform symptoms, because the compound dian-xian was occasionally used, and since

## PDF第71页

xian was consistently treated as an infantile disorder, it seems safe to conclude that most Song physicians followed Xu Sibo's interpretation of dian as the adult version of xian. $ ^{65} $

It is also during the Song that dian's transformation into a close equivalent of kuang started to draw criticism from physicians. Like Ding Deyong's 丁德用 commentary (1062) on the second-century Canon of Problems, $ ^{66} $ the "Yang commentary" glossed dian 癲 as "falling" (蹶), but it also showed that another usage had become common: $ ^{67} $ "People nowadays take [kuang] to be identical with dian: this is an error." $ ^{68} $ Wang Zhizhong 王執中 (fl. late 12 $ ^{th} $ century) added: "When you analyze dian and kuang illnesses, this is obviously so, yet people never believe it! Could it be that an error in transmission and usage is so difficult to correct?" $ ^{69} $ (其剖析癫狂之病曉然如此而人終不信！豈亦傳習之誤難以改歟？) Even if I found few Song medical sources that glossed dian as a form of madness, Wang's outcry gives the impression that calling madness "dian" was becoming established usage. $ ^{70} $ The next few sections and the next chapter explain how major transformations in Chinese medical thinking altered the way physicians understood and handled mad behavior, regardless of whether they called it kuang or diankuang.

## PDF第72页

### 3.5 The emergence of “mucus” and its new roles in Wind illnesses

How did madness, which had been classified as a Wind illness in all influential treatises until at least the eighth century, come to be attributed almost unfailingly to mucus and Fire by the fourteenth? The rest of this chapter and the next unravel the complicated process by which Wind medicine was displaced, and emetics and purgatives became the main treatments for insanity. This thorough re-scripting of the etiology of madness was surprisingly discreet, as we find little controversy concerning kuang in the medical works of the time. One key to this process was the emergence of the notion of “mucus” and the increasingly important part it played in physicians’ understanding of Wind illnesses.

From the beginning, mucus was considered to be a purely pathological entity. Unlike Qi and Blood, which were said to circulate in the vessels or the viscera, mucus blocked regular flows and displaced other fluids from their legitimate residences, either by long-term occupation or by sudden flushing. But late-imperial physicians' attribution of insanity to mucus found no root in the Inner Canon and its derivatives, which were compiled when the notion of mucus did not even exist. $ ^{71} $ Let us see how this notion appeared and developed.

From dan 淡 ("ripping") to tan 痰 ("mucus")

In the Canon of the Pulse, dan 淡 acted as a modifier in the compound danyin 淡飲

(“ripping fluid”), one of four disorders caused by ingested water. $ ^{72} $ Dan 淡 usually meant

## PDF第73页

“bland,” but was here used as an equivalent of dan 澹 (“moving water”), perhaps from an onomatopoeic compound. $ ^{73} $ The Golden Casket defined “ripping fluid” as when “a person who used to thrive has become thin; water runs amidst the intestines, making a gurgling sound” (其人素盛今瘦 水走腸間 瀝瀝有聲). $ ^{74} $

So how did a modifier that meant “ripping” come to refer to a thick and slimy substance? Let us pose a few pointers on a territory that will remain to be mapped in detail. The use of dan 淡 as a noun is attested in a late-sixth-century work that defined 淡 as “a fluid in the upper chest” (胸上液). $ ^{75} $ That work shows that the semantic shift toward mucus started before the graphic one from 淡 to 痰. The Treatise on Symptoms (610 CE) confirmed that dan was increasingly understood as a noun and probably used as short for danyin: “Tanyin originates when the Qi vessels are closed or obstructed: the vital fluids do not circulate, so that the Qi of drink-water stops in the chest area and congeals into tan [mucus? rippling fluid?” (痰飲者 由氣脉閉塞 津液不通 水飲氣停在胸腑 結而成痰). $ ^{76} $

## PDF第74页

Extant editions of the Taiping yulan 太平預覽 (984) and Taiping shenghui jang 太平聖會方 (992) both display the form 痰, but their original editions are lost. Two later rhyme books also had 痰. The Guangyun 廣韻 (1011) defined it as “a water illness in the upper chest” (胸上水病) and the Jiyun 集韻 (1037) as a “morbid fluid” (病液),⁷⁷ in which “fluid” referred to naturally occurring bodily fluids, not ingested water. The different pronunciations given for 淡 and 痰 in the Guangyun show that the two characters were probably no longer interchangeable.⁷⁸

In important medical treatises from the seventh and eighth centuries, dan/tan was still mainly associated with the stomach and chest or with the lungs—since it was thought to congeal into expectorate and manifest itself in cough. The key to understanding why mucus became a general pathological factor that could trigger madness and so many other illnesses was its association with the patho-mechanisms of Wind disorders.

## Mucus and saliva in Wind illnesses

The pathological actions most characteristic of mucus (congealing, blockage, sudden movements, flushing upwards, encroachment on the Heart) had originally been attributed to Wind. By a process that would deserve more investigation, saliva came to be imagined as the

## PDF第75页

substance through which Wind acted inside the body. $ ^{79} $ Mucus’s eventual merging with saliva was an important hinge by which most Wind illnesses—including madness—came to be attributed to mucus by the fourteenth century. $ ^{80} $

The transfer of saliva's pathological roles to mucus took place smoothly because both fluids were imagined to cause congestion in the chest area, and many of the illnesses they were thought to produce (both tanyin and Wind illnesses) were increasingly treated with emetics (see below). One trace of their progressive conflation was the widening use of the compound "mucus-saliva" (痰涎) in medical texts during the Song dynasty (960-1279). For example, the phrase appeared seventy-eight times in the influential Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (Heijiu fang 和劑局方)—which was compiled in the late eleventh century on the basis of existing formularies and became a widely used repertoire of remedies for centuries thereafter—and its 1208 appendix the Zhinan zonglun 指南總論.⁸¹

Saliva played a central role in Wind strokes, but also in the convulsive disorder xian

癎, the pediatric counterpart of dian that remained close to kuang in etiology throughout the

imperial era. Attributed to Wind in the Treatise on Symptoms, xian had shed its Aeolian etiology

by the eleventh century, by which time even the works that called it "Wind xian" traced it to

the effects of mucus or saliva, a new picture that may have derived from the observation that

epileptics "spat foamy saliva" (吐涎沫; foamed at the mouth). Physicians probably imagined

that this saliva surged through the chest before it was emitted through the mouth. "Saliva

surge" (涎潮) was the mechanism by which various strokes were also thought to take place:

## PDF第76页

saliva rushed into the chest or head, causing mental confusion, convulsions, vertigo, numbness and strokes, or headaches, blurred vision, and dizziness. $ ^{82} $

Several passages in Wang Gun’s 王衮 Recipes for Broad Sucor (Boji fang 博濟方; 1041/48) implied that saliva or mucus played the same blocking role as Wind. Almost all entries in the long section on “fright xian” cited mucus as both a pathogenic agent and a therapeutic target. Two recipes were said to “transform mucus (仳痰) and appease the Spirit” (鎮神 or 鎮心神; 4.31b and 4.34b), one to “appease the Heart and transform mucus-saliva” (鎮心臟仳痰涎; 4.37b). Another specified that in cases of “fright Wind and convulsions” (驚風搐搦), “mucus blocked in the Heart” (痰塞在心) caused blank staring, inability to open the eyes, and a twisted mouth, all typical symptoms of Wind disorders. $ ^{83} $ One recipe in the tanyin section treated “Wind congestion in the upper chest area” (上膈風壅), another “mucus-saliva congestion and dominance [in the chest]” (痰涎壅盛). $ ^{84} $

In the next two sections, I explain how the government's vigorous promotion of medicine from the tenth to the twelfth century created a community of medical debaters, and then how new controversies surrounding the treatment of Wind strokes with emetics marked another step in the long-term shift toward new etiologies and therapies for madness.

## PDF第77页

### 3.6 Song governance: the diffusion of medical knowledge and the creation of common references for debates

Increased elite fascination with medicine and the wide diffusion of printed works during the Song dynasty created a density of debate that had not previously existed. The government itself led the way by editing or re-editing medical treatises and formularies, and by sponsoring a number of medical activities. $ ^{85} $ The theme of universal relief was central to Song imperial rhetoric and official activism from the late tenth century until at least the 1120s. Two large formularies compiled at the behest of a medically inclined emperor set the tone for Song medical governance. The Divine Physician's Recipes for Universal Relief (Shenyi pujiu fang 神醫普救方, 1000 juan; completed in 986), and the Taiping-Era Recipes of Sagely Compassion (Taiping shenghui fang 太平聖惠方, 100 juan; completed in 992) emphasized the role of medicine in benevolent rule. $ ^{86} $ Privately compiled medical works used the same rhetoric. $ ^{87} $ The court also fostered medical education in the capital and the regions, while the Imperial Medical Service (太醫局) sponsored public pharmacies to relieve people during local and national epidemics. The court also edited and printed important medical works such as Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury, Sun Simiao's Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold, and

## PDF第78页

Wang Bing's annotated version of the Basic Questions. $ ^{88} $ The Treatise in particular described methods and recipes for the treatment of epidemics, a central problem of Song medical governance. $ ^{89} $ Local officials who tried to diffuse medical knowledge were recompensed $ ^{90} $ and, thanks to printing technology, formulas that were once transmitted locally or within medical lineages were made available to large circles of physicians. $ ^{91} $

One of the court’s priorities was to provide medical recipes to local officials to help them fight epidemics and other illnesses. By imperial command, Zhou Ying 周應 compiled the best recipes from the bulky Recipes of Sagely Compassion into the Simple and Essential Recipes to Sucor the Populace (Jianyao jizhong fang 簡要濟眾方), which was distributed to county governments in the summer of 1051. $ ^{92} $ A few decades later in the Yuanfeng era (1078-85), officials of the realm were asked to supply good recipes to the “Office of Cooked Drugs” (Shouyao suo 熟藥所), then subordinate to the Imperial Medical Service (Taiyi ju 太醫局).

## PDF第79页

The office would test them and sell the efficient ones at a low price at public dispensaries. $ ^{93} $

The formulas were collected in the Recipes of the Imperial Medical Service (Taiyiju fang 太醫局方), which replaced Zhou Ying's Simple and Essential Recipes as the main court-sponsored formulary. In the Daguan era (1107-10), the Recipes of the Imperial Medical Service were corrected, supplemented, and renamed Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (Hejiju fang 和劑局), a book that remained influential on medical practice until at least the fourteenth century. $ ^{94} $

Prodded by the court's vigorous promotion of medicine, by personal experience (death of parents, personal ill health), and perhaps by Fan Zhongyan's 范仲淹 (989-1052) motto that one should become a physician if not a prime minister, many literati developed an active interest in the healing arts. Official and poet Su Shi 蘇軾 (1036-1101), for instance, founded the first public infirmary of his time, wrote prefaces for medical works, and actively promoted medical over ritual healing. In a short opus printed in the late eleventh century, he vaunted the Sagely Powder (聖散), a recipe a mysterious man had granted him. $ ^{95} $ Su's text was soon included in his friend Pang Anshi's (1043-1100) General Treatise on Cold Injury Illnesses. $ ^{96} $ Ye Mengde 葉夢得 (1077-1148) reported that the recipe had been avidly embraced by students at the National University during the Xuanhe reign (1119-25), but that the

## PDF第80页

enthusiasm subsided after the pill had killed “countless people” during real epidemics. $ ^{97} $

Chen Yan 陳言 (fl. 2 $ ^{nd} $ half 12 $ ^{th} $ century) later argued that Ye’s criticism did not justify

discarding the powder. Noting that the Sagely Powder was used throughout the empire

because Su Shi had graced it with a preface, Chen explained that it was designed to treat

Cold Injury, not Heat-factor epidemics; he attributed its occasional success during a Heat-

factor epidemic in Yongjia 永嘉 (modern Wenzhou 温州) in 1151 to the concurrent

eruption of a Cold-injury pestilence. $ ^{98} $

The saga of Su Shi's Sagely Powder illustrates how print technology not only spread medical knowledge, but created common references for interested literati. Relayed by good literary propaganda, the renown of their official promoter, and claims of successful application, such books contributed to the diffusion of medical methods and triggered debates concerning both doctrine and therapy. The most crucial controversy for our purpose concerned the novel use of violent emetics to treat Wind strokes.

### 3.7 Wind illnesses and emetics in the 11 $ ^{th} $ and 12 $ ^{th} $ centuries

The increasingly common recommendation of emetics to treat Wind illnesses in the eleventh century signaled the emergence of a new etiology based on saliva and mucus. The inclusion of such recipes into court-sponsored formularies allowed the saliva/mucus etiology to spread in medical circles and facilitated its eventual application to the treatment of

## PDF第81页

madness, first in individual works (starting in the eleventh century) and, by the fourteenth century, in a number of influential medical works (see next chapter). Below, I demonstrate how government publications contributed to the diffusion of the method of induced vomiting for treating medical emergencies in the eleventh century, and I then explain the debate that this novelty triggered. These developments formed the background against which eminent physicians with good connections at the court started to recommend vomiting therapies for madness as early as the late eleventh century. But first, I show that emetics had been in common use long before the “vomiting method” (吐法) became a matter of medical debate, and that many of these early emetics had once been interpreted as anti-demonic drugs. $ ^{99} $ The use of emetics to treat madness partly resulted from a interpretation of old demonifuge remedies—which had been used for treating manic symptoms attributed to possession—into natural recipes whose use was justified on more naturalistic grounds.

## Emetics in early Chinese medicine: toxicity and anti-demonic drugs

An old Chinese medical conception tied the toxicity of drugs to their therapeutic effect—“toxic” (毒) also meant “medically effective.” $ ^{100} $ Shennong bencao jing 神農本草經 (1 $ ^{st} $ century CE) emphasized that these toxic “inferior” (下) drugs, emetics and purgatives avant la lettre, could expel illness. They removed accumulations, stopped seizures, and ended demonic afflictions, presumably because ghosts feared these noxious substances. $ ^{101} $ Zhouhou fang 肘後

## PDF第82页

方 contained a recipe designed to provoke vomiting in possession cases. $ ^{102} $ Michel

Strickmann (2002: 246-48) described early uses of black henbane (莨菪子) to treat

epileptiform dian and madness. $ ^{103} $ Sun Simiao's "Jade-vase pill of the immortals" (仙人玉壶丸) expelled spectral intruders; carried like a talisman, it kept demons at bay. $ ^{104} $ It was

composed of realgar (雄黄), false hellebore (藜蘆, an emetic), cinnabar (丹砂), arsenic ore (礐石), croton (巴豆; a purgative), and aconite (附子). This potent pellet purged and induced vomiting. Many demon-repellents had an emetic effect that was not theorized as such, and many of them were later used as vomitives without mention of their anti-demonic powers. $ ^{105} $

Pre-Song formularies also contained emetics that were not interpreted as anti-

demonic. A book titled Jiyan fang 集驗方 claimed that xian seizures caused by hidden Wind

and a surge of saliva (暗風癎病 潮作涎暈) could be terminated if the victim was made to

vomit with bajiao oil (芭蕉油) $ ^{106} $. All three recipes in the Waitai biyao's (752) section on

“moving and gargling fluids inside the chest” (胷中痰澼) and seven of eight recipes in

“headaches by mucus reversal” (痰厥頭痛) were emetics. The number of works from which

these recipes were drawn suggests that this method was widespread. $ ^{107} $ Some healers

## PDF第83页

probably observed that vomiting could end fits or excessive behavior, but too many formularies are lost to assess when emetics were first recommended for treating Wind illnesses, but an eleventh-century controversy over the use of vomitives to treat Wind strokes may reveal how widely they were employed.

## Novelty and controversy: the use of emetics to treat Wind strokes and madness

Many doctors who proposed treatments for Wind in the eleventh century focused their reflections on the action of mucus inside the body. The inclusion of many cheap and simple emetics in eleventh-century formularies suggests that their usage was not controversial. Two recipes from the widely distributed Simple and Essential Recipes to Succor the Populace (1051; see p. 60 above) were designed to treat Wind strokes by making patients vomit “Wind saliva.” $ ^{108} $ Other physicians with court connections advocated similar methods. Sun Yonghe 孫用和 was serving at the Medical Institute (翰林醫官院) in 1044 when he was selected to dispense medical education at the Imperial Medical Service (太醫局). $ ^{109} $ In 1057 his sons Qi 奇 and Zhao 兆 joined the new Editorial Bureau for Medical Books. $ ^{110} $

## PDF第84页

Either Sun Yonghe or Sun Zhao designed a vomitive called “Saliva-thinning powder” (稀涎散; made of honey-locust and alum) for treating “Wind strokes with confusion as in drunkenness, failure to retract the four limbs, and in which the corners of the mouth seem pointed (?) and a small amount of saliva comes out” (中風昏昏如醉 四肢不收 口角似利 微有涎出). $ ^{111} $ These symptoms, caused by “Wind saliva surging upwards into the chest area, causing Qi blockage” (風涎潮於上膈 氣塞不通), needed to be treated urgently (宜急救) or they would turn into an acute illness (轉為劇病).

Also in the second half of the eleventh century, vomitive therapies were also being applied to several former “Wind illnesses,” notably madness. A book by one of the Suns recommended the “Three-saints powder” (三聖散) to induce vomiting during “fits of madness” (發狂), the term by which the Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE) had referred to bouts of insanity caused by extreme Heat. $ ^{112} $ The “three saints” were the recipe’s three ingredients. In the version cited by Zhang Congzheng 張從正 in the 1220s, these were “avert Wind” (fangfeng), false hellebore, and melon pedicle. $ ^{113} $ The first two were the components of Zhou Ying’s anti-Wind emetic (see p. 65n108), melon pedicle the main

## PDF第85页

ingredient of the “Melon-pedicle powder,” the most famous emetic in the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{114} $ The use of fangfeng suggests that madness was still interpreted as a disorder caused by Wind, but the claim that insanity was curable by vomiting confirms the emergence of a new etiology based on saliva or mucus.

The vomiting method for treating Wind disorders became controversial in the late eleventh century, when the Suns were still active in palace medical circles. This now forgotten debate has left faint documentary traces. Chu Yushi 初虞世, who published his Recipes Necessary for Cultivating Life (Yangsheng biyong fang 養生必用方) in the last decades of the eleventh century, became known for claiming that “there is no vomiting method for Wind strokes” (中風無吐法). $ ^{115} $ Since saliva pervaded the entire body (as Chu argued), vomiting it could only have a crippling effect. $ ^{116} $ He raised strong objections against the

## PDF第86页

inclusion of potent emetics in the court-sponsored Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (see p. 61 and 61n94 above), which was distributed to officials and eventually local physicians throughout the Song realm.

Despite Chu’s warnings, the vomiting method remained a firmly established therapeutic practice and compilers of formularies continued to insert emetics for Wind strokes and other disorders into their books. Court compilers included the Suns’ “Saliva-thinning powder” in the Comprehensive Records from [the Hall of] Sagely Succor (Shengji zonglu 聖濟總錄; 1110/18), a gigantic imperial collection. $ ^{117} $ The same book recommended the use of “Chinese-quinine powder” (常山散) to induce vomiting in cases of intermittent fevers (niè瘧). $ ^{118} $ An emetic recommended in the Comprehensive Records was said to treat, among other symptoms, “crazy and disorderly language” (言語狂亂). Called Melon-pedicle powder from the name of the Shanghai lun recipe, it contained only melon pedicle (a recipe that was otherwise called “Lone-saint powder” 獨聖散). But the explanation of this recipe’s properties did not mention mucus.

Other twelfth-century prescription manuals teemed with such emetics. A book that Zhang Congzheng (d. 1228) called “a recent Puji fang” (近代普濟方)—perhaps Zhang Rui’s Jifeng puji fang 雞峰普濟方 (1133)—recommended the emetics “Wind-chasing powder” (追風散) and “vomiting-Wind powder” (吐風散) to get rid of “mouth tightly closed and loss of consciousness” (口噤不開不省人事), and “honey-locust powder” (皂角散) to induce

## PDF第87页

vomiting in cases of “saliva surge.” $ ^{119} $ A formulary titled Always-Successful Recipes (Wanquan fang 萬全方) recommended “turmeric powder” (鬱金散) to induce vomiting for “headaches, blackouts, head Wind, nausea, and Wind [contracted during] bathing” (頭痛 眩暈 頭風惡心 淋浴風). $ ^{120} $

Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154), another scholar close to court circles, continued to promote the Suns’ “Saliva-thinning powder” and even introduced an emetic of his own in Fact-Based Recipes for Universal Succor (Puji benshi fang 普濟本事方; 1132)—the “Shengjin pill” (勝金圓), which was made of raw mint, honey-locust, melon pedicle, and false hellebore. $ ^{121} $ Xu recommended that pill for people who had lost consciousness or were half paralyzed by a stroke. Its success was gauged by whether the patient vomited (以吐為度): “those who vomit come to; those who do not come to are untreatable” (得吐即醒 不醒者不可治). On this basis, Xu criticized Chu Yushi’s view that there was “no vomiting method for Wind strokes”:

## PDF第88页

If saliva appears abruptly, making the sound of a saw [in the throat or chest?]; when the teeth are tightly clenched; when Qi is shut and cannot circulate; when decoctions and drugs cannot be ingested and life hangs by one thread, is it acceptable to insist that there be “no vomiting method”? One should not use silver-powder drugs, lest they harm the Spleen and wreck the four limbs, $ ^{122} $ but that is all. I often use these three recipes: $ ^{123} $ they are efficacious each and every time.

如卒暴涎生 聲如引鉅 牙關緊急 氣閉不行 湯藥不能入 命在須臾 執以無吐法可呼 但不當用銀粉藥 恐損脾 壞人四肢耳 予常用此三方 每每有驗

And indeed, these recipes kept being transmitted for centuries after Xu wrote about them, whereas Chu Yushi's position was eventually forgotten.

Records of Xu Shuwei's treatment of people who had become mad reveal that he would have found the mucus etiology of madness intelligible even if he did not propose it explicitly. $ ^{124} $ A woman “lost her mind” (失心) while trying to escape bandits; and a man “lost his mind [and became] as if idiotic” (神失如癡) when he was released as he was about to be executed for a crime. Xu cured both with a “secret recipe” (秘方) transmitted in his family that he called “Fright-Qi pill” (驚氣丸), several components of which were commonly interpreted as anti-mucus. $ ^{125} $ To treat a patient who suffered from “kuang reversal” (狂厥; in

## PDF第89页

which “reversal” referred to a behavioral as much as a bodily process), Xu removed aconite from his “Fright-Qi pill” and added iron powder (鐵粉), an ingredient mentioned in the Inner Canon that Xu interpreted in terms typical of his time: it could “not only transform mucus and appease and guard [the Heart?], it is also distinctive in repelling and subduing Liver heteropathies; if one is excessively angry and Liver heteropathies thrive overmuch, iron powder can curb [them].... This is because Metal curbs Wood” (非但化痰鎮守 至如推抑肝邪 特異 若多恚怒 肝邪太盛 鐵粉能制之 金制木之意也).¹²⁶ This mention of the mucus-dispelling powers of iron powder shows that remedies could remain in use even when their original interpretation had been displaced. Xu’s explanation also shows that madness was starting to be attributed to the pathological action of mucus inside the body.

Since formularies mostly discussed recipes rather than illnesses, it is difficult to assess how medical writers and compilers like Sun Yonghe and Xu Shuwei understood the etiology of mad acts and “loss of mind.” In the few cases we have from the eleventh and twelfth century, physicians used emetics and anti-mucus remedies. In these works, however, recipes said to be efficacious against madness still appeared mostly under Wind illnesses. Xu Shuwei’s case records and Sun Yonghe or Sun Zhao’s promotion of the emetic “Three-sage powder” to treat madness hint that the mucus etiology of madness was emerging, perhaps because physicians like Sun or Xu had seen that emetics could successfully put an end to some patients’ manic behavior.

## PDF第90页

### 3.8 Conclusion

By the eleventh century, conceptions about how saliva worked inside the body had modified the “imagination of Wind” that Shigehisa Kuriyama described in The Expressiveness of the Body (1999). Saliva led to strokes when it surged upwards, rushed to the head to cause headaches, constrained the chest during coughing, and foamed from the mouth during epileptic fits. To treat these disorders, instead of chasing out Wind, physicians now tried to expel its internal agents—saliva, and eventually mucus—with remedies designed to induce vomiting. The use of emetic therapies for Wind disorders thus supported the rise of the mucus etiology and made it easier for later doctors to dismiss the very notion of Wind within which mucus had first expanded its pathological roles. Chapter 4 explains how medical writers from the twelfth to the fourteenth century shattered the category of “Wind illnesses.”

We have seen that the emergence of anti-mucus therapies for madness was not guided by doctrinal reflections on the causes of insanity. Doctrine caught up with practice only when physicians from the twelfth to the fourteenth centuries designed a mucus theory of madness that no longer depended on the notion of Wind. In this chapter, I traced events that made this doctrinal shift possible (roughly in chronological order, though most stages overlapped): 1. the emergence of the pathological fluid “mucus” in Chinese medicine; 2. in Wind illnesses, the displacement of therapeutic attention from Wind itself to its internal agent saliva; 3. the merging of the pathogenic role of mucus and saliva, which resulted in the widening use of emetics; 4. the first explicit use of emetics to treat madness in the late eleventh century. The next chapter continues to trace the rise of the Fire-and-mucus etiology of mad behavior, the increasingly routine use of purgatives and emetics to treat psycho-behavioral symptoms, and the final collapse of the category of “Wind illnesses.”

## PDF第91页

## 4 

## From Wind to mucus and Fire

The innovations of Jin and Yuan physicians, ca. 1150-1360

In Chapter 3, I traced the increasingly widespread association of mucus with Wind disorders from the tenth to the twelfth century and showed that several illnesses whose pathomechanisms had been considered close to those of kuang—Wind strokes and xian 痈 seizures—were often treated with anti-mucus remedies by the end of that period. Let me now explain how the mucus-and-Fire etiology of madness that dominated late-imperial conceptions of insanity developed. The mucus-and-Fire script emerged among doctrinally minded physicians who lived in north China under the Jin dynasty (1115-1234), and was consolidated by southern doctors who were influenced by these northerners in the early fourteenth century under the Yuan dynasty (1260-1368), after the Mongol conquest of both the Jin and the southern Song (1127-1279) had re-established contacts between north and south China. The innovations these medical thinkers proposed built on the trends I outlined in Chapter 3, but it was their doctrinal and therapeutic precepts that came to dominate later medical thinking. During these men's lives (and partly thanks to their efforts), "Cold Injury" (shangban 傷寒) replaced "Wind illnesses" as the new dominant class of illnesses.

The way Jin-Yuan physicians interpreted madness and handled mad people

depended on their broader understanding of illness and vitality. The development of Cold-

Injury medicine, for example, enhanced the importance of Heat and Fire as etiological

factors and motivated a number of doctors to use Cold-Injury remedies to treat psycho-

behavioral symptoms.

## PDF第92页

Part 4.1 discusses Liu Wansu's 劉完素 (ca. 1120-1200) general emphasis on the etiology of Fire and his reinterpretation of "Wind" as a form of Heat. Both reorientations were widely accepted by later physicians. The second section explains why Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) generally treated madness with emetic and purgative remedies drawn from the Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE). Zhang's medicine brought together for the first time all the major trends I have described so far: therapeutic focus on mucus, adoption of Cold-injury remedies, and the use of aggressive remedies against madness. The next part discusses how Li Gao's 李杲 (1181-1251) and Wang Haogu's 王好古 (1200?-1264?) identified depletion as a major cause of illness and how Wang applied these views to the treatment of a mad man. Their emphasis on depletion became important to medical understandings of madness from the sixteenth century onwards. As section 4.4 will show, Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) gave a central role to Fire and mucus in the creation of illness and in the production of mad behavior. His doctrinal amalgams defined fifteenth- and sixteenth-century medicine. Part five shows how widespread anti-mucus therapies of madness had become by the mid-fourteenth century. The sixth section explains the bodily mechanisms by which doctors thought mucus could trigger madness. In the conclusion, I explain the collapse of the category of "Wind illnesses" and present a few ideas on what is usually (but misleadingly) called "Jin-Yuan medicine."

### 4.1 Liu Wansu: the Heat etiology and the decline of Wind

Liu Wansu 劉完素 (ca. 1120–1200) was the first systematizer of the medicine of

Heat and Fire that was eventually applied to madness and many other illnesses that were

once attributed to Wind. His ideas on the origins of illness and his therapeutic

## PDF第93页

recommendations occupied a central place in Zhu Zhenheng's fourteenth-century medical synthesis, which in turn shaped later medical doctrines. Liu's discussions also contributed to making dian and kuang two quasi identical illnesses.

Liu Wansu was born in northern China under Song Huizong (r. 1100-1126), the famous painter-emperor whose sumptuous reign ended when he lost half of his empire to northern invaders, the Jurchens who founded the Jin dynasty in 1115. $ ^{1} $ From his mid-twenties, Liu pored through Wang Bing's annotated version of the Basic Questions and became engrossed in the complexity of its immensely influential cosmological chapters. $ ^{2} $ To those tenacious enough to decipher them, these chapters explained the convoluted workings of the “five circulatory phases and six seasonal influences” (wuyun liuqi 五運六氣; often referred to as yunqi) as they applied to medicine. $ ^{3} $ The “five circulatory phases” were Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water, the Five Agents familiar to those who have studied any aspect of Chinese thought. The six types of climatic Qi were Wind 風, Heat 熱, Dampness 濕, Fire 火 (or Summer Heat 署), Dryness 燥, and Cold 寒.

The problem of change had fascinated Chinese thinkers since at least the beginning of the written tradition. Wuyun liuqi provided tools to detect regularities within unfolding

## PDF第94页

processes and to intervene effectively in these processes. Doctors claimed that yunqi schemes helped them to understand the infinite transformations of illness and to know how to reestablish the dynamic balance that defined good health. Yunqi doctrines became the rage in medical circles soon after the imperial edition of the Basic Questions appeared in the late 11 $ ^{th} $ century, and reached a golden age when they were patronized by Huizong, who considered these cosmic cycles as key to health, government, and cosmic harmony (Yan Liang 2000). $ ^{4} $

Like so many practitioners from the late eleventh century onwards, Liu Wansu treated the yunqi chapters as the most important in the Basic Questions. He even claimed that "the essential of medical teachings lies in the wnyun liuqi" (醫教要乎五運六氣), and made this emphasis evident in his Treatise on the Essential Purport of Yunqi in the Inner Canon (Neijing yunqi yaozhi lun 內經運氣要旨論). $ ^{5} $ Liu's preface (Liu WYQ: 195) to this book stressed how subtle and confounding this system of transformations could be to the non-initiated. Few indeed could awaken to its meaning (悟得其意者鮮矣).

In several of his works, Liu called wuyun liuqi a “Mysterious Mechanism” (玄機), from a Taoist cosmological term that did not itself appear in the Inner Canon. $ ^{6} $ This notion

## PDF第95页

became the mainstay of Liu's most influential book: the Method for Tracing Illnesses to their

Origins with the Mysterious Mechanism of the Basic Questions (Suwen xuanji yuanbing shi 素問玄機原

病式; hereafter Yuanbing shi).⁷ There, Liu alleged that “in the method for distinguishing

illnesses, it can be seen clearly that the Qi of [all] illnesses can be reduced to transformations

of the five phases and six Qi” (識病之法 以其病氣歸於五運六氣之化 明可見矣).

Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia:

Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and is unable to curb it. Fire can thus overcome Metal, as a result of which Metal can no longer overcome Wood: Liver Wood thus becomes excessive of its own and joins with the heat of the Fire, causing [the] sudden and violent collapse [that is characteristic of Wind strokes]. Sudden illnesses often result from excesses in the Five Wills and Seven Emotions. $ ^{9} $

中風偏枯者 由心火暴甚而水衰不能制之 則火能克金 金不能克木 則肝木自甚 而兼於火熱 而卒暴僵仆 多因五志七情過度 而卒病也

## PDF第96页

By explaining illness on the plane of the Five Agents, Liu severed the causal chain from the organs themselves.

<div style="text-align: center;"><img src="imgs/img_in_image_box_293_375_944_941.jpg" alt="Image" width="53%" /></div>


<div style="text-align: center;">Fig. 4.1 Mutual conquest and mutual production cycles of the Five Agents and their associated viscera. (This diagram represents the system in a balanced state.)</div>


Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $

Liu's ambitious treatise was in fact an extensive commentary on a passage from chapter 74 of the Basic Questions (one of the seven "Grand treatises") where Qibo explained the origins of 19 disorders to satisfy the Yellow Emperor's wish to "hear about the

## PDF第97页

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold.

Extrapolating that the five illnesses the canonical excerpt attributed to the viscera actually originated in the movements of Fire, Liu Wansu took this passage as authoritative proof of the pathological power of Heat and Fire, which now represented 14 out of the original 19 entries—five from the viscera, four from Heat, and five from Fire. The short first section on “the five circulatory phases [as they] govern illness” (五運主病) (1a-3b) discussed the Five Agents as they succeeded each other during the year (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water). The rest of the work was devoted to “the six Qi [as they] bring about illness” (六氣為病). Heat (4b-31a) and Fire (33a-57b) made up almost three quarters of the entire book. $ ^{12} $

Liu took the six Qi not as climatic influences (their traditional meaning), but largely as manifestations of internal imbalances. The close attention he paid to internal pathological processes makes him representative of a larger trend in Chinese medical thinking that I started to explore in the previous chapter when I showed that Song physicians increasingly explained Wind illnesses by internal processes governed by saliva or mucus.

The Wind section of the Yuanbing shi contained only one entry, and it made no mention of Wind as a real gale that swirled inside the body. $ ^{13} $ Liu argued instead that Wind

## PDF第98页

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $

Liu Wansu's descriptions of the pathogenesis of a large number of illnesses gave a central place to internal Fire and Heat (instead of Wind). Liu's focus on Heat also explains his predilection for the use of cooling drugs, many of which he drew from the Treatise on Cold Injury. Liu's reinterpretation of Wind as inner Heat contributed to the collapse of the notion of "Wind illnesses" (see sections 4.2, 4.4, and 4.7 below), which in turn helped kuang to become one among many "miscellaneous illnesses" (雜病) that could be discussed independently.

A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." Following the passage from the Basic Questions that he was commenting upon, Liu discussed madness under the "Fire" section, where he gave kuang a strong moral meaning: "kuang' means wild unruliness, with no correct or fixed [behavior]; 'yuè' means violating rites and laws and having lost one's normal state" (狂者 狂亂而無正定 越者 乖越禮法而失常也). $ ^{15} $

Interpreting the common expression shìzhi 失志 (“to lose one’s mind”) as referring literally to the loss of “the Will” (zhi志, associated with the Kidneys), Liu explained the

## PDF第99页

onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. Therefore when Cardiac Fire is overabundant, Renal Water decays: one thus ‘loses one’s Will’ and becomes crazy” (臀水主志而水火相反 故心火旺則腎水衰 乃失志而狂越). $ ^{16} $ Liu also thought that excessive emotions caused by Heat or Fire could trigger madness. $ ^{17} $

Liu Wansu evaluated at some length two sources that disagreed on the nature of the association of dian and kuang: the  $ 20^{th} $ problem of the Canon of Problems, and one of Wang Bing's annotations to the Basic Questions (see 3.4). $ ^{18} $ Since neither the  $ 20^{th} $ problem nor Wang Bing's commentary mentioned the symptoms of dian and kuang, Liu Wansu could adduce or dispute either passage no matter what meaning he gave to dian. His interpretation of dian and kuang as two similar disorders was widely adopted in Ming medicine, when the great seventh- and eighth-century treatises that had described dian as an epileptiform illness had stopped being reprinted.

It is said that ‘the doubling of Yang is kuang and the doubling of Yin is dian’, but this differs from the explanation found in the Basic Questions. The Canon’s annotations say:¹⁹ ‘excessive joy makes dian, excessive anger makes kuang.’ Joy is the emotion associated with the Heart: when Heat in the Heart is overabundant, there is excessive joy, creating dian. Anger is the emotion associated with the Liver. When Fire is overreplete it curbs Metal, which cannot appease Wood [the Agent associated with the Liver]. Therefore the Liver becomes over-replete: one is excessively angry, creating

## PDF第100页

kuang. Since all five emotions generate Heat, kuang is triggered by any of the five emotions. It is simply that anger is [a] more common [cause]. $ ^{20} $

或云重陽者狂 重陰者癲 則素問之說不同也 經注曰 多喜為癲 多怒為狂 然喜為心志 故心熱甚則多喜而為癲也 怒為肝志 火實制金不能平木故肝實則多怒而為狂也 況五志所發皆為熱 故狂者五志間發 但怒多爾

After having cited many passages from the Basic Questions that were compatible with his own view of emotions as manifestations of Fire and of madness as caused by inner Heat, Liu concluded with a rhetorical question aimed to refute the Canon of Problems: “Can it really be, then, that dian is the doubling of Yin?” (夫豈癲為重陰者歟)

Liu Wansu's interpretations were not grounded in his (or anybody's) observation of patients. His reasoning relied on textual authority and on deduction following the association of the five viscera with the Five Agents and the emotions. Bypassing the entire "Wind illness" tradition, Liu confirmed his Heat etiology by citing passages from the Treatise on Cold Injury—another work he greatly admired—that attributed disorders of the Yang-brilliance tract to Heat. $ ^{21} $ I know of no major medical work after Liu's Yuanbing shi 原病式 that attributed madness to Wind, whereas Heat and Fire remained important identified causes of psycho-behavioral disorders until the end of imperial history.

Later physicians also took Liu Wansu's comparison of the Canon of Problems with Wang Bing's later annotation as an argument for the closeness of dian and kuang. Following Liu's disquisitions and for the rest of imperial history, the contrastive correlation between dian and kuang remained a stable conceptual frame into which new content could be filled. In medical circles, however, the consolidation of this new usage of dian as a disorder close to

## PDF第101页

kuang in symptoms (instead of an epileptiform illness) took place only in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, a process I shall describe in more detail in sections 6.5 and 6.6. $ ^{22} $

### 4.2 Zhang Congzheng: convergences and crystallizations

Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228), from Kaocheng 考城 in Suizhou 睢州 (Henan), was the first Chinese physician to attribute madness to Heat and mucus explicitly and to treat it consistently with purgatives and emetics. He also systematized counter-affect therapy, a kind of treatment that came to be discussed next to madness in the sixteenth century (6.4). Zhang's doctrines were inspired by Liu Wansu, but also by the Basic Questions, the Treatise on Cold Injury, and a variety of Song physicians whose books Zhang had read.

Aggressive therapies and the mucus etiology of madness

Like Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng gave pride of place to the precepts of the Inner Canon. $ ^{23} $ He pushed even further than Liu his reliance on the Treatise on Cold Injury, from which he borrowed a number of recipes and three therapeutic methods that he used to treat all illnesses: sweating (ban 汗), vomiting (tu 吐), and purging (xia 下). $ ^{24} $ As Zhang explained,

## PDF第102页

the choice of method depended on the location of the pathology: if deviant Qi was in the upper body, it should be expelled upwards (by vomiting); if in the lower body, it had to be brought down (purged); and when near the surface, it should be dispelled by sweating. $ ^{25} $

Zhang used these methods because he approached most illnesses as if they were caused by deviant Qi (xie qi 邪氣 or just xie 邪), either external or internally produced. $ ^{26} $

Whereas physicians of his time (“blithe people”妄人) reportedly claimed that “one should give priority to reinforcing primordial Qi” and that “once primordial Qi is replete, deviant [Qi] will depart of its own” (當先固其元氣 元氣實 邪自去), Zhang adopted the opposite method: “in discussing the three methods of vomiting, sweating, and purging, I first discuss attacking deviant [Qi]; once deviant [Qi] has been removed, primordial Qi will recover of its own” (今予論吐汗下三法 先論攻其邪 邪去而元氣自復也).

Zhang’s theories were controversial during his lifetime. Some derided his “vomiting method” as being reducible to the use of Melon pedicle (an emetic), whereas others claimed that therapeutic vomiting could render one hemiplegic by expelling saliva from the articulations (人身骨節皆有涎 若吐出骨節間涎 令人偏枯). $ ^{27} $ After the Jin had moved

## PDF第103页

their capital southwards to Kaifeng (the former Song capital) in 1214 to flee the Mongols, Zhang was renowned enough to have been hired in the Imperial Academy of Medicine, but he claimed he resigned because he was vilified. $ ^{28} $

Wind illnesses had been treated by vomiting as early as the eleventh century, but the Song physicians Zhang cited as his predecessors had used emetics mostly for emergencies, whereas Zhang deployed them against almost all illnesses he traced to blockage in the upper body. $ ^{29} $ Zhang thus broadened and routinized the use of emetics—“broadened” because he used them to treat more disorders than any of his predecessors; and “routinized” because he prescribed them regularly for these illnesses instead of as a last resort or for emergencies.

Defending “vomiting” explicitly must have been more controversial than using remedies that were de facto emetics without the name. As Zhang himself recognized, the main problem was that most patients disliked vomiting. Even the uneducated folks who knew that deviant Qi could not be left inside the body recoiled from attacking therapies and rejoiced when they heard of replenishing (其聞攻則不悅 聞補則樂之). $ ^{30} $ “[Induced] vomiting is something people fear. [Purging] downwards, in the natural direction, already makes people

## PDF第104页

uneasy, not to mention upwards, in a contrary direction: the unhappy become numerous"

(夫吐者 人之所畏 且顺而下之 尚猶不樂 況逆而上之 不悅者多矣) $ ^{31} $

In defense of his methods, Zhang insisted that only powerful emetics could dislodge glutinous mucus: “when one is greatly congested and over replete from the chest area up and mucus is like paste or gruel, fine pills or fine powders would be like children’s games. Without [induced] vomiting, how can the illness possibly be expelled?” $ ^{32} $ (然自胸已上 大滿大實 痰如膠粥 微丸微散 皆兒戲也 非吐 病安能出) His remedies came from an impeccable source: “The emetic recipes I have used are all [Zhang Ji] Zhongjing’s recipes” (余嘗用吐方 皆是仲景方). $ ^{33} $ His many-pronged defense of attacking therapies even contained entertaining examples of the felicitous effects of inadvertent vomiting. $ ^{34} $

Since Zhang followed Liu Wansu’s reinterpretation of Wind as rooted in Heat and Fire, he rarely treated Wind illnesses as Wind illnesses. $ ^{35} $ For example, Zhang cured

## PDF第105页

“postpartum Heart Wind” (產後心風; “Heart Wind” was usually a synonym of madness)

with “Qi-supporting decoction to regulate the Stomach” (調胃承氣湯), a Fire-purgative,

and if this recipe failed, with Three-saints powder, a vomitive invented in the eleventh

century. $ ^{36} $ In many other cases of Wind disorders, Zhang directly attacked mucus. $ ^{37} $

Zhang Congzheng was the first Chinese physician to attribute madness to Heat, Fire, or mucus, and to treat it consistently with purgatives and emetics. Apart from one brief mention of “Wind kuang,” $ ^{38} $ Zhang completely dissociated madness from Wind. It is through his books that the mucus etiology and the emetic treatment of seizures and Wind strokes were fully applied to madness.

Even when Zhang did not explain that insanity was induced by mucus or Fire, he deployed this idea concretely in his therapies, as when he placed one of his mad patients—who had become crazy after falling off a horse—on an elevated wheel and made it rotate until the man vomited "one or two dou of greenish-yellowish saliva foam" (吐出青黄涎沫一二斗許). The madness stopped entirely after the patient drank liters of cold water, which presumably brought Heat and Fire under control. The wheel was a makeshift vomiting device. $ ^{39} $ Zhang tied the use of emetics to an etiological view: the mucus that a patient

## PDF第106页

vomited was the substance that was causing madness or mental confusion. He thus drew on common conceptions of pathological fluids to create new illness concepts.

In two other cases of kuang, Zhang referred to the passage from the Inner Canon that attributed madness to Heat in the Yang-Brilliance tract (3.1), and treated his patients with “Qi-supporting decoction to regulate the Stomach.” $ ^{40} $ In one case, Zhang used a three-pronged strategy, purging his patient more than a hundred times, making him vomit 7 or 8 times, and making him sweat repeatedly in a warm room over two months. $ ^{41} $ Since it is likely that repeated purges and vomits led to a temporary abating of madness, the use of purgative and emetic drugs was justified not only by imagined etiologies, but also by perceived efficacy, the latter confirming the former. $ ^{42} $ As we shall see, vomits and purges would become the main treatment for psycho-behavioral pathologies by the fourteenth century at latest.

## “The hundred illnesses all start in Qi”: excessive emotions and their therapeutic manipulation

Famous for his attacking cures, Zhang Congzheng was also the earliest systematizer of "therapy by counter-affect," which consisted of counteracting one excessive emotion by

## PDF第107页

means of another. $ ^{43} $ Although emotional problems were easily distinguished from manic

kuang, by Zhang's time physicians had started to associate insanity with emotions, partly

because of a comment by Wang Bing claiming that dian and kuang were caused or manifested

respectively by joy and anger. $ ^{44} $ In Yuanbing shi 原病式, for instance, Liu Wansu had

explained that various emotions could stir a Fire that triggered fits of madness (4.1). $ ^{45} $ This

scenario did not turn emotions into a sub-species of madness, but it created a hinge between

emotions and kuang that made it natural for them to be discussed together.

Zhang Congzheng left a detailed explanation of his reflections on this subject in a text he wrote late in his life with the help of his friend Ma Jiuchou 麻九疇 (？-1233?).⁴⁶

Entry 26 of the original Rumen shiqin 儒門事親 was called “Elaboration on the illnesses brought forth by the nine Qi and how these can serve as treatments for one another” (九氣感疾更相為治衍).⁴⁷ After arguing that Qi pervaded both the cosmos and the human body, Zhang claimed that disorders in both the body and the natural world were caused by transformations (變) of Qi. “This is why the Yellow Emperor and Qibo say that all pains originate in Qi, that the hundred illnesses all arise from Qi, and that it is therefore said that the nine Qi are [all] different” (此軒岐所以論諸痛皆因於氣百病皆生於氣遂有九

## PDF第108页

氣不同之說). This statement summarized chapter 39 of the Basic Questions, $ ^{48} $ the last section of which Zhang then endeavored to quote at length. There, Qibo had explained to the Yellow Emperor the kinds of disorders that the nine types of Qi could cause. $ ^{49} $ The nine forms of Qi, mostly triggered by emotions, were anger (nu 怒), joy (xi 喜), sorrow (bei 悲), fear (kong 恐), cold (ban 寒), heat (jiong 炅), fright (jing 驚), fatigue (lao 勞), and pensiveness (si 思).

As Zhang remarked, this passage failed to reveal what illnesses were triggered by these disorderly movements of Qi. He complemented this lack with one passage from the Divine Pivot (pian 8) that explained the effects of emotions on the viscera: ratiocination and pensiveness (思慮), associated with the Heart, harmed the spirit (神); sorrow (悲哀; Liver), harmed the bone souls (魄); joy (喜樂; Lungs), the cloud souls (魂); despondency (愁憂; Spleen), intent or consciousness (意); whereas fear, associated with no particular organ, harmed refined Qi, or “essence” (精). Since this passage discussed neither Cold and Heat nor fright and fatigue, Zhang associated each of the nine forms of Qi defined in pian 39 of Basic Questions with the illnesses that the Divine Pivot claimed they could trigger.

Zhang’s argument reached a turning point: “There is a treatment method for these nine [Qi] in the Inner Canon: they can simply be treated with the principle of mutual conquest of the Five Agents” (凡此九者 內經有治法 但以五行相勝之理治之; 3.25a-b). Zhang explained how seven of these forms of Qi created illness according to the mutual conquest

## PDF第109页

order, and how another form of Qi could serve as a treatment (更相為治). He presented these interactions in a way that can be summarized in the following table. $ ^{50} $

word-wrap: break-word;'>Physical form (xing 形)</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yin</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yang</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Heat</td></tr><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Heat</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Qi</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yang</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yin</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Cold</td></tr></table>

Zhang’s explanations about how emotions interacted could reflect commonsense perceptions—anger will get rid of joy—but they were conceptually grounded in Five Agents theory and in the implied view that emotions supervened upon bodily processes (3.2). Synoptically represented, emotions, agents, and organs interacted as in Figure 4.2 next page). An excessive emotion first harmed the functions of its corresponding organ. $ ^{51} $ Anger, for instance, harmed the Liver and its functions. Excessive emotions also affected other visceral systems according to the mutual conquest order of the Five Agents. Since Wood was said to conquer Earth, undue anger (associated with Wood) harmed Spleen (Earth) functions.

## PDF第110页

Treatment acted along the same vector. Anger could thus overcome excessive pensiveness, the affect associated with the Spleen. $ ^{52} $ Anger could itself be assuaged by sorrow, the emotion correlated with the Lungs and Metal (the agent that was said to conquer Wood). The two forms of Qi that did not fit into this scheme were fatigue, which should be soothed with idleness (yi 逸), and fright, which was countered by habit (xi 習). $ ^{53} $

<div style="text-align: center;"><img src="imgs/img_in_image_box_336_511_937_1117.jpg" alt="Image" width="49%" /></div>


<div style="text-align: center;">Fig. 4.2 Mutual conquest and mutual production of the Five Agents, with their associated organs and emotions</div>


The section ended with examples in which Zhang had cured patients with these methods. Three of these cases were recorded in more detail in Ten [Illness] Configurations and

## PDF第111页

Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), a selection of medical files compiled by Zhang's disciples shortly after his death. Four of the six cases under “the inner-harm configuration” (内傷形) showed Zhang using counter-affect therapy. $ ^{54} $ Applying his idea that fright would disappear if the patient became accustomed to what scared her, Zhang arranged for people to hit the windows and door of a woman who fell unconscious every time she heard a big noise. $ ^{55} $ After diagnosing that a different woman's two years of insomnia originated in her pensiveness, Zhang did everything he could to infuriate her: he charged more fees than usual, came drunk to the consultation, and departed without making a prescription, leaving his patient sweating with outrage. $ ^{56} $ That night and for several days in a row, she slept soundly and eventually recovered. Anger had overcome pensiveness.

Zhang also recounted how he treated a notable who had developed an aching lump on his chest below his heart out of sorrow after his father had died at the hand of bandits. $ ^{57} $ He had appealed to Zhang because drugs had been useless and he feared needles and moxa. In his presence, Zhang aped an attending exorcist (巫者) while yelling mad words (狂言). $ ^{58} $ The patient had to turn toward the wall to hide his hilarity. Within two days, the lump on his chest had dissipated. $ ^{59} $ Zhang's originality was that he interpreted a physical illness (chest

## PDF第112页

pains and a lump) as being rooted in an emotional problem. Indeed, “Only by being

deceitful and eccentric and by being willing to go to any length can one rouse a person’s ears

and eyes and make that person watch or listen. One without abilities cannot use these five

methods.”(必詭詐譎怪 無所不至 然後可以動人耳目 易人視聽 若胸中無材器

之人 亦不能用此五法也).

Zhang also cured a woman—the wife of a magistrate—who had stopped eating out of anger and displayed typical symptoms of kuang. $ ^{60} $ She yelled at people irately, wanted to kill her attendants, and uttered ceaseless streams of vile words (常好叫怒罵 欲殺左右 惡言不輟). First, two courtesans (娼) powdered with rouge and dressed like actresses (作伶人狀) entertained her. The next day, the patient guffawed when she saw Zhang's female assistants wrestle (角觝). To stimulate her desire for food, Zhang then made “two women with big appetites” (兩個能食之婦) eat next to her and praise the excellence of the food. She soon requested to sample the fare. This is how, without any drugs, the patient's anger subsided and her appetite increased. We are told that she eventually gave birth to a son. As the case concluded: “Medicine values talent. Without talent, how can one respond to infinite transformations?” (夫醫貴有才 若無才 何足應變無窮).

### 4.3 “Inner harm” (内傷), replenishing, and the art of diagnosis in Li Gao's circle

Zhang Yuansu 張元素 (ca. 1140 – ca. 1220) and his followers Li Gao 李杲 (1182–1251) and Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) are not known for their discussions of

## PDF第113页

madness, but their conceptions of vitality and illness process had such a profound effect on Chinese medical thinking that they were eventually integrated into most explanations of cognitive and behavioral failures. $ ^{61} $ Here I introduce aspects of their medical doctrines that informed the way later physicians understood and treated madness, $ ^{62} $ focusing on how their emphasis on the pathological power of depletion emerged, and on how Wang deployed this notion in therapeutic encounters with two mad patients.

Zhang Yuansu was from Yi prefecture (Yizhou 易州) in what is now northern Hebei. Although he is usually classified as a “Jin physician” (because he lived his entire life under the Jin), like Liu Wansu he benefited from books that had been printed by the Song court when it was still reigning over north China. He studied (and eventually taught) the Basic Questions, the Canon of Problems, and Zhang Ji’s Treatise on Cold Injury, $ ^{63} $ as well as the Song pharmacological literature. He also thought highly of Qian Yi’s 錢乙 Xiaor yaozheng zhijue 小兒藥證直訣 (1119), and of Liu Wansu’s Yuanbing shi 原病式 (before 1186). $ ^{64} $

Zhang claimed that “old recipes” (古方) could not treat “present illnesses” (今病), but his critique targeted set recipes even more than old ones. Because he argued that inexhaustibly diverse illnesses should be handled flexibly, his main therapeutic principle was

## PDF第114页

“to use drugs and treat illness according to symptoms” (隨證治病用藥). $ ^{65} $ To help his disciples design suitable prescriptions, he trained them to understand the properties of individual drugs. $ ^{66} $ Zhang treated pulse diagnosis as the main technique by which to detect these unique imbalances that made individuals sick.

What Charlotte Furth has called the “art of diagnosis” is best understood as the art of designing prescriptions on the basis of case-specific diagnoses. $ ^{67} $ The flexible articulation of a prescription to a diagnosis is so often treated as a natural feature of “traditional Chinese medicine” that its historical origins are usually ignored. $ ^{68} $ Zhang and his disciples were in fact among the first to put this principle at the center of their doctrines. $ ^{69} $ Before them, the default mode of prescription (typified by the Song formularies that became their favorite foils) had been to prescribe ready-made remedies for predefined illnesses. Zhang Yuansu and his followers criticized contemporaries for not distinguishing between Cold and Heat or between depletion and repletion, and for consequently failing to prescribe the right remedies in specific illness cases. $ ^{70} $ They also criticized books like the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service,

## PDF第115页

whose format encouraged overlooking such distinctions. One of Zhang Yuansu's disciples became the most famous early upholder of this approach.

Li Gao 李杲 (1180-1251) was born in a prominent clan from Zhending 真定 (also in Hebei). It is said that Li studied the healing arts because his mother had died at the hands of incompetent physicians. He gave Zhang Yuansu, who was then renowned in the Yan 燕 and Zhao 趙 regions, a hefty sum of money to learn medicine from him. $ ^{71} $ With Zhang, Li studied the Inner Canon, the Treatise on Cold Injury, the Canon of Problems, and probably Zhang's digest of these works, Instructions on the Origins of Medical Learning (Yixue qiyuan 醫學啟源). $ ^{72} $

In 1202, Li became a minor official in charge of supervising tax collection, a post that was likely more highly regarded (and certainly more lucrative) than that of physician. While in service, he displayed his medical skills by curing the nephew of the local magistrate. $ ^{73} $ Like most Jin officials, however, he had to flee his post and find refuge in Daliang 大梁 (Kaifeng) in 1214 along with the Jin court, which moved there to dodge Mongol military threats in the north. $ ^{74} $ In Daliang, Li's medical competence and distinguished education allowed him to mingle with notables and renowned writers. $ ^{75} $

## PDF第116页

Li Gao lived in Daliang at the same time as Zhang Congzheng (4.2), but despite common acquaintances they seem not to have known each other. $ ^{76} $ A text compiled by Zhang's disciples either late in Zhang's life or after his death in 1228 observed that mediocre physicians often killed their patients when they tried to treat deviant Qi exclusively with ginseng and astragalus (若專以人參 黃耆治人之邪氣 此庸工所以常誤人命). $ ^{77} $ These happened to be Li Gao's two favourite drugs. As we saw above, Zhang's methods were usually designed to rid patients of the deviant Qi that he thought caused illness. Li Gao, however, used ginseng and astragalus not to remove deviant Qi, but to boost the Spleen and Stomach (more below). Blaming other physicians for killing patients was a universal accusation throughout imperial history. The comment by Zhang Congzheng's disciples suggests that some healers in Daliang were using these two drugs. Either Li Gao was not alone in using them or he had already become famous enough to be misunderstood.

Li’s famous notion that many illnesses originated in depletion (and that human vitality was rooted in the Stomach) allegedly emerged when Li witnessed the epidemic that erupted in Daliang in the spring of 1232, soon after a brief Mongol siege. $ ^{78} $ Both during and

## PDF第117页

after the siege, Li recounted, most of Daliang's dwellers became sick (only "one or two in a myriad" were supposedly spared) and every day for three months bodies were taken to morgues at two of the city's gates. $ ^{79} $ The fact—noted by Li himself—that several other cities also fell to illness after they were besieged between 1213 and 1222 suggests that the infection was spread by Mongol armies, but without a plausible notion of contagion, Li could not account for the concentrated casualties and asked instead how so many people could have been harmed by Wind and Cold. $ ^{80} $

The morbid event spurred Li to explain why traditional drugs had failed to prevent these droves of deaths. He proposed that people in the besieged cities had not eaten and drunk regularly and had been harmed by exhaustion (饮食不節及勞役所傷). Borrowing a term that Zhang Yuansu and Liu Wansu had used before him, he called this internal depletion "inner harm" (內傷). The problem to Li was that the city's physicians had mostly treated the 1232 illness with aggressive therapies based on the Treatise on Cold Injury, which

## PDF第118页

had been the main source of remedies to treat epidemics attributed to external factors like Cold or Wind. $ ^{81} $

Some dissipated the surface, $ ^{82} $ others [treated with] croton massages, $ ^{83} $ and yet others purged with Qi-stimulating decoction; $ ^{84} $ [these remedies] soon gave rise to thoracic blockage and yellowness of skin, $ ^{85} $ [the illness] was then purged with the Decoction or Pill to bring down thoracic [blockage], $ ^{86} $ and with Capillary-Artemisia decoction. $ ^{87} $ Everyone died. $ ^{88} $

有表發者 有以巴豆推之者 有以承氣湯下之者 俄而變結胸發黃 又以陷胸湯丸及茵陳湯下之 無不死者。

Many must have noticed these drugs' ineffectiveness, but Li Gao reached a more radical conclusion: it was the drugs themselves, not the illness, that had caused most of the deaths. "At the beginning, [the illness] was not Cold Injury, but because of therapeutic mistakes it was transformed and came to resemble the symptoms of real Cold Injury: this was all the fault of the drugs." (蓋初非傷寒 以調治差誤 變而似真傷寒之證 皆藥之罪也).

Li did not claim that the people of Daliang were predisposed to illness by prior depletion, nor did he explain the 1232 epidemic in climatic terms related to the theory of the

## PDF第119页

Five Periods and Six Qi, nor did he argue that treatments had failed because “ancient remedies could not cure present disorders.” And he did not say that the pestilence had been caused by Cold Injury and only made worse by famine or exhaustion. $ ^{89} $ Li Gao claimed unambiguously that people who had been weakened by hunger and exertion had been killed by wrongly prescribed drugs. $ ^{90} $ To Li, people suffering from “inner harm” died because they were mistakenly treated as if they had been struck by external contamination. Li’s point was to emphasize the necessity of distinguishing between outer and inner causes when curing illness.

Sometime after the Mongol siege was lifted in 1232, Li fled to Dongping (Shandong), a place renowned for harboring fleeing Jin literati. $ ^{91} $ During the few years he lived there, he wrote a work (now lost) called Assembled Essentials of Cold Injury (Shanghan buiyao 傷寒會要), as well as drafts of his reflections on the 1232 epidemic. $ ^{92} $ After returning home to Zhending prefecture (perhaps in 1243, when he was over 60), Li started to think of transmitting his thoughts and medical skills. $ ^{93} $ As he organized his ideas on etiology, diagnosis, and treatment,

## PDF第120页

he started to teach medicine to, as far as we know, only one disciple—Luo Tianyi 羅天益 (ca. 1220-1290), whose family was registered in Gaocheng 藁城, a county in Zhending. $ ^{94} $

In 1249, Li finished his most famous work, the Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach (Piwei lun 脾胃論), an attempt to systematize the view of vitality he had adumbrated in Dispelling Perplexities on Inner and Outer Harm. Citing canonical sources, Li argued that Stomach Qi was the foundation of vitality: "The Inner Canon says that the root of people is gastric Qi. This is because people take life from the water and grain they receive. What the Inner Canon calls 'clear Qi,' 'constructive Qi,' 'protective Qi,' and 'the Qi that rises in Spring' are all alternative appellations of gastric Qi."⁹⁵ (內經悉言人以胃氣為本 蓋人受水穀之氣以生 所謂清氣 榮氣 衛氣 春升之氣 皆胃氣之別稱也。) Since the Stomach and Spleen were so essential to maintaining normal vigor, Li argued, "inner harm" to these two viscera could cause a large number of illnesses.
