One stated goal of Dispelling Perplexities and the Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach was to attune physicians to the clinical differences between the over-repletion caused by “outer harm” (外傷; typified by Cold Injury) and the depletion occasioned by “inner harm.” The symptoms of inner harm were “somewhat similar to the symptoms of externally contracted Wind and Cold” (與外感風寒所得之證頗同), but their principles differed (理異). Inner harm to the Spleen and Stomach impaired Qi, whereas Wind and Cold damaged the body

## PDF第121页

(形); external contamination provoked over-repletion and was to be treated aggressively (by vomiting or purging), whereas inner harm led to depletion and was best treated by replenishing.

“Inner harm” was thus depletion caused by exhaustion or irregularities in diet. Such depletion typically affected the Spleen and Stomach and required a certain treatment: “replenishing the interior and making Yang rise by means of sweet and warming drugs” 惟大當以甘溫之劑 補其中 升其陽 甘寒以瀉其火則愈. $ ^{96} $ Taken together, this etiology, symptomatology, and typical therapy formed an illness script, a scenario that predicted how an illness would arise and unfold and how it should be treated. Li’s rooting of vitality in the functioning of the Spleen and Stomach and his focus on the mechanisms of inner harm gave Chinese medicine one of its most valuable scripting tools: the ability to attribute almost any illness to internal depletion. Physicians used this tool to redefine dian in the sixteenth century and to propose an emotional origin for some mad symptoms, a story we shall explore in Chapter 6.

Wang Haogu 王好古 (1200?-1264?) was the first physician inspired by Li Gao's ideas to have designed a replenishing therapy for a mad patient. Wang was from Zhaozhou 趙州, a few dozen kilometers south of Li Gao's native Zhending. He became a jinshi under

## PDF第122页

the Jin (at latest in 1230, when the last Jin palace examination was held) and served as a medical official. $ ^{98} $ He learned medicine from Li Gao some time before Li wrote his books. $ ^{99} $ Wang himself claimed he had studied with Li, $ ^{100} $ and in a 1236 text appended to his Elementary Examples of Yin Symptoms (Yinzheng lueli 陰證略例), he lamented that he could now find no enlightened person to give him advice on his draft: “I do not know when I will find my Dongyuan Li [Gao] xiansheng again and ask him about it; only then could my mind be at peace” (不知何日復得吾東垣李先生一問之，吾之心始可以少安矣). $ ^{101} $ A man who helped to publish Wang’s book in 1243 narrated how Wang had become a medical expert: Early on, [he] was recommended for jinshi because he was proficient in the classics; only later did he take an exclusive liking for talking about medicine. This is when he started $ ^{102} $ following Li [Gao] Mingzhi Dongyuan; all of [Li’s] learning was transmitted to him. Only then did [Wang] begin to scrutinize meticulously and to ponder thoroughly [medical] books by the many specialists since the Yellow Emperor and Qibo. He raced up and down through several thousands of years [of texts as easily] as if he were flipping his palms. When I was in Daliang [before 1232], I heard of his great renown among dignitaries. My only regret was that I did not get to meet him. $ ^{103} $

## PDF第123页

早以通經舉進士 晚獨喜醫言 始從東垣李明之 盡傳其所學 後乃精研極思軒岐以來諸家書 馳聘上下數千載間 如指諸掌 予在大梁時 聞其名諸公間籍甚 獨以未識之恨

# This account suggests that Wang studied with Li Gao many years before 1232, and that Wang may have learned Zhang Yuansu's precepts from Li rather than from Zhang himself.

Wang's Elementary Examples of Yin Symptoms was a collection of commented passages on "Yin symptoms" from famous authors, from the Yellow Emperor (i.e., the Inner Canon) to Zhang Yuansu. $ ^{104} $ Wang justified this compilation by arguing that the Treatise on Cold Injury had neglected Yin symptoms, which were associated with the Yin tracts and were mostly characterized by depletion. Wang's argument appears to have been related to Li Gao's distinctions between Yin and Yang symptoms (Li considered "inner harm" as a kind of Yin disorder), but Wang published his book before Li had written his, so he could not have been influenced by Li's writings.

Wang Haogu argued that Yin symptoms were more difficult to diagnose and to cure than Yang symptoms, which were associated with the Yang tracts and typically characterized by over-repletion. Since both Yin and Yang illnesses were manifested by Heat, only pulse diagnosis could distinguish between the two. $ ^{105} $ “Yin Heat” (grounded in an underlying depletion) had to be treated in a way that would get rid of Fire without further depleting the body. Wang’s approach resembled Li Gao’s, but Wang articulated it before Li did.

Despite his criticism of the Treatise on Cold Injury for its neglect of Yin symptoms,

Wang still fervently admired that book. In his preface to This Matter is Difficult to Understand

## PDF第124页

(Cishi nanzhi 此事難知), Wang claimed that the Treatise was the medical book he esteemed the most. In Medical Ramparts Against the Yuan Barbarians (Yilei yuanrong 醫壘元戎; preface 1237), Wang even attempted to use Cold-Injury etiology (classified according to the "twelve tracts" 十二經) to explain all illnesses, and proposed to treat "miscellaneous illnesses" (anything other than Cold Injury) with remedies derived from the famous Treatise.

Wang Haogu's two recorded cases of madness—one of "Yang madness" (陽狂), one of "Yin madness" (陰狂)—displayed both his admiration for the Treatise of Cold Injury and his emphasis on Yin symptoms. $ ^{106} $ The two cases illustrate very well the trends represented by the group of people I have been describing, and the two therapies Wang adopted to treat his patients (a man and a woman) foreshadowed what became the two main medical treatments for madness after the fifteenth century: one based on attacking, one on replenishing (see Chapter 6). As Wang explained, the case of "Yang madness" should not have been listed in a work on Yin symptoms, but he placed it next to a case of "Yin madness" to show that although the madness was the same (其狂則一), the two illness episodes still differed in their Heat manifestation (其為寒熱二).

In a case of “Yang madness,” the patient was the wife of a dignitary and the daughter of a man surnamed Xu who had placed first in the 1203 palace examination. $ ^{107} $ Wang purged

## PDF第125页

her repeatedly with the “Great Qi-supporting decoction,” a Cold-Injury remedy we have encountered several times. $ ^{108} $ Wang’s justification for this aggressive therapy shows that Zhang Yuansu had probably taught his students something about madness, perhaps as a gloss on a passage from the Inner Canon. As Wang recalled: “I once heard that old man Jiegu said: in the basic Canon’s [i.e., the Inner Canon] saying that ‘[madness] will cease when you wrest food away,” $ ^{109} $ wrestling food away does not mean giving nothing to eat; it means massively purging so as to make [the patient] unable to eat” (余昔聞潔古老人云 本經言奪食則已 非不與之食而為奪食也 常以藥大下之而使不能食也 為之奪食也). $ ^{110} $

In the more peculiar case of "Yin madness," Wang treated a Marquis who had served in the Jin imperial armies (親軍). Despite what looked like manifestations of Heat, Wang diagnosed him (purely from his pulse) as suffering from inner Cold. After assuaging the concerns of his patient's relatives, who used Cold-Injury terminology to argue that the symptoms were those of over-repletion, Wang treated the man with calorific drugs (ginger and aconite). As Wang concluded: "The Marquis's madness was not Yang madness, but loss-of-mind madness: it was [a] Yin [illness], but the only evidence for this was that the pulses were Yin. Scholars should ponder this: solely taking the pulses and not basing [diagnosis] on external symptoms is acceptable." 候公之狂 非陽狂之狂 乃失神之狂 即陰也 但

脈陰為驗　學者當審　獨取諸脈　不憑外證可也

## PDF第126页

Like Li Gao, Wang Haogu stressed the importance of a flexible choice between medications, of the use of warming drugs to treat illnesses caused by depletion, and of the pulse in discerning between depletion and over-repletion. One of the most important transmitters of these approaches was Zhu Zhenheng.

### 4.4 Zhu Zhenheng's synthesis

Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) $ ^{111} $ synthesized the doctrinal innovations of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries and created channels through which later readers could learn about the teachings of Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng, Wang Haogu, and Li Gao. Here I recount how Zhu built on the work of his predecessors to create a coherent set of medical precepts, and how his doctrines led him to attribute psycho-behavioral symptoms (including symptoms that most of his contemporaries attributed to demonic possession) to the action of Fire and mucus inside the body. $ ^{112} $

Zhu was born in Wu prefecture (Wuzhou 婺州, later known as Jinhua 金華) in Zhejiang a few years after the Mongols had conquered Hangzhou, the southern Song capital. He therefore lived his whole life in a reunified empire under the Mongol-led Yuan dynasty (1260-1368). Late in his life, after he had become a renowned scholarly physician, Zhu became known as “Danxi,” from the name of a brook that flowed near his home. $ ^{113} $

## PDF第127页

Song Lian 宋廉 (1310-1381), a scholar from Zhu's locality who would become a powerful official under the Ming dynasty (1368-1644), described Zhu in his early thirties as defiant and chivalrous in his opposition to local injustices. $ ^{114} $ When Xu Qian 許謙 (1270-1337), a fourth-generation disciple of the great Zhu Xi 朱熹 (1130-1200), came to nearby Dongyang 東陽 to lecture on the Dao in 1316, Zhu became one of his many followers. $ ^{115} $ Under his guidance, Zhu read what the disciples of Zhu Xi were then studying: the Four Books, Zhu Xi's Family Rituals (家禮), $ ^{116} $ Shao Yong's Yijing-based cosmology, $ ^{117} $ Zhang Zai's Correcting Youthful Ignorance (正蒙), Zhou Dunyi's Penetrating the Book of Changes (Tongshu 通書) $ ^{118} $ and his Explanation of the Diagram of the Great Ultimate (Taiji tushuo 太極圖說), $ ^{119} $ as well as teachings of the Cheng brothers as reinterpreted by Zhu Xi. Zhenheng might have thought that this curriculum gave him a good chance of passing the newly reestablished imperial examinations, which gave pride of place to Zhu Xi's teachings, but he failed both times he took part, probably in 1317 and 1320. $ ^{120} $ He then abandoned public service to devote himself to medicine, a task in which Xu Qian was said to have encouraged him. $ ^{121} $

## PDF第128页

Texts, travels, and teachers: the emergence of a synthesis

At forty sui (ca. 1321) Zhu re-read the Basic Questions methodically, leaving aside dubious passages and mastering what was worth mastering (缺其所可疑 通其所可通). $ ^{122} $ Probably in 1323, he was able to cure his master of an old illness with an “emptying-the-granary method” (倒倉法) that was redolent of Zhang Congzheng’s attacking therapies. $ ^{123} $ Zhu’s account of his own intellectual development claimed that he had been impressed by Zhang’s precepts sometime before he set out in search of a master in 1324. $ ^{124} $ In “On Zhang Zihe’s attacking methods” (張子和攻擊注論), $ ^{125} $ the last section of his Words Bequeathed by the Ancients on the Perfection of Knowledge Through the Investigation of Things (Gezhi yulun 格致餘論; 1347), $ ^{126} $ Zhu summarized Zhang’s ideas that all illnesses were caused by deviant Qi and that therapies should concentrate on expelling this pathogenic Qi from the body. $ ^{127} $

## PDF第129页

As Zhu recalled, “When I first read [Zhang’s] books, I considered that the methods of medicine were all found therein” (初看其書　將調醫之法盡於是矣; 67a). Zhu claimed he started to doubt Zhang’s methods after reading what the Basic Questions and the Treatise on Cold Injury said about depletion: “the Inner Canon says that ‘depletion’ is when essential Qi is depleted; ‘repletion’ is when deviant Qi is replete” (內經有謂之虛者　精氣為虛也　謂之實者　邪氣實也; 67a). $ ^{128} $ This definition implied that deviant Qi could only invade when one’s Qi was deficient, an issue that Zhang Congzheng had supposedly not pondered. $ ^{129} $ Zhu also alleged that Zhang Ji (the compiler of the Treatise on Cold Injury) had warned that replenishing drugs should sometimes be given before the beginning of sweating therapy, one of the three kinds of attacking treatments Zhang Congzheng had favored.

Zhang Congzheng's purported ignorance of the pathogenic role of depletion and the importance of the patient's vitality level in choosing the appropriate therapy suggested to Zhu Zhenheng that the editors of Zhang's works had overstated Zhang's support for attacking methods. "Otherwise, how could what is said in his books differ so much from the

## PDF第130页

meaning of the Inner Canon's and Zhang [Ji] Zhongjing?" (何其書之所言與內經仲景之意若是之不同也; 67b). "I thus resolved to find a famous teacher on whom I could rely to dispel my perplexity" (於是決意於得名師 以為之依歸 發其茅塞; 67b). $ ^{130} $

Zhu thus claimed he was prodded to seek a mentor because he found contradictions between the teachings of an admired physician (Zhang Congzheng) and two sources that many considered as the basis of medical education in his time: the Inner Canon and Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury. In his introduction to Gezhi yulun 格致餘論, Zhu recalled that he was looking for explanations of why the recipes found in the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (the formulary that was still widely used in Zhu's region in the fourteenth century) could not cure the illnesses of his day. When he left his native place in 1324, his education had in any case been largely based on texts that had been edited and distributed by the Song imperial court: the Basic Questions, the Treatise on Cold Injury, and the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service.

In his quest for answers, Zhu started “roaming on rivers and lakes. Whenever I heard that there was someone practicing medicine in such-and-such a place, I would go visit him. I thus went through several prefectures, but found no one [appropriate]” (遂游江湖但聞某處有某治醫便往拜而問之連經數郡無一人焉). Zhu’s “roaming on rivers and lakes” was quite literal, since waterways were the main thoroughfares in Zhejiang and the Lower Yangzi region. $ ^{131} $ From his county he went north on small rivers until he reached Hangzhou, where he crossed the Qiantang River into the Wu region, south of Suzhou. Zhu most likely traveled on the Grand Canal, a crucial axis to understand the southward diffusion

## PDF第131页

of northern medicine in the fourteenth century and the later circulation of Zhu's disciples (see Chapter 5). Zhu then went inland to Xuancheng 宣城 in Ningguo 寧國 Route (Anhui) and back to the Grand Canal. $ ^{132} $ From there he proceeded north to Zhenjiang 鎮江, $ ^{133} $ where the Grand Canal flowed into the Yangzi River, and from there moved westward on the Yangzi until he reached Nanjing (建業).

“Only later when I reached Dingcheng [?] did I obtain [Liu Wansu’s] Yuanbing shi and manuscripts of recipes by Dongyuan [Li Gao]. I then clearly realized [Zhang Congzheng] Zihe’s rashness, but I still had not found any conclusive doctrines. I was starting to think there was no one in [all of] Jiang-Zhe [province] whom I could take as a teacher.” $ ^{134} $ (後到定城始得原病式東垣方薰乃大悟子和之孟浪然終未得的然之議論將謂江浙間無可為師者; 67b-68a). From this account we know that by the 1320s Liu Wansu’s Yuanbing shi and manuscripts of Li Gao’s prescriptions were circulating outside of formal teaching lineages, and that they were available south of the Yangzi River, in large part because the empire had been reunified by the Mongols so that people and texts could now travel along a north-south axis. $ ^{135} $

After returning to Hangzhou in the summer of 1325 with a few books but still no teacher, Zhu heard of an old man called Luo Zhiti 羅知悌 (1238?-1327), allegedly a second-

## PDF第132页

generation disciple of Liu Wansu who had just returned to Hangzhou after a prolonged exile in northern China. $ ^{136} $ After three months of rejection, Zhu managed to become Luo's disciple, probably sometime in the fall of 1325.

Luo Zhiti was the key figure in the formation of Zhu Zhenheng's synthesis of earlier masters. In addition to being a second-generation follower of Liu Wansu, Luo was "broadly versed in the doctrines of the two experts Zhang Congzheng and Li Gao" (旁通张從正李昇二家之說) and possessed many books that Zhu had never seen. $ ^{137} $

[Luo] taught [Zhu] the books of Liu [Wansu], Li [Gao], and Zhang [Congzheng or Yuansu?], transmitting to him the main precepts of the three experts. $ ^{138} $ [When the three were at variance,] he always decided [who was right] on the basis of the [Inner] Canon. [To Zhu] he also said: ‘dispose of your old learning completely: it is wrong.’ $ ^{139} $ When the Old Man [Zhu Zhenheng] heard these words, [his doubts] dissipated and he had not the slightest misgiving left in his breast. $ ^{140} $

## PDF第133页

授以劉李張諸書 為之敷揚三家之旨 而一斷於經 且曰 盡去而舊學 非是也 翁聞其言 渙焉無少凝滯於胸臆

# Luo also revealed something that “few people knew”: “Damp Heat and Ministerial Fire make most illnesses” (濕熱 相火為病最多 人罕有知其秘者). He added:

Changsha [Zhang Ji's] books are detailed on external contamination, [whereas]

Dongyuan [Li Gao’s] books explain inner harm in detail. Only after you understand them both thoroughly can you treat illness without uneasiness. $ ^{141} $ If you limit yourself to the learning of Chen and Pei [the compilers of the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service] and follow it perfunctorily, you will kill people!

長沙之書 詳於外感 東垣之書 詳於內傷 必兩盡之 治疾方無所憾 區區陳裴之學 泥之且殺人

When Zhu heard this, Song Lian claimed, "his old doubts were dissipated" (夙疑為之釋然).

In his account of how Luo Zhiti cured a depressed young monk, Zhu showed that Luo had already synthesized the teachings of previous masters into a coherent approach. Luo's therapy (replenishing followed by purges) made Zhu realize that "attacking methods can be used only if one is sturdy and replete, or only if one's constitution is originally robust. Otherwise, primordial Qi is harmed as deviant [Qi] is removed: benign illnesses will always become grave and grave illnesses will cause death." (因大悟攻擊之法 必其人充實 禀質本壯乃可行也 否則邪去而正氣傷 小病必重 重病必死; 68b.) This last point resolved Zhu's prior misgivings concerning uniformly aggressive therapies.

For the year and a half in which Zhu assisted Luo in seeing patients, Luo did not use “a single ready-made recipe” (往來一年半 並無一定之方). Some of his recipes “used

## PDF第134页

attacking and replenishing drugs conjointly; $ ^{142} $ some attacked first and then replenished; others replenished before attacking. I came to the great realization that old recipes could not possibly tally with current illnesses.”(自有攻補兼用者亦有先攻後補者有先補後攻者又大悟古方治今病焉能吻合;68b-69a.) $ ^{143} $

The flexibility of Luo’s prescriptions rested on the notion that each illness instance was unique, a precept that Zhang Yuansu and Li Gao had previously defended. The critique did not target only old recipes: it was also directed against ready-made formulas such as those found in the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service. Following Luo’s teachings, Zhu later wrote a critique of the Recipes where he specially disparaged this book’s lack of discernment when it asserted that a remedy could treat “all Wind illnesses” or “all forms of xian.” $ ^{144} $

After his master's death in 1327, Zhu went to Shaoxing to meet Xiang Xin 項昕, a physician who had mastered most current doctrines but had not yet been exposed to northern medicine. $ ^{145} $ When Zhu “showed him the books of Liu Hejian [Wansu], Zhang Dairen [Congzheng] and Li Dongyuan [Gao] of the Jin dynasty,” Xiang expressed great interest, but mistrusted the claim that old recipes could not treat current illnesses. $ ^{146} $ Zhu then returned to his native region, where people still “stuck to the teachings of Chen and

## PDF第135页

Pei"—the compilers of the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service. But his clinical success quickly ended the disdain with which other doctors first greeted his doctrines.

Wu Yiyi (1993-94) gave many examples of physicians who learned medicine from books at the time, but he did not make it clear enough that these books represented a variety of medical approaches. His concentration on Liu Wansu kept him from noticing, for instance, that the nine physicians he named between pages 56 and 59 were all said to have studied Li Gao's works as well. Zhu Zhenheng, Xiang Xin, Ge Yinglei (see note 136), Ni Weide 倪维德 (1303-1377),$^{147}$ and Hua Shou 滑壽 (1304-86)$^{148}$ were all southern people who studied northern books in the 1320s. Their lives show that the transmission of northern texts into Jiangsu and Zhejiang first took place along the Grand Canal and mostly within networks of medical specialists, not from teachers to disciples. Adepts sought other specialists, as Zhu had sought Luo Zhiti and later Xiang Xin, and as Xiang had found Ge Qiansun. Zhu's direct instruction by a master appears to have been the exception rather than the rule. Let us now turn to the content of Zhu Zhenheng's doctrines, especially as they concerned madness.

## Madness, Fire, and “Ministerial Fire”

I showed above that Liu Wansu's attribution of madness to Heat and Fire (and his reduction of Wind to an effect of Heat) had rested on the Treatise on Cold Injury and Chapter 74 of the Basic Questions. Song medicine (both before and after Liu) had no equivalent

## PDF第136页

precept. Since Zhu Zhenheng's understanding of both illness and vitality was rooted in Liu Wansu's conception of Fire, Zhu's books became important channels through which later physicians came to focus on Fire in their discussions of Wind, madness, and the emotions.

Only a few passages allow to reconstruct Zhu Zhenheng's explanations of psycho-behavioral symptoms, but he apparently attributed insanity (顛狂) either to mucus or to "Fire," an invisible inner fluid that he defined as an excess of Qi (凡氣有餘便是火). $ ^{149} $

For sturdy people with replete Qi who become crazy as a result of a flourishing of Fire, direct therapy can be used, $ ^{150} $ or they can be given icy water with magnesium sulfate to drink. $ ^{151} $ If kuang [results from] the over-flourishing of Fire in a depleted person, ginger decoction can be administered; one who is given cold water or treated with direct therapy will die immediately. $ ^{152} $ The likes of roasted phellodendron and foxglove replenish Yin, as a result of which Fire descends of its own.

人狀氣實 火盛顛狂者 可用正治 或硝冰水飲之 人虛火盛狂 $ ^{153} $ 可用生姜湯與之 若投以冰水正治 立死 有補陰即火自降者 炒黃柏地黃之類

For madness caused by Fire that overlapped with depletion, Zhu proposed the use of two cooling drugs (Phellodendron and Foxglove) for replenishing Yin, an approach with which he would later be identified (5.6) and then criticized (6.2, 6.3).

## PDF第137页

To Zhu, Fire represented Yang, an active principle that sustained life but could also harm it if it moved out of control. Zhu articulated this view of vitality and pathology in an essay called “On Ministerial Fire” (相火論) in Gezhi yulun 格致餘論. $ ^{154} $ Because it merged the teachings of several medical masters with central precepts of the Daoxue tradition in which Zhu had been initiated, this essay was the epitome of Zhu’s synthesis. The first half of the text disserted on the cosmic meaning of Fire and of its correspondent phenomena on Earth and in the Heavens. $ ^{155} $ In the more medically oriented second half, Zhu’s answers to objections his theories might provoke relied almost entirely on Liu Wansu’s interpretation of the meaning of Fire in the Inner Canon. The thrust of Zhu’s demonstration was that Fire was present in all circulation channels and could cause almost any illness by consuming the fragile Yin aspects of human vitality. $ ^{156} $ Zhu was thus echoing his master Luo Zhiti, who had insisted that Ministerial Fire was one of the major causes of illness. $ ^{157} $

Like Zhang Congzheng before him, Zhu embraced Liu Wansu's reinterpretation of Wind as a form of Heat (see note 36 above): "When Qibo, Zhongjing [Zhang Ji], and Sun Simiao spoke of Wind, in general they seem to have referred to the influence of an external deviant [Qi]. When Liu Hejian [Wansu] spoke of Wind, he clearly referred to Heat

## PDF第138页

symptoms caused by inner harm" (岐伯仲景孫思邈之言風 大意似指外邪之感 劉河間之言風 明指內傷熱證). $ ^{158} $ Zhu even attributed typical "Wind illnesses" like "painful Wind" (痛風) and "Wind strokes" (中風) to excessive Heat in the Blood. $ ^{159} $ It is thanks to influential medical writers like Zhu Zhenheng that the etiology of Fire almost entirely displaced that of Wind in Chinese medicine.

Zhu even proposed his own theory on the origin of inner Wind: “In the south and west, the climate is warm and the earth is mostly damp. When ‘Wind illnesses’ occur, they are not caused by [real] Winds. It is dampness that generates mucus, which generates Heat, which generates Wind” (東南氣溫 而地多濕 有風病者 非風也 皆濕生痰 痰生熱 熱生風也). $ ^{160} $ Despite its humble beginnings in an analysis of the pharmacological effects of chicken, this passage became part of an influential re-conceptualization of Wind disorders that was widely debated in Ming medical circles, and thus also contributed to the collapse of the old notion of “Wind illnesses”—a trend I discuss in the conclusion below. $ ^{161} $

## Mucus and possession: a doctrinal novelty

By Zhu Zhenheng's time, mucus had become independent of Wind and, like Fire, was thought to trigger many illnesses that had once been attributed to Wind. To Zhu, there was nowhere mucus did not go (無處不到). For instance, all lumps on the body were

## PDF第139页

created by mucus (凡人身上中下有塊者 多是痰也). $ ^{162} $ Zhu also assumed that mucus could create madness. "Mucus in the chest area makes people mad or prone to forgetting: bamboo juice should be used; for Wind mucus, also take bamboo juice, which can nourish Blood as well" (痰在膈間 使人顛狂健忘 宜用竹瀝 風痰亦服竹瀝 又能養血). $ ^{163} $

The mucus section of Probing the Mysteries of the Golden Casket (Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄, where the above passage appeared) advocated the use of all sorts of emetic methods to get rid of mucus in the upper body, and recommended purgation for mucus in the lower body, a double therapeutic approach that approximated Zhang Congzheng's.

But Zhu's main interpretive novelty regarding mad symptoms was his influential formulation that some illnesses that looked like possession were actually caused by either mucus or depletion. Because the symptoms of possession were often considered similar to those of madness (see 8.2), and because fifteenth- and sixteenth-century physicians cited Zhu's views so reverently (see Chapter 5), Zhu's precept contributed to the idea that madness was caused by mucus and could be treated with emetics.

Zhu Zhenheng's discussion of this problem appeared in an essay called "On there being depletion illnesses and mucus illnesses that look like affliction by evil" (虚病痰病有似邪祟論), in Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 (1347), Zhu's main doctrinal work and one of the two books he published during his lifetime. $ ^{164} $ His position is worth explaining at length because

## PDF第140页

it became an influential view of how human consciousness could go amiss. Zhu first quoted a passage from the Inner Canon that later came to be cited as his own: “Blood and Qi are the body’s/person’s consciousness” (血氣者身之神也; 27a).¹⁶⁵ This sentence rooted consciousness and cognitive processes into the functioning of the whole organism and allowed for the possibility that Fire, mucus, and even food could affect thought by acting on Blood and Qi. In this view, human consciousness depended on the equilibrium of bodily fluids. To use vocabulary I explained in 3.2, consciousness “supervened” on Blood and Qi, the Yin and Yang aspects of vital fluids. Zhu continued:

When the Spirit is worn out or depleted, deviant [Qi] invades as a result. This all makes good sense. When both Blood and Qi are damaged, mucus encroaches on the middle-jiao area, obstructing the ascent and descent [of Qi], which can no longer circulate, leading to a situation in which each of the twelve officials [the sensory organs] lose their functions: there is vacuous wildness in vision, hearing, speech, and movements. When [this disorder] is treated as [a case of possession by] evil, the person will certainly die. Oh, what an injustice! Who could bear such responsibility!

神既衰乏　邪因而入　理或有之　若夫血氣兩虧　痰客中焦　妨礙升降　不得運用　以致十二官各失其職　視聽言動皆有虛妄　以邪治之　其人必死

吁哉冤乎　誰執其咎

In Zhu’s view, even the symptoms most typical of possession were often no more than confusion caused by mucus. Zhu’s theory was a new way of problematizing possession symptoms, an attempt to naturalize the etiology of acts that commoners, ritual specialists, and probably most physicians, commonly attributed to the action of malevolent forces.

## PDF第141页

Dressing into different explanatory trappings symptoms that were commonly associated with possession, Zhu tried to undermine the demonological diagnosis by explaining how he cured several “possessed” patients with simple drugs.

Older brother Fu, the son of a xianmu official, $ ^{166} $ 17 or 18 years old. Feeling thirsty during the hot summer months because he was exhausted from working, he drank lavish amounts of sour prune juice. He was also seriously startled three or four times in succession. As a result, his speech and sight became disorganized: the illness looked like [it was caused by] evil ghosts. On both wrists, his pulse was empty and strung, and, secondarily, sunken and accelerated. $ ^{167} $ Acceleration denotes the presence of Heat. I said. “Empty and strung pulses indicate great fright. In addition, the sour juice of the prune is in stasis in the upper Stomach. $ ^{168} $ The illness will abate if we replenish depletion, clear up Heat, and guide out the stagnant mucus.”

憲幕之子傅兄 年十七八 時暑月 因大勞而渴 恣飲梅漿 又連得大驚三四次 妄言妄見 病似邪鬼 診其脈 兩手皆虛弦而帶沉數 予曰 數為有熱 虛弦是大驚 又梅酸之漿鬱於中脘 補虛清熱 導去痰滯 病乃可安

Zhu administered drugs for a few days, adjusting treatment until the patient finally healed.

Zhu probably developed his new theory on the basis of deductions from other principles concerning the functioning of the human body (the recognized role of mucus and depletion in confusing the mind, for instance), from the experience of having cured

“possession” cases with drugs (would real ghosts have gone away?), and from precursors like

## PDF第142页

Zhang Congzheng (1156-1228), who had treated a woman who was dreaming of commerce with ghosts with three vomits, three purges, and three sweating sessions. $ ^{169} $ He may also have pondered on a passage from Chen Yan's 陳言 Sanyin fang 三因方 (1174) that had attributed strange behavior to the action of saliva generated by Qi stasis in the viscera. $ ^{170} $

In the next case, Zhu quickly dismissed the possession theory:

Junior relative on mother's side. On the first day of the New Year after he had drunk and eaten a lot, he started to speak wild words and to talk and see in a disorganized fashion. I enquired. [He said] it was his deceased older brother who had attached himself to his body and was talking about things [that took place] when he was [still] alive. His uncle who stood by his side berated: "This is not 'evil'. He ate too much meat and drank too much. It is only the doing of mucus." We made him drink a large bowl of very salty water: he vomited one or two pints of mucus, which made him sweat profusely. He felt drowsy, fell asleep for the night, and then was calm.

外弟歲一日醉飽後 亂言 妄語妄見 詢之 係伊亡兄附體 言生前事甚的乃叔在邊叱之曰 非邪 食腥與酒太過 痰所為耳 灌鹽湯一大碗 吐痰一二升 汗因大作 困睡一宵而安

In both cases, Zhu interpreted his patient's visions and delirium as having been caused by Heat and mucus occasioned by the ingestion of food or alcohol. The purely physical healing he performed was indistinguishable from Zhang Congzheng's. $ ^{171} $ Zhu's attribution of possession symptoms to mucus was widely adopted and remained central to Chinese medical treatments of madness and possession until the nineteenth century.

## PDF第143页

### 4.5 “The hundred illnesses all start in mucus”: new departures from the Canon

Zhu Zhenheng was not the only fourteenth-century physician who attributed psycho-behavioral pathology to mucus. Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) was among the earliest southern physicians recorded to have treated mad behavior with emetics. It may not be a coincidence that Ni had also read both Liu Wansu and Zhang Congzheng. Ni's female patient alternatively cried and laughed: "people thought she was possessed" (人以為崇). $ ^{172} $ Finding that "the pulses of her two wrists were sunken" (两手脈俱沈), Ni diagnosed that "there must be accumulations in the Stomach area" (胃脘必有所積). He gave her a mix of boiled and unboiled water to guide out the accumulation: "she healed after she vomited several sheng of mucus" (以生熟水導之 吐痰涎數升愈). Lü Fu 呂復 (late fourteenth century), from Ningbo in northwestern Zhejiang, prescribed vomitives (湧劑) to cure a crazy man. $ ^{173} $ He identified "Wind mucus" as the culprit.

Zhu's and Ni's therapies were signs of a general diffusion of the mucus etiology in the fourteenth century. Wang Gui 王珪 (1264-1354?), from Suzhou, published his Main Discussions on Nourishing Life (Yangsbeng zhulun 養生主論) in 1338. $ ^{174} $ Claiming that “the hundred illnesses all start in mucus” (百病皆由痰), $ ^{175} $ Wang's book presented a complete etiological system, as well as a view of “nourishing life” centered around the management of mucus. One of the pillars of Wang's construction was a remedy of his own invention: the

## PDF第144页

“Mucus-roiling pill”(滚痰丸). Besides recommending it for the treatment of many illnesses (including madness), Wang also advocated its use for “nourishing life,” an atypical usage for an emetic. This remedy was often recommended for the treatment of madness in Ming and Qing medical writings, where it was usually interpreted as a purgative.

Fourteenth-century formularies also showed traces of this spread of the mucus etiology. Unlike earlier works of its kind, many sections of Wei Yilin's 危亦林 Shiyi dexiao fang 世醫得效方 (first printed in 1345) contained entries on mucus. $ ^{176} $ Emetics were among the remedies Wei prescribed for madness. In the section on “Ailments of the Heart” (心恙), the “Uniquely effective bitter-clove powder” 獨效苦丁香散 was said to “treat sudden mental afflictions and persistent madness caught from extreme fright or sorrow, in which mucus Qi encroaches upwards on the Heart; the source [of this mucus] should be cut off” (治忽患心疾 癲狂不止 得之驚優之極 痰氣上犯心包 當伐其源). $ ^{177} $ That powder was designed to induce “massive vomiting” (大吐).

### 4.6 How mucus caused madness: the crystallization of an illness script

As I just showed, the mucus etiology of madness and other illnesses had become central to medical discourse by the middle of the fourteenth century, when formularies recommended emetic remedies for insanity and when several physicians were recorded as having treated mad patients with vomitives. But the doctors who had by then endorsed the

## PDF第145页

use of emetics had not specified how they thought mucus could cause madness. $ ^{178} $ Only Zhu Zhenheng had argued that mucus caused possession-like symptoms by impeding the normal circulation of Qi in the “middle jiao,” the area between the abdomen and the upper chest.

Yet, the expression that became common to refer to the madness-causing powers of mucus in late-imperial times was “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” (痰迷心觑). In this phrase, mucus caused confusion by affecting not only the chest, but specifically the Heart, the organ where most Chinese located consciousness and cognitive activities. Although Chinese physicians rarely discussed what the “openings of the heart” were and where they were located (as far as I know, these orifices never appeared in the illustrations of the viscera found in some treatises), medical texts unmistakably implied that they were physical structures that were being obstructed or occupied by tangible mucus.

The script of blockage of the Heart's openings emerged independently from the notion of mucus. A twelfth-century gynecological work had interpreted as follows some women's inability to speak in the first month after giving birth (產後月內不語).

The human heart has seven holes and three hairs. In many cases postpartum, depletion of Blood and weakness of Qi lead to stoppage and accumulation.

Damaged Blood $ ^{179} $ blocked in the Heart’s openings then makes the Spirit unable to attain clarity. Moreover, Cardiac Qi is connected to the tongue; when Cardiac Qi is blocked or obstructed, the tongue also becomes stiff, making one unable to speak. $ ^{180} $

## PDF第146页

人心有七孔三毛 產後血虛氣弱 多致停積 敗血閉於心竅 神志不能明了

又心氣通於舌 心氣閉塞則舌亦強矣 故令不語

The number of openings mentioned here alluded to the 42 $ ^{nd} $ problem of the Canon of Problems, a rare anatomical passage in the Chinese medical literature that said that the heart had “seven holes and three hairs” (七孔三毛). $ ^{181} $ Since qiao 竅 and kong 孔 were synonyms, xin kong 心孔 and xin qiao 心竅 can both be translated as “the Heart’s openings.”

The notion that the heart possessed seven orifices had appeared in the Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji 史記; compiled from 141 to 87 BCE), which had attributed it to ancient sources. $ ^{182} $ Trying to verify the rumor that “the heart of worthies has seven holes,” the evil last king of the Shang dynasty had supposedly eviscerated his reprobative paternal uncle Bigan 比干. This notion had made its way into the second-century Canon of Problems. One of Li Jiong’s 李駉 commentaries (13 $ ^{th} $ century) to that book had explained how many “openings” people of various levels of intelligence had in their hearts, claiming that only a sage’s heart counted seven. But the notion of “mucus confusing the openings of the heart” did not rest on the assumption that one’s intelligence depended on the number of one’s cardiac orifices. $ ^{183} $

## PDF第147页

The phrase “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” described a cognitive effect (confusion) on a physical structure (the Heart’s openings). $ ^{184} $ But how could mucus be said to “confuse” mindless orifices? To some extent, the answer lies in an idea I developed in 3.2: the word “Heart” referred both to the mind and to a physical structure, and could thus serve as hinge between physical and mental processes in descriptions of psycho-behavioral pathology. The expression tan mi xin qiao 痰迷心竅 thus probably sounded less dissonant in Chinese than in its English translation. $ ^{185} $

It is also possible that  $ \tan mi $ 痰迷 had originally meant “mucus gone astray” instead of “mucus confusing” followed by a direct object. Both instances of  $ \tan mi $ in Taiping shenghui fang 太平聖惠方 (992), for instance, appeared in the compound “mucus is astray, causing failure to eat” (痰迷不下食), which was probably synonymous with “mucus flows in reverse direction [i.e., upwards from the stomach area instead of downwards], causing failure to eat” (痰逆不下食) in the same book. $ ^{186} $ In these two expressions, both mi 迷 (“being lost,” “going astray”) and mi 逆 (“flowing in the reverse direction”) indicated that mucus flowed or lodged itself where it should not. In this light,  $ \tan mi \times \sin qiao t $ 痰迷心竅 could be interpreted as “mucus straying into the Heart’s openings” (痰迷於心竅). $ ^{187} $ But in late-imperial medical texts and popular parlance,  $ \tan mi $ 痰迷 was often used on its own to mean “mucus

## PDF第148页

confusion."$^{188}$ This semantic evolution of $\tan$ $\dot{m}$ is one of the factors that created the strange-

sounding expression “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart.”$^{189}$

The emergence of the phrase tan mi xinqiao reflected not only a medical innovation, but also the semantic convergence of several similar expressions into a dominant one. Puji fung 普濟方 (1390) recorded phrases such as “mucus confusing the Heart’s Envelope” (痰迷心胞), “Blood congestion confusing the Heart” (血壅迷心), “mucus confusing the orifices of the Heart” (痰迷心孔), and “saliva lodged in the Heart’s envelope” (涎留心包).¹⁹⁰ These all described the same process: a trespassing fluid that caused mental confusion (or loss of consciousness) by lodging itself in the Heart, its “Envelope” (the pericardium), or its “openings.”¹⁹¹ What mattered to those who spoke of “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” was perhaps less the anatomic reality of these apertures than the general illness script the expression described: “mucus appears inside the body → mucus flushes the Heart → madness ensues.”

### 4.7 Conclusion: “Jin-Yuan medicine” and the collapse of “Wind illnesses”

“Jin-Yuan medicine” and its “four masters”

Jin and Yuan physicians are usually portrayed as innovators who dissented from the Song medical mainstream, but many of their apparent innovations were built on earlier trends. Liu Wansu's reasoning on the five periods and six Qi, for example, cannot be

## PDF第149页

separated from Song Huizong's sponsoring of these cosmological schemes. Zhang Congzheng cited Song predecessors to his use of emetics and adopted Xu Shuwei's argument that Wind illnesses should not always be treated as Wind illnesses. And all the physicians I discussed based their thinking on Song editions of the Basic Questions and the Treatise on Cold Injury. Their precepts should be understood within broader thought currents rather than as bolts of intellectual brilliance against the grey sky of Song medical thinking.

What differentiated the luminaries of the Jin and Yuan from Song medical experts was the genre in which they wrote. Whereas Song doctors mostly compiled formularies and works on Cold Injury, Li Gao and others composed doctrinally minded treatises that discussed important etiologies (Fire, “inner harm”), human vitality (the centrality of Ministerial Fire or the Spleen and Stomach), clinical skills (pulse diagnosis, flexible formulas), and therapeutic methods (“replenishing the interior,” the use of Cold-Injury remedies to treat all illnesses).

Although explicit doctrinal positions are easier to identify with “schools” than the untheorized therapies found in prescription manuals, the thinkers I discussed did not found “schools” and should not be called anachronistically “the four masters of the Jin and Yuan period.” $ ^{192} $ The notion that there were four such masters is sometimes traced to Zhu Zhenheng’s friend Song Lian (1310-1381), who became a powerful official at the Ming court in the 1360s. In his introduction to Zhu’s Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 (1347), Song explained that Zhu had learned the teachings of Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng, and Li Gao from Luo Zhiti. Song thus portrayed his friend as the ultimate figure of a new kind of medicine whose efficiency and scholarly value contrasted with Song official medicine as represented by the

## PDF第150页

Recipes of the Pharmacy Service. But this assemblage emphasized Zhu’s debt to three medical thinkers; it was not a point about the medicine of any period in particular. $ ^{193} $

Much of what is now called “Jin-Yuan medicine” took shape in the 1320s in the Jiang-Zhe province: it was a synthesis of the precepts and methods of thinkers—Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng, Wang Haogu, and Li Gao—who had lived in the north in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries and who had highly valued Zhang Ji’s teachings on Cold Injury. The synthesis was created by doctors who had access to the books of these northern physicians. These books had themselves traveled down the Grand Canal as far south as Hangzhou, carried by physicians and officials. $ ^{194} $ Zhu Zhenheng, the emblematic figure of this doctrinal amalgam, learned this new medicine from Luo Zhiti as a synthesis and enhanced it with precepts from the “Learning of the Way.” Zhu’s doctrinal blend was rhetorically grounded in the Inner Canon and claimed to borrow the best from previous approaches: Zhang Ji’s remedies, Liu Wansu’s etiology of Fire and his interpretation of Wind, Li Gao’s notion of inner harm and reliance on pulse diagnosis, and Zhang Congzheng’s attacking therapies. It is this synthesis that eventually shaped Ming medicine, though we shall see in the next chapter that the process was far from simple.

## PDF第151页

Incidentally, “Jin-Yuan medicine” did not even dominate the Jin and Yuan periods. Since good arguments are insufficient to make ideas socially dominant, we should be careful not to focus too much on innovations. Zhang Congzheng was fiercely criticized during his lifetime, and Li Gao’s views contrasted with those of his contemporaries. And even if Zhu Zhenheng was arguably “the most eminent physician of his time” (Sivin 1995c: 10), his brand of medicine did not govern contemporary practice even in his native region. $ ^{195} $ Anecdotes in his writings remind us that his interpretation of Fire and the human body were not immediately accepted, and that other medical methods were more prevalent than his, if only because they had had more time to be transmitted. Zhu’s flexible approach to therapy was also difficult to learn, and despite his cogent critiques of the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service, Zhu wrote no therapeutic manual that could have decreased the dominance of this user-friendly book. This is why his precepts probably remained confined to a restricted elite until the late fifteenth century, when easier works claiming to represent his approaches were published. The next chapter explains how Zhu’s doctrines became dominant.

## From outer to inner etiologies (and the collapse of "Wind illnesses")

By the fourteenth century, mucus and excessive Heat had replaced Wind intrusion as the main causes of madness and the main targets of clinical intervention. This momentous shift in the etiology and therapy of insanity took place without much debate about the nature or causes of madness, but made sense within three converging trends: 1) a shift in the meaning of “Wind” that led to the marginalization of this once-central etiological category; 2) the rise to dominance of etiologies and therapies based on the Treatise on Cold Injury (where Heat was prominent), and 3) the general rise of inner etiologies. The latter trend meant more

## PDF第152页

emphasis on the inner conditions that made one vulnerable to illness, increased attention to the inner mechanisms of externally contracted illnesses, and the attribution to purely internal factors of illnesses that used to be understood as externally triggered.

The new etiology of madness was part of a general shift away from the script of external invasion by Wind to scripts describing the movements of mucus and Fire inside the body. Late imperial physicians who traced a fit of madness to “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” were unaware that this expression was the distillate of such complex transformations. By aggregation from diversity, so to speak, the mucus-and-Fire script coalesced from many trends in medical thinking. $ ^{196} $ 1. the emergence of mucus as a pathological fluid; 2. the merging of the pathogenic roles of saliva and mucus; 3. the displacement of therapeutic attention from Wind to its internal agents (saliva, mucus); 4. the resulting increase in the use of emetics; 5. Liu Wansu’s re-interpretation of Wind as a form of inner Heat; 6. the transfer onto madness of explanations of mental confusion (mucus surge, “damaged Blood,” or fright Qi) that had appeared in accounts of Wind strokes, convulsive seizures, or postpartum illnesses; 7. the large-scale borrowing of etiologies and treatments from the Treatise on Cold Injury, including the attribution of madness to excessive Heat and the use of purgatives to treat it; 8. the general rise of the mucus etiology; 9. Zhang Congzheng’s regular treatment of madness with purgatives and emetics; and 10. Zhu Zhenheng’s teachings on how mucus could cause possession symptoms.

The expansive category of “Wind illnesses” that had dominated medical treatises until at least the eighth century crumbled in part because of explicit critiques. Liu Wansu’s reinterpretation of Wind as a superficial manifestation of Heat and Li Gao’s distinction between illnesses that are “externally contracted” and those that result from “inner harm”

## PDF第153页

facilitated Zhu Zhenheng's critique of the notion of Wind deployed in the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service. How could a recipe cure “all Wind illnesses”—as this book claimed—when “Wind” was sometimes external and sometimes internal? To Zhu and the countless physicians who later followed his teachings, “Wind” now referred to disorders so dissimilar that they could no longer hold under such a large mantle. The collapse of “Wind illnesses” made possible new nosological groupings within the inclusive category of “miscellaneous illnesses.” These shifts endured because texts that embodied the new approaches eventually became central in elite medical education. This is the process I analyze in the next chapter.

## PDF第154页

“Danxi said…”

The textual life of medical ideas on madness, 1360-1530

Danxi said that dian belongs to Yin and kuang to Yang, that dian is excessive joy and kuang excessive anger. He also stated that the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang.

丹溪謂癲屬陰　狂屬陽　癲多喜　狂多怒　又云重陰者癲　重陽者狂

Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 in Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556) $ ^{1} $

Chapters 3 and 4 traced a momentous transformation in the etiology of madness from Wind to internal Fire and mucus between the twelfth and the fourteenth century. The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) was not a period of strong epistemological breaks in medicine, but one in which physicians re-orchestrated earlier approaches to form new doctrinal and therapeutic emphases. Xu Chunfu above was citing the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法, a composite work compiled and printed in his own Huizhou Prefecture (Anhui) in the early 1480s and that quickly became the most influential work attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 ("Danxi" 丹溪: 1282-1358). This and the next chapter show how the development of Ming medical thinking on dian and kuang was shaped by three important trends that are well symbolized by Xu's quotation: the rising influence of precepts attributed to Danxi, the importance of Huizhou as a center of medical theorizing and publication, and the profound intertextuality of the elite medical tradition.

## PDF第155页

### 5.1 Intertextual composition and the Danxi corpus

The “Danxi corpus” in the history of medical thinking on madness

The rise to prominence of a body of teachings attributed to Zhu Zhenheng deeply shaped Ming medical thinking and practice. Zhu's teachings appeared in a wide array of texts books and manuscripts by Zhu, notes by his disciples, works attributed to Zhu that were collated after his death by people who had known him, and later anthologies based on a mix of the previous elements and a dose of interpretation of what Zhu might have thought. These works were compiled and printed from the second half of the fourteenth century to about 1600, when the last book attributed to Zhu was published. I refer to these texts as the "Danxi corpus" because most of them contained Zhu's literary name "Danxi" in their title. If there was ever a Danxi school of medicine, it should refer to this body of works and to the physicians whose training, doctrinal thinking, and clinical practice were guided by them.

The Danxi corpus was one major channel through which the reinterpretation of madness that I traced in the previous chapter was further systematized and became available to a large number of physicians. I will explain how, between 1360 and 1530, the Danxi corpus kept attributing madness to mucus and Fire and recommending treating pscho-behavioral pathology with purgatives or emetics.

For a number of interrelated reasons, these doctrines became the enduring basis from which physicians explained and treated madness for the rest of imperial history. The notion of “Wind” upon which the older etiologies had relied had been discredited. Few of the works that had attributed madness to Wind were reprinted, and a dominant hermeneutic strategy (discussed below) allowed doctors to ignore earlier works that were not consistent with current understandings. Finally, a few dispersed statements on madness came to be considered authoritative simply because they appeared in texts attributed to Danxi. These

## PDF第156页

passages were bundled together in increasingly sophisticated sections on madness in the Danxi corpus, as well as in the works of the physicians who admired Danxi's works.

For my argument to be convincing, I must therefore demonstrate the ascendancy of Danxi precepts. Discursive dominance requires a certain density of social diffusion. Through texts and people (notably Zhu's own disciples), Zhu Zhenheng's medicine first spread like a rhizome along arteries such as the Grand Canal, feeding on social connections and networks of teachers and readers, growing more densely in some places than others, and presenting different faces in the many areas where it flourished. The Danxi corpus became dominant insofar as many important debates between medical specialists concerned the way in which Danxi's legacy should be interpreted. Physicians appropriated the name "Danxi" as a symbol of authority or used it as a foil to make their own doctrinal emphases appear more distinctive.

The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. We know almost nothing about the routes by which medical books circulated throughout the empire, carried by traders, officials en route to their posts, or wandering physicians. The role of manuscripts and hand copies of printed books can only be surmised from notes made en passant in various writings, and the private letters that contributed to spreading new medical conceptions have almost all disappeared. $ ^{3} $ Finally we know too little about the oral teachings

## PDF第157页

that made texts meaningful in particular teaching traditions, and about the process by which practitioners developed their skills through a mix of readings, instruction by one or several masters or seniors, and interaction with real patients. All these caveats make our understanding of received texts all the more important.

Intertextuality, zhezhong hermeneutics, and deliberate syncretism

To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $

Trying to prove the intertextuality of the Chinese medical literature may seem like laboring the obvious. After all, all texts everywhere are interdiscursively composed, and the authors I study never hid their reliance on previous works. Most literate Chinese traditions readily represented themselves as textually-based lineages of “transmission” (chuan 傳) and

## PDF第158页

relied on one of the most intertextual devices imaginable—the commentary. But I believe too few have drawn the consequences of these practices seriously.

My intertextual approach draws attention to a notion whose importance has escaped most scholars: zbezhong (折衷 or 折中), which I shall leave untranslated for now. Paul Unschuld's widely consulted survey of Chinese medical ideas (1985: 203) contains a brief account of "an eclectic tradition known as the School of Compromise (zbezhong pai)," whose "followers did not propose any of their own theories, selecting instead those insights and instructions from the extensive medical literature that they found useful." Unschuld gave Li Zhongzi's 李中梓 (1588-1655) discussion of the Gate of Life (命門) as an example of eclectic thought and called Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) "one of the earliest eclectics." By claiming that the adherents of this purported school shared "the concern of numerous Confucians that medical knowledge could become so highly specialized that only experts would be able to master the field," he implied that these eclectics sought to assemble a smattering of knowledge for practical purposes with little effort to integrate the results.

I will show in this chapter that zbezbong was not a lesser school of casual gleaners (and actually not a school at all), but arguably the dominant hermenetic strategy and mode of textual composition of the most sophisticated medical writers of the age. Systematic treatises were the most intertextual of all, and syncretism was a sign of distinction rather than amateurism. Partly because of the doctrinally ambitious nature of medical writing from the twelfth century onwards, many contradictory claims on illness and vitality circulated, all rooted in compelling interpretations of the early medical canons. Only sophisticated thinkers (not “Confucian” non-specialists) could re-orchestrate such an intricate textual heritage into books. Zhu Zhenheng taught this syncretic approach to his students, and his disciples deployed it in their

## PDF第159页

own writings. I will also show that  $ \underline{\text{zhezhong}} $ hermeneutics and textual composition rested on the notion that illness was constantly changing.

Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors.

Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

## PDF第160页

This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them proposed different interpretations of a common body of texts that included the Danxi corpus, but also the Inner Canon and the works of Liu Wansu and Li Gao. $ ^{11} $ Throughout, I try to recreate the doctrinal debates that make changes in their approaches to madness intelligible.

## PDF第161页

### 5.2 Madness in Lou Ying's 樓英 (1320-1400) $ ^{12} $ Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396)

The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

Lou Ying started to collect materials for his summa of current medical doctrine and healing methods in the 1360s and completed a first version in 1380; he achieved the final version in 1396. Lou did not have all the books he consulted at hand at any given time. He probably kept adding to his text as he consulted more and more works, something that was made more convenient by the proximity of Hangzhou (a major cultural center famous for its book-printing industry), Lou's living near the Grand Canal (a vital channel for the transmission of texts), and possibly his contacts at the early Ming court.

## PDF第162页

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $

Textual montages like Lou's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning were not amateurish endeavors. Composite texts professing to cover all aspects of illness and medicine were actually a new genre of medical writing at which Zhu Zhenheng's disciples excelled. $ ^{18} $ Many books claiming to be for beginners were sophisticated digests of complex medical teachings; they were not easy “primers,” but points of entry into the intricate traditions they summarized. Students of medicine found these compilations all the more useful since readers could not easily access all the texts these works cited.

Lou Ying's compilation was concerned with medical practice, not abstract doctrine. As a reasoned anthology of citations, the point of the Systematic Outline of Medical Learning was not to reconcile all medical approaches into a perfectly rational tableau, but to draw from the different strengths of past physicians in order to provide present healers with a flexible repertoire of methods that would allow them to face all eventualities. Lou mentioned six physicians whose texts excelled at curing illness:

## PDF第163页

Zhongjing [Zhang Ji] is detailed on external contamination that affects the interior and the surface or Yin and Yang; Danxi distinguishes himself on inner harm of Blood or Qi [that causes] depletion or repletion; Dongyuan [Li Gao] supports and protects central Qi; Hejian [Liu Wansu] removes the old to create the new; Mr. Qian [Yi] distinguishes clearly between the five Yin viscera; Dairen [Zhang Congzheng] is well versed in the three methods [vomiting, purging, and sweating]. Formularies from past ages are abundant: we can simply say that each has its strengths.

仲景詳外感於表裡陰陽 丹溪獨內傷於血氣虛實 東垣扶護中氣 河間推陳

致新 錢氏分明五臟 戴人熟施三法 凡歷代方書甚衆 皆各有所長耳

Since the same symptoms could occur for different reasons, it was plausible that many texts could contain useful methods of treatment. $ ^{19} $ “The benighted fail to appreciate this principle: their reckless trying [of recipes] on approximate illnesses has led many to an unjust and premature death” (昧者不悟是理 妄試疑似之病 每致夭橫者不少矣).

Armed with a definition of illness as a multifaceted and ever-changing entity that was typical of Zhu Zhenheng's followers, $ ^{20} $ Lou proposed an elegant solution to the controversial issue of whether “old recipes” could cure “current illnesses.” “Given that an illness with a single name [can require] different methods of treatment, when people use recipes from past ages to treat illness, their success or failure depends on whether [the illness at hand] corresponds to the strength [of the author of the book from which the recipe is drawn]” (故後用歷代之方

治病或效或不效者由病名同治法異或中其長或不中其長故也). Recipes

## PDF第164页

from the Treatise on Cold Injury (or any other source), for instance, were effective when they were used to treat the kinds of illness they cured best.

In typical zbezbong fashion, Lou also used this flexible conception of illness and therapy to justify a syncretic approach to the textual past and to clinical practice. His book counted forty fascicles or chapters. The first nine—all part of a large section called “Yin-Yang and the viscera” 陰陽臟腑部—explained general principles on pathomechanisms, diagnosis, and treatments. Followed twenty fascicles (10-29) describing various illnesses and methods to treat them, four fascicles on Cold Injury (30-3), two on the illnesses of women (34-5), four on those of children (36-9), and one with commentaries on the five periods and six Qi in the Inner Canon (40). $ ^{21} $ In the twenty chapters on disorders other than Cold Injury, Lou Ying classified illnesses into five sections (bu 部) named after the pairs of Yin and Yang viscera with which he associated them. There were six fascicles on the Liver and Gallbladder (肝膽), five on the Heart and Small Intestine (心小腸), five on the Spleen and Stomach (脾胃), two on the Lungs and Large Intestine (肺大腸), and two on the Kidneys and Bladder (腎膀胱). These sections did not present the anatomy of these viscera: they discussed illnesses that occurred when these organs failed to fulfil their functions.

The section called kuang dian 狂癲 appeared under Spleen and Stomach, somewhat arbitrarily since the entire section mentioned these viscera only once. Lou probably made such a choice because the Inner Canon and the Cold-Injury literature had traditionally discussed kuang as a disorder of the "Yang-Brilliance tract," which was associated with the Stomach. He first justified why he discussed kuang and dian separately:

## PDF第165页

Kuang means wild speech and wild running; dian means falling and lying down without waking. These are distinct disorders: although I now place kuang in the Spleen section and dian in the Liver section, the Canon contains words such as "kuang dian illness," "kuang triggering dian," and "dian illness makes kuang." These refer to suffering from kuang and dian concurrently. Among current illnesses, there are cases of wild speech and wild running quickly followed by falling down; there are cases of falling down followed by visions of ghosts and spirits that stop only after half a day. These are cases of suffering from dian and kuang concurrently.

狂謂妄言妄走 顱謂僵仆不省 各自一症 今以狂入脾部 顱入肝部 然經有言狂顱疾者 有言狂互引顱者 又言顱疾為狂者 此則又皆狂顱兼病 今病有妄言妄走 頃時前後僵仆之類 有僵仆後見鬼神半日方已之類 是以顱狂兼病者也

Having borrowed his short definitions of dian and kuang from the Canon of Problems, Lou cited the Inner Canon to show that they could occur at the same time to the same person. His description of “current illnesses” may have been based on patients he observed, but it was couched in formulaic phrases that would have made clear to his readers what illnesses he was discussing regardless of whether he had really observed such cases.

Like the rest of the treatise, sections on individual illnesses were created from fragments of other texts, a typical example of the intertextual composition I discussed above. After an introductory paragraph, Lou started each section by quotations from canonical texts, followed by selected citations from formularies and the works of medical authorities. He thus managed to present the several etiologies of each illness, as well as an array of useful therapeutic methods. A physician should determine which scenario was at work in a given patient and prescribe remedies accordingly. In no visible order, the section on kuang presented short descriptive passages from the classics, recipes from trusted works, and case

## PDF第166页

records from various sources. $ ^{22} $ Lou shortened and slightly modified some passages, but his own utterances were limited to his introduction (translated above), cross-references to other sections for remedies whose recipes were given elsewhere, and very brief (and themselves intertextual) “comments” (批) after some citations. $ ^{23} $

Lou’s section on kuang introduced a mix of approaches to madness, as well as recipes compiled at different periods that aimed to cure various imbalances. What justified this catholicity of approaches was not a theoretical reflection on madness in particular, but one on illness in general: any illness could be caused by a large array of imbalances and could therefore be treated in a variety of ways. Note nonetheless that Lou gave little room to kuang’s older etiologies—Wind was mentioned only in a “comment” on what Lou considered to be an anti-Wind remedy by Zhang Ji 張機. $ ^{24} $ The Wind etiology appeared discreetly in recipes that were once used to counter Wind strokes, but nothing in Lou’s work signaled that madness had once been considered as a Wind illness. $ ^{25} $

The cases of madness that Lou selected deployed mostly aggressive therapies. $ ^{26} $

Three of Zhang Congzheng's 張從正 four cases had used attacking methods. A case from the Shiyi dexiao fang 世醫得效方 (1337) used powdered melon pedicle (not to be confused with the multi-ingredient "Melon-pedicle powder") as a vomitive. $ ^{27} $ Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) used a purgative called the "Greater Qi-stimulating decoction" (大承氣湯) to

## PDF第167页

cure a female patient. $ ^{28} $ All these methods either became or remained widely used in late-

imperial medicine. Only one case deployed an approach that became marginal: Sun Zhao's

孫兆 (late eleventh century) cure of a monk with a recipe and method designed by Shen

Kuo 沈括 (1031-1095). $ ^{29} $

These mostly aggressive recipes and methods contrasted with the general therapeutic orientation of the Systematic Outline. Lou's "General discussion on methods of treatment" (Zhifa tonglun 治法通論, beginning of juan 3) cited 21 passages: eight from Wang Haogu, three from Li Gao, three from the Basic Questions, two each from Zhang Ji and Zhu Zhenheng, and one each from the Divine Pivot, the Majue, and the Canon of Problems. $ ^{30} $

Thirteen of the fifteen passages attributed to specific authors were from physicians whose priority, according to Lou, had been "to help and protect primordial Qi" (扶護元氣為主). $ ^{31} $

The contrast between Lou's general advice and the remedies he recommended for treating madness confirms the strength that the Fire and mucus etiology of madness had acquired by the fourteenth century. Madness was then largely seen as an illness caused by some kind of over-repletion. Be it Fire, mucus, Heat, or Wind, it had to be purged or attacked. $ ^{32} $

Lou Ying did not cite Zhu Zhenheng's ideas on madness, probably because he did not find any in the texts by Zhu that he possessed. When the Systematic Outline of Medical Learning was finally printed for the first time in 1565, much had changed in the medical

## PDF第168页

landscape since Lou Ying had compiled that book: the Danxi corpus had come to maturity and many of Danxi's books now contained lengthy sections on madness. The rest of this chapter traces the process by which this outcome became possible.

### 5.3 Dai Sigong 戴思恭, Fire, and the Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄

When Zhu Zhenheng died in 1358, he may still have been one of those physicians that local gazetteers recorded as famous in their region, but that posterity soon forgot. His teachings were transmitted during the early Ming through his own writings, through an unknown number of manuscripts made by his pupils (see note 79), and through the social activities of his disciples. It is difficult to trace the diffusion of specific precepts during this period, but we can trace the medical and editing activities of some of Zhu's illustrious disciples and the evolution of sections of madness in their relevant works. The previous section concerned Lou Ying, who studied with Zhu but whose most important treatise was not published until the sixteenth century. This section and the next one examine the lives of two disciples whose works were immediately influential on Ming medical thinking.

Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (Zì Yuanli 原禮,33 1324-1405), who like Zhu Zhenheng was from Wuzhou/Jinhua in Zhejiang, was arguably Zhu's most important pupil. Less than twenty sui when he followed his father Shiyao 士垚 (1307-1349) to Yiwu in 1343 to become Zhu's disciple, he received the master's closest attention.34 For fifteen years until Zhu's death, Dai

## PDF第169页

traveled many times a year to hear his teachings. $ ^{35} $ Dai's epitaph recounted that when Zhu would “tell him about the purport of the teachings of Zhou Dunyi and the Cheng brothers, [Dai] understood even the obscure meaning of the subtlest expressions” (告以濂洛授受之旨 微辭奧義 靡所不究). $ ^{36} $ Zhu then “selected medical books of the many masters for study. [Dai] had them all in his mind’s eye: as easily as he could point to black and white, he could distinguish what was right from wrong and what was accurate from what was off target” (取諸家醫書讀之 了然心目間 別是非得失 若指黑白).

Epitaphs contain the customary measure of clichéd praise, but Dai Sigong's command of both Daoxue precepts and medical doctrine must have contributed to his later success in elite circles. Zhu taught Dai to distinguish what was accurate from what was off target in the medical books of his predecessors. This selective appropriation of past teachings reflected Zhu's precept—a pillar of the zbezbong approach—that "when voices are in disarray, it is necessary to arbitrate between the many sages" (衆言浠亂 必折諸聖). $ ^{37} $

When he ventured into the Wu region in 1359, Dai Sigong carried with him some of Zhu Zhenheng's texts, including perhaps the incomplete draft of a work Zhu had not managed to finish before in died in 1358. Dai also bore notes that he took during the course of his training, hundreds of the master's case records, and many letters from Zhu, with whom he had entertained an abundant correspondence. $ ^{38} $ These letters probably contained

## PDF第170页

rich medical discussions. $ ^{39} $ Dai took disciples and, after the Ming dynasty had been established in 1368, regularly visited its new capital Nanjing.

An influential man from Jinhua (Zhu Zhenheng's and Dai Sigong's native place) enhanced Dai's reputation. When Dai traveled to Nanjing in 1374, he was received by Song Lian 宋濂 (1303-81), who knew Dai from their younger days in Pujiang county. Song, whom Ming founder Zhu Yuanzhang would later call "the foremost civil minister of the founding of the dynasty" (開國文臣之首; Mingshi 128: 3787-88), was then at the apex of his power. When Dai returned home that year after having declined recommendation to become a court physician, Song Lian composed a long piece called "Upon Sending Off Dai Yuanli on his Return to Puyang" (送戴原禮還浦陽序) that recorded some of Dai's successful therapies. In a text written around the same time, Song attributed to Dai's efforts the "fact" that "even children three-feet high knew of Zhu xiansheng's worthiness" (三尺之童亦知先生之賢). $ ^{40} $

Like Lou Ying, Dai Sigong repeatedly declined court employment under Zhu Yuanzhang (whom he had cured of some illness), but the Jianwen emperor, upon accessing the throne in 1398 after the death of Zhu Yuanzhang, hired Dai to the highest post in the empire's medical bureaucracy, that of Commissioner of the Imperial Academy of Medicine

## PDF第171页

(太醫院使, ranked 5a), in which quality Dai served until his retirement in 1403 at the age of 80 sui. $ ^{41} $ Dai was then replaced by Han Mao 韓茂, a Suzhou man Dai had recommended many years before. Dai returned home early in 1404. When Dai went back to the capital in the summer of 1405, the newly enthroned Yongle emperor (r. 1402-1424) sent Dai's former student Yuan Bao, who was now Administrative Assistant of the Imperial Academy of Medicine (太醫院判; the second highest post in the organization), to greet him. $ ^{42} $ Dai died in late 1405, soon after he had returned home again.

Earlier, Dai had collated a book that is now known as Probing the Mysteries of the Golden Casket (Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄). His main source may have been the manuscript of an incomplete work by Zhu Zhenheng. $ ^{43} $ Each section of Probing the Mysteries cited words by Zhu on individual disorders, sometimes followed by Dai's comments. Six appended “discussions” (論) are usually attributed to Dai. A sign that Probing the Mysteries was an edited version of an earlier text is that its section names were almost identical to those of a book published as Danxi michuan fangjue 丹溪秘傳方訣 in 1475 that did not contain Dai's notes and the six discussions. Passages from a miscellaneous section at the end of the Fangjue were distributed into the relevant sections of the Gouxuan, the sign of some editorial work.

Dai Sigong's medical views appeared in his six appended treatises, where he followed his master by arguing (among many other things) that Fire was a central cause of illness and that emotions supervened on Fire. In a text called "On whether there are really [so many fires as] Ruler [Fire], Ministerial [Fire], and [the Fire] of the Five emotions" (火豈君相五志俱有論), he credited Zhu for what was in fact Liu Wansu's 劉完素 association of Fire with

## PDF第172页

the five viscera. $ ^{44} $ Dai then cited the passage from pian 74 of the Basic Questions in which 5 of the 19 groups of illnesses mentioned (including kuang) were attributed to Fire, as well as Liu's development of this passage, which blamed even more disorders on the action of Fire. $ ^{45} $ Zhu had cited the same passage in his essay “On Ministerial Fire.”

The Gouxuan contained no general discussion of kuang, but many of its sections signaled the general importance of the mucus and Fire etiology in a large number of illnesses. $ ^{46} $ The section on “Summer Wind” (暑風), which Dai authored single-handedly, explained that sufferers lost consciousness because “mucus and rheum block and obstruct the openings of the Heart” (痰飲塞礙心之竅道), a sign that Zhu’s followers embraced the master’s idea that mucus affected consciousness by acting on the Heart. $ ^{47} $
