### 5.4 Another important channel in the diffusion of Zhu Zhenheng's approaches: Liu Chun's books

Like Dai Sigong's efforts, Liu Chun's 劉純 (1340-1412) works contributed to the

circulation of Zhu Zhenheng's ideas. This section contains more on the composition of

## PDF第173页

medical treatises and the transmission of medical knowledge than on madness per se, but Liu's life and texts provide important evidence on how Zhu's thoughts were interpreted and transmitted in the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries. Liu Chun was the scion of an illustrious family from Wuling 吳陵 (Jiangsu). In the 1340s and 1350s, he studied medicine with his father Shuyuan 叔淵, who had been Zhu's disciple. $ ^{48} $ At the same time or slightly later, he received further training from minor masters Feng Tinggan 馮庭干, Xu Zonglu 許宗魯, and Qiu Kerong 邱克容. The latter was said to have been from Yangzhou. $ ^{49} $

Liu's minor teachers played a role in transmitting "Danxi" doctrines. Feng Tinggan gave Liu a manuscript that Liu later expanded into the Yuji weiyi 玉機微義 (1396; more below), whereas Qiu Kerong taught Liu approaches similar to Zhu's, namely that "physicians of our days who exclusively follow Liu [Wansu] and Zhang [Congzheng] mainly treat with sweating, vomiting, and purging; they base themselves on Zhang Zihe's [Congzheng] Ten Configurations and Three Therapies" (今醫之專門於劉張者率以發汗吐下施治蓋本諸張

## PDF第174页

子和十形三療). $ ^{50} $ Qiu admired Zhang's attacking methods but (like Zhu Zhenheng) thought that physicians should also treat depleted patients with replenishing drugs. $ ^{51} $

Liu and his teachers were products of the Lower Yangzi region, but in the early Hongwu reign (1368-1398), Liu and his family left to establish themselves in Xi'an, the Shaanxi provincial capital. The sources are discrete on the cause of this relocation, and we know little about Liu's life between the time he moved to Shaanxi and his death in 1412, except that he followed the army to Liangzhou 凉州 (present-day Wuwei 武威 in Gansu) before finally settling further west in Ganzhou 甘州, where he was living in 1395. $ ^{52} $ His descendants still resided there in the late 1470s. $ ^{53} $

Like other disciples of Zhu's, Liu Chun re-orchestrated a complex textual heritage into a useful synthesis of medical teachings. His first work, Elementary Learning on the Medical Canons (Yijing xiao-xue 醫經小學; 1388), was one of the first to pack the essentials of medicine into one text. Aiming "to allow beginning students to seek the origin [of medical

## PDF第175页

learning] by tracing back its course, so that they not be lured into stray paths” (蓋欲初學者得以因流尋源而不蹈夫他岐之惑), $ ^{54} $ it was divided into six fascicles corresponding with six branches of medicine: pharmacology (本草), the pulses (脈訣), the tracts (經絡), illness mechanisms (病機), methods of treatments (治法), and the five periods and six Qi (運氣). $ ^{55} $

Julia Kristeva's claim that "any text is constructed as a mosaic of quotations" (1986 [1969]: 37) was here more literally true than she had intended in her critique of Western textuality. The pharmacology section of Elementary Learning was almost entirely based on Li Gao's writings. The first passage in the section on illness mechanisms was Liu Wansu's interpretation of pian 74 of the Basic Questions. The rest of that fascicle quoted from the Treatise on Cold Injury, Li Gao's Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach, Wang Haogu's Elementary Examples of Yin Symptoms, Zhang Congzheng's Scholar Serving his Kin, and Zhu Zhenheng's Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 and Danxi yulu 丹溪語錄 (see p. 165 below). The fascicle on healing methods was based on the Inner Canon (chiefly its cosmological chapters), but also on Zhang Congzheng, Li Gao, and Wang Haogu, whereas the yunqi chapter cited Zhang Congzheng's explanations and Liu Wenshu's 劉溫疏 Yunqi ao zhi 運氣奧旨.

This textual montage was no mere cut-and-paste. Liu Chun selected what he considered to be the best passages from a variety of writings that often disagreed with each other, and arranged them into a digest. To justify this doctrinal blending, Liu Chun cited Zhu Zhenheng's approach to diagnosis and therapy:

## PDF第176页

The Inner Canon exhausts the subtlety of Yin and Yang and infinite transformations: all books emerge from this. The Eighty One Problems [the Canon of Problems] developed by Yueren [a.k.a. Bian Que 扁鵲] only grasped one or two tenths of the Inner Canon. [Zhang Ji] Zhongjing selected one section on Cold Injury, Hejian [Liu Wansu] modified Zhongjing's methods with his Heat doctrine, whereas Dongyuan [Li Gao] based his doctrines on food-and-drink and exhaustion. I am afraid that if one gives primacy to Zhongjing's books and concentrates mainly on Cold Injury, one will mistake inner harm for Cold Injury. If one gives primacy to Dongyuan's books and concentrates on Stomach Qi, I am afraid one will mistake outer contamination for inner harm. Putting Hejian's books first and concentrating on Heat will, I am afraid, lead one to mistake Cold for Heat. Instead, one should first and foremost base oneself on the Inner Canon. this naturally makes one alert and flexible. $ ^{56} $

內經盡陰陽之妙 變化無窮 諸書皆出於此 如越人演八十一難 止得內經中一二 仲景取其傷寒一節 河間以熱論變仲景之法 東垣以飲食勞役立論恐先仲景書者 以傷寒為主 恐誤內傷作外感 先東垣書者 以胃氣為主 恐誤外感為內傷 先河間書者 以熱為主 恐誤以寒為熱 不若先主於內經則自然活潑潑地

In Liu’s view (which corresponded to Luo Zhiti’s, Zhu Zhenheng’s, Dai Sigong’s, and Lou Ying’s), the teachings of the previous masters had to be merged because no single master could exhaust the teachings of the Inner Canon, and because changing illnesses could not be cured by rigid adherence to any method, however excellent. Liu Chun did not see Zhu Zhenheng’s predecessors as the representatives of distinct lineages or “schools,” but as

## PDF第177页

specialists who excelled at treating certain types of illness. Disagreements between them could thus be interpreted as no more than differences in emphasis. In a spirit typical of the zbezbong approach I described in the introduction, Liu Chun aimed to find where the teachings of the old masters intersected, the final arbiter of validity being the Inner Canon. $ ^{57} $

This form of synthesis was not a natural tendency of Chinese thought. The deliberate reorganization of a textual legacy into a structured mosaic was a novel way of composing medical texts that rested on a view of illness promoted particularly by Zhu Zhenheng and his disciples. The treatises that resulted slowly displaced formularies. This new way of making texts became desirable because of the increasingly doctrinal nature of medical reflections from the twelfth century onwards, and because many available books articulated positions that seemed sensible in and of themselves, but contradicted one other. As the example of Lou Ying showed (5.2), writing a medical treatise in the late fourteenth century required delving into this intricate textual tradition.

The two mentions of madness in Liu Chun's Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學 were brief. The first was: “In dian and kuang, Yang burns intensely” (癲狂陽熾). As Liu explained, “when Cardiac Heat reaches an extreme, one ails from dian; when Hepatic Heat reaches an extreme, one ails from kuang” (心熱極則病癲 肝熱極則病狂). $ ^{58} $ This annotation paraphrased Liu Wansu's Yuanbing shi 原病式 (before 1186; 4.1), which had argued that both dian and kuang stemmed from excessive Heat in the viscera. The second passage appeared in juan 4 in a gloss to the Cold-Injury symptoms of “yellowness of skin and fits of madness” (發黃發狂): “Hepatic Heat is kuang, Cardiac Heat is dian” (肝熱者狂 心熱者癲; Liu YQ: 47A). Both

## PDF第178页

passages presented as self-evident the closeness of dian and kuang as two forms of madness, an interpretation that Liu Wansu had himself promoted in the twelfth century.

In 1396, Liu Chun published his most famous work, the Subtle Meanings of the Secrets Carved in Jade (Yuji weiyi 玉機微義), in 50 sections that spanned 50 juan. $ ^{59} $ Seventeen sections originated in a manuscript by Xu Yanchun 徐彥純 (d. 1384) that Liu had received from a teacher. $ ^{60} $ Liu added thirty-three sections to Xu's original seventeen, and wrote commentaries inside Xu's text. $ ^{61} $ He also chose the book's new title. It appears that Xu Yanchun had also been following Zhu Zhenheng's approach of selecting from each master what was valuable and rejecting the rest, taking the Inner Canon as the standard to decide what was to be preserved. Indeed, as Liu Chun's preface expounded: "selecting from the collected writings of Liu Shouzhen [Wansu] of the Jin and Li Mingzhi [Gao] and Zhu Yanxiu [Zhenheng] of the Yuan, [Xu] zhezhong'd their essentials on the basis of the main precepts of the [Inner] Canon" (摭金劉守真 元李明之 朱彥修朱氏論集 本乎經旨而折衷其要). $ ^{62} $

Xu Yanchun's mix of collation and explanation was exemplified by his section on "Wind strokes," which also shows how much the Wind etiology had changed since the

## PDF第179页

golden age of Wind medicine in the seventh and eighth centuries. After showing that Liu Wansu, Li Gao, and Zhu Zhenheng interpretations of Wind did not correspond with those found in the Inner Canon and other early texts, Xu, citing Wang Lü 王履 (ca. 1332-1391), said that the three masters had not spoken of real Winds that attacked people, but of illnesses that “looked like Wind strokes” (類中風) but were in fact triggered by inner causes. $ ^{63} $

Liu Chun's Subtle Meanings contained no section on madness, but the book gave no grounds for attributing kuang to anything else than mucus and Fire. $ ^{64} $ Juan 10 (devoted to Fire) started with Liu Wansu's (4.1) attribution of most illnesses—including kuang—to Fire. The second section was Zhu Zhenheng's "On Ministerial Fire" (4.4); the third cited Dai Sigong's appendices to Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄. $ ^{65} $ The mucus chapter contained a cross-reference to the section on "depletion and fatigue" 虚損 (juan 19) for a discussion of Zhu's idea that depletion and mucus could cause possession symptoms. The text recommended emetics for most disorders attributed to mucus, and purgatives for those caused by Fire. $ ^{66} $ Subtle Meanings of the Secret Carved in Jade was thus squarely part of the intertextual and syncretic Danxi approach to illness.

## PDF第180页

Liu Chun's writings were not immediately prominent, but official sponsorship soon allowed them to be printed and circulated. $ ^{67} $ They started to travel more widely after Chen Yi 陳鎰 (zì Youjie 有戒; jìnshi 1412) came to Shaanxi in 1435 as one of the newly established Grand Coordinators (xunfu 巡撫 or zhenfu 鎮撫) that the court was sending out to supervise provincial governments. $ ^{68} $ Having received copies of Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學 and Yuji weiyi 玉機微義 from Liu's descendants, Chen Yi decided to bestow the funds necessary to print them. He entrusted the project to Hao Hong 郝桁 and Wang Min 王敏 (these were their zì) of the office of the provincial treasurer. $ ^{69} $ Looking for a man of repute to write a preface, Hao Hong found no less a personage than Grand Secretary Yang Shiqi 楊士奇 (1365-1444), who was the most powerful civil official in the realm at the time. $ ^{70} $ Prefacing a book whose

## PDF第181页

printing Chen Yi subsidized may have been a way for Yang to encourage benevolent initiatives by the officials he sponsored. $ ^{71} $ As Yang’s preface stated: “without Mr. Liu [Chun], this book would not have been; without the Honorable Chen [Yi], it would not have been transmitted” (此書非劉氏莫之為　非陳公亦莫之傳).

One year after Chen Yi's edition of the Yuji weiyi appeared, the two Shaanxi treasurers paid for a new edition, claiming that the errors found in the first imprint "failed Censor [Chen Yi's] excellent intentions and were not enough to match Junior Preceptor xiansheng [Yang Shiqi's] benevolence for the people and love for things" 有辜負都憲之雅意亦不足以副少師先生恭體仁民愛物之心矣. $ ^{72} $ They reedited the book based on the original manuscript (原稿), which was probably in the Commission's possession already.

As the Commissioners exclaimed, Yang's preface "is truly a great honor and fortune for this book, which will undoubtedly become famous in this age and will be transmitted into the future" (真為此書之榮幸 名今傳後無疑也). $ ^{73} $ This is indeed what happened. Another edition of the Yuji weiji came out around 1449, and one more in 1451. The preface to a 1506 Fujian imprint of the same book said that the Shaanxi edition (1439 or 1440?) "was disseminated to the four directions" (傳之四方), though "only influential people in large

## PDF第182页

towns connected with the capital and places that are on the road to official posts could obtain it" (非仕路及通都大邑有力者不得; LiuChYQ: 76). Officials passed copies to their colleagues, who took them to their native place or to where they served.

A 1455 preface provides a rare opportunity to understand how Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學 circulated soon after it was re-edited. Wu Changyan 吳昌衍, who had served as Sichuan Administration Commissioner of the Left, received a copy of it from his Shaanxi homologue Xu Zi 許資. $ ^{74} $ Wu reported that after he retired to his native place of Linchuan 臨川 (Jiangxi), he met a group of prefectural officials (including the prefect himself) who were studying medicine with great diligence. $ ^{75} $ Wu gave them Liu's book, which they examined and eventually decided to reprint. $ ^{76} $ This is probably how most medical books circulated: first through large centers on important thoroughfares, and from there down to more regional centers, sometimes through new editions, but mostly from interested individual to interested individual. Unlike in later times, when private printers dominated the market for medical books, in the early Ming it was often officials who provided the funds, edited the texts for publication, and found renowned men to exalt the works in prefaces. $ ^{77} $ The way readers used Liu Chun's books can only be surmised, but judging from their contents, Liu's widely circulated works probably contributed to the rising dominance of the Fire/mucus approach to madness and to the broadening use of attacking therapies to treat fits of mania.

## PDF第183页

### 5.5 Knowledge in the making: the Danxi corpus and its sections on madness

When Liu Chun was working on his books, the only books by Zhu Zhenheng that had been published were Extrapolations on the Recipes of the Service (Jufang fabui 局方發揮; Zhu's critique of the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service, a formulary that was still influential in the fourteenth century) and Words Bequeathed by the Ancients on the Perfection of Knowledge Through the Investigation of Things (Gezhi yulun 格致餘論) a doctrinal work. Both had appeared in 1347. Another book was published at an unknown date (but after the death of Zhu in 1358): Probing the Mysteries of the Golden Casket (Jingui gouxuan 金匱要略), which had been edited by Zhu's disciple Dai Sigong and presented to a wider public the recipes that Zhu had purportedly recommended for the treatment of various illnesses.

Besides these printed books, a number of manuscripts were also circulating among disciples of Zhu's: Medical Notes by Danxi (Danxi yian 丹溪醫按, supposedly owned by Dai Sigong until they changed hands in the 1360s), Danxi's Bequest (Danxi yibo 丹溪衣鉢; lit. "Danxi's robe and alms bowl," in which the monastic robe and begging bowl represented transmission from a teacher) $ ^{78} $ and even the Recorded Sayings of Danxi (Danxi yulu 丹溪語錄). $ ^{79} $ Zhao Liangren 趙良仁 (b. 1315), who had studied with Zhu Zhenheng from 1343 to 1346 at the same time as Dai Sigong, postulated that the Recorded Sayings of Danxi "must have been the crude recordings of some disciples" (必門人粗所記錄), since instead of explaining the origin of every illness under consideration (as Zhu presumably would have), the book simply

## PDF第184页

appended one or two recipes under each illness. $ ^{80} $ Zhao had first seen the Recorded Sayings in the Suzhou area some years after Zhu's death in 1358, and again ten years later at many friends' places (遍見於朋友處), under a new title: Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao 丹溪藥要). Lamenting that this book was of such low quality, yet was circulating widely by virtue of its attribution (欲駕其名以傳諸徒 豈先生之意哉), Zhao decided to re-edit it in a question-and-answer format and to enhance it with his own comments. The result was Questions on the Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao buowen 或問), which, to judge from the date of its preface, was completed in 1384. $ ^{81} $

Other manuscripts attributed to Zhu Zhenheng also became books. In a text written in 1484, Lu He 盧和 claimed that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples had augmented Danxi's Bequest to publish texts such as Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄, [Pingzhì] huizui [平治] 薈蕞 (date unknown), and even the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (1482), which may have been the first printed work to have carried "Danxi" in its title. $ ^{82} $

The Danxi xinfa, which came out in three distinct editions from Shaanxi, Sichuan, and Anhui between 1450 and 1482, became the cornerstone of the group of texts I call the “Danxi corpus.” $ ^{83} $ Before its publication, “Danxi” teachings had been circulating among Zhu Zhenheng’s disciples, and among minor physicians like those who had taught Liu Chun or

## PDF第185页

those who had studied under Dai Sigong in Nanjing. The Danxi xinfu made Danxi medicine reach a considerable number of people across a larger territory. Medical approaches to a large number of disorders, as well as many debates that Ming physicians considered important, were rooted in the content of the Xinfu and its derivative works. Danxi xinfu also contained the first section on madness in all the books attributed to Zhu Zhenheng. Here I try to describe the practice of making texts, not only as material objects, but as meaningful objects, a process that Adrian Johns (1998) called “knowledge in the making.” Since my goal is to understand how influential statements on madness were fashioned, I concentrate on the texts themselves rather than on the interpretive and therapeutic practices of its readers.

### 1. The Shaanxi Danxi xinfa (1450s)

Sometime in the 1450s, a little more than 10 years after Shaanxi provincial officials had re-edited Liu Chun's works, a man from Shaanxi named Yang Xun 楊珣 completed a work called Danxi's Methods of Mental Cultivation (Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法) and had it printed in Xi'an. $ ^{84} $ We know that Yang Xun once worked at the Imperial Academy of Medicine in Beijing (which officially became the Ming capital in 1421). Medical historian Liu Shijue (in Liu, Li, and Yang 2004: 87-88) has postulated that Yang had learned about Danxi from people there who were familiar with Zhu Zhenheng's teachings, probably the likes of Sheng

## PDF第186页

Yin 盛寅 (who was Commissioner of the Imperial Academy of Medicine from 1436 to his death in 1441) and Jiang Zhushan 蔣主善 (who had entered the Academy in 1424 when his father Yongwen died, and eventually became Commissioner).

Like Zhu's Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 and Liu Chun's Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學, Yang Xun's Danxi xinfa referred to an important Daoxue concept in its title, but there was little trace of "mental cultivation" in the work itself, which was a digest of Zhu's precepts on various maladies. The book was divided into four seasons, each corresponding to one fascicle. "Spring" contained Zhu Zhenheng's Bencao yanyi buyi 本草衍義補遺 [Addenda to (Kou Zongshi's) Extrapolations on Materia Medica (1111)], $ ^{85} $ one section called "The symptoms that appear in the 12 tracts" (十二經見證), and five short essays: "Do not treat illnesses after they have appeared, but before they occur" (不治已病治未病),  $ ^{86} $ "When [one of the Five Agents] is in excess, it creates harm; its successor will restrain it" (亢則害承乃制),  $ ^{87} $ "When examining the mechanism of illness, never lose track of the harmony of Qi [in the viscera]" (審察病機無失氣宜),  $ ^{88} $ "If you can merge the complexion and the pulse [with the Five Agents, the four seasons, etc.], you will succeed in all cases" (能合色脈可以萬全),  $ ^{89} $

## PDF第187页

and “When treating an illness one must seek its root” (治病必求其本). $ ^{90} $ The first and fifth

essays had appeared in Gezhi yulun. The other three essays may have been based on Zhu's

teachings on the Basic Questions, which were themselves rooted in Liu Wansu's interpretations.

The five essays included in the first fascicle of Yang Xun's Danxi xinja are excellent examples of the way teaching lineages constructed coherent doctrines by drawing selectively from the vast and heterogeneous Inner Canon. Three of the passages chosen came from Wang Bing's "cosmological chapters." They explained the cosmic regularities behind illness mechanisms and described diagnostic methods that inscribed illnesses in the broader processes by which they were thought to unfold.

The remaining three “seasons” (fascicles 2 to 4) contained 106 entries on various illnesses, including the first section devoted to madness in the entire Danxi corpus. But how was one to compose such a section when Zhu had not left any focused discussion of kuang? Without claiming to represent Zhu’s point of view, Lou Ying (5.2) had quoted passages from various sources. Yang’s solution was to glean passages on diankuang from works he thought Zhu would have endorsed and to present them as if they came from “Danxi” himself. $ ^{91} $

The collage started with familiar lines from the Canon of Problems and Wang Bing's commentary to pian 40 of the Basic Questions: "Dian is Yin, kuang is Yang; dian is excessive joy, kuang excessive anger" (顛屬陰 狂屬陽 顛多喜 狂多怒). $ ^{92} $ An unwary reader may have thought these lines came from Zhu himself. $ ^{93} $ The section continued:

When the pulse is void, it is curable; when it is replete, [the patient] will die. [Dian and kuang] are mostly occasioned by mucus congealing in the chest. As a treatment,

## PDF第188页

one should pacify the Cardiac Spirit and open mucus knots. Strike by evil can also lead to the formation of this disorder: treat it with methods for treating evil.

脈虛者可治 實則死 大率多因痰結於心胸間 治當鎮心神 開痰結 亦有中邪而成此疾者 以治邪法治之

This passage was quite a blend. The sentence on pulses was from Basic Questions 28, where it applied only to epileptiform "dian illness." Attribution to mucus may have stemmed from the mucus section of Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄 (which claimed that "mucus in the chest" caused madness) or from discussions by Zhang Congzheng (4.2). The ghostly etiology (unattested in Zhu's own writings) was borrowed from Sun Simiao's Recipes Worth a Thousand in Gold.

The next passage is an excellent example of the textual roots of some medical interpretations of madness: “The Yuanbing shi’s discussion is particularly outstanding: the common saying that ‘the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang’ is right. In the main, [it is] Heat.” $ ^{94} $ (原病式所論尤精 蓋為世所以‘重陰者癲 重陽者狂’是也大概熱 ) But we saw that Liu Wansu’s Yuanbing shi actually disagreed with the Canon of Problems’ characterization of dian and kuang in terms of Yin and Yang. Here Yang Xun (or the notes he had relied on) garbled Liu Wansu’s reasoning, implying that Liu agreed, hence the strangeness of the phrase “in the main it is Heat” at the end of the reasoning. $ ^{95} $

The section on diankuang continued as follows:

Dian is when the Spirit does not hold to its lodging, when one speaks madly and seems to be seeing things. [Dian] fails to heal for many years and the Heart tract is damaged: this is a real illness. If [it is caused by] accumulation of Heat in the Cardiac

## PDF第189页

tract, one should clear the Heart to remove Heat; if it is mucus confusing the openings of the Heart, one should purge down mucus and appease the Will.

顧者 神不守舍 狂言如有所見 經年不愈 心經有損 是為真病 如心經

畜熱 當清心除熱 如痰迷心竅 當下痰寧志

This passage proposed a definition of dian without explaining how it differed from kuang.

The symptoms of dian were in fact borrowed from those of kuang in classical sources (e.g.,

Divine Pivot 22). The etiologies proposed were Fire (or Heat) and mucus, and the treatments

were purgation and anti-mucus therapies, all in line with the etiologies that Zhu Zhenheng

had mentioned in his own writings. The rest of the section sounds less characteristic of what

we know of Zhu's approaches, perhaps because it mentioned demonic etiologies and

advocated medico-exorcistic treatments:

If one cries in a dian fashion and wails, it is that one has been possessed by evil. This is not kuang: burn silkworm paper and ingest [the ashes] in one spoonful of fermented grain brew. $ ^{96} $ When one suddenly utters crazy words and speaks demonic language, insert a needle under the nail of the thumb [big toe?], and [the mad speech] will stop. $ ^{97} $ When in cases of Wind dian the side of the ribcage pulls and aches, and when in crises the ears ring, let Asparagus (core removed) dry in the sun and then pulverize it; ingest with one spoonful of fermented grain brew. When dian illness is treated in the spring, it will naturally quiet down as the summer starts. One should use drugs that help Cardiac Qi. When Yang is depleted and Yin over-replete, it is dian; when Yin is depleted and Yang over-replete, it is kuang. Kuang illness should be removed with massive vomiting and purging. Recipe: to treat kuang evil that erupts at

## PDF第190页

any time, when one is disheveled and screams loudly, when one wants to kill others and avoids neither water nor fire: take any amount of shrubby sophora; pulverize and, with honey, shape into pellets the size of Aleurites seeds. $ ^{98} $ Take 15 pellets each time, ingesting them with mint decoction.

若顧哭呻吟 為邪所憑 非狂也 燒蠶紙酒水下方寸匕 卒狂言鬼語 針大拇指甲下即止 風癲引脇痛 發則耳鳴 用天門冬去心 曬乾作末 酒服方寸匕 癲病春治之 入夏自安 宜助心氣之藥 陽虛陰實則癲 陰虛陽實則狂 狂病宜大吐下則除之 又方 治狂邪發無時 披頭大叫 欲殺人 不避火水 苦參不以多少 右為末 蜜丸如桐子大 每服十五丸 煎薄荷湯下之

<div style="text-align: center;">This montage contained few symptomatic descriptions of dian and kuang, and only one remedy for kuang. When symptoms were named (demonic words, wailing), they were used to specify etiologies. The claim that dian should be treated by helping Cardiac Qi and kuang by massive vomiting and purging anticipated a classification that would become prevalent in the sixteenth century (6.5 and 6.6). $ ^{99} $ As I will show below, many later readers treated this loosely integrated section as a coherent whole from which they could invoke Danxi's authority.</div>


### 2. The Huizhou edition of the Danxi xinfa (1482)

Yang Xun's Danxi digest was successful. Soon after 1465, in the early Chenghua reign, Wang Jihuan 王季瓛 republished it in Sichuan with additional recipes (西屬). $ ^{100} $ Yang's and Wang's editions were succeeded by the received version of the Danxi xinfa, which Cheng Chong 程充 published in 1482 in Huizhou (Anhui). In his preface, Cheng praised Yang's and Wang's benevolence, but he found their books ridden with errors and

## PDF第191页

somewhat confusing because they mixed recipes by Zhu Zhenheng with recipes from other sources. $ ^{101} $ Yang’s work supposedly contained redundancies and omissions, and its compiler had appended extraneous discussions to Zhu’s words, making “jade indistinguishable from stone” (楊之集 篇目或有重出 或亦有遺 附以他論 使玉石不分).

Cheng Chong explained in a different tone how he had collected recipes from the Pingzhi buicui 平治會萃 (also attributed to Zhu Zhenheng and close in content to the Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄; see note 81 above) and proofread the previous editions of the Danxi xinfa in light of Liu Chun's "Subtle Meanings of the Secrets Carved in Jade [1396], [Luo Tianyi's] Precious Mirror on Safeguarding Life [1281], the Florilegium on Saving Life [1315], $ ^{102} $ and various books by Dongyuan [Li Gao] and Hejian [Liu Wansu]"(竊取平治會萃經驗等方及玉機微義衛生寶鑒濟生拔萃東垣河間諸書校之). $ ^{103} $ Cheng thus clearly recognized that Zhu's approaches were rooted in the teachings of previous masters. The Subtle Meanings had been written decades after Zhu's death, but Cheng apparently granted its compilers some authority in interpreting Zhu's teachings. $ ^{104} $ Setting his mind on making a new version of Danxi's work, Cheng started "seeking recipes according to illnesses" (因證求方) to complement each section of the work. He claimed that he even managed, through an official contact in Zhu's native county, to convince Zhu's great-grand-son Zhu Xian 朱賢 to mail (寄) him some of the master's unpublished manuscripts.

## PDF第192页

Cheng’s version of the Danxi xinfa differed from its predecessors in many respects. First, it dispensed with Zhu’s Addenda to Bencao yanyi, which had opened the first fascicle of Yang Xun’s edition. Cheng also reduced the number of entries on illnesses from 106 to 100, though he ended up adding sections because he merged many of the previous entries together. As an example of his rearrangements, Cheng tinkered with the section on Wind strokes, clarifying which points of doctrine were from Zhu and which came from others (the “appended records” 附錄 where these other views were confined teemed with hidden citations from Liu Wansu’s works), and presented as “appended recipes” those that were not from Zhu himself. To the new work, Cheng appended Zhu’s epitaph by Song Lian 宋濂 (1310-1381) and Zhu’s biography by Dai Liang 戴良 (1317-1383). $ ^{105} $ Both explained how Zhu had learned medicine and included a few of his cases. In one of these cases (see Dai’s “Biography”), Zhu attributed to mucus the possession symptoms of a young man. The biography also included Zhu’s treatises “On Ministerial Fire” (相火論) and “Yang is often in excess and Yin in deficit” (陽有餘陰不足) (4.4).

Despite his critique of the earlier versions, Cheng Chong retained many of Yang Xun's editorial decisions. He preserved, for instance, the section on “The symptoms that appear in the 12 tracts” and the five short essays titled after the Basic Questions, and he did not modify Yang's “Diankuang” section, either because he found no documents to improve upon it or because he thought madness was not a common enough disorder to modify the original. $ ^{106} $ Cheng thus kept intact the counter-sensical interpretations of Liu Wansu, and the general advice to treat kuang with purgatives or vomitives. All that Cheng added was one

## PDF第193页

recipe: water in which hemp seeds (麻仁) had been boiled, a remedy that Prescriptions Worth a Thousand in Gold (652/59) had claimed could treat dian feng 顛風. $ ^{107} $ Like Jingui gouxuan and the Shaanxi Danxi xinfa, Cheng's work mentioned madness in passing in the sections on "mucus" and "Fire," advocating the use of vomitives and purgatives to cure it. $ ^{108} $ Since the Danxi xinfa was read by a large number of people, its "Diankuang" section must have further solidified the Fire and mucus etiology of madness.

### 3. The Danxi xiansheng yishu zuanyao 丹溪先生醫書纂要 (1484)

The Huizhou Danxi xinfa must have soon circulated to neighboring Zhejiang, because only two years after its publication another work attributed to Danxi appeared in Zhu's native place of Yiwu 義烏 (in Jinhua, Zhejiang): Lu He's 盧和 (1440-1515) Collected Essentials from Danxi xiansheng's Medical Books (Danxi xiansheng yishu zuanyao, a.k.a. Danxi zuanyao and Danxi yiyao 醫要). Lu criticized the Danxi xinfa for (what he saw as) its numerous omissions and for including much that he found far-fetched or absurd. On the basis of manuscripts by Zhu Zhenheng that Lu He's uncle (叔父) Lu Anze 安澤 had obtained, Lu He edited the pre-existing text more carefully, erasing the strained interpretations and absurd theories that he had claimed to have found in the Huizhou edition. He also augmented the book with selected material from the Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 and “discussions of recipes that disciples had recorded” (門人所錄方論).

## PDF第194页

Lu He's Danxi zuanyao (in 8 juan) has been ignored by modern readers because it has not been republished since the fifteenth century. $ ^{110} $ For the medical history of madness, this is a serious omission, because the Zuanyao proposed significant reinterpretations that were widely cited for centuries after its publication. $ ^{111} $ Lu He first corrected the Danxi xinfà's awkward explanation of Liu Wansu's position on dian and kuang by specifying that "the doubling of Yin and the doubling of Yang were words from the Canon of Problems" (重陰重陽 難經之言也). He rewrote the Xinfà's praise of Liu's Yuanbing shi accordingly: "On diankuang, the Yuanbing shi's discussion is particularly outstanding: current people are mistaken to take the doubling of Yin to be dian and the doubling of Yang to be kuang; in the main, both are [caused] simply by Heat" (癲狂原病式所論甚精 蓋以世以重陰為癲重陽為狂 誤也 大概皆是熱耳). The remaining of the section started by citing the Danxi xinfà, but it was shorter and better integrated. It also gave most emphasis to the Fire and mucus etiologies.

[Dian and kuang] are mostly occasioned by mucus congealing in the chest. One should free mucus and pacify the Cardiac Spirit. $ ^{112} $ There are also [cases] of strike by evil: $ ^{113} $ treat them with methods for treating evil. The Spirit does not hold to its residence; one speaks madly and acts wildly, for years without healing. $ ^{114} $ If there is accumulated Heat in the Cardiac tract, one should clear the Heart to remove Heat; if

## PDF第195页

mucus is confusing the openings of the Heart, one should remove mucus to calm the Heart. $ ^{115} $ One should use massive vomiting and purgation, and [one] will get better. $ ^{116} $

大率多因痰結於心胸間 宜開痰鎮心神 亦有中邪者 以治邪法治之神不守舍 狂言妄作 經年不愈 如心經蓄熱 當清心除熱 如痰迷心竅 當去痰安心 宜大吐下 愈

Lu He discarded passages on wailing being caused by possession and treated with burned amulets, on Qin Chengzu's exorcistic method, on pain to the sides, on dian's improving in the summer if it was treated in the spring, and on dian and kuang defined as deficiency or over-repletion of Yin and Yang, none of which had appeared in Zhu Zhenheng's books.

To the “Diankuang” section of the Danxi zuanyao, Lu He added a section on “therapy by counter-affect,” an aspect of medicine that had not been emphasized since Zhang Congzheng had written about it in the thirteenth century (4.2). The first sentence seemed to take the mucus-and-Fire etiology of madness for granted, even when madness occurred because of excessive emotions: “The Fires of the five wills arise because of the seven emotions; when in stasis, they create mucus, which causes the symptoms of dian, xian, and kuang” (五志之火 因七情而起 鬱而成痰 故為癲癇狂妄之證). Lu He did not explain how to distinguish between dian, kuang, and xian.

The rest of the passage explained how emotions could be brought under control by means of other emotions:

One should curb [these emotions] by means of human affairs: these are not things that drugs can treat. It is necessary to diagnose and observe their origin in order to balance them. Anger harms the Liver, causing kuang and xian: conquer it with anxiety. Joy harms the Heart, causing dian and xian: conquer it with fear or dispel it with

## PDF第196页

anger. Anxiety injures the Lungs, causing xian and dian: conquer it with joy or dispel it with anger. Pensiveness harms the Spleen, causing xian, dian, and kuang: conquer it with anger or dispel it with joy. Fear harms the Kidneys, causing dian and xian: conquer it with pensiveness or dispel it with anxiety. Fright harms the Gallbladder, causing dian: conquer it with anxiety or dispel it with fear. Dejection harms the Heart's Envelope, causing dian: conquer it with anxiety or dispel it with anger. Only the worthies are capable of this method. $ ^{117} $

宜以人事制之 非藥所能療也 須診察其由以平之 怒傷於肝者 為狂為癇以憂勝之 喜傷於心者 為癲為癇 以恐勝之 以怒解之 憂傷於肺者 為癇為癲 以喜勝之 以怒解之 思傷於脾者 為癇為癲為狂 以怒勝之 以喜解之 恐傷於腎者 為癲為癇 以思勝之 以憂解之 驚傷於膽者 為癇以憂勝之 以恐解之 悲傷於心包者 為癇以憂勝之 以怒解之 此法惟賢者能之耳

<div style="text-align: center;">The Zuanyao's section on mucus also included a case of "mucus possession" that had first appeared in the Gezhi yulun and had been refurbished by Dai Liang in the biography of Zhu Zhenheng that Cheng Chong appended to the Huizhou Danxi xinfa. All these discussions of Fire, mucus, and the emotions could only strengthen the precepts that Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng, and Zhu Zhenheng had defended in the twelfth to fourteenth centuries.</div>


### 4. The Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附餘 (Huizhou, 1536)

Many developments took place in medical thinking between 1484 (the date of the Danxi zuanyao) and 1536, the year in which the Attachments to the Danxi xinfu (24 juan) was completed by a Huizhou man named Fang Guang 方廣. I shall present these development

## PDF第197页

in the rest of this chapter and in the first sections of Chapter 6, but let me finish explaining the growth of the Danxi corpus here in order to call attention to the textual continuity of its sections on madness. Partly because of the popularity of the Danxi xinfa, by the sixteenth century Zhu Zhenheng had become so recognized a medical authority that, sometime in the Jiajing reign (1522-1566), he and Zhang Yuansu, Liu Wansu, and Li Gao had been enshrined in the temple of medical sages near the Imperial Academy of Medicine in Beijing. $ ^{118} $ As we shall see below and in the next chapter, most physicians from Fang's time still vowed respect to what they saw as Zhu Zhenheng's medical acumen, and many kept invoking the name "Danxi" to support their own arguments in doctrinal disputes.

Fang had started studying medicine when his mother had died at the hands of

ignorant physicians, whose mistaken prescription in a case of redness on the skin had

worsened her symptoms. Fang, who was known as a “scholarly physician” (Ruyi 儒醫),

often sojourned in Luoyang 洛陽 and in Chenliu 陳留 (in modern-day Henan), where he

had developed (or would develop) a good medical reputation. $ ^{119} $ In addition to Danxi xinfa

fuyu, he also compiled a work on Cold Injury and a book called Maiyao zhengzhi 脈藥證治,

neither of which appears to have survived.

Fang Guang considered Zhu Danxi superior to the other masters (which, following Wang Lun 王綸 [5.6], he named as Zhang Ji, Li Gao, and Liu Wansu) because Zhu had understood the precepts of all of these earlier masters. To Fang, only Danxi had “obtained the entirety of the medical Way” (得醫道之全者 丹溪一人), and the Danxi xinfu was the

## PDF第198页

book that expounded on the subtleties of Zhu's doctrines (發丹溪之蘊者 心法一書). $ ^{120} $

But Fang considered Danxi xinfu as deficient on recipes and pharmacological knowledge. As a palliative, he decided to add relevant works to the Xinfu. For this purpose, he augmented and annotated Zhu's Bencao yanyi buyi 本草衍義補遺, which had been appended to the Shaanxi Danxi xinfu (1450s) but had not been preserved in the Huizhou edition (1482) of that book. Since Fang considered Liu Chun as a faithful transmitter of Danxi doctrines, he included Liu's Yuji weiyi 玉機微義 (1396; discussed above) into his new compilation. To every entry in the original Xinfu, Fang also added relevant comments from Wang Lun's Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (Mingyi zazhu 明醫雜著; 5.6). At least ten different editions of Danxi xinfu fuyu were made in the hundred years that followed its initial publication in 1536. $ ^{121} $ Fang's book was one of the channels through which Danxi approaches remained influential into the seventeenth century even as other physicians were promoting new therapies and views of human vitality.

### 5. The Danxi zhifa xinyao 丹溪治法心要 (1543)

This book was edited by Gao Shuzong 高叔宗 from Shaoxing. In a preface written in 1543, Shuzong's uncle Gao Bin 高賓 compared Zhu Zhenheng's role in medicine to that of Zhu Xi in classical “Confucian” studies (醫學之有丹溪猶吾儒之有朱子). The same preface also claimed that Danxi xinfa had only presented the “heart” (xin) of Danxi's methods, $ ^{122} $ and the Danxi zuanyao the “essentials” (yao) of Danxi precepts. Gao's book, on

## PDF第199页

the other hand, purported to present both the “heart” and the “essentials” of Zhu

Zhenheng's teachings, hence its title Xinyao.

In the Zhiba xinyao, the diankuang section of the Xinfa and the Zuanyao was replaced by one titled “illnesses of the Heart” or “mental afflictions” (心病; numbered 79 in juan 5). $ ^{123} $

The editors proposed another remix of passages from previous books of the Danxi corpus.

People whose Cardiac Qi is depleted and feeble and who [suffer from] cardiac palpitations; $ ^{124} $ some feel troubled and confused, some are prone to forgetfulness, and some have stupefied wits and unclear [minds] after having lost their Heart: [prescribe] the Cinnabar Spirit-appeasing pill. For those who [suffer from] Cardiac Wind, Qi Heat, or a dominance of mucus: mucus-roiling pill. For illnesses of the Heart: turmeric, honeylocust, white alum, centipede.

心氣虛怯之人 怔忡 或煩亂 或健忘 或失心後神癡不清 辰砂安神丸

心風氣熱痰盛者 滾痰丸 心病 鬱金 猪牙皂角 白礬 蜈蚣

The next two sentences were taken directly from the “Fire” section of the Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄 [“Probing the Mysteries of the Golden Casket”], a work compiled by Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), perhaps on the basis of a manuscript by Zhu Zhenheng.

For sturdy people with replete Qi who become crazy as a result of a flourishing of Fire, direct therapy can be used, or they can be given icy water with magnolia and magnesium sulfate to drink. If kuang [results from] the over-flourishing of depletion Fire, administer ginger decoction; [one who is] given icy water will die immediately. $ ^{125} $

人壯氣實 火盛癲狂者 可用正治 或朴硝冰水飲之 虛火盛狂者 以薑湯與之 若投冰水 立死

To these sentences, Gao added a few words on how to appease inner Fire. “When Fire is extremely urgent, raw licorice will assuage it; it can drain Fire; ginseng and atractylodes are also acceptable. Whenever Qi is in excess, it is Fire; $ ^{126} $ when it is in sufficient, there is Qi depletion” (火急甚者 生甘草緩之能瀉火 參朮亦可 凡氣有餘是火 不足是氣虛).

I could not trace the origin of the case record that followed.

A sturdy, plump, and firm man was suffering from stupidity caused by Cardiac Wind. After vomiting, I gave him the following: fritillary, trichosanthes, Jack-in-the-pulpit, golden thread (one liang each), turmeric, gastrodia, qingzi [?], raw licorice, citrus fruit, forsythia, shrubby sophora (half a liang each), as well as white alum and honeylocust (2 qian each), to be ingested after being made into pills. Then take: centipedes (one yellow and one red each; roast them in fragrant oil until they are glazed yellow),

[honeylocust?] (one liang each), and turmeric (half a liang). Make the above

[ingredients] into pills, with cinnabar as a coating.

天麻 青子 生甘草 枳實 連翹 苦參 各半兩 白礬 皂角 各二錢 右作

The section ended with a remixed passage on the differences and relations between dian and kuang that Fang Guang mostly borrowed from Lu He's Danxi zuanyao 丹溪纂要 (1484), who had in turn modified them from the Xinfa. $ ^{127} $ The Danxi zhiba xinyao confirmed what so many texts had already affirmed: madness was occasioned by either mucus or Fire.

## PDF第201页

I now go back in time to the early sixteenth century to a writer, Wang Lun, whose significance medical historians have largely neglected. His interpretations of the “Danxi” legacy proved particularly influential and provoked the most debates.

### 5.6 Wang Lun's 王綸 (1453-1510) reinterpretation of the "Danxi" legacy

As we have seen so far, those who discussed the transmission of Zhu Zhenheng's teachings in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries consistently depicted Zhu as a collegial thinker who had integrated the precepts of many masters into a multifaceted approach to illness. This was also the view Zhu had given of himself when he had recounted his own medical training (4.4). A similar stance was affirmed in Song Lian's 1359 epitaph of Zhu Zhenheng, in Zhu's biography by Dai Liang (same year), in the opening section of Liu Chun's 1388 Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學, in Lou Ying's 1396 preface to his Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目, in Yang Shiqi's 1438 preface to Liu Chun's works (claiming that Zhu represented the zheng mai 正脈 of medical teachings, Yang placed Zhu in a lineage of teachers that started with Zhang Yuansu 張元素 [4.3]), and in Cheng Chong's 1481 preface to Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法. For more than a century after Zhu's death, people admired Zhu's syncretic approach to the past, and his medicine was transmitted as a synthesis.

1 he 1482 Danxi xinja, however, implied instead that Zhu had promoted his own array of doctrines and recipes to understand and cure all the illnesses commonly found in the medical literature. The Xinfa rarely cited by name the other masters whose teachings had

## PDF第202页

been so important to Zhu. This new independence helped to justify Wang Lun's 王綸 (1453-1510) far-reaching reinterpretation of the "Danxi" legacy and of Zhu Zhenheng's relations with other physicians.

Wang Lun, a jinshi in 1484, was from Cixi 慈溪 County, north of modern-day Ningbo in Zhejiang. A successful official, he rose to high provincial posts before he died suddenly in Suzhou at the age of 58  $ ^{128} $ Since the views he articulated are still shaping the way we understand the evolution of Chinese medicine from the twelfth to the seventeenth century, he should be considered a crucial figure in Chinese medical history, yet he and his works are now largely forgotten. The book that made Wang both famous and controversial in his own time was the Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (Mingyi zazhu 明醫雜著), which was completed (and probably printed) in 1502. $ ^{129} $ Wang Lun had little to say on madness, but his re-interpretations of Danxi provoked doctrinal reactions that would later shape the way the illnesses dian and kuang were classified and treated.

In the very first section of his book (titled “Of the books of Zhongjing [Zhang Ji], Dongyuan [Li Gao], Hejian [Liu Wansu], and Danxi, which are superior?”仲景東垣河間丹溪諸書孰優), $ ^{130} $ Wang compared the Inner Canon to the Six Confucian Classics, and the works of these four medical masters to the Four Books. Wang concluded that each was superior in treating a certain kind of illness, an interpretive move similar to those proposed

## PDF第203页

by Zhu Zhenheng, Lou Ying, and Liu Chun, and another sign of zhezhong hermeneutics: “for externally contracted disorders, emulate Zhongjing; for inner harm emulate Dongyuan; for Heat illnesses, use Hejian; for miscellaneous illnesses, use Danxi; tie them up with one string: this is the complete Dao of medicine” (外感法仲景 内傷法東垣 熱病用河間 雜病用丹溪 一以貫之 斯醫道之大全矣). $ ^{131} $ Instead of emphasizing that Zhu had borrowed most of his therapies from his predecessors, Wang singled out the value of his therapies for “miscellaneous illnesses,” that is, anything but Cold Injury and seasonal disorders. $ ^{132} $

Wang proposed that Danxi's medical treatments were directed against Qi, Blood, or mucus, any of which could occur together with a fourth problem: "stasis" (yu 鬱). Wang claimed that Danxi had treated these problems respectively with the "Four-ingredient decoction" (四物湯, for Blood depletion), "Four-gentlemen decoction" (四君子湯; for Qi depletion), "Two-aged [ingredients] decoction" (二陳湯; for mucus), and Yueju wan 越鞠丸 (for stasis), adjusting these recipes at bedside to adapt to every patient's particularities. $ ^{133} $ It is notable that these three decoctions came from the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service, a book that Zhu Zhenheng had criticized in all his works. Wang's focus on four adaptable remedies made Zhu Zhenheng's therapies easier to apply than they had ever been, since tweaking basic recipes at bedside was easier than designing unique prescriptions for every episode of illness.

## PDF第204页

Wang Lun promoted other simplifications of Zhu's interpretations of illness and human vitality. One of his most influential explanations was his equation of Qi with Yang and Blood with Yin, the simplest way of interpreting the relations of Yin and Yang with Qi, Blood, and Fire (see table to the right). $ ^{134} $ To Wang, Yin depletion meant Blood depletion, and replenishing Yang meant replenishing Qi. For cases of Yin/Blood depletion, Wang warned against using ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), two “sweet and calorific” (甘温) drugs that Li Gao had used to replenish Qi in cases of depletion of the Spleen and Stomach caused by “inner harm.” $ ^{135} $ Wang argued that when Blood was depleted, drugs that replenished Qi should be avoided lest their “Heat” gave rise to Fire that would further consume already-deficient Yin.






<table border=1 style='margin: auto; word-wrap: break-word;'><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yin 陰</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yang 陽</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">Blood 血</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Qi 氣</td></tr><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Fire 火 (excess of Qi)</td></tr></table>

To Wang Lun, illnesses resulting from Blood depletion led to visible loss of blood through the upper orifices, and should never be treated with warming drugs.

With symptoms such as coughing, vomiting mucus, nose bleeding (nüxiè),  $ {}^{136} $ vomiting blood (tuxie), coughing with bloody mucus (kexie), and bloody expectorates (kaxie),  $ {}^{137} $ if [one] mistakenly takes sweet and warming drugs such as ginseng and astragalus

## PDF第205页

root, the illness will grow worse by the day. $ ^{138} $ If one takes too much, [the illness] will become incurable. This is because sweetness and warmth help Qi, which is Yang. As Yang thrives, Yin disperses all the more. The illness symptoms itemized above result from the depletion of Yin Blood and the dominance of Yang Fire. [To treat them] one should take bitter or sweet cooling drugs in order to generate Blood and make Fire descend. Contemporaries do not recognize [this] and often take ginseng and astragalus root as replenishers [without distinction between depletion of Blood and depletion of Qi]: I have seen many die as a result. $ ^{139} $

咳嗽 吐痰 衄血 吐血 咳血 咯血等症 誤服參 芪甘溫之藥 則病日增 服之過多 則不可治 蓋甘溫助氣 氣屬陽 陽旺則陰愈消 前項病症乃陰血虛而陽火旺 宜服苦甘寒之藥 以生血降火 世人不識 往往服參芪以為補 予見服此而死者多矣

Wang based his interpretation of Blood disorders and his warning against these two drugs on selected passages from “Danxi” works, notably a few sections on Blood disorders in Danxi xinfa, a work that Zhu Zhenheng had not written. $ ^{140} $ This approach was not implausible, but it did not do justice to Zhu’s flexible therapies (as deployed in his case records), and not even to the Danxi xinfa, which recommended many recipes based on astragalus and ginseng even for the disorders Wang Lun warned could not be treated with drugs that replenished

## PDF第206页

Qi. $ ^{141} $ Chapter 6 explains how sixteenth-century thinkers developed new doctrines by citing Danxi texts against Wang Lun's interpretation of Zhu, particularly his lopsided warnings against the dangers of astragalus and ginseng.

Wang also popularized the notion (still accepted today among both scholars and practitioners) that Zhu particularly excelled at “making Fire descend by nourishing Yin” (滋陰降火). $ ^{142} $ In a section “On the Yin-replenishing pill” (補陰丸論), Wang gave direct clinical application to Zhu’s notion that “Yang is excessive and Yin deficient.” One of Zhu’s points in “On Ministerial Fire” had been that restraining desires and excessive emotions by means of self-cultivation would prevent the pathological stirring of inner Fire. Wang argued that, among his contemporaries, “few restrict their desires and many have excessive ones: Essence and Blood being both lacking, Ministerial Fire is bound to thrive, and thus further disperse Yin” (節欲者少 過欲者多 相火必旺 火旺則陰愈消), causing blood spitting, bloody cough, etc. Wang Lun’s recommendation for “nourishing life” (養生) was therefore that “Yin should be replenished constantly so as to balance Yin with Yang; this makes Water able to restrain Fire: as Water rises and Fire descends, there will be no illness” (故宜常補其陰 使陰與陽齊則水能制火 而水升火降 斯無病矣). $ ^{143} $ Wang Lun was

## PDF第207页

not the first to claim that Zhu had specialized in nourishing Yin, but he defended Yin-replenishing more explicitly and systematically than any of his predecessors. $ ^{144} $

Wang Lun credited Danxi's "doctrine of Yin-replenishing" for having "corrected a thousand-year-old error" (正千载之訛) by defining replenishing as the replenishing of Left-Kidney Water, in opposition to the views implied by recipes designed to replenish Ministerial Fire in the right Kidney. These recipes had failed to recognize that the left Kidney (which represented "Water" among the Five Agents) was essentially depleted and the right Kidney ("Ministerial Fire") fundamentally thriving. Replenishing both was thus not the correct way to rectify an imbalance between "Fire" and "Water." To Wang, Fire should be replenished only in the rare cases in which it waned: "among people today, in eight or nine out of ten cases, it is the thriving of Fire that leads to illness; whereas there are not even two or three cases out of a hundred in which the waning of Fire turns into illness" (世之人火旺致病者十居八九 火衰成疾者百無二三). In the next chapter, I will argue that the important "calorific-replenishing" movement of the sixteenth century emerged partly in reaction to Wang's interpretation of human vitality.

Wang Lun's book helped diffuse Zhu Zhenheng's precepts and methods, but not without modifying them. The Danxi therapies explained in the Miscellaneous Writings were simple to deploy because they consisted of basic recipes that could be modified at bedside according to a patient's condition. As I shall show in Chapter 6, Wang's Yin-replenishing

## PDF第208页

approach and his general fear of warming drugs were widely adopted by both patients and physicians. But the wide following Wang's work gained and its somewhat reductive interpretation of Zhu's therapies also made it a tempting target for critiques in the sixteenth century. A surprising amount of doctrinal debates surrounded Wang's interpretation of Fire, and controversies raged about how depletion Fire should be treated. Let me now turn to one of Wang's earliest critics.

### 5.7 Yu Tuan on Danxi, Wang Lun, and madness

Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517) was 46 years old when the Danxi zuanyao 丹溪纂要 appeared in 1484, and 64 when Wang Lun's Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians came out. Like Zhu Zhenheng and Lu He (the compiler of the Danxi zuanyao), Yu was born in Yiwu county in Jinhua prefecture. Since he came from a family of medical practitioners whose teachings were based on Zhu's, Yu may have read Lu He's compilation soon after it appeared in 1484. $ ^{145} $ In 1515, Yu published the Correct Transmission of Medical Learning (Yixue zhengchuan 醫學正傳), where he displayed sophisticated knowledge of current medical debates. This crucial book reasserted the syncretic aspects of Zhu's approach (contra Wang Lun, who had described Zhu as one among many competing masters), developed notions of vitality that later thinkers would recuperate, and proposed an influential argument against Wang Lun's interpretations of Blood and Qi. Yu Tuan also introduced a new ground for distinguishing between dian and kuang that was later cited in a number of medical treatises. The discussions of medical doctrines in this chapter provide links to the next chapter.

## PDF第209页

In his preface, Yu Tuan praised Lu He's Zuanyao for facilitating access to Zhu's ideas, but claimed that the book had not yet exhausted Danxi's legacy (獨未足以盡丹溪之餘緒): "Danxi's books only expressed what others had not expressed and complemented what others had not perfected; if they are not examined beside the writings of the other worthies and merged with them into one, how is this to be the full accomplishment of the Way of medicine?" (然丹溪之書 不過發前人所未發 補前人所未備耳 若不參以諸賢所著而互合為一 豈醫道之大成哉). Like Liu Chun before him, Yu aimed to design an approach not biased by any particular emphasis. Still, he kept putting Danxi at the center of his interpretations, often citing (without naming it) the Zuanyao to support his claims.

Yu Tuan dismissed people who equated Blood with Yin and Qi with Yang. He explained that Zhu's notion that "Yang is often in excess and Yin in deficit" applied to the cosmos (Heaven and Earth, the sun and the moon) and the myriad things, $ ^{146} $ as well as to the body as a whole (在人身該乎一體而論), but did not refer to Qi as Yang and Blood as Yin. In other words, Zhu had not implied that Yin should necessarily be replenished in priority. $ ^{147} $ Educated readers from Yu's time would have recognized that Yu was disputing Wang Lun's interpretations of Zhu Danxi.

The benighted say that Qi depletion is simply Yang depletion, that only the Four-

gentlemen can be used [to treat Qi depletion], and that Lovage and other acrid drugs

should be categorically prohibited; $ ^{148} $ [they say] that Blood depletion is Yin depletion

## PDF第210页

and that only the Four-ingredients can be employed [in its treatment], whereas astragalus and ginseng should be absolutely disallowed.

味者謂氣虛即陰虛 止可用四君子 斷不可用芎辛之屬 血虛即陰虛 止可用四物 絕不可用參芪之類

To Yu, those who so argued ignored Li Gao's words that "when Yang is dominant it can produce Yin Blood" (陽旺則能生陰血), in which "Yang" and "Yin" directly referred to Qi and Blood (此陰陽二字直指氣血言). Yu argued that Li had identified "benefiting Qi when Blood is depleted" as "the method of the ancient sages" (血脫益氣 古聖人之法也). In direct opposition to Wang Lun's warnings, this injunction meant that "Blood depletion has to be replenished with astragalus and ginseng, according to the principle that 'Yin grows when Yang is produced'" (血虛者須以參芪補之 陽生陰長之理也). The two drugs should be avoided only when True Yin was depleted. $ ^{149} $ Wang Lun had "not understood this principle" (未明此理). $ ^{150} $ After adducing more examples showing that the best remedies to prevent depletion were often precisely those that Wang had disallowed, Yu Tuan concluded: "Alas! [Wang Lun] Ruyan's discussions correspond with neither Dongyuan's nor Danxi's! It is always the case with those in this world who stiffly adhere to set approaches. I could not

## PDF第211页

bear not to refute them.”(噫　汝言之論　何其與東垣丹溪俱不合耶　世之膠柱調瑟

者比比皆是　予不容不辯也)

In the eminently intertextual section on dian, kuang, and xian of his book, Yu Tuan cited canonical passages that described the symptoms of dian and kuang. He also joined the debate on the comparative validity of two influential interpretations of these two illnesses:

“Dian” and “kuang” are discussed separately: do they indeed differ? The Canon of Problems claims that the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang. A note to the Basic Questions claims that abundant joy is dian and abundant anger is kuang. $ ^{151} $ However, joy belongs to the Heart and anger to the Liver: they are simply manifestations of an excess of Ministerial Fire in these two viscera. I am afraid the Yin-Yang doctrine of the Canon of Problems is unfounded.

曰顧曰狂 分而言之 亦有異乎 難經謂重陰者顧 重陽者狂 素問注云多喜為顧 多怒為狂 然則喜屬於心而怒屬於肝 乃二臟相火有餘之證 難經陰陽之說 恐非理也

This passage was a short recapitulation of Liu Wansu's reasoning in the Yuanbing shi, which had been re-presented in Lu He's Danxi zuanyao (1484).

Perhaps basing himself on the explanations of the Danxi xinfa, Yu Tuan was the first physician I have found who asserted so explicitly that dian and kuang should be distinguished not on the basis of their symptoms, but on the basis of the distinct imbalances that allegedly caused their symptoms: "In the main, kuang is [caused by] the over-repletion and dominance of mucus and Fire, dian by deficiency of Cardiac Blood; [dian] is contracted by those with far-reaching aspirations who do not obtain what they want" (大抵狂為痰火實盛 癲為心血

## PDF第212页

不足 多為求望高遠不得志者有之). Kuang should be treated by purging, and dian by
appeasing the Spirit and nourishing Blood, as well as by making Fire and mucus descend. $ ^{152} $
Yu Tuan's portrayal of dian as an autonomous disorder was enthusiastically adopted by
sixteenth-and seventeenth-century physicians (see 6.5 and 6.6).

### 5.8 The dominance of the mucus and Fire etiologies of madness in early-sixteenth-century Jiangnan

We have so far established that early-Ming medical literature (notably the influential Danxi corpus) mostly depicted madness as being caused by Fire and mucus, but it is difficult to determine when the mucus-Fire approach became generalized in practice. Although an almost complete lack of case records renders tentative any conclusion about the period between 1360 and 1520. Zhao Jizong's 趙繼宗 Essentials of Scholarly Medicine (Ruyi jingyao 儒醫精要), a short work in 33 pian published in 1528, hints at the prevalence of mucus medicine and the mucus etiology of madness in the Lower Yangzi region at that time. $ ^{153} $

Zhao Jizong adopted an unusually critical tone to expose “errors” in the central tenets of current medicine. He attacked Wang Shuhe’s theories of the pulse, criticized the notion that Cold Injury disorders were transmitted from one tract to the other, condemned “the error of vomiting, sweating, and purging, the three methods [for treating] Cold Injury” (論傷寒吐汗下三法之誤), and, contra Wang Lun’s interpretation of Danxi, denounced “the

## PDF第213页

error of replenishing Yin” (論補陰之誤), arguing that Wang’s pretense to “make Yin level with Yang” defied cosmological and human regularities. Since, Zhao maintained, Yang was the source of life in both the cosmos and the body, and Yin could not stand without Yang, Yin-replenishing was an ineffectual therapy. $ ^{154} $

One of Zhao's major targets was the mucus etiology of various illnesses. In a section called "On the mistake [of saying that] mucus causes the various illnesses" (諷痰為諸病之誤), he censured physicians' reliance on mucus therapies:

It has been said that mucus pervades the human body. $ ^{155} $ Now in later generations, whenever illnesses occur, regardless of whether there is mucus, they are all being treated as [if they were caused by] mucus. Sigh! When there is mucus, treating mucus means treating the branch instead of the root, which is already inadequate; but is treating mucus when there is no mucus not mistaking people's illnesses? $ ^{156} $

或曰人身渾身是痰 天下後世凡人身有病 不問有痰無痰 一切以痰治之

噫 若有痰而以痰治 謂之治標而不治本 然且不可 無痰而以痰治 豈不

誤人之病哉

In a section “On the mistake contemporaries make when they speak of mucus-Fire” (論世人言痰火之誤), Zhao explained that the expression “mucus-Fire” (痰火) wrongly implied that pathological Fire originated in mucus. To him, the right interpretation was that...

Mucus is water: it is a branch [i.e., superficial manifestation]. Fire is Heat: it is the root. Because contemporaries do not understand the notions of branch and root,

## PDF第214页

they speak of “mucus-Fire” as if mucus could produce Fire, not knowing that it is

Fire that can produce mucus. $ ^{157} $

痰者水也 標也火者 熱也本也 自世人不明標本之理而曰痰火 卻以痰能生火 而不知能生痰也

Zhao argued that doctors should rather speak of “Heat-mucus” (熱痰), since mucus

appeared when Heat (created by obstruction of the circulation tracts caused by an imbalance

between Blood and Qi) blurred the natural clarity of inner fluids (津液).

Zhao’s analysis of the roots of mucus supported his criticism of a generic anti-mucus recipe called Erchen (“Two-aged [ingredients]”) decoction, which both the 1482 Danxi xinfa and Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Englightened Physicians (1502) had promoted as Zhu Zhenheng’s favorite anti-mucus recipe:¹⁵⁸ “It is said that Erchen decoction treats mucus throughout the body.¹⁵⁹ Contemporary physicians stick to this: whenever there is mucus, they all treat it with Erchen decoction.”¹⁶⁰ (二陳湯謂一身之痰都治 世醫執之 凡有痰者皆以二陳湯治之) Zhao’s main critique was that this remedy contained pinellia (半夏), a warming and drying drug that Zhao agreed was excellent for treating mucus produced by Dampness, but not other kinds of mucus: “There are so many [anti-] mucus drugs: what need is there for contemporary physicians to stick to the pinellia of Erchen [decoction] when treating mucus?”¹⁶¹ (且痰藥數多 世醫治痰何必拘拘二陳之半夏哉)

In a short piece that formed part of his broader assessment of mucus medicine, Zhao denied that madness could ever be caused by mucus.

## PDF第215页

This illness has a source and when it flares up it has its manifestations, yet contemporary physicians do not investigate these principles and declare in unison that it is caused by mucus. Alas! They do not know what kind of thing mucus is and what kind of illness kuang is. If mucus could cause madness, everyone with mucus would be mad, but this is something I have never seen. $ ^{162} $

其病也有源 其發也有候 世醫不究其理 舉以為痰同聲相應 噫 不知痰是何物而狂是何病也 若以痰能為狂則凡有痰者皆狂 而予未之見

After citing purely textual and mostly canonical evidence that madness was always caused by Heat, Zhao concluded by asking how madness could possibly be treatable with anti-mucus remedies. His harangues suggest that the mucus etiology of madness had become widespread by the early sixteenth century. I will continue to cite evidence of this dominance as I trace the emergence of sophisticated alternatives to the Fire-mucus etiology in Chapter 6.

### 5.9 “Le travail du texte” in the emergence of illness scripts $ ^{163} $

Apart from Yu Tuan's new distinction between dian and kuang on the basis of their etiologies, no noticeable change took place in medical approaches to madness between 1360 and 1530. Since etiologies based on Fire and mucus had already become important in the late fourteenth century (end of 4.4 to 4.6) and were prominent in the 1520s (5.8), they probably prevailed in the period I have discussed here. Indeed, the main etiologies of madness mentioned in the Danxi corpus (besides possession) were centered on Fire and mucus.

The period between 1360 and 1530 was when approaches attributed to Zhu Zhenheng (“Danxi”) were solidified through textual densification, and disseminated by

## PDF第216页

prestigious disciples of Zhu's who circulated manuscripts and compiled treatises that were eventually printed. Books like the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (5.5) and Wang Lun's 王綸 Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (5.6), which purported to represent Zhu's doctrines and therapeutic methods, contributed to diffusing the Danxi approach.

I analyzed how specific statements concerning madness were elaborated in a growing body of texts attributed to Zhu Zhenheng and in the writings of important disciples and admirers of Zhu's. The section on diankuang that Yang Xun 楊珣 had patched together for his Danxi xinfā in the 1450s was included in the Huizhou Danxi xinfā (1482), and then emended and corrected to appear in books such as Lu He's 盧和 Danxi zuanyao 丹溪纂要 (1484) and Yu Tuan's 虞搏 Yixue zhengchuan 醫學正傳 (1515).

The “Danxi corpus” was one major channel through which the thirteenth- and fourteenth-century medical reinterpretation of madness as an illness caused by mucus and Fire was disseminated and became solidly anchored in the thinking of a large number of Chinese physicians. Many of the evolutions I analyzed here would have remained invisible had I looked solely for novelty or originality in representations of madness, or consulted only works that displayed a strong authorial presence.

Change did not take place as inexorably and uniformly as a tide rising over an even landscape, but it lasted because the works that advocated the new etiologies were printed, widely read, and integrated into teaching networks. The new approaches to madness became self-sustaining because previous ones no longer had any advocates. Indeed, the old treatises that had presented these etiologies were not easily available (since they were rarely re-edited and re-printed) and no teaching traditions that I could identify took them as their core. This does not mean that the new views could not be challenged, but they were challenged from

## PDF第217页

inside, on the basis of new conceptions of vitality, for example, not in the name of old etiologies like external Wind.

Highlighting the importance of textual dynamics in the formation of medical knowledge does not mean focusing on “theory” at the expense of “practice.” The writing of doctrinal texts was itself a practice. I paid attention to the mechanics of treatise compilation in order to understand how ideas on insanity emerged and were stabilized, in what forms they were presented, and why—partly by virtue of having appeared in the “Danxi corpus”—they were eventually discussed more than others, either in approval or disagreement.

Grasping the textual origin of influential statements on madness helps to understand why (and where) there were tensions between the knowledge articulated in medical treatises and the aptitudes that were necessary to treat mad behavior. I address this issue at more length in Chapter 6.

A few historians of Chinese medicine have contrasted case-reasoning with more general forms of specialist knowledge, $ ^{164} $ but scholars have done little to understand the nature of the more “general” knowledge found in treatises. Trying to compensate for this lack, I have shown that many (perhaps even most) illness scripts emerged from intertextual matrices even as they remained plausibly applicable to concrete cases.

Though most of the works I have analyzed were designed for practitioners, they usually aimed to solve interpretive issues by reference to other texts rather than to practice. Chinese treatise writers were thus more likely to borrow a description of illness from an earlier book than to write a new description based on his scrutiny of real symptoms, and more likely to create novelties by borrowing from his understanding of other illnesses than through observation of patients. Clinical experience probably guided a writer's choice of

## PDF第218页

what he would include in his compilations, but most treatises remained collages of textual excerpts rather than compendia based on practical experience. This was true even when treatises presented themselves as repositories of a physician's practical methods (as the Danxi xinfā did, for example).

Zhu Zhenheng shaped later medicine by training his disciples to adopt a deliberately composite approach to the legacy of past physicians. Their works were reasoned textual collages, the form implied by zhezhong hermeneutics. The re-orchestration of a complex textual heritage into extensive digests became perhaps the most highly valued ways of writing treatises in Ming times. These works were not brittle aggregates but supple amalgams, adaptable interpretations of the textual past held together by a flexible conception of illness.

Arguably, the reason why “mainstream Ming medicine never challenged the synthetic approach of the Zhu school” (Leung 2003a: 389) is that mainstream Ming medicine was the “Zhu school.” For almost two centuries after Zhu Zhenheng’s death in 1358, his disciples and admirers constantly redefined the content and contours of the Danxi synthesis. When physicians deviated from Danxi medicine, they still embraced the synthetic approach that Zhu had promoted, though they sometimes used it to integrate precepts that disagreed with Zhu’s. In other words, Ming thinkers were too busy reshaping the Danxi approach to consider standing outside it to challenge it.

But speaking simply of a “Zhu school” hides the complex social and textual ways by which an array of medical notions and techniques were shaped and diffused. Many strains of medicine co-existed in the Danxi corpus: the approach that consisted in designing a distinct therapy for each illness instance (as defended in Zhu Zhenheng’s Gezhi yulun and his Elaborations on the Recipes of the [Pharmacy] Service), the Danxi who used an array of set recipes to treat clearly defined illnesses (the view implied by the Danxi xinfā), and the Danxi who

## PDF第219页

modified a few basic remedies at bedside to fit the details of each individual case (Wang Lun's version). Wang Lun's interpretation of Danxi seems to have been widely followed, probably because selecting ready-made remedies was much easier than designing recipes from scratch on the basis of specific pulse diagnoses.

I stopped my investigations at several turning points: when “Danxi” was starting to be interpreted as an independent approach to therapy (rather than a synthesis of previous ones), when debates were forming as to the nature of depletion and the proper way of treating it (as in books by Wang Lun and Yu Tuan), when dian and kuang were starting to be distinguished again on new grounds, and when a brand of medicine that advocated differential prescriptions for each instance of illness was being endorsed in a growing number of printed texts. All these trends became interconnected in ways I discuss in the next chapter.

## PDF第220页

# 6 Replenish or attack? Competing etiologies and therapies of madness in Ming medicine

When there is depletion, replenish it; when there is repletion: drain it.

虛者補之 實者瀉之

Canon of Problems 難經, problem 69

This chapter explains the rich developments that took place in elite medical thinking and practice from roughly 1530 to 1650. In addition to writing long sections on diankuang in their treatises, physicians from this ebullient period recorded in some detail how they handled specific cases of madness. All of these men had read the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (1482), knew of Wang Lun's 王綸 (1453-1510) arguments on Yin-replenishing, and embraced a syncretistic approach to past medical teachings. Their clinical practice and most of the doctrinal debates in which they engaged were thus informed by the trends in medical thinking that I described in previous chapters.

In the domain that interests us, the main event of the sixteenth century was perhaps the re-definition of dian 癲 as a form of madness characterized by non-manic symptoms and best treated by replenishing. The illness dian had existed since at least the third century BCE, and it had been associated with kuang since the first century BCE, but its main symptoms had first been close to those of epilepsy. When dian had been re-interpreted as a form of madness in the twelfth century, it had become so close to kuang in both etiology and

## PDF第221页

symptoms that it could hardly be differentiated from kuang (3.4, 4.1). Dissatisfied with this confusion, many sixteenth-century physicians tried to reassert differences between dian and kuang. The clarifications proposed by a physician from Huizhou called Sun Yikui 孫一奎 (1522-1619) were eventually accepted by most elite physicians in the following centuries.

One major area of discord between sixteenth-century physicians was whether mad behavior should be treated with purges and induced vomiting (which had been the prototypical treatments since at least the fourteenth century) or by replenishing. Attacking cures usually weakened the patient, whereas replenishing aimed to protect vital forces. I use “replenish or attack” to refer succinctly to debates concerning the relative merits of these two strategies and to clinical competition between them, both in general and in insanity cases. I will show that these debates on therapy usually depended on reflections concerning the nature and sources of human vitality as well as the proper ways to treat “depletion Fire,” a kind of pathological Fire triggered by an underlying organic depletion.

As I will argue, the newly reshaped dian—a type of madness caused by depletion—developed in a rich eco-system that included debates on vitality, controversies on the proper way to treat depletion Fire, reflections on the pathology of emotions, and the perceived need to resolve contradictions between current usage—dian and kuang as similar illnesses—and textual sources that presented dian and kuang as complementary opposites.

The “medical case files” (yian 醫案) on which I rely to study the dynamics of therapeutic practice were more than illustrations of medical doctrine. These records of clinical encounters described specific symptoms instead of the prototypical ones found in general treatises. They also provide information on the social and moral significance of madness and on gender differences in the treatment of an illness that treatises typically portrayed as un-gendered and morally neutral. By revealing the methods physicians used to

## PDF第222页

treat mad people, case collections also tell us what parts of treatise descriptions that doctors found useful. The interactions between doctrinal knowledge and clinical practice—and the related but not entirely similar relations between treatises and collections of case files—are also themes that structure this chapter.

I start by explaining the flourishing of medical case files in the sixteenth century, as well as their significance for the study of Chinese medical history. Starting in the 1520s, a few doctors used case data to question the conception of vitality that underlay the attacking therapies that had dominated early-Ming treatments of madness. My second section focuses on Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), an early critic who recorded his distinctive treatment of two insane people. The following two sections explore how two themes from Wang's writings were exploited later in the sixteenth century. Part 3 explains how a therapeutic strategy called "calorific replenishing" (wenhu 溫補) emerged from debates about the sources of human vitality and the proper way to treat "depletion Fire." I then analyze the increased attention that many physicians from Huizhou in Anhui (including Wang Ji) paid to emotional disorders and the therapeutic use of emotions.

All the above developments—the proliferation of case files, the association of madness with emotions, the rise of new views of vitality and the concurrent emergence of calorific replenishing, and the continuing practice of composing medical treatises by culling passages from existing sources—converged to create the new madness prototype dian. But this concept did not stabilize immediately. Section 5 explains how some physicians (many of whom were from Huizhou) attempted to distinguish dian from kuang on new grounds now that doctors commonly took both to refer to mad behavior. The sixth section shows that

## PDF第223页

Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 (1522-1619) deliberations on the differences between dian, kuang, and xian built on the reflections of fellow Huizhou physicians and borrowed from other texts. His distinctions between dian and kuang were widely adopted later, notably by Wang Kentang 王肯堂 (1552-1638), whose enhancement of Sun's ideas I explain in section 7.

The rest of the chapter explains why the new notion of dian as a kind of madness caused by depletion did not become dominant. Zhang Jiebin 張介賓 (1563-1640), for example, defended calorific replenishing and scorchingly criticized previous conceptions of Fire, but still argued that madness was chiefly caused by Fire and should be treated with attacking drugs. In this chapter's last segment, I show that Li Zhongzi's 李中梓 (1588-1655) use of both attacking and replenishing drugs to treat kuang integrated the most common treatment for madness with concerns for preserving vitality.

This chapter shows how themes that had first developed in general reflections on pathology and vitality found their way into medical approaches to madness, first in individual cases, and eventually into treatises, where these new approaches were solidified. Scheme transfer thus remained the most important mode of change in the medical understanding and treatment of madness in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

### 6.1 The art of diagnosis and the rise of “medical case files” (yi’an 醫案)

Hans Ågren once remarked that Chinese physicians “did not use names of disorders as targets of therapy as in the West” (1982: 574). As Nathan Sivin (1987: 175) expounded, “The point of diagnosis in the early classics is not so much naming the illness as determining its manifestations.... and the dynamic character of pathological changes under way.” Many indeed consider that Chinese medicine does not recognize essential illnesses, but “a

## PDF第224页

multiplicity of symptomatic transformations” (Furth 1992: 769). Let me briefly historicize this attention to “the dynamic character of pathological changes” and explain how it relates to the emergence of case files as a kind of medical publication.

The notion that illnesses were constantly changing started to emerge in the late eleventh century when a few physicians applied to other disorders models that had been designed to understand Cold Injury.
