A prototypical case of Cold Injury unfolded in distinct stages that each called for a different treatment. Xu Shuwei's 許叔微 (1079-1154) Ninety Discussions on Cold Injury (Shanghan jiushi lun 傷寒九十論), written at the turn in the twelfth century but only printed for the first time in the nineteenth century, were early accounts of successful treatments based on unique diagnoses. $ ^{2} $

Zhang Yuansu 張元素 (12 $ ^{th} $ cent.) and his disciples were among the first to stress in their teachings that doctors should design prescriptions only after having determined what imbalance was causing the symptoms at hand (4.3). Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) presented this clinical flexibility as central to his own medicine, while emphasizing that he had learned this style of prescription from his master Luo Zhiti 羅知悌 (1238?-1327; 4.4). The variability of illness was also made plausible by the doctrine of the “Five periods and Six Qi” (highly influential in medical circles starting in the late eleventh century), which postulated that cosmic phenomena, disease, and human vital forces varied according to natural cycles. This cosmological scheme was enthusiastically supported by Liu Wansu 劉完素 (ca. 1120 – ca. 1200; 4.1), Zhang Yuansu, and Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228; 4.2) and remained prominent in medical thinking well into the Ming dynasty (1368-1644).

## PDF第225页

Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed.

The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century.

First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Case records were also an important part of Zhu Zhenheng's medical practice even if he did not collate them into a book during his lifetime. $ ^{6} $ Both his biographer

## PDF第226页

Dai Liang 戴良 and the author of his epitaph Song Lian 宋濂 concurred that Zhu kept

detailed records of his clinical encounters. As Dai explained in a text written in 1358 or 1359:

For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $

其所治病凡機病之狀何如 施何良方 飲何藥而愈 自前至今 驗者何人

何縣里 主名 得諸見聞 班班可紀

Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $

It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And how could one

## PDF第227页

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang

Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王

立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been

taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly

received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription.

Christopher Cullen’s argument (2001) that case collections started to flourish as a printed genre in the sixteenth century because an increasing number of educated men sought distinctive ways to foster their medical careers after they had failed the civil examinations is seductive, but not well supported enough to be convincing. First, less than a handful of case

## PDF第228页

collections were actually published in the sixteenth century, and all three of them were the work of men from Huizhou in Anhui. Apart from Wang Ji's book (6.2), I know only of the Categorized Case Files of Illustrious Physicians (Mingyi Leian 名醫類案 [1591], a florilegium of cases by many doctors) $ ^{13} $ and of Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 large collection (6.6), which his disciples collated in the 1590s. $ ^{14} $ Sun was himself a second-generation disciple of Wang Ji. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $

Before we inscribe the “rise of case files” within broad social trends, it may be more prudent to understand the motivations of the individuals who published such works, and to situate their case records in the broader context of their other medical works and of their contemporaries’ use of case files. Stone Mountain’s Medical Case Files was one of as many as eleven medical works that Wang completed from 1519 to the 1530s, and one of the eight he printed between 1523 and 1534 (see Bibl. A). Wang’s works were themselves among the numerous books that physicians from Huizhou compiled in the sixteenth century. Huizhou,

## PDF第229页

which had enjoyed a strong reputation for medical studies since at least the Song, was where the prominent third edition of the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 had been published in 1482 and a sequel called Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附語 appeared in 1536. In 1556, Huizhou man Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 completed a compendium called Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全, a mosaic of short texts or excerpts, and had it printed in Suzhou the following year. $ ^{17} $ Two editions of Wu Kun's 吴崑 Yifang kao 醫方考 (with a 1584 preface by Wu) were also printed in 1586, whereas Sun Yikui's works came out in the 1580s and 1590s. These Huizhou physicians were supported by a strong intellectual tradition, a network of active printers, and a growing commercial wealth that facilitated the sponsoring of medical books. $ ^{18} $

If we stop focusing on autonomous case collections, we also realize that Wang Ji was not the only man of his time to record his clinical encounters. Han Mao 韓懋 (fl. 1520s), Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520-30s), Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559), and certainly many others whose names are no longer remembered also did. $ ^{19} $ Although these men did not publish their own collections, their records circulated among specialists. Xue Ji interspersed his editions of earlier texts with cases of his own. $ ^{20} $ The practice of writing case files was thus fairly widespread in the most prosperous region of the empire. Wang Ji's decision to publish his own cases independently did not distinguish his cases from others' in all respects. As we

## PDF第230页

shall see in the next section, Wang Ji, just like both his predecessors (Zhu Zhenheng) and his contemporaries (Xue Ji), used his cases to support broader medical arguments.

I would say the “case files” genre emerged because an increasing number of doctors kept records of their clinical encounters, both for their patients and for their own reference. Their record-keeping depended on the notion that each bout of illness potentially required a unique therapy. It would thus seem that the sixteenth-century “bureaucratization” of case files that Cullen documented—and the common likening of medical intervention to judicial reasoning and judgment—was simply the repackaging along new metaphorical lines of a kind of record that had become increasingly important since approximately the thirteenth century, when some elite physicians adopted the flexible conception of illness I just mentioned. $ ^{21} $

Charlotte Furth (2007a: 19) has interpreted the case form (in medicine as well as other fields) as a “strategy of modernists in an intellectual tradition that gave awesome weight to the authority of antiquity,” and medical cases as tools to “negotiate the gap between… medical canon and therapeutic strategy.” $ ^{22} $ Without downplaying the freedom allowed by case files to go beyond canonical formulations, I would contend that, at least in the sixteenth century when they came into widespread use, case files served mostly in arguments between “modernists” rather than as devices to disregard the canons. In other words, the doctrines that case files served to relativize were often those of the recent past rather than those found in the medical classics.

## PDF第231页

Case files displaced the locus of medical authority from text to practice. Unlike the treatises they either criticized or complemented, case records were not composed from fragments of other texts (though they were intertextual in the more traditional sense that they deployed language and addressed issues found in other texts), and whereas treatises often proposed textual solutions to textual issues, cases records narrated the treatment of a non-textual element: a patient. Though the case record itself was textual, the logic of the clinical encounter was not. Once turned into “case files” (or records of “successful treatment”), these records could be brought to bear on the doctrinal issues of the day.

As I shall expound in the following sections, medical case files were ex post facto accounts of a doctor's clinical reasoning and actions, and were usually designed to demonstrate the diagnostic perspicacity of the practitioner who wrote them. The case form thus encouraged physicians to write about unique cases rather than about prototypes, while allowing for alternative treatments to be presented plausibly. As I noted above when I discussed Furth's interpretation of the case form, these prototypes were found not only in the medical canons, but also in officially sponsored formularies or influential recent works like the Danxi xinfa (1482) or Wang Lun's Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502).

Regardless of whether they were published in independent collections, case files shaped the history of approaches to madness because they embodied the notion that illnesses changed in countless ways. They were designed to prove that some instances of illness could have other causes than the “constant ones” that treatises emphasized. This case-based reasoning helped a new prototype of madness as caused by depletion to crystallize late in the sixteenth century and to start rivaling the prototypical etiology of Fire and mucus that had justified treating madness with attacking drugs.

## PDF第232页

### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning

Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms.

Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). In his work on poxes (Douzhi libian 痘治理辨; preface from 1531), Wang Ji had addressed Wei Zhi's 衛直 recently published Boai xinjian 博愛心鑒 (1525), whereas Wang's work on "external medicine" (Waike lili 外科理例; 1531 preface) integrated arguments he had found in Waike fabui 外科發揮 and Waike xinfa 外科心法, two books on the same topic (both of which carry prefaces from 1528) by Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559; from Suzhou), who was himself both a follower and a critic of Wang Lun. $ ^{24} $

Stone Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案), which Wang and his disciple Chen Jue 陳桷 completed in 1531 and had printed around 1533, was one of the

## PDF第233页

earliest case collections in Chinese medical history, $ ^{25} $ but it also served Wang Ji to make an array of doctrinal points on pathology and human vitality, and to defend his singular clinical approaches. The most distinctive and controversial aspect of Wang's medical practice was his use of calorific drugs (chiefly ginseng and astragalus) to treat cases of depletion that other physicians (following Wang Lun) would have treated with coolants. $ ^{26} $ Wang Lun had attributed visible emissions of Blood to "Fire" resulting from Blood/Yin deficiency, and argued that warming drugs only exacerbated this destructive Fire, and should therefore be proscribed when treating such cases (5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion.

Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. Not only did rival physicians routinely use coolants to replenish Yin and Blood, it appears that even patients cited Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502) to challenge Wang Ji’s prescriptions. $ ^{27} $ When he disagreed with a rival’s diagnosis in front of patients and their relatives, Wang Ji sometimes argued that loss of blood, a symptom commonly attributed to Yin/Blood depletion, was in this particular case caused by Qi deficiency and

## PDF第234页

should therefore be treated with warming drugs—a conventional treatment. But Wang was

at his most controversial when he agreed with a diagnosis of Yin depletion, yet insisted on

prescribing his drugs of choice instead of the coolants that his patients (who obviously had

an inkling of current medical doctrines) had come to expect in such cases.

Besides presenting case-based evidence that his own therapies worked and that replenishing Yin with coolants was not only ineffective but dangerous (since coolants could harm Stomach Qi), Wang sought to justify his approach with proper references to authority. In two separate refutations of Wang Lun's position on Yin-replenishing, he argued that Zhu Zhenheng's contextual warnings against the use of ginseng and astragalus did not amount to the sweeping interdiction that Wang Lun had turned them into. $ ^{28} $ His disagreement with Wang Lun was thus partly a conflict over the appropriation of “Danxi” doctrines. $ ^{29} $

Far from presenting only case files, Wang Ji also tried to theorize the conception of human vitality on which he based his successful prescriptions. Instead of equating Blood with Yin as Wang Lun had, Wang Ji maintained that “replenishing Yin” referred to replenishing the Yin aspect of Qi (see Table 6.1 below). $ ^{30} $ He could thus astutely argue that

## PDF第235页

“replenishing Yin” and Li Gao’s “replenishing Qi” both meant replenishing constructive Qi, the Yin aspect of Qi. This was why astragalus and ginseng, two Qi-replenishing drugs, could be used to replenish both Yin and Yang!


<table border=1 style='margin: auto; word-wrap: break-word;'><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yin 陰</td><td colspan="2">Yang 陽</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3">Blood 血</td><td colspan="2">Qi 氣</td></tr><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yin Qi 陰氣:</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Yang Qi 陽氣:</td></tr><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>I.e., constructive Qi (營氣)</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>I.e., protective Qi (衛氣)</td></tr></table>

<div style="text-align: center;">Table 6.1: Wang Ji's interpretation of Qi and Blood in terms of Yin and Yang</div>


Wang Ji’s two recorded cases of madness implicitly deployed his conceptions of vitality while making full use of the potential of case files for proposing novel arguments. Physicians best displayed their diagnostic prowess when their interpretations diverged from mainstream ones. Wang Ji’s first case was that of a “fat and pale” (肥白) man who had started suffering from a “Heart disorder” (心恙) after he had been humiliated while drunk. $ ^{31} $ The man would “hold a knife [threateningly] or climb walls, screaming with his hair unkempt” (或持刀或逾垣披頭大叫), as typical a case of wild madness as one could find. $ ^{32} $ His pulse (“soft, moderate, and depleted” 濡緩而虛) $ ^{33} $ told Wang that the symptoms were caused by depletion in the Yang-brilliance tract (陽明虛), a remarkable diagnosis, since any educated reader would have known that the Treatise on Cold Injury attributed these

## PDF第236页

symptoms to repletion of the Yang-brilliance tract!⁴⁴ As Wang remarked, “it was indicated to go against the precedents by replenishing” (宜變例以實之).³⁵

Wang Ji first described the recipes that previous practitioners had used on the patient. In case records, accounts of the abortive ministrations of rivals served as foils for the achievements of the narrator, whose success was all the more impressive if earlier therapies appeared to have been justified. One doctor had used the anti-mucus “Erchen decoction” augmented with purple perilla (紫蘇) and the peel of some citrus fruit (枳榖), two anti-mucus drugs. $ ^{36} $ Displeased when the patient’s family had invited Wang Ji for a second advice after this remedy had failed, he was allowed to continue treatment. He then used “Ox-gallstone Heart-clearing pill” (牛黃清心丸), which the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service had described as a Wind remedy with a broad range of applications, including anti-mucus action and the cure of madness, but the patient almost died from the heat the drugs generated. $ ^{37} $

The calamitous effects of these anti-mucus remedies convinced the patient’s relatives to hire Wang Ji. He designed a recipe “that takes the sweet and calorific drugs ginseng, astragalus root, and licorice as its main ingredients” (以参芪甘草甘温之藥為君). The use

## PDF第237页

of these drugs to remove Fire was based on Li Gao's 李杲 Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach (1249) and perhaps even more directly on one of Dai Sigong's 戴思恭 treatises at the end of Zhu Zhenheng's Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄. $ ^{38} $ As secondary components, Wang prescribed the sweet and cooling Lily-turf and Baikal skullcap (麥冬片黃芩甘寒之劑為臣), to which he added immature orange (青皮) to regulate the Liver (疏肝) and bamboo juice (竹瀝) to clear mucus (清痰). $ ^{39} $ From the above, it seems that Wang attributed the patient's symptoms to Fire-inducing depletion caused by emotional disorders, with Liver stasis and lodged mucus.

After a month of this regimen, the madness symptoms had mostly subsided, but the patient still suffered from momentary losses of consciousness. Wang explained that the patient’s mind was disorderly because “Qi and Blood had not yet recovered” (此氣血未復神志昏亂而然). $ ^{40} $ In addition to the first remedy, Wang prescribed the Spirit-calming pill

## PDF第238页

(安神丸) in the evening and the buqian pill 虎潜丸 in the morning. $ ^{41} $ The patient was cured after more than a year. Even if Wang used recipes and therapeutic measures drawn from the Danxi corpus, the therapy he designed went directly against the section on madness of the Danxi xinfa, which claimed that kuang should be cured by massive vomiting or purging. The Danxi corpus was indeed rich enough to sustain contradictory interpretations.

Wang did not present this case as representing a new type of madness, but as an exception made possible by the ever-changing faces of illness. He tamed the uniqueness of the illness by applying bits of familiar therapeutic scripts to it: sweet and warming drugs to treat inner harm, sweet and cooling drugs to avoid depleting vitality while curbing Fire, and anti-mucus drugs to control a typical agent of mental disturbance. Because it was based on a unique diagnosis, this prescription was exclusively adapted to the case at hand, yet it made sense within scripted understandings of illness process and therapy.

Indeed, even the most sophisticated physician’s reaction to symptoms did not start anew every time he encountered mad behavior. Doctors brought to clinical encounters some “long-term schematic knowledge” (Fauconnier and Turner 2005: 40) about similar disorders, types of patients (women or fat people, for instance), and types of etiologies that they expected to govern the kinds of symptoms they faced. “Transactions between explanatory models and clinical realities” (Kleinman 1980) thus took place through the clinical encounter, where the healer created a context-dependent bridge between doctrinal knowledge and observed symptoms. By an act of conceptual integration rooted in embodied knowledge (diagnostic methods, for instance), doctors created “blends” adapted to the case at hand.

## PDF第239页

Wang Ji’s cases remind us that the clinical encounter was itself a complex interaction between a healer, a sick person, and a patient’s relatives, and between a dynamic illness condition, the action of drugs, negotiations over efficacy, and the patient’s “meaning response” (Moerman 2002) to relatives, the healer, and the drugs. $ ^{42} $ Unfortunately, case records are often silent on the patient’s reaction to his or her own treatment.

In another case by Wang, a woman in her early thirties suddenly spoke mad words and ran around naked with her hair undone, knowing no decency (忽病狂言 披髮裸體不知羞惡). $ ^{43} $ Other healers had adopted aggressive treatments: “Everyone thought it was Heart Wind: some wanted to make her drink fecal juices, others to induce vomiting with anti-mucus drugs” (眾皆以為心風 或欲飲以糞清 或吐以痰藥). $ ^{44} $ Detecting a “submerged, soft, and moderate pulse” (脈浮緩而濡), Wang determined that the disorder was simply caused by hunger and fatigue that had harmed the Stomach (必忍飢或勞倦傷胃而然耳). $ ^{45} $ “Harm to the Stomach has stirred Fire, which has extended to the Heart and Spleen, bringing into disarray the Spirit harbored in the Heart and the Intent harbored in the Spleen because they have lost what they rest upon” (傷胃而火動 延及心脾 則心所藏之神 脾所藏之意 皆為之擾亂 失其所依歸): “how could she not be mad” (安得不

## PDF第240页

狂) $ ^{46} $ “The method for kuang caused by inner harm and in which Yang-brilliance is depleted and exhausted is to replenish” (内傷發狂 陽明虛竭 法當補之). The cure was simple: “Lone ginseng decoction” (獨參湯) accompanied by bamboo juice. The patient recovered.

Wang Ji was not the only physician of his time who devised new replenishing cures for madness manifested or occasioned by emotional excesses. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520-30s), from Taizhou in Zhejiang, cured a woman who had lost her mind and suffered from dian (失志顛疾), an illness Wu defined as a “disorderly heart (or mind)” (亂心).⁴⁷ She would either sob or laugh, wander around naked, or shut the door and speak a lot (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言). She was “emaciated and of an anxious nature, and excessive worry had consumed and harmed her Cardiac blood” (瘦弱性急 因思過度 耗傷心血), making the Spirit unable to hold to its lodging.⁴⁸ Wu replenished her with human placenta, which he called “Purple river-chariot” (紫河車) because the best placentas were purportedly purple.⁴⁹ The patient ate the boiled placenta and became well after Wu gave her “Will-fixing pill” (定志丸) and “Heart-replenishing decoction” (補心湯) for 100 days.⁵⁰

## PDF第241页

Around the same time, Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559), from Suzhou, was also developing a therapeutic approach based on some aspects of Zhu Zhenheng's and Li Gao's legacies. He had served as Commissioner of the Imperial Academy of Medicine in Beijing until he retired in 1530 to practice medicine. His strategy to treat a dian woman was similar to Wang Ji's. $ ^{51} $

A woman who had always been frugal and led a difficult life became dian because she was startled. Some [physicians] used drugs against Wind-mucus, which made [the disorder] worsen. I used ginseng, astragalus, angelica, and atractylodes brewed into a thick decoction, and assisted them with ginger juice and bamboo juice, of which she had to take more than three catties before she became better.

一婦人素清苦 因驚而顛 或用風痰等藥愈甚 余用〔人〕參〔黃〕芪〔當〕歸〔白〕朮濃煎 佐以薑汁 竹瀝三斤餘 方愈

Ginger juice and bamboo juice were the two ingredients the Danxi xinfā had recommended for getting rid of mucus. From the way Xue designed his prescription, it appears that he traced the disorder to mucus with underlying Qi depletion.

Despite the emergence of these gentler cures, most physicians kept promoting the use of aggressive drugs to treat mad behavior. Huizhou doctors were no exception. In Gujin

## PDF第242页

yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556), Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 identified Fire and mucus as the main causes of madness and most of his proposed remedies were emetics and purgatives. The eight recipes Zhang Siwei 張四維 recommended to treat dian and kuang all targeted mucus, $ ^{52} $ whereas Wu Kun 吳崑 (1552-1620) contended that both illnesses were caused by over-repletion: “they should be purged and not replenished” (宜瀉而不宜補). $ ^{53} $ He warned that in the aftermath of purges “one cannot, upon seeing patients’ thinness or weakness and the reduction of their food [intake], replenish them with warming drugs or sate them with food and drinks, for the illness will inevitably flare up again. Beware! Beware!” (不可見其瘦弱減食 便以溫藥補之 及以飲食飽之 病必在作 戒之戒之)

Yet Wu Kun's warning shows that replenishing had become a plausible alternative to the mainstream attacking treatments. Case records show that replenishing therapies were employed mostly when emotional problems had given rise to madness. This orientation is not surprising since emotions were considered to be forms of "inner harm," which by definition was occasioned by depletion.

Despite their atypical diagnoses, Wang Ji, Wu Qiu, and Xue Ji had no nosographic pretensions. They did not argue that their cases represented new "types" of insanity. We shall see, however, that a new prototype of madness as caused by depletion would soon crystallize in Chinese medical treatises. To understand its emergence, let me explain the rise of the "calorific-replenishing" movement (which was partly based on Wang Ji's and Xue Ji's precepts and case records) and the renewed focus on emotions as a possible cause of madness (which also rested on some of Wang Ji's texts and ideas).

## PDF第243页

### 6.3 The rise of “calorific replenishing” (wenbu 溫補)

As I mentioned in the introduction, calorific replenishing contributed to the emergence of a type of madness (dian) that treatise writers consistently attributed to depletion after the sixteenth century. Calorific replenishing referred to the use of warming drugs to treat depletion Fire (虚火). $ ^{54} $ Warm-replenishing challenged two kinds of therapies: attacking cures and the use of frigorific (i.e., cooling) drugs to “make Fire descend by nourishing Yin” (滋陰降火). $ ^{55} $ Wenbu’s disagreement with aggressive therapies was largely diagnostic. Calorific-replenishers blamed attackers for prescribing coolants in cases of depletion Fire that they had misdiagnosed as repletion Fire. $ ^{56} $ The quarrel between Wenbu advocates and healers who replenished Yin with coolants was more subtle, because both adopted different therapies to treat what they agreed was one imbalance: depletion Fire. $ ^{57} $ Their therapeutic disagreement was itself based on their competing understandings of vitality

The advocates of calorific replenishing developed reflections on the nature of human vitality and the Yin or Yang nature of primordial Qi; they debated the role of Fire in creating or destroying this vitality, and discussed the best ways to treat depletion Fire. Most rested their claims on cosmological arguments (especially those based on the Book of Changes) and gave cosmological, physiological, and pathological meanings to the notion of Fire. These debates were largely doctrinal, but they also took claims of clinical success as evidence, and they shaped the therapies that physicians on all sides adopted.

## PDF第244页

Historians of Chinese medicine usually neglect to mention that the main foil for proponents of Wenbu was Wang Lun's 王綸 (5.6) early-sixteenth-century construal of Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358; see 4.4) conception of Fire. As we just saw in the section on Wang Ji (retrospectively identified as one of the originators of warm-replenishing), doctrinally minded physicians developed the Wenbu approach largely as a critique of Wang Lun's interpretation of depletion and against Zhu Zhenheng's Yin-replenishing guise, an image they also owed largely to Wang Lun. Those who used warming drugs to remove "depletion Fire" opposed the view of human vitality centered on the notion of Ministerial Fire on which Yin-replenishing was founded.

Zhu Zhenheng had depicted Ministerial Fire as a dangerous inner brazier whose excesses could cause illness and even death, but he had also portrayed this Fire as the root of vitality and even consciousness. Wang Lun (1453-1510) had later argued that Yin should regularly be replenished with drugs that would not feed this perilous Fire. Unlike Zhu, Wang had associated Ministerial Fire with a concrete locus in the body: the right kidney, which he identified as the Gate of Life 命門 (or Vital Gate). $ ^{58} $ In Wang's view, the Gate of Life corresponded with True Fire, and the left Kidney with Water or True Yin. $ ^{59} $ Since Ministerial

## PDF第245页

Fire was almost never depleted and should almost never be replenished (a notion Zhu had advocated), Wang Lun had argued that replenishing should use cooling drugs to help only the left kidney (Water or True Yin), and that replenishing Yin could thus get rid of pathological Fire. For the purpose of “making Fire descend by nourishing Yin” 滋陰降火, Wang had promoted two major recipes: the “Four-ingredient decoction” (from the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service) and Zhu’s “Yin-replenishing pill.” Both contained phellodendron (黄柏) and anemarrhena (知母), two coolants that were said to curb Fire as they replenished Yin.

A large number of sixteenth-century physicians attacked Wang Lun’s arguments. $ ^{60} $ Xue Ji also found that people (including his illness-prone self) could be healed with warming drugs even in circumstances that did not seem to call for their use. He argued that the “Fire of the Gate of Life” produced Earth, the phase associated with the Spleen and Stomach to which Li Gao (1182-1251) had given so much importance. $ ^{61} $ Xue’s precept meant that the Spleen and Stomach could be boosted by nourishing the Fire of the Gate of Life without fear of creating havoc. Xue’s argument marked the beginning of long controversies on the meaning of the “Gate of Life,” which was part of a broader debate on the sources of human vitality and on the best ways to treat illness and to “nourish life” (養生). $ ^{62} $ Major proponents of theories concerning the Gate of Life were Sun Yikui 孫一奎 (1522-1619; 6.6), Zhao

## PDF第246页

Xianke 趙獻可 (early 17 $ ^{th} $ century), Zhang Jiebin 張介賓 (1563-1640; 6.8), and Li Zhongzi 李中梓 (1588-1655; 6.9). All are now recognized as supporters of calorific replenishing.

There were no strong advocates of Yin-replenishing after Wang Lun, yet this therapeutic approach seems to have remained widespread among common practitioners, probably in part because Wang's precepts were easy to apply and because cooling drugs had become closely associated with the influential Danxi approach. Wang's warnings against warming drugs were still taken seriously enough in the late sixteenth century for Sun Yikui to bemoan that despite repeated refutations, physicians of his day still "stuck to their exaggerated leaning for making Fire descend by nourishing Yin" (時師尚膠滋陰降火之偏). $ ^{63} $ Before I discuss Sun's doctrinal positions (including his views on madness) in more detail (6.6), let me turn to what his Huizhou predecessors said about another subject that converged into late-sixteenth-century explanations of madness: emotions.

### 6.4 “Disorders of the Heart” and counter-affect therapy in 16 $ ^{th} $-century Huizhou

New reflections on vitality and the rise of case records were not the only currents that led to the emergence of dian as an autonomous disorder attributed to depletion. One of the components of definitions of dian in the late sixteenth century was that it was occasioned by depletion caused by emotions. Not coincidentally, emotions figured prominently in discussions of diankuang in the writings of sixteenth-century Huizhou physicians.

We saw in 5.5 that the section on madness of the Collected Essentials of Master Danxi's Medical Books (Danxi xiansheng yishu zuanyao 丹溪先生醫書纂要; 1484) included a section on the way emotions could counter other emotions, a theme that Zhang Congzheng 張從正

## PDF第247页

(4.2) had developed in the thirteenth century. Many sixteenth-century writers from Huizhou added to these medical reflections on the therapeutic manipulation of affect. Wang collected relevant passages in a section of the Shishan yian 石山醫案 (1531) called “the five emotions” (五志). Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 proposed his own ruminations in a section called “Heart Wind” (心風), and Wu Kun 吴崑 in a section on “the emotions” (情志). $ ^{64} $ The Huizhou compilers of the Mingyi leian 名醫類案 (1591) collated a section on “diankuang and disorders of the Heart” (顛狂心病; in juan 8). This focus on emotions was part of the increasing attention Huizhou physicians paid to inner harm, a vast category under which Li Gao regrouped illnesses caused by exhaustion, emotions, and scarcity or excess in food and drink. $ ^{65} $

Wang Ji was the first writers since Zhang Congzheng to devote a section of his work to therapy by counter-affect.

It has been written that when the five emotions are excessive, they cause illnesses that cannot be treated by drugs; they must be overcome by [other] emotions.

Formularies past and present have mostly neglected to discuss this, so that when one comes across such illnesses, there are no precedents from which to infer [a method of treatment]. I have therefore sought examples of treatments from past worthies, which I record below for my successors to see. $ ^{66} $

書曰 五志過為病 非藥可治 須以情勝 古今方書多略而不言 遇有此疾

無例可推 因搜求前賢治例 著之於後 以示將來者焉

## PDF第248页

Without naming his source, Wang Ji started by quoting two consecutive passages from Zhang Congzheng's Rumen shiqin 儒門事親 (ca. 1228), one that explained how emotions harmed the viscera, and another that detailed the ways Cold, Heat, and the five emotions could be used to curb one another. $ ^{67} $

The first case Wang cited was also from Zhang’s work: a note about a man surnamed Zhuang who provoked terror (懼) in a patient to placate the excessive joy that had made him ill. Wang reproduced Zhang’s comment that this healer “could reach the Mysterious Gate” (可謂得玄關). $ ^{68} $ Two unattributed cases followed in which anonymous patients (a woman and a man) were treated with seemingly ad hoc techniques that Wang (or his source) inscribed in the mutual conquest system of the five emotions. $ ^{69} $ Wang narrated the next two stories in the first person, which may indicate that they were his own cases, unless he copied them verbatim from other works. $ ^{70} $ The last two cases, placed under the rubric “congealing of Qi” (氣結), were respectively from an unknown source and from Zhu Zhenheng.

## PDF第249页

Emotional etiologies and counter-affect therapy were progressively integrated in the sections on madness of medical treatises. Huizhou was the center from which the writings that made such a rapprochement emanated. Xu Chunfu 徐春甫, an illustrious Huizhou physician, had developed extensive contacts in the capital Beijing during his prolonged stays there. $ ^{71} $ He had read Wang Lun's Mingyi zazhu 明醫雜著 (1502), Yu Tuan's Yixue zhengchuan 醫學正傳 (1515), some of Wang Ji's works (1520s and 1530s), and of course the Huizhou edition of Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (1482). $ ^{72} $ Xu claimed that Heart Wind, an illness he related to madness, was caused by the long-term reverse flow of emotions and by emotional dissatisfaction (七情五志久逆不遂), just as when "Dairen [Zhang Congzheng] said that the Liver plots repeatedly, but the Gallbladder repeatedly fails to make a decision: vexations have nowhere to be redressed and anger nowhere to be vented" (戴人所謂肝屢謀 膽屢不決屈無所伸 怒無所洩). $ ^{73} $

The Heart’s office is to think. [When one thinks] too much, Cardiac Blood runs drier by the day, Splenetic fluid cannot circulate, and mucus confuses the openings of the Heart, as a result of which Heart Wind emerges; most [such cases] belong to depletion. As a method of treatment, one must use the seven emotions to overcome one another, make the five emotions follow the Heart, nourish Blood and dredge

## PDF第250页

mucus, and draw the Spirit back to its lodging, so that, as both the branch [i.e., mucus] and the root [i.e., emotions] are treated, this illness can be cured.

心之官則思 甚則心血日涸 脾液不行 痰迷心竅 則成心風 屬虛者多

治法須以七情相勝 五志遂心 養血豁痰 引神歸舍 標本兼治 此疾可愈

矣

Xu warned that using attacking therapies as in cases of dian or xian would accelerate death by compounding depletion, (致若潛同癲癇攻治 是謂虛虛 而速其死也). $ ^{74} $

Wu Kun 吴崑 (1552-1620) devoted a section to therapy by counter-affect in his Investigations on Medical Recipes (Yifang kao 醫方考, 1584), where he explained it in the language of pian 5 of the Basic Questions (Zhang Congzheng had based his method on pian 39 of the same book). He first cited fuller versions of the first two cases that Zhang Congzheng had mentioned in his original reflections, but he borrowed all the other cases except one from Wang Ji's Shishan yian 石山醫案. In addition to discussing emotions, Xu Chunfu and Wu Kun both struggled to establish a clear distinction between dian and kuang. I explain their struggle in the next section, and show how it led to fellow Huizhou physician Sun Yikui's further attempts at clarifications at the end of the sixteenth century.

### 6.5 The emergence of two prototypes of madness: new trends in nosography

The Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (1 $ ^{st} $ century BCE) had presented dian and kuang as clearly distinct illnesses, the latter as a broadly understood “madness,” and the former as a disorder characterized by seizures and other epileptiform symptoms. Many ancient medical sources had also associated dian with kuang, presenting dian as the Yin counterpart of Yang

## PDF第251页

kuang, for example (3.4). But this complementarity-in-opposition had been obscured by Liu Wansu's attribution of both dian and kuang to inner Heat triggered by emotions, and by discussions of dian and kuang together (and even as one single illness) in many medical books starting in the twelfth century, including in most parts of the Danxi corpus. Sixteenth-century writers who wanted to explain how dian and kuang both differed from each other yet were inter-related were troubled by the number of views about these two illnesses that appeared in print in their time, and by the general conflation between these two illnesses in recent sources.

Many writers strove to define dian and kuang as two types of madness that differed in their symptoms, but to do so they could not borrow from ancient sources, because these sources had never discussed dian as a type of madness. The difficulty of distinguishing dian and kuang on the basis of their symptoms is well illustrated by Xu Chunfu's Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556):

Danxi said: “Dian is Yin, kuang is Yang; dian is much joy, kuang much anger.” He also said: “The doubling of Yin is dian, the doubling of Yang is kuang.” $ ^{75} $ Thus considered, [dian and kuang] are like Yin and Yang or Cold and Heat, as different [from each other] as water is from ash: how can they be treated with the same drugs and even discussed together in the same day? [Danxi] said again: “It is mostly Heat. Dian is when the Spirit does not hold to its lodging, kuang when one is having visions.” $ ^{76} $

丹溪謂 癲屬陰 狂屬陽 癲多喜 狂多怒 又曰 重陰者癲 重陽者狂

由此觀之 則是陰陽寒熱 冰炭不牟 安得概以一藥治之 而何可以同日而

語也 又曰 大概是熱 癲者神不守舍 狂者如有所見

## PDF第252页

Having endorsed the clear difference that an authoritative source had proposed between dian and kuang (“how can they even be discussed in the same day?”), Xu immediately proposed a definition that brought the two illnesses back together: “Put succinctly, the illness dian-kuang is generally caused by the Heart’s being invaded by Fire and by the Spirit’s consequent inability to hold to its lodging” (顛狂之病 總為心火所乘 神不守舍 一言盡矣). This etiology accorded with Liu Wansu’s twelfth-century claim that this Fire was rooted in emotional imbalances. But if both dian and kuang meant “the Spirit’s inability to hold to its lodging,” why had Xu cited the Danxi xinfā’s definition of dian (“the Spirit’s inability to hold to its lodging”) as something that distinguished dian from kuang?

Xu Chunfu wanted to confirm contemporary usage (dian as a type of madness) by citing classical sources, but since classical sources did not treat dian as a type of madness, Xu could only cite canonical passages that discussed dian next to kuang without mentioning dian's epileptiform symptoms. His interpretation was inventive.

Dian 顛 means topmost 至高. $ ^{77} $ By the nature of Fire, flames rise. Just as the Canon [Basic Questions 49] says, “When Yang Qi is at its highest, one is kuang dian 狂顛.”

Kuang is the same kuang as when Confucius speaks of kuang juan 狂搰. $ ^{78} $ The Divine

Pivot Canon [pian 22] says: “When kuang begins to manifest itself, one sleeps little and

does not feel hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd

and wise, or as being highly ranked.” Therefore it is said that those who are kuang are

## PDF第253页

assertive, $ ^{79} $ have great aspirations, $ ^{80} $ and speak exaggerated words. What was said above—that kuang is when one has vision $ ^{81} $—captures this. This is because when Cardiac Fire bursts into a blaze, speech easily becomes vile and one stops distinguishing between kith and kin: this is chaos in Spiritual-clarity. Its being called kuang is probably because the acuteness of the blaze and Yang Qi's being at its highest make the rising flames of Yang Fire ride on the sick person. Therefore he sheds his clothes and climbs heights 登高, going from kuang to dian [顱 = 巔 = the top?]. This is what the sages meant when they named [these illnesses], between which we can simply say that there are differences within similarities.

巔者 至高也 火性炎上 正如經云 陽氣太上則狂巔 狂則孔子所謂狂狷者之狂也 靈樞經曰 狂病始發 少臥不飢 自高賢也 自辯智也 自尊貴也 故曰 狂者進取志大 而大言者也 前謂狂言如有所見 斯得之矣 蓋心火暴熾 言語善惡不避親疏 此神明之亂也 此之所謂狂也 蓋謂火熾之甚 陽氣太上 則病人亦乘陽火之上炎 故棄衣而登高 由狂而又癲 此則聖人命名之義 而有同中之異耳

Xu's seems to have named “climbing heights” as a symptom of dian in order to close the circle back to his definition of dian 巔 as “the top.” $ ^{82} $

In the process of proposing different treatments for dian and kuang, Xu came up with yet another distinction between the two, this time on etiological lines.

Dian is climbing heights and shedding clothes, lifting heavy things and transporting them far: [these] appear because mucus Fire is blazing. To treat it, purgative remedies such as Mucus-roiling pill are indicated. Kuang is regarding oneself as elevated or as

## PDF第254页

shrewd and wise; it is wild words and crazy speech; it is failure of Spiritual brilliance to hold [to its lodging]. $ ^{83} $ To treat it, remedies that pacify the Heart, appease the Spirit, and clear the upper body are indicated, such as Ox-gallstone and cinnabar pill.

登高棄衣 引重致遠者為癲 乃痰火亢熾之而然 治宜降下之劑 滾痰丸之屬是也 自高辯智 妄語狂言 神明失守為狂 治宜鎮心安神清上之劑 牛黃朱砂丸之屬是也

Since Heart-calming and Spirit-appeasing drugs were replenishers, Xu was suggesting that kuang could be caused by depletion, but most of the 14 pills, powders, and decoctions he cited under “Clearing and pacifying remedies” (清鎮諸劑) were purgatives and vomitives. $ ^{84} $ Xu’s understanding of dian and kuang consisted of extrapolations from classical references and citations from the Danxi xinfa, which was itself a hodgepodge. Xu’s argument, a good example of the intertextual composition of medical knowledge, shows that the distinction between dian and kuang was difficult to establish on strong textual foundations.

Wu Kun 吴崑 (1552-1620), another Huizhou physician, also attempted to distinguish between dian and kuang a few decades after Xu Chunfu. Like Xu had done, Wu simply split the usual symptoms of kuang between the two disorders. Desire for symmetry trumped the observation of illness, or even, in this case, cogent textual argument.

Much anger is dian, much joy is kuang. $ ^{85} $ Dian is when the essential spirit does not hold [to its lodging], when speech is disorderly, when one sees and speaks wildly, when one climbs heights and shouts insults. When kuang starts to manifest itself, one sleeps little and rarely feels hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd

## PDF第255页

and wise, or as being highly ranked. One laughs and moves rashly, climbs heights and
sings, sheds clothes and runs.

多怒為癲 多喜為狂 癲者 精神不守 言語錯亂 妄見妄言 登高罵詈是也

狂之始發 少臥少飢 自賢自貴 妄笑妄動 登高而歌 棄衣而走是也

These symptoms clearly overlapped, but determining which illness went with which symptoms mattered little, because Xu claimed that both disorders were caused by Fire and mucus and should be treated with purges. $ ^{86} $ According to Wu, the only etiological difference was that deviant Qi attacked the Liver in dian cases and the Heart in kuang cases. Neither Xu Chunfu nor Wu Kun left case files that would tell us by what term they designated actual mad behavior. It was Sun Yikui, another man from Huizhou and Wu Kun's older contemporary, who proposed the definitions of dian and kuang that would be widely adopted in the following centuries.

### 6.6 Sun Yikui's stabilization of these two prototypes

Sun Yikui 孫一奎 (1522-1619) was from Haiyang county in Huizhou. In the late 1530s, at his father's behest, he served as an apprentice merchant in Taizhou prefecture (Zhejiang), where—he later claimed—a singular man handed him "secret" recipes. $ ^{87} $ Seeing these as a means to act filially toward his sickly father, Sun returned home to announce his

## PDF第256页

intention to devote himself to the healing arts instead of commerce. His father was

reportedly elated, for, as the saying went, if one could not be a good prime minister, one

should become a good physician. $ ^{88} $ Finding the strange man’s recipes stilted and difficult to

use, however, Sun endeavored to look for the true sources of medical knowledge, starting

with the Inner Canon and the Treatise on Cold Injury, as well as “the books of various great

masters” (諸大家載籍). This may have been when Sun studied with Huang Gutan 黃古潭,

one of Wang Ji’s former pupils, but Sun claimed that he studied mostly alone for three years.

As he developed insights, Sun Yikui realized that there were many excellent physicians in this vast world that he could consult. Probably in the early 1540s, $ ^{89} $ he embarked on a long trip that took him to Henan (Peng-Ji 彭蠡), Hunan (Yuan-Xiang 沅湘), Nanjing (Qinhuai 秦淮), Zhejiang (Yue 越), and the large region he called the “Three Wu’s” (san Wu 三吳), all of which were located along the Grand Canal or one of its adjuncts: Suzhou 蘇州 (“Eastern Wu”), Runzhou 潤州 (i.e. Zhenjiang 鎮江 on the Yangzi River east of Nanjing; “Central Wu”), and Huzhou 胡州 (south of Lake Tai 太湖; “Western Wu”). $ ^{90} $

Judging from his case records (which I shall discuss below), Sun practiced medicine chiefly in his native Huizhou 徽州 (which he called Xindu 新都) and in Huzhou 胡州, south of Lake Tai. $ ^{91} $ From Huzhou, he occasionally crossed Lake Tai northwestwards to Yixing 宜興—in Changzhou 常州 prefecture—mostly to treat two notables that he knew

## PDF第257页

there. $ ^{92} $ The Huzhou region, where he sojourned in the 1570s and the late 1590s, was particularly important to Sun. $ ^{93} $ It was Huzhou notables in the 1570s who authored the earliest “prefaces” that Sun eventually appended to his works, and them again who urged him to publish his case records in the 1590s. $ ^{94} $

In 1584, Sun Yikui published two doctrinal works: Pearls of Wisdom from the Crimson Sea (Chishui xuanzhu 赤水玄珠) $ ^{95} $ and Residual [Discussions] on the Purport of Medicine (Yizhi xuyu 醫旨緒餘). $ ^{96} $ In the former, Sun collected passages from medical texts he considered important, and amalgamated these excerpts with his own views. $ ^{97} $ Sun compiled them to

## PDF第258页

satisfy his father's will to see him save lives, to transmit methods to his descendants, and to reduce the fatigue of having to read expansively when he needed to solve a medical issue. $ ^{98} $ The latter goal was common at this time when libraries were not easily accessible, and when extensive travels were sometimes needed to access the texts that held the answers to a physician's interrogations. $ ^{99} $

The short Residual [Discussions] on the Purport of Medicine (Yizhi xuyu 醫旨緒餘)

consisted of critical vignettes on important doctrinal issues: the location and function of the

Gate of Life (命門; see 6.3), the importance of not sticking to set formulas (an important

sign of distinction for elite physicians; 6.1), Wang Lun's warnings against ginseng and

astragalus (5.6), the pathological and vital roles of Ministerial Fire (4.4, 6.3), etc. A later

(1599?) edition of Sun's two works also included a collection of his case files, which was

published at the behest of Huizhou notables who provided the funds necessary for the

cutting and printing because they admired Sun's medical thinking.

Thanks to his years of extensive travels and medical practice, Sun was well read in the rich textual tradition he tried to summarize. The list of 178 medical texts and 92 non-medical writings that he consulted to compile the Pearls of Wisdom included works by Zhang Yuansu (12 $ ^{th} $ century), Liu Wansu (ca. 1120 – ca. 1200), Li Gao (1181-1251), and Wang Haogu (13 $ ^{th} $ century), Liu Chun's (1340-1412) Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學 (1388) and Yuji weiyi

## PDF第259页

玉機微義 (1396), Zhu Zhenheng's Jufang fabui 局方發揮, Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 (both from 1347), $ ^{100} $ and Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄 (1360s?), as well as the entire “Danxi” series. $ ^{101} $ Sun also cited many texts that I have discussed in this or the previous chapter: Lou Ying's Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396, but printed for the first time in 1565), Wang Lun's Mingyi zazhu 明醫雜著 (1502), Han Mao's Yitong 醫通 (1522), Wang Ji's Shishan yian 石山醫案 (1531), $ ^{102} $ Xue Ji's works on external medicine, $ ^{103} $ Xu Chunfu's Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556), Li Chan's Yixue rumen 醫學入門 (1575), and Li Shizhen's Bencao gangmu 本草綱目 (1578; first published in 1596).

Sun Yikui praised Zhu Danxi for his understanding of lixue 理學 and his reliance on the Book of Changes, a book without knowledge of which one could not excel at medicine, $ ^{104} $ but he criticized those who, claiming to follow Danxi, almost automatically “replenished Yin to make Fire descend” (滋陰降火) when they encountered depletion and exhaustion (虛怯), often causing people to lose their voice, have fits of diarrhea, and die. To Sun, those who advocated such therapies (or blamed Danxi for their failures) forgot Danxi’s insistence that one should not hold to set recipes. Sun explained that Zhu had recognized illness in the

## PDF第260页

most truthful way and made the aptest prescriptions; he nonetheless lamented that people followed (宗) the Danxi xinfa just as they followed the Divine Pivot and the Basic Questions. $ ^{105} $

Many sections of Sun Yikui's two works attempted to clarify doctrinal issues. One point he concentrated on was the distinction between dian, kuang, and xian. By the sixteenth century, as most books in print treated dian as madness, it had become difficult to distinguish dian from kuang by citing the Inner Canon, which had discussed dian as an epileptiform illness. $ ^{106} $ And since sixteenth-century texts also tended to conflate dian with kuang, the dian-kuang pair had become a somewhat contrived classificatory device. Because of these difficulties and the textual inconsistencies he identified in the works of his predecessors, Sun Yikui attempted to propose clearer distinctions between dian, kuang, and xian. I will argue that Sun's text was the main source of the prototype of dian as a kind of madness attributed to depletion, associated with emotions, and said to be treatable with replenishing drugs. $ ^{107} $

Sun’s “distinctions between dian, kuang, and xian” (顛狂癇辨) in Residual [Discussions] on the Purport of Medicine were followed by three entries that purported to clarify (明) the meaning of each illness. $ ^{108} $ His overture provides a good summary of the tangled vocabulary he found in the works he was reading. I contextualize Sun’s claims in the footnotes.

## PDF第261页

Some books speak of “diankuang,” $ ^{109} $ some of “dianxian,” $ ^{110} $ some of “Wind xian,” $ ^{111} $ some of “Wind dian,” some of “fright xian,” whereas some place dian and xian in two [separate] sections. There is no set [way of] discussing [these disorders]. This is because those who speak of dian on its own follow the Inner Canon [Basic Questions], whereas those who speak of dian-xian and dian-kuang follow the Divine Pivot. $ ^{112} $

Basically, dian, xian, and kuang are quite unlike one another: they are not the same reality under different names. $ ^{113} $ Although the Divine Pivot puts dian and kuang together in a single section, their forms and symptoms are discussed separately $ ^{114} $ and their acupunctural treatments follow different paths. [Both dian and kuang] are also different from xian. Many books say that “in adults it is called dian, in children, xian.” $ ^{115} $ This is emphatically not the case. Basic Questions [pian 47] calls dian a fetal illness, brought about when the mother is subject to fright during pregnancy. Can we say now that there is no dian for children? $ ^{116} $ Adults often have xian illness, especially women. $ ^{117} $ Therefore I base myself on the text of the canon to split [these] into three entries, so that healers will have somewhere to make distinctions from.

諸書有言癲狂者 有言癲癎者 有言風癎者 有言風癎者 有言驚癎者 有分癲癎為二門者 畧無定論 究其獨言癲者 祖內經也 言癲癎言癲狂者 祖靈樞也 要之 癲 癎 狂大相逕庭 非名殊而實一之謂也 靈樞雖編癲狂為一門 而形症兩具 取治異途 較之於癲又不相侔矣 諸書有云大人為

## PDF第262页

癲 小兒為癎 此亦大不然也 素問謂癲為胎病 自母腹中受驚所致 今乃曰小兒無癲 可乎 癎病大人歷歷有之 婦人尤多 予故據經文分為三目庶治者有所辨別云

Sun’s classification suited contemporary usage, but because the strict distinction between dian and xian was the result of a later semantic shift, he could not find a single canonical citation to support his definition of dian as a type of madness. $ ^{118} $

In Sun Yikui's scheme, dian, which had served as a hinge between xian and kuang (paired with but distinguished from kuang, and likened to xian), became a distinct third illness between them. He reestablished the classical symmetry between dian and kuang by giving it a different content. Like Xu Chunfu in 1556 and Wu Kun in 1584, Sun defined dian by means of symptoms that had been conventionally associated with kuang.

Dian is being kuang and idiotic, singing and laughing, gloomy and tearful, as if one were drunk or stupid. One's words are incoherent and one does not know cleanliness from filth. [This illness] does not ameliorate even with the passage of time. It is popularly called “Heart Wind.” $ ^{119} $ Many people with high ambitions but [whose realizations] do not follow their aspirations suffer from it. $ ^{120} $

夫癲者 或狂或愚或歌或笑或悲或泣 如醉如痴 言語有頭無尾 穢潔不知 積年累月不愈 俗名曰心風 此志願高大而不遂所欲者多有之

This attribution of dian to the failure of high ambitions found ample confirmation in the rising number of failed examination candidates, especially in the developed regions of the

Lower Yangzi, where low quotas failed to accommodate the rising number of participants.

## PDF第263页

Sun's analysis provided good reasons to treat dian-kuang as a bipolar illness category. Sun's definition of dian as caused by depletion and kuang by repletion was adopted in Liu Quande's Kaozheng bingyuan 考證病源 (1599), Wang Kentang's Zhengzhi zhunsheng 證治準繩 (1602; see 6.7), Zhang Sanxi's Yixue linyao 醫學六要 (1609), the Korean work Dong-ui bogam 東醫寶鑑 (1610), Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Mingyi zhi-hang 名醫指掌 (1622), Sun WenYin's 孫文胤 Dantai yu'an 丹臺玉案 (1637), Li Zhongzi's 李中梓 Shanbu yisheng weilun 刪補頤生微論 (1642; see 6.9), Pan Ji's 潘楫 Yideng xuyan 醫燈續焰 (1652), Luo Mei's 羅美 Gujin mingyi buicui 古今名醫薈萃 (1675), Chen Shiduo's 陳士鐸 Shishi milu 石室祕錄 (1689), Zhang Lu's 張璐 Yitong 醫通 (1695), Chen Qi's 陳岐 Yixue chuandeng 醫學傳燈 (1700), Luo Denggao's 駱登高 Yilin yizhi 醫林一致 (1703), Cheng Guopeng's 程國彭 Yixue xinwu 醫學心悟 (1732), and countless other works. From the late sixteenth century on, this new prototype of dian calling for a replenishing treatment co-existed with the routine attribution of insanity to Fire or mucus that demanded an attacking treatment.

Disappointingly, Sun’s only recorded case of madness (dated 1579) simply spoke of diankuang. $ ^{121} $ Sun may have recorded this case before he pondered on the distinctions between dian and kuang (his doctrinal were published in 1584 at earliest), or he may simply have found the distinction clinically irrelevant. Wu Jiyang 吳霽陽, a man of earnest determination (篤志士), suffered from mental fatigue (勞心) occasioned by constant studies. An astrologer (星士) had told him he would pass the examination in 1579, but when came the session Wu was stricken by intermittent fevers that made him too sick to participate. He “became despondent at heart and this turned into madness. During the day, he would mope

## PDF第264页

or sing; he would clap his hands or stamp the ground with his feet [a gesture of grief, restlessness, or frustration]. [When the illness became] severe, he would yell and scold without sparing kin or kith, the prominent or the lowly” (遂心優而成癲狂 日間或悲或歌 或鼓堂 或頓足 甚則罵詈不辯親疏貴賤).

Following the definitions Sun proposed in his doctrinal texts, Wu Qiyang's illness corresponded to dian in some symptoms (the grief and the singing) and to kuang in others (the indiscriminate scolding). Considering that Sun mentioned that the kuang symptoms appeared when the illness became "serious," he seems to have adopted the position Wang Kentang defended in 1602 (6.7), namely, that kuang was simply a more serious form of dian.

Then [Wu's] father's brother invited me to observe him. His face was pale and greenish and his two Inch pulses short and weak. This means that the Cardiac and Pulmonary Spirits were insufficient. When one's ambitions are high but one's achievements do not follow one's desires, $ ^{122} $ there is stasis and congealing that fail to disperse; the bodily fluids thus produce mucus instead of Blood. Besides, there has been excessive attacking of mucus and aggressive therapies. Even less able to get nourishment, the Cardiac Spirit has become disorderly and lacks anything to sustain itself. The Inner Canon says that when the ruler [i.e., the Heart] is not enlightened, the twelve offices are imperiled. Accordingly, the treatment should consist of replenishing and nourishing [in order] to let the Cardiac Spirit return, combined with clearing mucus, which will allow complete success.

乃叔邀予視之 面白而青 脈兩寸短弱 此心肺之神不足 志願高 而不遂其欲 鬱結不舒 津液生痰而不生血 又攻痰克伐太過 心神愈不得養 故昏亂而無所攝持 内經云 主不明 則十二官危 按此 則治宜補養 收敛心神 而兼之清痰 可萬全也

## PDF第265页

Like most of his contemporaries, Sun Yikui attributed cognitive problems to the action of mucus on the Heart, but he warned that attacking the viscous trespasser too violently may further deplete the Spirit, thereby worsening the root cause of the symptoms at hand. The treatment that he designed (“replenishing the Heart and appeasing the Spirit”) targeted mucus, but more fundamentally it aimed to eliminate the depletion that had originally led to the production of mucus. Sun followed no particular recipe. He prescribed six drugs to replenish the Heart and appease the Spirit, $ ^{123} $ three more drugs to clear Fire in the Liver and Gallbladder [tracts] (清肝膽之火), $ ^{124} $ and a large amount of pulverized ingredients shaped into pills, “to regulate” (調理). $ ^{125} $ The case closed with the claim that the patient was cured.

### 6.7 More intertextuality: Wang Kentang on madness

Wang Kentang 王肯堂 (1552-1638) is often credited for having regrouped many illnesses (including dian, kuang, and xian) in a section titled “Spirit and emotions” (神志) in the first volume (1602) of his Plummet and Level of Diagnosis and Treatment (Zhengzhi zhunsheng 證治準繩). Wang’s editorial decision is considered an achievement by scholars who argue that the notion of “Spirit and emotions” covered approximately the same ground as the modern category of “mental illness.” Yet since these disorders of the “Spirit and emotions” were said to supervene on the viscera (especially the Heart), these illnesses did not have to be

## PDF第266页

psychological or mental. $ ^{126} $ Wang's category of sbenzhi thus included disorders such as dissomnia (both insomnia and narcolepsy) and epilepsy (xian). These and many other illnesses from the same category were not attributed to mental processes, had no mental contents, and were not treated by psychological methods.

This section of Wang's text included a long discussion of dian-kuang-xian, the lengthy opening of which (titled "general discussion of dian, kuang, and xian" 癫狂癎總論) was taken almost verbatim from Sun Yikui's work. $ ^{127} $ To Sun's introduction, Wang only added one sentence, which he took from Yuji weiyi 玉機微義 (1396; see 5.4): "The Basic Questions only speak of dian and do not mention xian. In the Divine Pivot, on the other hand, there are names like 'xian convulsions' and 'xian contraversion'" (素問止言癲而不及癎 靈樞乃有癎瘈 癎厥之名). $ ^{128} $ Wang also followed the Zhengzhi yaojue 證治要訣 (1443?; attributed to Dai Sigong 戴思恭) in claiming that dian was a lighter version of kuang. We could thus translate dian in Wang's works as "madness" and kuang as "severe madness," or dian as lesser madness and kuang as mania.

Wang should not be scorned simply because one of his most fêted contributions to Chinese medical doctrine was chiefly a work of cut-and-paste. He was composing texts as everybody else was at the time. His book helps to understand the intertextual history of statements on madness, but Wang should not belong in the center of our historical investigations. Sun Yikui's and Wang Kentang's analyses deserve to be discussed, but not as repositories of definitions, and certainly not prima facie as "clarifications." Their arguments

## PDF第267页

aimed to establish usage, not to trace its historical complexity and vicissitudes. And in this endeavor they succeeded, since most medical treatises from the seventeenth century onwards included the definition of dian as based on depletion that Sun had solidified.

### 6.8 Zhang Jiebin and depletion Fire

Zhang Jiebin's 張介賓 (1563-1640) works arguably marked the culmination of elaborate reflections and controversies on the meaning of replenishing that started with Wang Lun in the early sixteenth century. Zhang's arguments on insanity reveal both the resilience of aggressive treatments of mad behavior and the maturing of replenishing approaches to emotional disorders attributed to depletion. Zhang was from Shaoxing 紹興, a city in northern Zhejiang connected to the Grand Canal. Trained in "Danxi" approaches, he later discarded important aspects of these precepts, especially Zhu Zhenheng's conception of Fire.

A strong proponent of returning to the medical classics, Zhang took forty years to recollate the eclectic Inner Canon into thematic sections, making the work much easier to consult. A later commentator called the result, the Canon Arranged by Categories (Leijing 類經), “an astonishing book” (海內奇書). $ ^{129} $ Jingyue’s Complete Books (Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書; in 64 juan), titled after Zhang’s social name, was an even longer work that regrouped Zhang’s insights on recent medical issues, on all the documented illnesses about which he could find information, and on the action of individual drugs and mixed remedies. Its systematic reflections on human vitality centered on the Gate of Life inscribe Zhang squarely within the

## PDF第268页

“calorific-replenishing” movement, but Zhang went further than his predecessors by inventing his own recipes instead of sticking to the “Six-flavor pill” (六味丸) that Wenbu advocates from Xue Ji 薛己 to Zhao Xianke 趙獻可 had reverently advocated.

As with the other Wenbu thinkers, the guiding thread of Zhang's discussions was the vital and pathological role of Fire. Some of his fiercest attacks targeted Liu Wansu's (12 $ ^{th} $ century) conception of Fire (4.1). $ ^{130} $ He faulted Liu for ignoring depletion Fire and claimed that Liu's Yuanbing shi 原病式 was woven around 176 characters from one chapter of the Inner Canon because Liu was unable to understand "the entire purport of the basic Canon" (本經全旨). $ ^{131} $

Illnesses caused by repletion Fire are indeed fearsome, but those provoked by depletion Fire are especially to be daunted. For repletion Fire, one should use coolants; removing it is not difficult. For depletion Fire, however, cooling drugs are the most counter-indicated. They are deadly if used rashly. Now, considering that people with depletion Fire are today the majority and those with repletion Fire the minority, can we really say that their illnesses are all caused by excesses and discuss them generically as being [caused by] “Fire”?132

夫實火為病固為可畏　而虛火之病尤為可畏　實火固宜寒涼　去之本不難也

虛火最忌寒涼　若妄用之　無不致死　矧今人之虛火者多　實火者少　豈皆

屬有餘之病　顧可概言為火乎

In other words, Zhang alleged that most physicians attacked Fire without bothering to understand whether it was caused by repletion or by depletion.

## PDF第269页

Zhang Jiebin proposed a wide-ranging critique of Zhu Zhenheng's fourteenth-century synthesis and of its later interpretations. Other writers had disagreed with Zhu's "body of Yang surplus and Yin deficiency," but Zhang formulated an explicit alternative position: "Yang is not excessive, and true Yin is deficient" (陽非有餘 真陰不足). Yang Qi was the basis of life, a precept that Zhang justified by quoting the Book of Changes (the same source as Zhu) as well as his own abundant clinical experience. As with most Ming doctors, Zhang's view of vitality was tied to his understanding of pathological Fire: $ ^{133} $

Since Hejian’s [Liu Wansu] doctrine giving priority to Fire started circulating, and since [Zhu] Danxi used cooling and bitter drugs to make a numinous pill to replenish Yin, people have aligned themselves with them. $ ^{134} $ This is all because Heat symptoms are so clear and manifest that people detect them easily, whereas depletion symptoms are concealed and subtle, so that many people fail to recognize them. Depletion Fire and repletion Fire are particularly difficult to differentiate. Who would have thought that illnesses caused by repletion Heat represent no more than 3 or 4 out of 10 cases, whereas afflictions caused by depletion Fire occur in 6 out of 10! $ ^{135} $ Repletion Heat is deviant Fire. Its thriving causes no harm to primordial Qi. [This Fire] can thus be broken by means of bitter and cooling drugs, though these still cannot be used excessively, for if they are, they will inevitably harm primordial Qi. Depletion Fire, on the other hand, is a deficiency of True Yin.

## PDF第270页

自河間主火之說行 而丹溪以苦寒為補陰之神丹 舉世宗之 盡以熱證明顯人多易見 寒證隱微 人或不知 且虚火實火之間 尤為難辨 孰知實熱為病者 十不過三四 而虚火為患者 十嘗有六也 實熱者 邪火也 邪火之盛 元氣本無所傷 故可以苦寒折之 亦不可過劑 過則必傷元氣 虚火者真陰之虧

As was customary in the treatises of his time, Zhang's Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書 contained entries on dian and kuang. In a long section called "dian, kuang, and idiocy" (癲狂痴呆), he reasserted the classical meaning of dian as an epileptiform illness. $ ^{136} $ Thus disagreeing with Sun Yikui and Wang Kentang, who had treated dian as a form of madness, Zhang proposed no counterpart to manic kuang. He also disagreed that dian and kuang were always provoked by Heat: "it is necessary to distinguish between Yin and Yang [manifestations] and to treat [cases] independently" (不可不辨察陰陽 分而治之). $ ^{137} $ Zhang otherwise agreed with Zhu Zhenheng that depletion often caused possession-like symptoms (similar to those of insanity) and that mucus could play an important role in causing madness symptoms.

Zhang identified many possible etiologies of kuang and tied them to appropriate therapies. It is significant that although he abandoned the Danxi model of the human body (and its fear of the power of repletion Fire), Zhang preserved the traditional Fire etiology and purgative therapies of kuang. His insistence on these more conventional causes against the grain of his own leanings toward calorific replenishing shows that the Fire-and-mucus etiology of insanity and the aggressive therapies which it required were still dominant in the

## PDF第271页

seventeenth century, more than one hundred years after the beginning of the calorific

replenishing movement.

Kuang illness is mostly caused by Fire. $ ^{138} $ It is [occasioned] either by pondering leading to loss of mind, by ratiocination causing stasis, vexations with nowhere to extend themselves, or anger that cannot be vented, causing contraversion of Liver and Gall Qi and the heteropathic union of Wood and Fire: this is an Eastern repletion disorder. This heteropathy’s riding on the Heart renders the Spirit unable to hold on to its lodging; when it rides on the Stomach, it makes people violent and strong. Therefore, to treat such [an illness], one should treat Fire as a priority, and then examine whether either mucus or Qi is dominant and treat them in conjunction.

凡狂病多因於火 此或以謀為失志 或以思慮鬱結 屈無所伸 怒無所泄 以致肝膽氣逆 木火合邪 是誠東方實證也 此其邪乘於心 則為神魂不守 邪乘於胃 則為暴橫剛強 故治此者 當以治火為先 而或痰或氣 察其甚而兼治之

The rest of this section, where Zhang recommended specific recipes to treat madness caused by a variety of factors is translated in Appendix 5.

Using Zhang's own classifications, the seventeen recipes he named comprised eleven coolants (seven old and four new), four "attacking" remedies, one recipe from the section on children (the anti-mucus "Dragon-embracing pill"), and only one replenisher (the Double-Yin decoction). In the sentence where he mentioned a therapeutic approach without specifying a recipe, he recommended the vomiting method. Although some remedies Zhang classified as coolants (like the Cinnabar Spirit-appeasing pill) were also replenishers, his emphasis on cooling drugs and attacking methods was still overwhelming.

## PDF第272页

That the most illustrious of the warm-replenishers did not recommend a single warming remedy for kuang may sound surprising, but Zhang's choice of recipes probably came from the way he interpreted dian. Unlike his recent predecessors, who had classified gentler symptoms of madness under dian, Zhang placed similar symptoms in a section on "Depletion and wasting" (虚損) where he attributed emotional disorders to "depletion of true Yin" (the main root of "depletion Fire") and advocated his typical "warm-replenishing" approach. $ ^{139} $ Zhang thus preserved a depletion etiology for odd behavior without making any explicit links between manic kuang and emotional disorders, and without having to stretch the Inner Canon by interpreting dian as a form of madness. Unfortunately Zhang did not leave case files to show how he treated specific instances of emotionally induced behavior.

### 6.9 Li Zhongzi's dual therapies

Yu Zhen 俞震 (b. 1709), the compiler of an influential collection of case files by other physicians, attributed to Li Zhongzi 李中梓 (1588-1655) “the method of the combined application of attacking and replenishing” (攻補兼施之法) to treat madness. $ ^{140} $ Li was from Nanhui 南匯 in Songjiang 松江 Prefecture, south of Shanghai. $ ^{141} $ After becoming a precocious xincai 秀才 at 12 sui in 1599, he failed the triennial provincial examination nine times before the age of 40. He was thus still young when he gave up on an official career to pursue a medical one. Like many Jiangnan physicians, Li traveled to nearby Suzhou and its

## PDF第273页

surrounding towns to seek teachers and develop contacts with local notables, many of whom became his patients. Li published works on “nourishing life” $ ^{142} $ and on the nature of drugs, $ ^{143} $ compiled a typically eclectic treatise on all aspects of medicine, $ ^{144} $ wrote on the essentials of the Inner Canon, $ ^{145} $ and composed a work on Cold Injury. $ ^{146} $ Li hoped that his case files, which he collected into a book, would help to further “the study of tracing origins” (窮源之學), by which he meant tracing each illness to its unique origins by means of skillful diagnosis. $ ^{147} $

Li generally adopted the warm-replenishing approach, including the argument that vitality was rooted in the Gate of Life. Though he attributed most disorders to depletion, he also argued that mucus was present in six to seven out of ten cases of illness. $ ^{148} $ He often emphasized that past masters had used multiple therapeutic approaches, arguing for instance that Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559), who by Li Zhongzi's time had become famous for his replenishing treatments, did not replenish all the time, and that Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228), who was known for his aggressive therapies, had not exclusively used violent treatments. Xue's and Zhang's practice was more variegated than could be seen from their case records, which Li argued they had compiled to support doctrinal points. Li Zhongzi also lamented physicians' habit of following only one of the “four masters” (which he identified as Zhang Ji [3 $ ^{rd} $ century], Liu Wansu [12 $ ^{th} $ century], Li Gao [1151-1181], and Zhu

## PDF第274页

Zhenheng [1282-1358]) for creating biases in the treatment of illness. In good zbezhong fashion (see 5.1), Li advocated the use of the best methods from each physician.

The consequences of Li Zhongzi's interpretations appeared clearly in his recorded cases of madness. A student called Zhu Wenzai 朱文栽 was in pain and suffered from purulent mouth sores. $ ^{149} $ Since two ghosts appeared to him every morning, he worried that he would soon die, leaving his aging parents without a caretaker. His changeable inch-pulses (“now large, then small”乍大乍小) suggested this was a case of “demonic affliction” (鬼祟). $ ^{150} $ Since the strung, slippery, and large (弦滑且大) medial-pulse indicated the presence of mucus, Li first prescribed the “Mucus-roiling pill” (4.5), but its purgative effect proved too weak to fight off the specters. Li then tried the “Lesser Stomach-elixir” 小胃丹, an anti-mucus he probably borrowed from Danxi xinfu. $ ^{151} $ The apparitions dissipated after the patient discharged a dozen bowls of accumulated “mucus and water.” Li enhanced the same recipe with ginseng and atractylodes, two warm-replenishing drugs, and used this mixture to purge the patient more than ten times. For weeks in the aftermath, Li replenished him with the “Six gentlemen” (六君子), a mix of atractylodes, ginseng, China-root, licorice, tangerine peel (橘紅), and pinellia (半夏) that was usually served as a decoction, but that Li prescribed as pills. The Six gentlemen contained the four ingredients of the calorific Four-gentlemen decoction plus two anti-mucus ingredients from the “Two-aged[-ingredients] decoction.”

## PDF第275页

The “combined application of attacking and replenishing” had been praised in general terms since at least Zhu Zhenheng, but not explicitly recommended for the treatment of madness. $ ^{152} $ Li Zhongzi created a dual therapy for insanity by combining the most emblematic (and dramatic!) elements of the purging and the replenishing approaches: generic anti-mucus recipes (the Mucus-roiling pill, Lesser Stomach-elixir, and Two-aged-ingredients decoction) and two calorific replenishers—attractylodes and ginseng.

Although Li Zhongzi's case appeared in the section on mucus of his Required Readings on the Pedigree of Medicine (and later in the section on "demonic infestation" 鬼注 of Yu Zhen's Gujin yian an 古今醫案按 [1778]), it matched the way Li described therapies for madness in his other writings. In his work on Cold Injury, he explained that fits of madness "will not stop unless [the patient] is massively purged" (非大下之不能已也). $ ^{153} $ Another book supposedly authored by Li attributed both dian and kuang to mucus and advocated the use of emetics and purgatives, but insisted that depleted patients should first be replenished with "Will-calming paste" (寧志膏) and drugs like ginseng, jujubes (棗仁), cinnabar (辰砂), or frankincense, to be taken with White-honey pill (白蜜丸) or Mint decoction (薄荷湯). $ ^{154} $

## PDF第276页

Li also discussed an apprehensive scholar named Zhang Fangzhi who had a fit of insanity after he had been suddenly frightened following a long period of sorrow (久憂暴驚遂發癲妄). $ ^{155} $ He was given “drugs to replenish the Heart and Spirit” (補心神藥) and other remedies “to chase mucus and saliva” (逐痰涎藥), but to no avail. $ ^{156} $ After determining that his “six pulses were knotty and strong” (六脈結而有力), Li announced that no cure would take place unless mucus was massively “brought down” (非大下其痰 無由痊也), that is, purged. As he did with the patient who had been harassed by apparitions, Li prescribed both replenishers and anti-mucus drugs: “First take Will-calming paste for three days, and then purge with the Lesser Stomach Elixir” (先服寧志膏三日 遂以小胃丹下之). Only after the patient took the Elixir a number of times over three months was “mucus accumulation” (痰積) eliminated. $ ^{157} $ Li replenished not only as a therapy, but to help the patient prepare for—or recover from—the attacking drugs that would chase mucus.
