His approach stood out because he prescribed replenishing drugs before or during purges instead of afterwards.

It is probably no coincidence that Li Zhongzi's use of a dual method emerged when physicians (including Li himself) were emphasizing the fragility of human vitality and the danger of aggressive cures. It is also likely that replenishing treatments were easier for some

## PDF第277页

elite patients to accept than violent purges. $ ^{158} $ In 7.3, I shall discuss the possible decline of induced vomiting (one particularly disagreeable form of aggressive therapy) in the elite medicine of the Qing dynasty, as well as the continued use of cheap attacking remedies among local physicians well into the nineteenth century.

### 6.10 Conclusion

By the early seventeenth century, most medical treatises defined dian and kuang as two kinds of madness, attributing dian to depletion and kuang to over-repletion. Zhang Jiebin begged to differ, but current usage prevailed over textual correctness. This etiological distinction gave a new content to the traditional dian-kuang pair and corresponded with two rival ways of treating mad behavior: the dominant attacking therapies and the more occasional calorific replenishing, which doctors used chiefly when they attributed mad behavior to an emotional imbalance. $ ^{159} $ The plausibility of this therapeutic division was reinforced by physicians' claims to success with both approaches, whereas the frequent failures of both approaches allowed promoters of either therapy to criticize their rivals at will. The distinction between these two prototypes of madness, however, was a division of nosographic labor; it does not seem to have guided physicians' interpretation of real episodes of illness. Indeed, healers tended to disregard these distinctions when they faced concrete cases. Even Sun Yikui, who affirmed this division in his doctrinal writings, did not abide by it in his only recorded case of madness.

I also tried to show that although madness was rarely a controversial illness, medical discussions of madness quickly absorbed doctrinal developments. I explained how the

## PDF第278页

bipolar etiologies and treatments of dian and kuang crystallized in the late sixteenth century because of broader changes in medical doctrine and practice: increased competition between physicians, the rise to dominance of the notion that each instance of illness was unique, the increasing use of the case-file form to record exceptional cases, the emergence of the calorific-replenishing approach to treat depletion Fire, and the interpretation of emotional disorders as a kind of “inner harm” treatable with replenishing methods. What started as distinctive diagnoses by a few physicians (6.2) could thus become, under the name of dian, one of two prototypes of madness. These medical trends did not cause the new classification to appear, but they made it sound naturally plausible.

Building on a theme I introduced in chapter 5, I kept calling attention to the intertextual dynamics that generated medical knowledge. Medical writers formed a discursive community, partly through personal connections, but mostly through their common references, at the center of which was the Danxi corpus and works that discussed it. Sixteenth-century medical debates became denser because physicians had access to a large number of printed medical works. This multitude of texts also encouraged doctors to publish their own arguments in a growing debate on human vitality, patho-mechanisms, and the appropriate way of curbing the latter without harming the former.

Madness came to be discussed more intensively in the sixteenth century, not because it was somehow rediscovered, but because of a process of textual densification that started to accelerate in the late fifteenth century after the publication of the Danxi xinfa and its motley section on madness. Because excerpting other texts had become a highly valued way of composing medical treatises, because an increasing number of printed books (many of which included case records) circulated in the sixteenth century (thus augmenting the number of possible sources for intertextual compilation), and because the elite physicians

## PDF第279页

who composed these treatises believed that any illness could be caused by a number of processes, most illnesses came to be described in more detail than ever before. $ ^{160} $ As we shall see in the following chapters, this process of textual proliferation and densification continued in the following centuries.

Another issue I have emphasized in this chapter is how the therapies described in medical case files could diverge from the approaches found in general treatises. One of the points of publishing case records was indeed to demonstrate that a physician could succeed in curing patients even if he adopted methods that differed from those proposed in treatises. The two genres of syncretistic treatises and collections of medical case files are often presented as diametrically opposed because the former served as repertoires of medical precepts and methods and presented mostly nosographic prototypes, whereas the latter recounted healers' success at treating unique cases of illness.

In the sixteenth century, however, both case collections and general treatises exploited the notion that illnesses were ever-changing. Treatises were written like anthologies because physicians recognized that illnesses had several possible causes. More than most people suspect, the prototypical scripts contained in these florilegia often found their way into clinical practice—as was their purpose, after all. By the same token, when the case files of contrarians like Wang Ji (6.2) circulated, the interpretations they proposed could be adopted by later physicians and themselves become prototypical scripts in future treatises. $ ^{161} $

The notion that every illness instance was unique could potentially have made illness names irrelevant, yet most physicians kept using terms like dian or kuang to refer to the

## PDF第280页

pathological entities they were treating, because these names referred to well-known clusters of symptoms that did not imply predetermined etiologies. $ ^{162} $ The notion that illnesses could have multiple causes, and the clinical flexibility allowed by this multiple scripting explain why deviations from prototypes did not incite Chinese physicians to create new “types” of madness every time they encountered a peculiar case. Indeed, their concept of illness agreed in advance that actual disorders would deviate from prototypical forms. $ ^{163} $

Despite the notion that a disorder could be caused by almost any imbalance, there were a few well-defined approaches to any illness. Each illness was thought to be occasioned by a limited number of prototypical causes. $ ^{164} $ The records of common physicians show that therapy usually targeted these illnesses with a few basic remedies adapted to a few illness scripts. In clinical practice, the pulse allowed physicians to detect sets of causes and to map them onto the bodies of patients. Thinking of diagnosis as a choice between several plausible scripts (and sometimes as a blend between approaches that treatises presented as distinct) helps to understand the challenges of clinical practice without idealizing it as an entirely new reaction to every symptom encountered, and helps to relativize case files, which were often designed to highlight the uniqueness of a physician’s approach to every case he faced.

## PDF第281页

## Shapes of practice

Medical trends surrounding dian and kuang, 17th-19th century

The profusion of medical works that appeared after 1650 are an irritating windfall. As medical publications boomed, vectors of knowledge transmission and the doctrinal debates that interested physicians became more intricate and numerous. It thus becomes difficult to find narrative threads to explain how Qing medical reflections on madness changed. Far too many texts were published for me to base my conclusions on comprehensive data as I tried to do for earlier periods. But the extensive array of short treatises, collections of case files, formularies, and medical primers that appeared in Qing times hint to the thinking and practice of minor physicians who made no “contributions to the development of Chinese medicine”—to borrow a phrase from a less critical historiography. These works allow us to see how much medical luminaries had in common with their less famous counterparts.

I first present a few important trends in Chinese medical thinking and practice from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. After showing that the mucus etiology remained dominant over that period, I show how resilient and ubiquitous the script of blockage of the Heart by mucus was. I map the vivid “imagination of mucus” $ ^{1} $ through which physicians—from treatise writers to village healers—continued to envision madness from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. I then turn to a pivotal figure in eighteenth-century medicine—Ye Gui 葉桂 (1667-1746)—and to what an influential work attributed to him said about

## PDF第282页

madness. Next, I explain how nineteenth-century physicians (a number of whom proclaimed their admiration for Ye Gui) turned the Liver into a new pathological nexus and eventually claimed that madness and emotional disorders were rooted in Liver imbalances. Then I outline how Qing medical writers increasingly distinguished dian from kuang by symptoms instead of etiology, and how this displacement eventually provoked another transformation in the meaning of dian. The last section concerns a physician called Wu Tang 吳瑭 (1758-1836), another admirer of Ye's approaches, who claimed to have cured several mad patients. I translate and analyze one of his cases at some length, focusing on the dynamics of therapy and the efficacy of the drug-based treatments he deployed.

This chapter is the last of six chronological chapters about medical approaches to madness. It shows what approaches Chinese physicians adopted on the eve of the state's intervention in redefining "Chinese medicine" in the twentieth century, and explains what conceptions and treatments were contemporaneous with the laws on "killing because of madness" whose emergence I explain in Chapter 11.

### 7.1 Qing medical trends

Danxi and the other "masters"

By the early seventeenth century, “Danxi”—the label by which doctors referred to precepts and methods they attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358; 4.4)—was no longer the awe-inspiring name it had been a hundred years earlier, and the “Danxi corpus” no longer shaped medical debates. $ ^{2} $ Physicians gave less credence to Danxi’s views of vitality.

## PDF第283页

which they summarized as “Yang is in excess, Yin in deficit” (陽有餘陰不足). Zhu’s reflections on vitality had been criticized by advocates of “calorific replenishing” (6.3), but even those who opposed warm-replenishing therapies did not return to Zhu’s conceptions.

Danxi precepts had become popular partly thanks to the easy availability of user-friendly treatises like the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (1482; see 5.5). In the seventeenth century, however, new primers like Gong Tingxian's 龔廷賢 compilations (late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries) and the works of Li Zhongzi 李中梓 (1588-1655) started to displace Danxi works. They remained popular into the nineteenth century (see 7.4).

Just as the works of Danxi and other masters from the twelfth to fourteenth centuries stopped being reprinted, Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Essentials of the Golden Casket (third century) became the main focus of medical studies. One trace of the shift in prominence from Danxi toward Zhang Ji was the severance of Zhang from a group of specialists with which Ming physicians had associated him. Wang Lun 王綸 (1463-1510) had coined the phrase “the four masters” (四子) to refer to Zhang Ji 張機 (d. ca. AD 220), Liu Wansu 劉完素 (ca. 1120-1200), Li Gao 李昇 (1181-1256), and Zhu Zhenheng, who Wang claimed had each perfected therapies for one kind of disorder. $ ^{3} $ Li Zhongzi (1588-

## PDF第284页

1655) later called the same men the “four great experts” (四大家). $ ^{4} $ It was probably Suzhou doctor Zhang Lu 張璐 (1617-1699) who first wrote of “the four great experts of the Jin and Yuan” (金元四大家), a group in which he included Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) instead of Zhang Ji, observing that Zhang Ji should not be placed among doctors he had influenced. Zhang Lu saw the doctrines of these “four masters” as considerably inferior to Zhang Ji’s. Many surveys of Chinese medical history note that Qing doctors studied the Treatise on Cold Injury intensely, $ ^{5} $ but they are usually silent about the shift away from Jin-Yuan medicine that this new concentration on Zhang Ji implied.

For Zhang Ji this new autonomy was an astonishing promotion, since his works were now discussed on a par with the Inner Canon, which for centuries had been treated as the ultimate source of medical knowledge. $ ^{6} $ In a 1732 preface to one of You Yi's 尤怡 (d. 1749) many works on Zhang Ji's medicine, Xu Dachun 徐大椿 (1693-1771) ridiculed the common concept that Zhang had specialized only in the treatment of Cold Injury and that he could be put on the same (low) footing as Liu Wansu, Li Gao, and Zhu Zhenheng. $ ^{7} $ Xu developed this idea a few years later in a pugnacious assessment of the “four great experts” in his Treatise on the Origin and Currents of Medical Studies (Yixue yuanliu lun 醫學源流論; 1757). $ ^{8} $ Chen

## PDF第285页

Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823) also adopted Zhang Lu's new interpretation of the “four

masters” in his Three-Character Classic of Medicine (Yixue san zì jìng 醫學三字經; 1804), pointing

out that Li Zhongzi's interpretation of them as including Zhang Ji was “erroneous” (誤). $ ^{9} $

## Other Qing trends

Qing Suzhou was an important center of medical studies supported by a prosperous printing industry and mature scholarly networks. As one of the main cities on the Grand Canal, it had long been an important hub of trade and communication, and many doctors had traveled there to find books and exchange ideas. But in Qing times Suzhou physicians claimed to represent the most advanced medical approaches in the empire. Although this pride predated the eighteenth century, it became particularly evident after the publication in 1766 of some of Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746; see 7.5 below) case files by men who claimed to be his disciples. I discuss Suzhou doctors' approach to madness in the next chapter. $ ^{10} $

One trend in the eighteenth and especially the nineteenth century was the rising number of case collections in the surviving medical literature. Many of them were never printed. They allow us to continue comparing the propositions found in treatises with the way physicians actually handled mad patients. Through them, we can also understand better how women were treated when they behaved in ways that were identified as mad (see Ch. 9).

Many practices I described in previous chapters persisted into the Qing period.

Competition between physicians and rivalry between therapies continued unabated and may even have sharpened as doctors grew in numbers. As some kept replenishing their patients

## PDF第286页

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $

Medical writing also remained profoundly intertextual. Several treatises from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries included long and increasingly complex sections on madness. They were longer and denser partly because they could borrow from a larger number of texts, but also because they applied to more disorders than ever the old claim that any illness could be caused by multiple factors. Two representative examples are Feng Zhaozhang's 馮兆張 (from Haiyan 海鹽 in Zhejiang) Jinnang milu zazheng daxiao becan 錦囊祕錄雜證大小合參 (in 20 juan; 1694) and Shen Jin'ao's 沈金鰲 (1717-1776; from Wuxi 無錫 in Jiangsu) Zabing yuanliu xizhu 雜病源流犀燭 (in 30 juan; 1773), both of which concentrated on explaining miscellaneous illnesses.

Besides these exhaustive accounts of manifold etiologies, we find attempts to simplify—even unify—medical doctrines, books with titles like Unity in the Forest of Medicine (Luo Denggao's 駱登高 Yilin yizhi 醫林一致; 1703), Knowing Medicine in One Glance (Zhu Shijin's 朱時進 Yijian nengyi 一見能醫; 1769), and Medicine for Sots (Long Huitang's 龍繪堂 Chunzi yi 蠢子醫; 1882). Their authors tried to distinguish these handbooks from difficult treatises such as those I mentioned in the previous paragraph. Let us now turn more directly to madness, starting with the way Qing physicians defined kuang in relation to dian.

## PDF第287页

### 7.2 The continued dominance of the mucus etiology

In earlier chapters, I examined the rise of mucus in Chinese medical thinking and its eventual use in explanations of madness. I also showed how the Danxi corpus had given pride of place to the mucus etiology in general. On the basis of these now mature theories, seventeenth-century physicians continued to discuss how mucus emerged, what disorders it caused, and how these illnesses should be treated. Some did so in special sections on this viscous intruder, others in commentaries on the notion of “mucus and rheum” (痰飲; 3.5) in Zhang Ji’s Essentials of the Golden Casket (3 $ ^{rd} $ century CE).

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

## PDF第288页

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $

This perceived ubiquity of mucus generated more explanations of its pathological origins. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $

## PDF第289页

### 7.3 Shapes of practice: the scope of mucus therapies of madness

The above section showed that mucus played an important role in Qing medical discussions of many illnesses. Starting with an examination of seventeenth-century medical primers that were popular under the Qing, let me now demonstrate from many different angles that mucus conceptions also dominated the way doctors thought about insanity.

Since the practice of medicine was not regulated, virtually anyone could take it up. All that was needed was a medical primer and a few patients willing to pay for a cure. Physicians who sought a more elite clientele usually needed to claim a teacher and to be able to couch their arguments in classical terms drawn from the medical canons or the Yijing, but the success of medical primers suggests that medicine was easily learned outside of teacher-student relations. The large number of surviving editions of books like Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌) or Gong Tingxian's Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, and the fact that other books lamented their excessive influence, suggest that they guided the practice of more physicians than the treatises modern historians rely on in their studies of Chinese medical thinking. $ ^{21} $

## Shapers of practice? What seventeenth-century medical manuals said about madness

There is no lack of people within and without the four seas, but how are the children and elderly of the eight directions to be saved if only one or two widely read people with a good knowledge [of medicine] can be found within a thousand li? One may say that there are medical texts, but the number of books

## PDF第290页

# is limitless and the impoverished roam the markets; $ ^{22} $ [moreover] the meanings and principles [expressed in these books] are profound and subtle, so that the ignorant have failed to examine them.

薄海内外 雅不乏人 而千里之間 博綜淹貫者 僅得一二 何能濟八方之老幼 即曰醫籍在也 卷帙浩淼 貧者弗遑市 意理淵微 愚者未及察

Xu Shirou 許士柔, 1622 preface to Shao Da's 邵達 re-edition of Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌 $ ^{23} $

As its title indicates, the Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhi-bang 明醫指掌, 1579), which was re-edited and augmented in 1622, purported to be a digest of authentic medical doctrines and techniques. $ ^{24} $ Compiled in a format that put the way of medicine “in front of one’s eyes” (所謂道在目前; Xu preface), this manual sought to remedy the two problems Xu lamented in his preface: the dispersion of knowledge over too many books, and the difficulty of mastering these convoluted doctrines. In the Enlightened Physician, short and memorable “songs” (ge 歌) on individual illnesses were followed by discussions (lun 論), information on the pulses (mai 脈) most characteristic of these illnesses, and the recipes for recommended formulas.

We owe the book's design to a man named Shao Da 邵達, a Suzhou physician whose father had also practiced medicine. $ ^{25} $ Shao recounted how his father had enjoined him

## PDF第291页

to study Huangfu Zhong's original Enlightened Physician (1579) in order to understand Zhang Ji's Cold-Injury medicine, Li Gao's notion of inner harm, Liu Wansu's Heat disorders, and Zhu Zhenheng's miscellaneous illnesses. $ ^{26} $ As Shao recalled, he learned as much as he could from Huangfu's work, but deplored that it relied on illustrations (圖) at the expense of discussions (論), described the symptoms clearly but said little about pulses, and gave detailed recipes but neglected to explain the therapeutic methods that justified this choice of remedies. $ ^{27} $ Shao also found inconvenient that the recipes appeared in a volume separate from the descriptions and illustrations. $ ^{28} $ In his 1622 edition, Shao eliminated the pictures, regrouped the descriptions of illnesses with the recommended recipes, and added distinctions between the many possible origins of every illness he discussed.

Shao Da's version of the Enlightened Physician (in 10 juan) was republished many times throughout the Qing, testifying to its popularity among practitioners, as well as among amateurs who sought a digest of medical knowledge for use at home. A catalog of medical books extant in Chinese libraries indicates that at least 19 Qing editions of Shao Da's work have survived: 2 from the seventeenth century after 1644, 4 from the eighteenth, 10 from the nineteenth, 2 from the early twentieth, and one from an unknown date. $ ^{29} $

The Enlightened Physician contained a section on dian and kuang on which its readers may have relied to understand insanity. It started with a mnemonic song:

Rambling, singing, running, suddenly jumping walls,

譫語歌走忽逾墻

## PDF第292页

Blithely calling oneself venerable: this is called kuang. 妄自稱尊號曰狂

Dian is a stiff body and an unhappy mind. 癲者身僵心不樂

You should know these two illnesses are Yin and Yang. 须知二證有陰陽

These verses squeezed together the symptoms of dian and kuang as explained in the Basic

Questions and the Canon of Problems (problem 59). More focused on current usage, the

discussion that followed cited the same passages at more length before quoting an excerpt

from the "diankuang" section of the Danxi xinfa and the Canon of Problems' claim that dian

was the doubling of Yin and kuang the doubling of Yang. $ ^{30} $ Two sections on the respective

symptoms of dian and kuang cited more of the Danxi xinfa, but added two recipes to those

that this book had recommended. $ ^{31} $

To this unsystematic rehashing of Danxi medicine, Shao Da added “notes” (an 按) where he attributed madness to the pathological action of Fire and mucus.

These two illnesses [dian and kuang] necessarily arise from something. It can be great anger stirring Hepatic Wind or great fright stirring Cardiac Fire, making Fire rise suddenly. When [Fire] arises but fails to descend, it obstructs the openings of the Heart and makes Spiritual-clarity $ ^{32} $ unable to circulate in and out; the sovereign loses his [power to] issue commands and the Heart is instead conscripted by mucus; [this process] immediately [makes the illness] manifest itself.

## PDF第293页

前二證 必有所因 或因大怒動其肝風 或因大驚動其心火 卒為火升 升而不降 壅塞心竅 神明不得出入 主宰失其號令 心反為痰所役 一時發越

<div style="text-align: center;">This story of Fire and mucus was another variant of the familiar process by which the upward movement of an inner fluid was thought to hinder the functioning of the Heart. Shao Da then explained how the classical symptoms of kuang were produced.</div>


If one scales walls and climbs on roofs, tries to kill people with a knife, strips naked and shouts around without discrimination between kin and kith, scampers around rapidly, $ ^{33} $ and walks in water as if it were soil; it is because Hepatic Qi is overly dominant and Wood comes to straddle the Heart. This is called knang or “severe dian.” $ ^{34} $ The method is to repress the Liver and pacify the Heart, chiefly with the “Elixir that brings down the dragon.” $ ^{35} $

若逾垣上屋 持刀殺人 裸體罵詈 不避親疏 飛奔疾走 涉水如陸者 此肝氣太旺 木來乘心 名之曰狂 又謂之大顛 法當扣肝鎮心 降龍丹主之

Shao Da's attribution of manic symptoms to Liver Qi was not typical of his time, but it made good sense because the hepatic emotion was anger, which had been explicitly associated with kuang since at least the eighth century. $ ^{36} $ I show below (7.6) that the Liver played an increasingly central role in medical explanations of emotions and madness in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

Shao Da reiterated the common association of dian with Joy, but described the symptoms with words that did not sound borrowed from other texts.

## PDF第294页

One claps hands with joy and laughs out loud, speech is incoherent, and one looks left and right as if seeing ghosts and spirits, but after a short time one's correct nature is again manifest and one feels deeply ashamed. Then, after a short moment, one's condition returns to how it was before: this is stubborn mucus from the upper chest $ ^{37} $ that is overflowing and brimming over, blocking the pathways, so that the Heart is hindered by it. When the mucus descends slightly, one's correct nature is again manifest, but when mucus rises again, [the symptoms] flare up again. $ ^{38} $ This is called dian. The method is to free the Lungs and appease the Heart, chiefly with the "Heart-appeasing mucus-roiling pill."

若撫掌大笑 語言不倫 左顧右盼 如見鬼神 片時正性復明 深為赧悔

少傾而狀態如故者 此為上膈頑痰氾濫洋溢 塞其道路 心為之礙 痰少降

則正性復明 痰復升 則又發 名之曰癲 法當利肺安心 安神滾痰丸主之

The mucus-roiling pill, a mixture of replenishing and purgative drugs that had been designed by Wang Gui 王珪 in the fourteenth century (4.5), but the pill had long become a run-of-the-mill remedy for mucus. "Freeing the Lungs" meant getting rid of the chest-congesting mucus whose "overflowing" was thought to cause the periodical symptoms of dian.

Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) was another Ming writer whose books

circulated widely during the Qing. $ ^{39} $ Although he originated from Nanchang 南昌 in Jiangxi

province, where he learned medicine from his father Gong Xin 龔信, Gong spent much of

his adult life in the Suzhou region and in Nanjing. Gong is not remembered as a doctrinal

## PDF第295页

innovator, but his widely read works probably shaped the practice of many physicians from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. At least seventeen Ming and Qing editions of his Wanbing buichun 萬病回春 (first ed.: 1587) have survived in Chinese libraries, whereas no fewer than 66 editions of his Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元 (first ed.: 1615) were made in China between 1667 and 1911. $ ^{40} $ Wanbing buichun was one of the books Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823) accused his contemporaries of using as “shortcuts” (捷徑) to medical practice, $ ^{41} $ and Shoushi baoyuan one of the texts Wu Tang 吳瑭 (1758-1836) blamed his colleagues for reading instead of the Inner Canon and the works of Zhang Ji. $ ^{42} $ Both of Gong’s works contained a version of Gong’s “Songs on the Nature of Drugs” (Yaoxing ge 藥性歌), a text that Shao Da also included in his 1622 edition of the Mingyi zhizbang 明醫指掌. $ ^{43} $

In both Wanbing buichun and Shoushi baoyuan, sections on specific illnesses opened with a description of their characteristic pulses. They then presented a mix of classical citations and common knowledge on these illnesses, including long and detailed sections on madness. In Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, Gong cited the symptoms of kuang in the formulaic language of the classics—naked running, climbing heights, etc.—and attributed these symptoms (variably) to “over flourishing of Yang Qi” (陽氣太盛), “repletion Heat in the Stomach and the Large Intestines” (胃與大腸實熱) and “Dry Heat in stasis in the center”

## PDF第296页

(燥熱鬱結於中). $ ^{44} $ He then cited (without naming his source) the passage from Yu Tuan's 虞搏 Yixue zhengchuan 醫學正傳 (1515) that rejected the Canon of Problems' explanation of dian as doubling of Yin and kuang as doubling of Yang, as well as Yu Tuan's attribution of dian, kuang, and xian to Fire and mucus (5.7). Finally Gong recommended treating xian with emetics and kuang with purgatives.

The rest of the section consisted in short discussions interspersed with the recipes of the remedies Gong endorsed. Most discussions mentioned the action of mucus on the Heart, and the vast majority of the recipes were designed to counter mucus and Fire: the purgative “Heart-clearing mucus-roiling pill” (清心滚痰丸), said among other things to treat “all strange symptoms” (一切怪證); “Avert-Wind tongsheng powder” (防風通聖散), an attacking remedy popularized by Liu Wansu and Zhang Congzheng; “Shrubby-sophora pill” (苦參散), a vomitive recommended in the Danxi xinfa; the mineral-based “Gall-embracing pill” (抱膽丸), an anti-xian purgative that was presented here as targeting “all dianxian seizures and Wind kuang” 一切癲癇風狂); “White-gold pill” (白金丸), a Song-dynasty emetic made with alum and turmeric that “opens static mucus” (開鬱痰); “Melon-pedicle powder” (the main emetic from the Treatise on Cold Injury); the “Wind-chasing and mucus-transforming decoction” (驅風化痰湯); and the “Decoction of the General” (將軍湯, another name for Wu Kun’s 吳崑 “One-ingredient decoction,” a purgative entirely made of rhubarb; 6.5). Gong Tingxian also cited at length Wu Kun’s discussion of the need to purge mad people and not to rush to replenish them. Gong presented a few remedies that could replenish or

## PDF第297页

appease the Heart in cases of mad behavior caused by depletion, $ ^{46} $ but almost all the

remedies he recommended were attacking drugs designed to dislodge Fire and mucus, which

he saw as the two main sources of insane behavior.

Li Zhongzi's 李中梓 (1588-1655; 6.9) works were also common guides for medical practice in the Qing period. $ ^{47} $ Fifty-one editions of his Required Readings on the Pedigree of Medicine (Yizong bidu 醫宗必讀; 1637) have survived in Chinese libraries, as well as twenty-six different editions of Three Books by Shicai (Shicai sanshu 士材三書), which Li's Suzhou disciple You Cheng 尤乘 published 12 years after his master's death. $ ^{48} $ Three Books by Shicai was one of the works Wu Tang 吳瑭 (1758-1826) claimed his contemporaries read when they studied medicine, $ ^{49} $ whereas a contemporary of Wu Tang commented that although Li's works "may be called shallow and rough, they hold to the constants: they are something that beginners do not dispense with" (雖曰淺率 却是守常 初學者所不廢也). $ ^{50} $

Required Readings on the Pedigree of Medicine said little directly about madness," but the Sand Seal on the Mechanisms of Illness (Bingji shazbuan 病機沙篆; 1667), one of the Three Books by Shicai, contained a detailed section on dian and kuang $ ^{52} $. The book copied the descriptions of

## PDF第298页

dian and kuang found in Wang Kentang's Zhengzhi zhunsheng 證治準繩 (1602; see 6.7), but proposed a new distinction: "dian is of the Yang viscera: mucus is in the [Heart's] Envelope tract, therefore it flares up and stops intermittently; kuang is of the Yin viscera: mucus enters the Heart, therefore when it flares up it does not stop" (顛屬腑 痰在包絡 故時發時止狂屬臟 痰入於心 故發而不止).⁵³ The "Yin viscera" (called zang臟) were thought to be deeper in the body than the "Yang viscera" (fu腑). To Li Zhongzi, the presence of mucus inside the Heart rendered madness permanent, whereas its being lodged in the more superficial Cardiac Envelope explained why madness could also be intermittent.

The Sand Seal proposed to treat these two illnesses with recipes from various periods: the “Scraps-of-cast-iron beverage” 生鐵落飲 (one of the few remedies mentioned in the Inner Canon); $ ^{54} $ Song recipes that used cinnabar, turmeric, and alum; emetics and purgatives from the Treatise on Cold Injury (3 $ ^{rd} $ century), and common recipes for fright and convulsive seizures like the “Dragon-embracing pill” (抱龍丸) and the “Will-appeasing paste” (寧志膏). $ ^{55} $ The recommended therapy for dian was to “induce the vomiting of mucus and saliva with emetics” (宜以吐劑涌吐其痰涎) and only then to use “spirit-calming remedies” (然後用安神之劑) such as ginseng, China-root, amber, calamus, [Chinese] senega, jujubes, frankincense, and cinnabar. For dian behavior caused by excessive pensiveness (思慮):

## PDF第299页

“Return-to-the-Spleen decoction” (歸脾湯); for dian caused by “emotionally-induced static mucus that produces Heat” (七情所致 鬱痰為熱者): pulverized turmeric and alum made into a pill to be swallowed with mint decoction. When caused by “mucus confusion brought about by Qi blockage” (氣滯痰迷): “Four-seven decoction” 四七湯. The same focus on mucus—and, less emphatically, Fire—was found in the section on kuang of the Sand Seal.

How (and why) Qing physicians recommended targeting mucus in madness cases

The wide circulation of primers is not enough to confirm that Qing physicians agreed with what these popular handbooks said about the mucus etiology of madness. But other medical works written from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century can provide supplementary information. Extensive reading of Qing documents has convinced me that mucus was the most commonly identified obstacle to normal consciousness both in Qing medical discourse and outside of medicine (as we shall see in 13.5). A medical editor in the late 1890s was probably not exaggerating when he claimed that "as soon as contemporary physicians see mental confusion, most of them apply themselves to [treating] mucus and Fire" (世醫一見神志昏亂 多從事於痰火). $ ^{56} $

Most late-imperial physicians attributed madness and other disorders involving consciousness to the obstruction of the Heart (心), the Heart's envelope (心包／心胞 or 心包絡), or the Heart's openings (心竅) by mucus. Mucus caused madness by keeping the

## PDF第300页

Heart’s resident “Spirit” (神) from playing its regular functions. $ ^{57} $ As I have explained above, these doctors conceived of mucus as a concrete substance that created symptoms by lodging itself in tangible structures. One writer commenting on a case of insanity called mucus a “tangible obstacle” (有形之阻), $ ^{58} $ whereas others used language that clearly implied that mucus was a substance: “mucus-Fire obstructed his spiritual openings” (痰火阻其靈竅) $ ^{59} $ or “the Heart’s openings were clogged by it” (心竅為之閉塞). $ ^{60} $

Most scripts by which doctors explained the occurrence of madness thus had a prototypical core—the blockage of the Heart by mucus. The view that mucus and Fire caused mad behavior dominated medical circles, and it was also widespread among the educated population about which we have records (see 13.5). “Mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” (痰迷心竅) was thus not only an abstract medical model designed to explain confusion. The conception on which this expression rested helped to make sense of the acts of others and even of one’s own experiences.

Qing medical writers treated the cases of madness they attributed to mucus and its pathogenic sidekick Fire in a variety of ways. Doctors usually thought that mucus resulted from a prior pathological imbalance. Sometimes, Qing physicians attempted to correct this imbalance in the hope that mucus would disappear of itself, but they usually recommended removing mucus first before tackling the imbalance that had given rise to it. Huang Yuanyu

## PDF第301页

黄元御 (1705-1758) explained that if the stagnant mucus that appeared in mad people was particularly sticky and obstinate (膠固), it should be expelled with “Melon-pedicle powder,” a powerful emetic from the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{61} $ As we saw above, the Sand Seal on the Mechanisms of Illness (Bingji shazhuan 病機沙篆; 1667), which was probably composed in large part by Li Zhongzi’s Suzhou disciple You Cheng 尤乘, insisted that dian should first be treated with emetics, and only then with Spirit-appeasing medicine. $ ^{62} $

Many texts proposed to treat kuang with anti-mucus or anti-Fire remedies without mentioning the imbalances that had given rise to these pathogens. Chen Qi's Transmitting the Lantern of Medical Learning (Yixue chuandeng 醫學傳燈; 1700) treated mucus as the more superficial cause: "although this illness [kuang] is one in which there is mucus, both mucus and Fire are abundant, so clearing Fire should be the main treatment, and transforming mucus the secondary one" (此證雖屬有痰但痰多火多當以清熱為君化痰為佐).

In a book he wrote in the early eighteenth century to instruct his disciples, Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 stated that kuang was “occasioned by the congealing and accretion of mucus and Fire” (痰火結聚所致) or by “deviant Heat in the Yang-brilliance [tract] in cases of Cold Injury” (傷寒陽明邪熱所發). $ ^{64} $ Instances of the first scenario should be treated with the “Scraps-of-cast-iron beverage” or the “Mucus-roiling pill,” the second with the

## PDF第302页

classic Cold-Injury purgative called the "Great Qi-replenishing decoction." Lin Peiqin's 林佩琴 (1772-1839) Leizheng zhi cai 類證治裁 (author's preface from 1839) discussed the etiology of kuang and dian extensively, but summarized his account by saying that "dian and kuang are all [caused by] the burning of Cardiac Fire and by mucus confusing the [Heart's] openings or the [Heart-envelope's] tract" (總之 癲狂皆心火自焚 痰迷竅絡). Most of the therapies he proposed targeted Fire and mucus. $ ^{65} $

Most of the recommendations I have cited so far appeared in general treatises that did not discuss specific instances of madness. In real cases, mucus was usually treated along with the rest of the imbalances that physicians had identified. Drugs against mucus were thus decocted together with replenishing, anti-Fire, or Spirit-appeasing medicine, and administered in a single decoction. Let me continue to show that physicians who left no writing also tried to treat madness with anti-mucus and anti-Fire strategies.

## Non-elite physicians’ use of attacking therapies

Thanks to the rich legal records of the Qing dynasty, which I analyze in detail in Part Two, we know that physicians who did not write books also treated madness with attacking therapies. Depositions by doctors in cases of homicides committed because of madness show that legal officials were aware of medical approaches to insanity. $ ^{66} $ Here I give three examples of common healers who tried to treat insanity with drugs in 1816. These three cases took place in the Fengtian 奉天 region, a province-sized area around the Qing's

## PDF第303页

secondary capital of Shengjing 盛京 (Mukden). There is not enough data to compare this region with others. My point is to show how widespread the mucus and Fire etiologies of madness had become by Qing times in at least some areas.

In 1816, a man named Tian Zhi 田治 suddenly became crazy. Witnesses claimed that he looked possessed: he would “go out and run in a disorderly fashion, saying with his eyes wide open that someone was scolding him” (出外昏跑 瞪著兩眼說有人罵他). $ ^{67} $ One doctor Zhao 趙醫生, who made a living out of practicing medicine, was invited and he diagnosed Tian as suffering from “mucus-confusion disorder” (痰迷病症). $ ^{68} $ This attribution of possession symptoms to mucus was typical of late-imperial medicine (4.4, 8.3). As Zhao recounted at Tian’s trial (Tian had later killed his wife and children), “I gave him one dose of a saliva-transforming drug; after he vomited some mucus he became a little more lucid” (給了他一服化涎的蘼 吐些痰涎纔明白些). $ ^{69} $

Also in northeast China, Yang Ying's 楊英 brother took him to a drugstore to treat his madness. Doctor Liu, the drugstore's owner, took Yang's pulse and determined that "Cardiac Qi was not at peace and mucus-Fire was in excess; I used the old recipe called "Three-huang Heart-purging decoction"; I gave him one dose and he left" (心氣不寧 痰火太甚 使用三黃瀉心湯的古方 給了他一劑湯藥 他就走了). $ ^{70} $ This prescription shows that doctor Liu was trying to purge Fire and mucus from his patient's heart. $ ^{71} $

## PDF第304页

Somewhere in Chengde 承德 county, near the imperial summer residence, another man

bought his son-in-law Niu Dafu 牛大幅 a “Heart-clearing and mucus-transforming pill” (清

心化痰丸), a remedy that was designed to purge mucus and Fire. $ ^{72} $

That the medical novelties of the Song, Jin, and Yuan periods were available in ready-made pills shows how matter-of-fact the mucus etiology had become by the early nineteenth century. In 1816 in a single region, a common physician, a drugstore owner, and a common person all tried to treat a mad person with drugs that targeted mucus and Fire. The remedies were all purgatives or emetics designed for a quick improvement of the patient's condition. In all cases, the family bought only one dose. I have not found a single legal case in which a witness reported having attempted to treat insanity with replenishing drugs, perhaps because these remedies were more expensive and took more time to act.

## The decline of emetics?

Attacking remedies probably continued to dominate outside elite circles because they were cheaper and had a more immediate and spectacular effect. Yet it appears that the elite physicians who wrote about their therapies stopped treating madness with vomit-inducing drugs. The doctors who did presented themselves as exceptions. In one case of “mucus xian” characterized by madness symptoms, Yu Jinghe 余景和 (1847-1907) explained that his master Fei Lanquan 費蘭泉 (d. 1880; from Menghe 孟河 in Jiangsu) $ ^{73} $ used to treat similar illnesses with heavy emetics, but he remarked that “people today have discarded the vomiting method and left it in disuse: one of Zhongjing’s [Zhang Ji] six methods is now

## PDF第305页

missing”(今人於吐法廢而不用 仲景六法中已少一法矣). $ ^{74} $ Long Huitang 龍繪堂, a minor physician from the late nineteenth century, testified to the same trend when he claimed that people had long stopped using the vomiting method (久不用). According to Long, patients were always surprised when he wanted to use emetics (我若用時人盡詫), but he pleaded that "the vomiting method cannot be discarded" (吐法不可廢), especially for treating illnesses in which mucus had lodged itself in the upper body. $ ^{75} $

Two citations do not make a trend, but since physicians who attributed madness to the action of mucus prescribed mostly purgatives (i.e., “mucus-transforming” remedies), since extant case files contain little trace of the use of emetics to treat insanity, and since vomiting had never been popular among patients, it is plausible that emetics had indeed fallen into neglect by the late nineteenth century, at least among the customers who could afford the service of the physicians who wrote case files.

Qing physicians used fewer Cold-Injury remedies to treat insanity. Few if any writers of case files used the emetics “Melon-pedicle powder” and “Three-saints powder,” for three possible reasons: these recipes were too obvious to be worth recording in a genre that stressed the flexibility of diagnostic and therapy; powders were no longer in fashion (they had been largely replaced by decoctions); $ ^{76} $ and the use of violent vomitives may have

## PDF第306页

declined. $ ^{77} $ The anti-Fire therapies that doctors used were rarely based on the series of Qi-supporting (承氣) decoctions drawn from the Treatise on Cold Injury (3 $ ^{rd} $ century) that physicians from the twelfth to at least the fourteenth century had used so widely to treat all kinds of severe disorders including madness. $ ^{78} $

### 7.4 Ye Gui 業桂 (1667-1746) and his case files

If there was ever a Qing equivalent of an elite network of physicians whose reflections and methods were guided by a well-defined set of texts like the Danxi corpus, it was the group of Suzhou-centered doctors who became stout followers of Ye Gui's approaches starting in the second half of the eighteenth century. Ye's family was registered in She 歙 County (Huizhou, Anhui), but he was born and he grew up in Suzhou, an active publishing center where lived a large number of doctors. $ ^{79} $ Like many healers of his era, Ye had learned medicine by traveling. His reputedly unassuming demeanor helped him to become the pupil of many masters—some say as many as seventeen. He also took a number of disciples late in his life. Studying how Suzhou physicians adopted Ye's formulations and therapies would be interesting but would take us on long tangents. $ ^{80} $ Here I focus on a few of Ye's ideas that are relevant to our investigation.

## PDF第307页

The two most influential works attributed to Ye are a brief Treatise on Warmth and Heat (Wenre lun 溫熱論), a critical reflection on externally contracted disorders, and Medical Case Files to Guide Clinical Practice (Linzheng zhinan yian 臨證指南醫案; 1766?), a posthumous edition of some of Ye's files by men who claimed to be his disciples. $ ^{81} $ The Guide to Clinical Practice (Linzheng zhinan), as it was usually abbreviated, was the most influential of a number of works purporting to contain Ye's case records. Though there are questions about who wrote these cases and about whether its compilers were really Ye's disciples, $ ^{82} $ the Guide became so widely admired that it was edited and re-published no fewer than 38 times between 1766 and 1911. $ ^{83} $ Publishers also used the eye-catching Ye surname in the title of other case collections, only some of which may have really been compiled by Ye's disciples, and most of which tried to highlight the “authentic” or “secret” character of the case files. $ ^{84} $ Major physicians like Xu Dachun 徐大椿 (1693-1771) and Zhou Xuehai 周學海 (1856-1906) also wrote commentaries on Ye's cases. $ ^{85} $

Ye’s brief case files recorded only the medically relevant aspects of therapeutic encounters—pulse, projected etiology, proposed treatment—but not the social circumstances in which the encounter took place. $ ^{86} $ They were probably the notes that Ye left

## PDF第308页

to his patients, either at their bedside or when they came to consult him at the “Hall of Sowing Good Fortune” (種福堂), where he practiced medicine in Suzhou. $ ^{87} $

The editors of the Guide to Clinical Practice sorted Ye’s cases by illnesses. In a section called “dian xian” 癲癇, they classified thirteen cases into eight entries identified by etiology. $ ^{88} $ Four cases were placed under the name “Fright and fear, mucus and Fire rising” (驚恐痰火升), three under “Yang Qi in stasis, the openings and conduits are obstructed” (陽氣鬱竅絡阻), and one each under “Wood and Fire are stirred, the Cardiac Spirit is depleted” (木火動心神虛), “Wood and Fire are in stasis, Blood is blocked” (木火鬱血滯), “Hepatic and Renal Yang are rising” (肝腎陽升), “Fire is in stasis, the Heart and Kidneys are not in contact” (火鬱心腎不交), “Wind Yang, Yang is ardent” (風陽陽亢), and “Excessive mental exhaustion” (勞心太過).

Since Ye Gui probably wrote them for patients or their relatives—people who already knew the symptoms at hand—these case files did not describe the symptoms in detail. The only description of a teenage patient surnamed Lu 盧, for instance, was “A mucus disorder has turned into xian illness; difficult to cure” (痰病已成癎疾 難愈) (YeYQ: 232B). To judge from the illness names and the few symptoms that were recorded, most of the files in the “dian xian” section concerned xian, not insanity. $ ^{89} $ The only two possible cases of mad behavior were attributed respectively to anger and fright (the latter rooted in the movement of Wood and Fire) and treated respectively with the bitter “Angelica and aloe pill” (當歸龍薈丸) and the “Heart-replenishing elixir” (補心丹).

## PDF第309页

An interesting addition to these cases was co-editor Gong Shangnian's 龔商年 long elaboration on the symptoms, causes, and treatments of dian, kuang, and xian. This text (which I translate in Appendix 6) notably claimed that dian and kuang could both be caused by either depletion or over-repletion. $ ^{90} $ Before Gong, physicians had mostly classified dian as madness caused by depletion and kuang as more manic insanity occasioned by over-repletion.

In Gong's explanation, dian and kuang were not separated by their etiology, but by their symptoms and pathomechanisms. Kuang and xian were no longer described as necessarily caused or manifested by repletion. Gong defined them as clusters of symptoms with recognized locations and causes, but also as clinically variable disorders that would have to be treated differently depending on what caused them. $ ^{91} $ The action of Fire and mucus on the Heart or the tracts nonetheless remained at the center of his explanations.

Gong’s characterization of dian as calm and unconscious and kuang as agitated and awake was in line with the shift in the meaning of dian that I describe in 7.6 below. The Guide to Clinical Practice distinguished between dian, kuang, and xian along the lines that stabilized in modern times: kuang as mania, dian as mostly quiet, and xian as epileptiform. But Gong also retained part of the older approaches when he associated kuang with the Liver (tied to anger) and dian with the Heart (linked with joy). He interpreted dian’s millennial association with Yin (see 3.4) as dian’s grounding in three Yin tracts. $ ^{92} $ Section 7.6 will explain what other physicians said of this Yin-Yang classification of dian and kuang, but for now let me show

## PDF第310页

how physicians who had read Ye Gui's Guide to Clinical Practice added complexity to the common attribution of madness to mucus and Fire.

### 7.5 A new pathological nexus: Liver imbalances and disorders of consciousness in the  $ 18^{th} $ and  $ 19^{th} $ centuries

Physicians had long associated kuang with anger, the Hepatic emotion. $ ^{93} $ Doctors sometimes re-iterated the ancient claim that the “cloud souls” (魂), which were partly responsible for mental activities, resided in the Liver. $ ^{94} $ But these correlations had not noticeably shaped the way in which physicians explained or treated mad behavior. In the eighteenth and nineteenth century, however, medical writers gave increasing importance to Liver imbalances in the etiology of mad behavior and many other disorders. These changes were not revolutionary—because most literati doctors still couched their explanations within the conceptual framework of the Five Agents, continued to rely on pulse-taking as their main diagnostic method, and prescribed mostly decoctions to perform cures—but the transformations I describe are so little-known that I would rather focus on them than emphasize the undeniable continuity of Chinese medical practice at the time.

Many nineteenth-century physicians (especially those from the Suzhou area) added a Hepatic first act to the prototypical scenario of many disorders, including madness. Though most causal chains in cases of madness still ended with mucus confusing the Heart, doctors often traced this pathological event to deeper imbalances like Liver stasis and Liver Fire. Liver Fire, Liver stasis, and Liver Wind could all disturb other functional processes, either directly (by rising) or by creating pathologically polyvalent mucus that could invade other

## PDF第311页

parts of the body. The Liver thus became a dense pathological nexus, the hub from which important illness-causing imbalances arose.

A section mysteriously named “Wood-riding-on-Earth” (木乘土) in Ye Gui’s Guide to Clinical Practice may help to explain this new focus on the Liver. $ ^{95} $ In the mapping of the Five Agents on internal organs, Wood was paired with the Liver and Earth with the Stomach. “Wood-riding-on-Earth” referred to an internal process by which excessive Liver Wind (or more rarely excessive Qi from the Gallbladder, which was the Yang counterpart of the Yin Liver) encroached on the functions of the Stomach (or the Spleen). Depending on which scenario unfolded after that, the symptoms included vomiting and stomach pains or abdominal distension and coldness of the limbs.

Hua Xiuyun 華岫雲 (1697-1773), the editor of the Guide, composed the entry that explained “Wood-riding-on-Earth.” $ ^{96} $ It started as follows:

The Liver is the organ of Wind and [the] Wood [Agent]; its official post is that of General; its nature is [to be] anxious and [prone to] movement. Compared with the illnesses of other organs, those of the Liver are thus abundant, especially among women. Liver illnesses always encroach on [the] Earth [Agent] because it mistreats what it overcomes. $ ^{97} $

肝為風木之臟 又為將軍之官 其性急而動 故肝臟之病 較之他臟為多

而於婦女尤甚 肝病必犯土 是侮其所勝也

With his explanations and Ye's case files, Hua aimed to show that “illnesses such as vomiting and unwillingness to eat, feelings of costal distension, and stomach obstruction” (嘔吐不食

## PDF第312页

脅脹脘痞等恙), which most physicians attributed to imbalances in the Spleen and Stomach, “were

actually caused by deviant [Qi] from the Liver” (實由肝邪所致). Liver etiologies were

themselves not new, $ ^{98} $ but Hua and Ye were making an important addition to the script of

Stomach-based disorders that Li Gao had developed in the thirteenth century (4.3), when he

had theorized that “inner harm” of the Spleen and Stomach was a major cause of illness (4.3).

Many influential medical writers followed Ye’s and Hua’s emphasis. Lin Peiqin 林佩琴 (1772-1839), a great admirer of Ye Gui, also suggested that “all Qi that rises arises from the Liver” (凡上升之氣 自肝而起). $ ^{99} $ Rising Qi included Fire and inner Wind, whose upward movements Lin attributed to Liver-Wood stasis. When Ministerial Fire attached itself to Wood, Wood stasis gave rise to Fire that could trigger, among other things, mad behavior (kuang). $ ^{100} $ Like “Wind” or “depletion Fire,” Liver imbalances were now thought to create a wide variety of effects: headaches, madness, vomiting, pains, etc.

Ye Gui's admirers were not the only ones to maintain that the Liver was a central cause of illness. Li Wenrong 李文榮 (b. 1772; see 8.5), who disliked the Guide to Clinical Practice because it did not mention the outcome of treatments, still claimed that the Liver was the organ whose imbalances most affected other viscera. $ ^{101} $ Wang Tailin 王泰林 (1798-1862) compiled “thirty methods to treat the Liver” (治肝卅法), at the beginning of which he

## PDF第313页

explained that “all three of Liver Qi, Liver Wind, and Liver Fire have the same origin but a different name” (肝氣 肝風 肝火 三者同出異名). $ ^{102} $ He applied his methods to many illnesses that doctors would have interpreted very differently two hundred years earlier. $ ^{103} $

This focus on the Liver became so widespread that Lu Yitian 陸以湉 (jinshi 1836, d. 1865; from Tongxiang 桐鄉 near Jiaxing 嘉興 in northern Zhejiang) claimed in the 1850s that "contemporaries [say that] so-called cardiac pains, stomach pains, and costal pains are all sufferings [caused by] Liver Qi" (今人所謂心痛 胃痛 脅痛 無非肝氣為患). $ ^{104} $ Lu explained why Liver illnesses were so prevalent:

He Xichi $ ^{105} $ said that the three phrases “the hundred disorders all arise from stasis,” “all disorders belong to Fire,” and “Wind is the chief of the hundred disorders” represented a single principle. This must be because there is no case of stasis in which one does not ail from Fire, and not one case of Fire that does not come from stasis. When stasis is not eased, Liver-Wood disorders are bound to occur. This can be taken as another piece of evidence that Liver disorders are abundant. $ ^{106} $

何西池曰 百病皆生於鬱 與凡病皆屬火 及風為百病之長三句 總只一理

蓋鬱未有不病火者也 火未有不由鬱者也 第鬱而不舒 則皆肝木之病矣

此又可為肝病多之一證

## PDF第314页

For such a novelty to appear in writing so fast, it must have been based on something that made obvious sense to practitioners and treatise writers. Liver Fire may have become a new name for internal Wind. Conveniently, this understanding of the human organism could be read back into the early medical canons.

Like most new medical emphases, Liver imbalances were eventually transferred to explanations of mad behavior. This focus on hepatic functions helped to reconcile old texts that explained the Wind etiology with current approaches that discussed mucus and Fire. In retrospect, the Liver seemed like an obvious candidate for causing madness and emotional problems, because it was a converging point for almost all the processes that had been thought to lead to madness: it was associated with anger, Wind (formerly identified as the prime cause of madness), Fire (the second most identified cause of madness in late imperial medicine after mucus), $ ^{107} $ stasis (associated with emotional problems), $ ^{108} $ the “cloud souls” (bun), and of course mucus (which was sometimes said to arise from Liver stasis).

When found in cases of madness, Hepatic Fire was often said to act in concert either with Cardiac Fire or with mucus, as in this case by Lin Peiqin 林佩琴 (1772-1839). $ ^{109} $

A youth had unfulfilled aspirations, so that his mind progressively became stupefied and his words lost coherence. His face was red and his eyes oblique; he shed his clothes and tore his drapes (?) He was given herbal drugs to make him vomit and purge him, and the mucus and Fire were slightly settled, but now he has encountered

## PDF第315页

the rising Fire of noon and he speaks and laughs to himself, half dazed and half aware. His left pulses—stringy and elongated—indicate [that the illness] comes from the reverse [i.e., upward] flow of Liver-and-Gallbladder Fire, $ ^{110} $ which directly encroaches upon the chest. The mind is thus obscured by mucus-saliva. The Canon says that the Liver is where deliberations issue from, and the Gallbladder where decisions originate. When the Heart, Liver, and Gallbladder are deliberating, but remain indecisive, where can frustrations be extended and anger vented? When Wood and Fire are burning intensely, the sovereign [i.e., the Heart] has no power, hence the revolt. $ ^{111} $ When there is doubling of Yang, there is necessarily madness.

少年懷抱不遂 漸次神明恍惚 言語失輪 面赤眼斜 棄衣裂帳 曾服草藥吐瀉 痰火略定 今交午火升 獨言獨笑 半昧半明 左脈弦長 自屬肝膽火逆 直犯膻中 神明遂為痰涎所蔽 經調肝者 謀慮所出 膽者決斷所出 心肝膽謀慮不決 屈何所伸 怒何所泄 木火熾煽 君主無權 從此厥逆 重陽必狂

Instead of giving his patient strong purgatives, Lin decided to pacify the Fire from his Liver and Gallbladder and cleanse mucus from his Cardiac Envelope (擬平肝膽之火, 滌心包之痰). He claimed he achieved success after a few adjustments in the remedy.

Lin Peiqin placed mucus and Fire at the center of his explanations of madness, but he grounded their action in Liver imbalances. Like most Chinese physicians, he also assumed that emotions supervened on the dynamics of inner fluids. $ ^{112} $ His interpretation of the “doubling of Yang” (重陽) in the last line of his case leads us to our next section.

## PDF第316页

### 7.6 Dian and kuang: new definitions and variations

The doubling of Yin and the doubling of Yang: “irresistible analogies”

“Irresistible analogy” is the title of a chapter in Pierre Bourdieu’s Logic of Practice (1990) where he argued that the structure of our conceptions often determines the paths by which we make analogies. Here I want to show that Qing writers who strove to define the relations between dian and kuang framed their argument according to the Canon of Problems’ (second-century) claim that “Yin is the doubling of Yin and kuang the doubling of Yang.” $ ^{113} $

Not everyone agreed that the Canon of Problems' pronouncement was useful. Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), for instance, had argued in the early sixteenth century that this Yin-Yang distinction made little sense (難經陰陽之說 恐非理也), $ ^{114} $ whereas Li Chan 李梴 (late sixteenth century) had dismissed it as “idle considerations” (謾議). $ ^{115} $ Others, often cited it without comment from the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 or one of its successors. $ ^{116} $

But many more writers from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries draped new ideas in the antique wrappings of the Problems' formulation. Since the Yin-Yang scheme had no inherent content, doctors could give almost any meaning to it. In one of his typically unusual arguments, Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758), a prolific Shandong physician who enjoyed good contacts at the imperial court, proposed the following explanation.

Dian illness is [characterized by] calmness and stillness and most often by

despondency and fear: this is because the Qi of the Lungs and Kidneys is thriving.

## PDF第317页

Kuang illness is [characterized by] irritability and movement and most often by joy or anger: this is because the Qi of the Liver and Heart is thriving. The Lungs and Kidneys are Yin, the Liver and Heart are Yang. This is exactly the meaning of the twentieth problem [of the Canon of Problems] when it said that double Yin is dian and double Yang is kuang. $ ^{117} $

顛病者 安静而多悲恐 肺腎之氣旺也 狂病者 燥動而多喜怒 肝心之氣旺也 肺腎為陰 肝心為陽 二十難曰重陰者顛 重陽者狂 正此義也

Huang ignored the common association of dian with joy and the Heart, and kuang with anger and the Liver (see 3.4, 4.1). Instead, to preserve symmetry and to make his interpretation of "doubles" plausible, he collapsed joy and anger under kuang and associated dian with despondency and fear. Although Huang Yuanyu's definition had little influence on later writers, it was no less plausible than the theories other medical authors were proposing.

Gong Shangnian 龔商年, who wrote the gloss on dian, kuang, and xian in Ye Gu's Guide to Clinical Practice (1766; see 7.4 above) explained dian and kuang utterly differently.

Heaven-and-Earth is one Yin-and-Yang. When Yin and Yang are in harmony, Heaven is clear and Earth peaceful. As soon as there is imbalance, abnormal transformations take place. The human body is also one Yin-and-Yang. When Yin and Yang are harmonious, the Spirit is clear and Qi is settled. As soon as there is imbalance, unpredictable ailments naturally occur. This is why the Inner Canon says: heavy [重 read as zhong] on Yang is kuang, heavy on Yin is dian. $ ^{118} $

天地 一陰陽也 陰陽和則天清地寧 一有偏勝 遂有非常之變 人身亦一陰陽也 陰陽和則神清氣定 一有偏勝 自致不測之疴 故內經曰 重陽者狂 重陰者癲

## PDF第318页

Suzhou physician Lin Peiqin 林佩琴 (1772-1839) implied that the “doubling of Yang” meant double repletion of Wood and Fire—the Liver and the Heart. $ ^{119} $ Chen Qiu 陳虬 (late  $ 19^{th} $ century), for his part, adopted a symmetrical position when he explained that a patient suffered from dian because of pensiveness and anger, the result of both stasis and depletion: “this is what is meant by ‘the doubling of Yin is dian’” (所謂重陰者巔也). $ ^{120} $

Wang Yixiang 汪藝香 (1838-1900; from She county in Huizhou), proposed another interpretation: madness was Yin when it was caused by mucus, Yang when it was caused by Fire (陰者痰之類 陽者火之類). $ ^{121} $ In a case file, Jin Zijiu 金子久 (1870-1921) explained: “concerning the division between dian and kuang: restlessness and movement belong to Yang: they are kuang; calmness and silence belong to Yin: they are dian; this is why the Canon of Problems says ‘the doubling of Yin is dian, the doubling of Yang is kuang’” (癲狂之分 躁動屬陽為狂 靜默屬陰為癲 此難經所謂重陰則癲 重陽則狂是也). $ ^{122} $ His definition of the complementarity of kuang and dian reflected a new shift in the latter’s meaning.

## Toward apathy: a new shift in the meaning of dian

Dian had first been characterized by seizures (3.3). In the first few centuries CE, however, this “falling sickness” had come to mean “insanity,” first outside of medicine (3.4), and then in the medical literature, where dian thus became indistinguishable from kuang (4.1). Physicians used dian and kuang as quasi synonyms until some distinctions between the two

## PDF第319页

were adopted in the sixteenth century (6.5 and 6.6). As defined by Sun Yikui 孫一奎 (1522-1619; 6.6), dian was insanity caused by depletion, and kuang was mania caused by overrepletion. In this section, I recount how dian changed meanings again in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries to the point that it came to refer to apathy rather than insanity.

The modern polarity between dian as apathetic and kuang as manic is a recent avatar of the old functional bipolarity that has united dian and kuang for more than two millennia. Through an undoubtedly complex process that I have not tried to trace thoroughly, this likening of dian to pathological apathy became widely accepted in the nineteenth century and came to justify a new association of dian with tranquility and kuang with activity. $ ^{123} $

This new transformation may have started when some symptoms of idiocy were blended into definitions of dian. Cheng Guopeng's 程國彭 (fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century), for example, likened the appearance of dian to that of idiocy (痴呆之狀). $ ^{124} $ The next step in the emergence of a new meaning for dian was the convergence of its symptoms around the notion of apathy, or pathological calmness. Following this change of focus, the new line of opposition between dian and kuang became the contrast between tranquility (jing 靜) and activity (dong 動). In a mnemonic poem that appeared in his Medicine is Decidedly Easy! (Yixue shi zai yi 醫學實在易; 1808), Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823) versified: “dian: the appearance of idiocy, the person is usually quiet; kuang: insufferably edgy and rash, the

## PDF第320页

person is usually active" (癫者 痴呆之状 其人常静 狂者 躁妄不堪 其人常動). $ ^{125} $

Wang Tailin 王泰林 (1798-1862; better known by his zì Xugao 旭高) proposed a similar definition in My Humble Views on Medical Learning (Yixue chuyan 醫學芻言): “Dian is the appearance of idiocy, crying and laughing atypically, incoherent language; the person is usually quiet. Kuang is berating and reviling without discriminating between kith and kin; the person is usually active.” $ ^{126} $ (癲者痴呆之狀 哭笑無常 語言無序 其人常靜 狂者罵詈不避親疏 其人常動) Mo Meishi’s 莫枚士 “explanation of dian” (癲說), written in 1856, confirms that a new interpretation of dian was circulating: “the ancient dian ji is the current [epileptiform] ‘xian’ and the ancient ‘dian’ is the current ‘idiocy’” (古之巔疾今之癎古之癲今之痴也). $ ^{127} $

This new emphasis on apathy and stultified spirits may have been a pathographic spin-off of sorts. Specifically, the new definition of dian borrowed from common symptoms of xian. This offshoot was not proposed by a single physician who had decided to neaten the prevalent classifications of illness, but we can see it in shared usage. Ye Gui's (7.5) cases hint to this usage in the first half of the eighteenth century. $ ^{128} $ "A woman née Ye. Every time the

## PDF第321页

menses come, they are purplish and black; xian illness is bound to flare up”(葉氏 每遇經來紫黑 癇疾必發). Xian was usually understood as a convulsive disorder, but not here: "At dusk and at night she screams in fright and her voice trembles. In the morning, her spirits are stultified, her face is dark and she smiles a lot. [This is] Fire and Wind arriving from the Liver.” $ ^{129} $ (暮夜驚呼聲震 晝則神呆 面青多笑 火風由肝而至)

Two more of Ye's cases used the old compound dianxian to refer to apathetic symptoms. A man surnamed Ni who "had the signs of dianxian" (有癫癎之象) suffered from "stultified spirits and failure to sleep, sudden screaming, no eating but no hunger, and no excretions" (神呆不寐 倏爾叫喊 不食不飢 不便). $ ^{130} $ An unnamed person who suffered from emotional problems (grief, worry, fright, etc.) "became progressively stupefied in spirits, looking somewhat like dian xian" (漸漸神志恍惚 有似癫癎). $ ^{131} $

Another trace of this borrowing of symptoms of xian into apathetic dian appeared in Sun Cailin's 孫采鄰 (from Suzhou; fl. mid 19 $ ^{th} $ century) long handwritten casebook. In a case that other physicians had attempted to treat as xian but that Sun identified as a case of dian, Sun felt it necessary to explain how the two illnesses differed. $ ^{132} $ To Sun, dian meant looking apathetic. He noted that the patient herself thought she was suffering from xian, but if even physicians did not realize this was a case of dian, how could the patient be expected to know better? In another case, Sun Cailin made one of the rare comments I have found that distinguished dian from kuang in a collection of case files, though this type of comment probably increased in the nineteenth century after dian had increasingly come to refer to

## PDF第322页

apathy rather than odd behavior. $ ^{133} $ Once this new transformation in the meaning of dian was completed, kuang was once again the only illness in Chinese medical classifications that we can plausibly translate as madness.

From nosography, let us now turn to medical books that aimed to shape how doctors would treat their patients. My goal is to show that Qing physicians continued to attribute madness to the pathogenic action of mucus and Fire.

### 7.7 Diagnostics, therapy, and efficacy in an early-nineteenth-century medical case

In the rest of this chapter, I translate and analyze a case of madness by Wu Tang 吳瑭 (1758-1836), who practiced medicine for at least forty years between 1793 and 1833. $ ^{134} $ Wu was from Huai'an 懷安 in northern Jiangsu on the Grand Canal, but he lived mostly in Beijing from 1783 till his death in 1836. An avid admirer of Ye Gui's writings and of the sophisticated doctrines he believed they embodied, he was particularly struck by Ye's work on Warm-factor illnesses. While Wu had worked as a proofreader for the Complete Library of the Four Treasuries (Siku quanshu 四庫全書; completed in 1782), he had pored through medical works of all ages in search of more information on this kind of disorder. Few Chinese physicians had ever had access to so many books at once. In 1798, when Wu was 40, a friend encouraged him to write a book on Warm-factor disorders. Wu had then started

## PDF第323页

compiling what would become Distinguishing Warm-Factor illnesses (Wenbing tiaobian 温病條辨), a treatise he completed in 1813. $ ^{135} $

Wu's general views on medicine are less reputed than his work on Warm-factor disorders, but more relevant for our purposes. In Curing the Ills of Medicine (Yi yi bing shu 醫醫病書), which he completed late in his life, Wu denounced many of the evils he claimed marred contemporary medical practice, and proposed ways to palliate them. An important part of his critique was to blame his contemporaries for seeking to circumvent the difficulties of medical doctrine by relying on digests.

What contemporaries read are Mr. Tao's Six Books, $ ^{136} $ Preserving the Primordial in Helping

the World to Live, $ ^{137} $ Li Zhongzi's Three Books, $ ^{138} $ Wang Ang's Essentials of Materia

Medica $ ^{139} $ and Collected Explanations on Medical Formulas, $ ^{140} $ as well as Wu Youke's

Treatise on Warm-Factor Epidemics. $ ^{141} $ Some even go so far as practicing medicine after

## PDF第324页

reading only the Rhapsody on the Nature of Drugs $ ^{142} $ or the Rhymed Songs on Decoctions. $ ^{143} $

The case files of Mr. Ye [Gui], of recent times, are mostly refined and rarely coarse; they far surpass Tao's, Gong's, and Li's books, and recently southerners have enjoyed reading them. But those who do not read ancient books cannot obtain the main meaning [of these case files]: they call themselves "Ye school" by donning only its skin and hair. $ ^{144} $

近人所讀者 陶氏六書 壽世保元 李士材三書 汪訒庵本草備要醫方集解吳又可溫疫論 甚至只讀藥性賦 湯頭歌訣 便欲行醫 近代葉氏醫案 精詳者多 粗疏者少 遠勝陶 龔 李三氏等書 近日南方人多喜讀之 然不讀古書 不能得其要領 但襲皮毛以謂葉派

# Perhaps because he had learned medicine from books and clinical practice rather than from teachers, Wu thought little of the “factions” by which practitioners divided their allegiances.

Mencius said that the way of Confucius would not shine until the ways of Yangzi and Mozi disappeared. Since the Tang dynasty, the way of medicine has been damaged to the extreme. First it was in the late Tang and the Five Dynasties, when most physicians used toxic drugs but were unable to target the facts of illness accurately and thus harmed a lot of people. This [problem] went as far as the preparation of the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service, which included remedies with toxic drugs into its secret recipes—this was one impairment to the medical way. Later, famous physicians appeared from time to time, such as Li Dongyuan, Zhu Danxi, or Liu Wansu; $ ^{145} $ they each established a faction (門戶) around one partial view that

## PDF第325页

became known as [representing] one “school” (家) $ ^{146} $—this was another impairment. In the Ming era, Mr. Tao’s Six Books conflated the six Qi, calling them all “Cold Injury”; [these Six Books] established recipes and treated illnesses with imprecision, [yet] everyone followed them, rejoicing at their convenience and simplicity—this was another important impairment. Then as soon as Wang Ang’s Collected Explanations on Medical Recipes appeared, everyone was speaking of them and reciting them, rejoicing in their convenience and simplicity. Few manage not to kill people when they treat illnesses according to the [explanations found therein]. $ ^{147} $

孟子謂 楊墨之道不熄 孔子之道不著 唐以後醫道之壞極矣 先是唐末五代之際 醫者率用毒藥 不能對準病情 傷人實多 以致有和劑局方之設將毒藥之方俱收入禁方 此醫道之一壞也 繼則代有名醫 如李東垣 朱丹溪 劉河間輩 各以一偏之見 各立門戶 以成一家之名 又一壞也 下而至於明季 陶氏六書之作 混合六氣 均曰傷寒 籠統立方 籠統治病 世咸宗之 樂其簡便 又一大壞也 至汪訒庵醫方集解一出 家語戶誦 樂其簡便 若以之治病 鮮有不殺人者

Embracing a wide approach grounded (at least rhetorically) in the Inner Canon, Wu criticized famous physicians for adhering to doctrinal sects: “The foremost ill of famous physicians is factional learning” (名醫之病 首在門戶之學). $ ^{148} $

## PDF第326页

Like most late-imperial practitioners, Wu Tang was expected to treat all ailments. $ ^{149} $ His Medical Case Files (Wu Jutong yian 吳鞠通醫案) were thus more than a clinical companion to his treatise on Warm-factor illnesses—they aimed to demonstrate the validity of Wu's approach to all illnesses. $ ^{150} $ A section called “Madness” (diankuang 顛狂, in a single word since the book did not contain any instance of dian) appeared in fascicle 2 of Wu's casebook, enclosed between a short section on “Liver-related swellings” (肝癰; 2 cases) and one on “Depletion and Wasting” disorders (虚勞; 14 cases). The seven cases it contained were from these dates: year unknown (Mr. Tuo), 1809 (Mr. Qi), year unknown (no name), year unknown (Mr. Bao; translated below), year unknown (woman née Zhang; see 9.3), 1827 (Mr. Fu), and 1827 (Mr. Yan). $ ^{151} $ The chronological arrangement of the dated cases throughout the collection suggests that the undated cases were also chronologically arrayed. $ ^{152} $ The undated cases that appeared between the 1809 case and the two cases from 1827 probably took place between those two years. With one exception, every case in the madness section provided the name and age of the patient and continued with a description of the illness, multilevelled discussions of etiology (food, emotions, cosmic configurations), and details on diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. All cases contained follow-up treatments and all but one led to a successful outcome. As we have come to expect, Wu Tang applied to mad

## PDF第327页

behavior some of the methods of treatment he had developed for Warm-factor disorders and other illnesses.

Records of clinical encounters did more than illustrate general doctrines. By recording how physicians treated actual mad patients, case files bring to light important aspects of medical approaches that did not appear in treatises. $ ^{153} $ The madness case of a man surnamed Bao, which appeared between cases dated 1809 and 1827, illustrates some of these themes very well. Although I emphasize what was specific about Wu Tang's approach, let us not forget that many of his assumptions were widely shared. Like so many other physicians, Wu understood madness as the destruction of social roles, placed the burden of diagnosis on pulse taking, and chiefly used drugs to treat behavioral symptoms. $ ^{154} $

The second day of the 12 $ ^{th} $ month. [Mr.] Bao, 32 sui. Has been seriously mad for seven years. He first fell ill because his achievements did not follow [his ambitions]. A number of foremost physicians, city doctors, $ ^{155} $ and scholarly doctors of this capital [Nanjing?] have come in succession. $ ^{156} $ If you count doctors from Huizhou, Hangzhou, Suzhou, and Hubei, no fewer than several tens, even hundreds, of physicians have inspected him. $ ^{157} $ By and large, most of them replenished depletion and few attacked repletion. [Bao’s madness] flared up again after a very short time. $ ^{158} $

## PDF第328页

十二月初二日 鮑 三十二歲 大狂七年 先因功名不遂而病 本京先醫

市醫 儒醫 已歷不少 既而徽州醫 杭州醫 蘇州醫 湖北醫 所閱之醫

不下數十百矣 大概補虛者多 攻實者少 間有已時 不旋踵而即發

# Wu was telling readers that some healers had succeeded in alleviating Bao's symptoms, but that the imbalance that was causing Bao's disorder invariably generated more outbursts.

Wu's description also informs us about the social context in which the encounter took place. Bao's household must have been relatively well-off to have him examined by a large number of doctors, including some of the city's most notorious practitioners, as well as southern physicians—from places as distant as Hubei, though many were probably sojourners. $ ^{159} $ Second, there seems to have been serious competition between elite healers in early-nineteenth-century Beijing. $ ^{160} $ Wu was only the latest physician to visit Bao, and would presumably be sent away like the others if he failed. If we believe Wu, the competition opposed two major therapeutic approaches: “replenishing” (used when an illness was attributed to depletion) and “attacking” (when the ailment was attributed to over-repletion) (see Chapter 6). Although manic symptoms like Bao's usually called for depleting therapies, perhaps physicians had decided to replenish him because—following a common definition of dian—they interpreted his madness caused by failure of ambitions as being rooted in depletion. $ ^{161} $ By stating that most of the unsuccessful physicians had used the replenishing method, Wu implied that they were wrong and he prepared to emphasize the extent of his success where so many of the best had failed. $ ^{162} $

## PDF第329页

Wu Tang described as follows the first time he saw his patient:
