When I first examined him, I saw that his hair was disheveled, his face dirty, and the lower half of his body exposed. His clothes did not cover his person. No sooner did he wear his clothes that he destroyed them. The door and the windows were in pieces. As soon as they were nailed up, he would tear them down. Hands and feet shackled, he was chained to a large grindstone by several lengths of steel chains. The wildness of his words, $ ^{163} $ his body's emaciation—no need to say more.

余初診時 見其蓬首垢面 下體俱赤 衣不遮身 隨著隨毀 門窗粉碎 隨釘隨拆 鐐靠手足 外有鐵索數根 錮鎖於大石磨盤上 言語之亂 形體之贏 更不待言

In the medical literature, recognizing madness was never presented as a technical issue.

There was nothing latent about Bao’s illness—Bao’s relatives, Wu Tang, and readers of the case history could all see that Bao was mad. As was customary with violent and troublesome madmen, he was chained up in a room. $ ^{164} $

Consciously or not, Wu’s exposé followed the “four diagnostic methods” (四診):

“looking” (shi 視), “asking” (wen 問), “listening / smelling” (wen 聞), and “palpating” (qie 切)

or pulse-taking. $ ^{165} $ Wu had looked, now he asked and listened:

Making detailed queries about the circumstances $ ^{166} $ [of his disorder, I was told that]

he absolutely had to see his wife every day. $ ^{167} $ When she was unwilling to see him, he

## PDF第330页

would become unspeakably uproarious! $ ^{168} $ He would yell himself hoarse, wailing in ways one could not bear to hear. There would be no alternative but to make his family members reluctantly minister to him, $ ^{169} $ after which he would be slightly appeased. [Things] would be the same again the next day, without one day of lull.

細詢其情 每日非見婦人不可 婦人不願見彼 竟鬧不可言 叫號聲嘶哀鳴 令人不忍聞 只得令伊家人強侍之 然後少安 次日仍然 無一日之空

So far, Wu was describing Bao's appearance and his excessive behavior.

Wu and other physicians who treated madness in Qing China did not make much use of what Jan Goldstein (2001 [1987]: 383) has called “the power to classify,” that is, the ability to choose accurately between different illness entities in a context in which identification of the illness by name had great explanatory power. For Wu Tang and most Chinese doctors, there was no taxonomy of madness, no “types” to choose from: only one kind of madness (kuang or diankuang), or sometimes two (dian and kuang) but with similar symptoms. Second, in the practice of elite physicians like Wu, therapy did not derive from identification of the illness but from recognition of the organic imbalances that were generating the disorder. $ ^{170} $ Simply naming kuang ensured no more than superficial intelligibility and had no therapeutic consequence. In other words, even when everyone agreed on the name of the illness at hand, the doctor still needed to diagnose the patient.

It is from the patient's pulse—usually the most important of the four diagnostic methods—that Wu grasped the root of the illness and designed a therapeutic strategy.

## PDF第331页

I examined his pulse: the Six Sections were strung, extended, and sturdy. I said: “This is a manifestation of over-repletion, not depletion.” $ ^{171} $ Therefore I used extremely bitter medicine $ ^{172} $ to drain Fire from the two tracts of the Heart and the Gallbladder. $ ^{173} $ When draining the Heart [tract], it is necessary to drain the Small Intestine [tract] as well, $ ^{174} $ because “when the disorder is in a Yin visceral system, regulate its [corresponding] Yang visceral system.” $ ^{175} $ [Moreover,] because the Gallbladder has no outlet, $ ^{176} $ I borrow the Small Intestine as an outlet: [this is] also [a reason] for draining the Small Intestine. $ ^{177} $

診其脈 六部弦長而勁 余曰 此實症 非虛症也 於是用極苦以瀉心 膽二經之火 瀉心者必瀉小腸 病在臟 治其腑也 膽無出路 借小腸以為出路 亦必瀉小腸也

Like many contemporaries, Wu Tang recognized that intellectual or emotional frustrations could cause serious madness, $ ^{178} $ but he still viewed this process as physically grounded—Bao's disappointed ambitions had turned into an excess of Fire. The target of Wu's therapeutic intervention was thus be the same body that suffered from Warm-factor illnesses or urinary discomfort. Wu did not have to think about his patient's mental condition yet.

To purge Fire from the Heart, Gallbladder, and Small Intestine tracts, Wu Tang recommended the following formula:

## PDF第332页

Three qian each of gentian root, Chinese asparagus, minced raw foxglove, picrorhiza, lily turf (with the kernel), and powdered tree peony. Decoct three cups; take in three separate doses.

龍膽草三錢 夭冬三錢 細生地三錢 胡黃連三錢 麥冬不去心三錢 粉丹皮三錢 煮三杯 分三次服

Wu's prescription was no mix-and-hope. Gentian root, asparagus, and picrorhiza were all considered "bitter" (ku 苦) and "cold" (ban 寒) or "cool" (liang 凉) drugs. Lily turf and tree peony were also "cold," and respectively sweet (gan 甘) and acrid (xin 辛). $ ^{179} $ All these frigorific drugs were chosen for one chief purpose: draining Fire. $ ^{180} $

“Two doses proved very efficient: [Bao’s] rash talking diminished and he carried himself more calmly” (服二帖大效 妄語少而舉動安靜). Perhaps the purgatives had made Bao excrete until he became so weak that he started to behave in a more subdued manner, but speaking of the effects of a remedy without specifying its intended effects obscures why Wu prescribed it at all. As Wu understood it, Bao’s illness was wild behavior. The fact that the patient “carried himself more calmly” was not seen as the temporary result of a debilitating purge, but as the quick and successful reduction of the dominance of Fire that was causing Bao’s wild behavior. The patient was indeed getting better.

## PDF第333页

Chinese medicine in its most sophisticated form adapted remedies to the changes in illness configuration that resulted from the ingestion of drugs. Wu's follow-up treatment (one day after the first prescription) illustrates the dynamism of therapy.

The third day [of the twelfth month]. Considering the efficacy [of the drugs], [I thought that] the body would become depleted because of the prolonged illness. I was thus afraid [that the formula] would be too hard $ ^{181} $ and would break [the patient]. Taking advantage of the abatement of his illness, I reduced the strength of the formula, decreasing the [amount of] Bitter drugs in the original prescription and adding to it sweet and moistening [drugs] that replenish Yin.

初三日 見其效也 以為久病體虛 恐過剛則折 用病減者減其制例 於原方減苦藥 加補陰之甘潤

Although Wu did not give the detail of his new prescription, we know he was trying to avoid creating a pathological depletion in the process of getting rid of over-repletion. He was attempting to replenish Mr. Bao's depleted Yin aspects. $ ^{182} $

The effects of the adjusted prescription were dramatic and probably more unexpected than Wu cared to admit in his well-woven narrative:

The fifth day [of the 12 $ ^{th} $ month]. The patient's relatives came to report: “Yesterday we administered two doses of the modified prescription, but the disorder has become much more serious: his wailing and chaotic talk are many times worse than before, and there isn’t a moment of calm. We think these symptoms are impossible to treat: we are afraid he will almost certainly die. You need not examine him again.”

初五日 病家來告 云 昨服改方二帖 病勢大重 較前之叫哮妄語加數倍

之多 無一刻之靜 此症想不能治 諒其必死 先生可不必再診矣

## PDF第334页

Since patients often had a sophisticated understanding of medicine and the functioning of the body, and considering that Mr. Bao's family had already seen countless physicians, we can assume that Baos' relatives understood the gist of Wu's approach. They had not disagreed a priori with Wu's methods, but they probably decided to relieve him because his therapies had not improved the patient's condition. Wu of course disagreed.

I said: “Not so. At the beginning, I used a strong remedy with great efficacy, then continued with a milder one with Yin-replenishing additives, [which made the disorder appear] much more serious; [but] I know what is proper.” $ ^{183} $ When I took his pulse again, it was strung, extended, and accelerated. Therefore I made heavy use of Bitter drugs.

余曰 不然 初用重劑而大效 繼用輕劑加補陰而大重 吾知進退矣 復診其脈 弦長而數 於是重用苦藥

On the verge of being dismissed as another physician who could not keep Bao's illness from flaring up again, Wu affirmed his grasp on process by convincing Bao's relatives that the imbalance that was causing the illness was being invisibly modified even as the patient appeared more vehement. $ ^{184} $ Wu was negotiating his position in a dynamics of “blame and vindication” that probably existed in the medicine of many times and places. $ ^{185} $ Nonetheless, he prudently renounced replenishing Yin for the moment and went back to prescribing Fire-draining drugs in heavier amounts and for a more sustained stretch than the first time.

## PDF第335页

Gentian root (6 qian) [bitter and cold], asparagus (5 qian) [bitter and cold], true yalien (5 qian) [bitter and cold], foreign [?] aloe (6 qian) [bitter and cold], lily turf (with the kernel; 2 qian) [sweet and slightly bitter; cold], Wumei flesh (5 qian) [acidic and either warming or cooling depending on definitions], picrorhiza (5 qian) [bitter and cold], and urine deposit preparation (2 qian) [salty and cold]. Boil in three bowls [of water] and take in separate doses. One course of this remedy should be taken in six doses. The efficacy [of the drugs] improved daily, so that on the eleventh day [of the month],  $ {}^{186} $ he was largely lucid.

龍膽草六錢 天冬五錢 真雅連五錢 洋蘆薈六錢 麥冬不去心二錢 烏梅肉五錢 胡黃連五錢 秋石二錢 煮三碗 分次服 服此方一氣六帖 一日較一日大效 至十一日大為明白

Bao's return to lucidity marked a sudden turn in the narrative. As both the clinical encounter and the case file drew to an end, for the first time Wu reported talking to his patient.

He contracted his illness because his ideas fell short and because he could not reach the highest degree in the knowledge of texts. [But] since being able to reach the highest is not something one can forcibly achieve, what is there to be angry about? In life, filial piety means caring for the heart of one's parents. $ ^{187} $ I severely reprimanded him: he lowered his head, without reply. After that, I progressively decreased the bitter drugs and added Yin-replenishers. Half a day later we removed his penal implements, $ ^{188} $ he put on his clothes and cap, $ ^{189} $ and we kneeled and bowed together. His spirits $ ^{190} $ were no different from those of a healthy person. He took one

## PDF第336页

course of the “Special harmonizing and vitalizing paste” and became greatly stronger. $ ^{191} $ He [even] succeeded in the following session of the examinations!

於是將其得病之由 因伊念頭之差 因未識文章至高之境 即能至高 非人力所能強為 何怒之有 人生以體親心為孝 痛乎責之 俯首無辭 以後漸減苦藥 加補陰 半日以後 去刑具 著衣冠 同跪拜 神識與好人無異 服專翕大生膏一料而大壯 下科竟中矣

The entire treatment lasted two weeks, perhaps less. As the crucial last sentence stressed, Bao's fits did not resume as they had after the other physicians had left. The cure lasted.

Mr. Bao's case is a typical example of the therapeutic optimism we find in medical case files: the physician designed the appropriate therapy from a correct diagnosis and expertly modulated drugs to rectify the organic imbalance that he claimed was causing illness. Clearly no bedside jotting, the case history was carefully emplotted to lead to final success. Wu may have been boasting or lying, but let us assume for now that he really cured Mr. Bao from his seven years of manic behavior. How are we to explain his recovery?

Arthur Kleinman has called the cultural construction of efficacy “one of the most difficult issues in all of medical anthropology” (1995: 200). Historians have barely started to address it, perhaps because they lack the thick ethnographic material that anthropologists can elicit. $ ^{192} $ Ritual and symbolic healing are crucial ways in which the elusive afflictions we call “mental illnesses” are treated in all societies. $ ^{193} $ Anthropologists have also studied the

## PDF第337页

issues of ritual healing and the therapeutic effect of symbols. $ ^{194} $ In recent developments, Thomas Csordas has observed that in ritual healing, the cure often does not consist in removing an illness, but in transforming the person. $ ^{195} $ He has also insisted that symbols cannot effect a cure without going through the patient’s embodied experience. His reflections make sense in a broader shift of anthropologists’ attention from the analysis of symbols and meaning to the study of embodied selves. $ ^{196} $

There are at least three levels to the problem of efficacy in the case of Mr. Bao: the effects of drugs, the way efficacy was negotiated during the cure, and how the patient reacted to his treatment. The continued use of vomitives and purgatives to treat madness in Chinese medicine was probably related to their perceived efficacy. These drugs “worked” insofar as they produced a visible effect on the patient. The weakness that followed their intake was part of the expectations created by the etiological conceptions on the basis of which these drugs were prescribed. As we saw in earlier chapters, for example, the mucus that a patient vomited was thought to be the direct cause of madness or mental confusion; the Fire that the purges had got ridden of was the direct cause of mad behavior. $ ^{197} $

Wu’s claims to efficacy (and other people’s perceptions of therapeutic success) also rested on his ability to account for failure. Instead of admitting the fiasco of his hasty resort to Yin-replenishing drugs that led to a redoubling of Bao’s symptoms, Wu convinced Bao’s

## PDF第338页

relatives that this setback was a necessary step toward recovery. $ ^{198} $ The fact that Bao's relatives believed in Wu's ability to achieve therapeutic success made them more likely to enact “scenarios of efficacy” (Kleinman 1997: 33) that helped healing to take place. In the case of Bao, we have no evidence that such a process took place, but this is not the kind of information that Chinese doctors would have recorded in their files.

Most readers will probably still believe that emetics and purgatives, two of the oldest and most reviled ways of treating “the insane,” cannot effect real, lasting cures, even with the assistance of compassionate relatives. But something in Wu’s approach went beyond the mechanical ingestion of laxatives. After Bao had become “largely lucid” as a result of repeated purges, Wu both rebuked and exhorted him. Filial behavior, Wu proclaimed, was more important than examination success and Bao had failed his family in his pursuit of something over which he had no control. Wu’s moralizing was more than ex post facto embellishment for his elite audience. The re-assertion of proper social roles was part of the cure itself. In a way, Wu provided Bao with a possibility to re-symbolize his experience (see Corin 1993) so that it was no longer defined purely by failure.

Wu Tang's dialogue with Mr. Bao may be a relevant example of what French ethnopsychiatrist Tobie Nathan (1993: 101) has called "the effect of the therapist's implicit theoretical construction in the triggering of therapeutic interaction."$^{199}$ Unfortunately, the dynamics of Bao's local world, his embodied experience of illness and therapy, and the way he personally reacted to Wu Tang's exhortations—all key factors in his possible cure—are crucially missing, so we cannot go far beyond a few speculations. But let us at least examine what Wu Tang himself thought he was doing.

## PDF第339页

In a remarkable section of Curing the Ills of Medicine called “On the Necessity of Announcing the Cause when Treating Inner Harm” (治內傷須祝由論), Wu Tang emphasized the importance to the healing process of making patients change their point of view on themselves. $ ^{200} $ Wu’s text was a rare medical articulation of the importance of dialogue in effecting a cure.

The term  $ \underline{\text{zbuyou}} $ 祝由 ("summoning the cause") usually referred to the ritual removal of ghosts, but Wu gave it a peculiar meaning better suited to his medical orientation.

The two characters “zhu you” 祝由 come from the Basic Questions. $ ^{201} $ Zhu means “to announce” (告); $ ^{202} $ you means that from which an illness appears. In later ages, $ ^{203} $ “zhu you department” was taken to refer to exorcists and was placed among the thirteen medical specializations. $ ^{204} $ Yet the Inner Canon says that if you believe exorcists and do not believe physicians, [illnesses] are untreatable. $ ^{205} $ How then can exorcists be ranked among the medical specialties! I say that whenever one treats inner harm, it is necessary to “announce the cause” (zhu you), to announce in detail where the illness came from and to make this known to the sick person so that [he or she] will dare not have another fit. $ ^{206} $ In addition, one must meticulously examine

## PDF第340页

and understand departures from the correct $ ^{207} $ and scrutinize in depth $ ^{208} $ the hidden emotions of exhausted men and pensive women. $ ^{209} $ [One must then] exhort them with amiable words, $ ^{210} $ caution them $ ^{211} $ with solemn words, and inspire reverential fear in them with words of danger. You need to make them happy at heart and sincerely compliant, after which marvelous results can be obtained. In my life, I have been served by this [approach] in innumerable instances of illnesses that could not be treated, such as lone abdominal swelling, $ ^{212} $ Wood-riding-on-Earth, $ ^{213} $ dry-Blood phtisia, $ ^{214} $ dysphagia, $ ^{215} $ upturned stomach [i.e., nausea], and madness. $ ^{216} $ This is what is meant in Mr. Ye [Gui]'s case files when it is said that herbs and plants without emotions cannot treat emotional illnesses. According to the common saying, there

## PDF第341页

are four types of persons that are difficult to treat: old monks, widows, maidens, $ ^{217} $ and young boys; and four types of illnesses that are difficult to treat: [those related to] alcohol, sex, money, and anger. To treat these difficult people and illnesses, you must rely on that chatty three-inch tongue. [Here I have] dared to assess for people of my inclination the heart-racking [that is needed for] saving people. $ ^{218} $

按祝由二字 出自素問 祝告也 由病之所以出也 後世以巫家焉祝由科并列於十三科之中 内經謂信巫不信醫 不治 巫豈可列之醫科中哉 吾謂凡治内傷者 必先祝由 詳告以病之所由來 使病人知之 而不敢再犯 又必細體變風變雅 曲察勞人思婦之隱情 婉言以開導之 莊言以驚覺之 危言以悚懼之 必使之心悅誠服 而後可以奏效如神 余一生得力於此 有必不可治之病 如單腹脹 木乘土 乾血痨 噎食 反胃 癲狂之類 不可枚舉 葉氏案中調無情之草木 不能治有情之病 亦此義也 俗語云有四等難治之人 老僧 寡婦 室女 童男是也 有四等難治之病 酒 色 財 氣是也 難治之人 難治之病 須憑三寸不爛之舌以治之 救人之苦心 敢以質之同志

Most Chinese physicians accepted that illnesses of all kinds could be triggered by emotional imbalances, but very few worked directly on emotions during therapy. They focused instead on the processes upon which they thought emotions supervened. Wu Tang, however, worked through emotions to ensure that even illnesses that had no emotional manifestations—dysphagia, “lone abdominal swelling”—would not recur. Talking was an adjunct to the medicinal cure, but by dialoguing with Mr. Bao after the drugs had improved his condition, Wu tried to make sure the patient would not fall into the same problem.

### 7.8 Conclusion: the nature of clinical practice

Wu Tang's case told us about aspects of medicine that general treatises usually ignored: the nature of diagnoses, the moral aspects of madness, the shifting of therapeutic

## PDF第342页

strategies over a few days of treatment, and the complex negotiation of medical efficacy that took place between a physician and his patient's relatives. Some of Wu's approaches were common, but his use of dialogue and his attempt to reinstate Mr. Bao into meaningful social relationships were almost unique in the rich clinical records of Chinese medicine.

Physicians who understood mental activities as supervenient upon organic processes rarely considered their patients' psychology (or their words) relevant to diagnostic and treatment. The patient's pulse and general bodily state counted more than his or her feelings. $ ^{219} $ More than cognitive dispositions, behavior was the primary target of treatment. Few pre-twentieth-century Chinese medical thinkers focused on the dynamic self when they explained the appearance of mad acts and mad words. Although they sometimes considered mental processes relevant in causality (as when they traced insanity to emotional imbalances), their treatment rarely attempted to work on the patient's psychology. $ ^{220} $

Chinese doctors also gave little role to speech in either diagnosis or therapy. $ ^{221} $ The sole presence of “crazy words” indicated there was a disorder, but the content of this speech was almost never recorded, with one notable exception. Doctors who treated possession symptoms usually found the content of speech revealing of an underlying pathology. Some believed that words revealed the identity of the ghost that was causing trouble. Even physicians who did not think any ghost was at work sometimes interpreted a patient’s words as pointing to the physiological imbalance that was causing the symptoms, as when the color of a patient’s visions was thought to point to the cause of an illness. Knowing the name of a ghost was often necessary if one wanted to dislodge it by ritual means, and knowing the

## PDF第343页

ghost’s grievances could be crucial if one wanted to “summon [and remove] the cause”

(祝由) of the affliction. Acquiring this information required talking to the afflicted person in

some detail. I study medical treatments of possession symptoms in Chapter 8.

The way Chinese physicians understood their patients deemed “mad” lends itself to many of the criticisms that have been leveled at psychiatry. The few doctors who addressed the question of subjective meaning and the possible personal or social roots of psychopathology did so ad hoc, that is, they almost never constructed a doctrinal position that would explain why people became mad for emotional or mental reasons. They sometimes remarked that social causes occasioned organic ones, but they took organic ones as the direct causes of symptoms. They usually thought it sufficient to intervene on these organic processes without consideration for the social and personal roots of suffering.

Ian Hacking has claimed that even the organic is interactive. People can be “made up” (2002 [1983]) and are interactive, not indifferent kinds (1999: 115-17). In therapeutic encounters, the condition of patients may be modified by their ability to react to the way they are interpreted and treated. One way of triggering such an interaction was to talk to patients on purpose, as Wu Tang did with Mr. Bao. But triggering a “meaning response” (Moerman 2002) was usually a more central part of the cure for patients whose visions and delirium had a certain consistency. Healers then tried to improve their patients’ condition by working through what they perceived. Some of this re-symbolizing could contribute to some people’s recovery (7.7) or simply feed their delirium (14.3). This kind of re-contextualization was not unique to medical approaches, since ritual treatments also transposed social sufferings into a language that gave agency to demonic agents. But as we shall see in the next chapter, even physicians could accept that ghosts made people sick, and they designed methods to treat patients who appeared possessed.
