## 8 

## Ghosts or mucus?

Possession symptoms and their treatments in Ming and Qing medicine

I also saw a bearded figure lifting my oilcloth screen, but he suddenly disappeared. I was startled and I started to feel befuddled inside: I wanted to seek death.

又見一鬍子將油簾揭起 倏忽不見 吃了一驚 心內恍

惚起來 自欲尋死

Licentiate Xu Ding 徐鼎, recounting a vision (1768) $ ^{1} $

How can demonic affliction make people sick! It is just that some disorders look like demonic affliction.

鬼祟豈能病人 不過病似鬼祟耳

Yu Zhen 俞震 in a note to a medical case file (1778) $ ^{2} $

### 8.1 Spectral affliction in late-imperial medicine

Qing men and women often caught sight of “ghosts” and felt the result of their malicious actions. To banish these demons, the relatives of afflicted people could hire rituals specialist, doctors who claimed they could oust specters with drugs, or physicians like Yu Zhen who denied the demonic etiology altogether, but claimed they knew methods to cure possession symptoms. Chapter 13 will explain in more detail the ways in which common people tried to cure their mad-acting relatives, including cases in which they sought the help of both exorcists and physicians.

## PDF第345页

In this chapter, I discuss the ways in which physicians explained and handled symptoms that resembled those of involuntary possession. $ ^{3} $ By possession, I refer to invasion by a non-human (or no longer human) force that could supposedly control the thoughts, acts, and speech of their victim. Possession was expressed by verbs like ping 憑, yi 依, or fu 附, which meant “attaching to” or “relying upon” a person. Ghosts were also often said to “enter” (ru 入) the body of their victims. All these terms were not part of specialized medical terminology, but physicians used them when they discussed cases of “possession.”

Regardless of whether they believed in ghosts, most scholarly healers included sections on “demonic affliction” (鬼祟), “affliction by evil” (邪祟), “spectral illnesses” (祟病), or “evil demons” (邪鬼) in their treatises or case collections. Ancient texts had attributed all kinds of ailments to spirits, from toothaches to painful childbirth. $ ^{4} $ Most late-imperial doctors, however, attributed to ghostly possession chiefly a cluster of symptoms that looked like those of madness. These “possession symptoms” included delirium, strange speech, unprovoked grief, extreme apathy, hallucinations and the desire to injure oneself.

The concept of sui 崇 is almost as old as the Chinese writing system itself. Donald Harper has glossed sui (in ancient usage) as a calamity brought about by spirits in response to human conduct of which they disapproved. $ ^{5} $ Because of the several layers of meaning it acquired through time, sui is difficult to translate, but for late imperial times I shall gloss it as “specter” or “spectral affliction,” or as “affliction” when it occurs in compounds like xiesui 邪祟 (“affliction by evil”) and guisui 鬼祟 (“demonic affliction”).

## PDF第346页

We know from oracle-bone inscriptions that the ruling elite of the Shang “dynasty” (16 $ ^{th} $-11 $ ^{th} $ century BCE) had attributed most illnesses to curses (sui) by royal ancestors (Unschuld 1985: 19-28). By late imperial times, the possible sources of such misfortunes had diversified: they could now be caused by the souls of dead people, by shape-shifting animal spirits (often foxes), and by the looming population of goblins and malicious wraiths that were thought to roam along thoroughfares or near old temples. The ghosts of the dead typically looked menacingly hirsute, but “foxes” preferred to assume well-trimmed human shapes—alluring emanations that could dupe maidens and make scholars drool.

Whereas ritual texts that proposed methods to remove these beings gave little detail about how they affected the body, medical sources often described ghosts as entering the body of their victims and occupying specific organs. As such, late-imperial sui (“spectral afflictions”) thus differed from the sui (“calamities”) of the Shang era, which did not depend on control over the body of the victim. $ ^{6} $

This chapter broadens the sphere of “madness” as an object of historical inquiry. Although “mad” behavior could be understood in different ways, people who construed such behavior differently still knew that they were discussing the same acts. I aim to show that no two approaches to these symptoms were inherently incompatible, and that all of them could be blended through practice, as when physicians claimed they could treat possession with drugs, or when they argued that ghosts triggered a series of pathological reactions identical to those that occurred during purely organic illnesses.

To emphasize the uncertainty that existed in many people's minds about whether crazy acts were caused by illness or demonic invasion, I first explain how the symptoms of possession resembled the manifestations of medically understood insanity. I then discuss

## PDF第347页

Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) influential view that possession symptoms were commonly triggered by mucus. $ ^{7} $ The third section tries to interpret the dramatic decrease in mentions of exorcistic healers in late-imperial cases that physicians or their patients' relatives explicitly attributed to "evil ghosts" or designated as "spectral affliction." I suggest that shamans and elite physicians no longer competed for the same patients. Yet, as I show in the fourth section, even in the late nineteenth century many elite physicians still ascribed illness to the action of various kinds of ghosts. The "medical exorcisms" they performed often borrowed from ritual therapies that today we associate more readily with Daoism than with scholarly medicine. $ ^{8} $ The final section explains how some physicians explained and treated hallucinations, a special type of possession symptom.

### 8.2 The ambiguous closeness of madness and possession symptoms

People from the times and places I study often found resemblances between what they called dian or kuang and what they called “spectral affliction” (sui). These categories were not equivalent, but both specialists and commoners found them meaningfully related in a number of contexts. First, acts designated as “mad” could be attributed to possession, because the terms that meant “madness” did not necessarily entail a medical explanation. Even “illnesses” (bing病) were not always explained in ways we would call medical. Taoist texts routinely attributed “madness” (diankuang 颠狂) to possession and proposed treatments for it. Diankuang was one of the many ills that texts from the Daofa buiyuan 道法會元—a ritual compendium probably compiled in the early 15 $ ^{th} $ century—professed they

## PDF第348页

could treat. $ ^{9} $ Some passages warned that many kinds of spirits could make people crazy. $ ^{10} $

Others called “mad words and blithe speech” kinds of “spectral afflictions” (狂言妄语…

此等之祟) and said that “specters with a form… render people mad” (諸有形之祟…令人

顛狂). $ ^{11} $ If these texts did not assert that madness was always caused by demons, at least they

implied that specters could cause some cases of mad behavior. What is crucial is that religious,

medical, and legal texts alike could use terms like diankuang to refer a similar set of symptoms.

Medical texts expressed the similarity between the symptoms of possession and those of medical madness in several ways. Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

## PDF第349页

Yongcui 李用粹 wrote an entry called “Madness resembles spectral affliction” (癫狂似祟)

in his Collected Supplements on Symptoms and Treatments (Zhengzhi buibu 證治匯補; 1687).

Another sign of the ambiguous meaning of possession symptoms was that many physicians

treated as the result of organic imbalances symptoms that patients attributed to possession. $ ^{14} $

In non-medical contexts, mad behavior was often understood and handled as if it resulted from possession. When Lin Shiyuan started to “speak and dance madly” (狂言跳舞) in 1762, his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪); she sent a relative to invite a Taoist master (道師) to chant scriptures and implore deities (誦經祇禳) on his behalf. $ ^{15} $ The Kangxi emperor (r. 1662-1722) was so perplexed by his son Yinren’s reckless behavior that he first attributed it to sorcery and evil spirits. $ ^{16} $ When in 1713 he decided to remove Yinren as heir-apparent after decades of trouble, he had resigned himself that his son’s madness was an illness (瘋狂成疾 and 狂疾) and that Yinren would never recover. $ ^{17} $ The attribution of madness to possession seemed natural in literature as well, as when the son of a corrupt official in a story by Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1797) was said to have “suddenly caught madness” (忽得瘋疾) and it later turned out that the young man was being possessed. $ ^{18} $

## PDF第350页

###### 8.3 Possession... or mucus?

Considering the resemblance between the symptoms of mania and possession, it is not surprising that physicians devised ways to strengthen their interpretive claims on such symptoms. By arguing that disorders that looked as if they were caused by demons could in fact be caused by either mucus or depletion, Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) managed to discuss possession symptoms without discussing ghosts (4.4). Zhu's argument started its rise to textual dominance in the writings of his disciples and intellectual followers. The sections on "mucus" and "depletion and wasting disorders" (虚損) of Liu Chun's Yuji weiji 玉機微義 (1396) discussed the idea that mucus caused possession-like symptoms. $ ^{19} $ Dai Liang's "Biography of the Old Man of Cinnabar Creek" ("Danxi weng zhuan" 丹溪翁傳), which contained one of the mucus-possession cases I translated in 4.4, was appended to the widely circulated Huizhou version of the Danxi xinfā 丹溪心法 (1482). Wang Ao's 王鏊 Gu danfang 古單方 (completed in 1496) contained the earliest section on "affliction by evil" that I have found. $ ^{20} $ In it, Wang acknowledged Zhu's view that the symptoms of such afflictions could indeed be caused by mucus, and cited Zhu's warning that those afflicted by mucus possession might die if they were treated by exorcism.

At the beginning of their sections on spectral afflictions, many late-imperial treatises cited verbatim the passage from Zhu's Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 that started with "Blood and Qi are the body's/person's consciousness" (血氣者 身之神也; 27a). Most major medical writers of the sixteenth century discussed Zhu's pronouncement in their writings. Yu Tuan's

## PDF第351页

虞搏 Yixue zhengzhuan 醫學正傳 (1515; 5.7), the composite Danxi xiansheng zhifa xinyao 丹溪先生治法心要 (1536; 5.5), Li Chan's Introduction to Medical Learning (1575), Zhang Siwei's 張四維 Yimen mizhi 醫門秘旨 (ca. 1576), Zhang Sanxi's 張三錫 Yixue liuyao 醫學六要 (1585), and most of Gong Tingxian's popular books (7.3) probably enhanced the dominance of Zhu's idea in medical circles. $ ^{21} $

The claim that mucus could create strange disorders had thus became part of mainstream medical discourse by the sixteenth century, and it became such commonsense in the Qing period that it no longer needed attribution. An Anthology of Strange Illnesses (Qizheng bui 奇症匯) compiled in 1786 recounted the case of a man who saw lions in front of him. Without further remarks his illness was attributed to mucus and treated with emetics. $ ^{22} $ About a case of “demonic infestation” (鬼疰) included in his Medical Case Files Ancient and Present, With Commentaries (Guijin yian an 古今醫案按; 1778), Yu Zhen remarked that “This is what is called ‘mucus [causes] many strange symptoms’; it is not something to be marveled at” (此所謂痰多怪證亦不為奇). $ ^{23} $ He commented elsewhere that the commonest treatment for possession-like symptoms in his time was “clearing Heat and guiding mucus” (清熱導痰). $ ^{24} $ Medicine for Sots (Chunzi yi 蠢子醫), a book that one man wrote for his grandchildren in the late nineteenth century, observed twice that the main way to treat possession

## PDF第352页

disorders was to attack mucus: “when mucus and saliva make a demonic affliction and Cold

and Heat go back and forth, the methods of guiding or vomiting [mucus] will be the only

ones to work” (痰涎為祟 寒熱往來 非用導吐法不行). $ ^{25} $

### 8.4 Diminished competition between physicians and shamans?

Even if Zhu Zhenheng was never part of an organized campaign to root out popular practices and did not speak in the name of a profession, he clearly aimed to reject exorcism as a treatment for possession symptoms. His understanding of body and illness disqualified shamanic healers to the benefit of people who—like himself—claimed they could reestablish organic balance with herbal or mineral drugs. Though there is no reason to doubt that he was genuinely concerned with his patients' well-being, his interpretation of possession symptoms can also be understood as a move in a competition with other kinds of healers.

Ascribing mad symptoms to mucus was a way of saying they were not caused by ghosts and thus of invalidating any recourse to ritual specialists. One of Zhu's cases explicitly mentioned this contest between healers. $ ^{26} $

An adult woman surnamed Jin. During the hot summer months, she went to a banquet. When she returned, her mother-in-law reprimanded her for not sitting at the proper place. She then felt so ashamed that she contracted this illness. In the midst of incoherent words, she often repeated one phrase: "your servant is wrong." All her pulses were accelerated and strung. I said: "This is not evil, but simply an illness. All we need is to replenish her Spleen, clear Heat, and channel mucus: in a few days she will naturally become calm." But her family did not believe this. They

## PDF第353页

invited several shamanic healers, who spurted [charmed] water and [recited]

incantations for her. A little more than ten days later, she died.

金氏婦壯年 暑月赴筵 歸乃姑詢其坐次失序 遂赧然自愧 因成此病 言語失倫 其中又多問一句曰 奴奴不是 脈皆數而弦 余曰 此非邪乃病也 但與補脾清熱導痰 數日當自安 其家不信 邀數巫者噴水而咒之 旬餘而死

Zhu proposed a medical explanation of why such rituals could fail.

One may ask why treating as though it were evil an illness that was not caused by evil could so swiftly lead to death. I say: She attended a banquet during the hot summer months, when the outer realm was muggy and hot. After she ingested acrid and pungent [aliments], her inner realm also had Heat in stasis, not to mention an old accumulation of mucus, in addition to which she was shamed and sullen. How could this mucus and Heat be vanquished? Now she was frightened by ritual invocations, which frightened her Spirit and made her Blood uneasy. Being spurted upon by ritual water cooled down her body and made her skin denser, making it impossible for sweat to expel [Heat]. Since sweat could not be expelled, muggy Heat started blazing inside. Since her Blood could not come to peace, her Yin [vitality] was dispersed, and since Yang cannot stand alone, how could she not die!

或問曰 病非邪而邪治之 何遽至於死 余曰 暑月赴宴 外境蒸熱 辛辣適口 内境鬱熱 而況舊有積痰 加之愧悶 其痰與熱何可勝言 今乃驚以法尺 是驚其神而血不寧也 噴以法水 是冰其體 密其膚 使汗不得泄也 汗不泄則蒸熱内燔 血不得寧則陰消而陽不能獨立也 不死何俟

As he insisted that food and heat were the sole culprits, Zhu made the chilling point that unfittingly deployed rituals were not only useless, but could be life-threatening.

Zhu's argument made sense in a general debate between the merits of demonic and organic approaches to illness, but it also reveals competition between different experts.

## PDF第354页

Almost all the Jin and Yuan physicians who recorded madness cases alluded to this rivalry. Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) explained that the relatives of a man had burned paper talismans (燒符), organized Taoist rituals (作醮), and hired a dancing medium to exorcise the ghost (問鬼跳誣) they thought had crazed him after he had fallen off a horse. $ ^{27} $ Wang Haogu 王好古 (fl. first half of the 13 $ ^{th} $ century) noted that the relatives of a woman who had become crazy “all thought it was a ghost’s making” (人皆以為鬼魔). $ ^{28} $ His younger contemporary Luo Tianyi 羅天益 (ca. 1220-1290) similarly reported that people around his mad patient all said that Wind demons were causing the problem (時人皆言風魔了), though the entreaties of an exorcistic master (巫師) had failed to cure him. $ ^{29} $

Whereas most Jin-Yuan physicians invited to treat mad behavior noted that their patients had previously sought the help of shamanic healers, Ming and Qing doctors almost never mentioned exorcists as their competitors. A complete lack of medical case files between the late fourteenth century and approximately 1515 makes me unable to follow this trend in the documents. All we see is an end result: after 1500, demonology was no longer the most criticized approach to mad symptoms in medical treatises and case collections. Instead, medical writers almost always contrasted their own approach with that of other physicians. It is as if shamans had gone underground.

What had happened to make exorcists disappear from medical case records? The evidence is limited. One clear tendency was that families of means now often employed several physicians (either succession or concomitantly), probably because there were enough

## PDF第355页

doctors and enough diversity of medical approaches to justify replacing a failed physician with another one, and because these families could afford the multiple treatments. Perhaps the relatives of patients now tended to ascribe mad behavior to organic causes, thus embracing the supervenience of mental events on organic processes that had long characterized learned medicine (3.2). This possible change in belief (and corresponding change in therapeutic choices) is a little understood phenomenon that remains difficult to explain, and even to ascertain. The widespread adoption of the mucus etiology of a large number of disorders may well have extended to people's perceptions of possession symptoms. $ ^{30} $ It could also be that the wealthy and the educated (the main customers of scholarly physicians) had widely embraced Zhu Xi's naturalistic view of ghosts. $ ^{31} $

But these possible transformations in the outlook of patients probably had its counterpart in changes in medical discourse. Instead of denying the idiom of invasion by evil, medicine after Zhu Zhenheng recuperated it. Zhu had proposed a natural explanation of how possession symptoms occurred and suggested an appropriate treatment. As his views spread in medical circles, doctors told readers and patients that visions, odd speech, and unprovoked grief were not always caused by possession.

This re-scripting of signs need not have reached hegemonic dominance to be convincing to some. Although many people undoubtedly still believed that ghosts and evil influences could cause madness, many others no longer did, and now first turned to physicians to treat those symptoms. $ ^{32} $ Zhu's approach had allowed doctors not only to criticize the conceptual ground on which exorcism depended, but to claim ability to cure the

## PDF第356页

same symptoms on the basis of a mucus-based medical explanation, which became central to medicine by the fourteenth century (4.5, 4.6) and even became respectable in non-medical circles by the eighteenth century (13.5).

Among people that elite physicians treated after the fifteenth century, it thus seems that fewer tried to handle mad behavior and possession symptoms with exorcistic rituals. More accurately, those who sought the services of scholarly physicians from the sixteenth century onwards were more likely than before to turn to physicians first, and many perhaps only to physicians. This contention, of course, depends on cases recorded by physicians, not on a broad survey of people's therapeutic recourses. We know indeed that Qing people who sought to treat madness often hired (first or exclusively) shamanic healers (13.4), and that plague demons were still widely feared throughout the empire in the nineteenth century. $ ^{33} $

The lack of mention of shamans in Ming and Qing case records may also indicate that highly literate physicians no longer viewed exorcistic healers as serious competitors for patients. Instead of defending the superiority of medicine over other forms of healing, physicians now used case records to defend the merits of certain kinds of medical therapies over others. Those who replenished criticized those who purged, whereas those who preferred attacking therapies insisted that replenishers had worsened their patient's madness. $ ^{34} $ This shift in the nature of medical argument may have corresponded with a change in the social dynamic of medical practice. At least in the most developed regions of the empire, ritual approaches seem no longer to have threatened the practice of some scholarly doctors, who therefore preferred to focus on more direct competitors, that is, physicians who shared their general vision of illness but adopted different therapies to treat

## PDF第357页

patients. Diminishing competition between elite physicians and shamans does not mean that people everywhere stopped turning to ritual specialists or that shamans and physicians practiced in impermeably separate social spheres; only that many physicians had patients who were committed to hiring doctors. In the next section I argue that this diminished competition between exorcists and physicians in some social circles did not depend on a decrease in the belief in ghosts among doctors.

### 8.5 Scholarly exorcists

Although exorcists probably became less active among patients who sought the services of the most literate physicians, the demonological explanation of mad behavior did not disappear from elite medicine. The changes I have traced so far concerned some patients' choice of healers, not the beliefs of all physicians. Although some doctors denied the validity of demonology altogether, many others proposed medical explanations of spectral behavior as well as medical ways to treat what they saw as real demonic affliction. This section should show that medical healers had a variety of ways of interpreting possession symptoms, and therefore madness.

## From demonic Qi to karmically-driven avengers: three kinds of ghosts

Xu Dachun 徐大椿 (1693-1771), from Suzhou, was a great admirer of the Daodejing

道德經 (to which he wrote a commentary during his youth) and an expert at water control

whom authorities readily consulted when came time to renovate dikes and canals in the

vicinity. Xu also helped compile two local gazetteers, for which he wrote sections on water

control and geography. His medical knowledge was also profound enough for him to have

## PDF第358页

written a commentary to the Canon of Problems before age 35. $ ^{35} $ In a biography he wrote after Xu's death, his friend Yuan Mei (1716-1797) claimed that of all the arts in which Xu was proficient, medicine was his strongest. $ ^{36} $ Perhaps because of Xu's connections at court, his were the most recent texts included in the medical section of the Siku quanshu 四庫全書 (completed in 1782). $ ^{37} $ The publication of a collection of his works in 1857 magnified his renown. $ ^{38} $ This compendium included Xu's Case Files from Hui Brook (Huixi yian 洄溪醫案), which were published no less than 40 times in the following fifty years. $ ^{39} $

Xu discussed demonic illnesses in several parts of his extensive works. His support for three different kinds of ghostly afflictions reveals the range of options available to eighteenth-century medical thinkers on this issue. I explain what assumptions supported Xu's positions and try to show how he deployed his doctrines in therapeutic practice.

In a discourse called “On there being illnesses [caused by] ghosts and spirits” (病有鬼神論), Xu presented three interpretations. $ ^{40} $ Attempting to contest both the belief that ghosts could cause all disorders and the doctrine that no harm could come from demons, he first naturalized demons into a form of Qi that inflicted illness by taking advantage of prior depletion: “Ghosts and spirits are just like the deviant [Qi] of Wind, Cold, Summer Heat, and Dampness. When one’s protective Qi is depleted, one is afflicted by Cold; when one’s

## PDF第359页

constructive Qi is depleted, one is afflicted by Heat; when one's spiritual Qi is depleted, one is afflicted by ghosts” (夫鬼神猶風寒暑濕之邪耳 衛氣虛則受寒 營氣虛則受熱 神氣虛則受鬼). $ ^{41} $ Xu's explanation rested on the tenet that everything in the world was made of Qi: the sun and the stars, animals and insects, winds, breath, Blood, and so on. $ ^{42} $ In this scheme, “ghostly Qi” (鬼氣) was just another form of environmental Qi that caused illness by invading depleted areas of the body as heat or wind were known to do. Following Michel Strickmann (2002: 24), we could call this ghostly Qi “spectral pathogens.” $ ^{43} $

The claim that demons could intrude only through a depleted area may have made ghosts less frightening. It meant that demonic illnesses could be prevented and, if they ever happened, treated with drugs that eliminated the depletion that had facilitated invasion. But Xu Dachun described two more ways in which ghosts could affect people.

Besides [the disorders presented above,] there are also illnesses [that result from having] caused offense to demons and spirits; these can be cured by prayers. There are also several origins to [harm caused by the] ghosts [of people who have died] of injustice: there are [cases in which] one's own wrongdoings have [created] deep and inextricable rancor;⁴⁴ there are [cases in which people are] implicated by [the deeds of] their ancestors; there are [also ghosts who will] harm people accidentally or by mistake.⁴⁵ Such matters can be demonstrated and verified, but seem to be something

## PDF第360页

that scholars [Ru 儒, “Confucians”] will not discuss. Nonetheless, abundant [cases]

of this sort—such as those of Gongzi Pengsheng and Bo You—can be found in the

classics and histories. Not a few such occurrences have been witnessed. Such

[afflictions] can be relieved with neither pharmacological minerals nor prayers. $ ^{46} $

其外更有觸犯鬼神之病則祇禱可愈 至於冤遣之鬼則有數端 有自作之孽深仇不可解者 有祖宗貽累者 有過誤害人者 其事鑿鑿可徵 似儒者所不道然見于經史如公子彭生 伯有之類甚多 目覩者亦不少 此則非藥石祇禱所能免矣

Since these ghosts had died a wrongful death, their revenge was purposeful and possibly even justified. Because they were there willfully, vengeful ghosts were much harder to eradicate than those that had moved in to occupy a void. Xu's conclusion about the impossibility to remove these wraiths echoed a statement he made at the end of a text "On Sudden Death" (卒死論): "As for sudden meetings with spirits and demons or unexpected encounters with [the ghosts of people who have died] a wrongful death, these are occurrences of a strange nature that do not belong to the order of illnesses" (至於暴遇神鬼適逢冤譴此又怪異之事不在疾病之類矣). $ ^{47} $

Taking Xu’s views into account, we can identify four distinct positions on ghosts and possession symptoms in late-imperial medical writings: 1) The utter denial of ghosts, attributing possession-like symptoms (including visions) to either mucus or depletion; 2) the naturalization of ghosts by reducing them to a form of Qi, but denying that ghosts could harm people willfully; 3) the belief in real ghosts and in animals that could take human form; both could possess people but could be removed with prayers or medicine; and 4) the belief in karmically driven ghosts who could purposely (and even righteously) harm people, and

## PDF第361页

could be removed with neither medicine nor prayers. Individuals (including doctors) could embrace several of these views at once or blend them together.

Xu Dachun's collection teemed with cases of "roaming souls" (游魂), "loss of soul" (失魂), and "spectral illnesses" (崇病). For instance, a man who had been Xu's classmate but had not succeeded at the official examinations despite his talents for calligraphy heard day and night a voice that vilified him with such odious invectives that he wanted to take his own life. $ ^{48} $ No remedy proved useful. Xu Dachun took no measure other than trying to convince the man that there was no one there, but his classmate eventually killed himself. The frequently noted suicide of possessed patients in the medical literature may have confirmed Xu's view that vengeful ghosts were difficult to extirpate. $ ^{49} $

The idea that spectral illnesses were often characterized by the desire to injure oneself was old and well-established. Ancient medical and religious texts discussed it as a matter of course, and even educated people commonly attributed to possession the sudden desire to commit suicide. In 1768, Manchu Governor of Zhejiang Gioro Yongde 覺羅永德 interpreted licentiate Xu Ding's 徐鼎 suicide attempt in the examination compound as a case of “confusion of mind brought about by encountering a specter” (實係遇祟心迷). Yongde's conclusion matched the most popular explanation of unmotivated suicide attempts. The view that resentful ghosts tried to inveigle people into killing themselves was even

## PDF第362页

integrated into the Administrative Regulations of the Board of War (兵部則例), in an entry

concerning “soldiers who commit suicide after encountering a specter” (兵丁遇祟自盡). $ ^{50} $

Another case by Xu Dachun involved the wife of a doorman in an urban residence. She was being enticed to kill herself by an old female spirit Xu called the "ghost [of someone] who died by hanging" (缢死鬼). $ ^{51} $ Twice, Xu forced his patient to ingest the demonifuge "purple metal ingot" (紫金錠). The ghost left her body each time. Xu told the woman that she should spit the same drug at the spirit if it came again. He recounts that the next time it came, the ghost left angrily, lamenting that its victim wanted to harm her. The therapy Xu proposed combined the gesture of the exorcist with the power of mineral drugs. The ritual expert was the patient herself, but she spurted medicine on the demon instead of water magically empowered with the ashes of a burned talisman, as Taoists would have.

## Nineteenth-century physicians on ghosts

Angelika Messner's decision to end the section on possession of her book (Messner 2000: 111ff) with Xie Xinghuan's "critique of the demonological conception" (1860s) is misleading, because it seems to imply that late-imperial medicine culminated with such a critique. $ ^{52} $ But similar critiques were formulated long before that (for example by Zhu Zhenheng in the 1340s), and many Chinese physicians still accepted demonic etiology late into the nineteenth century.

## PDF第363页

Xu Dachun was indeed not the only educated physician who agreed that ghosts could make people ill. On this issue, Zhang Lufeng 張魯峰 (mid 19 $ ^{th} $ century) made an interesting clarification. After mentioning that the Man 蠻 (a generic name for southern, “non-Chinese” populations), the Yao 瑤, and the Muslims (Huibu 回部) strongly believed in shamans and ghosts, that they prayed every time they became ill, and that Zhejiang women were just the same, he claimed that they “did not know that one can become ill because of ghosts, but also produce ghosts because of illness” (不知有因鬼而得病者 亦有因病而生鬼者). $ ^{53} $ If a healthy person suddenly fainted but recovered after imploring deities, this had been a case of “becoming ill because of ghosts.” But when one became possessed because spiritual Qi was depleted, this was a case of “producing ghosts because of illness.” In the latter case, patients could expel the invader through prayers and sacrifices, but the original disorder still had to be treated medically. When encountering “ghostly illnesses” (鬼疾), Zhang argued, physicians “should not stick to the Confucian doctrine that there are no ghosts” (切無恃迂儒無鬼之論), but they should still use chiefly medical means. If they encountered karmic retribution or unavenged ghosts, however, even sutras, mantras, and Taoist scriptures (仙經) would prove useless. Zhang’s conclusion sounded close to Xu Dachun’s, except that Xu claimed he could get rid of regular ghosts by medical means, whereas Zhang advocated the use of prayers. Both avowed their powerlessness to expel karmically driven wraiths.

Some physicians who adhered to the existence of ghosts even inverted Zhu Zhenheng's warning (8.3) that trying to exorcise people who were not possessed could kill them. In a short section on "spectral affliction" in his Words on Medicine from the Cold Furnace

## PDF第364页

(Lenglu yibua 冷盧醫話; 1858), Lu Yitian 陸以湉 (jinshi 1836, d. 1865) warned that in the “spectral stroke” (中祟) of a man from Hangzhou—where Lu lived until it fell to the Taiping army in 1860—the ingestion of medicine had led to death. $ ^{54} $ Many physicians mentioned in a case by Gu Jinshou 顧金壽 (fl. turn of the 19 $ ^{th} $ century) likewise had not dared to use drugs to treat possession symptoms and had resorted to prayers instead. $ ^{55} $

Patients who thought they were possessed sometimes refused pharmacological treatments. $ ^{56} $ Refusal to ingest drugs could be interpreted as the ghost’s refusal to swallow substances that might harm it. Xu Dachun noted that ghosts saw cinnabar as fire and were burned by it. $ ^{57} $ Li Wenrong’s 李文榮 (b. 1772; see next sub-section) younger cousin, who displayed possession symptoms, refused to take Li’s drugs, saying they would kill him. $ ^{58} $ Li attributed these words to the ghostly entity that he believed was controlling his nephew.

Zhao Zhuquan's 趙竹泉 Complementary Essentials of Medicine (Yimen buyao 醫門補要; 1897) explained why spirits could cause illness: "Those who have been arrogant toward the spirits will either be affected by an evil curse or be harassed by [the ghosts of people they

## PDF第365页

have] wronged by their acts in previous incarnations" (凡有褻慢神祇 或染邪祟 或前身冤孽來纏). $ ^{59} $ These purposeful ghosts created symptoms like “confusion of spirit and agitation” (神昏煩亂), talking about ghosts at night (夜間專言鬼神事), sporadic fevers (乍發熱 乍不發熱), lack of sleep, ulcers, incommodious pains, and purulent sores. Zhao maintained that “Sufferers will need to accumulate good [deeds], quickly confess and regret [the acts that provoked the illness], or burn paper money, and they can save themselves” (患者後須積善 早為懺悔 或焚冥鑑 自可挽救). He even cited Confucius himself to elucidate why spirits could cause illness: “The Sage Kong (i.e., Confucius) said ‘keep ghosts and spirits at a distance by revering them’ (孔聖云 敬鬼神而遠之 是當虔誠以盡禮非可諂瀆以邀福也). $ ^{60} $ This [means that one] has to be pious and sincere in conducting the rites, and that one cannot invite good fortune by sycophancy or arrogance. $ ^{61} $ Dissecting this passage to highlight supposedly “Confucian,” “Buddhist,” and “popular” influences would only show that these categories have no explanatory value. The next section presents a few cases by an early-nineteenth-century doctor to show that even a self-proclaimed “Confucian” physician could integrate ghostly pathologies into scholarly medicine.

## Li Wenrong's medical exorcisms

The case I made for a diminished competition between physicians and shamans in late-imperial times is reinforced by the fact that even patients (and their relatives) who

## PDF第366页

attributed their problems to demonic possession sometimes turned to physicians to cure their ills. Perhaps because all healers must to some extent respond to the expectations of their patients and their families (including their beliefs about the causes of illness), many late-imperial physicians had integrated “ghost talk” and demonic etiologies into their medical interpretations. Some patients sought the help of medical specialists (rather than—or in addition to—ritual experts) because they believed doctors had ways to get rid of at least some demons. Fang Lue 方略 (fl. first half 19 $ ^{th} $ century) could thus plausibly recount that the relatives of a patient who suffered from postpartum madness “all called this ‘evil affliction’: they called me to examine her” (咸稱邪祟 迎余診視). $ ^{62} $

A physician named Li Wenrong 李文榮 (b. 1772) was so confident in his exorcistic abilities that he even convinced many patients and their relatives that non-human entities—the “Three Corpses” (三屍), the souls of wrongfully dead people, spectral Qi, or simply errant hungry ghosts preying upon depleted people—were causing illnesses the patients had thought were natural. Li lived and practiced chiefly in Zhenjiang 鎮江, a city on the south bank of the Yangzi River at the confluence of the Grand Canal, downstream from Nanjing. $ ^{63} $ He also treated patients in Suzhou, Nanjing, and Yangzhou, usually on special invitations. $ ^{64} $ Since Li Wenrong did not “hang a gourd” 懸壺 (i.e., did not own a medical shop), most of his patients were relatives, friends, neighbors, or people who had been introduced to him by

## PDF第367页

dignitaries he knew. $ ^{65} $ Among his patients was the powerful official Tao Shu 陶澍 (1779-1839), whom Li treated in late 1825 soon after Tao had become Governor of Jiangsu (residing in Nanjing). $ ^{66} $ Tao later sent for Li in difficult situations, as toward the end of 1831 when he was vomiting Blood. $ ^{67} $ In 1835 Tao Shu wrote a preface to Li's case records, where he praised Li for having saved his life and explained that he had become friends with Li (遂成契好) and liked to discuss literature and medicine with him in his time off from official matters (公餘之暇 輒與暢談文字 窮究崎俞). $ ^{68} $

Sometime after 1811, Li had also befriended Wang Zhizheng 王之政 (1753-1830?; better known by his bao Jiufeng 九峰).⁶⁹ Wang was from Dantu 丹徒 (a county in Zhenjiang prefecture) and practiced in Yangzhou, on the north bank of the Yangzi River opposite Zhenjiang. It may have been Wang who transmitted his well-known admiration for Yu Chang 喻昌 (1585-1664) to Li Wenrong.⁷⁰ In his own writings, Li hailed Yu’s explanations of the Treatise on Cold Injury over hundreds of others⁷¹ and praised Yu’s case files with particular intensity for their meticulous recording of names, recipes, and effects of

## PDF第368页

prescriptions, qualities that he found lacking in Ye Gui's Guide to Clinical Practice (Linzheng

zhinan 臨證指南, 1766; 7.4). $ ^{72} $ Li eventually named his own collection of case records

Emulating “Notes on My Judgment” (Fang Yuyi cao 仿寓意草), after Yu's opus. $ ^{73} $

Li presented himself as a “scholarly physician” (or “Confucian doctor” 儒醫). In 1825, a local official recommended Li to an administrator who had arrived in Zhenjiang and asked to consult a “scholarly doctor” for his persistent diarrhea. $ ^{74} $ Li himself claimed he was a “classical scholar” (儒生) rather than a doctor, $ ^{75} $ and compared medical books to the Confucian classics: the Inner Canon corresponded with the “Five Classics,” Zhang Ji’s 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Essentials of the Golden Casket (third century CE) were counterparts to the Four Books, and Yu Chang’s writings matched Zhu Xi’s commentaries on the Classics.

The three cases of madness that Li Wenrong recorded from memory in his collection of case files (which contained 34 cases) all involved ghosts. Li recalled that these three cases had taken place in 1811, 1819, and 1828. $ ^{76} $ All three patients were from Zhenjiang. Atypically for a physician, Li called their madness feng 瘋, fengji 瘋疾, or fengzheng 瘋症 instead of kuang, dian, or diankuang. He even referred to one of his patients as a “madman” (fengzi 瘋子), but he used feng to refer to crazy behavior, not to a form of madness distinct from kuang. $ ^{77} $

## PDF第369页

In 1811, neighbor Li Dengming 属登銘 had suffered from intermittent fevers and Li Wenrong conventionally treated him by sweating therapy. $ ^{78} $ Since Li Dengming “was fond of his wife and enjoyed drinking” (好内嗜飲), the doctor diagnosed that he was probably “Yin depleted” (陰虛居多). $ ^{79} $ The sweating had further weakened him so that “his essential spirits were also seriously diminished” (精神亦甚減). When a fire occurred one night in the southern part of Zhenjiang, Li Dengming ordered servants to accompany him to the door to observe the conflagration. Suddenly he exclaimed: “The God of Earth just passed by, did you see?” (適地坊老爺過去汝等見否) When the servants answered they hadn’t, Li became excited: “How could you not see him! He was clearly wearing a high hat and a dark blue robe, carrying an umbrella on his left shoulder and a palm-leaf fan in his right hand. He just passed by! Let’s quickly shut the door and get in.” (如何未見明明戴高冒穿青袍左扛雨傘右持芭蕉扇適才過去我等速關門進去) He became crazy (feng) that night, making a ruckus and destroying objects around the house. He even tried to attack his beloved wife with a kitchen knife, but she managed to find refuge under a bed. The next morning, she sent someone to invite Li Wenrong to come “save [her] life” (救命).

When Li Wenrong arrived, his neighbor was trying to attack people with a broken bowl, but "when [Li Dengming] saw that I arrived, he immediately put it down" (見予至忽然放下). Seeing that the man looked frightened, Li Wenrong decided to keep him in awe. "With a righteous voice and a stern look, I told him to sit down" (正言厲色謂之曰坐下) and to extend his hand to take his pulse. The patient complied and the doctor continued by asking him why he was making such a fuss and why he had tried to kill his wife. "Then with

## PDF第370页

filthy words he told me that his wife née Wang had done such-and-such with a fox inside the walls, and that a white monkey was fanning his [her?] feet with a large fan" (伊則穢語謂妻王氏與狐狸在牆內如何 又白猴子持大扇扇伊腳). Calling his friend's ravings "crazy words" (瘋語), Li stopped asking questions.

As soon as the doctor left the room, the patient resumed his uproar. "Relatives and friends filled the hall and asked me what could be done" (親朋滿座問予何法). Li Wenrong explained that evil specters (邪祟), like other ills, could enter the body through depleted areas (從虛而入). The intermittent fevers that had afflicted Li Dengming had manifested themselves in the "Minor-Yang Gallbladder tract" (少陽膽經), so that his Gallbladder was certainly depleted. When Dengming had encountered a specter, it had entered his Gallbladder, and he had become crazy (適遇邪祟乘虛入膽而成瘋).

Li Wenrong's explanation of his neighbor's possession played on the conventional Chinese association of the Gallbladder with courage.

Older brother Li is usually most timorous ('his Gallbladder is small'). He does not dare to utter a single insulting word and his fondness for conjugal life can be called most earnest. Now he suddenly wants to kill someone, and of all people it is one he has always loved and respected. Madness is temerity ('large Gallbladder'). How can it not be a specter residing in his midst that is making him do it?80

且夫厲兄平日之膽最小 一語不敢傷人 琴瑟之好 稱為最篤 今忽欲殺人

且為素所愛敬者 瘋則膽大 豈非祟據其中而有以使之耶

As far as I know, religious explanations of possession rarely included details on which organs demons settled in. Li's account was thus clearly a medicalized account of possession.

## PDF第371页

Starting with a gloss of the term he used to refer to his friend's madness (feng 瘋), Li explained the bodily mechanisms by which ghosts could cause sudden changes in the personality of their victims.

The character feng 瘋 [“madness”] comes from feng 風 [“Wind”]. The severity or lightness of madness are like the strength or weakness of Wind, just like the sudden fits and stops of madness compare to the sudden rise and fall of winds. Evil specters cause madness in the person they have struck mostly by relying on the force of the person’s internal Winds. Wood gives rise to Wind: this [takes place in] the Liver and Gallbladder [i.e., the Yin-Yang pair of viscera associated with Wood in the scheme of the Five Agents]. The Liver and Gallbladder are in a relation of surface and depth to each other. $ ^{81} $ In the present case, evil has entered the Gallbladder, and it will inevitably borrow the strength of the Gallbladder to stir up the Liver. Wood creates Wind and Wind creates Fire, which creates mucus. As mucus and Fire wrestle with each other, their potency (shi 勢) increases greatly, $ ^{82} $ so that the person’s Cloud and Bone souls and Spiritual-clarity are all disturbed and cannot hold on their own.

夫瘋字從風　有風象　然瘋之或重或輕　猶風之或大或小　瘋之忽發忽止

猶風之忽起忽息　邪祟之中人而成瘋也　未嘗不憑藉人身內風之力　惟木生風　肝膽是也　肝膽相為表裡　今邪入於膽　必將借膽之力而鼓動乎肝　因木生風　因風生火　因火生痰　痰火相搏　勢乃大張　而人之魂魄神明皆擾亂而不能自守

These ghosts worked in ways curiously similar to the prototypical script of disturbance by mucus and Fire (4.6, 7.3). But they could still speak and control their victim's actions.

Li resorted to more metaphors to explain why there was still hope:

## PDF第372页

In the present case, the evil specters have fortunately just entered. They are comparable to bandits who have just arrived in a hostel and are still not acquainted with the neighbors on either side. Their potency is still solitary and it is easy to chase them away. But if one fails to treat them $ ^{83} $ immediately and lets them occupy their position securely for some time, the brigands’ den will become sturdy. Wind will rouse their potency, Fire enhance their awesomeness, and mucus assist their strength, as when wicked people have settled in a place for a long time and their gang has formed completely. Chasing them away will then be truly difficult.

今幸邪祟初入 譬如匪人初至旅邸 左鄰右舍並無相識 其勢尚孤 驅逐亦易 若失今不治 盤踞既久 巢穴已固 風鼓其勢 火張其威 痰助其力 如惡人居久定而黨已成 則驅逐良難也

To treat his patient, Li Wenrong prescribed the “Gallbladder-warming decoction” 温膽湯 enriched with two ingredients, one of which was gentian (“dragon-gallbladder herb” 龍膽草), a strong purgative. “This recipe specializes in purging the Gallbladder, making the specters unable to live at ease. It also clears Fire and transforms mucus, so that the specters will have nothing to rely on” (此方專於瀉膽使邪祟不能寧居又兼清火化痰使邪祟無所憑藉). By using vocabulary like “relying on” (憑), which usually referred to possession, Li Wenrong was perhaps suggesting that possessing entities rested not on a generalized body (體, 身), but more specifically on pathological substances like Fire and mucus. In any case, one dose of the purgative put an end to Li Dengming’s mania. The physician then replaced the recipe with the “Ten-ingredient Gallbladder-warming [decoction]” (十味溫膽), in which he replaced cooked foxglove with raw foxglove because raw foxglove would supposedly replenish the Gallbladder. Li called this “the method of shutting the door after the bandits

## PDF第373页

are gone” (贼去關門法也), which aimed to prevent the return of specters by securing the depleted organ through which they had first gained a foothold into the body.

With an undeniable talent for story-telling, Li Wenrong had first managed to convince his neighbors that specters had possessed Li Dengming, but then explained the working of the specters in largely organic ways and proceeded to remove the invaders by purely medical means! Li Wenrong's cure of his neighbor was the case that made the illustrious Wang Zhizheng 王之政 (1753-1830?) notice Li (see above, p. 349).

Another patient of Li's was his younger sister's son, who in 1819 suddenly came back from Taixing 泰興—about 50km east of Zhenjiang—looking disconsolate. $ ^{84} $ He would not explain the reason of his gloom, but he became crazy a few days later and tried to kill himself. His speech was normal but “he said there was a female ghost in his belly telling him to seek death and that he had no choice but to obey her” (惟言有女鬼在其腹中 教之尋死 不得不依). A few days later Li took his pulse, which was “alternately loose and accelerated, but thin and weak when pressed. I knew that his Yang Qi was greatly depleted, and that there was indeed a demonic entity possessing [lit. ‘relying upon’] him” (診其脈乍疏乍數 而按之細弱 知其陽氣大虛 實有鬼物憑之). To the Yang replenishers he prescribed, Li added a few ingredients that were known to repel ghosts: cinnabar (a red mineral that ghosts took for fire), orpiment (one form of toxic arsenic ore), and winged spindle tree, a drug whose Chinese name (鬼箭羽) meant “demon-arrow feather.” $ ^{85} $

Unlike the physicians I discussed above, Li did not believe that “vengeful ghosts” were inexpugnable. He therefore recommended to his sister a two-fold treatment: “This is

## PDF第374页

the soul of someone who has been wronged. You can start by inviting eminent monks to perform a feeding ceremony and then make him [Li's nephew] ingest these drugs, and he should become better" (此冤魂也可先請高僧施食因服此藥當可愈也). After a few complications (the ghost tried to convince the young man that the drugs would kill him), the family proceeded according to Li's suggestions and the wraith left. After one month of taking replenishing drugs, the patient went to see his uncle, who asked him where the ghost had come from. It turned out that while working in Taixing, the young man had met "a young married woman from a small household" (小戶少婦) who often took her family's clothes to be cleaned by the shop where the young man worked. Her husband suspected that they had an illicit relationship and started to blame his wife, first scolding her, than beating her. Unjustly accused, she killed herself a few days later. Li related that when his nephew had "heard of this he had become frightened and had soon felt that his Spirits became unsettled" (伊聞一嚇遂覺神魂不定). This was when Li's nephew had chosen to return home.

Li Wenrong's third case of madness (1828) was that of Wu Yusheng 吳預生, a licentiate who was in charge of the redaction of documents (or perhaps accounts 書啟) in a salt shop of the Huaibei region. $ ^{86} $ Wu lived in an empty room whose tranquility he was fond of, but one day at work he suddenly slashed himself several times on the chest with a blade and had time to gore himself seriously before the shop clerks saved him. An escort of ten people sent him home to Zhenjiang. There, he stopped trying to injure himself, but his mad uproars became so serious that his father decided to seek the help of physicians. For more than two months, their remedies proved useless. Wu eventually found out about Li Wenrong, who had just come back from Suzhou and happened to live nearby. When the father called

## PDF第375页

upon Li, Li told him that “when a madman sees me, he dares not act madly” (瘋子見予即不敢瘋).

Just before dusk on 1 June 1828 (DG8.4.19), Li sent people to tell Wu's father that he could bring his son to Li's house for an examination.

Several people brought in the madman, holding him by the arms. He entered dancing. When I came out to the hall the madman became silent and stopped moving. Using the same method as when I examined Li Dengming, I sat high and made him sit low. With an upright appearance and a severe expression, I took his pulse. The pulse configuration was alternately large and small, loose and tight, knotty and rushed: I could tell without a doubt that this was affliction by evil.

數人將瘋子挾持而來 舞蹈而入 予出至廳 瘋子即寂然不動 予如診厲登銘法 予上坐 使之下坐 正容壯色以診其脈 脈象或大或小 或疏或密 或結或促 知其邪祟無疑

Like other illnesses, possession could be diagnosed by pulse taking. Even physicians who did not believe in ghosts knew that the “spectral pulse” (祟脈) was one that changed rapidly, which, to many doctors, indicated that there were more than organic processes involved. Li Zhongzi 李中梓 (1588-1655) had once met a possessed person whose “six pulses were now large and then small, now floating and then submerged: this was indeed the spectral configuration” (六脈忽大忽小 忽浮忽沉 確為祟象).⁸⁷

## PDF第376页

What is to be noted here is that until Li took Wu's pulse, nobody had attributed Wu's illness to ghosts. The case continued.

With a stern voice, I told [him]: "Now that you have encountered me you must leave. Otherwise I will cauterize you and needle you on the 'crying-ghost' point. $ ^{88} $ But since you have come a long way [from the salt shop in Huaibei], I will urge his [Wu Yusheng's] father to give you extra travel expenses." To my every sentence, he answered with a sound, making all my relatives marvel.

厲聲謂之曰‘爾遇我即當去不去我將在鬼哭穴灸汝針汝雖然爾來路遠我當囑伊父多贈汝盤纏’予說一句伊應一聲予眷屬乃皆稱奇

This time, the ghost was not karmically driven; it was one of those that were known to dwell in empty rooms like the one Wu had enjoyed living in. Since such ghosts possessed people in order to get fed, the feeding and the paper money were designed to satisfy its desires. But inducements would not suffice, because Wu Yusheng's Qi and Blood had also been harmed by the protracted possession. To ginseng and foxglove brew, Li added many costly ingredients: rhinoceros horn, antelope horn, amber, cinnabar, fossil teeth (龍齒, "dragon teeth"), tiger bones, land-tortoise shell (龜板), and deer horn. Like most animal-based drugs, these were considered to be "precious drugs that unblock one's spiritual dispositions" (諸多靈通寶貴之藥). Li explained his prescription with another allegory:

A devious person will not be at ease in a hall full of stern and awesome gentlemen.

Once these drugs enter the abdomen, the evil specter will be forced to cohabit

uneasily and to think of leaving. I also emulated Yu Xichang's [i.e., Yu Chang 喻昌,

see p. 349] method of using mutton broth as a guide [for the drugs], so as to make

## PDF第377页

the demon depart along with the [meat's] pungent odor. I did not let the patient know [what the recipe was] lest the "two youngsters relocate between the heart and the diaphragm."$^{89}$ I then urged [Wu Yusheng's father] Jianlin: "This is real demonic affliction. The road from Xinyang [in Huaibei] is long. I earnestly advise you to invite an eminent monk to perform a ghost-feeding ceremony and to burn sepulchral currency to enact my promise of giving [the ghost] extra travel expenses. Only then will the drugs be efficacious."

正人君子巍然满座 邪人自不能安 此藥入腹 邪祟自逼處不安而思去 又仿喻西昌法 用羊肉湯一碗為引 使邪祟借腥膳之氣味而出 惟藥不與病人知 恐二竪避入膏肓也 又囑鑒林曰 此實鬼祟 信陽來路甚遠 務請高僧施食 多燒冥資 以踐予多贈盤纏之言 服藥始靈

The father burned some paper money, but not enough to satisfy the ghost, which still had no choice but to leave because of the drugs Li had administered. The ghost then decided to possess Wu Yusheng's younger brother and to make him walk away from Zhenjiang. The next morning, the younger brother woke up in Jurong, about fifty kilometers away, with no recollection of what had happened. But otherwise the solution proved effective.

### 8.6 The contents and interpretation of demonic visions

The previous sections discussed medical explanations of cases in which ghosts were said to control the acts of their victims. I now turn to medical interpretations of demonic visions, a particular kind of spectral affliction in which ghostly entities mostly controlled what their victims saw. Of course what one saw could also lead to actions. In a case that took

## PDF第378页

place in 1816, Chen Zhongyou 陳中有 killed someone while he “was dancing and slashing wildly, saying there was a ghost” (口稱有鬼 亂舞亂砍). $ ^{90} $ In the same year, Li Tinghuai 李廷槐 grabbed a hatchet to chase another apparition and eventually killed a woman. And still in 1816, Zhu Jinhe 朱進和 killed someone he thought was a “spectral emanation” (妖怪). Although the law opted to interpret such visions as the result of “madness illness” (瘋病), many people must have concluded that real ghosts were causing harm. $ ^{91} $

Medical treatises often mentioned hallucinations among the symptoms of madness, but they usually spoke vaguely of “wild seeing” (妄見) or of people who acted “as if they had seen something” (如有所見). Physicians were often called to treat people who suffered from such visions, and they offered their patients a variety of interpretations. Only case histories that described what patients actually saw can tell us how doctors interpreted and handled specific visions. Notably, most of these specific descriptions appeared in sections on demonic illnesses and included terminology that usually pertained to demonic possession. I have already explained that hallucinations, like most possession symptoms, were often treated with simple emetics. $ ^{92} $ Here I explore other possibilities.

Many interpretations of visions, both medical and religious, gave some importance to the color of these visions. An early-twelfth-century work called Daoshan qinghua 道山清話

## PDF第379页

reported that a man often saw a white-garbed figure who looked like a Buddha (白衣人如佛相者) draped in a flashing white light (白光閃閃). The man responded to his vision by refusing to consume meat and alcohol. For this reason, the story went, he became prone to illness and decided to consult physician Wang Shouqing 汪壽卿, who prescribed a course of remedies. After the patient took the drugs, the godly-looking man of his visions first turned yellow, then faded, and ultimately disappeared, allowing the patient to recover health by resuming the consumption of meat and fermented brew. $ ^{93} $

A case by Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517; 5.7) concerned a woman who was also seeing a man garbed in white in her dreams. Yu identified the culprit as a white dog who lived in the house. In an interesting blend of approaches, he ordered the dog killed and instructed that blood from its heart and bile from its gallbladder be used to wrap some “Spirit-appeasing and Will-settling drugs” (安神定志之藥) that were to be swallowed with the help of the Eight-ingredient decoction (八物湯), a brew often prescribed to women who suffered from Blood depletion. $ ^{94} $

The above approach differed from a theory Yu Tuan had expounded elsewhere:

The unusual ghosts of five [different] colors that people see all simply [arise from] the inability of one's own essential Spirit to keep [to its lodging], and from the

## PDF第380页

incompleteness of Spiritual brightness (神光). $ ^{95} $ They do not actually [arise from] oppression by external evil; they are none other than the manifestation of extreme depletion of primordial Qi. Because the sufferers see them with their own eyes, they effectively believe they have really been confused by evil ghosts, but the fact that those who walk or sit with these persons do not see anything is enough to resolve this doubt. $ ^{96} $

人見五色非常之鬼 皆自己精神不守 神光不完故耳 實非外邪所侮 乃元氣極虛之候也 因患者親目所視 故實信真為邪鬼所迷 但當推其同行同坐者不見有物 足可以決此疑耳

According to Yu's explanation, visions gave the key to a person's affliction, not by making sense of their psyche or desires in a coded fashion, but by displaying signs of the organic imbalance that was causing the visions.

Zhang Jiebin 張介賓 (1563-1640) deployed a similar interpretation in a clinical encounter, but he explained more specifically how the color of a vision allowed the physician to identify the specific imbalance that was causing it: $ ^{97} $

I once treated a scholar whose Metal and Water viscera [i.e., the Lungs and the Kidneys] were deficient in the aftermath of a Cold-injury illness. One day at noon he suddenly sighed to me: "I have been a scholar for my entire life and never oppressed or harmed anyone. Why, then, has this white-whiskered old man dressed in [white] mourning garb and holding a fan been staying with me for three days without leaving? I think this must be the result of some karmic grievance. What can be done about

## PDF第381页

this?" I smiled: "Is the fan he is holding a white paper fan?" Startled, he replied: "Do you see him too?"

又治一儒生 以傷寒後金水二臟不足 忽一日正午 對余嘆曰 生平業儒無所欺害 何有白鬚老者 素服持扇 守余不去者三日矣 意必宿冤所致 奈之何哉 余笑曰 所持者非白紙扇耶 生驚曰 公亦見乎

Zhang Jiebin could not see the old ghost, but he explained that one often saw ghosts of the color associated with a deficient visceral system:

"Use your Pulmonary Qi is deficient, in your eyes you see mostly white flowers, and thus a white ghost. If [as in your case] Renal Qi is deficient, you will see black ghosts. This is all because correct Qi is deficient and the Spirit and Cloud-souls cannot remain attached to the body, so that the color of the relevant organ appears a result: what [karmic] grievance is there to speak of?"

outside as a result: what [karmic] grievance is there to speak of?98

君以肺氣不足 眼多白花 故見白鬼 若腎水不足者 眼多黑鬼矣 此皆正氣不足 神魂不附於體 而外見本臟之色也 亦何冤之有哉

Zhang’s patient admitted that there was indeed a detestable black ghost by his bedside, but that “Now that I have received your teaching, I feel relieved” (今得教可释然矣). Elated, he called Zhang’s reasoning an “ingenious doctrine” (妙理). $ ^{99} $ After treating his patient with drugs designed to replenish his deficient Lungs and Kidneys, Zhang Jiebin concluded: “From this, we know where the illness arose from, and that it can be relieved by indirect remonstrance” (此知其病所從生 而微言以釋之也).

Zhang Jiebin had not dismissed the terms of the old scholar's visions. He had made them revealing in a different way. His explanations (and Wang Shouqing's in the twelfth-century story) sounded discerning precisely because the diagnosis of demonic possession or

## PDF第382页

religious visions had been plausible, not laughable. Zhang Jiebin's insistence on explaining (or explaining away) the content of visions probably denoted competition with other specialists (probably exorcists) who proposed different interpretations of the same symptoms. Zhang's attribution of possession symptoms to purely organic processes is reminiscent of Zhu Zhenheng's argument that mucus could cause disorders that appeared to result from spectral affliction.

I have argued that the same symptoms of “possession” were recognizable across domains of expertise, that various specialists proposed different treatments for these symptoms, and that medical interpretations were attempts on the part of physicians to claim competence in the treatment of symptoms that had once justified recourse to ritual experts rather than doctors. As a result, when physicians faced possession symptoms, they often proposed solutions that differed from those they adopted in cases they classified under “madness” in their case collections. Cases of “demonic afflictions” usually gave more importance to dialogue with the patient and to dissolving his or her doubts. This was not because physicians gave no importance to visions in cases of madness and paid attention to them during cases of possession. I think that causality ran in the opposite direction. Namely, doctors classified a case under “madness” when they gave little importance to the content of these visions, and under “possession” when they considered visions central to an illness and relevant to its treatment. $ ^{100} $

### 8.7 Conclusion

Michael Macdonald’s claim (1981: 8) that in seventeenth-century England “the natural and supernatural approaches coexisted uneasily, championed by rival groups of

## PDF第383页

professionals, to be sure, but not yet incompatible to many minds" applies well to Qing China. I showed that just as the use of medical drugs was not limited to elite circles, the belief in invisible beings was by no means the preserve of the non-educated masses. "Religious" and "medical" explanations remained mutually intelligible because they usually referred to the same culturally recognized symptoms with similar terms. Taoist texts could thus attribute the illness "diankuang" to demons while medical thinkers explained "spectral affliction" by the action of mucus. But although late-imperial physicians commonly attributed possession symptoms to the action of mucus, I showed that even in the most scholarly medical circles the demonic explanation was never entirely replaced by organic ones. Even scholarly physicians from the culturally sophisticated Lower Yangzi region often shared popular beliefs in ghosts and had developed medical means to counter them.

We should keep in mind that the documents we exploit were produced after the cases they discussed had ended. This was the case with both medical case files and legal reports. Raymond Williams (1977: 128) once noted that converting experience into “finished products” (like those found in documents written ex post facto) was “the strongest barrier to the recognition of human cultural activity.” And indeed I suspect that even in medical documents, the distinction between “mania” (kuang, an illness) and “affliction by evil” (邪祟) was often an ex post facto distinction. Cases were classified in different sections of the medical literature because writers decided this was where they belonged, not because these cases were different by essence. The clarity of these distinctions hid a good amount of “human cultural activity,” namely the conflicts over the interpretation of possession symptoms that could take place when patients were still under treatment.

For example, when a woman was speaking in ways that made her relatives think she was sick, no one knew for sure whether she was possessed, was suffering from an organic

## PDF第384页

illness, or was simply acting up. The outcome was also far from certain. Would the women recover? Would she try to commit suicide? Would she go out naked into the street, bringing shame to her family? Would she need to be beaten or physically restrained? Physicians intervened in these fluid situations, often by imposing an interpretation of the pathology at hand and by acting on the patient in the light of this interpretation. $ ^{101} $ But physicians often needed to convince their patients of the validity of medical approaches.

Chinese physicians who proposed medical interpretations of possession were thus not only criticizing less prestigious idioms of illness: they were also trying to assimilate them into higher-order explanations in order to convince patients who believed in ghosts to seek the help of physicians to treat symptoms that looked like those of possession. The dramatically decreasing mentions of exorcists as competitors in late imperial cases suggest that they may have succeeded.

This chapter has discussed different possible interpretations of the same “possession” symptoms. The next one concerns possible differences in medical approaches to madness when the sufferers were women.

## PDF第385页

# Gendered mania? Women's madness in late-imperial case records

A notorious adage circulating in late imperial medical circles declared that the women's illnesses were more difficult to cure than men's. $ ^{1} $ Two other sayings claimed that women's bodies were "governed by Blood" (以血為主 or a variant) and that treating women necessitated "separate prescriptions" (别方). Ming and Qing physicians cited these sayings to set up concurring arguments, to introduce objections or qualifications, or to clinch points with the help of a well-formulated piece of common knowledge.

In this chapter, I want to explore whether the gender of the sufferer was one of the fault lines along which the understanding and treatment of mad acts and mad people were divided. Did physicians from the Song (960-1279) to the Qing (1644-1911) describe madness as occurring to women by different mechanisms than to men? Did the treatments they prescribed differ according to the gender of the patient? What other aspects of medical approaches to insanity were “gendered”?

Since mad behavior was obviously less frequent than menses and pregnancy, relatively few cases of madness where the patient was a woman have survived. Many arguments in this chapter are based on 35 case files—from 23 physicians—of female patients who suffered from mad symptoms (see Table 9.1 next page), as well as a few general

## PDF第386页

comments in medical treatises about how madness occurred to women. I selected cases from the twelfth to the early twentieth century that appeared in sections on madness of case collections, as well as other cases in which women were said to be suffering from kuang or dian or in which their behavior was described as kuang or dian, and finally cases that did not conform with the above criteria but in which the symptoms corresponded with those mentioned in descriptions of dian and kuang, that is, manic running, discarding of clothes, excessive anger or grief, and the like. Since it would be cumbersome to reinsert each case in its intellectual and social context, I will refer to relevant sections in other chapters for more information on the physicians who wrote these cases. I consider “postpartum madness” as distinct, and I therefore discuss it in its own section at the end of this chapter.
