Since the cases span more than 800 years, the problem of change inevitably poses itself, albeit a bit intractably since my sample for each period is so meager. There is an

## PDF第387页

average of 4 cases per century and barely more than 2 if we exclude the last hundred years I cover. I found only two relevant cases between 1588 and 1821. The 35 cases represent the way elite doctors (mostly from the Lower Yangzi region) explained the way they understood and treated mad women. They give only a small glimpse of how society handled women who were called mad. Indeed, the main treatment for manic women may not have been medical. There is too little evidence to tell, but home confinement was certainly an option, though it may have come only after the failure of medical or exorcistic treatments. Since the documents tell us precious little about the way female patients experienced their own condition, we are also deprived of the “first-person” data that have informed recent studies of women in Chinese history. $ ^{2} $ I will remind the reader of these limits when I propose tentative conclusions or try to point to possible trends.

This chapter’s opening section tries to determine whether and how the diagnoses and therapies of “mad women” were gendered. One guiding issue will be whether physicians found relevant the notion that the body of women was “governed by Blood.” The second section focuses on nudity and excessive desires, two symptoms that were likely to provoke extra-medical responses. Perhaps unsurprisingly, we shall see that the gendered character of medical encounters could go beyond the issue of how the body itself was understood. Third and finally, I present medical interpretations of “postpartum madness,” a form of insanity that could (by definition) only happen to women. I explain how postpartum madness was theorized and handled, and how it was understood in relation to similar symptoms happening to men and non-parturient women. For my purpose, it makes no difference

## PDF第388页

whether postpartum insanity was “really madness,” since late-imperial physicians tied it explicitly to what they called madness.

### 9.1 Non-gendered bodies, gendered etiologies

Medical explanations of mad behavior in treatises purported to apply to both men and women. But in practice, did doctors consistently trace cases of women's mad behavior to the roots they identified in their treatises? Did their descriptions and treatments apply to the "androgynous body" (Furth 1999: 25-29, 44-48 and 56-57)—a body without anatomical sex—that characterized elite Chinese medicine? Charlotte Furth has indeed argued that late-imperial physicians focused increasingly less on the uniqueness of women, and tended to understand the women's illnesses in the same way as they understood men's.

I shall agree with Furth in general, but I will also show that the etiology of madness was already ungendered in the Song dynasty (960-1279), and that glimpses of gendered etiologies only appeared in late-imperial times, especially in the nineteenth century. In recorded symptoms, identified etiology, and adopted therapy, a spectrum of more or less gendered solutions were available. Some symptoms (notably nudity and grief) were proportionally more common in women's cases, probably because these acts were seen as socially more problematic, and were therefore more likely to be brought to the attention of a physician. But although many nineteenth-century cases of mad women did not conform to what treatises said about insanity, most of them did. $ ^{3} $

## PDF第389页

In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files.

Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

## PDF第390页

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $

A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity or excesses, and emotional imbalances—and the devising of new ways to treat it. Wu's therapy based on human placenta differed from those proposed in contemporary books, but, as great specialist of medicinal drugs Li Shizhen 李時珍 (1518-

## PDF第391页

1593) maintained, placenta could “treat all cases of wasting disorders and extreme fatigue as well as loss of mind and bewilderment caused by dian and xian, in both men and women” (治男女一切虚損勞極 癲癇失志恍惚). $ ^{12} $ Wu also gave his patient the “Will-fixing pill” (定志丸) and a daily regimen of “Heart-replenishing decoction” (補心湯), a treatment that he recommended elsewhere in a general explanation of therapies for dian. $ ^{13} $ The way he handled his female patient was thus not significantly gendered. After 100 days of treatment, the young woman was married, and one year later she had a child. Although this outcome had been enabled by drugs that were designed to act on the body’s inner dynamics, the proof of the cure was social, and therefore gendered.

Xue Ji's 薛己 (1487-1559) treatment of a young woman typified his "calorific-replenishing" (6.3) approach to many disorders. $ ^{14} $ His remark that other physicians had attempted to give her "Wind-mucus" drugs is another sign that gender-specific treatments may not have been common at the time, since, as we have seen, these drugs then represented the most conventional medical treatment for insanity. I can also find no meaningful gender distinctions between Wang Ji's 汪機 (1463-1540; see 6.2) two cases of madness caused by inner harm, one of a man and one of a woman. Wang's contrarian attribution of both cases to depletion of the Yang-brilliance tract shows that he was assuming that the bodies of men and women functioned in the same way.

Only in the late sixteenth century did a few physicians start to identify particular scenarios by which madness could happen to women. Li Chan's 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575) explained that "when a woman's menstrual waters

## PDF第392页

flow out in excess, Blood and Qi confuse the Heart; or when postpartum noxious dew

flushes upward and makes speech jumbled and the mind unable to stay steadfast, this is the

result of depletion of Blood and exhaustion of Spirit” (有婦人月水崩漏過多 血氣迷心

或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was

among the first to ascribe mad symptoms to pathological menses, but the text explained how

one specific imbalance could lead to insanity, not how mad symptoms should be interpreted

in general. In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a

contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as

caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from

menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum.

Although the rare sources we have are difficult to bind into a single narrative,

dispersed writings indicate that at least some physicians thought that women suffered from

madness for reasons particular to them. Zhuang Lüyan's 莊履嚴 Fuke baibian 婦科百辨

(early 17 $ ^{th} $ century?) claimed that the mucus that made women mad (顛狂) by encroaching

on their Heart was brought about by extreme fright or sorrow (驚憂之極 痰犯心胸所

致), $ ^{16} $ an emotional theory of mucus that was still relatively rare at the time. In the late

seventeenth century, Chen Shiduo 陳士鐸 argued that fits of madness caused by depletion, a

kind of madness “from which women suffer much” (論陰虛而發狂者 此症婦人居多),

appeared because “Qi in stasis is not extended [i.e., emotions are not expressed], yearnings

are not satisfied, and there is temporary sorrow and anger” (鬱氣不伸 思慕不遂 一時

## PDF第393页

薹憤 遂成此疾). $ ^{17} $ Sorrow appeared in both Zhuang's and Chen's accounts, but neither said much more about it than the lines I just cited. It is tempting to speculate that women isolated from their kin by patrilocal marriage and with limited social relations because of the necessity to stay at home were more likely than men to suffer from “sorrow.”

Several cases by Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (fl. 1570s-1610s) indicate that one physician could propose many different explanations for similar behaviors, an aspect of late-imperial medicine with which we are now familiar. In two records published in 1576 and 1587, Gong treated female patients with the “Avert-wind tongsheng powder” 防風通聖散, a formula that a book by his father had claimed could “treat the suddenly erupting symptoms of all madness and Wind disorders” (治一切癲狂風疾 暴發之症).¹⁸ When he re-edited his father’s work, Gong added that this remedy “treats diankuang that has just started to arise” (此方治癲狂初起), when “in most cases there is repletion-Heat and Wind-heteropathy” (多有實熱風邪).¹⁹ In later books, Gong explained that women’s dian behavior (singing at strange times and “climbing on rooftops” 歌唱無時 簫垣上屋) was “caused by constructive Blood (?) lost in the Heart’s Envelope” (營血迷於心包所致也).²⁰ I do not know what Gong Tingxian meant by “constructive Blood,” but it may have been unique to women. As we have seen, however, blockage of the “Heart’s Envelope” was one of the most commonly identified causes of disorders of consciousness for both men and women in late-

## PDF第394页

imperial times. $ ^{21} $ Gong recommended treating such a disorder with an enhanced version of the “Carefree powder” (加味逍遥散), which was often used to redress Liver imbalances, but he did not record ever using this treatment in a clinical case.

I found only one case of a “mad” woman between 1615 and 1822: a file by Suzhou physician Zhang Lu 張璐 (1617-1699).

A woman spoke madly, screaming and scolding. She would sing and laugh unusually, as if she were possessed. Her eye and the corner of her mouth on one side were raised in a stupid grin. Some treated her as if these were convulsive fits, others as if these were fits of Heart Wind, all to no avail. It turns out that she had long had a Head-Wind illness, and that it was the acting up of Wind mucus that had made her this way. I used [Sichuan-] lovage-and-wild-ginger decoction with an addition of avert-wind, and she quickly became better after a few doses. $ ^{22} $

一婦人狂言叫罵 歌笑非常 似崇憑依 一邊眼與口角呆起 或作癎治 成作心風治 皆不效 乃是舊有頭風之疾 風痰作之使然 用芎辛湯加防風 數服頓愈

This way to treat madness symptoms was unusual, but not obviously gendered. The record also indicates that previous physicians had used methods that could have applied to men.

Following a disappointing lack of cases for two centuries after 1615, we have ten cases of “mad women” written by five men between 1822 and 1859. $ ^{23} $ Lin Peiqin 林佩琴 (1772-1839) was one of very few doctors who published extensive discussions of madness

## PDF第395页

and left several case files to supplement their general explanations. $ ^{24} $ His long survey of causes of insanity included a claim that “women suffer from dian because of Blood imbalances” (婦人患癲 由血不調), $ ^{25} $ but none of his three cases of “mad women” mentioned Blood. He diagnosed that a woman who spoke and laughed to herself was suffering from “abundance of mucus, and Qi in stasis” (痰多氣鬱), and attributed to fright the dian case of a despondent and emaciated (神衰肌削) patient who had been “sitting and lying as if stupid” (坐臥如癡) for more than ten years. $ ^{26} $ Lin noted that “only the massive replenishing of primordial Qi will bring results” (非大補元氣不為功), but since many physicians considered that prolonged disorders led to general depletion, Lin’s approach did not apply solely to women.

In another case by Lin, a woman surnamed Zhang was “confused and manic” (恍惚狂妄). In addition to “seeing her husband as an enemy” (视夫若仇), she menacingly “held an instrument and shed her clothes, so that no one dared to approach her” (持械棄衣 莫之敢近). Lin adopted the conventional remedy for such acute crises: an emetic. Pure melon pedicle “removed a lot of viscous saliva and lodged sputum” (去粘涎宿沫頗多), but the violent vomiting caused the patient chest pains. When he saw that she still “spoke and laughed to herself,” Lin “knew that.... the Heart-lodging was [still] depleted and that the Spirit-souls had not yet recovered” (知其痰沫去而心舍虚 神魂未復也). One more recipe put an end to the chest pains, and the last prescription brought the patient back to

## PDF第396页

normal, purportedly for good, all according to a script that would have applied to a man as well as to the woman concerned.

As far as I know, the only physician who ascribed instances of madness to causes unique to women was Fang Lue 方略 (from Wuning 武寧 in northern Jiangxi), who published his Case Files from the Hall of Venerating Friendship (Shangyou tang yian 尚友堂醫案) in 1846. $ ^{27} $ He recounts that a woman had stumbled to the kitchen one morning with her hair disheveled and tried to cut herself with a knife. After her family members had stopped her, she had “screamed madly that there had been a great wrong, and that eating people was pleasurable” (狂呼有大冤枉 食人則快). Though these words and acts could easily have been interpreted as those of a ghost, Fang did not mention the possibility that the woman was possessed. $ ^{28} $ Instead he attempted to use medical means to treat the symptoms gradually. First he prescribed a mix of anti-mucus and calorific drugs that diminished the state of madness (癲態稍定), but because the patient was now “stultified and mute” (痴呆不言), Fang poked into her throat with duck feathers dipped in tong oil to make her vomit “more than a bowl of sticky mucus” (又以鴨翎蘸桐油攪喉中 吐出膠痰碗許). Although her spirits were now clear (神識雖清), Fang noted that her menstruations (經信) had not come for half a year. One more remedy brought things back to normal and Fang concluded his case on the news that she had recently given birth to a child. Whereas induced vomiting was typically prescribed to patients of both genders, Fang Lue’s ultimate focus on menstruation distinguished him from most physicians.

Fang's second case gave Blood an even more obvious pathological role. The patient was the wife of a man he knew.

## PDF第397页

Because her sedan chair had been attacked by robbers, $ ^{29} $ she had headed into the forest and started shouting and scolding in anger. When she returned home, she became crazy, speaking chaotically and incoherently. She then went so far as trying to light fires and holding a knife [menacingly], and she had no scruples about anything. Her family locked her up in an inner room and tied up her hands and feet. They all said this was affliction by evil.

因人盗窃 赴林中呼號怒罵 歸即發狂 亂言無次 遂至縱火持刀 無所忌憚 家人扃鎖內室 縶其手足 咸稱邪祟

This is when Fang Lue was invited to treat her. After calming her down by making her inhale vapors of vinegar, he took her pulse. The middle location of the radial pulse (關脈) was slippery, whereas the next one up the wrist (尺脈) was accelerated. Fang's interpretation avoided the diagnosis of "fright," which would have been conventional.

These [symptoms] are because just as the menses were about to arrive, she shouted loudly and with great anger. As a result, Qi rose up and Heat entered the Blood chamber. When stagnant Blood flushed directly [into the Heart] she had a fit of mania. This is actually menstrual blockage, not spectral affliction. $ ^{30} $

此因經期適至 大呼大怒 氣從上升 熱入血室 瘀血直冲 故發狂妄證 實阻經 非祟也

Fang’s reduction of a triggering event (an attack by bandits) into an organic imbalance must have been convincing, because the woman’s relatives let her take the drugs Fang prescribed: the “Peach-kernel Qi-stimulating decoction” (桃仁承氣湯), a Heat purgative to which Fang

## PDF第398页

added eight drugs. $ ^{31} $ Three doses were enough to “make menstrual blood flow down” (经血下行). Her illness vanished (其病如失). We are told that she had a child the next year.

Although nineteenth-century physicians proposed increasingly gendered diagnoses of mad behavior, the dominant etiology for all manic people remained Fire and mucus, not Blood. $ ^{32} $ It thus seems that most physicians believed that diankuang happened to men and women largely according to the same organic processes. Wang Shixiong 王士雄 (1808-1868) attributed one case of insanity in a woman to the combined action of Summer Heat, exhaustion, and lodged mucus. He cured it with an anti-mucus remedy enhanced with Spirit-clearing drugs. $ ^{33} $ In the early twentieth century, several healers treated one woman suffering from manic delusions with anti-Fire and anti-mucus remedies. “Ox-gallbladder Heart-clearing pill” (牛黄清心丸) and the “Very precious elixir” (至寶丹) made her mouth as hot as the breath of a dragon (口如龍噴). Thinking that it was Fire and mucus that were disturbing her “Spiritual brilliance” (痰火擾亂神明), another physician gave her rhubarb (a purgative) and “Yuanming 元明 powder,” which was made of licorice and radish root treated with magnesium sulfate (芒硝), a coolant. Shen Zufu 沈祖復 (1862-1925) claimed that he finally cured her with loquats (枇杷). $ ^{34} $ Fei Chengzu 费承祖 (1851-1914), from Menghe 孟河 in Jiangsu, also diagnosed that a woman’s “mania caused by despondency and

## PDF第399页

stasis’(憂鬱病紐) had stemmed from “mucus and Fire’s having obscured and blocked the [Heart’s] Envelope tract” (痰火蒙蔽包絡). $ ^{35} $ He treated her by the purgative method of “clearing Fire and transforming mucus” (清火化痰).

Despite the rarity of gendered etiologies for madness (the exception being Fang Lue's 方略 cases), some illness scripts had an unmistakable gender bent to them. The most gendered aspect of medical cases was the frequency by which emotions were recorded to have given rise to disorders. We can guess at women's disappointments and failures behind terms like "sorrow" (you 憂), "pensiveness" (si 思), and "stasis" (yu 鬱). The latter term usually referred to depressed affect supervenient on internal blockage, but could also mean "pent-up" as in "pent-up anger." Their prevalence probably stemmed from some women's being confined to the inner quarters or being reduced to subsidiary roles in patrilineal families. Other women may have experienced distress when they failed to conceive heirs. $ ^{36} $

The common observation that women's emotions were "pent-up" may also have come from the necessity for some women to restrain emotional displays. The threshold by which emotional manifestations would be considered pathological varied with age and with a woman's status in the family. The incessant anger of a matriarch, for example, would have been more easily tolerated than the furor of a young bride.

Some symptoms were prominent because the patient's relatives considered them the most disturbing and tried to put an end to them by seeking the help of doctors. Accordingly,

## PDF第400页

excessive emotional manifestations were often considered like symptoms of dian or kuang.

The next section shows that families were probably more likely to look for help when a

female member acted uncontrollably and in improper fashion.

### 9.2 Gendered symptoms and therapies: improprieties, morality, and violence

Nudity and excessive desires

As we have seen, Chinese physicians often reduced unusual acts to the organic imbalance they thought was causing this behavior. The validity of this reduction was confirmed by the proclaimed success of the pharmacological cure that was designed to redress this imbalance. The attribution of moral meanings to mad behavior thus went against the grain of medical doctrines. But “madness” was chiefly a behavioral illness. It had very apparent symptoms whose primary effect was to make one socially inept. Or more accurately, it was the sudden appearance of social ineptitude that made one deserve the label of madness. Because mad symptoms were social, even when physicians tried to reduce madness to purely organic processes, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{37} $

More shame probably came upon a family when a woman suffered from overt madness than when a man did. In 1746, Tian Shiqi 田士奇, a man from Taihe 太和 county in Jiangxi, killed his wife with a knife because she had twice escaped outside without her clothes on. He explained to the authorities was that he was angry at her and “afraid” (怕) and “worried” (慮) that “people would shame and mock” (人耻笑) him. $ ^{38} $ In a different case

## PDF第401页

that took place in 1833, a son mistakenly injured his naked mother while he and his father were struggling to keep her from escaping outside during a fit of madness. $ ^{39} $

I found two medical cases in which morality was explicitly adduced to justify inflicting pain upon a “mad” woman. In both cases, the patient had recurrently acted in ways that her relatives found unacceptable. Take the case of an unmarried woman of about 20 who, “every time she saw a man, would bite and not let go; she would then faint and cold essence would flow out of her vagina; she would awaken shortly afterwards” (患每見男子咬住不放 後昏倒 陰戶流出冷精 頃間即醒). $ ^{40} $ Her pulse told Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (1538-1635) that “this was exuberance of Yin: she thinks of a man but cannot get one” (乃陰盛 思男子不可得也). In the sixteenth century, a purely organic interpretation of this disorder would have called for restrictive action on “Liver Fire,” or perhaps for purges, but Gong took extra-medical measures: “I made her father punish her painfully with a stick every time, so as to make her lose desire as a result of thinking of the pain” (令其父每用棍痛責 使之思痛失欲也). Only after this quasi-Pavlovian beating did Gong prescribe a remedy to curb the Liver. The patient, we are told, “became better” (愈).

Beatings may have been a common way of treating a woman's unbridled desires, a symptom that threatened family order more directly than (for instance) muttering to oneself. By contrast, in all the medical cases of "mad men" that I found, only Mr. Bao (7.7) and an eighty-year-old man were recorded as having overtly expressed excessive desires. The "cure" for the latter was a young concubine, whereas Mr. Bao's improper desires were not the focus

## PDF第402页

of the medical and moral treatment that his physician Wu Tang 吴瑭 (1758-1836) advocated. $ ^{41} $

"Flower madness"

Chen Shiduo 陳士鐸, a late-seventeenth century physician who attributed some of his books to ancient immortals, introduced the notion of “flower dian” (花瓣) to discuss symptoms similar to those of the young woman whose case I just translated. Rare as it was, this expression had an obvious meaning because of the sexual connotation of flowers in Chinese. $ ^{42} $ In his earliest known work, Chen had briefly defined “flower dian” as “when a young woman thinks of someone and her heart deviates” (乃女子思想其人而心邪), $ ^{43} $ but in later books he discussed these excessive desires in a separate entry where he also mentioned a symptom that had been included in descriptions of mad behavior since antiquity: “discarding one’s clothes and running” (棄衣而走; 3.1). $ ^{44} $ Physicians familiar with the medical canons knew that the ancients had attributed this behavior to excessive Heat. $ ^{45} $

Chen explained the excessive displays of some women in a language as flowery as his subject, far from the formulaic descriptions usually found in Chinese medical texts. In so doing he proposed a sophisticated—if somewhat laborious—attempt to explain the appearance of specific behaviors and thoughts.

## PDF第403页

A woman who has a fit of dian does not know shame: she becomes candy-like $ ^{46} $ when she sees a man and very angry when she sees a woman. She will go as far as going naked and exposing her body. In such situations, Liver Fire is ardent and [the woman] thinks about a man but is unable to get one: this causes stasis and congealing that turn into dian. How does ardent Liver Fire turn into dian? It must be because, in women, Liver-Wood is flourishing: $ ^{47} $ Liver Fire coerces the Heart, causing the Heart’s ruler to descend from the palace. But the Heart’s Envelope keeps guard on the outside, so how can it let [the Heart, i.e. the ruler] be intimidated? $ ^{48} $ It is little known that Liver Fire is none other than depletion Fire. $ ^{49} $ Depletion Fire and Ministerial Fire are of the same sort. When [Fire?] associates with brigands, $ ^{50} $ it forgets [the ruler’s] sagely brilliance $ ^{51} $ and it directly sets the palace ablaze. But once the Heart-ruler walks out, why is there only dian and not death? This must be because Kidney Water comes to the rescue. Thinking of a man and being unable to get him is because the Kidneys are flourishing. Although this is Kidney Fire, Kidney Water is actually exhausted. When Liver Fire becomes coercive, the Heart [should be] provided for by Kidney Water: this is why there is only dian and not death. The treatment method is to purge the Liver root and replenish Kidney Water, as well as to channel static Qi. $ ^{52} $

婦發癲不識羞 見男如飴 見女甚怒 甚至赤身露體 此肝火熾 思男不可得 鬱結成癲也 肝火熾 何成癲 蓋婦女肝木旺 肝火逼心 則心君下殿然包絡外護 何任威逼 不知肝火乃虚火 虚火與相火同類 庇比匪 忘聖明 直燒宫殿 然心君走出 何但癲不死 蓋腎水救援 思男子不得 因腎旺 雖是腎火 腎水實涸 然肝火逼 心有腎水資 所以但癲不死 治法泄肝本並補腎水 兼舒鬱氣為得

Chen's elucidation depended less on past therapeutic success than on a metaphorical mapping of the Five Agents on internal organs. Stretching the explanatory power of supervenience to its limits, Chen traced behavior to purely organic dynamics, and reduced thoughts, ideas, and decisions to the ways the viscera were supposed to interact. In the case to which I now turn, metaphorical language was used to cover the failure of a therapy based on the usual reduction of behavioral symptom to an underlying organic imbalance.

### The thorny case of Ms. Zhang

Chen Shiduo did not mention the moral implications of mad acts and did not propose a clearly gendered etiology of “flower madness,” but as we shall now see, doctors who confronted cases in which madness had severe social consequences could more readily resort to moral disquisitions and integrate them into the therapies they proposed. In the following case, which took place in the early nineteenth century, the boisterous behavior of a woman embarrassed both her brother and physician Wu Tang 吴瑭 (1758-1836). $ ^{53} $

Ms. Zhang, 42 sui. $ ^{54} $ At first, in the second month, her spirits were stultified. [She] wrongly took replenishing drugs such as cassia bark and steamed foxglove root, as a result of which she became seriously crazy.

## 章氏 四十二歲 先是二月間神識恍惚 誤服肉桂熟地等補藥 因而大狂

## PDF第405页

As in the case of Mr. Bao (7.7), Wu Tang was facing a patient who had reacted badly to calorific drugs, a common remedy for apathetic or stupefied states. We do not know if the remedy was a home prescription or had been recommended by a physician, but we are told that it had turned the patient's stupor into serious madness. Wu tried to reverse the course of the illness.

During the third month, I used extremely bitter [drugs] to break the dominance of [Ministerial Fire’s] flourishing in the upper body. Shortly afterwards, I prescribed aromatic [drugs] to unblock the Heart’s Envelope. After thirty days of medical treatment, she was better, but her pulse was still swollen and accelerated, [so] instructed that she abstain from alcohol and meat and prescribed “Special harmonizing and vitalizing paste” to replenish Yin [so that it will] balance Yang. $ ^{55} $

余於三月間用極苦以折其上盛之威 間服芳香開心包 醫治三十日而愈 但

脈仍洪數 余囑其戒酒肉 服專翕大生膏補陰配陽

This attacking therapy suggests that Wu thought Zhang's mad behavior was caused by Fire in the upper body and mucus blocking the Heart's Envelope.

Apparently, Wu had first aimed to put an end to the most obvious symptoms of madness, but Zhang's relatives either ignored Wu's prescriptions or they were unable to enforce them:

Not only did she not take the drug pellets, but at noon $ ^{56} $ she also took lots of meat and alcohol, upon which she became so mad as to be unrestrainable. Her feet reeked so much that the neighbors could smell them from a distance. Frequently she would take off all her clothes and pants and take to the street. One or two men were not enough to catch her and make her return.

ording to Wu, Zhang's madness resumed because she ingested Fire-rousing food and
k. We could also postulate that Zhang relapsed because she stopped taking purgatives.

Since Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358), it had become conventional to understand the movements of inner Fire as the physical basis of excessive emotions (4.4), but doctors also recognized that emotions could be triggered by anything Yang and excessive, including food and cosmic Qi. Wu explained that the Qi of cosmicly dominant Ministerial Fire had stirred Zhang's internal Fire, which had been curtailed by thirty days of depleting therapy.

The long disquisition that he proposed about the ties between virtue, “bearing,” and pain played on the quasi homophony between rén仁 (“benevolence”) and rén忍 (“to bear,” “to endure”). Wu’s comparison was also an attempt to convince the patient’s brother to resort to therapeutic violence.

## PDF第407页

[This situation] continued until noon on the sixteenth day of the sixth month, when she once again tore her pants to pieces. People did not have time to stop her, and she exited through the main gate. I sat watching and could not bear (rěn) [what I saw] I was also embarrassed to have no technique by which to put an end to her illness, so I told her younger brother: “With such symptoms, there is no other way than to beat her and make her feel extreme pain in order to make her wear her pants herself. This is because the mind that is ashamed of evil draws its origin in benevolence (rěn); when one can be benevolent, one will not bear (rěn) [evil ways and acts]; when one can bear [them], one is not benevolent; when lack of benevolence reaches its utmost, one’s shame of evil is completely lost. The extreme pain of beating makes one unable to bear [the pain?]; when one is unable to bear [it], the benevolent mind is restored. This being so, the mind that is ashamed of evil is restored accordingly. This is the reason why the ancient sage-kings created teachings and punishments to complement each other.” Her brother’s shame at seeing his older sister make such a scene turned into anger. $ ^{58} $ Taking the preservation of his parents’ dignity as his duty, he beat her on the thighs with a small bamboo stick and made her wear her pants. Having experienced pain, she put on her pants on her own, after which she became slightly more lucid. At the “beginning of autumn” on the seventeenth day of the following month, I gave her one large dose of bitter medicine and she was completely cured. The beating probably achieved success because it was reinforced by heavenly autumnal metal Qi and the force of the drugs. $ ^{59} $ Then [I] reaped complete success with the “Special harmonizing and vitalizing paste.”

延至六月十六日午刻 復自撕碎其褲 人不及防 而出大門矣 余坐視不忍 復自慚無術以已其病 因謂其胞弟曰 此症非打之使極痛 令其自著褲也不可 蓋羞惡之心 亦統於仁 能仁則不忍 忍則不仁 不仁之至 羞惡全喪打之極痛 則不能忍 不忍而仁心復 仁心復而羞惡之心亦復矣 此古聖王補作教刑之義也 伊弟見其乃姊如是景況 羞而成怒 以保父母體面為義 於是以小竹板責其腿 令著褲 彼知痛後而自著衣 著後稍明 次月十七日立秋 余與大劑苦藥一帖而全愈 蓋打之功 與天時秋金之氣藥之力相須而成功也 後以專翕大生膏而收全功

Ms. Zhang's case displayed evident gender differences in the treatment of an etiologically non-gendered illness. As in the case of Mr. Bao (7.7), Wu Tang prescribed bitter drugs, but the extra-pharmaceutical methods he adopted were poles apart. Whereas Bao had been taught virtue and proper deportment anew, Zhang was bastinadoed into submission, "cured" against her will by a cosmically assisted beating approved by the ancient sage-kings.

Wu’s claims to success also rested on his ability to explain failure. He had attributed the short-lived effect of the purgatives, first to the persistent domination of inner Fire, and then to Ms. Zhang’s gormandizing and complete lack of shame. Although he had traced her disorder to purely organic imbalances (disregarding the social interactions that may have led to her odd conduct), he eventually attributed the failure of the treatment to Zhang’s willful behavior. A psychological explanation could have led to more sympathetic insight into a patient’s condition, but Wu’s reasoning instead justified recourse to morally restorative violence, a beating that Wu rationalized by expostulating on the relation between physical pain and a virtuous heart.

In the struggle to secure the approval and trust of their educated patients, they wrote case histories that stood for social correctness and defended the hierarchical ethics of filial piety. Indeed physicians dealt in goods less tangible than the drugs they sometimes prepared at their patients' bedsides. Wu Tang sometimes portrayed himself as a life saver, but in his madness cases he also appeared as a herald of moral respectability. His presented his success

## PDF第409页

in Zhang's and Bao's cases as social achievements. Mr. Bao could again take responsibilities as a household head and he purportedly even obtained the elusive examination title in the pursuit of which he had become mad in the first place. In Zhang's case the cure was less triumphant. Wu Tang and his patient's brother considered as an improvement the sole fact that she was no longer stirring up trouble. This, perhaps, was the only success they were striving for.

Treatises did not insist on the social significance of mad symptoms, but case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. For women even more than for men, this was especially the case with behavioral afflictions like madness. Since the label of "madness" was often apposed to sudden and obvious failures to act in a proper way, the meaning of mad behavior could potentially be as gendered as social roles themselves. It may be that failed social roles more than disorganized organic processes were the main difference between the madness of men and women.

Yet even in their case files, most physicians represented their therapeutic success without mentioning the possible social causes of the pathological behavior and the gendered circumstances in which therapeutic encounters had taken place. Regardless of whether they treated men or women, Chinese doctors rarely mentioned the possible social roots of madness, doubtless in part because their readers could also be future customers who would dislike analyses of family dysfunctions, but mostly because Chinese medical theory treated mental disorders as supervenient upon organic imbalances.

The kind of madness I discuss in the next section was, as far as I know, never described in moral terms in medical sources, probably because both physicians and patients agreed that it occurred in conjunction with well-known bodily imbalances.

## PDF第410页

### 9.3 Postpartum madness

The postpartum condition and “postpartum madness”

For a Chinese wife of late imperial times, the weeks that followed childbirth were a critical period when illness was feared and perhaps even expected. The dominant medical conceptions insisted that delivery exhausted vital fluids and created a seriously depleted condition that made women vulnerable to external invasion (by Cold, Wind, or ghosts), to the spontaneous formation of pathogenic imbalances, and to the inappropriate movements of fluids like “failed Blood” (败血) or “noxious dew” (惡露) inside the body. Taboos against bathing, washing, and exposure to wind or cold in the first postnatal month were meant to prevent the occurrence of the ailments that could strike women at this crucial time.

Medical treatises usually discussed postpartum disorders together, probably because physicians considered the parturient body to be so specifically configured that it deserved consideration as a whole. Most authors named postnatal illnesses after their most conspicuous symptoms: cases in which restless ranting was the main problem were characterized as “postpartum delirium” (產後譫語), but delirium also appeared in descriptions of “postpartum madness” (產後顛狂), “postpartum fevers” (發熱) and “postpartum sweating” (自汗). All these phrases that sounded like illness names did not refer to separate disorders, but to various symptoms that occurred to women soon after they had given birth.

“Postpartum madness” was dominated by mental confusion, indiscriminate scolding,

visions of ghosts (見鬼), and “fits of madness” (發狂). These problems occurred separately

or in various combinations, often associated with vertigo (眩暈), fevers, excessive loss of

## PDF第411页

blood, and abdominal pains. $ ^{61} $ Unsurprisingly considering how weak women were postpartum, the wildest forms of behavior found in formulaic descriptions of diankuang—climbing roofs, taking off one's clothes, etc.—were almost never mentioned in postpartum cases. Physicians sometimes used expressions like “resembling madness” (如狂), “like madness” (若狂), and “as if having a fit of madness” (如發狂) both to emphasize a resemblance with the symptoms of insanity and to express their awareness that the disorder at hand was not ordinary madness. We could accentuate the temporariness of “postpartum madness” by translating chanbou diankuang 產後癲狂 as “acting madly postpartum.”

The “madness” that sometimes followed parturition had long been noted in medical records. For example, a section of Tracing the Symptoms of Illnesses to their Origins (completed in 610) discussed “The symptoms of postpartum Wind-depletion, [epileptiform] dian, and mania” (產後風虛癲狂候). $ ^{62} $ Most discussions of women’s disorders published thereafter included a discussion of the symptoms of postpartum insanity.

Below I do not try to trace the entire history of Chinese medical approaches to postpartum disorders, nor will I attempt to explain all the complexities in the treatment of postpartum madness in Ming and Qing times. As I analyze the similarities and differences between “postpartum madness” and the gender-neutral disorder after which it was named, I want to explain what options were available to treat it and what general rationales were given to support these approaches. I conclude with evidence about how cases of postpartum

## PDF第412页

insanity were actually treated. This investigation should help to determine both the limits and the polyvalence of the common script of blockage of the Heart by mucus or Fire that dominated medical interpretations of madness in late-imperial China.

## Rival etiologies of postpartum disorders

There were two dominant medical explanations of postpartum disorders in late imperial times: 1) general depletion, and 2) the incomplete expulsion of pathological fluids, the postnatal discharge (blood, tissue, and mucus) that English speakers call lochia. Anxieties over blocked circulation and concerns over pollution pushed most physicians to purge these failed fluids. $ ^{63} $

The two main fluids that were identified as causing postpartum pathologies were “failed Blood” (败血) and “noxious dew” (惡露). The latter referred not only to “offensive lochia,” but to all postpartum emissions, whereas “failed Blood” meant Blood that had lost its vitality and could no longer circulate. $ ^{64} $ According to these conceptions, when “noxious dew” did not flow outward properly or was not properly purged, it could cause a variety of disorders, from abdominal pains to fainting spells. “Noxious dew attacking upwards” (惡露上攻) could also affect the Heart, causing delirium or odd behavior. A similar postpartum pathology was called “failed Blood assaulting the Heart” (败血衝心): lifeless Blood rushed upwards to strike the Heart and block its openings or chase the Spirit away from it.

## PDF第413页

Attribution of postpartum disorders to pathological fluids dominated Song medicine Wang Gun's 王滾 Recipes for Broad Succor (Boji fang 博濟方; 1047), for example, presented recipes for curing “postpartum noxious Blood attacking the Heart, with intermittent fits of anxiety” (產後惡血攻心時發燥) [4.5a], “noxious Blood assaulting the Heart” (惡血衝心) [4.4b], “postpartum oppression and anxiety in the Heart and chest, and stagnant noxious Blood” (產後心胷煩燥惡血不快) [4.5b]. The use of “noxious Blood” where other texts used “failed Blood” or “noxious dew” suggests that these terms were close equivalents.

This concentration on pathological fluids to explain postpartum disorders was

influentially explained in Chen Ziming's 陳子明 Complete Book of Excellent Recipes for Women

(Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; mid 13 $ ^{th} $ century). $ ^{65} $ Both explanations of “postpartum

madness” that Chen presented gave a role to “failed Blood.” He first spoke of how “failed

Blood flushes the Heart postpartum because of fright, [causing] confusion and feelings of

oppression, fits of madness, and the appearance of demonic affliction” (產後因驚 敗血沖

心 昏悶發狂 如有鬼祟). He also discussed the symptom of “seeing spectral entities

because failed Blood and deviant Qi have entered the Heart postpartum” (產後因敗血及邪

氣入心 如見祟物). $ ^{66} $ He then attributed both problems to failed Blood’s being “blocked

in the Heart’s openings (閉於心竅). $ ^{67} $

One of the main remedies to expel “noxious dew” in both Song and later times was the “Black-spirit powder” (黑神散), which was also known as “Black-metal (or Black-gold)

## PDF第414页

powder” (烏金散). It was composed of black beans (黑豆), steamed foxglove root (熟地黄), angelica root (當歸), cassia bark (肉桂, or cinnamonum), dried ginger (乾薑), licorice (甘草), peony (白芍), cat-tail root and pollen (蒲黃), and, in some versions of the recipe, raw foxglove (生地黃). $ ^{68} $ The wide popularity of this remedy probably came from its having been recommended in the widely influential Recipes of the Pharmacy Bureau. $ ^{69} $ In the late fourteenth century, an advocate of “Danxi” methods (see Chapter 5) included this recipe in his compilation because “contemporaries use it in a blanket manner to treat all postpartum disorders.” $ ^{70} $

A few Song physicians also emphasized that postpartum illnesses were rooted in depletion. Chen Yan 陳言 (a 12 $ ^{th} $-century physician from Zhejiang), for instance, explained that “after childbirth Qi and Blood are exhausted and the five organs are all damaged” (產後氣血既衰 五臟俱損). $ ^{71} $ But these medical writers were in the minority.

## PDF第415页

The most influential statement about the grounding of postpartum disorders into an underlying depletion was attributed to the Yuan-dynasty physician Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358), or “Danxi.” A doctor who sought relevant information in the popular Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (Huizhou edition: 1482) would have come upon these lines: “One ought not to be rendered [even more] depleted postpartum. The massive replenishing of Qi and Blood should take precedence. Treat miscellaneous symptoms as offshoots.” Most [postpartum] disorders are [caused by] Blood depletion: they cannot [be treated by] ‘dispersing the surface’.” $ ^{73} $ (產後無得令虛　當大補氣血為先　雖有雜證　以末治之　一切病多是血虛　皆不可發表) But the postpartum section of Danxi xinfa did not mention madness; it attributed “postpartum blood-dizziness” (血暈) to “failed Blood flowing into the Liver

## PDF第416页

tract” and warned that this kind of fainting was not caused by Wind but by Blood flowing backwards against the Heart (逆上湊心). $ ^{74} $

Most late-imperial practitioners accepted Danxi's injunction to treat postpartum disorders primarily with replenishing drugs, but with qualifications. Many healers, for example, continued to attribute these illnesses—especially those involving consciousness—to the imperfect ejection of fluids. Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540) adopted a replenishing strategy in a postpartum case in which his patient, however, begged to differ. $ ^{75} $ Sun Yikui 孫一奎 (1522-1619) refused to replenish a parturient women because he attributed her possession symptoms to the incomplete expulsion of noxious dew. $ ^{76} $ Huizhou doctor Cheng Jie 程玠 (late 16 $ ^{th} $ century) warned that cooling drugs could not be used postpartum: “after childbirth, the massive replenishing of Qi and Blood must be given precedence. When there are miscellaneous symptoms, cooling remedies cannot be used” (產後者 須大補氣血為主或有雜證 不可用寒涼之劑). $ ^{77} $ Pei Yizhong 裴一中 (17 $ ^{th} $-century Hangzhou) contested the Danxi xinfá's 丹溪心法 precept that miscellaneous disorders should be treated “as offshoots,” but instead of questioning Zhu Zhenheng himself, Pei said he “feared [these words] did not come from Danxi’s brush” but represented an error of transmission. Pei claimed that the expression should instead read, “if there are miscellaneous disorders, treat them as appropriate” (如有雜病 以權治之). $ ^{78} $

But not everyone agreed with the Danxi line. Zhang Jiebin 張介賓 (1563-1640), who rejected many of Zhu Zhenheng's approaches after having followed them in his youth (6.8),

## PDF第417页

started the postpartum section of his Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書 with a critique of Danxi's influential formulation.

In my early years, I sincerely respected [these views] and I used them steadfastly, but they caused difficulties every time. Only after I tried them many times did I realize that these words were probably excessive even if they were reasonable. Although this is the only method that is followed today in obstetrics, I have myself observed quite a few ways in which it is mistaken and have found many explanations and correctives for it. This is why I venture to scrutinize it [here] for posterity. If there are real insights [in my findings], I must discuss them: I am not willfully trying to shine or to slander the ancient worthies purposely. If I were, I would only become a target of mockery and insult. Although I am quite benighted, this should not happen.

Observers will carefully examine this intention [of mine]. $ ^{79} $

余於初年誠然佩服及執而用之則每為所困經者數次始悟其言雖有

理 而未免言之過也 即今產科所宗 無非此法 余目睹其誤 及親為解救

者 蓋不少矣 故敢剖析於後 實有所見 不得不言 非存心自炫 故毀先

賢若然則徒為笑罵之招爾賓雖至愚必不為也觀者其深察此意

Zhang's critique was part of the sixteenth-century questioning of some of the immoderate claims found in the Danxi corpus, but his concern not to "slander the ancient worthies" shows that Zhang was far from dismissing the Danxi approach. In emphasizing the need to distinguish between depletion and over-repletion before deciding on a cure, he was applying to postpartum disorders the approach that physicians trained in the most sophisticated version of the Danxi approach deployed when they tackled any medical problem. $ ^{80} $

## PDF第418页

Despite Zhang’s critique, Danxi’s position remained the default one against which physicians situated their views. Huai Yuan 懷遠 (turn of the  $ 19^{th} $ century) extolled the Danxi doctrine but lessened its sphere of application.

After a new delivery, Danxi says to massively replenish Blood and Qi, and that even if there are other symptoms, they should be treated as offshoots. This is an excellent doctrine, but it is acceptable only when noxious dew has come down completely. It does not apply to cases in which noxious dew has not been exhausted. $ ^{81} $

新產之後 丹溪謂大補氣血 雖有他症 以末治之 其說甚善 然此謂惡露

下盡而言則可 惡露未盡而言則不可

Zhao Zhuquan 趙竹泉 (from Dantu 丹徒 in Jiangsu), who wrote a lot about postpartum disorders in his Complementary Essentials of Medicine (Yimen buyao 醫門補要; preface from 1897), proposed a version similar to Danxi's: "After delivery, the hundred vessels are all empty: it is not fitting to disperse the surface. Moreover remedies that are either greatly Hot or greatly Cold harm Qi and Blood and aggravate the [original] depletion. If there are other symptoms, they should all be treated chiefly by regulating Blood."²⁸ (產後百脈皆空 不宜

發表　並大寒大熱之劑　傷敗氣血　再虛其虛　雖有他症　皆以調血為主)

Postpartum madness: the dominance of common scripts

Let us finally turn to more concrete discussions of postpartum mad symptoms. I want to show that physicians traced puerperal insanity almost exclusively to the imbalances and pathological fluids that dominate the postpartum body, but that their explanations were still couched in the well-known script of attack on (or blockage of) the Heart, which I

## PDF第419页

explained at length in previous chapters. I found two exceptions: Wang Yingzhen 王應震 (fl.

Guangxu period [1875-1908]) attributed one woman's manic behavior to the invasion of the

Heart Envelope and the Liver tract by mucus that predated parturition. $ ^{83} $ And his older

contemporary Wang Leting 王樂亭 attributed one woman's postpartum insanity to mucus

and Fire, the two most common sources of madness in late-imperial medicine. $ ^{84} $

Most doctors who faced postpartum madness agreed that Danxi's construal of the postnatal condition as one of dangerous depletion applied only to situations in which lochia had already been expelled. Few physicians attributed postpartum insanity primarily to depletion. Chen E 陳鄂 (b. 1794), from Hunan, was one of the few specialists who did. In Understanding Medicine at One Glance (Yi jian zhi yi 一見知醫; compiler's preface from 1868), he argued that "postpartum rash speech and visions" (產後妄言妄見) were caused by the double depletion of Qi and Blood, leaving the Spirit and Cloud-souls nothing to rest upon (氣血兩虛 神魂無依). $ ^{85} $ This disorder required an extended "massive replenishing" (大補) treatment. The same book traced postpartum fits of madness (發狂) to "deficiency of Blood and depletion of the Liver causing extreme [inner] Fire" (血虧肝虛火炎之極). However, Chen E conceded that chest oppression, anxious agitation, as well as visions of spirits and ghosts occurred together when failed Blood attacked the Heart (敗血攻心). $ ^{86} $

## PDF第420页

Some doctors ascribed mad symptoms to several possible etiologies. Feng

Zhaozhang 馮兆張 (fl. 2 $ ^{nd} $ half of the 17 $ ^{th} $ century) included a brief entry on “postpartum fits

of madness” (產後發狂) in his Secret Records of the Brocade Sack (Jinnang milu 錦囊秘錄; ca.

1694), where he outlined four possible causes for postnatal madness: “sudden collapse of

Yin Blood, and Fire caused by Liver depletion” (陰血暴崩 肝虛火炎), “blockage by failed

Blood” (敗血停滯), “depletion and exhaustion of Cardiac Blood” (心血虛損), and “Yin

Fire caused by Renal depletion, forcing itself upwards” (腎虛陰火上迫).^{87} Cheng Guopeng

程國彭 (early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) gave two explanations. $ ^{88} $ “Mad words or delirium and sudden

visions of ghosts and spirits” (狂言譫語 乍見鬼神) originated either in “failed Blood

flushing upwards” (敗血上沖) or in “Blood depletion making the Spirit unable to hold to its

lodging” (血虛神不守舍), though failed Blood was “generally” (大抵) the culprit.

Despite these diverse explanations, in Qing times the flushing of the Heart by “failed Blood” was by far the most commonly identified cause of postpartum insanity. $ ^{89} $ Many case records show the prominence of this etiology. Wang Zhizheng 王之政 ( $ \ddot{z}i $Jiufeng 九風; 1753-1830?), from Dantu in Zhenjiang prefecture, had served at the imperial court in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. After he had returned to Yangzhou a few years later, he had taken numerous disciples and frequented many notables and high officials. $ ^{90} $

## PDF第421页

Not all his patients were illustrious, however, and some were women who had just given birth. One of them suffered from mad symptoms.

After parturition, noxious dew did not circulate and [she] was therefore bedridden.

Suddenly, failed Blood flushed upwards, where it perturbed the Heart's Envelope

and congealed to cause bloating. She was unaware of things, as if drunk or crazy.

Liver Wind was roused and she laughed and talked abundantly, her mind ill at ease. $ ^{9} $

胎產之後 惡露不行 因於臥 卒敗血上沖 擾亂心胞 瘀凝作脹 人事不

省 如醉如瘋 鼓動肝風 多笑多語 心神不安

Wang detected traces of stasis. “Qi and Blood cannot flow harmoniously, so that Qi is turning into Wind and Fire. Failed Blood follows them [upwards], and madness manifests itself” (氣血不得和暢 氣化風火 敗血隨之 癲狂見矣). The mechanism Wang described was the same as in a number of his other cases of insanity, but he managed to tie the well-known script of “attack on the Heart” to his patient’s postpartum circumstances.

Xie Xinghuan 謝星煥 (from Jinxi 金溪 in Fuzhou 撫州 prefecture, Jiangxi) traced his patient's postpartum mad symptoms to the "flushing of the Heart's Envelope" (中心胞) by "dirty stagnant Blood" (污瘀之血). $ ^{92} $ Some practitioners exceptionally located blockage elsewhere than in the Heart. Shen Juren 沈菊仁, from Suzhou, described his patient as the victim of Blood accumulation in the Liver tract and determined that the Blood had coalesced in this way because of incomplete elimination of noxious dew. $ ^{93} $

Since the “incomplete expulsion of lochia” usually manifested itself by external bleeding, its internal counterpart “failed Blood” easily became the first candidate cause for

## PDF第422页

whatever illness occurred after such bleeding had taken place. This ascription of madness to

pathological fluids was so deeply entrenched that it often preceded descriptions of the

postpartum symptoms at hand, as if this etiology were assumed to be true. One of the

disorders mentioned in Li Shengchun's 李盛春 (Hubei) Yixue yanyue 醫學研悅 (1626) was

“when Blood enters the Heart tract and language is upside down” (血入心經 語言顛

倒). $ ^{94} $ Lin Peiqin's 林珮琴 (Suzhou) Leizheng zhicai 類證治裁 (1839) contained an entry

called “when failed Blood encroaches upon the Heart and one speaks madly and sees

ghosts” (敗血干心 狂言見鬼). $ ^{95} $

I showed in 4.6 that the idea that failed Blood could block the Heart's openings

predated the script of blockage by mucus. A passage I cited there as an early example of an

illness's attribution to blockage of "the Heart's openings" happened to be about a

postpartum disorder: "After delivery, in many cases depletion of Blood and weakness of Qi

lead to stoppage and accumulation: failed Blood blocks the Heart's openings, making the

mind unable to attain clarity" (產後血虛氣弱 多致停積 敗血閉於心竅 神志不能明

了). $ ^{96} $ This was a case of muteness, not madness, but the process described was similar: an

inner fluid caused mental confusion by blocking the Heart. This passage suggests that the

usual attribution of madness to blockage of the Heart by pathological fluids originated in

explanations of postpartum disorders. It is therefore not surprising that explanations of

postpartum madness resembled explanations of ordinary madness.

## PDF第423页

### 9.4 Conclusion

Medical historians have been debating whether late-imperial physicians believed that women's disorders were dominated by Blood. Charlotte Furth (1999) has argued that fewer and fewer doctors after the thirteenth century portrayed the bodies and illnesses of women as being governed by Blood. She also contended that the scope of prescriptions specially designed for women narrowed in Ming times. Without explicitly addressing the issue of "separate prescriptions," Joanna Grant (2003) tried to show that Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540) did not attribute non-reproductive disorders to Blood, but Marta Hanson in turn questioned Grant's claims in a long review (2005) of her book.

I have shown that in both treatises and case files, physicians from the eleventh century onwards only marginally attributed the mad behavior of women to Blood pathologies. Very few physicians attributed insanity to menstrual imbalances (one major script of Blood pathologies), or even mentioned Blood as a factor. Fang Lue's 方略 attribution of a woman's mania to menstrual blockage in the mid nineteenth century was exceptional among cases that adopted largely the same etiologies as those found in general treatises and in men's cases, namely some version of the mucus-Fire explanation.

The only circumstance in which doctors consistently traced women's madness to Blood pathologies was after parturition, but although “noxious dew” and “damaged Blood” occurred only in gestating and postpartum women, they were described as causing madness through the same processes as Fire and mucus did. Postpartum madness thus shared many similarities with the prototypical form of kuang: it was not explained psycho-dynamically, was temporary and extreme, was thought to be caused by displacement of the Spirit or blockage of the Heart, and was treated with drugs. Blockage of the Heart by pathological fluids was

## PDF第424页

common to both sexes, and to both postpartum and non-postpartum insanity. Even physicians who emphasized the uniqueness of the postpartum condition embraced the script of cardiac blockage. What differed were the blocking agent and the circumstances in which that agent was created.

## PDF第425页

Part Two

# LEGAL AND POPULAR PRACTICES

Qing

(1644 – 1911)

## PDF第426页

# 10

## Prologue to Part Two

Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts.

In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

## PDF第427页

designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂.

## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced

Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled.

In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect.

In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ^{5} $ Even when he agreed with the magistrate’s verdict, the prefect still had to write his own report and send it to the provincial judge, the highest legal official in the province. After having made sure that the sentence meted out was consistent with the evidence presented, the judge passed the case to the governor or

## PDF第428页

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries),

transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand

Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final

confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments”

(Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by

the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the

killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even

cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men

who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

But routine memorials have limits as sources of information. Since they were written only after a sentence had been meted out, the information they presented was designed to lead seamlessly to the proposed verdict. These highly structured reports left no room for debate, unless an official from the Ministry of Punishments had raised a point that required debate. When doubts were expressed, it was usually to show that they had already been resolved. The limits of these documents depend on the information we seek from them. They may provide little information on the daily life of “ordinary madmen,” but they are are excellent sources to study how Qing law treated homicides committed because of madness.

The term tiben is usually translated as “routine memorial” in opposition to “palace memorials” (奏摺), which concerned matters demanding immediate imperial attention.

## PDF第429页

Created in the late seventeenth century, palace memorials exploded in numbers after the creation of the Grand Council during the Yongzheng reign (1723-1735). The Grand Council, which unlike the Grand Secretariat worked in direct collaboration with the monarch, became the most important agency in the central government from the 1730s until the end of the Qing. Legal cases judged grave (like parricide or sedition) had to be reported to the Grand Council by palace memorial. Such cases were processed more quickly than those reported through routine memorials, because palace memorials were considered more urgent, and because the sentences meted out in them necessitated no repeated reviews. Unlike tiben, palace memorials could also contain documentary attachments, such as seized texts or more detailed transcriptions of depositions. The cases I discuss in Chapters 14 and 15 were all brought to the attention of the throne through palace memorials.

Besides “routine” and “palace” memorials, I also found information on Qing official views of madness in successive editions of the Qing Code (called Dai Qing liü 大清律, Dai Qing liü jijie fuli 大清律集解附例, or Dai Qing lüli 大清律例 depending on when they were published), commentaries on the Code, imperial edicts, memorials on policy issues, as well as several collections of legal cases.

As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will refer to Qing legal articles by their numbers in Huang Jingjia's 黄靜嘉 1970 re-edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes (Duli cunyi 讀例存疑; 1905). Xue's work was originally not an edition of the code, but an unpublished critical commentary on the "sub-statutes" (例) as they existed after 1870, when the Qing Code had last been overhauled. $ ^{9} $

## PDF第430页

Lingering Doubts contained neither the diagrams (on family relations, legal instruments of punishment, technical terms, table of monetary redemption, etc.) included at the beginning of the official editions of the Code, nor the running commentaries that topped each page in both private and state-sponsored editions of the Code. It is Huang Jingjia's inclusion of these tables and his numbering of all the statutes and sub-statutes that make his edition (not a "reprint") of Lingering Doubts so convenient. Xue's analyses themselves are invaluable for tracing the origin of most sub-statutes, though he was imprecise about Kangxi-era (1662-1722) legislation.

## The pre-Qing handling of mad homicides

Let me now discuss pre-Qing legal treatments of mad homicides and trace at the same time the emergence of the character feng 瘋, by which Qing law officially referred to insanity. The terms by which "madness" was designated in Chinese imperial law changed through time, yet no word in particular implied either distance from or closeness with medical approaches. The legal treatment of mad people under dynasties that used medical terms like diankuang 顛狂 and fengkuang 風狂 seems to have been no more (or less) compatible with medical approaches than that of eras that used less medical concepts like xinfeng 心風 or kuangyi 狂易.

Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as crimes, Chinese imperial law sentenced killings committed because of madness differently depending on the circumstances. $ ^{10} $ For the larger part of the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), people who killed in a state of insanity were sentenced to death. Only in the early second century CE

## PDF第431页

were mad killers granted an unspecified reduction of punishment (狂易殺人 待減重論). $ ^{11} $

No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

## PDF第432页

Unlike the Tang and Song, the Yuan dynasty (1260-1368) promulgated several explicit laws concerning homicides committed by mad people. Yuan courts were instructed to diminish the sentence of mad killers, but not to absolve them from penalties entirely. For instance, a man who killed one of his parents or grand-parents in a state of insanity (風狂) was to be simply “executed” (處死) rather than put to death by dismemberment. $ ^{14} $ In 1269 a man killed four people in a fit of “Heart Wind” (心風). $ ^{15} $ Legal officials petitioned his case to the emperor (上請) “in accordance with the old precedent” (照依舊例) that instructed to do so in cases of killing because of insanity (顛狂殺人). $ ^{16} $ Khubilai Khan eventually absolved the killer from the death penalty by imperial edict (敕裁) and condemned him to paying 50 ounces of silver (per person?) in “cremation or burial fees” (燒埋銀) to the family of his victims, the same amount as for people who committed a regular homicide. $ ^{17} $

Khubilai's reasoning may have been the same as the one behind another Yuan law stating that someone who, in a state of madness (病風狂), injured another person during a fight, leading to that person's death (毆傷人致死), should be "absolved of crime" (免罪),

## PDF第433页

though cremation or burial fees should still be collected (徵燒埋銀) from the culprit (History of the Yuan 105: 2678). Early Qing laws handled mad homicides in a similar fashion, though the legal reasoning that justified monetary compensation differed. $ ^{18} $ Another Yuan law

absolved those who “spoke inappropriately” (失口亂言) as long as they had done so while

“suffering from Wind madness” (害風狂疾) and if there was nothing else behind their

words. $ ^{19} $ As I will show in Chapters 14 and 15, the Qing attitude toward seditious words

pronounced by mad people would be much harsher.

Yuan legal documents referred to madness as xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”), feng kuang 風狂 (“Wind kuang”), dian kuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), and perhaps even feng bing 瘋病, the term that Qing law would officially adopt to discuss insanity. $ ^{20} $ Despite their different origins, these names did not have different legal implications. $ ^{21} $

## PDF第434页

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $

As mainstream medicine stopped attributing madness to Wind, $ ^{25} $ feng in the above compounds became an etymological reminder rather than an etiological marker. In other words, the term fengkuang no longer implied a belief that madness was caused by Wind, just as “lunatic” no longer refers to people who have lost their mind because of the moon. $ ^{26} $ In the late seventeenth century, for example, a physician from Huizhou in Anhui explained in his medical treatise that kuang people were often called fengzi 風子, a term that became widely used in Qing times, though it tended to be written 瘋子. $ ^{27} $ That doctor’s use of the

## PDF第435页

character for “wind” (風) rather than the homophonous one for “madness” (瘋) probably shows that the term “fengzt” came from the spoken vernacular. This and the examples I cited in the previous paragraph also show that feng 瘋 was a graphic novelty, not a semantic one.

Fengbing 瘋病 in Qing law: the word and its referents

Although the sound feng commonly referred to madness, feng bing 瘋病 (“madness illness”) was still rare when Qing legislators decided to adopt it as the official name for madness in 1667. $ ^{28} $ Before that, fengbing 瘋病 and fengji 瘋疾 had been used in memorials of the Shunzhi reign (1644-1661), which were written in either Manchu or Chinese depending on the language of their author. $ ^{29} $ For archiving purposes and to make them readable to all officials, they were then translated into the other language. Now the earliest instances of fengji and fengbing I found appeared in memorials by Manchu officials. If these officials wrote in Manchu (which is likely so early in the Qing period, when few Manchus had mastered classical Chinese), then both fengbing and fengji may have been translations of a Manchu word for insanity. $ ^{30} $ Central institutions may then have started to use fengbing as a matter of course, until it became the official Qing term for madness after the penal denomination “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人) was created in 1667 (see 11.2).

## PDF第436页

Feng bing (written 瘋病) may first have sounded as inelgant in Chinese as it does in its literal English translation "madness illness." The continuous use of fengdian 風顛 and fengkuang 風狂 (both written with the character for "Wind" 風) in legal documents well into the eighteenth century suggests that the form 瘋 still rarely referred to madness in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. $ ^{31} $ In 1736, one memorialist cited the law on "killing because of madness" as fengdian sharen 風顛殺人 (instead of fengbing sharen 瘋病殺人), and as late as 1816, a copyist of the Ministry of Punishments was reprimanded for writing yin feng shan as 因風殺人 (literally, "killing because of Wind") in the summary of a memorial. $ ^{32} $

A few scholars have suggested that the choice of fengbing over common medical terms like fengdian, kuang, or diankuang shows that Qing law targeted another kind of madness than medicine did. Angelika Messner (2000: 22), for example, has suggested that Qing laws did not target "insanity as illness," but a kind of non-medical "raging madness" (rasenden Wabnsinn). $ ^{33} $ This distinction does not hold. First, non-legal authors spoke of fengbing without hinting that it meant a kind of violent insanity. $ ^{34} $ Furthermore, the medical literature had long noted the homicidal potential of mad people. $ ^{35} $ Qing legal documents consistently described insanity as a medical disorder that people could catch (de 得 or ranhuan 染患) and recover

## PDF第437页

from (yu 愈), and that could flare up (fa 發, fan 犯) or be treated medically (yizhi 醫治).

Finally, Qing legal reports often designated madness by common medical terms like fengkuang or fengdian, or compounds like “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” (痰迷心竅), which referred to the most common medical explanation for mad behavior. The “illness” in “madness illness” was clearly more than a manner of speaking.

Feng was a non-technical term with as broad and vague a semantic field as “mad” in English that just happened to become Qing legalese for madness. After fenghing became a category of legal practice, legal operations shaped it into a unique object. Unlike doctors, for example, Qing officials handled mad people as if their illness habitually led to killings (11.3). But this novelty would have emerged even if Qing law had called madness “diankuang” or “Heart Wind.”

## Language, objects, and practices

In the following chapters, I shall compare legal and medical treatments of madness and its sufferers. I will not describe two different sets of categories, but two fields of practice with different priorities that happened to use different terms to refer to insanity. I try to give as much significance as possible to the practices through which all documents (and therefore all the objects described in these documents) were produced, because these practices often took for granted certain views of reality and tried to present certain courses of action as natural. Both medical and legal case files—which were not disinterested factual reports but records of intervention—were sometimes interactively involved in the events they described. Legal reports, for example, both justified and implemented judicial verdicts. $ ^{36} $

## PDF第438页

The outside realities that legal and medical documents referred to and that officials and physicians acted upon were not pre-imagined and ready to be acted upon. The development of the legal objects “madness illness” and “killing because of madness” was shaped by the operations of the Qing legal system. Legal discourses on madness differed from medical ones in one important aspect: they were bound by the rules and habitus of an institution. The meaning of “madness” in legal practice is thus easier to trace to institutional logics than comparable objects in the medical field. By formalizing procedures and imposing the use of certain categories (like “killing because of madness”), Qing legal institutions tried to ensure that individuals would not deal with cases through their own habitus and categories. The law therefore handled people found guilty of “killing because of madness” in more uniform ways than doctors treated mad patients, and tended to use more formulaic language to describe mad acts. $ ^{37} $

In the following chapters, I shall propose more concrete comparisons between legal and medical approaches to mad acts and mad people, and discuss interactions between medical and legal specialists. My approach will bring fresh insights to comparisons between law and medicine, which have so far mostly concentrated on the forensic handbook The Washing Away of Wrongs (Xiyuan lu 洗冤錄).

## PDF第439页

# 11 Errors and projections

The emergence of “madness” in Qing law, 1654-1750

Because they are produced in concrete and particular social relations, the meaning and the materiality of law are inseparable. $ ^{1} $

Previous scholarship on Qing madness laws has largely focused on the question of the “criminal responsibility” of mad people, in reference to an early scholarly debate: in riposte to an adverse review (van der Valk 1948) of his annotated translation (Bünger 1946) of important legal texts, Karl Bünger (1903-1997) maintained that “the exemption of lunatics from punishment [.... has been] a principle of the Chinese law from the Zhou dynasty until nowadays” (Bünger 1950: 8-9). This argument, based chiefly on two texts separated by more than a millennium, can be disproved with counter-examples. $ ^{2} $ More serious is the fact that even refutations have not significantly modified Bünger’s framework. Nakamura Shigeo (1973d), Martha Li Chiu (1981), Vivien Ng (1980, 1984, and 1990), and Erhard Rosner (1991) have all implied that the way the Qing legal system treated insane offenders was the system’s approach to the problem of their “criminal responsibility.”

As I shall show, Qing laws that mentioned insanity applied only to killings and were not based on any principle that defined responsibility at large. Using “criminal responsibility” as a category of analysis has called disproportionate attention to minor phenomena and

## PDF第440页

made significant ones more difficult to perceive. Since we still lack an accurate description of how Qing “madness laws” appeared and changed and what kind of object they targeted, our comparison of law with medicine and other Qing fields suffers accordingly.

Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. Gravity was itself assessed by measuring the degree of intention of the culprit, the kinship proximity between offender and victim, and any other circumstance that officials found relevant. To mete out sentences, legal officials deployed categories such as the “six killings” (11.1) and assessed criminal intention or the relative status of offender and victim. Legal practice had to respect certain procedures (e.g., the necessity to obtain a criminal’s confession before sentencing) and took place within institutional constraints—the annual review of death sentences at the Autumn Assizes, multiple sources of law, the proliferation of ad hoc substitutes, etc. To consider the role of all these factors in the formation of the legal object “madness illness” is to appreciate the inseparability of “the meaning and materiality of law.”
