This chapter explains how a new crime called “killing because of madness illness” (瘋病殺人) emerged in the 1660s, how it developed, why it was almost immediately likened to “accidental killing,” and what views of madness and mad people it generated. I mainly discuss laws and cases from between 1667 and 1762, roughly a hundred years during which the punishment for “killing because of madness” remained a simple monetary payment to the family of the victim. Chapter 12 explains the evolution of “killing because of madness” after 1762.

## PDF第441页

The first section, on the conceptual antecedents of Qing madness laws, describes the continuous interest Chinese law displayed for criminal intention and the network of laws in which “killing because of madness” was eventually integrated. In section two, I explain how the first laws concerning insanity emerged in the 1660s, and how Qing officials soon had to find solutions to problems that the laws themselves had created. Part three analyzes the view of madness that criminal trials generated and deployed, and the last section explains the emergence and development of measures of preventive confinement against “people with madness illness.” Chapter 12 continues to examine how Qing laws concerning madness became more severe starting in the late eighteenth century even though the conception of insanity that underlay them remained the same. Chapters 11 and 12 should be read alongside Appendix 7, a detailed chronological account of the evolution of the relevant laws.

##### ... The centrality of criminal intention in Qing homicide 1-

Sophisticated legal traditions have found many ways to integrate the distinction between voluntary and involuntary acts into law. In most Western legal traditions, to be considered legally responsible, someone must be shown to have displayed mens rea—evil intent. Insistence on intention as a condition for criminal responsibility is what distinguishes current systems from hypothetical ones that insist on “strict liability,” an approach in which offenders are punished purely on the basis of the outcome of their acts. But mens rea was never a Chinese legal principle. Intention was never the object of any entry in the “Definitions and general principles” (名例) section of any Chinese code, and no rule ever claimed that intention should always be relevant to sentencing. Yet, Chinese interest in criminal intention in fact arose as early as the Zhou, when a clear distinction was made

## PDF第442页

between intentional (gu 故) and non-intentional (sheng 售) acts. $ ^{4} $ This distinction was later preserved in successive codes under different names. $ ^{5} $ Before I explain how Qing homicide laws were structured according to their degree of intention, yet did not rely on the principle of mens rea, let me briefly explain the terms by which Qing law discussed intentions.

A network of key categories defined the semantic field of criminal intention in Qing homicide laws. Xin 心, a word I have translated as “Heart” in medical texts, is best rendered as “mind” or “intent” in legal contexts because legal discussion of xin surrounded intentions, not the functioning of any organ. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). Gu 故 was used adverbially in “killing intentionally” (故殺).

These terms formed the core of numerous legal rules and they were deployed in myriads of judicial cases through history, from the county level where the first provisional sentence was meted out, to the Autumn Assizes (秋審) where intent was often crucial in determining whether a death sentence would be executed or commuted. Concern with

## PDF第443页

intention was deployed particularly clearly in the network of laws on homicides in which “killing because of madness” would be integrated.

The six killings: a hierarchy of intentions

Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer.

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming

(謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected

such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse.

“The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the

Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the

main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

## PDF第444页

person was what differentiated “killing by design” from “intentional killing” (故殺), which applied when a killer’s homicidal intent was “not known to others” (非人所知). $ ^{10} $ No matter whether this intent to kill was premeditated or arose during a fight (臨時有意欲殺), the lone culprit was judged as having committed “intentional killing” and was sentenced to the same punishment as for “Killing by design”: effective decapitation after the Assizes.

“Killing in an affray” (鬪殺) meant killing unintentionally during a fight. $ ^{11} $ In Qing law, intent to kill had become the main difference between “killing in an affray” and “intentional killing.” As a result, those who had killed during a fight had to demonstrate that they had not intended to kill their victim. $ ^{12} $ Under most circumstances, the ensuing sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (絞監候) was eventually commuted to the highest sentence below death, namely exile to 3000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy bamboo.

In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting in an automatic commutation to exile at 3,000 li. “Strangulation after the Assizes” was also the basic sentence for “mistakenly killing” (誤殺) a bystander while fighting somebody else. Since this crime was committed during a deliberate act of violence, the sentence was adjusted depending on the gravity of the act during which the lethal

## PDF第445页

mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $

“Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $

Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). (Accident means that which the ear or eye does not extend to, or what was not contemplated. For example, when one is shooting wild animals or when, for a reason, one is tossing bricks or tiles and unexpectedly kills another; or again if, when one is climbing on precipitous [terrain], one's foot slips and one falls, thus involving [i.e., harming] others; or if one is aboard a ship that has been driven by the wind, or is riding a horse that runs [wildly] in a

## PDF第446页

fright, or is driving a cart at a rapid speed on a descent, when the situation makes it so that one cannot stop; $ ^{19} $ or if several persons are carrying a heavy object and the strength [of one] is insufficient to control it so that the other persons carrying it are hurt. In all [these] cases where initially there was no intention to  $ barm^{20} $ another, but which led by chance to the killing or injuring of another, sentence as if for killing or wounding in an affray, and grant [the privilege of] monetary redemption according to law. [The silver] will be given to the household [of the person] who was killed or injured, to serve as funds for burial or medical treatment.)

若過失殺傷人者 （較戲殺愈輕） 各准鬥殺傷罪 依律收贖 給付其 （被殺傷之） 家 （過失 謂耳目所不及 思慮所不到 如彈射禽獸 因事投擲甎瓦 不期而殺人者 或因升高險足有蹉跌 累及同伴 或駕船使風乘馬驚走 馳車下坡 勢不能止 或共舉重物 力不能制 損及同舉物者 凡初無害人之意 而偶致殺傷人者 皆准鬬毆殺傷人罪 依律收贖 給付被殺傷之家 以為營葬及醫藥之資）

<div style="text-align: center;">The clause specifying that death could not be foreseen pointed to the difference between accidental killing and “Killing in play.” Accidental death was entirely coincidental.</div>


### 11.2 The first Qing laws on madness: creating a new legal object

An early case of parricide

The earliest Qing case of madness I have identified was a somewhat enigmatic parricide that took place in 1654, about ten years after the Manchus had seized Beijing and officially named it the capital of the newly established Qing dynasty. $ ^{21} $ I find it enigmatic

## PDF第447页

because it was sentenced in a way that differed from (as far as I know) all earlier and later cases of parricide in a state of madness in Chinese imperial history, and because it involved a particular class of people that was unique to the early Qing. $ ^{22} $

Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $

Since this affair concerned tribes under the direct supervision of Manchu institutions like the Imperial Clan and Imperial Household Departments, the memorial reporting the crime may have been written in Manchu before it was translated into Chinese. As I suggested above (10, p. 417), it is possible that feng ji 瘋疾 and feng bing 瘋病 used in

## PDF第448页

Shunzhi-era (1644-1662) memorials were translations from Manchu. The use of feng bing in these Chinese reports may then have pushed the government to adopt feng bing as the official Chinese term for "madness" instead of the more common feng dian 風癲 and feng kuang 風狂, or the more classical diankuang 癲狂 that had been used in Tang and Song law and was still being used in seventeenth-century medicine.

A momentous inaccuracy: the 1667 creation of “killing because of madness”

The earliest Qing law concerning madness was a terse rule from 1667 mandating that, “Whenever [people with] madness illness kill or injure another, exempt from sentencing” (凡瘋病殺傷人者免議). $ ^{25} $ In its lack of both context and definition, this rule was typical of the “Currently operative regulations of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例) of the Kangxi era (1662-1722), but unlike most Kangxi rules, which usually consisted in precedent-based guidelines for modulating the punishment of existing crimes, the 1667 law created a new penal denomination (罪名): “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人), which I shall render as “killing because of madness.” $ ^{26} $

## PDF第449页

In imperial Chinese law, a “penal denomination” was not the charge brought against one at the onset of a trial, but the name of the crime for which one was sentenced. Officials selected the fittest “crime name” by assessing the circumstances of the crime, but “[o]nce an offense was judged to fall into a certain category of crime [罪名], the punishment was predetermined.” $ ^{27} $ Both ideally and in practice, the officials who administered this system of fixed punishments were concerned “to maintain a balance between the offense and the penalty.” This judicial approach differed from that of physicians, who gave little importance to denominations in identifying the cause of an illness, and who aimed to put an end to pathological acts rather than find an apt punishment for them.

Like the 1654 judgment in the parricide case involving a “hunter,” the 1667 rule on “killing and injuring another because of madness” disregarded the way in which the code dealt with homicides. As we saw above, even those found guilty of “Killing by accident,” the least serious of the six killings, still had to pay compensation to the family of their victim. If the officials who reviewed the 1667 case had been more concerned with the general structure of the code, they should have rejected the verdict of complete exemption. $ ^{28} $

The imbalance in sentencing introduced by the ad hoc 1667 rule was quickly noticed and corrected. In 1669, the Ministry of Punishments decided that, as in cases of accidental killing, one who killed in a state of madness would have to pay 12.42 ounces of silver to the victim's family before being released. $ ^{29} $ This new rule marked the time when “killing because

## PDF第450页

of madness” was integrated into the network of the six killings. $ ^{30} $ From then on, and until the end of the Qing, killings committed in a fit of insanity were legally viewed as lacking both awareness and criminal intention.

The 1667 and 1669 laws concerned only homicides, not madness and mad people in general. The 1669 rule in particular integrated “killing because of madness” within the six killings. Note that in both rate and rationale, the compensation the 1669 rule sought for “killing because of madness” differed significantly from the privilege of monetary redemption granted to some disabled people in statute 22 of the Qing code, which was called “Allowing redemption for the old, the young, and the disabled” (老小廢疾收贖). $ ^{31} $ Under statute 22, one could redeem a death sentence with 0.525 ounces of silver, much less that the 12.42 ounces sought for “killing because of madness.” $ ^{32} $ People who were the object of legalized pity under statute 22 could redeem a real sentence with money; whereas in the case of “[people with] madness illness killing another,” the payment was the sentence, imposed for an inadvertent act that had led to a grave outcome. Compassion was not a factor in the 1669 rule, which likened “killing because of madness” to “accidental killing” on the basis of their common lack of criminal intention. $ ^{33} $

For decades, the 1669 law governed the sentencing of most killings identified as having been committed “because of madness,” even when there were multiple casualties or

## PDF第451页

when the victim was the killer's senior relative. In 1697, for instance, a woman who had killed her husband during a bout of insanity had been sentenced to death by the Governor of Zhili (the region surrounding Beijing) according to the statute on "A Wife or Concubine Striking her Husband" (妻妾歐夫). $ ^{34} $ In other words, that Governor had not found madness relevant to his judgment. The Ministry of Punishments, however, thought otherwise and ordered him to sentence the woman under "killing because of madness" after having made sure that she had indeed been insane at the moment of committing her crime. $ ^{35} $ No monetary compensation was necessary because husband and wife formed a single family. $ ^{36} $

Until 1776, multiple killings committed in a state of insanity were also sentenced under the 1669 rule that sought a monetary redemption from the family of the killer. $ ^{37} $ This way of sentencing is remarkable, because the killing of several persons from a single family was legally defined as one of the “ten abominations,” and as such was usually considered to deserve death by dismemberment, the most severe penalty in the entire code. $ ^{38} $

But the 1669 law was not applied to all homicides committed in a state of insanity. I have identified, for example, three distinct ways in which mad people who had killed an elder brother were judged before 1766. They could be 1) exempted according to the law on “killing because of madness”; $ ^{39} $ 2) sentenced to 3 years of penal servitude (and 100 strokes of the heavy stick) for “Accidentally killing an elder brother”; $ ^{40} $ 3) sentenced to immediate

## PDF第452页

decapitation for “Killing one’s elder brother,” but with a declaration that the killer had been mad and that his sentence should therefore be commuted to decapitation after the Assizes. $ ^{41} $ As I shall explain in Chapter 12, only in the second half of the eighteenth century did the Qing judiciary develop clear guidelines for adjudicating crimes against senior relatives and forbid sentencing under the 1669 law on “killing because of madness.”

### 11.3 The nature of “madness” in early Qing cases

The legal view of mad acts as absolutely unconscious and unintentional that emerged in the late seventeenth century persisted until the end of the Qing. Though it was nowhere theorized, this perception reliably guided the handling of homicide cases, the kind of practice from which this view had emerged in the first place. This legal understanding of mad acts in turn determined what aspects of madness were noticed in trials.

Unlike doctors, official investigators were more interested in the nature of mad acts than in the symptoms of madness. Above all, they needed to determine whether an act of killing had been committed in a state of madness. Physicians displayed little interest in determining whether a patient was mad, because relatives had usually made this diagnosis for them already. But the essence of a judicial decision in cases involving madness was to decide whether or not the penal denomination "killing because of madness" applied.

Since any motive or intention behind an act put a case outside the scope of “killing because of madness,” officials had to establish that a homicidal act had been committed for no reason whatsoever. This view of mad acts as purely unintentional was sought and confirmed in concrete cases, a fact that points again to the powerful role of legal operations

## PDF第453页

in shaping the Qing legal discourse on madness. As the following three cases will show, no case was inherently a “madness case.”

## Three cases

One morning early in 1735, the chief of a “Miao” village in Dali prefecture (Yunnan) went to the magistrate’s office to report that a house had caught fire and that Jia Chandou 叚昆斗, his wife, and their two daughters had disappeared. $ ^{42} $ No one knew if they had fled or perished in the fire. The next day, the magistrate and his assistants went to the village and started to collect the names of the house’s dwellers while asking about the cause of the fire. They found that the house was built around a courtyard, and that two of the building’s four sides had burned to the ground—that of the missing man and that of his aunt. Chandou’s father said that the fire had started in the side that belonged to his son, who, he remarked, had been suffering from madness. A bone was discovered in the debris, as well as (the coroner determined) the remains of a liver, pieces of intestines, and a metal axe. At the bottom of a well in the courtyard where they were drawing water, people soon discovered the bodies of Jia’s wife and younger daughter, both naked and showing traces of injuries. Since it was too dark outside to examine them, only in the following morning did the coroner record the blade-inflicted wounds on the chart of the human body that had to be filled out when dead bodies were found. It was determined that Jia’s wife was still alive when she fell into the well, but that the girl was already dead.

The magistrate then sent his first report (called xiang 詳) to his superiors, saying that Jia and one of his daughters were still unaccounted for and that runners had been sent to capture the “killer-arsonist vicious criminal” (殺人放火兇犯), but that it was difficult to

## PDF第454页

determine whether Jia had killed his relatives for vengeance or robbery, or if he had set his house on fire in a fit of madness. His superiors instructed to capture Jia swiftly, to understand the circumstances of the multiple deaths, and to mete out an appropriate sentence before transferring the criminal to the prefecture for a review of the case.

A few days later, Jia was found haggard in a mountain ditch, chewing on pine needles and holding a spearhead (鎗頭). He was in a stupor (精神恍惚), “speaking in a tiny and weak voice, as if sick and mad” (語言細微 似病若瘋), his wits scattered (神氣靡定). What was important was not Jia’s mad appearance and deportment, which were described in a language that could have been medical and which a physician confirmed were not simulated, but whether he had been mad the night of the fire. Officials suspected that Jia had killed his wife and daughter, thrown them into the well, and then set the house on fire before escaping, a series of acts that appeared too deliberate to have been the result of madness (似非瘋病形景). The magistrate’s superiors thus ordered that Jia be strictly interrogated after he had been made to recover.

After Jia became better a few months later, the witnesses—the village chief, neighbors, Jia's relatives, and his wife's brother—were gathered for the interrogation. It was now obvious that the remains found in the house were those of Jia's elder daughter (since the family did not raise animals), and that the wounds on his wife's and other daughter's bodies had been inflicted by Jia's axe. Since Jia had already admitted in private to having axed his wife and first daughter and set fire to the house, the question was not whether he had committed the act, but whether he had been mad when he had. The magistrate's purpose in finding this out was not to make a difference between criminality and lack of responsibility, but to determine the penal denomination under which he would sentence Jia.

## PDF第455页

When the magistrate asked more about Jia's condition, all the witnesses told him that Jia had started suffering from madness in 1730, after his son had died of smallpox. As his brother explained, Chandou missed his son so much that “he caught a madness illness. I think it was [because] he was so upset $ ^{43} $ that it confused his heart” (得了一個瘋病 瘋想是氣迷了心). Jia had been suspicious of people since his youth and had become even more mistrustful after he had become mad, but his illness flared up only two or three times a year. Jia's fits forced his relatives to tie him up and to keep him confined in the house for a few days until he got better and could return to his normal tasks of tilling the soil and collecting firewood. Since his illness was not “lasting madness” (並不是常瘋), Jia's father and brother had not given him any medicine $ ^{44} $ and had not declared him to officials. $ ^{45} $ “His madness would flare up for two or three days and then he would be fine. He could then work and take care of the household as before. If we had reported him and he had to be kept under custody, there would have been no one to manage the household” (瘋發兩三日好了 依舊要做活治家 若是報官保管了就沒有人當家了).

Looking for motives, the magistrate asked everyone whether Jia and his wife had been in good terms (they had) and if Jia's madness had acted up that night. Those who had talked to Jia the day before the incident said he had looked normal (白日裡是好好的), and that the best way to know whether he had been crazy that night was to ask him directly. This

## PDF第456页

is what the magistrate did, but there was a problem: Jia was deaf. "Even when you speak loudly in front of him, he doesn't hear clearly," said his brother. Although he did not speak Chinese (不會說漢話), since he had studied for a few years in his youth he could write some characters: Jia's deposition would not come from his mouth but from his brush—a written deposition (寫供).

Jia recounted becoming mad and deaf after his son had died of smallpox.

Occasionally, his relatives would say he was crazy (他們又說我瘋了) and would tie him up.

The night of the incident, he was sleeping alone in his room when his wife suddenly came to wake him up. He was a little scared. He saw his wife light a fire, get dried fish from the wooden closet, and offer some to him, saying "You're hungry." He heated up the fish in the fire. After his wife went back to sleep in the other room, he heard what seemed to be the voices of many people outside. Frightened, he grabbed a torch and went to look, but found nobody and went back to sleep. Soon, his wife came again and poked him awake, making him panic even more (我更慌了起來).

When Chandou heard more sounds outside, he rose again and only returned after he had searched in vain through the entire courtyard. But just as he went through the door he bumped into his wife, who fell on him. Fearing that she was trying to tie him up, he became very angry (惱恨起來), pushed her aside, grabbed an axe from under the closet where she had fetched the fish, and hit her twice with it. She sat leaning against the closet, groaning in pain. Annoyed (煩躁) that his daughter was standing besides, sobbing, he axed her twice too. His other daughter hid under a pile of grass in another room. "Since I had killed my wife and daughter, I thought I would not get to live." He grabbed a torch, went to set his aunt's house

## PDF第457页

on fire $ ^{46} $ and came back to look for his other daughter, but he failed to find her so he set fire to the pile of grass. Finding it too painful to thrust a spearhead into his chest, he tried to flee, but the fire had become so large that he could only escape from the roof. He jumped into the street behind the house and fled to the mountains, where he ate pine needles and drank muddy water for days before he was caught.

The magistrate found Jia’s deposition difficult to believe:

The oral deposition you wrote [sic] does not look like that of someone who has suffered from madness illness. Clearly, you axed your wife and daughter to death on purpose (故意) for other reasons (為別的情事) [than those you mention] and set fire to the house, planning to pretend that the house had caught fire [accidentally] and that they burned to death. If this is indeed something you did because you became confused at heart out of anger (氣得心迷), how can you still recall so clearly [what happened] at the time?

看你寫的這些口供 不像患過瘋病的人 明是你與你女人另為別的情事故意把楊氏母女砍死了 放火燒房 希圖捏做失火燒死 若說你果然氣得心迷做的事 如何此時還能記憶得這樣清楚呢

Under torture because the magistrate suspected he was lying, Jia replied he had no other reason to kill. Had he wanted to kill his wife, he would have done so on the spot. After a long series of counter-interrogations, the magistrate determined that Jia had not suffered from madness on the night of the incident. His prior history of mad fits, his mad appearance when he was arrested, and his account of panic the night of the crime were not sufficient, since he had had enough awareness to be afraid of the pain when he tried to commit suicide, and he was lucid enough to avoid the fire and flee into hiding.

## PDF第458页

As for the above circumstances [recounted] in the written deposition, not only does [Jia] remember them clearly, but he also answered [all questions] without error. This cannot be compared with unawareness of people and things, and is [therefore] difficult to sentence as madness.

今寫供前情 不但記憶明白 抑且登答無訛 並非不醒人事者可比 難以瘋論

After determining that Jia had hit his wife in anger and intentionally set his house afire, the magistrate had to select the most fitting penal denomination. "Killing three junior relatives in the same family who are not [guilty of a crime that deserves] the death penalty" $ ^{47} $ (殺期服卑幼一家非死罪三人) did not apply, because neither Jia's wife nor his elder daughter had died from his hands. A more suitable law was: "Whenever one intentionally sets fire and burns the house of an official or a commoner, and thereby kills or injures someone, sentence like 'intentional killing'" 凡放火故燒官民房屋者 致殺人者 以故殺論. $ ^{48} $ Killing one's wife intentionally was punished by "strangulation after the Assizes."

As the Governor of Yunnan concluded in his report to the Ministry of Punishments, "since this criminal's illness was intermittent and he could still till and drill, it was not a case of insanity with complete unawareness of people and things" (因該犯之病時發時愈 尚能耕鑿 非顛狂不醒人事者). The higher authorities that reviewed the sentence concurred with the decision, including the exemption of Jia's relatives from the punishment they should have incurred for not declaring a mad relative. $ ^{49} $

## PDF第459页

Just as someone who had suffered from madness could be punished severely if his acts were considered intentional, the law also distinguished mad acts from acts committed by simpletons. Like Jia Chandou, Jia Yicai 叚一才, who also lived in the Yongzheng period (1723-1735), had suffered from fits of insanity, during which he would go crazy and run around wildly (病發起來就痴痴癲癲 胡走亂跑了).⁵⁰ When his fits stopped occurring for good, his father released him from confinement (從先原是鎖禁的 後因他不瘋了纔放開了), but by then Jia had become "stupid by nature" (賦性愍獸). He spent most of his time with the children of his small village (有些愍頭愍腦的 好與村裡娃娃們頑耍). An eight-year-old boy often called him a wangba 忘八 or an idiot (你是個獃子), and insulted him by saying that his five-year-old daughter was someone else's (你的女兒是別人養的).⁵¹ Having heard these words, Jia Yicai started suspecting that his wife had engaged in lecherous behavior (淫行). Many times, he cornered the boy and asked him to explain where he had heard this rumor, but the child would not respond.

One day, Jia lured the boy into a derelict temple outside the village and gave him a peach to entice him to talk, but in vain. Jia decided to scare him by taking off his clothes and tying him up, but the child would only cry. Jia took out a small knife and started to slash him, but the child started bleeding and screaming. Jia then panicked and decided to finish him off and pretend nothing had happened. He stabbed the child in the belly many times, disemboweled him, and returned home. Jia's father saw that something was wrong and made his son admit to his deed.

## PDF第460页

During trial, the possibility of madness was discussed, but as the magistrate

wondered, “How is this something that someone would do in a fit of madness?” (那裡是瘋

發的人做的呢) Jia’s actions were indeed far too organized to pass for mad: he had

purposely lured the boy to the old temple and talked to him for some time, had tied him up,

slashed him with a knife, and finally killed him in a panic. But panic was not madness: Jia

Yicai was sentenced to decapitation after the Assizes for “intentional killing.”

Agreeing that madness was out of the question, the Ministry argued that Jia's acts were out of proportion with what had triggered them. Why torture and disembowel a child over a few harmless insults? Had the investigators failed to discover hidden motives, perhaps a grudge between the two families? The child was naked when he died: had rape been attempted? A new trial cleared the doubts concerning rape and premeditation and confirmed that the intention to kill had indeed arisen “on the spot” (臨時起意; 頓起殺心), the defining feature of “intentional killing” in the code. Jia's father clarified that “after Yicai's madness healed, he became stupid by nature: his thoughts and actions differ from common expectations” (因一才曾患瘋癲病愈之後 性本憨獃 其心思舉動實有出於尋常意計之外者), but madness was not intentions gone awry—a mad act contained no intention.

Our third example shows an act that did legally qualify as mad. Deng Tingzhu 鄧廷柱 was counting about one thousand copper coins he and his brother Tingmei 廷梅 had earned selling firewood. $ ^{52} $ Sometime in 1735, Tingmei suddenly became severely crazy (風狂大發), ran to his brother, grabbed 300 coins and started spilling them around. Tingzhu ran

## PDF第461页

after him to try to stop him, but Tingmei picked up a heavy branch that was lying on the ground and hit Tingzhu on the head. Tingzhu fell and died soon after.

Killing a senior relative was legally graver than killing an unrelated person. Simply hitting one’s elder brother without inflicting injury deserved 2 years of penal servitude and 90 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{53} $ The law on “killing because of madness,” however, purported to apply to all killings regardless of the identity of the victim. $ ^{54} $ If Tingmei was found to have been aware of what he was doing when he had killed his brother, he would be sentenced to immediate decapitation. If unaware (and thus confirmed as mad), he would be handed back to his family for care and surveillance. And because killer and victim were relatives, even a monetary compensation would not be necessary. Killing a senior relative because of madness was probably the only legal situation in which a crime against an older family member was punished less severely than the same crime committed against an unrelated person. $ ^{55} $ Deng was found mad in a first trial and sentenced accordingly, but the Ministry of Punishments wanted to make sure. The staggering gap in punishment between complete exemption and immediate decapitation made officials study Deng’s case thoroughly in a new trial.

The problem was to determine whether Deng had indeed been unaware of having hit his brother. Taking complete lack of consciousness as a standard rendered suspicious any act that seemed even slightly purposeful. Deng had not just grabbed any object: he had stolen money. What had made him do so this time and not in his previous fits? Possible inconsistencies in the depositions from the first trial were strictly examined: how could Tingmei have hit his brother behind the head if Tingzhu was running toward him to stop him.

## PDF第462页

from spilling the coins? Tingmei in his first deposition said he had seen the money and grabbed it, and had seen his brother come to him: "this situation does not look like a case of suffering from madness" (看這情形就非病狂情狀). "If you could recognize nothing, why is it that you saw money and stole it?" Deng replied that this is what he had heard from his mother afterwards, but that he himself had no recollection of this fact.

Witnesses were interrogated repeatedly. Deng's mother said something important: "when we have money, we use it together, as one family. Why would one need to steal it?" The very fact that Tingmei had killed his caretaker brother for no good reason was a sign of madness. A physician who had treated Deng twice was also summoned to testify:

[Sometime] in the 8 $ ^{th} $ month of the 11 $ ^{th} $ year [of Yongzheng; i.e. 1733], Deng Tingzhu came to my house and said his younger brother Tingmei had contracted insanity (fengkuang) illness. $ ^{56} $ He invited me to come along to give [Tingmei] treatment. At the time, I diagnosed that his [eart's] openings were blocked by Wind mucus. I burned moxa on the proper loci and he was cured.

〔雍正〕拾壹年捌月內鄧廷柱到小的家稱說胞弟廷梅身染風狂的病 延小的前去調治 那時診得他係風痰閉竅 小的艾火照穴灸愈了

As we have seen, one of the most important causes of insanity in medical doctrine was mucus, a thick and viscous fluid that caused fits of madness by congealing into the “Heart’s openings.” $ ^{57} $ It seems that Deng Tingmei himself thought of his ailment in the same terms.

My insanity illness is just mucus-confusing—the-openings—of—the-heart. When the mucus withdraws, I get better.... I don't know how, I got confused by mucus,

## PDF第463页

grabbed the money, and beat my brother to death. I was genuinely unaware of people and things.... Only when the mucus dispersed did I become lucid and start to become a little aware of people and things.... When I cried for my brother, it was because the mucus had dispersed.

小的風顛病症 只是痰迷心竅 若痰退下去 就會好了···不知怎樣痰迷就搶錢打死哥子 小的實不省人事···若痰散了纔明白 略省人事···哭哥子時 是痰散了

Although Qing law did not rely on physicians to determine whether a “criminal” had been mad at the moment of committing a homicide, Deng Tingmei’s medically plausible explanation of his own madness may have been crucial to the final verdict. $ ^{58} $

After analyzing the depositions, the official in charge concluded that Deng had indeed been crazy when he had killed his brother, but instead of directly applying the law on "[people with] madness illness killing another," the magistrate found an ingenious way of justifying a disregard for the close family relation between Tingmei and his victim. One law in the "Denominations and general principles" (名例律) section of the code concerned crimes in which the offender did not know that the victim was a relative. $ ^{59} $

In a situation when the punishment should be heavier, $ ^{60} $ but [one], at the moment of committing a crime, did not recognize [that the victim was a relative], sentence according to [the law concerning the same crime committed against] an unrelated person (as for an uncle and a nephew who grew up and lived in different places and never knew each other: the nephew hits and injures the uncle, but only at the time of

## PDF第464页

the official investigation does he learn that this was his uncle; sentence [such a case] according to the laws on affrays [between] unrelated persons....).

其本應罪重 而犯時不知者 依凡人論〔謂如叔姪別處生長 素不相識 姪打叔傷 官司推問 始知是叔 止依凡人闘法……〕

This law was found to apply to the Deng brothers even if they had grown up together:

“[They] are not people who ‘never knew each other,’ but when Tingmei became unconscious because of madness, it was indeed the case that he did not recognize who Tingzhu was. [This situation] is no different from [the one described as] ‘not recognizing’ [that the victim was a relative] at the moment of committing the crime’ (非素不相識之人 但廷梅風病昏迷時實已不識廷柱為何人 正與犯時不知無異). Like someone who had killed an unrelated person, Deng Tingmei was thus sentenced according to the law on ‘killing because of madness,” and would not have to pay 12.42 ounces of silver because he lived with his victim’s widow: He was entrusted to the care of his uncle, who was instructed to confine him strictly every time he suffered from bursts of madness.

The Ministry of Punishments agreed with the finding of madness, but rejected the sentence that the governor of Guangdong had proposed. $ ^{61} $ The final sentence for Deng Tingmei was “decapitation after the Assizes,” slightly lower than the “immediate decapitation” that was imposed to people who killed their older brother in a regular affray. Deng was eventually saved by the general amnesty that followed the accession of the Qianlong emperor in 1736 and sent home to his county’s prison for confinement.

Our three cases have shown that “idiots” did not fall under the laws on “madness illness” and that people who were recognized as suffering from insanity could still be

## PDF第465页

sentenced severely if their homicidal acts were judged to have been intentional. The following observation by Nakamura Shigeo (1973d: 213n9) is therefore misleading:

When [legal documents] speak of fengbing [“madness illness”], they of course refer to handicaps to mental functions (精神機能の障害) of various levels of seriousness: naturally, there must have been cases (事例) involving people who suffered from light fengbing (瘋病輕度), but in specific cases (事案) we can scarcely discern these degrees of seriousness; we know of no case (例) in which consideration of this point [i.e.,

But there could be no degrees of “madness illness,” because mad acts were characterized by absolute lack of awareness and intention—“light fenghing” in Qing law would have been a misnomer. One was considered either completely mad, or not at all. Some cases were debatable (and debated), but the final decision accepted no in-between.

The nature of sentencing in Qing trials probably played a role in the preservation of this unqualified view of madness. In a field in which a single “penal denomination” had to be chosen, there was no intermediary between “killing because of madness” and other forms of killing, no categories like “diminished responsibility,” “partial insanity,” and “guilty but insane” to moderate the absoluteness of madness, and no “extenuating circumstances” to modulate punishment once a penal denomination had been chosen.

This view of madness differs radically from the one outlined in the McNaughtan Rules, a test of insanity that is still used today in many American states. $ ^{62} $ According to these Rules, even a clearly intentional act can fail to qualify as a crime if its perpetrator did not know that this act was morally wrong and if this failure to tell right from wrong came from

## PDF第466页

illness—instead of ignorance or drunkenness, for instance. In 1843, the defense insisted that McNaughtan’s attack on a Tory Prime Minister (McNaughtan had killed his secretary instead), though clearly intentional and premeditated, would have been morally “right” if his delusion of being persecuted by the Tories had been real. The jury agreed and found McNaughtan “not guilty on the grounds of insanity.” $ ^{63} $ Qing law, by contrast, said nothing about the ability to distinguish right from wrong, and focused solely on the killer’s awareness of his own acts. Since McNaughtan was lucid all along, in Qing times his killing of the Prime Minister’s secretary would have been interpreted as a case of “Mistaken killing,” not “killing because of madness.” Committed while trying to “kill by prior design,” McNaughtan’s act would have been punished by “decapitation after the Assizes,” where it would have been classified as “effective.” $ ^{64} $ And since the assassination attempt was directed against a high official, a Chinese McNaughtan would have been sentenced to death by dismemberment.

## “No different from madness”: fits of madness caused by Cold Injury (傷寒發狂)

According to most medical treatises, the symptoms of “fits of madness [caused by] Cold injury” (傷寒發狂) resembled those of regular madness, but they were often discussed separately because they appeared under special circumstances: high fevers. $ ^{65} $ After kuang had become increasingly attributed to Fire in medical circles, the boundaries between insanity and fits of madness caused by fever had become more blurred, but the distinction between outwardly contracted and inwardly developed madness had been preserved. Seventeenth-century Huizhou doctor Luo Mei 羅美, for example, claimed that madness, “like becoming

## PDF第467页

crazy because of extreme Heat occasioned by Cold injury, belongs to the inner dominance of Fire and mucus” (與傷寒熱極發狂 同屬痰火內盛).⁶⁶ In a book on Cold Injury, Li Zhongzi 李中梓 (1588-1655) attributed both madness 狂 and “fits of madness” 發狂 to Heat, and advocated treating them both with strong purgatives.⁶⁷ Indeed many physicians described in the same way the process by which the Spirit left its residence (the heart) in cases of madness and in cases of becoming crazy because of Cold injury.⁶⁸

Febrile agitation could easily lead to inadvertent killings. The legal treatment of such cases illustrates my point that Qing law was more interested in mad acts themselves than in their origin. The earliest case of killing because of a febrile illness I have found dates to 1765 (in a report dated 28 Aug. 1765 [QL30.7.13]), but many must have occurred before that.

A later case produced more legal debate. $ ^{69} $ Early in October 1786 (QL51.8.12) in Anhui, hired laborer Shao Yuan contracted a Cold-injury disorder (伤寒病證). When feverish, he would become confused (昏迷) and unaware of people and things (不省人事). $ ^{70} $ He would dance and shout exactly as if he were insane (酷類瘋顛), and become clear-minded (清醒) again once the fever receded. Six days into his illness, when the people who had hired him thought he was sleeping, a sudden fit of Heat pushed him to get up suddenly (熱極發狂猝起). He grabbed a stick with a metal point (used for defense against animals or bandits) and started dancing. He killed a child who playfully mimicked him.

## PDF第468页

The magistrate who first judged the case thought it inappropriate to sentence Shao Yuan according to the existing law on madness, "because this criminal was only momentarily confused because of extreme Heat and was not in fact suffering from madness." On the other hand, sentencing to death as in a case of "killing in an affray" would feel like an unbalanced case of "light circumstances and [overly] severe law" (情輕法重), and so he judged by analogy (比照) to the statute on "killing in an affray." In judgments by analogy, the sentence was diminished by one degree of severity, which meant that Shao would be punished by exile at 3,000 li preceded by a beating. Twenty ounces of silver in burial fees would also be demanded and paid to the victim's family. After the usual prefectural and provincial reviews, the case was sent to the Ministry of Punishments for final approval.

Ministry officials disagreed with the judgment of the field officials. Observing that Shao Yuan had been “confused and unaware” at the moment of committing his crime, they argued that his case was “no different” from cases of “killing because of madness” (與因瘋殺人者無異). The inappropriate sentence the Governor of Anhui had approved was changed to permanent confinement and the payment of burial fees, as for “killing because of madness.” $ ^{71} $ No sub-statute confirmed that similar cases should be judged in this fashion in the future, but they still were. Five similar cases that took place in 1815 and 1816 were also sentenced according to the law on “killing because of madness,” and the Ministry approved of the verdict every time. $ ^{72} $ Various compilations of “completed cases” (成案) also

## PDF第469页

confirmed that killings caused by madness induced by another illness were consistently judged by analogy to the law on “killing because of madness.” $ ^{73} $

The problem of possession: an aside

Unlike Qing medicine, Qing law made no room for the widespread belief that mad acts could be caused by spirit possession. The fact that the Qing officially treated madness as an illness is important, but should not be considered as militant refusal of religious explanations. This omission indeed probably had little to do with the fact that “the officials who wrote the codes and casebooks were a self-consciously Confucian elite,” as Martha Li Chiu (1981: 76) once maintained. $ ^{74} $ Chiu’s comments rested on a note by legal scholar Xue Yunsheng 薛允升 (1820-1901), who stated that “criminal statutes have no clause concerning possession.” $ ^{75} $ But when one pays attention to the context (the “text above and below” 上下文, as the Chinese say), it becomes apparent that Xue’s remark was part of an astute reasoning on the citing of legal articles across sets of regulations:

[Instances of] ‘killing because of madness’ occur occasionally. The underlying circumstances of some cases, however, are [so] strange and unusual [that they] cannot appear in official documents; there is no choice but to close [these cases] as [cases of] madness. I have read in novels cases that made absolutely no sense and were determined to be [cases of] spectral affliction. $ ^{76} $ Criminal statutes have no clause concerning possession, [and possession] can [therefore] not be adduced or

## PDF第470页

mentioned [as a relevant circumstance]. In the Administrative Regulations of the Ministry of War, however, there is a passage [saying that] “if a soldier commits suicide after having encountered a specter, grant an indemnity according to the rule on ‘dying because of illness.’” $ ^{77} $ Though it is true that criminal statutes do not contain [any such reference to possession], [in] other [sets of] regulations it is routinely permitted to cite [possession] as evidence, just as it is allowed to request that [the cases of] women who commit suicide because they have been made to suffer shame $ ^{78} $ be processed according to the regulations of [the Ministry of] Rites. These are all regulations established by the court. How is it that officials in charge of trials are so inclined to being contrived, $ ^{79} $ yet do not dare to cite by analogy?

瘋病殺人 事或間有 然有案本奇異 不能形諸公牘 不得不以瘋病完結者嘗閱小說內載有無情無理之案 而斷以為祟 刑律無遇祟之條 不能聲說然兵部則例內有兵丁遇祟自盡 照病故例一體償卹之語 則刑律雖無 他例自可援以為證 即如婦女羞忍自盡 准用禮例請旌 均為 朝廷定例 司讞者何甘心扭捏 而不敢比引耶

Xue was not arguing that possession was an unacceptable explanation of insanity. He maintained on the contrary that in rare and extraordinary cases, certain acts might have been best attributed to possession. $ ^{80} $ Cases that had to be settled by default as cases of madness might have been better judged by analogy to the article of the Administrative Regulations of the Ministry of War in which possession was seen as a suitable explanation for suicide. $ ^{81} $

## PDF第471页

### 11.4 Preventive confinement: its emergence, transformations, and application

Projection: the confinement of “people with madness illness”

One of the most spectacular effects of the 1669 law on “killing because of madness” was to create an official Qing conception of insanity as an illness. In 1689, a new rule ordered families to keep precautionary watch over “people with madness illness” (瘋病之人). $ ^{82} $ If “negligence or laxity” (疏縱) allowed a mad man or woman to kill someone, the careless relatives would be punished with 80 strokes of the heavy stick according to the statue “what ought not to be done, heavy case” (不應重). $ ^{83} $ Counting on the self-evidence of insanity for those who saw it, the law did not define “madness illness.”

Why target all mad people with a preventive law even when they had committed no offense? As I noted above, medical texts had long noticed that madness could lead to homicidal violence, but doctors had not for that reason considered all mad people as potential killers. The insane patients they faced rarely attacked them, and these patient's relatives did not seem to think insanity was necessarily dangerous. $ ^{84} $ The only cases of madness that came to legal attention, however, were those in which mad people committed crimes, especially killings. Based on the outcome of a few acts, the view of madness as violent was projected onto all “mad people,” including those who had committed no crime. $ ^{85} $

## PDF第472页

The dangers of madness became legally conspicuous only after “killing because of madness” had started to be sentenced like “accidental killing.” $ ^{86} $ Officials in the Ministry of Punishments were probably trying to emphasize that insane men and women were different from other people who “killed by accident.” The fact that one occasionally dropped a brick on someone’s head while working on a platform did not make bricklayers a dangerous group. The murderous gesture of the mad killer, however, was officially understood as being the direct result of his madness. Contrary to other accidents, “killing because of madness” could therefore be prevented by strict confinement measures.

This new emphasis on the danger of “people with madness illness” is a good example of the “autonomous epistemic power” (Teubner 1989) of legal practice. Through its operations (handling crimes committed by mad people) and categories (“killing because of madness”), it generated referents outside of legal institutions, which it then treated as real. This is how, after 1689, all “people with madness illness” had to be confined preventively.

The development of preventive confinement measures

Qing confinement rules would be difficult to understand in isolation, for instance as part of an independent “policy on the insane.” The increasingly restrictive measures that were imposed against non-criminal madmen in the eighteenth century sprang from the

## PDF第473页

conduct of judicial activities, namely from the way in which multiple killings induced by madness were sentenced.

In 1731 in Sichuan, a man called Wei Juzhen 韋巨珍 killed a neighbor's wife and three younger members of her family. $ ^{87} $ Although the killing of three or more people of the same family was usually sentenced with death by dismemberment, $ ^{88} $ because Wei was recognized to have been mad when he committed his act, he was only asked to pay 12.42 ounces of silver per victim to the neighbor whose family he had decimated. This sentence was pronounced in the name of the 1669 law on “killing because of madness.” The contrast between the lightness of Wei’s punishment and the gravity of his act made Governor-general Huang Tinggui 黄廷桂 (1691-1759) think of a way to prevent similar crimes in the future. $ ^{89} $ In a memorial to the Yongzheng emperor, Huang exposed the inadequacy of the current preventive rule: $ ^{90} $

Your servant thinks that the exemption from the death penalty of [people with] madness illness originates in the ill person’s lack of awareness (無知), hence the sub-statute that mandates their confinement so as to prevent unexpected rash actions. $ ^{91} $ However, if the close relatives of ill people cannot bear to subdue and confine them; if neighbors and li [heads] $ ^{92} $, concerned to protect good relations and face, observe but do not speak; and if most local officials pay no attention [to these matters] because they are unrelated to their evaluation, so that harm is inflicted on innocent

## PDF第474页

people to no end: this truly does not represent my Emperor's highest purpose of cherishing and nurturing the people. I prostrate myself and plead that [His Majesty] order the Ministry [of Punishments] to establish that when people with madness illness kill someone, besides punishing their direct relatives, the neighbors, and the li head according to the [existent] sub-statute, administrative sanctions also be strictly established against local officials who ordinarily do not carry out inspection of confinement [matters].

臣思瘋病免抵本係原病者之無知而例應禁錮 所以防意外之妄行 其如病者之親黨骨肉不忍鎮禁而鄰里俱瞻顧情面 觀望不言 地方官又因無關考成率多膜視 以致害及無辜 將無底止 甚非我 皇上愛育生民之至意 伏乞 敕部定義凡瘋病之人有殺人者 其本家親族鄰里照例懲治外 並請將平日不行稽查鎖禁之地方官嚴定處分

<div style="text-align: center;">In early 1732, the emperor authorized a new rule that ordered families to declare their mad members to the government and augmented sanctions on those who did not comply. $ ^{93} $</div>


Vivien Ng saw this rule as marking the transformation of madness from an illness to a form of deviance, a point that seems difficult to substantiate since precautionary measures had been in place since 1689. Besides, the 1732 rule barely modified the conditions under which mad people were to be detained. They were still to be kept at home, though the new rule specified that they were to be “confined” or “chained up” (鎖錮) instead of “preventively guarded” (防守). $ ^{94} $ Neither did the emperor who authorized the new rule seem to see madmen as inherently dangerous. In a message he wrote in 1730 to Guangxi Governor Jinhong 金鉄, he opined that it was unnecessary to impose the death penalty on a

## PDF第475页

madman who had called himself the True Brilliant Son of Heaven (真明天子). $ ^{95} $ The Governor had proposed to execute him “to warn all madmen in the regions” (以儆地方所有瘋人), but the emperor considered there was no need for a death sentence if this were indeed a case of madness illness (若實系瘋病 何必至於處死).

The 1732 law was simply a stricter version of the 1689 rule. The Yongzheng emperor probably approved of Huang Tinggui's proposal because it would make an existing rule more effective rather than as a result of a new Qing perception of insane people as dangerous or as a solution to instability in Sichuan, as Ng has suggested. It imposed heavier punishments on relatives, neighbors, and village chiefs when a madman they had failed to declare committed a crime, as well as new administrative sanctions against officials who failed to take proper measures when someone's madness was declared. $ ^{96} $ Deterrence would breed compliance, even at the cost of some legal contradictions. $ ^{97} $ But despite these novelties, the punishment for “killing because of madness” remained unmodified.

Preventive confinement became more severe in the Yongzheng era because high officials and the emperor agreed that killings committed in a state of madness should continue to be sentenced leniently. Far from making madness appear more deviant, Huang Tinggui agreed that mad killers should be exempted from death because they “had no

## PDF第476页

cognizance” (無知) of their own actions. A mad killer would still be returned home after making a compensatory payment to his victim's family. Physical punishments for “killing because of madness” now targeted everyone except the insane themselves. The reason for punishing mad people lightly and for strictly confining them was the same, since their illness made them both excusable and dangerous. They were not newly deviant and no longer sick: they were hazardous because of the illness that exculpated them.

This official fear of the homicidal potential of mad people also toughened the way mad killers were handled after having killed someone. In 1740 the Ministry of Punishments decided that someone who had committed “killing because of madness” would be “handed to their relatives for confinement” (交犯屬鎖錮) instead of being released. $ ^{98} $ In 1753 the Ministry decided that culprits would instead be kept in the county prison, and would be handed to their relatives only one year after having recovered. $ ^{99} $ In 1762, permanent imprisonment (永遠鎖錮 or 永遠監禁) became the mandated sentence for “killing because of madness.” As the Ministry observed, “madness illness alternately flares up and heals; it is difficult to guarantee that it will not flare up again [even] after more than one year of imprisonment” (瘋病原係時發時愈 霸禁逾年 難保其不復再發). $ ^{100} $ Liberation would not be authorized even if the killer had recovered. $ ^{101} $

In 1766, a case of killing three people of the same family led to more debate about the proper way to sentence “people with madness illness.” As law mandated, the mad killer was allowed to redeem his act with money. Again, the officials involved highlighted the stark contrast between the “circumstances” of the crime (qing 情, including the fact that an entire

## PDF第477页

family had been eradicated) and the punishment specified by law (fa 法). That year, Sichuan provincial judge Shi Lijia 石禮嘉 memorialized to request that cases of killing three or more people or three people of the same family because of madness be sentenced according to the relevant statutes on multiple killing. The Ministry rejected his proposal $ ^{102} $ but heeded it in another way by increasing the severity of precautionary actions against insane people.

Like the 1732 sub-statute, the 1766 rule tried to enhance measures that had failed to prevent mad people from committing crimes for which they could not be physically punished. The 1732 rule had imposed stricter punishments on those who could prevent the crimes—relatives, village leaders, and officials. The 1766 law tried to achieve the same effect by imposing stricter confinement conditions. Officials who received a declaration now had to make sure that the family had a secure room (嚴密房屋) wherein to confine a mad member, and officials were ordered to provide chains and locks to families who had the room but not the gear. $ ^{103} $ The main novelty was that households without a secure room would have to entrust their mad relatives to the county jail, where they would be “strictly chained up and confined” (嚴加鎖錮監禁) until “several years” (數年) after they had recovered. Women were allowed to stay at home. As far as I know, this was the only Qing case in which imprisonment was imposed when no criminal act had been committed.

The increasing rigor by which non-criminal mad people were confined during the eighteenth century did not originate in Qing or Manchu attempts to enhance social control. $ ^{104} $ There is a more specific explanation. Some officials wanted to confine mad

## PDF第478页

people preventively because they had committed killings for which they could not be sentenced to death. Simply put, prevention became stricter because even the gravest cases of multiple killing could be redeemed with money when they were committed “because of madness.” Preventive confinement was not a response to the perennial threat of mad people, the result of a shift in official perceptions of insanity, but a decision that made sense within the judicial context from which it emerged. The preventive policies remained in place even after their original raison d’être (the light sentencing of mad killers) had disappeared. $ ^{105} $ Xue Yunsheng argued in the late nineteenth century that penalties against mad people’s relatives had become excessive after mad killers had started to be sentenced to death, and could even incur “actual death sentences” (實抵). $ ^{106} $ In 1908, legal reformers who adopted many of Xue’s proposals decided not to include these measures of preventive confinement in the new Currently Operative Criminal Code of the Great Qing (Dai Qing xianxing xingli 大清現行刑律). $ ^{107} $

## Were mad people confined?

The fact that the policy of registration and confinement was made stricter in 1732 and 1766 is a sure sign that high officials found it ineffective. Every killing committed

## PDF第479页

“because of madness” indeed testified that preventive measures had failed. Vivien Ng (1990: 75-78) attributed their failure to the general weakness of the baojia system as a mechanism of local control, to the general resistance of families to letting the government deal with their affairs, and to the inertia of local officials in implementing the program. Most of her argumentation, however, is circumstantial, her main proof for non-compliance being the fact that most of the crimes committed “because of madness” were committed by non-declared madmen. Although I support Ng’s general conclusion that the rule was not widely respected, I would like to present different evidence on why most mad people were not declared, and on how their families handled them instead.

The “success” or “failure” of preventive measures against mad people is difficult to evaluate. The fact that homicides happened does not imply that mad people were never declared. Many in fact were, and some were even confined in local prisons as mandated. We know of people who applied for the liberation of insane relatives, $ ^{108} $ of “mad” people who stirred trouble after they had been released (they had therefore been confined), $ ^{109} $ and of high officials who complained that registered madmen were often released as soon as they were imprisoned (another sign that they had been declared). $ ^{110} $

There is unfortunately no way to determine how many people were officially registered as mad and what proportion of people known locally as “madmen” they represented. Since most of those who committed homicides “because of madness” had not been declared, and since the vast majority of Qing people whom their relatives and neighbors called “mad” probably did not commit crimes, it seems correct to assume that

## PDF第480页

those who were kept at home without being declared far outnumbered those who were

locked in official chains. So why were these “mad people” not declared?

It appears that most people in the localities knew about the duty to declare made relatives. The rare exceptions were noted: Deng Tingmei's 鄧廷梅 uncle called himself "a foolish commoner" to explain why he did not know that people suffering from "madness" (風顛) had to be declared. $ ^{111} $ An old woman who lived more than 300 li from the nearest county seat made a similar claim in 1816: "I ('this little woman') did not know the established rule, [so] I did not declare [my son] to officials" (小婦人不知定例 没有報官). $ ^{112} $ But these cases were rare, and I found no case in which local headmen and leaders (who would be punished if a non-declared madman committed a crime) claimed similar ignorance.

Failure to declare probably did not stem from people’s unwillingness to chain their mad relatives. It seems in fact that confinement at home was the most common way to handle insane people during the Qing dynasty. $ ^{113} $ A nineteenth-century household head was confined in a room, “hands and feet shackled, and chained to a large grindstone by several lengths of steel chain” (鏌靠手足外有鐵索數根 鋼鎖於大石磨盤上) $ ^{114} $ A woman who displayed violently mad behavior was tied up in a room. $ ^{115} $ Jia Chandou 段昆斗 (see 11.3 above) was also chained for the duration of his fits. As Michael MacDonald (1981: 142) put it, “The manacled lunatic was not a sign of the cruelty and stupidity of ordinary villagers; he was an emblem of their fear.” And helplessness, I would add.

## PDF第481页

Many who were not chained were nevertheless kept from leaving the house. In 1785, Liu Lin's 劉林 elder brother confined him to an empty room (關鎖空屋) after a relapse of his madness. $ ^{116} $ Ma Ajin 馬阿金 (1841) and Gu San 顧三 (1816) were treated similarly. Wen Tingfa 文挺法 (1841) killed one of those who were trying to lock him up in a room (關閉房內). In 1815, He Cheng'ao stabbed someone to death after barging out of the room in which he had been detained for more than a year. $ ^{117} $ Even non-violent madmen could be confined for long periods without being declared. For years, Bai Shengqi 白生奇 was "locked up at home" (鎖禁在家) by his father. When his father died, his mother relaxed the conditions of his detention (關鎖稍懈) but did not free him. $ ^{118} $

We should not confuse non-declaration with secrecy. $ ^{119} $ Madness was probably a problem in the treatment of which strangers were not welcome to meddle, but it could hardly be kept secret. People who suddenly turned mad attracted the most attention, as when the friends and relatives of a newly crazy man “filled the hall” (親朋滿座) and asked a visiting physician what could be done. $ ^{120} $ Since wacky speech, wild cursing, and violent fits could hardly go unnoticed in small communities, neighbors and local leaders usually knew when someone suffered from madness. Bai Shengqi was known as “madman Bai” 白瘋子 in surrounding villages even if he had been confined at home for five or six years. $ ^{121} $

## PDF第482页

When local leaders’ lack of knowledge was noted, the circumstances were always peculiar. In 1816, the head of community compact (鄉約) from Ningqiang department in Shaanxi explained that he had failed to note that a man from his district was suffering from madness because that man lived more than 50 li away. In 1891, another local leader from Shenyang 瀋陽 in the northeast claimed he had not known of Guo Chunyang’s 郭蓁洋 intermittent madness because the Guo brothers were “guest people” (客民) from Zhili. $ ^{122} $

So if fits were so obvious, if people were aware of their duty to report mad relatives, and if local leaders usually knew insane people in their immediate area, why were “people with madness illness” so rarely declared? The social content of this disobedience must have varied from concrete case to concrete case, but several distinct explanations recur from the hundreds of memorials I have consulted.

Crucially, people's perception of madness differed in several ways from the official view of it as dangerous and unpredictable. By ordering the declaration and confinement of "people with madness illness," the government neglected a socially relevant distinction: that between intermittent and permanent insanity. Simply put, most "mad people" were healthy most of the time. As Jia Chanxing 叚昂星 explained in 1736: $ ^{123} $

Although my brother [Chandou 昆斗] has had madness illness for a few years, [it] is intermittent. It is not lasting madness with unawareness of people and things. His madness flares up for two or three days and he is fine. As before, he must [then] work and manage the household. If he had been declared and confined, there would have been no one to take care of the household, and so we never declared him.

## PDF第483页

小的兄弟雖然有了瘋病幾年 乃是時發時好的 並不是常瘋不醒人事的 瘋發兩三日好了 依舊要做活治家 若是報官保管了就沒有人當家了 故此不曾報官

Even if Jia's case took place before the possibility of long-term confinement in the county jail, his relatives still saw officially supervised custody as affecting their normal activities.

The law saw the fits, the families the intervals between them. $ ^{124} $ What the government came to see as the most dangerous aspect of madness (its unpredictable intermittency) $ ^{125} $ was precisely what many people considered the best reason not to declare a mad relative. Mad people confined at home could be freed as soon as they got better. The comparative advantage of private confinement must have appeared even greater after 1766, the year from which declared madmen had to be confined for several years (without fits) before they could be liberated from local jails. If the rule was strictly enforced, one yearly fit would have meant imprisonment for life. $ ^{126} $

Those who recovered quickly after their first fit were also rarely declared. The fact that a madman had regained health after a medical encounter, for example, was often judged sufficient not to warrant declaration. A man named Dou Tong 竇桐 (1816) was treated and cured (醫治好了) by a doctor his father had invited. Wen Tingfa 文挺法 (1841) started

## PDF第484页

suffering from madness in early 1840 but became better after his son called in a physician.

Zeng Yitai 曾一汰 (1891) was also cured (治痊) by a physician his son had invited. $ ^{127} $

Pity may have been an additional factor in some families' decision not to declare a mad member. Consider the case of Han Chun 韓春, who became crazy and "often talked to himself" (時常自言自語) after his young daughter died of disease in the summer of 1814. $ ^{128} $ Since Han "made a living as a hired laborer" (傭工度日), his family (his old mother, his wife, and a brother) probably did not have the legally mandated "secure room" wherein to confine him. Declaration would have meant long-term confinement in the county jail for the man who had just lost his child. (Of course, losing a pair of hands was also a burden many families could not afford.)

Where no specific reason like intermittence or rapid recovery was mentioned, most people justified non-declaration by claiming that the madman “was not inciting trouble” (並不滋事 没有報官). I found this formula in more than 90 percent of the trial reports I consulted. $ ^{129} $ Many social facts hide behind this formulaic statement. Many madmen were obviously “inciting trouble.” Yang Wenhe 杨文河 injured his head and face jumping down a small cliff during a burst of insanity caused by a febrile disease, and Lian Ha Laosi 連哈老四 almost drowned after throwing himself into a nearby river. $ ^{130} $ Tian Zhi 田治 had injured himself with scissors (剪刀). Others engaged in threatening behavior. $ ^{131} $ Deng Tingmei 鄧廷

## PDF第485页

梅 chased dogs and chicken. An urban landlord made Jixiang's 吉祥 family move because he was afraid Jixiang would "stir up trouble" by using his knife on someone (動刀鬧事).

Perhaps owing to the obvious dangers of such acts, families often had to argue their decision with neighbors and community leaders who thought that having a madman around meant trouble. In 1890, the baolin 保鄰 of a Hunan village wanted to report Zhou Youbin 周有彬, who had just suffered a burst of insanity, but Zhou's son promised he would take the appropriate precautions. $ ^{132} $ Everybody eventually agreed that Zhou would be kept at home. In 1878, Mo Daoshou 莫導受 suddenly went mad. His old mother (76 sui) pleaded to the dibao 地保 and the linzu 鄰族 not to declare him and they agreed to let her take care of him in a room at home. Negotiations about whether to declare an insane person could even take place within families. When He Cheng'ao suddenly became crazy in 1814, a cousin who lived nearby wanted to report him to officials, but He Cheng'ao's uncle (胞叔, or father's younger brother) refused, arguing that Cheng'ao would cause no disturbance if he were kept in an empty room at home and a physician were invited to treat him. $ ^{133} $

The widespread decision not to declare suggests that most people either thought of madness as a manageable disorder or were compelled to treat it as such out of economic or practical necessity. Neighbors and local leaders who agreed on taking measures locally probably thought it was not worth risking neighborhood conflict to avert a danger they perceived as slight. I noted above that the law counted on people's fear of punishment to

## PDF第486页

incite them to declare insane relatives, but since no penalty was meted out to anyone as long as a mad person did not get in trouble, many families were willing to take the risk. $ ^{134} $

### 11.5 Conclusion: the emergence of cultural objects from institutional practices

As I hope to have demonstrated, the Qing legal discourse on madness can be understood only by paying attention to the legal concerns, categories, and procedures from which it emerged. The process may be summarized as follows. In 1667, an improvised judgment led to the creation of a rule granting exemption to those who had killed or injured someone else because of “madness illness.” Two years later, the new crime of “killing because of madness” was likened to “accidental killing”—the culprit would be released after paying 12.42 ounces of silver to their victim’s family. The parallel with “accidental killing” also meant that mad acts would be defined as entirely devoid of intention. The judicially relevant aspects of some mad acts (physical violence, lack of awareness of one’s acts, and the unpredictability of fits, all of which were observed during homicide trials) soon became the legally recognized features of madness and mad people in general. In 1689, a rule required that “people with madness illness” be confined preventively.

The early Qing laws on madness were not a “response to insanity.” They were a reaction, first, to a specific killing (in 1667), then to killings that were officially judged as having been committed during fits of insanity, and finally to the illness that was perceived to have led to these crimes. “People with madness illness” and “killing because of madness” were categories of legal practice. Pierre Bourdieu (1990: 141) once noted that institutions “produce what they recognize,” but the reverse seems to be true as well. Qing officials

## PDF第487页

responded to an outside object that legal practice had generated through its operations and categories. That the law produced objects relevant to legal practice is something of a truism. What is surprising is that no one had previously noticed these institutional and performative features of madness in Qing law.

The structuring role of institutions does not make individuals irrelevant. The changes I have outlined were neither automatic nor systematic. The 1667 rule that gave birth to “killing because of madness” as a penal denomination was proposed by a governor of Shandong. This initiative cannot be explained by the logic of early-Qing legal institutions. Officials usually strove to judge cases in ways approved by the institution they represented, but sometimes they pronounced special verdicts or decided to legislate where their predecessors had not. In Chapter 12, I will show that the radical increase in severity in the sentencing of killings committed because of madness after 1750 cannot be understood without taking individual initiatives into consideration, but that individual choices made sense within the broader concerns of the judicial institution.

## PDF第488页

## 12 

Punish, but how?

Mad acts and their punishments, 1750-1911

Chapter 11 explained why most killings committed because of insanity between 1669 and 1750 were sentenced under a law called “killing because of madness,” which requested that killers pay a monetary compensation to the family of their victims. In the second half of the eighteenth century, however, death sentences became common for the same acts, while ever stricter measures of preventive confinement were imposed on “people with madness illness.” By 1850, nine sub-statutes discussed mad homicides, and an increasing number of “circulars” (通行) and “memoranda” (説貼) from the Ministry of Punishments instructed legal officials on how to handle these crimes. Why were so many different punishments imposed for homicides that used to be sentenced in the same way until the 1750s?

The diverse sentences meted out for killings committed “because of madness” did not reflect Chinese ambiguities about the criminal responsibility of mad people, but changing assessments of the justness of sentencing in specific cases. Although the legal view of mad acts did not change, the insanity of killers was no longer the only circumstance deemed relevant to sentencing. The diversity of punishments resulted from unsystematic attempts by Qing officials to impose severe punishments for acts that led to grave outcomes, and to maintain equilibrium between the penalties required for proximate crimes. Some of these balancing efforts created new discrepancies that required further tweaking. $ ^{1} $

## PDF第489页

I first clarify the legislative and judicial importance of preserving the right balance between criminal acts and their punishments, and of grading punishments between crimes that the Code treated as proximate. I then explain how two types of killings committed because of madness—multiple homicides and the killing of senior relatives—came to be sentenced more severely in the second half of the eighteenth century. Part 3 describes the increasingly systematic manner in which madness was recognized in trials in the nineteenth century, as well as the roles that doctors played in this process. The last section discusses false accusations and suicides, two acts that Qing officials sometimes attributed to insanity.

This chapter will reinforce my general point that the legal discourse on and judicial treatment of mad acts and mad people emerged from individual initiatives that were both enabled and restrained by institutional rules and priorities.

### 12.1 The balance of punishments in Qing law
