The most important drive for the proliferation of madness laws in the second half of the Qing dynasty was the desire to mete out the right sentence for various killings committed “because of madness.” As is well known, one of the priorities of the Chinese judicial system in imperial times was to preserve the equilibrium between crime and punishment. $ ^{2} $ This concern for balance shaped the Code, the works of jurists, and judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ Balance had to be preserved on two fronts. First, between the circumstances (qing 情) of a criminal act and the penalty it incurred. Second, between the punishments imposed for related or proximate crimes like the “six killings” (六殺) which were graded in order of severity

## PDF第490页

according to the degree of criminal intention they involved (see 11.1). The first type of balance was enforced through judicial practice, the second one through legislation.

The seriousness of crimes was defined comparatively by the main statutes (律) of the Code, which were supposed to form a well-balanced network of crimes and punishments. The Code also provided a table of “mourning degrees” according to which the gravity of crimes against relatives was assessed. $ ^{4} $ As Table 12.1 (next page) illustrates, the order of severity of the six killings was preserved even after the degree of family relation between killer and victim had been factored in. In the table, the punishment increases downwards (toward more serious kinds of homicides) and toward the right (from junior relatives to senior relatives as victims). The middle column defines the standard punishment for the killing of a single unrelated person (凡人). The punishments meted out therefore reflected not only the nature of the act itself, but also the relative status of offender and victim. $ ^{5} $ In the terms of our table, the hierarchy of the punishments imposed for proximate crimes was thus observed both vertically and horizontally.

In trials, it was an official's duty to select the “penal denomination” (罪名) that most appropriately described an unlawful act. The sentencing of a crime was called kezui 科罪 or duanzui 斷罪, in which zui referred both to the crime as defined by the Code, and to the penalty that this crime legally demanded. $ ^{6} $ Many legal articles and countless manuals for

<table border=1 style='margin: auto; word-wrap: break-word;'><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>If the victim had scolded or hit the killer beforehand: no punishment.</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>100 strokes of the heavy stick, 1 year of penal servitude</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>depending on the circumstances of the case] Decapitation after the Assizes [effectively executed]</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Lingchi</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Lingchi</td></tr><tr><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Killing by design 謀殺 [treated like intentional killing]</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>No clause [60 strokes of the heavy stick, 1 year of penal servitude</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>No clause [100 strokes of the heavy stick, 2,000 li [100 strokes of the heavy stick, 2,000 li] at 2,000 li]</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Decapitation after the Assizes [effectively executed] Decapitation after the Assizes [effectively executed]</td><td style='text-align: center; word-wrap: break-word;'>Lingchi</td></tr></table>

magistrates instructed officials on how to judge cases. $ ^{8} $ The selection of the most fitting crime name was based on a careful assessment of the circumstances of the crime, including the degree of intention involved, the relative status of offender and victim, and anything else that could be relevant—conflict over property, particular cruelty, the number of times one had hit a victim, etc. For “circumstances” (情) to be balanced with “law” (法 or 律), “light” (輕) crimes had to be punished lightly, and “severe” (重) crimes sentenced severely. Officials who selected the wrong penal denomination or punished either too severely or too lightly could face administrative sanctions. $ ^{9} $

When an illegal act did not correspond with an existing penal denomination, an official could sentence by analogy (比照) to a crime that was judged similar in circumstances,

## PDF第493页

or to one that imposed a punishment that was deemed appropriate to the crime at hand. $ ^{10} $ Thanks to analogies, the judicial system could sentence a potentially unlimited number of criminal acts with a limited number of laws. But judicial analogies could only be deployed when no law was found relevant to the punishment of a specific act. This strictly regulated process differed from what doctors did when they treated an illness by analogy to their understanding of another disorder. In Part One, I argued that this kind of “scheme transfer” was the main way in which medical approaches to madness changed.

For almost a hundred years after 1669, homicides committed in a fit of madness were sentenced as “killing because of madness” regardless of the identity and number of the victims, because the law considered that the lack of intention of mad acts justified a light punishment. $ ^{11} $ Starting around the mid-eighteenth century, however, a few concerned officials argued that treating in bulk all killings committed because of madness created an imbalance between some crimes and their punishments. The acts of legislation we are about to examine did not stem from new reflections on madness, but from efforts to reestablish consistent and well-balanced penalties for homicides committed because of madness.

### 12.2 Increasing severity in sentencing crimes committed “because of madness”

Starting in the 1750s, the punishment for crimes committed because of madness started to drift toward more severity and away from the crime of “accidental killing,” to which “killing because of madness” had originally been likened. In Sex, Law, and Society in Late Imperial China (2000), Matthew Sommer argued that many laws concerning sexual

## PDF第494页

misbehavior were promulgated during the second half of the Qing in response to an increase in the number of unmarried men. It is more difficult to find social roots for transformations in the legal treatment of insane killers. I try to show instead that the dynamics of madness as a legal object was governed by the way the Qing legal system functioned.

In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed.

Legislative developments stemmed from the initiatives of the emperor, provincial judges and governors, and officials from the Ministry of Punishments. The more highly ranked the proponents of change, the more likely new measures were to be implemented. It was usually the Ministry of Punishments that decided whether to turn such proposals into laws. Eighteenth-century changes may thus have stemmed from the habitus of the Ministry of Punishments and the provincial bureaucracy under the Qianlong reign (1736-1796), a topic we know almost nothing about. $ ^{14} $

One specific motor of change was the Qianlong emperor's promotion of filial piety in his policies. The legal manifestation of this emphasis was to increase punishment for

## PDF第495页

crimes committed against senior family members. Once some changes had taken place, officials knew that the Ministry would likely approve of more proposals along the same lines, and change therefore followed from change. Since this policy theme did not automatically determine legislation, I explain each increase in punishment individually. As I observed above, this general increase in severity did not stem from a change in the legal conception of mad acts or mad people. For an act to qualify as “mad,” it still had to have been devoid of awareness, and the officials of the Ministry of Punishments still pressed field officials for clarifications when they detected traces of intentions in the actions of a potentially mad killer.

The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長),  $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother,” $ ^{17} $ but with an inserted “slip” 簽 declaring that the killer had been mad and that his sentence should therefore be commuted to “decapitation after the Assizes” (斬監侯). $ ^{18} $ The first kind of judgment reasserted the likening of mad homicides with accidental killings that had founded the 1669 law on “killing because of madness,” whereas the second way of sentencing treated madness only as a

## PDF第496页

secondary circumstance within the homicide of a close senior relative. These sentences were bolstered by two equally valid legal logics, namely the importance of intentions and the weight of seniority in intra-family crimes.

Finding these judgments “particularly inconsistent” (殊未劃一), Jiang proposed to calibrate punishment according to the degree of family relation between killer and victim. $ ^{19} $ The killing of distant relatives would be redeemed monetarily according to the statute on killing relatives by accident. $ ^{20} $ One who killed a third- or fourth-degree relative (大小功尊長) whose death usually mandated “immediate decapitation” should be punished by the lower “decapitation after the Assizes.” $ ^{21} $ Finally, the sentence of “immediate decapitation” for killing second-degree seniors should be preserved, but an inserted slip should declare the insanity of the killer. The final decision should be left to the emperor.

The Ministry of Punishments rejected Jiang’s proposal, but provincial officials soon had to face again the issues that Jiang had tried to disentangle. In 1756, the governor of Zhejiang confirmed a sentence of “decapitation after the Assizes” for a mad man who had killed his older brother. $ ^{22} $ This was one degree of lower than the penalty of “immediate decapitation” meted out to those who killed an older brother in a regular fight. But the Ministry disagreed with this reduction, suggesting that it had rejected Jiang Zhou’s earlier proposals because it had found them too lenient.

Unlike provincial judges like Jiang Zhou, provincial governors (ranked 2b) were considered equal in rank to the Ministry in judicial discussions. And since in 1756 the Zhejiang governor had raised a legitimate issue about mad killers, the Ministry did not

## PDF第497页

impose its judgment, but referred the case to the emperor, who entrusted the Nine Chief Ministers (九卿) with the final decision. $ ^{23} $ The congregated Ministers agreed with the governor to postpone death until “after the Assizes.” This way of sentencing madness-induced homicides of second-degree seniors remained in force until the end of the Qing.

What made Jiang Zhou, the Ministry of Punishments, and the Zhejiang governor reluctant to treat the killing of seniors as cases of “accidental killing” was probably the view that a killing “because of madness” had an extra component that other “accidental killings” did not possess: direct aggression. Madmen killed with violent gestures.

This feature of mad killings may have been what justified the Qianlong emperor's decision in 1761 to make the crime of "killing one's parents because of madness" punishable by dismemberment (凌遲), the harshest form of death penalty allowed by the Code. $ ^{24} $ His pronouncement was part of an edict that instructed provincial officials to execute all parricides swiftly—for fear they would take their own lives or die of starvation in prison—and to report the execution afterwards by palace memorial. $ ^{25} $

The law had long stressed the importance of hierarchical family relations, but in the 1760s the emperor pushed the same logic even further. By ordering dismemberment for

## PDF第498页

those who killed their parents during a fit of madness, he ignored the lower punishment that the 1756 law had granted to mad people who killed senior relatives of the second-degree. Qianlong’s edict, which was designed to redress a perceived disproportionate between a crime and its punishment, introduced new imbalances between the sentences imposed for proximate crimes. But the emperor was not seeking perfect consistency. In a new edict promulgated in 1763, he also increased the punishment for accidental parenticide from exile to immediate strangulation. The 1763 law created a new disparity in punishment for the proximate crimes of “killing one’s older brother accidentally” and “killing one’s father or mother accidentally,” which used to be separated by one degree of punishment (see Table 12.1). $ ^{26} $

As Qianlong's edicts from 1761 and 1763 show, the personality and priorities of the Qing sovereign could play a legislative role. Having been “schooled more thoroughly than any Qing emperor before him in Confucian values” (Kutcher 1999: 194), the Qianlong emperor sought to “preside over a grandiose revival of Confucianism” (Kutcher 1999: 191). His legislation by edicts shows how he intensified the focus that his two predecessors had put on filial piety in their policies. $ ^{27} $ The Kangxi emperor’s Sacred Edict 聖訓 had enjoined people to respect family values, but his initiatives had not been on the same scale as his grand-son Qianlong, and did not clearly manifest themselves in legislation. $ ^{28} $

Meanwhile, the insanity-induced killing of several people of the same family—which, like parricide, was considered as one of the “ten abominations” (十惡)—could still be

## PDF第499页

redeemed with money. $ ^{29} $ In 1766, the Ministry of Punishments rejected a proposal by Sichuan provincial judge Shi Lijia 石禮嘉 to sentence the mad killing of three or more people according to the root statutes on multiple killings. $ ^{30} $ To justify its refusal, the Ministry explained that “people who are insane and without awareness cannot be sentenced in bulk with ordinary people” (瘋狂無知之人與常人不能一體科斷). $ ^{31} $ This analysis contradicted the Ministry’s reluctance to diminish the sentence of “immediate decapitation” of a mad fratricide in 1756 (see p. 478), and re-asserted the importance of intentions in the sentencing of homicides, which the Qianlong emperor had ignored in his 1761 and 1763 edicts. This discrepancy illustrates how two legal logics—the importance of intention and of the degree of closeness between killer and victim—created two distinct ways of sentencing crimes that the Code considered equally grave when they were not committed because of madness.

In 1776, the Ministry of Punishments finally accepted a request to punish multiple homicides committed because of insanity more severely. The proponent, this time, was a former president of the Ministry who had recently risen to the presidency of the Censorate (see Appendix 8 under 1776). According to the new law, those who killed two or more people in a state of insanity would be punished by “strangulation after the Assizes.” $ ^{32} $ This new way of sentencing invalidated the old equation of “killing because of madness” with “accidental killing” in both punishment (from monetary compensation to a death sentence) and rationale (the statutes on multiple homicides were defined according to the number of the victims, not to the lack of intention of mad acts).

## PDF第500页

In 1784, the Shandong governor sentenced to permanent imprisonment a man who had slain a fourth-degree uncle during a fit of madness, but the Ministry of Punishments overturned his decision. $ ^{33} $ Explaining that the sub-statute on “killing because of madness” only applied to the killing of a single unrelated person—this was a new distinction—the Ministry decreed that all killings of senior relatives committed in a fit of insanity should now be sentenced according to the root statutes (本律) on the homicide of senior relatives. $ ^{34} $ From then on, the identity of the victim, not the presence of madness, would determine the choice of penal denomination. The Ministry’s decision became law in 1786.

From 1786 onwards, the madness-induced homicides of senior relatives were consistently judged in accordance with the relevant statutes, but the death sentence mandated by these laws was diminished from “immediate” to “after the Assizes” because the crime had “not been committed intentionally” (非有心干犯). $ ^{35} $ As this reduction of punishment indicates, the Ministry still recognized that killing relatives in a fit of madness was less serious than killing them while in a normal state. But insanity was now treated only as a secondary circumstance instead of a defining one.

By 1786, only the killing of a single non-relative was still sentenced under “killing because of madness,” the law that had likened mad homicides to accidental killings. In 1796, permanent imprisonment became the standard punishment for killing a junior relative in a fit of insanity. $ ^{36} $ This new sentence resulted from the fear that a mad killer would commit

## PDF第501页

violent gestures after being liberated or transported to a site of exile. This legislation thus gave more importance to the perceived danger of madness than to the act of killing itself.

In late 1802 or early 1803, the Ministry severed the last bond that remained between “accidental killing” and “killing because of madness.” In a circular that was sent to all provincial governments, the Ministry declared that even one who killed a single unrelated person in a burst of madness and recovered in time for trial would now be sentenced to death by “strangulation after the Assizes,” as in affray cases. $ ^{37} $ This guideline became a substitute in 1804.

The new law aimed to prevent criminals from trying to avoid the death penalty by faking madness after having killed someone. $ ^{38} $ But because officials were instructed to classify it as “suspended” at the Autumn Assizes, this death sentence was nominal, and since it could not be commuted to exile—lest the “mad criminal” stirred up trouble while in exile—the killer had to remain in prison for the rest of his life just like those who never recovered from madness. With permanent imprisonment as a safety net since 1762 for handling mad killers, this fear of fakers seems difficult to explain. $ ^{39} $ Perhaps the new law allowed to classify death sentences as “effective” at the Autumn Assizes when the circumstances were judged grave enough, or commoners were probably not supposed to know that the suspension of the death penalty was quasi automatic. $ ^{40} $

## PDF第502页

From the late eighteenth century to the end of the Qing, the nature of mad acts was no longer the primary circumstance (qing 情) guiding sentencing. The identity and number of the victims were what determined the proper penal denomination. Mad killers who were not executed were imprisoned permanently because their illness was considered dangerous.

Liberation measures were soon promulgated. In 1792, a man who had killed his wife in a fit of madness was released from his permanent confinement because he had recovered from madness and because “his parents were old and he was a single son” (親老丁單) (see Appendix 8, 1792 [2]). In 1797, the Ministry confirmed that single sons who had recovered from madness and whose parents were old could be allowed to “remain at home to take care” of their folks (留養).⁴¹ This measure of liberation became law in 1801.⁴²

In 1796, a Shandong governor asked whether mad killers should be released when imperial amnesties were promulgated. The Ministry decided that only killers who had recovered from their madness for 20 years and were older than 70 sui could benefit from imperial mercy. $ ^{43} $ In 1800, an edict of clemency (恩旨) asked that prisons be “cleared up” (清理監獄) and that all permanently imprisoned people be released in bulk, but the Ministry suggested that mad killers who had been imprisoned for less than 5 years should not fall under such edicts. This proposal became the official guideline (章程) by which permanently imprisoned mad killers would be treated until the end of the Qing.

Men who had killed a senior relative because of madness were excluded from these rules of liberation. $ ^{44} $ As we saw above, the killing of a senior relative was usually sentenced to

## PDF第503页

either decapitation of strangulation after the Assizes, where the death penalty was to be classified as “effective.” But before men so condemned could be executed, the emperor still had to cross off their names—御筆勾除, more commonly 勾决—on a list of criminals whose death sentence had been classified as effective. It appears that even those who had killed a senior relative in a state of madness were routinely “exempted from the hook” (免勾) and therefore not executed. $ ^{45} $ They were eventually sentenced to permanent imprisonment like most people who had killed someone because of madness. The legal institution thus managed to preserve a hierarchy of punishments between different homicides committed because of madness—decapitation being more severe than strangulation, and effective sentences more severe than suspended ones—yet continued to recognize the utter lack of criminal intention of mad acts by not executing insane killers. Permanent imprisonment thus served as the catch-all punishment for almost all mad killers, regardless of whom they had killed, but the Ministry reasserted some sort of hierarchy by refusing to release people who had killed a senior relative in a fit of madness.

To summarize the measures of liberation that appeared between 1792 and 1801. Mad killers who had been sentenced to permanent imprisonment could be released if they were single sons and had recovered from their illness. No time limit was specified, but a memorandum from 1849 instructed to process such cases only five years after the killer had recovered. $ ^{46} $ For other mad killers, the only hope for liberation was an edict of grace coming out at least five years after they had recovered from their madness. The increase in number of such edicts in the late nineteenth century made such liberation almost certain. $ ^{47} $

## PDF第504页

Just as the similarity between mad gestures and accidents was denied in homicide cases, it was reasserted in cases of injury in the name of the law on “killing because of madness.” $ ^{48} $ Whereas mad acts that led to someone’s death were punished by permanent imprisonment and even death, the same acts resulting in injuries could still be redeemed monetarily. The reasserted equivalence between mad acts and accidents in injury cases shows that madmen were not necessarily treated as deviants in the nineteenth century, when the punishments for mad homicides were at their highest.

When the victim of an injury was a close relative, however, monetary redemption was not allowed. Some officials even explicitly said that injuring because of madness was not the same as a true accident (若因瘋刃傷，即與真正過失不同). $ ^{49} $ This distinction, which was ignored when injury victims were not relatives, served to rationalize the severe punishments that were imposed to mad people who killed or injured close seniors.

At the very end of the nineteenth century, Xue Yunsheng 薛允升 (1820-1901) blamed Qing legislation for having disturbed the network of statutes that the Qing had inherited from the Ming, and ultimately the Tang. $ ^{50} $ Xue's commentaries in the Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes (Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 (1905) repeatedly insisted that sub-statutes were based on single cases rather than on a concern to preserve balance between punishments. $ ^{51} $ Almost every new law concerning madness was indeed designed to solve a specific problem. How to punish those who had killed their husband? How to handle those

## PDF第505页

who had killed their younger brothers? How to treat those who had recovered in time for trial? In 1732, concern to prevent multiple killings justified more severity in preventive imprisonment. The 1804 law purported to deter fakers. Contradictions are to be expected in a system that changed slowly and incrementally, but ironically it was often the concern to impose the right punishment for certain acts that led to discrepancies in the sentencing of proximate crimes.

Two targets of Xue Yunsheng's critique were the preventive confinement of “people with madness illness” and the penalties imposed on their relatives when mad people committed suicide or a homicide. $ ^{52} $ I showed in 11.4 that measures of preventive imprisonment were tightened in 1732 and 1766 because cases of multiple killings were judged under “killing because of madness,” a law that allowed killers to redeem their crime with a payment to their victim’s family. Because mad people were not physically punished for this crime, penalties were imposed on the relatives and local headmen who had failed to prevent it. Death penalties were introduced for multiple killers in 1776 (see above, p. 481), yet precautionary measures and physical penalties for incautious relatives remained in place until the last decade of the Qing dynasty, long after their original raison d’être—light punishments for insane criminals, making better prevention necessary—had disappeared.

Another locus of inconsistencies was measures pertaining to the liberation of imprisoned mad killers. $ ^{53} $ They did not apply to people who had killed junior relatives in a fit of madness, because their cases were not treated under the sub-statute on “killing because of madness,” to which the liberation measures applied. Those who had killed an unrelated person in a fit of madness (a crime more serious than the killing of a junior relative) could thus be released more easily than those who had killed their younger brother.

## PDF第506页

Xue Yunsheng's work on sub-statutes was at the foundation of the Currently Operative Criminal Code of the Great Qing (Da Qing xianxing xingli) 大清现行刑律) that Yu Jiansan and Xue Yunsheng's disciple Shen Jiaben 沈家本 published in 1908 on imperial commission. $ ^{54} $ For the first time since 1740, the Code was systematically overhauled. Redundancies and discrepancies between articles were eliminated, terminology updated, and all the sub-statutes re-arranged so that they would again form a network comparable to that of the root statutes. All the laws on madness in the New Code of the Great Qing (Da Qing xinli) 大清新律), which appeared in 1909 under the direction of Shen Jiaben, were adjusted according to Xue Yunsheng's critical commentaries, many of which Shen and Yu cited in their Explanatory Annotations. $ ^{55} $ The most important change in madness laws was perhaps the elimination of preventive confinement and of the punishment imposed to relatives and officials when a non-declared mad person committed a crime.

### 12.3 The legislative and judicial effect of the fear of fakers

Let us now return to the 1804 law that punished by “strangulation after the Assizes” people who were found guilty of “killing because of madness.” As I explained above, this death sentence was meant to discourage fakers from exploiting the alleged leniency of permanent confinement. But the outcome for mad killers was still permanent confinement,

## PDF第507页

because the death penalty was not executed. The main outcome of the 1802 law was to formalize the types of evidence that would confirm that a killer had been “mad” at the moment of committing a crime. These new rules often confirmed practices that had been used unsystematically until then.

The fear of fakers that this sub-statute institutionalized had been adumbrated by provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 in 1754. As a way to “stop the fraud of pretending madness and of falsifications” (杜裝瘋假捏之弊), Jiang had recommended to treat like ordinary affray cases the cases of undeclared mad people who had killed someone but had recovered after their act. $ ^{56} $ Jiang had also proposed to sentence to permanent imprisonment mad killers who had been particularly violent and who may thus kill again. The Ministry of Punishments had rejected his suggestions, but in 1762 had decided to sentence to permanent confinement all mad killers who were not executed for parricide or the killing of close senior relatives.

The 1804 law distinguished between two types of mad killers: those who recovered from madness after their crime, and those who did not. They constituted “types” only insofar as the law made a distinction between them. Killers who recovered from their madness immediately after having killed someone were the most suspicious, but the law treated them in the same way as those who regained their senses in time for the “retrial” (覆審), and as madmen who recovered only a few years after their crime. $ ^{57} $ In all such cases, simple claims to insanity uncorroborated by other evidence could not be trusted. The 1804 law started by specifying that the best kind of evidence for madness was a previous

## PDF第508页

declaration to officials for which there “was a file” (有案) at the county yamen. But as we saw in 11.4, prior declarations to officials were exceptionally rare.

By 1766, the Ministry had found that a reliable way to ensure that someone had indeed killed because of insanity was to ask the killer's relatives to write “bonded statements” (甘結) to that effect. $ ^{58} $ The 1804 law instructed officials to obtain the same kind of affidavit from the relatives of the victim. $ ^{59} $ The signatories need not have witnessed the crime, because if the killer had had motives to kill, the victim's relatives would presumably know it and say so. And since those who lied in a bonded statement were liable to punishment, legislators assumed that people would not sign one lightly. This reckoning was best expressed by a signatory whom officials accused of covering up: “if madness was faked or there were other such things, since Mrs. Niu née Xu is my own daughter, I would naturally seek to extend grievance for her. How could I be willing to help my son-in-law Niu Dafu to fake madness in order to escape a severe punishment?” (若有裝瘋別的緣故 鈕徐氏是小的親生女兒 自然求給女兒伸冤 怎肯替女婿鈕大幅捏瘋圖脫重罪呢) $ ^{60} $

In continuation with the 1766 law, the county magistrate still had to provide a "stamped bonded statement" (印结, so called because it was stamped with the county's official seal [yin 印]) certifying that homicide had indeed been committed in a state of insanity. The local official would be punished if his superiors found that he had either failed to identify a faker or covered up for one.

## PDF第509页

The second group of mad killers that judicial officials encountered was those who never recovered from madness after committing homicide. Chronic delirium kept a killer from providing the confession that was necessary for a sentence to be pronounced. When such cases occurred, legal reports almost invariably noted that the killer's "eyes were inexpressive and his words incoherent: no confession can be extracted" (两目直瞪 語無論次 不能取供). $ ^{61} $ People like this were considered "permanently crazy" (始終瘋迷). When they were not executed for a serious crime like parricide, they were sentenced to permanent imprisonment.

I mentioned in Chapter 11 that the criminal's appearance hinted, but did not prove, that he had been crazy when committing his crime. To make sure that the killer was not faking, an officially hired physician (警官) assessed his condition. To do so, the doctor used the most common diagnostic method in Chinese medicine—he took the killer's pulse. $ ^{62} $ This measure implied that the body could speak for itself, its intelligibility detached from the mad person's (or the faker's) will. Since no pulse was unique to madness, however, we do not know how yamen physicians could distinguish a real madman from, say, a feverish faker. In the next section, I discuss in more detail the role of physicians in trials involving madness.

Torture could be applied to mad men who had killed close relatives. Lack of fear and lack of reaction to pain usually confirmed that the “criminal” was genuinely insane. Li

## PDF第510页

Caizuo 李裁左, who had killed his paternal uncle in 1889, was submitted to torture but he showed no fear (加以刑嚇 罔知畏懼). $ ^{63} $

To Qing legislators, madness was an extreme illness characterized by complete incoherence, absolute unawareness of one's own actions, and imperviousness to pain and threats. Hired physicians corroborated this definition by showing that madness was unambiguously inscribed in the body's vital signs. We are now ready to say more about the role of doctors in trials in which the killer was suspected to have been insane.

### 12.4 The judicial roles of physicians

As I mentioned in the Prologue to Part Two, the majority of studies on the interaction between law and medicine have so far concentrated on The Washing Away the Wrongs (Xiyuan lu 洗冤錄), a thirteenth-century forensic manual. Forensic medicine, the practice of examining corpses to determine the causes and circumstances of a suspicious death, played an important role in the Chinese judicial process. In 1694, the Washing Away of Wrongs became the official handbook by which “coroners” (wu zuo 仵作 or xing wu 刑仵) determined what weapon (or poison) had been used in a killing and which blow had been fatal to a victim. $ ^{64} $ As Qu Tongzu once pointed out, these coroners could simply be local clerks or runners put in charge of filling out the location of injuries on a chart of the human

## PDF第511页

body based on the Washing Away of Wrongs, $ ^{65} $ but Pierre-Étienne Will (2007) has shown that activist officials also worked to improve the official handbook on the basis of their own experience with forensic inquests.

Much less well-known than the activity of coroners is the fact that one or more physician was assigned to each county yamen 衙門, or government office. Depending on the size of the county, up to three of these physicians—called either yisheng 醫生 or yixue 醫學 in official documents—could serve there. Their tasks differed from those of the coroners. Instead of dealing with dead people, they treated victims of injuries, criminals who had fallen ill in prison, and probably even convicts and their relatives when they became sick on their way to a site of exile or penal servitude. $ ^{66} $

After laws concerning “killing because of madness” started to appear in the late seventeenth century, these yamen doctors came to play fresh roles. I am not sure when or how they came to perform them, but they were doing so routinely by the mid eighteenth century. Coroners were rarely involved, since madness (not a physical injury) did not fit under the forensic categories of the Washing Away of Wrongs.

## PDF第512页

The first task yamen physicians were asked to fulfill was to ascertain insanity. In the formulaic order in which legal documents reported the procedure, the magistrate, having found that a criminal was too delirious to stand trial, summoned a doctor to examine (診視, or 診脈) the killer. Any abnormal pulse was enough to guarantee that madness was authentic, as long as the physician was willing to sign a “bonded statement” to that effect (see above, p. 490). I shall explain below how this approach diverged from that of physicians toward their regular patients. In legal contexts, physicians conducted no interviews, presumably because the mad prisoners were incoherent, but also because officials, not doctors, were in charge of conducting interrogations.

The self-evidence of madness is one important reason why Qing trials expected physicians not to recognize madness, but only to confirm that it was not faked. Since legal madness was absolute, it was easy to identify. Relatives and neighbors diagnosed someone as crazy and enforced this informal classification by treating that person as mad. $ ^{67} $ The law counted on this self-evidence when it called for the preventive imprisonment of “people with madness illness” (11.4). Most madness trials also gave weight to the testimony of “community experts” (Eigen 1995: 7), the point being precisely that people did not have to be experts to distinguish something as obvious as insanity.

After ascertaining that the criminal was indeed mad, the official physician was asked to “treat him medically” (醫治) to make him fit for trial. Most routine memorials cited the magistrate’s order to treat the mad person and to report when the “criminal” had recovered, but they said little about how treatment was supposed to take place. The magistrate may simply have waited until the killer became better naturally.

## PDF第513页

One third role for doctors in madness trials did not involve yamen doctors, but physicians who had examined the would-be killers in the past and could testify to their history of insanity. These rare depositions give interesting insights on how non-elite medical practitioners tried to cure mad behavior; $ ^{68} $ they reveal familiar medical approaches: pulse-taking, the belief that insanity happened when mucus obstructed the Heart, and the use of drugs for therapy. But fundamental differences between legal and medical concerns divested medical observations of what they would have meant in a therapeutic context.

Whereas physicians concentrated on identifying and treating the organic roots of insane behavior, officials were mainly concerned with criminal intention, the possibility of fakery, and the potential violence of mad people. The legal language of intentions gave great importance to the concept of  $ xin \text{心} $ (“mind,” “intention”). Since medicine also recognized that madness appeared when the Heart (also  $ xin \text{心} $) was affected, the law could have displayed an interest in how illness made one unaware of one’s own acts, and could have asked physicians to determine the link between madness and specific criminal actions or mad people’s lack of memory. $ ^{69} $ But the nature of the relation between intentions and “madness illness” was already decided by the law. From physicians, officials simply wanted confirmation that killers who appeared insane were indeed insane, rather than a legally irrelevant explanation of how madness made people unaware of their own actions.

Medical authority was used to ascertain madness, but the medical language used to express this authority was deprived of its therapeutic implications. Law and medicine drew different conclusions from pulse data. In law, a number of different pulses were seen to

## PDF第514页

prove that madness was real: the swollen and large (洪大) pulse, which was the most common, but also the floating and accelerated (浮数), the floating and swollen (浮洪), and the strung and slippery (弦滑). $ ^{70} $ In reports, the specification of the pulse was always followed by the phrase “the madness is authentic” (患瘋屬實; 實係患瘋; 實患瘋病, etc.), implying that the bureaucratic need to determine the authenticity of madness was more important than consistency in pulse taking. By the last decades of the nineteenth century, the stock phrase “the six pulses are swollen and large, the madness is authentic” (六脈洪大 患瘋屬實) was included in almost every medical deposition. This evolution toward the formulaic indicates that medical language in legal reports had become deprived of most its meaning, and only retained the force of an administrative guarantee, which was indeed the purpose of the affidavits where yamen physicians recorded their depositions.

As far as I know, physicians almost never used the pulse to prove that someone was crazy. Madness was indeed so manifest that simple observation sufficed to ascertain it. The pulse served instead to indicate what type of dysfunction (including demonic possession) was causing the mad behavior at hand. In medical practice various pulses led to different therapeutic strategies, but in trials they were all taken to prove one thing – that madness was not faked. Pulse-taking was thus severed from medical action and grafted instead onto a string of legal actions.

Unsurprisingly, the law snubbed the medical belief in the curability of madness.

Doctors expressed this therapeutic optimism in their treatises (where they presented

methods that were supposed to work) and case files (which were almost always records of

success). Therapeutic failure was generally blamed on failure to identify the right etiology, a

## PDF第515页

plausible explanation in a tradition that emphasized the infinite variety of illness causes. $ ^{71} $

Whereas for physicians insanity could be successfully treated with drugs in the same manner as diarrhea, in the official view madness was unpredictable and dangerous. As the Ministry of Punishments had reasoned in 1762, “madness is an illness that periodically flares up and heals; it is difficult to guarantee that it will not flare up again [even] after more than one year of imprisonment” (然瘋病原係時發時愈 霸禁逾年 難保其不復再發). $ ^{72} $ Prudence was undoubtedly bolstered by the common occurrence of killings by people who had previously been “cured” by local physicians.

Medical testimonies in Qing law had nothing to do with an “insanity defense.” In the absence of an adversarial trial system, no “defendants” could “plead” insanity and invite expert medical witnesses to support their case. In Victorian England, “the evolving role of the medical witness [in insanity trials] coincided with a fundamental reconceptualization of the relation between criminal responsibility and madness” (Eigen 1995: 6), and “the insanity plea amounted to a repeated exploration of the meaning of responsibility” (Smith 1981: 161). $ ^{73} $ In Qing law, by contrast, doctors served the local government and produced evidence the interpretation of which was determined in advance.

Qing physicians were responsible for confirming the authenticity of a killer’s madness, but the framework in which they did was the product of the legal institution. In the absence of a forum for debates, the legal definition of madness remained absolute, and trials amounted to the repeated confirmation of the prototypical script of “killing because of madness.” Boundary cases only appeared when the Ministry of Punishment found that a

## PDF第516页

provincial governor had not adequately supported his conclusion that a killer had been either mad or not mad. The debate would then oppose the Ministry to a governor who wanted to avoid the administrative consequences of being proven wrong. $ ^{74} $ Physicians never had a role in resolving these issues.

### 12.5 Other “mad acts” in Qing legal contexts

Given how extremely the Qing judiciary defined madness, it could not attribute crimes like theft to insanity as in contemporaneous England. $ ^{75} $ Theft necessarily implied an intent, a target (money, property), and a purpose (getting enough to eat until the next day, etc.), precisely the kind of details that made Qing officials reject the possibility of madness in homicide cases. In what other circumstances than homicides could the Qing legal system consider madness relevant? What other acts than killings could Qing law consider to have been committed “because of madness”? Here I discuss false accusations and suicides.

## False accusations

People usually deposed slanderous allegations out of personal resentment, greed, a desire for revenge, or disputes over property. Their targets could be overbearing relatives, a hated landlord, or simply a member of the local gentry who might prefer to surrender a sum of money than let his name be defiled in a trial. $ ^{76} $ A law mandated that those who submitted

## PDF第517页

false accusations would be sentenced to the punishment the accused would have incurred if the accusations had proven true. $ ^{77} $

Language was central to false accusations because accusations had to be formulated coherently. Yet madmen in homicide cases were described as entirely unaware of their acts, as entirely incoherent, and as being unable to remember what they had done. Was the law on false accusations always strictly applied, or did Qing officials accept that people could submit false accusations "because of madness"? The answer varied from case to case.

In 1776 Li Zhu 李珠, a forty-four-year-old farmer from Shanxi, went to the yamen of a high military official to depose grave accusations. $ ^{78} $ He told the provincial military commander $ ^{79} $ that Li Lin 李林 (a neighbor), Liu Sizhong 劉思忠 (a farmer from another village), and Yang Jiesan 楊接三 (a short-term laborer from yet another rural community) were using spells of the forbidden White Yang teachings (白陽教) and collecting money to contribute to Wang Lun's "wayward teachings" (邪教). Yang Jiesan was further accused of inciting people to join Wang Lun's movement. Less than two years earlier, Wang Lun's uprising, which was rooted in popular religious practices, had been suppressed in Shandong and other northern provinces. $ ^{80} $ Besides punishing the leaders and releasing most of the followers, as was regular practice in such cases, the government had banned the sect and forbidden people from practicing its spells. $ ^{81} $

## PDF第518页

Interrogated by Shanxi governor Yang Jingsu 楊景素, Li Zhu spoke incoherently (語言顛倒). As the governor reported, Li “appeared insane” (似有瘋狀), but his accusations could not be neglected. Had some of Wang Lun’s followers escaped suppression? Yang sent orders to find and arrest Li’s relatives and the people Li had accused. “The house of each of these criminals was searched: there were no hidden scriptures, [religious] scrolls, images, or effigies, nor illicit objects that violate prohibitions” (搜查各犯家內並無藏匿經卷圖像及違禁不法器物). All three “criminals” (so called by virtue of being accused in a trial) said in their deposition that Li Zhu was crazy and had absurdly accused them.

As his father Li Sijing explained, Li Zhu had been a student in his youth, but had never gone beyond the basics. He attributed his son's madness to his failure to become a licentiate. Because Li Zhu was not violent (all he did was “speak nonsense” 說話顛倒), his father had not reported him to the magistrate for preventive confinement. In the spring of 1768, Li had escaped from home but had been quickly sent back, after which his father had forbidden him to go out. His madness then got better naturally. In the fall of 1775, however, Li's three-year-old son had died. As Li Sijing reported, Li Zhu had then become a little confused, “but since his madness illness was intermittent, I did not make much of it” (小的因他瘋病原是時發時止的 也不放在心上). He could not have foreseen that Li Zhu would escape to Beijing to accuse three acquaintances of participating in sectarian activities.

As it turned out, the three accused were not former members of Wang Lun's gang, but "good people satisfied with their lot" (俱係安分良民). Li Zhu's only recorded deposition is short:

I am not confused by madness. I don't know why everybody says I am a madman. In

1765, I became confused at Jinghai county. They said I was a madman, and they

## PDF第519页

transported me back. I have a three-year-old son; he died last year. My father and brother don't treat me well. Li Lin, Liu Sizhong, and Yang Jiesan all ignore me.

小的並不瘋迷 不知人家為什麼都說小的是瘋子 乾隆三十年上小的在靜海縣間 [?] 愰 他們說小的是個瘋子 把小的遞解回來的 小的有個三歲兒子上年死了 小的父親 兄弟待小的不好 李林 劉思忠 楊接三都不理小的

As the governor concluded, "after repeated interrogation, the criminal is staring blankly; now he smiles and then he becomes angry; he is without fear or apprehension: [he] is indeed confused by madness" (覆加詰訊 該犯兩目直視 忽笑忽怒 毫無畏懼 實係瘋迷).

Li Zhu's deliberate acts would not have been considered mad in a homicide case. He knew that the people he accused would be in trouble if they were suspected of having joined Wang Lun's uprising, and he purposely traveled to a yamen to lodge his accusations in coherent language. He also remembered what he had done. But treating this case as one of false accusation would have meant sentencing Li to death, a punishment that governor Yang Jingsu may have found excessive. Yang decreed instead that Li would be permanently confined in the county prison "in accordance with the sub-statute on madness illness" (照瘋病例), $ ^{82} $ to prevent him from stirring up further trouble (復滋事端).

Li Zhu might have been sentenced differently if he had accused his father of sectarian involvement, but in this case those he had accused were neither relatives nor prominent members of the community who may have demanded heavier punishment. With the 1762 sub-statute as a convenient way to dispose of troublesome madmen, the governor concluded that Li "was indeed confused by madness" and that his condition naturally warranted permanent imprisonment. In the seamless final report, he did not even mention the possibility of treating Li Zhu's case as one of false accusation.

## PDF第520页

In a different case of false accusation, the mad offender was punished more severely than Li Zhu even though the accusations he leveled were even more gratuitous than Li's. In the late spring of 1785, Liu Lin 劉林 left home to present a petition in front of the imperial cortege, which he had heard would pass in the vicinity on its way to the imperial summer resort of Rehe. $ ^{83} $ Claiming to be involved in a land dispute with licentiate Wang Shiwan 王十完, Liu accused Wang of using magic methods to bewilder him (用法迷惑). $ ^{84} $ Since the event took place near the capital, Liu was handed directly to the Ministry of Punishments for interrogation. The fact that he seemed “stupefied” (精神恍惚) and that “his deposition was disjointed” (供詞支離) made officials express fear that he was “falsely assuming the appearance of madness and not spitting out the real story” (假捏瘋狀，不吐實情). $ ^{85} $

The officials who investigated in Liu's native place determined that he had broken out of the "empty room" (空屋) in which he had been confined after his madness had flared up a few days before. It turned out that Liu did not even know the person he claimed was magically stupefying him; he had only heard that some of the land near where he lived belonged to Wang Shiwan. Placed in front of Wang and a few other men, Liu failed to recognize him. The governor-general of Zhili proceeded to a second interrogation. Liu could not answer how Wang practiced his magic. When Liu started "talking nonsense" (信口酒) the governor-general concluded that it now made no doubt that Liu had fled home "because of madness" and blithely lodged an accusation (實係因瘋逃逸，妄行控告，毫無疑義). False accusations were often used as tactical diversions in feuds over property, but in Liu's

## PDF第521页

case even the feud was false. Liu Lin's accusation seemed even more groundless than Li Zhu's, whose case involved personal resentment toward people he knew.

Daring to present a legal plaintiff to the emperor for no good reason, reported the governor-general, showed that Liu “recognized no law nor restraint” (目無法紀): “It would not be suitable to be in the least lenient even on account of his suffering from intermittent madness” (未便因有時發時止之瘋□⁸⁶ 而即稍為寬貸). For “breaching the imperial procession,” Liu was sentenced to 100 strokes of the heavy stick and deportation to a “nearby frontier” (近邊) to serve as a slave in a military garrison for the rest of his life.⁸⁷ As in Li Zhu’s case, the false accusation was disregarded, but so was the accuser’s madness. No one expressed fear that Liu Lin would “stir up trouble” while in military exile.

Liu Lin was punished more severely than Li Zhu for reasons external to the false accusation itself. His main offense was not the accusation, but his disruption of the imperial procession. By insisting that Liu's madness was "intermittent," the Governor implied that Liu may not have been mad when he came to seek the emperor. He attributed Liu's acts not to madness but to his "recognizing no law or restraint." If he mentioned madness as a possible reason for leniency, it was only to reject it immediately.

## Suicide

Whereas in early modern England “self-murder” was punished posthumously by confiscation of property, desecration of the corpse, and profane burial (MacDonald 1992:

## PDF第522页

87), in late-imperial China suicide was not considered a crime. It could even be glorified as an expression of political loyalty, since part of an official's duty was never to serve another master than the emperors of his own dynasty. $ ^{88} $ A parallel for women was suicide for honor. $ ^{89} $ Matthew Sommer (2000: 183) points that the analogy between chastity and political loyalty was explicit in the language used to discuss both.

More peculiarly, Chinese codes imposed heavy punishments on those who, with threats or violence or for lack of filial submission, incited others to commit suicide. $ ^{90} $ The relatives of a dead man, for instance, could coerce his widow to remarry against her will, driving her to take her own life to protect her threatened virtue. $ ^{91} $ The main problem in cases of suspicious suicides was therefore to determine why it had been committed.

I found a large number of cases of “suicide because of madness” in the archives of the Ministry of Punishments (Xingbu dang'an 刑部檔案, hereafter XBDA), dating mostly from the last decades of the Qing dynasty. A woman was so scared when Westerners (洋人) invaded Beijing in 1900 to suppress the Boxers' uprising that she became crazy (成顛). A year later, she jumped into a well. $ ^{92} $ A man testified that his brother, who “sought death” during his fits (發時就要尋死) of madness (顛狂病症), took his own life by swallowing “Western drugs” (洋藥). $ ^{93} $ An old woman from the Bordered Red Banner who had suffered

## PDF第523页

from “mucus-confusion disorder” (痰迷病症) hanged herself in a temple. $ ^{94} $ Hundreds of such cases provide detailed and invaluable information on the social life of commoners and Banner people in Beijing at a time of impoverishment and social breakdown. $ ^{95} $

It was well known that mad people sometimes committed suicide, $ ^{96} $ but hundreds of cases of “suicide because of madness” in a few years in a single city demand explanation. Witnesses’ claim that the suicide had been “brought about by madness” (實係瘋病所致), $ ^{97} $ which appeared in almost every final report, often did not appear in the depositions written during the investigation. $ ^{98} $ The phrase was simply put in the mouth of all the witnesses in the final report, often followed by “there are no other reasons” (並無別故). Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007), whose research is also based on the archives of the Ministry of Punishment, has shown that the legal secretaries who wrote reports strove to maintain consistency between testimonies “in details, wording, and plot.” In a complex case from 1865, none of the original confessions even mentioned madness as a possible cause of suicide, $ ^{99} $ yet the final report constructed a seamless narrative in which a man suffering from extreme insanity had swallowed poison.

Officials did not necessarily believe that these suicides were actually caused by insanity, but they handled the cases as if this were so. The well-known late-Qing legal

## PDF第524页

backlog (see Zhao Xiaohua 2001) may have been a factor in expediting the treatment of these cases. There are countless examples of such a procedure, which may also have been a convenient way for judges and relatives to avoid complications in a system that could punish those who pushed others to commit suicide, since suicides committed "because of madness" could not be imputed to others.

This expedient way of handling suicides resembles the evolution traced by Michael MacDonald and Terence Murphy (1990), who found that the percentage of suicides attributed to insanity (non compos mentis) in England went from less than 5% before 1660 to above 95% in the last three decades of the eighteenth century (data summarized in MacDonald 1992: 92, fig. 5.2 and text). They attributed this transformation to changes in social perceptions of suicide, but also to the unwillingness of jurors to condemn it as a crime, because the state would then confiscate the dead person's property.

The mandated punishment for letting a non-declared mad relative commit suicide was almost never applied. There are exceptions: in 1903, a man was punished with 80 strokes of the heavy stick for letting his mad father kill himself. $ ^{100} $ Considering how central filial relations were to Qing law, the fact that the suicide was the man's father probably determined the punishment.

### 12.6 Conclusion

Increasingly severe punishments did not reflect changes in Chinese attitudes toward madness (or even in the legal view of mad acts), but a diversification of the circumstances that were deemed relevant to the sentencing of homicides committed “because of madness.” Mad acts had been equated with “accidents,” inadvertent gestures that led to unforeseeable

## PDF第525页

results. After 1750 in cases where the victims were senior relatives, the same acts were increasingly treated like assaults minus the intention. Madness was still considered relevant to sentencing, but only as a secondary circumstance.

The early Qing had represented a particularly low level of severity in the sentencing of crimes committed in a mad state. The level of severity reached by the mid-nineteenth century was probably only a return to the level of Ming punishments, when madness was not officially deemed relevant in the sentencing of homicides, but perhaps still justified a suspension of the death penalty. The main difference between the Qing and earlier dynasties that concentrated only on punishments was that the Qing imposed permanent imprisonment to mad killers, though by the end of the dynasty the time of imprisonment had been diminished to five years starting from the moment of recovery.

Most new rules came from proposals by officials who thought that the law did not (or should not) apply to the cases they had to solve. Since sub-statutes were rarely adjusted as a whole, the new body of laws may appear to have been “patchy,” but besides cases that led to the creation of new laws, cases were consistently judged according to the laws in place. Disparities in the severity of sentencing were inconsistencies in legislation, not adjudication. We could therefore say that judgments were consistent with incompletely consistent laws.

I explained why the punishments for homicides committed because of madness suddenly became more severe in the late eighteenth century after about 100 years of unprecedented clemency. As in Chapter 11, I argued that the functioning of legislation and trials shaped the way mad men were legally handled in the second half of the Qing. None of these changes was inevitable, but their irreducible premise was the existence of the 1669 guideline that allowed “killing because of madness” to be redeemed with a payment to the family of the victim. By its very existence, this rule made other issues visible. Perceived

## PDF第526页

problems led to new solutions that created new dilemmas, for which yet other solutions were adopted, in a non-systematic way.

The changes I have described resulted from individual initiatives grounded in one or several of the many logics that Qing legal institutions accepted as valid. The main issues that interested legislators were the fear of fakers, the problem of mad people's recovery, the perceived danger of an illness that could lead to blind acts of violence, and the concern to impose severe punishments for acts that had a severe outcome (as when the victim was a close relative or when several people were killed). The Ministry of Punishments institutionalized individual decisions that became the guiding principles for the handling of future cases. The next chapter will show that the way common people handled their mad relatives differed from the law's emphasis on prevention and punishment or medicine's on explanation and therapy.

## PDF第527页

## 13 

## Life trials

Handling mad people, speaking of madness

In this chapter I explain how Qing people interpreted and handled a variety of excessive or abnormal behaviors they called “mad.” I concentrate on three issues in the following order: what acts were seen to deserve that label (more below on terminology), how non-specialists explained the origins of what they called madness, and how relatives of affected people dealt with these afflictions. The last section is a broad survey of the use of the notion of “mucus” in explanations and representations of madness in a variety of late-imperial sources. Since almost all the information presented in this chapter (except for its last section) comes from legal reports, I first explain how the way these reports were produced shaped the information they contained. For one thing, because women committed far fewer “killings because of madness” than men, evidence on the way “mad women” were handled is scanter than comparable information for men.

Here and in the next two chapters, I attempt to shed some light on the fears, hopes, and emotional lives of less educated commoners, who usually appear in the historical records only as rebels, criminals, or nameless economic actors. Presenting all these views as representative of “Chinese popular conceptions” would be simplistic. The men and women I discuss lived in different times and provinces, spoke different languages, and were born in different family structures. Conceptions of illness and the range of therapeutic options available certainly varied between Suzhou, coastal Fujian, and Yunnan market towns. For

## PDF第528页

lack of data, we shall have to assume that differences existed without being able to analyze them concretely.

As we have seen, the official Qing legal term for insanity was feng bing 瘋病 (“madness illness”) and the main penal denomination under which “mad killers” were sentenced was “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人), which I have been referring to as “killing because of madness.” Although the word feng bing was not particularly prominent in common parlance (especially in the early Qing, when it was rare even in written documents), it was used in legal documents side-by-side with terms like fengkuang 風狂 and diankuang 顛狂, which had appeared in earlier legal texts and were in some cases still current in medical discussions of madness. It thus appears that different fields referred to madness with overlapping sets of vocabulary.

Since the vocabulary that physicians, judicial officials, and ordinary people used to refer to madness and mad people overlapped, various social actors from different backgrounds probably agreed that they were talking about the same things (madness, crazy acts, insane people) even when they embraced different conceptions of these things and handled them for different purposes. Throughout, I will try to explain how the way ordinary people treated their mad relatives differed from what physicians (whose main purpose was to cure) and legal officials (who chiefly aimed to prevent or punish crimes) did to people they identified as mad in their own field of practice. This chapter should thus bring to light the wealth of cultural models that were available to interpret and handle mad acts and mad words in late imperial China, as well as the intelligibility of linguistic labels like illness names across fields of practice and social contexts.

## PDF第529页

### 13.1 Discussions of madness in legal reports

Judicial depositions and their biases

Much of what we know about the kinds of behavior Qing people called “mad” comes from “routine memorials of the Office of Scrutiny for the Ministry of Punishments” (刑科题本). $ ^{1} $ These reports were written by the highest officials in the provincial bureaucracy (governors and governors-general) as the final step in the sentencing of a crime, or by the Ministry of Punishments in response to these reports. $ ^{2} $ Each memorial gave a detailed account of an entire case and of the way the judicial system had processed it. Most cited the “depositions” (gong 供) of the people involved: the “criminal” (as a person under trial was usually called), his or her relatives and neighbors, local leaders, and anyone the magistrate found relevant to interrogate. $ ^{3} $ These statements illustrate how common people spoke of madness and dealt with it, but they should be used with great circumspection.

Depositions were not neutrally collected “representations of madness.” As we have seen, Qing law accepted as “mad” only acts that were entirely devoid of awareness. This extreme definition and the law’s narrow interest in killings explain why depositions focused disproportionately on fits of furor. Because the content of a mad killer’s words was usually

## PDF第530页

irrelevant to a homicide investigation, legal reports usually said little about what may have constituted mad language, but this vagueness about mad speech does not mean that the people under trial were always unable to speak coherently, or that only the utterly delirious were identified as mad in village communities. Legal documents also tell us little about the non-violent gestures and words that may have characterized local madmen, and about the daily interactions between them and their relatives, even when the depositions purported to be precisely about that. Certain derangements that people may have called “mad” (unmotivated sorrow, love-sickness, flights of fancy, etc.) were rarely recorded in legal sources because they were not seen as having led to the killings, or when they were, the cases in question were not sentenced as if they had been committed “because of madness.” Finally, these documents give no access to the “discourse processes through which social and emotional realities are constituted in ordinary talk and interaction” (White 1992: 38).

These are external limits to what “routine memorials” (tiben) will tell us. Even more important, the content of the depositions recorded in the reports was also pre-oriented. Statements found their way into tiben by a complex process. During an official interrogation, the “criminal” and the witnesses gave “oral depositions” (□供), which a clerk recorded “in a mixture of spoken and written style corresponding to what was actually said.” $ ^{4} $ If witnesses disagreed or if there were discrepancies between their depositions and the material evidence, the magistrate would press everyone to tell the true story. Once all doubts and contradictions were cleared, he “ordered a clerk to write down for the criminal a formal confession of his offense, including key-points in the story and composed in a particular style.” For a condemnation to be acceptable, the criminal had to endorse this version of events.

## PDF第531页

The magistrate composed a summary of the entire affair and proposed a temporary sentence according to the relevant laws. He sent his report to his immediate superior the prefect, along with the formal depositions (招狀) of the criminal and of all the witnesses, reconstituted from the clerk's notes. I have already discussed the several reviews this preliminary sentencing had to go through before it could be confirmed (see 10). The depositions we read in these provincial reports were thus several times removed from the words that the parties had originally uttered.

Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007) has noticed broad discrepancies between draft depositions and the way they were cited in later reports. $ ^{5} $ He has shown that during the course of the Qing the legal secretaries who composed the final drafts of tiben increasingly tended to edit testimonies so as to confirm the magistrate’s (and the governor’s) findings, removing from the depositions lies, smokescreens, gaps or contradictions in evidence, and all the possibly truthful details that did not appear in the official narrative of the case (which in turn purported to be based on the confessions).

In some madness cases, all the witnesses were cited as having used the same words in the same order to describe what happened on the day of a crime. Even the killer used the same words despite claiming that he had no memory of what had happened. Most people who had not declared a mad person to the authorities explained their decision by claiming that their mad relative or neighbor "was not inciting trouble" (並不滋事). $ ^{6} $ However, when a father explained that he did not declare his son "because he is my only son," we may presume that the report was not citing a legal secretary playing ventriloquist. The language itself may not have been authentic, but the content probably was.

## PDF第532页

Depositions were edited translations of orality rather than direct transcriptions. Conventions of legal reporting such as the use of the written style and the selective editing of depositions make it difficult to assess what terms common people used to describe madness. Very specific depositions and those that “sound oral” were probably closer to the original words of the witnesses than the conventionally formulated ones. The memorials that quoted the witnesses as having said different things with different words in two different trial sessions are also more likely to have been authentic. The fact that these depositions consistently sounded more oral seems to confirm this view. It remains possible, however, that in many cases some seemingly oral usage reflected a secretary’s use of a vernacular style of writing as a device to enhance the authenticity of the depositions.

Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $

## The interest of the formulaic

The expressions concerning madness in tiben were of five different sorts: citations from the Code, verbatim transcriptions of speech, edited translations of oral depositions, bureaucratic jargon, and other expressions used by the officials who wrote and transcribed

## PDF第533页

the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity.

The concepts we see in documents were more than “background noise” that should be reduced before we can get to the undistorted facts. These objects were precisely those that institutions and practitioners purported to discuss and act upon. Formulaic phrases may have given little idea of what the person they described looked and sounded like, but they spoke eloquently of what the legal field expected madness to sound and look like. Judicial reports almost invariably noted that the eyes of killers who had just been arrested “were staring straight” (兩目直瞪 or 兩目直視) and that their “words were incoherent” (語無論次). These words described the stereotypically stupefied-and-rambling madman who could not provide the deposition that was necessary for sentencing to take place. Since these reports were written only after painstaking investigations had confirmed that the person in question had indeed been mad when he had committed a killing, claiming that madness was

## PDF第534页

recognized by these outward signs would be putting the result (an ex post facto report phrased in unequivocal terms) before the process of identification.

This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language.

Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad homicide started.

## PDF第535页

Since only absolute insanity was legally recognized, it is possible that many people whom trials determined were mad probably looked the way set phrases described them. But these formulaic expressions were meant to attest more than to describe. By establishing a seamless correspondence between specific events and a general scenario, officials used these phrases to make the choice of the penal denomination “killing because of madness” appear as indisputable as possible. This “fool-proofing” of judicial sentences was important, because officials could be imposed administrative sanctions if they sentenced a crime too lightly, too heavily, or according to the wrong legal article. Concrete observations funneled into a few turns of phrase aimed to convince reviewers that the madness of the “criminal” under trial was indeed as extreme as the law mandated. $ ^{15} $

The criminal's recovery from his incoherence made it possible to extract a confession. In most reports from the late Qing, when testimonies were rewritten to confirm the final verdict, few words were the criminal's own. The killer and most of the witnesses were reported to have said about the same thing in about the same words. The killer insisted that he "did not know how" (不知怎樣), he had grabbed an object – a club, a vegetable knife (菜刀, very common), a carrying pole, a staff, an axe, a sickle, a stool, a cane, a knife to peel grain (剝穀刀), a stick with a metal head used for defense at night, a flail, a hoe, etc. – and stabbed, slashed, flailed, poked, bludgeoned, or hacked his victim to death. He did not remember what had happened. Only after he had recovered had he learned of his action, and he admitted (in the language of penal denominations) to having "assaulted his elder brother because of madness, leading to his death" or "killed his wife because of madness."

## PDF第536页

The confession was not an admission of guilt doubled with repentance (though depositions sometimes contained statements of regret, dismay, or contrition), but the killer's formulation of the crime for which he would be sentenced—his act as it was characterized by the law. Since by definition he did not remember his acts, his confession could only be the ratification of the version of the crime that officials had settled upon after investigation.

Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mold when the uniqueness of each treatment was precisely what brought them distinction.

### 13.2 “Witnessing insanity”: commoners speaking of madness

Terminology: the illness and the fits

Legal reports hint at the diversity of expressions that commoners used to refer to

mad fits. In a 1736 report, for instance, various people referred to Deng Tingmei's 鄧廷梅

## PDF第537页

affliction by nine different terms. $ ^{17} $ Putting aside those related to “mucus” for the moment,

they were “madness illness” (the official term for madness), fengbing 風病 (lit. “Wind illness,”

but homophonous with the term that meant “madness illness”), fengdian 風顛 (“madness”; lit.

Wind dian), $ ^{18} $ fengkuang 風狂 (“madness”; lit. Wind kuang), diankuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), $ ^{19} $

fengkuang bing 風狂病 (“madness illness”), fengkuang bingzheng 風狂病症 (“madness disorder”),

and fengdian bingzheng 風顛病症 (“madness disorder”). $ ^{20} $

We cannot assume that people used the term “madness illness” orally. Considering that fengdian and fengkuang were intelligible on their own and were widely used in non-judicial contexts, we should probably interpret the compounds fengkuang bing, fengkuang bingzheng, and fengdian bingzheng—which were clearly not oral—as bureaucratic jargon. The suffixes bing and bingzheng may have been used to make clear that the law considered madness to be an illness. $ ^{21} $ Apart from the pair-turned-compound diankuang, all the above terms contain the sound feng. A clerk or official who wanted to transcribe the sound feng could choose between the homophonous 瘋 and 風. The fact that the report on Deng Tingmei’s case used 瘋 only when quoting the letter of the law suggests that the 瘋 usage was still marginal at the time. $ ^{22} $

The last two “terms for madness” employed in our 1736 memorial were tanmi xinqiao 痰迷心竅 (“mucus confusing the openings of the Heart”) and fengtan biqiao 風痰閉竅

## PDF第538页

(“Wind-mucus blocking the openings [of the Heart]”). In accord with medical conceptions I have already analyzed, tanmi xinqiao identified mucus as the cause of madness and the Heart as the seat of cognitive and perceptive activities. $ ^{23} $ “Wind-mucus blocking the openings” was a variant specifying that mucus had been caused by Wind. $ ^{24} $ This Wind etiology, which had become exceptional in Qing medicine, was reflected in the way the report explained the onset of Deng’s madness.
