Whereas most memorials said that the would-be killer “got madness” (得了瘋病), the 1736 memorial claimed Deng “contracted” (run 染) it, a term usually reserved for illnesses caught by contact with some kind of pathogenic Qi. $ ^{25} $

Although not all these terms for madness were technical medical categories, most of them had equivalents in the medical literature. The use of medically acceptable terminology by commoners and legal specialists alike does not imply that these people embraced medical conceptions. It confirms instead that the same set of terms could accommodate many different conceptions. The non-technical medical words used in legal depositions differed from expressions like “Liver stasis” (肝鬱), which referred to a specifically medical understanding of pathology rather than to a general kind of behavior like fengkuang. $ ^{26} $

In the same 1736 memorial, several people described the onset of Deng Tingmei's fits with eleven different expressions, all linked to the illness terms I just surveyed. Most of these expressions would have been medically intelligible. The most atypical one appeared in

## PDF第539页

a deposition by Deng's wife: "he went to the mountain to collect firewood; after he returned home wet he went crazy again" (往山取柴 濕身回家 又顛起來). Since qilai placed after a verb referred to the onset of the action expressed by that verb, dian qilai would seem to make sense, but this is the only instance of dian qilai I have seen in all my documents.

Seven more expressions contained a term that meant “to act up”: “went crazy” or “had a fit of madness” (發狂), “madness acted up” (顛發), “madness illness acted up again” (瘋病復發), “madness illness acted up” (風狂病發), “madness acted up severely” (風狂大發), “acted up suddenly” (一時發起來), and “madness/Wind illness flared up severely” (風病大作). The remaining three terms had to do with mucus and the Heart: “[he became] confused by mucus” (痰迷), “mucus confused his heart” (痰迷了心), and “his heart’s opening [became] blocked and hectic” (心竅閉亂). Some sentences blended legal jargon with unmistakably oral phrases, as when Deng Tingzhu (1736) said that his brother “had a nasty fit of madness illness” (瘋病發得很厲害).

Even if it is impossible to trace the use of all these terms to specific witnesses with any certainty, the semantic field as a whole seems authentic. All the terms are broadly attested elsewhere in late imperial fiction, law, and medicine alike, and only two terms not mentioned here (namely xinfeng 心風 and fengji 瘋疾) appeared in other documents to refer to madness. We should not insist on giving all the above terms discrete meanings, because they were all used to refer to the illness and fits of a single person. Only in technical contexts that required more precision were the distinct meanings of these expressions accentuated.

“Madness illness” and the odd “madness-confusion disorder” (瘋迷病症) and

“mucus-confusion disorder”(痰迷病症) became increasingly dominant in judicial reports in

## PDF第540页

the eighteenth century. Since these terms did not appear in other kinds of documents (including medical ones, from which “mucus confusion” 痰迷 had however originated), the rising uniformity of madness-related language in memorials probably reflected less a change in popular parlance than bureaucratic efforts to make reports terminologically consistent.

## Manifest madness: appearance and acts

We cannot extract clear and consistent definitions of madness from witnesses' statements—since giving definitions was never their point—but we can identify regularities in usage and demarcate the scope of the acts that commoners typically described as “mad” with the terms I just analyzed. Like most physicians, witnesses described madness not as a hidden affliction or a disposition of the entire self, but mostly as an intermittent illness that acted up in obvious ways. Perhaps because of the bias inherent in homicide reports toward the violent crises that had led to killings, the witnesses cited in these reports described madness as characterized by conspicuously strange acts, fits, frenetic running, wild cursing, and stupefied looks so obvious that officials did not have to ask witnesses how they knew it was madness, though not all agreed that these acts were the result of an illness. Madness expressed mostly by speech was rarely described in homicide reports. $ ^{27} $

Madness as discussed in judicial memorials can be interpreted as “chronic susceptibility to obvious, temporary mad fits” (Padel 1992: 35). During fits, Lu Bingjian 陆丙尖 (1897), “was unaware of people and things, but worked as usual when he became better” (發時不省人事 愈時照常工作). Jia Chandou’s 叚昆斗 (1736) father explained that his son’s illness “flares up two or three times a year.... Two or three days later he

## PDF第541页

recovers and takes care of the fields, cuts firewood and works as before" (一年也發兩三次

一發起來關鎖 過兩三日就好了 仍舊料理田地砍柴做活) $ ^{28} $ As in medical case

records, we are rarely told how madness sufferers felt when they had recovered from their

most obvious fits.

Even after such “mad” people had recovered, they may still have suffered from various mental afflictions, but people of the time do not seem to have called these conditions “madness.” Madness was in the fits, not in the intervals. The possibility remains that reports did not call these conditions mad because the law did not interpret them as mad. Still, as far as I know, sensations of oppression or depression (烦悶, 憂鬱, 抑鬱) were almost never described as mad in any kind of document, though they were sometimes said to have led to insanity in some medical cases.

Witnesses usually concentrated on two aspects of insanity: mad people's appearance and the things they said and did during their fits. $ ^{29} $ After the killing, Yang Ying 楊英 (1816) was found sitting in the courtyard "looking as if he had gone mad" (發瘋的樣子). $ ^{30} $ His brother specified that Yang Ying was "staring straight and screaming that there were people coming to kill him; he only looked frightened and scared; he was [indeed] suffering from the disorder of madness-confusion" (看他兩目直瞪 口裏嚷說有人來殺他了 只是驚怕是患瘋迷的病症). Dou Tong's 竇同 (1816) "eyes were staring dumbly; he was rambling to himself, and he came out dancing wildly" (兩目呆瞪 自言自語 亂舞出來). $ ^{31} $

## PDF第542页

People labeled “mad” engaged in frantic acts that immediately seemed abnormal to external observers. Pei Xiuyun 裴修雲 (1816) woke up one day and started breaking furniture. $ ^{32} $ Deng Tingmei ran after dogs and chickens, cursing and beating them chaotically (將雞狗亂打亂罵). When Jia Yicai’s 叚一才 (1736) “illness flared up, he would become all dumb and crazy and run about wildly” (病發起來就痴痴癲癲 胡走亂跑了). $ ^{33} $ This frenzied behavior could include violence against other people. A few days before he killed his father, Shan Dou had attacked a woman from the neighborhood. $ ^{34} $ Zhan Sanzai had assaulted a beggar. $ ^{35} $ Jixiang 吉祥 (1816)—a Manchu soldier garrisoned in the capital—and his family had been evicted by their landlord because, in sudden states of mental confusion, he had often threatened to use his knife (心發糊塗 常要動刀). $ ^{36} $

Wildly “dancing about” for no reason was among the most commonly recorded acts, probably because many killings took place when relatives or other people tried to stop the dancing. Just before he killed someone, Ding Yuanlong 丁元澹 (1816) had been “dancing about and screaming wildly” (亂舞 □裡亂嚷). $ ^{37} $ Mo Daoshou 莫導受 was dancing about in the fields, trampling vegetables (在田跳舞 毀踏菜蔬). Ji Wenyou 紀汶有 (1889) became crazy and swayed wildly with a metal axe (鐵斧). $ ^{38} $ Many reports recount that the killer was still dancing above his victim after committing his act.

Many who flayed and swayed were seeing demonic or human aggressors. One day during a fit, Tian Zhi 田治 (1816) had “his eyes wide open and said that someone was

## PDF第543页

shouting at him. He came out and ran around for a while and then he was all right” (瞪著眼睛 說有人罵他 出來跑了一會 好了). $ ^{39} $ Yang Ying explained that on the morning of the day he killed his wife and two daughters, his wife had opened the window. When he saw his brothers taking care of newly cut wheat (麥子) with hay cutters outside, “I suddenly became anxious and confused.... I saw 4 or 5 people entering the room to kill me (小的一時心裡煩悶 就迷糊了···見有四五人進屋要殺小的). $ ^{40} $ And Niu Dafu 牛大幅 (1816): “I only heard that there was someone calling my name, wanting to kill me. I became even more confused and I wanted to hack the person who wanted to kill me. I don’t know how, I hacked to death my wife, née Xu.” $ ^{41} $ (只聽有人叫小的姓名 要把小的殺死 小的越發糊塗 要砍殺小的的人 不知怎樣把女人徐氏砍死了) A man believed his mother was a tiger. $ ^{42} $ Two brothers who killed their mother claimed that they wanted her to “leave her ordinary body and rise to the heavens” (脫凡胎昇天) with them. $ ^{43} $

Many people tried to commit suicide during their fits. Late in 1885, Wu Hongfa (1891) tried to cut his throat. Tian Zhi (1816) injured himself with scissors, whereas Yang Wenhe 楊文河 (1816) injured his head and face jumping down a small cliff during a burst of insanity caused by a febrile illness. $ ^{44} $ A trace that sudden suicide was often attributed to madness is that after 1732, relatives, neighbors, and local leaders were all to be punished if a madman under their responsibility committed suicide.

## PDF第544页

A commonly reported symptom of madness was “rambling to oneself” (自言自語). Han Chun 韓春 (1816) “got madness-confusion disorder and often rambled to himself” (得了瘋迷病症 時常自言自語).⁴⁵ This kind of behavior occurred during non-violent episodes or during the fits that led to killings. Song Yan 宋諺 (1891) was found “rambling to himself” on his kang bed with his dead wife by his side. Chen Zhongyou 陳中有 fell to the ground, “rambling to himself,” after he was hit by the people he had attacked.⁴⁶

As I pointed out above, homicide reports rarely recorded the content of "mad language." Even depositions described mad talking with expressions such as "upside-down speech" 語言顛倒, "incoherent speech" 語無倫次, "crazy words" 瘋話, "mad speech" 狂言, and "reckless speech" 妄言. Ren Ni 任妮 (1816) was described as "staring straight with a dumb expression; his speech was jumbled ('upside down') and he chaotically spoke mad words" (目瞪神呆 還是言語顛倒 亂說瘋話). $ ^{47} $ These phrases seem to have meant that the people identified as mad could utter nothing intelligible, but we shall see in Chapters 14 and 15 that they often said much that was coherent. Expressions like "upside-down speech" and "crazy words" also do not imply that only utterly delirious people were identified as mad. Although officials rarely cared to record what a mad person had said months before committing a killing, they did not hesitate to take detailed notes on a mad man's words when these words were relevant to the case under trial. $ ^{48} $

## PDF第545页

### 13.3 “Catching madness”: the onset of insanity

Because the kind of madness discussed in homicide cases was by definition so brusquely obvious, people could easily pinpoint when it had started and identify the circumstances that had led to the first fits. When no explanation was given, we get the impression that madness just happened—an unpredictable catastrophe that befell the ill-fated. Some claimed to have been intermittently mad “since childhood” (從幼), but most traced their affliction to specific occasions. $ ^{49} $

Readings and conversations with specialists of Chinese religion had made me suspect that most Chinese must have attributed madness to possession, but the way in which commoners explained their own madness in their depositions was often surprisingly social and personal. The following passages should convince us that ordinary Chinese people did not always attribute madness to possession.

In contrast with medical texts, which usually focused on organic processes, most people cited in legal reports attributed madness to an extreme emotional experience. Some became crazy from fright, Men Fa 門發 (1816) because he had almost drowned in his buffaloes' water pit, Bai Shengqi 白升奇 (1751) because his boat had capsized. $ ^{50} $ A physician was said to have gone mad because other doctors had mocked his failures. $ ^{51} $ Many reported falling ill because they had lost a young child. Bannerman Jixiang (1816) became crazy in 1815 soon after his son was killed by the collapse of a wall in the Beijing house where they lived: "I often missed my son, then I got mucus-confusion disorder" (我時常想念兒子

## PDF第546页

就得了痰迷病症). $ ^{52} $ Han Chun (1816) was 48 sui and worked as a hired laborer when his young daughter died in 1814. His grief was so profound that it turned into madness (悲痛過切 得了瘋迷病症). $ ^{53} $ Lin Zhigong 林志功 started acting strangely after his wife and young son both died of illness in 1735. $ ^{54} $ Jia Chandou's son died in 1730. As his father explained, Chandou's son "had poxes and died. [The] Qi [of grief] confused his heart and he got a feng [madness] illness" (出痘死了 氣迷了心 就得了一個瘋病). $ ^{55} $ In 1760, a few years after Chai Shijin's 柴世進 wife had died, his only surviving son fell into a river and drowned. He "missed his son so much that [his grief] turned into madness illness" (他想念兒子 致成瘋病). $ ^{56} $ Liu Yuhou's 劉裕後 father and younger brother made a living operating a wooden raft on the Yellow River (在黃河運放木筏營生). In 1738 their raft was crushed by the current and they drowned. Liu, who could not even find the bodies, cried day and night until he "got madness illness, with intermittent fits" (得了瘋病 時發時止). $ ^{57} $

Business failure and financial strain were common sources of madness. $ ^{58} $ Relatives reported that Yao Zeng 姚增 (1777) lost his mind because he had lost money he had invested (因虧本歇業 一時失志). $ ^{59} $ Despite renting out part of his house to three people

## PDF第547页

from “inside the pass,” Tian Zhi (1816) feared his family would not make it through the Manchurian winter: “I had a lot of debts and no money to pay them. Come the autumn’s cold, my wife and sons would have no clothes to wear. All I did was worry. Then I got ‘madness-confusion disorder, with intermittent fits’” (小的因欠人賬多沒錢償還一到秋凉 女人兒子又沒衣穿只是發愁就得了瘋迷病症時發時好). Niu Dafu (1816) went out to sell cotton with his wife’s brother, but when they counted their money they realized they had not even regained their investment (並沒本銀). He tried to collect outstanding debts, but since “nobody returned money, I became angry and my mind became confused.” (都不還錢 小的氣惱心迷)

Li Hong 李洪 (1891), a “guest resident” (客民) in Shanxi, was often depressed because of poverty (因家裏窮苦時常愁悶). One day he felt oppressed at heart (心中發悶) and asked his associate to finish the accounts for him (代將帳目算畢). As he was resting, he suddenly became mad. Zhang Keming 張克明 had left Shanxi to do commerce (貿易) in neighboring Henan. $ ^{61} $ His wife, younger cousin, and neighbors explained that soon after he returned home a few years later to till the soil (perhaps upon the death of his parents), he was “allocated” more than twenty mu of land (旋將分受地二十餘亩), which he sold for 300 ounces of silver. Because he lost it all after lending it out, he became so dejected that his glumness turned into madness (放債圖利 因折本憂鬱致成瘋疾 時發時愈). Zhang’s own explanation differed: “I have studied since I was a child. I have read the Yijing, but because its meaning and doctrines are too profound and subtle, I could not get to the bottom of it. Instead I suffered from a bout of illness. Only after a year of constant learning

## PDF第548页

did I come to understand it (自幼讀書 平日看過易經 因義理深奧 摸不著根子

倒害了一場病 終年摩熟才曉得) $ ^{62} $

Other reported causes of madness included the taking of medical drugs. According to his father, one man from Beijing who had been mad “since childhood” often became confused because he had taken too much cooling medicine (自幼得有瘋病 吃涼藥太多因此常發糊塗), $ ^{63} $ he committed suicide in 1902. One could also contract madness from another illness, as Yang Wenhe (1816) from a febrile disease he called “Cold injury.” $ ^{64} $ He jumped into a cliff and hurt his head and face. But attribution of insanity to the intake of drugs was extremely rare in both medical and legal documents.

### 13.4 Recourses

In China as elsewhere, until recently it was the community (relatives, neighbors, classmates, etc.) that judged which individuals were abnormal enough to be called “mad,” and it was families themselves that handled their mad members. $ ^{65} $ In the absence of relatives, insane people were probably reduced to an itinerant and destitute life. Perhaps some temples or charitable institutions had a tradition of harboring such men, but I have found no textual record of such activities. As these were not primarily medical institutions, they may not have provided lasting custody and treatment. At no time did people called mad fall under the care of specialists like the “alienists” of Victorian England. This is not to say they were never treated by specialists. They were, but not by specialists of madness.

## PDF第549页

Since popular perception (and even medicine) often attributed insane behavior to possession, ritual healing was a common treatment for those who acted strangely or in violent fits. People could seek the help of Daoist priests (道士), specialists who possessed spells, amulets, and a variety of techniques to counteract curses or drive out demons. $ ^{66} $ One Daoist invoked Guandi to “save” (救) Lin Zhigong. $ ^{67} $ When Lin Shiyuan suddenly went crazy in 1762 and “spoke and danced about madly” (狂言跳舞), his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪). She sent a relative to invite a Daoist priest to chant scriptures and to implore deities (誦經祇禳). As Lin himself said: “They [Lin’s relatives] all said I was crazy, so they called a Daoist priest to treat me” (他們都說我瘋癲叫道士替我醫治). $ ^{68} $

The cases of Lin Zhigong and Lin Shiyuan, who had become mad after the death of a child, shows that people could attribute madness to possession even when they had also a social or emotional cause to insanity. A woman suspected that her son Kong Yunjiu 孔雲久, who spoke strangely (語言不經), slept all day, and ate less than usual after he had fallen into water and almost drowned himself, was afflicted by a demonic curse (疑為遇祟). She made an older son organize a ceremony of propitiation called jiao (“grand sacrifice”) (設醮祈保).

## PDF第550页

Many people experienced their own affliction as the result of possession and believed that exorcisms acted against the root of their disorder. Physician Cheng 程從周 Congzhou (fl. 1640s) heard that monks (沙彌) were chanting scriptures at the home of a patient he had been invited to cure. His patient's relatives were also burning incense and paper money (mingxi 冥資: "spectral currency" or "netherworldly funds"). In his treatment of a few possession cases in the early nineteenth century, Li Wenrong 李文榮 (b. 1772) adopted a mix of drugs and exorcistic methods that included the organization of a ghost-feeding ceremony. $ ^{70} $

People who lived in south China could also take their mad relative to be “put on trial” in a temple to the Emperor of the Eastern Peak (東嶽廟) or at a City God temple (see Katz 2009: 105-7, who calls these rituals “trials of the insane” 審瘋子). One of a few “penitentiary rites that mimic the judicial system” (Katz 2009: 105), these ceremonies tried either the mad person or the possessing ghost. The latter was often the soul of a dead person who had been wronged by the insane man or woman in this life on in a previous one. Such rituals targeted those karmically driven ghosts that physicians usually admitted they could not extirpate by medical means (see 8.5 and previous paragraph).

But a fairly large number of ordinary people also consulted physicians or simply bought drugs. $ ^{71} $ A woman from Guizhou used the service of a “passing doctor” (過路醫生) to treat her son. $ ^{72} $ A woman from Liaodong sent her eldest son (who was 17 sui and lived in his employer’s place 8 li away from his parents’ house) to a nearby settlement to fetch a

## PDF第551页

physician to treat her husband Tian Zhi, who had suddenly become crazy. $ ^{73} $ All the witnesses cited agreed that the doctor cured (治好) Tian with a mucus-transforming remedy.

During trials, doctors testified to having used pulse diagnostics, explained the etiology of madness in medical terms, and prescribed the same emetic or purgative drugs that were recommended in medical treatises. In three cases I cited in 7.3 (pp. 284-86), the pills used were purgatives or emetics designed for quick improvement of the patient's condition. In all cases, the family only bought one dose. Buying ready-made pills or standard decoctions cost less than inviting physicians who would prescribe long courses of remedies made with potentially expensive drugs. Some consulted specialists in acupuncture or moxibustion. $ ^{74} $ Many changed their approach when one strategy had failed. Wang Xianbi 王獻璧, a self-taught medical healer, had first taken drugs to treat his own “madness,” but when the remedies proved ineffective he sought the help of a wandering monk who recited Buddhist scriptures next to him for three days until he recovered. $ ^{75} $

Out of fear, poverty, or helplessness, some families simply chained up their mad relatives for the duration of their fits. Confinement meant restraint, not cure—families simply waited for the fits to end. I have already explained in 11.4 how some mad people were confined at home even though families did not declare them to authorities as a rule promulgated in 1732 and made more severe in 1766 had mandated. $ ^{76} $ I found no physician who advocated the confinement of a mad patient.

## PDF第552页

When mad men were the heads of their families, they had to be attended to as family heads. This task was often a wife's responsibility. Biographies of virtuous wives often presented the care that some women provided to their mad husbands as brave sacrifices. $ ^{77} $ A woman née Feng, the daughter of a Shandong licentiate, was married at sixteen to a man surnamed Liu. After he “became ill with madness” (病瘋癲) three years later, she served him food and medicine compliantly and indefatigably for seven years (氏蓬首垢面躬侍飲食藥餌 七載不倦) until he died. She then committed suicide. So did a woman née Lin, who refused to remarry after her husband, who had suffered from madness for three years (患瘋三年), finally killed himself by jumping into a well. A woman surnamed Peng was registered in the county's registers of chaste wives (節婦清冊) for having served her mad husband. Soon after they had married, the man had became mad to the point of being unable to speak or move (患病癲狂不能言動) and he had never recovered. Despite his insanity, a magistrate reported, Madam Peng still served him and his relatives for 35 years without complaining. $ ^{78} $

Isolated incidents indicate that some mad people were left to wander freely, though some may have become free by escaping detention. $ ^{79} $ Others may have been chased away from home, and many may have been among the growing number of men who could not find a wife in late-imperial times, and therefore had no family to take care of them. One

## PDF第553页

evening in 1815, Hua Sanyuan 華三沅 and two friends were keeping watch over ripe melons in their fields. Suddenly a naked man ran toward them, laughing and cursing, waving his two arms wildly (兩手亂舞), “looking crazy” (狀屬瘋顛). He grabbed Hua and spat at him. In the struggle that followed, the madman (who was about 40 s/i old) was injured and died. $ ^{80} $

In a macabre case that took place in Fujian in 1736, a man who was described in the trials as “having suffered from madness for a long time” (素患瘋癲) broke into a house in the middle of the night and was killed by the house owner. Instead of reporting the case, the dead man’s wife agreed with the killer that she would keep silent if he gave her 15 ounces of silver in “burial fees.” The victim’s brother later tried to obtain even more money from the killer, who had no choice but to comply. Altogether, he paid 75 ounces of silver until the madman’s wife and brother were discovered and punished.

### 13.5 Mucus and “mucus confusion” outside medicine

Coming in, Doctor Wang saw a large company of people. Busily he came forward, saluting the Lady Dowager. He took Baoyu's hand and palpated it for some time. Zijuan [a maid] could not help lowering her head. Not understanding the meaning of this gesture, Doctor Wang rose and talked: "The ancients once said that there were distinct forms of mucus confusion. There is mucus confusion because food and drinks cannot be transformed due to weakening and softening of Qi and Blood, there is confusion because a mucus pocket is created in anger, and there is blockage caused by intense pain. Our case here is also a mucus-confusion disorder: it was brought about by intense pain. But it is only temporary obstruction. Compared with the other forms of mucus confusion, it is relatively minor." The Lady Dowager said: "Just tell us if we should be worried. Who wants to recite medical books with you?"

## PDF第554页

81 Xiao Shi 蕭奭, Yongxian Lu 永憲錄 (completed in 1752), an unofficial book that chronicled important political events that took place between 1722 and 1727 (Zhonghua shuju ed.: 247). The text was presented as Nian Gengyao's own confession, preceded by the confession of a diviner who had helped foster Nian's ambitions by predicting he would overthrow the Qing. The confession format seems authentic, but the content may not have been.

82 Wilt Idema (1977: 42) has observed that medical explanations in some novels were "pure nonsense" that "helped] characterize the professional as a quack." Here, the quack effect did not come from the use of medical balderdash, but from the contrast between a pedantic medical explanation and what the readers (and

Nian Gengyao imputed his misplaced imperial aspirations to the action of mucus, whereas in Dream of Red Mansions, a highly realistic eighteenth-century novel, a physician explained the main character Jia Baoyu's affliction as "mucus confusion" produced by pain and anguish. In his lecture to Baoyu's relatives about the many ways in which mucus could muddle the heart, doctor Wang made distinctions worthy of his real counterparts. $ ^{82} $ The fact that mucus was often found in the mouth of fictional physicians perhaps hinted that mucus was still considered a specialized explanation of unconscious acts or downcast moods, but neither in fiction nor in reality was mucus lingo the monopoly of doctors.

Nian Gengyao 年羹堯, on trial for treason [1726], trying to explain why he had once said that white fumes (白氣) he had seen were signs that he would

是邪祟所憑

不知是病是瘋 及至今日 回想這些信口胡言 竟是痰迷心竅 或

I don’t know if I was ill or mad. When today I recall these senseless words, [I think] it must have been mucus confusing the openings of my heart, or else my being possessed by an evil specter. $ ^{81} $

Dream of Red Mansions 紅樓夢, ch. 57

王太醫進來見許多的人 忙上去請了賈母的安 拿了寶玉的手診了一回 那紫鵑少不得低了頭 王大夫也不解何意 起身說道 世兄這症乃是急痛迷心古人曾云 痰迷有别 有氣血虧柔 飲食不能熔化痰迷者 有怒惱中痰裏而迷者 有急痛壅塞者 此亦痰迷之症 係急痛所致 不過一時壅蔽 較諸痰迷似輕 賈母道 你只說怕不怕 誰同你背藥書呢

13. Life trials

## PDF第555页

I showed above that the use in judicial reports of medical terms like diankuang 顛狂 and fengdian 風癲 did not indicate that the people who used these words adopted the medical explanations that could accompany this terminology. The exception is the language of mucus, which I showed was also used in legal records, including in depositions by witnesses. Unlike diankuang and fengkuang 風狂, which were only labels to refer to madness or mad behavior in general, mucus already hinted at an explanation of insanity. I show in this last section that the mucus explanation of madness was very widespread not only in medical circles, but also among educated people—and probably much more widely—in the Qing period. To prove this point, I have to show that expressions using “mucus” were more than figures of speech.

The expression “mucus confusing the openings of the heart” could probably be used by people who were not concerned whether mucus was the agent of medical confusion, as English speakers use the term “melancholy” even when they do not attribute gloomy moods to black bile. This could be the case in chapter 50 of Jinghua yuan 鏡花緣 (an early nineteenth-century novel) when a man pleaded with bandits to excuse his words, which he said had been caused by “mucus confusing the openings of the heart after drinking too much” (剛纔只因多飲幾杯 痰迷心竅 酒後失言). In Dream of Red Mansions, one character told another: “mucus has confused your heart, grease has obscured your openings” (你痰迷了心 脂油蒙了竅)! And in Another Dream of Red Mansions (Honglou fumeng 紅樓復夢; which bears a preface from 1798), a woman who had given birth less than a month earlier grabbed a stick and started beating everyone around her because “Blood had rushed upwards and mucus confused the openings of her heart” (血往上衝 痰迷心竅).

## PDF第556页

These turns of phrase did not necessarily denote a belief that a substance called mucus had really obstructed the “openings” of a person’s heart, but enough examples from a number of fields suggest that mucus talk was not semantically empty. The hero’s mother in The Scholars was so thrilled to see that so many pieces of richly decorated ceramics belonged to her now that her son passed had the official examination, that she “laughed out loud and fell backwards. Suddenly mucus surged up and she lost consciousness” (大笑一聲 往後便跌倒 忽然痰湧上來 不醒人事).⁸⁴ A passage in chapter 12 described the same process: “unexpectedly, he had a serious fit of his mucus illness; when he ascended [received his examination degree], it struck the viscera, and he lost consciousness” (不想痰病大發 登時中了臟 已不醒人事了). Fan Jin 範進, one of the main characters in the same novel, woke up in a place to which he did not remember having traveled. Many neighbors congratulated him on passing the examinations and explained to him that his joy “had somewhat stirred up mucus” (有些引動了痰) and that “now that he had vomited a few mouthfuls of mucus, he was all right” (方纔吐出幾口痰來 好了).⁸⁵

Disorders that caused discomfort in the chest (including cardio-vascular disorders) were easily experienced as the doing of mucus. The Jiaqing Emperor (r. 1796-1820) died on the evening of September 2, 1820 at the age of fifty-nine, one day after he had felt “discomforted” (不豫) upon arriving in the imperial summer residence of Rehe. $ ^{86} $ A

## PDF第557页

“bequeathed edict” (遗招) that was probably composed by officials present at his bedside recounted in the first person how the emperor had felt.

My body has always been sturdy and I have never been sick. Although I am now more than six decades old [in sui 歲], I feel no fatigue when I scale [mountains and trek across] plains. [But] during this journey, I was occasionally affected by summer heat. Yesterday, I was still spurring my horse across the Guangren Mountains [west of Rehe], but upon arriving at the Mountain Estate I felt mucus Qi [or "mucus and Qi"] rise and cause congestion. By this evening, my condition has worsened. I am afraid I may be unable to recuperate.

朕體素壯 未嘗疾病 雖年逾六旬 登陟川原 不覺其勞 此次蹕途 偶感

喝暑 昨仍策馬度廣仁嶺 迨抵山莊 覺痰氣上壅 至夕益甚 恐弗克瘳

No matter who wrote this account, it seems to be an authentic expression of how someone may have experienced this kind of discomfort. Congestion (壅) in “I felt mucus Qi rise and cause congestion” referred to a feeling of congestion in the chest, as well as to the way in which mucus was imagined to cause blockage. $ ^{87} $ The emperor’s symptoms coincided with those of “heat stroke” (中署, the Summer Heat that the Rehe retreat was designed to escape), a disorder that physicians of the time commonly attributed to mucus.

A number of legal reports spoke of “mucus confusion.” A memorial from 1656

announced that the Vice Commissioner of Daliang Circuit had “contracted a mucus illness,

and all the openings of his heart are confused” (感患痰症心竅俱迷), and that he would

take time to recover. $ ^{88} $ In the case of Feng Qiyan 馮起炎, which took place in 1783: “This

## PDF第558页

criminal became poorer and more troubled day by day. His stasis ["depression"] turned into an illness and he contracted 'confusion by mucus.' In the seventh month of Qianlong 47 [1782], he jumped from the city wall and hurt his leg falling. $ ^{89} $ (該犯日益貧難 抑鬱成病得有痰迷之症 曾於四十七年七月間 跳墮城牆 將腿跌傷) Finally, a terse entry in a body of administrative regulations explained that since the conduct of an unnamed juren 舉人 was caused by "mucus confusion," he should not be deprived of his degree. $ ^{90} $

“Confusion by mucus” was even said to have a certain appearance. In the course of his interrogation (probably under torture), Liang Sanchuan's 梁三川 “eyes stared blankly, his words were incoherent: he looked as if confused by mucus” (两目直视 語無倫次 狀類痰迷). $ ^{91} $ In 1768, a commoner thus described a home teacher he had hired: “When I see Xu Ding, his language is sometimes a bit confused: this looks like [the doing of] mucus Qi” (平素見徐鼎語言偶然有些恍惚 像是痰氣).

Writers of legal documents often used the mucus approach to explain how people could become mad enough to commit violent crimes. One official reasoned that “killing because of madness should be considered as a crime entirely devoid of criminal intention” because people with madness illness are confused by mucus [and thus] act insanely; they cannot control themselves” (蓋以瘋病之人 痰迷顛狂 不自主持). $ ^{92} $ One provincial judge also referred to mucus in a memorial from 1754:

## PDF第559页

As for people with madness illness: when [their illness] flares up, they become confused by mucus and their acts are insane. Upon more investigation, we find that there is more than one type of madness illness. In one case, when it flares up, people become wild and try to hurt others with objects or their hands and feet; in another case, they simply speak nonsense: mucus confuses the openings of the heart so that one is unaware of people and things. The severity of the illness varies in each case. $ ^{93} $

原瘋病之人 舉發痰迷 行動顛狂 再查瘋病之類不一 有舉發猖狂 隨執器物手足傷人者 有止語言顛倒 痰迷心竅不醒人事者 病患之輕重各有不同

The official who made such a clear distinction between wild acts and nonsensical speech (a distinction rarely considered relevant in medical treatises) ascribed both to mucus.

A rare first-person testimony I cited in Chapter 11 (p. 452) indicates that madness could even be experienced as being caused by mucus. It seems that the man in question genuinely perceived his affliction as having been caused by the flaring up of mucus, though it is difficult to determine whether he developed these views only during interrogation or after he was diagnosed by a physician as suffering from mucus confusion. $ ^{94} $

The problem of how patients experience illness has long occupied anthropologists and historians of medicine. $ ^{95} $ Although leaping from written expressions to “experience” is notoriously difficult, the above passages suggest that many people (not just doctors) attributed seizures, fits of madness, and even the confusion that came with old age to the action of mucus. In any case, it is clear that the expression “mucus confusion” that we find

## PDF第560页

in so many late-imperial texts was a living metaphor, not the lexical remnant of a dead

conception.

### 13.6 Conclusion

It would take painstaking research to trace how the mucus etiology of madness made its way from elite medical texts into popular literature and common parlance. The simplicity of the way in which mucus created illness (and the therapeutic correlate of this conception: anti-mucus therapies) certainly helped, and so did the presence of this straightforward mucus script in the popular medical works that found such a ready market in late imperial times (see 7.3). Since the core of the mucus script could be tacked onto the most complex doctrines concerning the origin of mucus, even physicians who presented themselves as sophisticated did not sneer at the seemingly simple mucus etiology.

In this chapter, I looked for accounts of madness in documents that sought to confirm the legal definition of madness by finding it in real homicide cases. Partly for documentary reasons, I concentrated on the way common people explained violent fits of madness. This kind of madness was plainly noticeable to all, and people proposed a range of both social and supernatural explanations for its origins. I also showed that there were many ways of handling mad people: confinement for the duration of fits, the purchasing of drugs, the reading of sutras, elaborate exorcistic ceremonies, and so on. But violent outbursts were not the only kind of behavior that people designated as “mad.” In later chapters, I shall explore the realm of mad speech, which homicide reports almost entirely neglected.

Since a family could seek the service of different kinds of specialists to treat the same person, we know that an identified cause did not necessarily give rise to a particular treatment. A naturalistic explanation did not imply blamelessness, and emotional etiologies—

## PDF第561页

—loss of a child, great fright caused by a near-death experience—did not necessarily lead to secular therapies. Local relations were complex enough not to be reducible to logical deductions like “she is acting this way because of mucus, therefore we shall not see her as morally guilty.”

However estranged they were by their odd behavior, most mad people kept living in the social circles in which they had been born. Madness may have led to failure to fulfill certain duties—tilling the fields, having children, passing examinations—but it rarely led to one's permanent removal from circles of social solidarity like the family. Even mad men could have a family and even remarry when their first wife had died.

We know little about the way people themselves understood and “scripted” their own sufferings. Legal documents contain depositions by mad people explaining their own sufferings, but to an extent that varies from case to case, these explanations were re-scripted to fit the pre-determined models by which the law expected killings committed “because of madness” to unfold. Moreover, the fact that reports concentrated on homicides obscured the array of scenarios that “mad people” enacted in daily situations that did not lead to crimes, including in contexts where they were not identified as insane.

In the next chapter, I use depositions given by mad people in non-homicide cases and texts written by “insane people” themselves to attempt to reconstruct what it was like to be identified as mad in Qing China.

## PDF第562页

## 14 

## Distress and power

Being (considered) mad in Qing China

I emulate Guandi, behaving loyally and uprightly; therefore I style myself the Great King of Shanxi.

我學關帝做人忠直 所以自稱山西大王的

Lin Zhigong 林志功 (1762)

Because it imagines power, madness is both impotence and omnipotence.

Roy Porter (1987b: 39)

Meaning is inescapable: that is to say, illness always has meaning.

Arthur Kleinman (1988: 144)

In Chapter 13 I explored the ways in which ordinary people treated their insane relatives, described and explained their madness, and coped with their disorder. Here I try to view the situation from the point of view of the men (I have no data on women) who were considered mad. Again, my main source of information is legal documents, “palace memorials” that reported on crimes that the authorities considered serious. Since these crimes were committed through speech, writing, and fantasy, officials tended to record more details about the words and fancies of the “criminals” involved than in homicide cases.

The plight of the eighteenth-century people I study in this chapter was not purely private anguish, the inner torments of the self, madness as “the most solitary of afflictions.” $ ^{1} $ Much of their vexation arose from being taken as mad, an inflexible diagnosis they fervently

## PDF第563页

disputed. The little that has survived of their voices was recorded during trials and in a few texts they had written that were seized during criminal investigations. It may be impossible to understand their outlook on life, but these texts still give us the best glimpse we will ever get on the world of people that their contemporaries considered mad.

Unlike the “madmen” that official homicide reports described, those I discuss here and in Chapter 15 were quite “aware of human affairs.” “Unaware of human affairs” (不醒人事) was the state of mind in which a “killer” had to be for Qing law to classify his homicidal act as “killing because of madness.” Lucid most of the time, the people I discuss here could interact with people and express complex opinions about their world. The writings and confessions that I use to reconstruct these views also give us a more concrete understanding of what was taken as “mad language” in eighteenth-century China.

This chapter does not explain the functioning of madness in any particular field (though the Qing judicial system is constantly in the background), but focuses instead of various aspects of what it meant to be considered “mad” in eighteenth-century China. I first analyze the main grievances that people who were treated as mad expressed and what these tell us about the way they were treated. The second section shows examples of how they constructed personas that others interpreted as crazy. My last section is devoted to the case of Lin Zhigong 林志功, a peculiar man who wrote several texts in which he spoke of key events in his life in a way that sounded both poignant and utterly crazy. As Roy Porter said, I do not study these people’s unconscious, but what they said (or wrote) about themselves and others, about the way they were treated, and about the symbols that struck them. This chapter should be read along with the next one, which presents more of “mad” people’s voices but focuses on the way officials interpreted their words and writings. Both chapters are about self-aggrandizement, writing, and power.

## PDF第564页

### 14.1 Grievances and reproaches

As we have seen, some individuals in Chinese villages were identified as madmen (瘋子) or fools (痴子). More than a simple act of naming, this identification was a diagnosis sustained by the community—close relatives, neighbors, classmates, etc., who probably thought they were simply stating the obvious. "The local community called [Yang Huaizhen] a madman and stopped interacting with him" (鄉黨呼為瘋子 不與往來). $ ^{2} $

Too much is missing from these people's local worlds to understand the difficulties they faced, the way people related to them daily, and the way their treatment differed from the way other poor people were treated. But some themes recur. The commonest grievance I found in mad people's statements was a disagreement with this informal but persistent identification as mad or idiotic. Some people emphasized that this diagnosis came from others. Deng Tingmei 鄧廷梅 (1736): "My son had poxes and died. At once I became so angry that my mind became confused. My ears also became deaf. They said I had gone mad." $ ^{3} $ (出痘死了 一時把心氣成昏迷 耳朵也氣韜了 他們說我瘋了) Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (1753), whose trial I discuss in 15.4, claimed to have been designated the Son of Heaven by the descendant of Confucius on orders of Shangdi the Lord on High, a title Ding's relatives did not recognize: "[My older brother] is an unreasonable person; he often calls me a fool" (他是不明道理的人 常罵我癡子). $ ^{4} $ And elsewhere: "I am not a fool, damn it! At home everybody says I'm a fool." (並不是癡子 可恨 家裏人人說我是癡子) "Fools" (癡子) were not necessarily persons of low intelligence. Those called "fools"

## PDF第565页

were often people who harbored fantasies that their abilities or positions would never allow them to fulfill (as in the expression "the fool who speaks his dream" 痴人說夢). $ ^{5} $ This was the case of "Great King of Shanxi" Lin Zhigong 林志功 (1762): "everybody says I am a fool and nobody believes me" (人人說我是痴子都不信我). $ ^{6} $

The fact that protests did not change their neighbors' and relatives' opinions probably vindicated or intensified some people's sense of injustice. $ ^{7} $ Lin Shiyuan's 林時元 statement was vehement: "In the third month of last year [1762], I suffered from some dizziness. They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it.

People whom others considered mad often objected to the way their relatives beat them, ignored them, wronged them, locked them up, and (maybe above all else) called them what they insisted they were not: mad. Any rough treatment received under the pretext that they were insane probably intensified their feelings of injustice. Lin Zhigong wrote in a poem that "turning human relations upside-down, the younger brother beats his elder" (顛倒人倫弟打兄). He Cheng'ao's 何成敖 words (1817), recorded after his arrest, denoted distress and a confused sense of grievance: "From childhood I haven't been able to untie this knot

## PDF第566页

and it has wronged me and entangled me (?) It is I who killed uncle He Dongrun. Actually this is an injustice. Right now, I am also being wronged." (我叫何成敖 我自幼解结不清把我冤枉缠住了 叔子何東潤是我打死的 實是冤枉 我今也是冤枉)

None, as far as I know, protested against medicine. The difference in the treatment received in Western asylums and that received from Chinese physicians can probably explain this lack of protest: whereas internment in an English asylum often meant a prolonged regimen of vomits and purges (even for completely lucid “mad people,” who, when they protested, were confirmed in their condition), treatment by Chinese physicians was not institutional and was most of the time quite short: one or a few doses of an emetic or a purgative often sufficed to conclude to failure or success. Until the very late nineteenth century, no one in Qing China was interned in public asylums where they might be mistreated by strangers or administered medicine against their will. The life of mad people was neither significantly medicalized nor institutionalized. This does not mean they lived an easy and happy life. Mistreatments were simply of a different sort. $ ^{9} $

### 14.2 Recuperating symbols: virtue, emperorship, and cosmic destinies

Both madness and literature can be understood, historically, as ways of producing the world.... Madness is a pathology that derives, in part from the capacity for literature.

Alan Thither (1999: 322)

“When madness starts to manifest itself.... one considers oneself to be elevated or of superior virtue, to be shrewd and wise, or to be of high rank; one acts in a conceited manner.”

狂之始發···自高賢也自辨智也自貴倨也

The Canon of Problems ( $ 1^{st} $ century CE), problem 59

## PDF第567页

Many people who were identified as mad constructed a superlative persona that made them glorious and excellent. Either before they were socially diagnosed as crazy or as a reaction to this label, they spoke of their dreams and visions and often sought official or imperial recognition for their mission or their virtue. Here I use a few cases to illustrate what a “crazy” persona could be like.

Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $

Bai Shengqi 白升奇 was born in Xianning 咸寧 (modern Xi'an, Shaanxi) in 1724 and had acquired some literacy when he was young, but soon "abandoned books for commerce" (棄書貿易), said the official report. $ ^{13} $ Since his case is better documented, let me recount at some length what happened to him and what compelled him to seek the help of Qing officials to justify himself. On his way south to Sichuan to sell pottery (販賣瓷器) in 1751, his boat had capsized and he had been greatly frightened. While traveling back home on the Han river, he had lost his belongings and his luggage (將衣物行李遺失). This loss was either the first sign of his madness or the event that triggered it. When Bai returned, his father confined him at home because he thought his son was mad.

## PDF第568页

Bai remained confined for years, but when his father died of illness in 1760, his mother "slightly slackened his confinement" (關鎖稍懈). In the late summer of 1761, he asked his ten-sui daughter—was she born after he had "become mad"?—to give him a bamboo rake and a bamboo pole (竹爬一柄、竹竿一根) and later a knife. The child gave him a sickle (!), with which he cut the bamboo into strips (劈碎削成竹簽). Responding to more of her father's requests, she also gave him a brush, an ink stone, and some slips of scrap paper (殘帖廢紙). On the strips and slips, Bai wrote the names of people he knew and the names of official posts (官職), sometimes next to the names of his acquaintances. On the two pieces of paper with which he wrapped 211 bamboo strips and 25 slips of paper, he wrote, "to be delivered to the Shaanxi provincial treasurer." Having managed to escape undetected, Bai went to find his old acquaintance, shopkeeper Yang Xuan 楊選. Since Yang was gone, Bai, claiming to be surnamed Li 立, left the two packages to a man who was responsible for "making fire at Yang Xuan's store" (於楊選舖內燒火) and instructed the man to deliver these urgent documents to the treasurer's office.

When Yang Xuan's employee did what Bai had told him to do, he was immediately arrested. Seeing names of official posts next to the names of unknown people, the provincial treasurer probably worried that the slips hid a rebellious plot or the seed of events for which he would be blamed if he did not handle this matter swiftly. Even more ominous was the phrase "fifth year of Liqi" (立契五年), which sounded like the name of an imperial reign period. Since the taking of a reign title was often the decisive step organized groups took when they decided to move into open rebellion, this case demanded a serious and thorough investigation, the first step of which was to identify the author of the mysterious slips.

## PDF第569页

The officials in charge first found Yang Xuan, the person to whom the slips were originally destined, but Yang did not know anyone called Li Ⅶ. He only knew two people called Bai:¹⁴ the head of a “community compact” (郷約) and “Bai the madman” (Bai fēngzi 白瘋子), who Yang claimed knew how to write. Governor Zhong Yin 鍾音 sent deputies to arrest Bai and search his house, where they found eleven bamboo strips and five slips of paper with the same handwriting as on the slips that had been delivered to the treasurer’s office. Bai Shengqi was then taken to the Governor’s complex, where, as was customary in cases of possible sedition, he was interrogated by the three highest officials in the province—the governor, the provincial judge, and the provincial treasurer.¹⁵

It turned out that Bai's motives for presenting the slips were far from those of a rebel. Asked what his intentions were (究屬何心), he answered as follows:

I lost my clothes and things in the Han River and no one pursued [this matter]. I have also been locked up for many years, which has been ghastly and difficult to bear. I wrote the names of people I know on bamboo strips and paper slips, and filled out the official posts, hoping to make them look for the lost objects for me. Because I do not see many people from year to year, I had [the slips] sent to the provincial treasurer's yamen, so that he could help me search the things and return them to me.

因在漢中遺失衣物 無人追究 兼被鎖禁多年 苦楚難堪 每將認識之人並寫竹簽紙條填以官職 欲令代找失物 又因每年不見眾人 交送布政司衙門即可為我查傳 教給返獲東西

The three provincial officials asked more specifically why he had written such things as “the fifth year of Liqi” and “Liqi prefectures and counties” (立契府縣).

## PDF第570页

I used to write contracts for people. On them I would write the two characters "Liqi" ("Establishing the agreement"). Formerly, I used to be called Bai Shengqi, but because everybody calls me Bai the madman, I changed my name to Bai Liqi. It is because they have been confining me for five or six years that I wrote "fifth year of Liqi" on the paper strips. As for "prefectures and counties".... this is something I just wrote offhandedly.

我曾替人寫過文約 上面俱寫「立契」二字 我從前原叫白升奇 因人都叫我白瘋子 我就改名立契 又被他們鎖禁有五六年 因此紙條上寫「立契五年」 那府縣名 □是我隨手潛寫的

To make sure Bai Shengqi was not lying or hiding other relevant details, the officials interrogated him under torture, but as they reported, all he said was that "all the strips and slips in the packages were the injustices [he had undergone and] that he wanted to submit and protest about. Otherwise his recorded statement is confused and upside-down, without any coherence." (包內簽條俱係伊的冤枉 急要伸訴 此外供詞 率多糊涂顛倒 毫無倫次) Bai Shengqi was eventually bastinadoed to death in the marketplace.

“Bai the madman” had made sense of his predicament with meaningful terms and semantically rich symbols. His words were not inane prattle, but their meaning was quite different for Bai than for the officials who condemned him. Bai could not have been unaware that “the fifth year of Liqi” sounded like a reign title. This title—and the phrase “prefectures and counties,” which he “wrote offhandedly”—make it appear as though he was not only extending his grievances (lost objects, years of home confinement), but was trying to compensate for them by draping himself in the trappings of imperial authority. We know too little about Bai to tell how much he “believed” in his (admittedly indirect) claims

## PDF第571页

to emperorship or if the self-aggrandizing “fifth year of Liqi” only sounded comforting in distressing times. $ ^{16} $

Zhang Tingyong 張廷用 also managed to sound politically subversive even as he constructed his "delirium" from daily objects and local names of people and organizations. $ ^{17} $ Of course his words were also designed to sound politically meaningful. As a youth, "Zhang Tingyong had participated in a [semi-annual local] examination to become a licentiate but had not been admitted. $ ^{18} $ This turned into madness/idiocy: he would constantly chant the Buddha's name; [the illness was] intermittent" (張廷用曾考試式童未能進取 染成瘋癡隨口念佛 時發時止). In 1748, having shaved his hair and queue (自剃髮辦), he went to Wutai Mountain to become a monk, but the bonzes in charge (當處僧人) made him return home. In 1756, when Zhang's illness became more serious, his family declared him to the county office through the head of the local community compact (經鄉約報官). The county issued chains and locks and delivered Zhang to his relatives, who were instructed to lock him up and keep watch over him (發給鎖封 交伊親屬鎖錮看守). $ ^{19} $ In 1760, he twisted his locks open and managed to flee to the city (扭鎖脫身 奔入城市), where he was captured by the magistrate's men. He was sent back home with new locks (仍發鎖封看守). The county government had files on both events (各有案).

In 1767, while everybody was working in the fields, Zhang broke his chains again and fled from Shanxi to Huaian 懷安 county in Zhili (near the Great Wall), carrying 10

## PDF第572页

manuscript notes that he may have written over the years or along the road (the report did not tell). He was caught again. As the magistrate in charge of his case reported:

The text is mediocre and pedestrian, preposterous and confused, constantly redundant and not forming full sentences. But among the words we find "White-rain dragon-king association," "Society of the Ten Buddhas," and the names of people in those associations, such as "Liu Guangrong," "Association Leader Cao," and "Association Leader Wang." I am very afraid that [these names] hide something else, such as [sutra?] reading associations.

閱其鄙俚誕妄迷係重複雷同 不成語句 惟字內載有白雨龍王會、十佛會及會中人劉光榮 曹會長 王會長名 甚恐有念□會別情

Symbolically loaded language made officials apprehensive. Zhang Tingyong's slips seemed to mention the kind of religious association that was often banned under the label of “devious cult” (邪教). Such religious groups were common in Qing China, but the state feared they might develop into large-scale organizations that could rival the state in their sway over “people’s hearts.” $ ^{20} $

The officials who interrogated the local leaders and the people Zhang Tingyong named in his notes soon found that there was no rebellion in the making. The “Society of the Ten Buddhas” was the name of a long-banned association that was named after ten days representing the birthdays of ten gods and Buddhas (神佛誕日 十項相傳). The report specified that “this association no longer exists” (現無此會). Liu Guangrong was the name of a “hearth-dwelling Taoist priest” (火居道士) who pleaded to gods in the name of those

## PDF第573页

who sought his services and chose auspicious days for marriages and funerals (代人酹神祈禱 并選擇婚葬日期), but practiced no occult arts.

The lofty-sounding “dragon-king association” turned out to be an agricultural cult and “white rain” the name local peasants gave to hailstones (冰雹). From the fourth to the eighth lunar month, the villagers went to the neighboring temple of the dragon spirit.... to burn incense and paper to [?] and pray (村農至附近龍神廟內□帶香褚靼流祈禱) that the hail would not harm the crops: “there are in fact no such things as gatherings of crowds to read sutras or the establishment of devious cults” (實無聚眾念經、創立邪教情事). The practice of presenting incense at the local dragon-god temple was in fact consistent with what the Code called “charitable societies that conduct prayers in the spring and fall” (在本村神龍廟靼月進香 與律載春秋義社以行祈报者相同 似無庸置議). $ ^{21} $

The Governor of Shanxi clarified small details: under interrogation in Zhili soon after he was caught, Zhang Tingyong had mentioned the existence of “one effortless knife kept by xianzi [i.e., a sage]” (顺刀一把是賢子收著). The “knife kept by the sage” was not the fantastic weapon or religious object that officials feared it might be. The Shanxi Governor, who had a copy of Zhang’s statement, discovered that Zhang’s house was near a mountain and that the family owned an old knife for protection against wild animals, which was kept in the house of “this criminal’s younger brother Zhang Qixian” (原有防獸舊刀一把 在該犯之弟張企賢家內收存). The last character in the name of Zhang Tingyong’s brother was xian (“sage”). Xianzi (“a sage”) came from the common Chinese practice of addressing one’s siblings by adding zi 子 to the last character of their personal name.

## PDF第574页

Because Zhang had not impinged on the sanctity of imperial symbols, he was not physically punished. As the 1767 report concluded, “it has been established that this criminal Zhang Tingyong is indeed mad; according to the [1762] sub-statute [on those who have committed a killing because of madness], the Yingzhou magistrate shall be ordered to confine him and take the proper precautions, so that [Zhang] will not be allowed to break out and stir up trouble again” (该犯张廷用實屬瘋癲 應飭令應州知州照例嚴加鎖禁防範 毋使再以出境滋事). We know of Bai Shengqi and Zhang Tingyong through third-person reports on their words and acts. We now turn to someone who left his own writings, and whose view on life we therefore know much more about than Bai’s and Zhang’s.

### 14.3 “Idle steed” Lin Zhigong and his writings

Lin Zhigong's 林志功 case is one of the most interesting to have survived in the records. $ ^{22} $ Lin was born in 1708 in Changshan 常山 county in Quzhou 衢州, the westernmost prefecture of Zhejiang, near the border of Jiangxi province. He became mad with grief after his wife and one of his two sons died of disease early in 1735. His relatives tried to have him exorcized, but without success. A new marriage was nonetheless arranged between Lin and a young bride from Yushan 玉山, a county west of Changshan in Jiangxi.

At an unspecified time, Lin's relatives declared him to the county government and confined him at home, but eventually released him. In 1755, magistrate Qin Tingji 秦廷基

## PDF第575页

somehow found out that Lin's condition had worsened; he ordered Lin's relatives to "keep watch" (收管) over him. $ ^{23} $ Lin was probably not seriously restrained, because in 1756 he managed to go to the residence of former prefect Lin Minglun 林明倫 to "acknowledge" him as part of the same patrilineal clan (前守林明倫署妄認同宗). $ ^{24} $ The retired official promptly had Lin sent home for preventive guard. Angry that Zhigong had stirred up trouble, his uncle (叔) Lin Fengrui locked him up in an empty room (鎖錮空房), but Zhigong was eventually liberated because his mother pitied and missed him (後因伊母憐念仍行鬆放). Zhigong dreamed of becoming a virtuous official. A plan to build a small dam near his home prompted him to submit a petition to the county office, but instead of being allowed to help, he was (again) captured by county magistrate Zhang Youtai 張又泰, who turned him over to his relatives and the local baojia head for proper supervision.

Lin Zhigong's final escape took place in April or May 1762 (the fourth month of Qianlong 27) when his relatives let him go to Yushan county to visit his wife's father. But once in Yushan, Lin did something he had not announced: he went to place a note in county magistrate Rao Jinjun's 饒晉均 sedan chair. The note read:

Of all-under-heaven I am the idle steed, 我是天下無事馬

Arriving in Hangzhou $ ^{25} $ the emperor to greet. $ ^{26} $ 特到浙杭迎帝駕

The Han teacher a thousand years ago had so predicted, 漢師千年前定數

## PDF第576页

The prophecy is now unraveled, and it is attested. 今解數語寔不假

Even if he was nowhere near Hangzhou, Lin Zhigong was immediately arrested because his

cryptic title and his claim that he came because of a prophecy (and the charismatic power it

could potentially carry) sounded inherently suspicious. Officials seized one sheet of paper

that Lin was carrying and examined it for traces of subversive formulas or insinuations. They

also sent runners to search Lin's home, where they found eight manuscripts "hidden"—

possibly just inserted—in Lin's old copy of the Four Books, the only book he owned.

Officials tried to determine whether Lin had “borrowed the guise of madness to dissimulate” (借瘋掩飾) rebellion or other illicit activities. Lin was sent to Hangzhou (the capital of Zhejiang) for trial. Lin was eventually sentenced to military deportation to the empire’s northern borders for “writing apocryphal auguries and books about sorcery in order to circulate them and confuse people, [cases in which the materials] did not reach the multitudes” (造纖緯妖書傳用惑人 不及衆者).²⁷ But let me concentrate on Lin’s writings and what they reveal about his outlook on the world.

The eight texts that officials seized in Lin Zhigong’s house form a fantastic array. $ ^{28} $ They show us how Lin created a relationship with his world, and they give as good an insight as we will ever get into an eighteenth-century man’s experience of what others called “madness.” Written in clear and simple language, they are nonetheless extremely difficult to

## PDF第577页

interpret, perhaps because Lin Zhigong's texts were eminently authored. I have no way to understand the significance of particular words or images to their author, and although I may have retained passages in which Lin "sounded mad," seemingly innocuous details—a date, the name of a person, the sound of a string of words, etc.—may have been even more important to Lin himself.

At some unknown time, Lin started claiming that he had received a revelation. Many of his texts purported to be copies of a thousand-year-old stele inscription that had supposedly come to light in 1735 when a section of the city wall of Ji'an 吉安 (Jiangxi) had collapsed. 1735 happened to be the year when Lin's wife and son had died of illness, the shock that had driven Lin mad. Lin claimed that the text of the stele had been written by "Zhuge the teacher of Han" (诸葛漢師)—Zhuge Liang, the brilliant strategist who had helped Liu Bei during the Three-Kingdoms period and was one of the heroes of the immensely popular Romance of the Three Kingdoms and of many plays based thereon. $ ^{29} $ Zhuge was also a popular god who had the reputed ability to possess people and speak through them. $ ^{30} $ After an investigation showed that the city wall of Ji'an had not collapsed and that no stele had been discovered there, officials asked Lin who had composed the text of the "inscription." Lin told them it had been dictated to him by "the three Mao lords" (三茅君), three deified brothers after whom Mount Mao (Maoshan 茅山) was named. $ ^{31} $

Lin claimed that he had not understood the text at first, but that he was eventually able to explain it and even annotate it line by line (從前總解釋不通後來才能解釋就

## PDF第578页

逐句註解出來), as one would a canonical book or a sacred text. Lin worked on the text of this revelation on many occasions, rewriting and re-annotating it at least three times. $ ^{32} $ Here is a translation of the main text, which was written in rhymed seven-character stanzas, with explanations of how Lin thought the prophecy was about himself:

Dense drizzle, the fields cannot be seen $ ^{33} $ 細雨霏霏不見田

My heart is connected, at Nine-rings $ ^{34} $ 自心相接凡九連

Three lights, fear that a heart will die of itself $ ^{35} $ 三光恐有心自死

Five horses coming from the east, five going south $ ^{36} $ 五馬東來六馬南

A sword and a halberd buried facing ten-mouth mountain $ ^{37} $ 十口山前埋劍戟

A pitiable scholar on a bright-and-dark islet $ ^{38} $ 光陰洲上茂士怜

In Shanxi there is again a guest from the West mountain $ ^{39} $ 山西又有西山客

Endured for many years and endured censure $ ^{40} $ 边受几年边受弹

The year of five-nine, there was much [on?] five-nine $ ^{41} $ 五九之年五九多

To the swallowed (?) across-the-sea to receive a pineapple $ ^{42} $ (?) 到吞横海受波羅

The three-seven kid-child; three-seven died $ ^{43} $ 三七孩兒三七死

## PDF第579页

Two-eight beauty, two-eight wonder $ ^{44} $ 二八嬌娥二八奇

The world's idle speed must be known $ ^{45} $ 要知天下無事馬

Only after the steed fell did we know it appeared at Nine-rivers $ ^{46} $ 落馬方知出九河 For Lin, this poem was charged with personal meaning. It asserted his virtue, compared him to Guandi, and gave him a title (the “idle steed” 無事馬) that likened him to a gentleman.

Like most texts written in a prophetic style, Lin's "stele inscription" could sustain multiple interpretations, but only Lin himself could decipher it properly, as he did in his annotations. Lin also claimed that his interpretive aptitude endeared him to the ultimate authority figure: the emperor. As he wrote at the end of a non-annotated copy of the revelation:

This stele poem was made by Zhuge Liang 造此碑詩諸葛亮

A halberd buried facing the mountain: Guan's clouds are long 山前埋戟関雲長

Although there is an optimus at every session $ ^{47} $ 狀元雖然科 " 有

Not one can expound this poem 見着此詩無能講

Though the poem's meaning has been divulged 詩中意義雖然洩

If you want someone who can explain this poem 若要得此解詩人

He comes from Chang[shan] in Quzhou in Zhejiang 出在浙江衢州常

If a registered student can explain this poem 童生解得此詩來

Grant him a real licentiate title $ ^{48} $ 赐他一名真秀才

Lin’s privileged access to meaning in this case contrasted with his daily situation, where neighbors and relatives refused to believe him and simply called him mad. His texts expressed his grievances in terms that mixed powerlessness with omnipotence, distress with self-aggrandizement. Once again the emperor was a key focus of Lin’s calls for recognition.

People must know of this idle gentleman 無事君子人要知

If so they should report and notify the emperor in detail 知得詳奏皇上知

Filial, brotherly, loyal, trustworthy, humane, righteous, and frugal 孝弟忠信仁义廉

Public-minded, selfless, and not suspicious 公直無私無可疑

Some of Lin's texts were written in response to the way others treated him. Lin's uncle, for instance, had once sought the help of a Daoist priest to treat his madness. The priest had invoked the powers of Guandi, the popular deification of Guan Yu 關羽 (1622–220), a hero and general who, like Zhuge Liang, had lived in the Three-Kingdoms period that followed the fall of the Eastern Han dynasty. $ ^{50} $ In his commentary to the revealed poem, however, Lin Zhigong recounted a therapeutic encounter with Guandi (through a child medium) and a separate meeting later with a Daoist priest. Lin's agonizing account of his

## PDF第581页

experience with the Daoist priest did not include Guandi. There is now way to determine whether the encounter took place the way Lin said it did.

Most of my words are good words 我☐多言是善語

But my parents always suspect I am mad 父母总疑我是颠

They went to Kaihua to seek a Daoist priest $ ^{51} $ 又到開化接道士

Took off my clothes and burned me with oil and fire 脫我身衣油火煎

The priest used fire, I water 道士用火我用水

I swallowed cold water to protect my heart and liver ☐吃凉水保肝心

Burned like that for two nights, the pain was unbearable 連燒兩夜痛難當

I called to my father, but the heart was masterless $ ^{52} $ 口叫我父心無主

Those who attended trembled in fear 来看之人心戰競

I called to my mother, but she did not answer 又叫我母母不應

The Daoist priest asked me what ghost was following me 道士問我何鬼跟

the line "Burying a sword and halberd in front of ten-mouth mountain" (十口山前埋劍戟)

After his encounter, Lin felt much better and told his friends how efficacious Guandi was, but the more he said so the more they thought he was crazy.

Despite having felt transformed, Lin was not healed. Healing in this case would have had to be in the eye of Lin’s relatives, those who had made the diagnostic in the first place. But Lin’s texts expressed resentment that these people kept calling him mad. The fact that he was sent home many times with orders to take precautions shows that local officials agreed with this diagnostic. Depositions made during Lin’s trial concurred that he was crazy, and the officials in charge even worried that he “used madness” to dissimulate other plans. $ ^{61} $ In all these documents, the only voices saying that Lin was not crazy were Guandi’s and Lin’s own.

## PDF第584页

Exorcism has a higher symbolic content than drug intake, and symbols offer more to relate to than purgatives. Lin was clearly transformed by his ritual encounter and he integrated the symbols of his therapy into his delirium. Although his friends and relatives made little of his claim that Guandi was efficacious, Lin's texts were permeated with the images he had encountered in his (fictional?) session with a child medium.

Popular gods also gave Lin Zhigong an idiom by which to express his hopes and grievances. As he told the magistrate who arrested him:

I emulate Guandi, behaving loyally and uprightly, therefore I style myself the Great King of Shanxi. $ ^{62} $ Because everybody says I am an idiot and nobody believes me, I wrote one page and crossed the mountains from Zhejiang to come to your hall. I put [the note] into your empty sedan chair so that you would see it and recommend me to become an official. $ ^{63} $

我學關帝 做人忠直 所以自稱山西大王的 因人人說我是痴子都不信我故此寫了一張 從浙江走過山 來到你堂 上撩在你空轎子裏 要你看見好保舉我做官的意思

Lin Zhigong was attracted to the prestige and claims of superior virtue that went with service in the bureaucracy. During trial, his relatives and neighbors testified that he would “often brag and speak crazy words in front of people, saying how loyal and filial he was and how much good he did, but that there was still no one who recommended him to be an official” (時向人前大言狂語 謂其如此忠孝行善 竟無人保薦做官). $ ^{64} $

Lin expressed his privileged contacts with the gods and his desire for recognition in an account of his encounter with magistrate Zhang Youtai concerning the building of a dam,

## PDF第585页

in which Lin wanted to help. If Lin Zhigong managed to talk to the magistrate in person, it was probably because he did not appear obviously mad at first sight. The fascinating text takes the form of an order by “the teacher of Han” (Zhuge Liang) to “Emperor-King Lin” (帝王林) to “investigate the matter of the befuddled, unfilial, unbrotherly, and unbenevolent minister” (為查朦朧、不孝、不弟、不仁宰賭事): $ ^{65} $

Master Zhang of Changshan is truly confused, $ ^{66} $ wildly taking loyalty and filiality for madness. On the 26 $ ^{th} $ day of the 10 $ ^{th} $ month of yimao year [1735], $ ^{67} $ my presenting a petition in his official hall was for the public good, $ ^{68} $ I had one petition to present at the same time. The master looked at the report and laughed, asking me who had written it: I said it was of my own brush. Yang Gao did not know what matter the petition reported, $ ^{69} $ in a muted voice he told the master I was crazy. I had a report to present so I ignored him; hearing him speak I feigned deafness. When Master Zhang finished asking, he told me to leave. I returned home that very day. Unexpectedly on the 27 $ ^{th} $ came a vermilion rescript, $ ^{70} $ ordering the bao and the family head to restrain me as mad, with no permission to let me out to cause more trouble.

常山張主真朦朧 亂指忠孝為癲瘋 十月廿六已卯年 當堂遞呈是為公 有一首呈共時遞 主看稟呈口嗜笑 問我此呈何人做 我說是我自筆踪 楊高不知稟何事 輕聲主前說我瘋 我有稟呈不理他 我聽他說假耳聾 張主問

## PDF第586页

了叫下去 當日即回到家中 不料廿七出硃卑 著保家長管束瘋 不許縱放出生事

Even if his writings were calls for recognition, in a way, Lin dismissed society's judgment in the name of its highest values. His pure advocacy of virtue and filiality and the fact that people rejected him or beat him up instead of praising him only confirmed his belief in his oppressors' confusedness or lack of virtue.

Lin’s fantasies organized his experience by imposing the terms by which he wished reality would unfold. Lin shaped this symbolic material as much as he was shaped by it. Since details of his fate had been engraved on a stele more than a millennium ago, Lin’s life had cosmic meaning. Yet his implementation of the greatest virtues (loyalty in public service, filial piety) were dismissed—“slandered”毁, as he said—as madness. One of Lin’s texts started: “My relatives say I am muddled in my heart; clearly they mistake my words; Zhuge knows I am true, loyal, and filial [or truly loyal and filial]; [because] my ancestors accumulated merit, [Zhuge] composed a stele inscription” (家人說我心中潛明”枉我之言論 諸葛知我真忠孝 祖父積德造碑文). $ ^{71} $ When Lin’s relatives called him “mad” and dismissed him as an idiot, he countered with contentions of pure virtue. Both parties remained inflexible. The esoteric yet redoubtable coherence of Lin’s text confirmed him as a virtuous person, but condemned him to remaining misunderstood.

### 14.4 Conclusion

Modern psychiatry tends to give little role to the content of pathological language.

The view that disorderly speech says little about etiology and is of little help to therapy

corresponds with the common psychiatric attribution of mental illnesses to neurochemical

## PDF第587页

imbalances. This problematization (or deproblematization) of speech can easily turn the coherent words of mad people into “an irredeemable babble.” $ ^{72} $ Even psychoanalysis, which is bent on working with and through language, does not take psychotic delirium at face value. This chapter has shown that speech that some Qing people labeled as “mad” was not always unintelligible, $ ^{73} $ and that some officials often took it quite literally.

The ways in which the eighteenth-century men I discussed in this chapter expressed their grievances and constructed powerful symbolic selves shows that “even the mad are men of their times” (Porter 1987b: 5). People like Bai Shengqi expressed themselves in intelligible idiom with symbols they borrowed from their readings, from the little formal education they had received, from the plays they had seen and the stories they had heard, and no doubt from the multiplicity of unrecorded little events and encounters that marked their lives.

Lin Zhigong's case has shown that the words of a "madman" (i.e., a man whom others treated as "mad") could sound crazy, yet be both meaningful and interactive. Daniel Moerman (2002) has attributed the well-known "placebo effect" (by which the health of patients improves even if the remedies they take are pharmacologically inactive) to what he has called "meaning response." In Lin Zhigong's case, "meaning response" clearly took place, but it failed to modify the diagnostic of "madness" (顛; 瘋) or "foolishness" (痴) that others had imposed upon him. Instead of the therapeutic "effectiveness of symbols" (Lévi-Strauss 1966 [1958]), we could thus speak of the transformative power of symbols and broaden Moerman's notion of "meaning response" to include all transformations (not only beneficial ones) that result from "meaning-full" responses.

## PDF第588页

Interestingly, those who suffered mental collapse and displayed symptoms of madness used the same symbols in their fantasies as literati and successful examination candidates did in their own stories or dreams. $ ^{74} $ Benjamin Elman (2000: 368) has argued that “the cultural boundaries of mental health were drawn differently in late imperial China than in early modern Europe.” Fighting against reductionist accounts of Hong Xiuquan 洪秀全 (1813-1864)—the leader of the Taiping rebellion—as “mentally ill” (see Yap 1954), Elman explained that

Hong's visions are less evidence of an individual Han Chinese man's paranoia than they are comprehensible narratives drawing on the rich symbolic traditions of Chinese religious and cultural life, which well-adjusted Ming zhuangyuan and Qing literati equally engaged in and which were deemed socially acceptable among upper elites until the late seventeenth century.

Lin Zhigong's (14.3) and Ding Wenbin's (15.4) visions also borrowed from "the rich symbolic traditions of Chinese religious and cultural life." Like Hong Xiuquan, Lin and Ding claimed to have received revelations from gods and they draped themselves in imperial regalia. Yet, their relatives still dismissed them as either crazy or "foolish."

One crucial difference between Hong Xiuquan and the men I discuss in this chapter and the next was in the support society gave them. Hindsight probably made a significant difference. As Elman himself remarked (2000: 368-69), only after Hong became the leader of a large-scale rebellion were his dreams reinterpreted as “a sign of his special future and confirmed his fate as the anointed leader of the Taiping.” Before that, Hong’s relatives had made the same decision as Lin Zhigong’s: they had sought a Daoist priest to treat him. We

## PDF第589页

lack details on Hong Xiuquan's early life, but we must assume that his relatives had also interpreted Hong's words as the result of some sickness, perhaps possession.

Because some labels carry social or institutional consequences, there can be large stakes to the way people are labeled. And because labels are contextual, forms of behavior that could be called mad were not always called mad. Labeling decisions were made in practice. Hong Xiuquan was not considered mad because he found a social role in which he could integrate his visions. It is indeed important not to label Hong as “mentally ill” too hastily, but we also need to realize that in some Qing contexts Hong could have been (and even was, by his family) taken as a madman.

The same themes (revelations, access to the divine, etc.) expressed by a rebel leader were interpreted differently when they came from the mouth of a destitute man rejected by his family, even if both drew from common symbols and deployed these symbols through conventional genres. My point is that the boundaries of “mad speech” (and “mental health”) were perhaps more social than cultural. More accurately, no words or acts are inherently either mad or not mad on a purely cultural level. As in the case of Hong Xiuquan, it was social interactions that led other people either to accept these acts or to dismiss them as insane. The next chapter extends this point by showing how words that villagers considered as signs of madness could acquire a subversive force in politicized contexts.

## PDF第590页

# 15

# Madmen talking?

Symbolic subversion in the eighteenth century

### 15.1 The fear of mad speech?
