“If his madness is averred, will there be anything he cannot say, anyone he cannot slander?” $ ^{1} $

設使瘋病果實 又何事不可言 何人不可毀

Yue Zhongqi 岳鍾琪, Governor-general of Shaanxi and Sichuan, 1727

Yue Zhongqi's lamentation is a typical expression of the intriguing anxieties that form the core of this chapter: officials' and the emperor's fear that mad people could speak subversively, and their ensuing concern to distinguish between real and faked insanity. These misgivings are best observed through legal cases in which "mad people" were sentenced for the allegedly subversive content of their words or writings. Many of the men who faced these trials were dismissed as crazy even as they were executed for sedition. The gesture of executing "madmen" for what they said or wrote may go against our sensibilities, but simply pointing that they may have been mad still fails to explain why they were put to death.

Many of the documents I use in this chapter come from Archival Documents on Qing-Dynasty Literary Trials (Qingdai wenziyu dang 清代文字獄檔; hereafter QWD), an early anthology documenting trials that involved writings. $ ^{2} $ But I find misleading the category of

## PDF第591页

“literary trials” (文字獄) under which these cases have been classified and analyzed. Since the term “literary trials” was a nineteenth-century creation, QWD gives an artificial unity to a wide array of legal cases that were not classified together when they occurred. $ ^{3} $ The writings condemned could be an entire text or an isolated passage, or even a single character in a poem. Reasons for condemnation and the severity of punishment also varied significantly. The scholars who have attempted to categorize these cases as “types” of literary trials (e.g., Zhu Meishu 1979) have not questioned the decision to discuss these cases together in the first place. Moreover, other cases that did not involve script were so similar to those that did, that focusing solely on the “literary” aspect of some trials makes us miss broader patterns. To supplement 14 cases from QWD in which madness was at issue, I found 14 archival cases in which madness was also discussed but in which writing was not a factor.

Since many of the documents I use here have been discussed chiefly in studies of literary trials, my first section focuses on this scholarship. I will argue that a strong political orientation and the uncritical embracing of the category of “literary trials” has kept historians from noticing the diverse circumstances in which people became involved in these cases, and has blinded them to the possibly subversive contents of some words, including seemingly “mad” ones, for certain types of political regimes.

My second section examines why many mad people decided to present their own writings to imperial officials. I explain what kinds of texts were offered and what kinds of words could be considered subversive. Part three recounts Ding Wenbin's sedition case (1753), which started when Ding contacted the descendant of Confucius to announce his intention to renounce the title of "emperor." In the next segment, I pay more attention to

## PDF第592页

what some people were trying to achieve when they sought the help of officials by

presenting texts. Their writings and confessions provide a rare glimpse into the fantasies and

writing practices of common people. My final section tries to understand why officials

treated the words of individual mad men as signs that “local customs and people’s hearts”

(風俗人心) had gone awry, and tried to correct this problem with coercive measures.

## orography of "literary trials"

Western scholarship on literary trials is limited in both volume and scope. Luther Carrington Goodrich's The Literary Inquisition of Ch'ien-lung (1935) tried to show that the compilation of the Complete Books of the Four Treasuries (Siku quanshu 四庫全書; 1773-1782), despite its immense contributions to scholarship, "went hand in hand with a systematic campaign to find and eradicate seditious writings" (1966 [1935]: 23). More than forty years later, R. Kent Guy (1987) demonstrated that the censorship campaign was more than the emperor's personal crusade, was not caused simply by Manchu intolerance, and could not be understood without a grasp of the relations between intellectuals and the state in the late Qianlong era. But Guy's book focused on the compilation of the Four Treasuries, and was therefore no more than marginally concerned with earlier trials for subversive writings.

Recently, Pierre-Henri Durand's remarkably detailed inquiry (1994) on the Dai Mingshi case (1711) and Jonathan Spence's account (2001) of the Zeng Jing case (1728) have finally brought attention to these important events of eighteenth-century political and cultural life. $ ^{4} $ But I know of no Western-language study on the numerous cases of less prominent people who were condemned for their writings in the eighteenth century.

## PDF第593页

Literary trials have, however, fascinated generations of Chinese historians and intellectuals, who have commonly portrayed wenziyu as both arbitrary and unduly ferocious. Twentieth-century writer and essayist Lu Xun 鲁迅 (1961 [1934]: 35-6) observed that most of the targets of these trials had no intention of deriding or criticizing the ruling dynasty.

Some were uncultured, others crazy. Some were obscure pedantic scholars from the countryside who truly did not know about prohibitions, and some were crude ‘foolish commoners’ who genuinely cared for the imperial family. But their fates were generally tragic: if not death by dismemberment or the extermination of their line, it was immediate execution or “decapitation after the Assizes”; In any case they did not escape alive.

有的是魯莽；有的是發瘋；有的是鄉曲迂儒，真的不識諱忌；有的則是草野愚民，實在關心皇家。而命運大概很悲慘，不是凌遲，滅族，便是立刻殺頭，或者‘斬堅侯’，也仍然活不出。

One author has called the eighteenth-century a “golden age” written in fresh blood” (鲜血寫成的 “盛世” ; Zhu Meishu 1979: 61), whereas two historians (Qiu and Xue 1980: 15) have claimed that wenziyu were by definition based on trumped up or far-fetched charges.

Most Chinese who have written on wenziyu have done so in highly politicized contexts. For Han nationalists of the early Republican period, condemning “literary trials” was a way of denouncing the brutal nature of Manchu rule, which had just been overthrown. For most of the twentieth century, writers and intellectuals were also potential victims of the literary trials of their times. Perhaps the most striking example of the connections between political contexts and Chinese scholarship on literary trials is the wave of wenziyu writings, both scholarly and popular, that followed the end of the Cultural Revolution (1966-1976). One way of censuring the excesses of these ten years was to condemn similar events in the

## PDF第594页

past. A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $

An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general.

There may be issues that partisan debates and anecdotal accounts have kept us from noticing in these trials. What kinds of words or writings were considered seditious enough to deserve death even as those who uttered them were recognized as crazy? What type of political regime could feel threatened by such words? In what contexts were expressions of loyalty unacceptable? Are we being misled by what we feel is a clear boundary between madness and crime?

I seek answers to these questions in four interrelated domains of investigation that run through this entire chapter. First, the status of mad speech in Qing time: since dissenters could follow a long tradition of concealing their disapproval behind ostensibly insane words (Schneider 1980), odd speech was not to be considered mad automatically. Officials knew that mad words resembled the discourse of rebels or were at least susceptible to dangerous

## PDF第595页

reinterpretations. Since committing words to writing made them self-supporting, the status of writing will be my second field of investigation. I will also argue that the execution of apparently insane people for sedition has much to reveal about the nature of Qing imperial power and legitimacy. The final point of view by which I will explain the harsh treatment reserved to madmen is the government's perception of the common people, of their beliefs, their level of education, and their role in society.

### 15.3 Harangues, lectures, and verses: dangerous writings in the Qianlong era

“Writing for success”

I showed in Chapter 13 that one of the main grievances of people others called “mad” was that they were ignored. Some of them tried to obtain recognition through writing. Kai-wing Chow (1996) used the phrase “writing for success” to refer to examination success and more broadly to the prestige associated with composing books. Late-imperial literati composed and published countless anthologies of their poems or prose (see Ke Yuchun’s vast anthology [2002]). Judith Zeitlin (2007: 169) has also argued that Ming and Qing doctors edited their case collections in order to “appropriate for the literatus-physician the prestige and power of authorship in a society that prized the written word so highly.”

In this society that exalted authorship, it is not surprising that even people who were not educated enough to compete in examinations or to versify during inebriated poetry evenings still harbored dreams of literary achievement. Literati commonly offered texts to friends for appreciation and comments and sought prefaces (and therefore patronage) from illustrious persons, including officials. This practice also took place at a lower level, among men who had only studied for a few years. Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (15.4) handed his texts to

## PDF第596页

his friends and shop-keeping neighbors in the 1740s and 1750s until he came to the attention of the government. Wang Qi 王琦 made enormous efforts to find an official who would accept his brother Xun's 珣 text until an illiterate military official agreed to receive it. $ ^{7} $ In the rest of this chapter, I examine the writing practices of men who were not classically literate but shared dreams of achieving something through writing. Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers.

Presenting letters and petitions (tou shu 投書, tou ci 投詞)

This truly is something he did because his madness flared up. It he had been lucid, would he have dared to burst into a yamen carrying a rebellious note, throwing himself to his death?

這實是他因瘋發做出來的事 若是明白 還敢執持逆帖

闖入衙門投死嗎

Chai Shilu 柴世祿, 1762

As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

## PDF第597页

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $

Official suspicion about these texts contrasted with the hopes of those who presented them. Wang Zhaoji 王肇基 presented a poem in praise of the emperor to a Shanxi prefect in 1751, but his eulogy was found to include "recklessly seditious passages" (狂妄悖逆之處) and words that "slandered the sages" (毀謗聖賢). $ ^{13} $ As he confessed, "In offering a poem respectfully wishing ten thousand years to the Empress Dowager, I was only doing my utmost as a small commoner. All I wanted was to please His Imperial

## PDF第598页

Majesty”(我獻詩恭祝皇太后萬歲 不過盡我小民之心 欲求皇上喜歡的意思). He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin.

For officials who were concerned with sedition (or advancement), one issue was how to prevent commoners from presenting “seditious petitions.” Chen Hongmou 陳宏謀 (1696-1771), activist official par excellence, noted in a 1763 memorial that non-violent madmen were rarely confined, because they were unlikely to commit a homicide. $ ^{15} $ Expressing his fear that some men would exploit this “weakness” of the policy of preventive confinement to foster disorder, Chen proposed to imprison as strictly as violent madmen those insane people who were meek but literate. They should also be denied access to writing implements. We know of Chen’s memorial through an appreciative edict by the Qianlong emperor: $ ^{16} $

There is some vision in what is reported in Chen Hongmou's memorial requesting the locking up of mad people. A sub-statute was once established [that imposed] strict restrictions on mad people whose illness had flared up, but its has been in effect for a long time and local officials are unable to enforce it effectively, $ ^{17} $ so that [mad people] are [now] freed as soon as they are detained, and even permitted to mess about with brushes and ink to stir trouble. $ ^{18} $ Those among bandits who have

## PDF第599页

truly lost their heart and become sickly mad also turn to writing and [pretend to suffer from] madness illness, hoping to bend the law. $ ^{19} $ This [matter] has to do with local mores and people's hearts. $ ^{20} $ The original memorial has been copied and sent to every Governor and Governor-general for their perusal, so that they can more easily report [on the current situation in their province?]. They have been ordered to instruct their subordinates immediately that when they encounter such people suffering from madness illness, they should take strict preventive measures of guard and precaution, and that no slackness and idleness will be allowed.

陳宏謀請鎖錮瘋人一摺　所奏亦有所見　向來此等瘋人病發　原定有嚴加約束之例　但行之日久　地方官不能實力奉行　以致旋鎖旋釋　甚至任其播弄筆墨　滋生事端　而匪人中實系喪心病狂者亦轉藉詞瘋病　冀為骫法　與風俗人心甚有關係　著於各督撫奏事之便　將原摺抄寄閱看　令其即飭所屬遇有此等瘋病之人　應預為嚴行看守防範　毋得稍有弛懈

Chen knew that the mix of madness with a brush had already led to sedition cases, but the measures he proposed were not very concrete. Indeed, as far as I know, his proposal was never implemented. His claim that some criminals pretended to be mad in order to escape punishment made little sense, since, as we shall see, men who were recognized as mad were commonly executed when they wrote seditious texts.

## Lecturing the emperor: two cases

Inappropriate statements did not have to be treasonous in order to infuriate the sovereign. Yan Zeng 嚴譜, a man from Shanxi, had heard a rumor that the Qianlong emperor, while visiting the Jiangnan region in one of his Southern Tours, had patronized famous courtesans, and that his Empress—from the Ula Nara clan (Wula Nala 烏喇那拉

## PDF第600页

Chinese)—was so grieved by his conduct that she had committed suicide. $ ^{21} $ Ula Nara was born in 1718 and had become Empress in 1750. She had indeed accompanied the emperor on his fourth Southern Tour in early 1765, but for reasons that are still unclear, the monarch suddenly had ordered her to return to Beijing. She either died of grief or committed suicide in the palace the following year, after she had obviously fallen out of favor. $ ^{22} $

Yan presented his message to a Shanxi official in 1777. Written in the form of a palace memorial, it was a sympathetic reproof of the sovereign's alleged misconduct:

Empress Na[la] was plain and excellent, principled and chaste, and had been blessed with much doting. Seeing that His Majesty, despite being over five-decades old and busy with numerous and complicated matters of state, still showed special favor to ladies as in the past, she was afraid this was not a good way of nurturing the Sagely imperial body. For this reason, and because she had to endure such an offense, she made light of her own life.

納皇后質美節烈 多蒙寵愛 見皇上年過五旬 國事紛繁 若仍如前寵幸

恐非善養聖體 是以故加挺觸輕生

Yan was sentenced to death by dismemberment, probably as much for his unbecoming address as for having forged a palace memorial.

The case of Feng Qiyan 馮起炎 took place in Shanxi in 1783, when Feng was 31

sui. $ ^{23} $ He had been born a Zhao 趙, but when his father had died his mother had remarried

and made her two young sons adopt her new husband's patronym. Despite his family's

poverty, Feng had managed to pass the county examinations in 1776, but one year later his

## PDF第601页

second father also died of illness, leaving him and his mother without means. For years he was unable to find work or to progress further in the examination system. In 1781, however, he moved to a school attached to the military garrison of Pingyang, where he made a living copying documents (借住平陽營子識文 在茲家學堂抄寫文冊餬口). There, he kept a crumbling copy of the Four Books, an Old-Text version of the Classic of Poetry, and a copy of the well-liked Romance of the Western Chamber (Xixiang ji 西廂記).

Despite having found employment, Feng grew so impoverished that he became depressed and restless (贫苦難度 憂鬱無聊) and his despondency eventually turned into an illness (抑鬱成病). $ ^{24} $ In 1782, after he caught what later reports called “mucus-confusion disorder” (痰迷之症), he hurt his legs trying to commit suicide by jumping from the Pingyang city wall. A few months later, just after the lunar New Year, he left for Beijing, planning to rejoin the Zhao line (意欲歸宗) and to study trade (學習買賣). He arrived 15 days later, carrying commentaries he had written on the Classic of Poetry and the Book of Changes. Since Feng had been a student for his entire life, his Beijing relatives urged him to abandon his commercial plans. His uncle gave him 1000 in copper cash and his younger brother 500 in Beijing currency (大錢).

In an inn on the way back home, Feng Qiyan heard that the imperial carriage was coming hither to visit the imperial tombs. This may have been when Feng decided to compose a note of self-presentation in which he called himself "Your Minister" (臣) and addressed the emperor as Your Majesty (陛下). Having heard that the Qianlong emperor

## PDF第602页

was an avid reader, Feng proposed to tell him the truth about the Classics of Poetry and Change, especially the latter, “the greatest book of all, which has words on the Duke of Zhou, Confucius, King Wen and King Wu” (I cite these words from Feng’s confession). At the end of his letter, Feng expressed his wish that imperial recognition would allow him to marry two young cousins, but he was arrested before he could implement his plan of kneeling by the side of the road to present a poem to the imperial convoy.

Reporting to the emperor about this case, Zhili governor-general Yuan Shousi said that Feng's commentaries on the Classics were “absurd and disjointed” (荒謬支離), and compared Feng's words to the wild barking of a dog (語言實屬狂吠). Under interrogation, Feng was unable to explain what the Classics meant, insisting that he had no other motives in writing his commentaries than to attract his cousins' attention. (Note that Feng explicitly gave prestige to authorship.) Was Feng just a besotted fool? Yuan Shousi did not think so:

Because he planned to marry but was unsuccessful, he seems besotted but he is no He confusedly wrote [on] the two classics of Change and Poetry, and intended to commit sacrilege against the Heavenly sovereign. Daring as in this case to be so wildly seditious in broad daylight is really to disregard all laws.

因圖娶不遂 似痴非痴 混寫易詩二經 意欲上瀆天聰 當此光天化日之下

膽敢狂悖致此 實屬目無法紀

The Governor-general ordered Feng's younger brother and uncle arrested in Beijing, and directed Shanxi officials to seal and search Feng's house in Pingyang for evidence of illegal activities. Conducting the search in person, the provincial judge, the Pingyang prefect, and the Linfen magistrate (who was also based in Pingyang) found Feng's three books, but no illegal writings (不法字跡) or drafts (底稿) of his commentaries. Despite their best efforts, they detected no trace of banditry or other unlawful activities.

## PDF第603页

Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $

But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. Although Feng Qiyan was eventually recognized to have suffered from “mucus confusion,” his punishment was not lenient.

Feng Qiyan’s having illicitly written a petition for a trifling marriage matter and intended to present it in front of the imperial convoy was truly stupid, reckless, and illegal. Though his planning to marry someone’s daughters resembles besotted confusion, it remains that his words are clear. And how can we know that he is not cunningly pretending to be confused in order to unleash his mad barking? It would thus be unsuitable to be even slightly indulgent. At the beginning of his petition, he also dared to lecture on the classics in a muddled way in front of the sagely sovereign; the language at the end of the petition is exceptionally wild and outrageous. Reviewing these circumstances, the crime is even more serious than breaching the imperial procession. $ ^{26} $ The criminal Feng Qiyan should be sentenced severely, to deportation to Heilongjiang or another [north-eastern military] post, where he will serve as a slave to infantry soldiers.

## PDF第604页

查馮起炎以婚姻細故 私寫呈詞 欲於儀仗前呈遞 實屬蚩妄不法 其圖娶人女 雖係跡類癡迷 而語言尚屬清楚 又安知非色令智昏肆其狂吠 自未便稍為寬貸 且閱其呈首 膽敢於聖主之前混講經書而呈尾措詞 猶屬狂妄核其情罪較衝突儀仗為更重 馮起炎一犯應從重發往黑龍江等處 給披甲人為奴

Life exile to Manchuria for service as a slave was the highest form of military exile (充軍), the most severe penalty below death. Before being sent away, Feng was to be tattooed on the forearm (Bodde and Morris 1967: 97) and dealt 100 strokes of the heavy stick.

Though both Yan Zeng's and Feng Qiyan's cases appeared in the Qingdai wenziyu dang, it is difficult to consider them as "literary trials." Yan and Feng were punished harshly because they had addressed the emperor in an unsuitable manner, in both form and content. Symbolically less grave actions could be punished more lightly. For now, I analyze cases in which the main suspects were executed for sedition.

## ‘Record of Nianquan’s Singular Grievances’

Liang Nianquan 梁念泉 (1738-1781) was a licentiate who became “confused by mucus” (患痰迷病症) in 1773 while working as a teacher at someone’s house. $ ^{27} $ He “claimed he had already become a provincial or metropolitan graduate, and that his parents and neighbors wanted to harm him in revenge; he constantly squabbled with people” (自稱已中舉人、進士 親鄰皆要仇害 不時與人吵鬧). His father Liang Xuewen confined him to a room, but Nianquan escaped and went to a yamen to lodge a complaint. He was caught, registered as a mad man, and sent back home for confinement. In early 1776, after his symptoms had abated, he was freed and eventually hired as a teacher by a man he had

## PDF第605页

met at an inn, but his illness soon flared up again and he had to resign. Since he did not want to return home, he started to earn a living doing divination (賣卜算命餬口).

When he was arrested in Xinxing 新興 county by a “country patrol deputy” (巡鄉委員) who found him suspicious, Liang was carrying drafts of poems and a book titled Record of Nianquan's Singular Grievances (Nianquan qiyuan lu 念泉奇冤錄). The deputy reported the arrest to the magistrate, who sent the prisoner and a report to his superior the prefect of Zhaoqing 肇慶 (on the West River, about 100km west of the provincial capital Canton), saying that Liang “looked mad and confused,” or “confused by madness” (人似瘋迷).

Liang claimed to be a licentiate from Jiaying 嘉應, a landlocked department in eastern Guangdong near the border of Fujian. He said his name was Liang Sanchuan and that he was a Bannerman (旗人), the son of Liang Nianquan, a "former permanent general of Guangdong" (？前任廣東永將軍), but that as a child he had become the son of Liang Xuewen from Jiaying. The prefect duly reported these findings to Guangdong governor Li Hu 李湖. "Because the circumstances [described] in the report are absurd" (因稟內情節荒謬), Li Hu ordered the prefect to have Liang and his writings carried to Canton for closer examination. Li found Liang's text and poems full of "wildly rebellious words" (詩詞所載語多狂悖). The book in which Liang expressed his grievances claimed that Liang's real father was from the imperial family (派出天潢), a pretense that Li Hu found "wildly absurd and in great violation of the law" (狂謬 大干法紀). $ ^{28} $

The investigation revealed that as Liang's madness had developed, "every time he suffered fits, he would speak of the way of the spirits, announcing that his parents were

## PDF第606页

high-ranking persons from the heavens and that he was not Liang Xuewen's natural son; that he had been abducted by [Xuewen] in childhood and locked up repeatedly, that he had been tormented and wronged, but had nowhere to redress the injustice."(每遇瘋發輒稱神道告知伊父母俱係天上貴人並非梁學文親子自幼被伊拐帶屢遭關禁挫磨冤屈無伸) In his writings, Liang also expressed his intention of going to the yamens of the Governor and Governor-general to "express how he had been wronged" (喊冤). A search of Liang's home revealed that he was "bare poor" (實屬赤貧) and that he owned no other "illicit writings" (並無別項不法字跡) than the book he had written.

Concluding that Liang Nianquan was mad, was not involved in an uprising, and did not have any followers did not mean forgiving him for his seditious words or writings. The fact that someone was found crazy did not make him less guilty, only less dangerous. This is why Li Hu called Liang Nianquan a "mad seditious criminal" (瘋迷逆犯) and sentenced him to death by dismemberment for sedition (大逆). $ ^{29} $ His male relatives above 16 sui were all executed, and his female relatives and younger male relatives were enslaved.

## "Mad monk" Wang Jiyuan

Many tales circulated in China about “itinerant monks on the fringes of the monastic community,” and about an “almost bewildering variety of religious eccentrics” (Shahar 1998: xiv and 30) whose prophetic wisdom or therapeutic powers were disguised by unconventional language and curious behavior. $ ^{30} $ The critical or even comic potential of these figures of madness did not escape Pu Songling 蒲松齡 (1640-1715), one of whose

## PDF第607页

But no stories or parables developed on “mad monk” Wang Xianbi 王獻璧, a self-taught healer from Shaanxi who had acquired literacy by studying with a tribute student (貢生) when he was young. $ ^{31} $ In 1754, Wang had suffered from bouts of “madness illness” (瘋病) that drugs could not cure, but later that year a traveling monk named Zhao Tingzuo 趙廷佐 (his secular name) had managed to cure Wang from his ills by chanting sutras. Because he “found scripture-chanting effective” 疑為念經有效 (and, the report specified, because his family was impoverished), Wang willingly adopted the Buddhist diet (自願吃齋念佛) and took the wandering bonze as his master. Zhao gave Wang a note of ordination (戒單) and an ordination name (or “Dharma name” 法名)—Wang Jiyuan 王寂元. For more than a year, Wang and Zhao traveled together, “making a living by chanting scriptures and reciting prayers of repentance” (念經拜讖餬口). Wang’s illness flared up again in 1756. Despite quick recovery, his acts became odd and people stopped seeking his services.

Although Wang was never taken as an “unwitting oracle” (Midelfort 1999: 23), he may perhaps be called an unwitting rebel. In 1761, he threw a letter into the sedan chair of Shaanxi education commissioner Zhong Lanzhi 鍾蘭枝 just as Zhong was entering an official compound one evening in Jiezhou prefecture (Gansu, near the Shaanxi border). With the compulsory trepidation, Zhong reported to the emperor that Wang’s note contained “too many treasonous words to enumerate” (悖逆之詞 不可枚舉) and made him “very ___”

 $ ^{31} $ Wang’s case is recounted in three palace memorials: a) by Shaanxi education commissioner Zhong Lanzhi 鍾蘭枝, dated 6 Nov. 1761 (Q126.10.10); b) by Gansu governor Mingde 明德, dated 28 Nov. 1761 (Q126.11.3); and c) by Shaanxi-Gansu governor-general Yang Yingju 楊應居 and Gansu governor Mingde 明德, dated 28

stories featured a “crazy Daist priest” (颠道士) who made fun of a haughty official with words as transparent to the reader as they seemed cryptic to the mocked man.

## PDF第608页

frightened and surprised” (大為駭異). Zhong also reported the case to Gansu Governor Mingde 明德 and to governor-general of Shaanxi and Gansu Yang Yingju 楊應居, but without forwarding the letter, which he preferred not to spread because of its content. $ ^{32} $ When three weeks later Mingde reported that he had caught Wang, the emperor responded with a public edict (旨諭) ordering his officials to find out why Wang had presented a rebellious note, whether he was part of a faction of evildoers, and whether he had gathered other people. “Investigate meticulously and rigorously to bring these matters to light, and then swiftly execute this criminal” (詳細嚴鞫明確 速將該犯正法).

The three highest officials in Gansu (the governor, the provincial treasurer, and the provincial judge) conducted a strict interrogation in which they learned about Wang's past. Governor Mingde and governor-general Yang Yingju made a point of including answers to the emperor's question in the joint memorial they wrote to close the case. Wang had spoken under torture: "It is truly that I became confused because my illness flared up. I could not control myself. When I recall now, I cannot explain it. If I had [done it] lucidly, would I have been willing to write my own ordination name into the note?" (實係病發餬塗 不由自主至今回想 小的不能自解 若是明白 豈肯將自己法名寫入帖內). The rest of his confession portrayed Wang as willing to die for his "crime," which "enraged spirits and men alike" (神人共憤). It ended with Wang's declaration that he deserved "ten thousand deaths" (該萬死), perhaps a counterpart to the common exclamation that the emperor should live "ten thousand years" (萬歲).

For his crime of “grand sedition” (大逆) Wang was executed by dismemberment in

the market place and his head subsequently exposed. According to the law on collective

## PDF第609页

implication (緣坐) that automatically applied in sedition cases (see Art. 254, in DLCY 25:555), all his other male relatives over 16 sui, including illiterate sons who knew nothing about the letter, were sentenced to immediate beheading. His younger male relatives and all his female relatives (except for an already married daughter, who was allowed to stay in her new family) were enslaved.

In the cases of Liang Nianquan and Wang Xianbi, it is difficult to determine what words officials considered seditious, because Liang's and Wang's texts were both destroyed and their confessions were not very specific. The case of Ding Wenbin, to which I now turn, provides much more information on what the Qing state feared about the words of possibly mad people. Ding's case should also make us doubt the existence of a clear boundary between madness and sedition.

### 15.4 The sedition case of Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (1716-1753)

## Playing with symbols

Ding Wenbin was executed by dismemberment in the marketplace of Ji'nan in the presence of the highest officials in Shandong province in the summer of 1753. $ ^{33} $ After days of interrogation under torture, “his face carried the color of death” (面帶死色). Modern Chinese books on “literary trials” have presented Ding as the epitome of the poor man who was violently executed by the Qing government on far-fetched charges. We hear that Ding’s case “established a horrible precedent for the execution of madmen by dismemberment for trifling matters of writing during the Qing” (開清朝因文字細故而凌遲瘋人之惡例), and

## PDF第610页

that Ding was executed despite having been “a madman” (瘋子). $ ^{34} $ But the “cruelty of feudal

rule” argument explains little, since, as we have seen, madness was not incompatible with

sedition, and writing was anything but a “trifling matter” in eighteenth-century China.

In 1749, Ding Wenbin had offered a text to Jiangsu education commissioner Zhuang Yougong 莊有恭 (1713-1767; optimus in 1740), but Zhuang had ignored him. At the time Zhuang was visiting Songjiang (Jiangsu) for an examination session. $ ^{35} $ Ding had stopped Zhuang's sedan chair to present his text, but when Zhuang had asked who the man was, his escort had supposedly said: "a madman" (瘋子). Zhuang later reported that after he had looked at Ding's clothes ("appallingly filthy" 齷齪不堪) and hurriedly glimpsed at the dirty paper full of jottings, on which he saw the words "Master Ding said" (丁子曰), he had thought there was not enough to warrant any action, and had therefore left without asking the grimy man his name.

By 1753, four years later, Ding had been living in Songjiang for more than ten years, earning money by begging, writing New Year's couplets for stores, and telling fortune by glyphomancy (测字: "analyzing characters" in people's names). At 38 sui, he was unmarried, his parents were dead, and he lived with his brother Wenhui, who used to sell pancakes (烧饼) but was now working in a noodle shop. Perhaps grieved that his "stubborn" (顽) sibling and "raucous" (囂) sister-in-law often called him a fool (癡子), he resolved to travel to Qufu to meet the direct descendant of Confucius, who was the object of a state cult as the "Sagely Duke of Yan" (衍聖公).

## PDF第611页

Having set out one day when his brother was in Hangzhou to sell eels, $ ^{37} $ Ding hired a man named Tian Yinglong to carry his light luggage. On June 29 (QL18.5.28), out of money and probably tired from several long days of walking, $ ^{38} $ Ding presented himself at the gate of the Kong residence, requesting an audience with Kong Zhaohuan 孔昭焕—the current Sagely Duke of Yan—to offer Kong some books he was carrying. Seeing how indigent Ding looked, the gatekeeper did not convey his request, $ ^{39} $ but Ding refused to leave and made such a fuss that the porter finally informed the Duke that a “relative of his from Zhejiang” was asking to see him. Unable to recall any relative surnamed Ding, Kong asked the gatekeeper to learn more about the visitor. He soon received a handwritten note in which Ding stated his identity and purpose. $ ^{40} $ As Ding recounted a few days later during his trial: “Only after I wrote and presented a note did [the gatekeeper] take my books to look at them. Who would have known that because he disliked my being destitute, he was unwilling to transmit and show [them to the Duke], but instead enjoined the county officials to capture me!” (小子寫了說帖送進纔取書看 誰知他嫌小子窮苦 不肯傳見 反囑縣官拿住了) But it was the Duke himself, not the porter, who had sent the guards to capture Ding. Ding’s writings were seized and the man who carried his luggage arrested.

One text by Ding called Hongfan chunqiu 鴻範春秋 elicited forceful reactions from the descendant of Confucius, who berated and rejoiced:

Reading [these texts], I could not help being angry to the tip of my hair and fingers. I did not expect there to be such mindlessly seditious fellows under the sun and the

## PDF第612页

bright heavens. The fact that he threw himself here today to fail and be exposed shows that Heaven robbed him of his souls.

臣閱視 不勝髮指憤恨 不意光天化日之下竟有此等喪心悖逆之徒 今其自投到此 得以敗露 未始非天奪其魄也

# Kong Zhaohuan never hinted that Ding Wenbin may have been "just a madman."

This seditious criminal who takes the false guise of a mad demon is suspicious and crafty. The seditious books he wrote may not all be the product of a single hand. Moreover, someone was carrying [his luggage] and following him: we do not know yet whether there are more members of a seditious faction besides them [Ding and Tian, who carried his luggage]. We urgently need [to conduct] a strict, swift, and thorough investigation, so as to prevent any escape from the net.

該逆犯狀托瘋魔 踪疑詭譎 所造逆書未必盡出自一人之手 且有人擔負相隨 或在此外尚有同夥逆黨亦未可知 亟宜嚴速窮究 庶不致使有漏網

Kong’s analysis elicited imperial praise: “very accurately perceived and decisively managed; excellent and pleasant to peruse” (所見甚正 所辦甚決 嘉悅覽之).

A few days later, Shandong Governor Yang Yingju came to the seat of Yanzhou prefecture (near Qufu), where Ding had been transferred, to interrogate Ding in person and to read the remarkable texts in which Ding had consigned his elaborate construction of himself as a neglected emperor. Ding's books had much more to enrage officials than the stale language (陳言) they also noted. Besides having fabricated a dynastic name and a reign name, Ding had designed a calendar and written a chronicle of his reign in which his words were introduced by "the Son of Heaven and Kingly Emperor said" (天子王帝曰). $ ^{41} $ He had also distributed official titles, claimed to have sent written memorials to notify his friends

## PDF第613页

that he had become emperor, designed a set of capping, wedding, and burial rituals, and even made models for minting coins! Every one of these actions was a direct encroachment on imperial prerogatives and constituted a crime of the highest gravity. As governor Yang reported, “The words in the books are rebellious and absurd, and wildly seditious” (書詞悖謬狂逆); “this is wild and reckless grand sedition” (狂妄大逆; QWD: 14).

Ding Wenbin, the self-claimed Tianyuan 天元 emperor of the Great Xia and Great Ming, claimed that he had come to Qufu eight years earlier and heard the former Duke of Yan “lecture on the way of Yao and Shun and refute the deviant teachings of Buddhism and Taoism” (講堯舜之道 闢佛老邪教). $ ^{42} $ Ding believed the Duke deserved to be called “emperor” (帝) for “guarding the way of the ancient kings” (守先王之道). He also explained that the Duke was so awed when he heard Ding “discuss the Way and Virtue” (講道論德) that he decided to bestow his imperial title upon Ding as Yao had passed it to Shun. But Ding’s eight years as an emperor had been strenuous: “In fact, because nobody has helped, I barely have anything to eat” (實因沒有人幫助 連飯也沒得吃). $ ^{43} $ Thus he had come to Qufu to seek a demotion by passing his title (傳位) back to Kong Zhaohuan.

## Ding's confession: the coherence of "mad speech"

In a long confession that was extracted over several days, Ding not only did not deny any of his actions, but he explained his deeds almost matter-of-factly, sometimes giving the impression that it was the Governor who was asking eccentric questions. Yang Yingju asked:

## PDF第614页

You were born to a small household and now you look like a beggar: how could the Duke of Yan have accepted to become your relative through marriage that year?⁴⁴

Not to mention that, in becoming relatives through marriage, there has never been anything like granting one’s two daughters to a single man. Besides, there is no matchmaker [to testify to the marriage]. This is clearly reckless denial. Confess!

你既是小家出身 現在看你形如乞丐 當年衍聖公怎肯與你結親 況結親從無兩女同許一人之理 又並無媒妁呢 顯屬妄賴了 供來

Ding replied: "My becoming his relative was in obedience to Shangdi's order: why would there need to be a matchmaker? As for two wives for one man, this is nothing but the way of Yao and Shun. Shun took Yao's two daughters as his wives: this is clear and verifiable." (小子结親是奉上帝命 何須媒妁呢 那一夫二婦 乃堯舜之道 堯妻舜之二女 班班可考; Ding's entire confession is translated in Appendix 9.) After making sure that Ding Wenbin had written his texts alone, $ ^{45} $ Governor Yang Yingju sentenced him to death by dismemberment for grand sedition (大逆不道). All his male relatives would be executed and his female relatives would become slaves in the families of meritorious officials. $ ^{46} $

When it was discovered that Zhuang Yougong had once encountered Ding but let him escape, the emperor ordered a strict investigation into all aspects of Zhuang's life. $ ^{47} $ Zhuang was imposed a fine that probably amounted to 100,000 ounces of silver, since he had paid three or four tenths of his dues and still owed more than 60,000 thousand ounces.

## PDF第615页

when the emperor, as a recompense for the excellence of Zhuang's service as a Governor, decided to exempt him in early 1763. $ ^{48} $ Officials' fear of being punished for negligence was one way through which the fear of faked madness became a bureaucratic habitus. $ ^{49} $ The next section analyzes the official reaction to Ding's words.

## Public symbols and imperial authority

Modern Chinese historians of literary trials seem to consider that “madmen” like Ding Wenbin simply spoke “mad words” and that these words were mad and only mad. Arguing that Qing officials executed mad people for sedition has led to the frightening observation that “even madmen” did not escape literary trials, and to the conclusion that Qing rulers were brutally paranoid. These claims are based on widespread modern views that mad words are hollow and harmless. Ding Wenbin’s words would today be interpreted as psychotic delusion, as a composed delirium saturated with Confucian imagery of good kings and virtuous people. Typical “wish fulfillment” appears throughout, from Ding’s matrimonial fantasies to his likening himself to ancient sages.

Both law and psychiatry have schooled us into distinguishing between the innocence of delirium and the culpability of criminals—someone is either bad or mad, guilty or crazy. But it takes a certain conception of madness to divest coherent words of their transgressive contents and to consider them as no more than the consequence of individual pathology (Rose 1992: 146). Our attitude toward what we see as “mad words” is partly the result of a phenomenon Colin Gordon (1992), developing some of Michel Foucault’s insights, called

## PDF第616页

the “disenchantment of transgression”: with the development of medical and psychiatric views, the words of mad men have progressively lost their power to reveal or transgress. Mad speech is dismissed as empty, a process to which the creation of mental asylums undoubtedly contributed by removing some people (and their words) from social dealings.

Even in the eighteenth century, some people's assessment of Ding Wenbin did not differ radically from ours. His brother and sister-in-law called him a "fool," and none of Ding's acquaintances who had read his texts believed he was really a candidate to the imperial throne. So how could a man who was dismissed as an idiot at home be seen to deserve "ten thousand deaths" once he entered the political sphere?

For one, Qing officials had reasons to believe Ding was not “simply mad.” The regime did not tolerate individuals who gave themselves grandiloquent titles or praised themselves with terms reserved for emperors, because the leaders of real rebellions also took reign names and titles, sent written orders, claimed to follow revealed texts, and surrounded themselves with the paraphernalia of imperial rule. $ ^{50} $ Words did not easily pass for crazy if they were structured enough to make so much sense. And words that ostensibly carried meaning could of course attract followers.

Moreover, the coherence of Ding Wenbin's fantasies never disintegrated, even under torture. As Governor Yang Yingju reported after the interrogation, Ding's "besotted mind grew more ardent day by day, forming delusions so steadfast they cannot be shattered" (妄想富贵女色 痴心日熾 結為幻影 牢不可破). Finally, none of Ding's relatives and acquaintances mentioned at any time that Ding had once "caught madness." To officials, it must have seemed that Ding understood very well what he was doing.

## PDF第617页

In any case, as we saw in the case of Liang Nianquan, the fact that someone was recognized as crazy did not make his words any less seditious. Da ni (“sedition” or “great defiance”), the crime for which Ding was executed, was one of the “Ten abominations” (十惡). Ni did not mean putting together a full-scale rebellion; $ ^{51} $ it meant “going against,” with the additional sense of going in the wrong direction, against the flow of a river, for instance. Ding’s writings were transgressive not in the sense of having “gone too far,” but of having “gone against.” He “went against” because he played on the same symbolic field as the emperor. Ding was not seen as an inoffensive parody of imperial symbolic prestige, because he challenged the reigning dynasty by recuperating its main symbols of legitimacy. But these “public symbols” were for public view, not for public use.

Intriguingly, Ding's claims were dismissed as absurd and stridently condemned as seditious at the same time. Yang Yingju's final comment on Ding's words was that "the entire deposition is seditious and rebellious, fallacious and confused, strange and absurd, empty and false" (擦吐供詞皆屬悖逆乖舛 怪誕虛誣; QWD: 14). Almost every term in this description had numerous connotations. Guai 乖 could mean crafty as well as fallacious, whereas chuan 舛 implied not simply "confusion," but also "self-contradiction." Similarly, wu 誣 meant "false" with a connotation of slander and fabrication, and guai 怪 carried an undertone of outlandishness in addition to its usual sense of "strange."

The translation problem these words pose results from an interesting semantic overlap of factual falseness with deliberate deception, and thus, in a way, of madness with rebellion. Ding Wenbin's claims that he had received instructions from Shangdi were thus not only considered false and crazy—they were also deemed deceptive and seditious.

## PDF第618页

At first sight, it would appear that the “boundary” between madness and sedition was somewhat “blurred” in these eighteenth-century trials. This formulation rightly conveys that it was never clear what constituted mad speech, but it runs into the awkward problem of why people whom officials explicitly recognized as mad were still executed for sedition. $ ^{52} $ Indeed, Qing officials were not interested in determining whether someone was either crazy or seditious: one could be both at the same time, equally clearly.

The condemnation of people like Liang Nianquan and Ding Wenbin was a move on several strategic fields. By sentencing them to death, the government eliminated not only perturbing men, but also the content of their discourse. And by executing them despite recognizing they were crazy, officials also attempted to abolish the discursive position of the critical madman, which others might be tempted to occupy. Three targets were thus reached: the man himself, the content of his subversive discourse, and the position from which similar discourses could be formulated in the future.

### 15.5 Deadly fantasies

In addition to telling us about officials' fear of symbolic subversion, the cases reviewed above reveal much about the fantasies of those who could not achieve normal social success. A large number of men in late-imperial China must have nourished dreams of examination success, anxieties concerning marriage, and the desire to increase their own power in society. But in the wrong context and expressed in a certain way, as we have seen, these fantasies could become deadly to those who entertained them. Feng Qiyan had dreamed of marrying two cousins of his. Ding Wenbin also desperately wanted a wife, to the point of claiming that he had married the two daughters of a descendant of Confucius.

## PDF第619页

Other visions and daydreams also appeared in our documents: aspiration to virtue, reveries about fame (even posthumous), and the simple hope of being heeded or liked.

The way the Qing officialdom reacted to these fantasies is as telling as the fantasies themselves. The varying severity of the punishments meted out to these men reveals the kinds of words that the eighteenth-century Qing state—here represented by the emperor and high provincial officials—found subversive.

## Virtue and officialdom

The prestige of emperorship or officialdom proved extremely attractive to the “mad” people I have discussed in this and the previous chapter. As I argued above, the very act of presenting texts to officials already betrayed some people’s admiration for representatives of the state. Many men whom their families considered mad embraced the fantasy of serving as officials. Wang Zhaoji (see above, p. 579) thought that by presenting a text praising the sovereign, he may be allowed to become an official (進了此書 我就有官做了). Lin Zhigong, whose case I described in 14.4, had long cherished the same ambition. $ ^{53} $

Zhang Keming's 張克明 case is another illustration of some mad men's appeal to the prestige of imperial officials. $ ^{54} $ As Zhang said after he was captured, "I have read the Yijing, but because its meaning was so profound, I couldn't make sense of it. Instead, I suffered from an illness that left me feverish and confused for an entire year before I came to" (看過易經 因義理深奧 摸不著根子 倒害了一場病 終年摩熱才曉得). But then Zhang started to figure it out. Based on the meaning of the eight trigrams (especially qian 乾 and kun 坤), he formed theories concerning the happiness of the common people

## PDF第620页

and what officials could do about it. Zhang Keming understood his own ignorance. Since he “only understood half of the Yijing” (只晓得半本易經), he decided to “seek someone with learning and virtue to explain the entire work to me, [thinking] there may be some benefits” (要尋個有學有德的人 求他將全部講解明白 或者有些好處).

As a mentor, Zhang selected Liu Yong 劉墉 (1719-1804; jinsbi 1751), whom he thought was the best person to ask, since well-known (聞名) Liu was “a man of learning, loyal and upright” (是個有學問、忠正之人). Liu was indeed renowned for his scholarship and calligraphy and was even known to be in good terms with the Qianlong emperor. Zhang Keming had walked from Shanxi to Liu’s home in Zhucheng 諸城 county, Shandong. On the way, he had suffered from a twenty-day illness that had left his body swollen (至今身體手足還是腫的). After pawning his clothes to pay for the traveling fees, he had arrived in Zhucheng on August 12, 1775 (QL40.7.17), but on that day his request for an audience was refused. After spending the night at the Confucian temple, he came back with a copy of the Yijing and a written note explaining his purpose. When he was let into the first hall of the Liu residence, he was quickly arrested by the difang 地方 (who was usually responsible for local police work over about 100 households in the baojia system of the Qing dynasty), sent to the county prison, and then to the provincial capital for trial. Liu Yong “is a good person, who gives charity and helps the poor. How is it that instead [of helping me] he arrested me?” (是個好人 施恩周濟 怎麼反把小的拿了呢)

Shanxi Governor Yang Jingsu, who also be in charge of the 1776 case of false accuser Li Zhu 李珠 (12.5), made the following remarks: "Because this criminal speaks

## PDF第621页

clearly and seems mad but is not, I am concerned that he may have committed a crime in his native place and fled, that there might be other illicit matters, and that he may be feigning madness, hoping to escape punishment" (因該犯言語清楚 似瘋非瘋 恐有在籍犯罪脫逃 及別有不法情事 假裝瘋疾希圖避罪情□). I argued above that expressing such suspicions was the preferred way for officials to deal with apparently "mad" men who presented potentially subversive texts.

Zhang Keming's home was searched (investigators found the Analects, the Yijing, and paper and ink) and his relatives interrogated: Keming had studied when he was young, but had eventually become a merchant. In 1773, he had returned home from Henan to till the land, but had soon lost several hundred ounces of silver in a failed attempt to speculate on land. He had then become so dejected that his gloom had turned into intermittent madness (憂鬱致成瘋疾 時發時愈). When he suffered from an episode, his relatives explained, all he did was talk pompously (止是高談闊論).

Since Zhang Keming was engaged in no illegal activities, concluded the Governor, "[Zhang] is doubtlessly a man with madness illness" (其為瘋病之人無疑). $ ^{56} $

Although the note that this criminal presented at Liu Yong's residence did not contain any seditious passages, right now his whole body is bloated and his spirits are confused. This is indeed incompletely healed madness. Moreover, seeing the declaration he made in the letter he left when he visited, he boasted to understand the meaning of the Yijing and came to Shandong from afar for multiple visits with families of status: this is indeed stirring up trouble because of besotted confusion.

## PDF第622页

雖該犯在劉墉處所投說帖尚無悖逆語句 但現在遍體浮腫、神氣昏迷 委係瘋疾尚未痊愈 且因見其訪遺書告示 輙自詡明於易理 遠來東省千謁縉紳實屬癡迷滋事

Despite the lack of any kind of crime and a naively expressed desire to increase his knowledge of a pivotal text, Zhang Keming was sentenced to permanent confinement "to prevent his creating more disturbance" (以免復出滋生事端), the same punishment as the one that had been meted out since 1762 to those who "killed because of madness."

## Making contributions: Wang Tieshan repairs river dikes

Wang Tieshan 王鐵山, from Shanxi, twice went to Henan to offer his assistance to officials in charge of hydraulic engineering. $ ^{57} $ Claiming that he wanted to help the people by repairing dikes on the Yellow River (修河工), he presented specific plans about stone supplies, but perhaps because he maintained it was the Buddha (老佛爺) who had told him to come, he was taken for a madman and sent back to his home county. He was released by the magistrate both times, but fell ill again when he went home, appearing to confirm the Buddha's directive that he should repair the dikes before he could become better.

The third time Wang went out for river conservancy work, he was seized and brought to trial. This time, he had gone directly to Beijing with a written note claiming that the people would revolt if river work were not done properly. He also maintained that a two-headed fish, a monster (妖怪) called "Emai Long" (惡麥龍, [too] literally, "Wicked-wheat dragon"), was digging holes in the dikes from under ground near the river mouth. As a result,

## PDF第623页

white fumes were rising to the sky and disturbing the Jade Emperor, the uppermost Daoist divinity. The Buddha had entrusted Wang with the task of solving this problem.

Capital officials found Wang's note "particularly absurd" (尤屬荒誕), and expressed the usual fear that he was using madness to dissimulate other purposes. Asked how he that knew the monster had two heads and was called Emai Long, Wang admitted he was not sure the name was correct, but explained that the Buddha had told him about this two-headed fish in a dream. Wang, who had seen the fumes with his own eyes, was "unexpectedly" arrested just as he was about to accomplish a great deed by catching the monster (我正要拿住惡麥龍雙頭魚獻功).

The officials of the Ministry of Punishments who were in charge of the interrogation (which was partly conducted under torture) found Wang's testimony "sometimes clear, sometimes incoherent" (时而清楚 时而颠倒), making it difficult to ascertain whether he was genuinely mad. They therefore decided to call upon a medical official (醫官) from the Imperial Academy of Medicine (太醫院). Doctor Li Deming 李德明 testified that "Wang Tieshan's pulse is empty and small; his Qi is weak and his body emaciated: this is because there is mucus in the Heart tract. The illness configuration is indeed very serious" (王鐵山 脈息虛細 氣弱形枯 精神恍惚 由心經有痰 病勢險大屬實).⁵⁸ Officials concluded that given his mad appearance and this diagnostic, Wang was probably ill. They did not mention that his weak pulse and lack of vigor may have been caused by torture. But officials found that they could not ignore Wang's writings. Despite constant insistence during the trial that Wang had not shown his text to anyone except the officials who were trying him,

## PDF第624页

Wang Tieshan was sentenced to immediate decapitation for “recklessly circulating deviant words, and agitating and muddling people’s hearts” (妄布邪言 煽惑人心). $ ^{59} $

### 15.6 Madness and “local customs”: educate and punish

“Can those who serve as high officials not give great importance to people’s hearts and moral customs?”

為大臣者可不以人心風俗為重哉

Gu Yanwu, “Songshi fengsu” 宋世風俗, in Rizhi lu 日知錄 17

“I, in ruling the world, must first rectify people’s hearts.”

朕惟治天下必先正人心

Qianlong Emperor $ ^{60} $

This matter concerns local customs and people’s hearts.... This is a coastal area, where we should enact government by chastisement particularly strictly in order to manifest a clear and severe warning. His madness illness does not warrant being in the least indulgent.

此事與風俗人心有關···邊海地方尤當嚴行懲治以昭炯戒不得因其瘋病稍為輕縱

Case of Lin Shiyuan, 1763

One of the unique responsibilities the imperial Chinese state gave itself was the transformation of “people’s hearts and local mores” (人心風俗). Rectifying “local mores” meant reforming practice and fostering certain types of behavior, preferably as permanent dispositions through the transformation of “people’s hearts.” $ ^{61} $ But as Gu Yanwu 顧炎武 (1613-1682) had emphasized in a letter to a friend, transformative moral education was a

## PDF第625页

never-ending pursuit, in which one day of failure could jeopardize one hundred years of

effort. $ ^{62} $ Gu's view suggests how difficult it must have been for the imperial state to

transform popular customs to its satisfaction.

These setbacks were not for lack of efforts. In 1670 the young Kangxi emperor (r. 1661-1722) issued a Sacred Edict (聖諭), the first of a long series of similar exhortations with which Qing emperors tried to instill into their subjects the basics of filial piety and obedience to the state. $ ^{63} $ These “bare bones of Confucian orthodoxy” (Mair 1991: 326) were posted throughout the empire and quickly translated into the vernacular by educated men who approved of the values expressed in these imperial aphorisms. Bimonthly lectures were even organized in which the Sacred Edict and its Amplified Instructions (廣訓) would be explained to villagers throughout the empire. As Victor Mair (1991: 351) has observed, these lectures often stressed the need to curtail the spread of heterodox sects, probably the teachings and groups that deployed the same imagery and language that the people I study in this chapter harnessed in their fantasies.

But these endeavors to ameliorate customs through education were paired with a deep suspicion toward practices that could foster social disorder. “Local customs” were also adduced in vengeful rhetoric that was deployed in cases of sedition involving madmen. Officials and the emperor did not interpret the utterance of treasonous words as a failure of transformative education, but as a deep moral failing on the part of the people, and more specifically as defects in local customs. When sedition had already occurred, it was too late to rectify customs, but not too late to impress upon people that the government would not tolerate any pretension (even symbolic) to political power.

## PDF第626页

In 1761, Bai Shengqi was executed in the market place to “awe people’s hearts and rectify local customs” (警人心 正風俗). $ ^{64} $ Wang Zhaoji (1751), whose poem of praise to the emperor had been considered highly seditious (see p. 579), was to be transported to the market place of Tongqu 通衢 to be beaten to death in public (當衆杖斃) “to proclaim imperial kindness, to let it be known and to exhort, so that all the populace will know of this clear warning” (宣布皇恩 曉諭勸勉 俾愚民共知炯戒). $ ^{65} $ And Wang Jiyuan’s sentence to dismemberment in 1761 (see pp. 588-90) was justified as follows: “it appears that this seditious criminal is abhorred by spirits and men alike.... He should be swiftly executed so that the uneducated and the stubborn will all know, and so as to make manifest the civilizing of customs” (似此大逆之犯 神人共憤 · · · 應即速正典刑以 愚頑共知而彰風教). $ ^{66} $ In these angry words we may feel both a strident indignation toward people who had the nerve to challenge imperial power and a deep anxiety about the perfidious and violent forces that lay deep in uneducated hearts.

Often represented as the grateful object of imperial benevolence, the people were supposed to remain silently obliged. Even their expressions of loyalty could be greeted with distrust. At times we get the impression that social order was for commoners to be voicelessly “satisfied with their lot.” Restiveness was scorned, and overtly disgruntled people rarely went unpunished. Though Long Fengxiang’s 龍鳳詳 text was originally thought to contain “many wildly absurd and groundless words” (語多狂誕不經), after investigation it was found that “there were no seditious or illegal phrases,” and that the case should therefore not be processed as a sedition case (並無勃逆不法字句 無庸照逆案辦理).

## PDF第627页

However, the emperor found that “many of his words are arrogant” (語多傲慢), and concluded that this person “is certainly an unrestrained and frivolous fellow” (必系疏狂浮薄之徒). Despite not having committed any crime named in the code, Long Fengxiang was sentenced to military exile in Ili 伊犁.

Behind the common failure of moral indoctrination lay a deeper success—that of the imperial model. It is difficult to recreate a world in which rebel doctrines, the theatre, pantheons, and even the fantasies of the distressed all adopted the potent symbols of the imperial metaphor. Imperial and Confucian imagery appeared in most of the writings I have analyzed in this and the previous chapter. Ding Wenbin's elaborate construction constantly appealed to the forms of power and virtue that he knew: memorials, the granting of official titles, the minting of coins, dynastic and reign names, Confucius and his descendants as the protectors of virtue, etc. The fact that commoners presented writings to high officials was another sign that the imperial regime had managed to convince many people of its legitimacy. Dreams of examination success and of serving in the bureaucracy testified to the symbolic power of the dynasty and its institutions.

As Herman Ooms showed with Tokugawa Japan, ideology is not something that necessarily comes from the top (Ooms 1985: esp. Ch. 4, “Disseminators of Ruling Ideas”). It is also recuperated from below by all kinds of people, who, regardless of whether they believe in the official ideology, end up praising those in power, glorifying the position of the ruler, and participating in activities that further the power of elites. From the elite’s point of view, the downside of this success was that people could try to recuperate these ideals for their own purposes and use them against the government.

## PDF第628页

### 15.7 Conclusion

Many of these men's worlds ended when they tried to reach the source of their fantasies—the emperor, who reminded them that these were fantasies, and that these fantasies competed with the fantasies of the state expressed in similar terms. My study of the subversive symbolic content of speech (even mad speech) provides a fresh outlook on the old topic of "literary trials." The mad people whose cases I examined were punished less for seditious intentions than for the potential outcome of their use of symbols. Officials knew that symbols conveyed meaning no matter who used them and for what purpose. The target of their repression was neither beliefs ("heterodoxy") nor practices ("heteropraxy"), but meaningful statements that could proliferate. This official attitude reveals a particular form of political power, a certain problematization of mad speech, and a certain conception of the common people as vulnerable to manipulation.

As I showed in my study of medical approaches to madness in previous chapters, physicians eliminated the meaning of “mad speech” by viewing it reductively as the product of morbid physiological processes, to the point where medical case files rarely cited mad words. Political trials, on the other hand, exacerbated meaning by splitting words from context and intentions, and focusing only on the subversive value of self-standing symbols. So whereas one field (medicine) blocked meaning, another one (political trials) intensified it.

In the trials I discussed in this chapter, no stigma was imposed on people for simply being mad. Some people whom the state recognized as mad were sent home for permanent confinement, some were sentenced to execution by dismemberment, some to exile, and others to being beaten to death. This range of punishment depended on the content of the "criminal's" utterances, but there were no pre-defined standards to judge the level of rebelliousness of one's words.

## PDF第629页

Mad acts may have been easy to recognize in some contexts, but language posed a different problem. Unlike a killing, a text contained discursive meaning and could be transmitted; written words could also mobilize popular support regardless of who had written them. Officials in charge of investigation knew that readers gave meaning to texts, and thus were usually ordered to find other copies or drafts of the seditious writings that had come to their attention.

As I argued above, the safest way for an official to deal with suspicious writings, even when these texts were presented by manifestly crazy people, was to assume that a rebellion was under way and that the “criminal” was hiding more writings. The main concern of officials seems to have been that some people would fake madness in order to hide larger plots, or that a crazy person who knew how to write would eventually find real followers. Officials who knew that sedition (which was by definition symbolic) was a crime of the utmost gravity often anticipated the emperor’s rhetoric by displaying their utmost repugnance toward the texts they had received. Eighteenth-century officials thus expressed amazement that such crimes “abhorred by men and the spirits alike” would be committed at the height of benevolent imperial rule. They always insisted that what these suspicious men said was not caused by madness, or that madness was irrelevant because these words made sense. The fact that officials sometimes found necessary to emphasize that “the criminal’s madness” was no reason to be lax shows that some people may have perceived madness as a potential cause for pity, but this possibility of “laxity” was mentioned only to be rejected immediately.

The outcome of some of the trials I described in this chapter was probably dictated by the attitude of the Qianlong emperor (r. 1735-1796), to which his officials were keen to adapt. By contrast, the Yongzheng emperor (r. 1722-1735) had dismissed as a madman

## PDF第630页

someone who had claimed to be the "true Son of Heaven" (真天子). The sovereign had not claimed that madness in general was innocent, but he must have at least believed that the rambling of that particular madman was harmless. His son the Qianlong emperor proved that he thought otherwise by ordering the execution of many men he himself concluded were mad. The presence of writing may have been an important differentiating factor, but the Qianlong emperor also insisted that mad men who had not presented any writing be punished severely.

Yet the Qianlong emperor was not exceptional in his attitude toward “seditious words.” Early in 1822, the Regional Commander (總兵官) of Taiwan Defense Command (臺灣鎮) sentenced a young man to death by dismemberment for having “composed a seditious petition to vent his anger and hatred” (編造逆詞 圖洩憤恨). The Daoguang emperor replied by edict that the man should indeed be executed, but only by decapitation, because he had been crazy, was only eighteen sni, had no co-conspirators, and was not plotting to rebel (惟該犯究係瘋狂 年甫十八 並無另有夥黨及豫蓄逆謀情事).⁶⁷

Symbolic attacks on imperial prestige and claims of divinity or infinite virtue could rouse the masses, trouble people's hearts, and disturb peaceful customs. Or so officials imagined. But as Ding Wenbin said, what made him go to Qufu to transmit the title of emperor to the Sagely Duke of Yan was that "because nobody has helped, I barely have anything to eat." In this light, official fears appear to have been exaggerated.

## PDF第631页

## 16 

## Madness multiple $ ^{1} $

Language, illness, and behavior in law and medicine

Madness is not an entity or an essence, but an ascribed characteristic of various acts, words, or people. In practice—while treating an illness, judging a legal case, or taking care of a sick relative at home—people could label specific men or women, acts or words, as being diankuang 癫狂, fengdian 風顛, or fengbing 瘋病. But these labels were contextual. Several different words could thus be used to refer to the same person. Depending on what they wanted to emphasize, villagers could call a man's manic behavior kuang 狂, the oddness of his deportment dian 顛, his unrealistic aspirations chi 癡, or his more uncanny acts guai 怪. Conversely, two different terms did not have to refer to mutually exclusive realities. A woman in rural Henan, for instance, could easily accept that the legal category fengbing (“madness illness”) applied to her son even if she labeled him dian and considered him possessed. Even judicial reports used fengbing side by side with non-technical terms like fengdian 風癲 or medical expressions like “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” 痰迷心竅 as if they were all equivalent.

My focus on practical usage eased the problem of definitions, but even as I tried not to base my investigations on a universal, I had to struggle with how swiftly and easily Qing people—be it a man who sought medical treatment for his mad brother or an official who dealt with a homicide—identified insanity. This self-evidence was what allowed a multitude

## PDF第632页

of labels to be recognizable from one field to another, but it also suggests that most people agreed about what madness was.

Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

I tried to develop an approach that would allow me to understand both the obviousness of village madmen and the complexities of technical objects like legal fenghing and medical kuang. For this purpose, I tried to show how these common words acquired specific referents through medical and legal practices. This meant showing how, despite the common equivalence of terms like kuang and fengdian in daily contexts, some fields of practice could turn these words into more technical notions that had specific consequences for action that their daily counterparts lacked.

Showing how technical objects emerged from specific practices was more easily done for law than for medicine. In Chapters 11 and 12, I showed that the penal denomination “killing because of madness” (瘋病殺人) did not appear in the early Qing because of a new

## PDF第633页

rigor in observation or as a response to the supposedly universal problem of what to do with

violent mad people. “Killing because of madness” was created in the 1660s when some

officials started considering as legally relevant the fact that some killings were committed in a

state of madness.

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

When the Ministry of Punishments ordered all families to confine “people with madness illness” preventively (first in 1689, and then more rigorously in the 1730s and 1760s), it was thinking of madness from the point of view of its occasional outcome—a homicide committed during a fit (see 11.4). The apparent danger of insanity had been made obvious by the very law that treated mad killers leniently, because each application of this law showed that someone had killed “because of madness,” and because the lack of physical punishment for mad killers seemed to make better prevention necessary. Through categories like “killing because of madness” and “madness illness,” a specific object madness thus emerged from the functioning of legal institutions.

## PDF第634页

Less constrained by institutional dynamics, the object madness in medicine was far more diverse and it emerged in more complex ways. Chinese physicians constructed explanations of illness out of a blend of observed symptoms, memories of therapeutic success and failure, deductions from familiar concepts, and borrowings from texts that proposed similar kinds of explanation. Etiological claims were better supported by textual reference than by the scrutiny of patients. The intertextual dynamics by which Chinese medical knowledge emerged (see Chapter 5) should point to the intertextuality of all Chinese scientific traditions, from mathematics to botany.
Under the name kuang, medical treatises gathered an array of symptoms, etiologies, and explanations of disease process. I showed that kuang in the Inner Canon (1 $ ^{st} $ century BCE) contained behavioral, emotional, and cognitive symptoms that medical writers included in their descriptions of kuang for the rest of imperial history.
The stability of these medical descriptions hid spectacular changes in explanations of insanity. Unlike the Inner Canon, which attributed madness to a wide array of factors, the formularies that proliferated in the first centuries CE narrowly traced insanity to invasion by Wind or demons. Another transformation in the etiology of madness started in Song times, when doctors redistributed the pathological roles of Wind onto saliva and mucus. Some of their reflections helped physicians like Liu Wansu, Zhang Congzheng, and Zhu Zhenheng (see Chapter 4) to reinterpret Wind as inner Fire between the twelfth and the fourteenth century, and to argue that madness was caused by Fire and mucus. The doctrines of these innovators were widely adopted starting in the fifteenth century, especially through the decisive influence of the “Danxi corpus,” a group of texts attributed to Zhu Zhenheng that often carried the word “Danxi” in their title. The mucus-Fire approach to madness still dominates Chinese medicine.

## PDF第635页

I showed that change in medical approaches to madness took place mostly by “scheme transfer.” The medicine of Wind was transferred to madness from more general formulations found in the Inner Canon. Before they were applied to madness, the pathological roles of mucus developed within reflections on the patho-mechanisms of Wind strokes. These new explanations of madness became dominant in their time because they were supported by the general evolution of medical doctrines and because they were advocated in books with a wide diffusion, like those of the Danxi corpus. Legal change was easier to describe than medical change, because acts of legislation forced all judicial officials to adjust the categories through which they processed cases.

The legal and medical objects madness functioned differently. Legal concentration on the utter lack of intentions of mad acts differed from the way in which other spheres of Qing society understood insanity. Since lack of awareness of one's acts was a central theme of legal reflections on madness, the symptom "to be unaware of people and things" (不知人事 or 不省人事) had a more prominent place in law than in the medical literature, where it was rarely noted. Medical and legal texts both recorded that some madmen were prone to violence, but whereas doctors used this observation to emphasize the severity of a medical imbalance, law used it to underline the need to take preventive measures. In medicine, behavior was the most manifest symptom of madness and the first target of therapeutic interventions, whereas for the law specific acts had to be not diminished, but prevented or punished.

The legal institution left little choice about what scripts officials could use to interpret reality. Magistrates had to judge cases through the procedures and categories of the Qing Code, and their interpretations had to stick to a corpus of laws. In judicial contexts, only the prototypical definition of madness as complete lack of awareness applied. In the

## PDF第636页

policy of preventive confinement, however, it was left to the discretion of families to identify “people with madness illness.”

Medical practice was not bound by explicit institutional rules. Whereas law required that cases be reduced to prototypical scenarios, physicians delighted in treating people whose illness deviated from the descriptions found in treatises—or at least enjoyed reporting their success at doing so. Physicians were free to adopt different therapies depending on how they diagnosed the case at hand. They could even modify their approach in the course of treatment if their first choice had proven useless.

The purpose of the legal and medical fields also shaped what doctors and judges noticed in their respective practices. The words of a person deemed mad were of little import to physicians, who usually placed the diagnosis on a physiological level.
