——. Wenshi yishu sanzhong 溫氏醫書三種 (#11777). 1886. Family's edition from that year at Shanghai Library.

Wen Yanbo 文彦博. Yaozhun 藥準, in 1 juan. Lost.

Wu jun zhi 吳郡志. Song-era gazetteer of the Suzhou area.

Wu Kun 吳崑 (1552-1620). Yifang kao 醫方考 [Investigations on Medical Recipes] (#03039), in 6 juan; 1584, first printed in 1586.

WuKYQ. Wu Kun yixue quanshu 吳昆醫學全書. Editor in chief Guo Junshuang 郭君双. Ming-Qing mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1999.

Wu Qiu 吳球 (zǐ Jiaoshan 茭山; fl. early 16 $ ^{th} $ century; from 括蒼 in Zhejiang (present-day Lishui county in Chuzhou prefecture). Huoren xintong 活人心統, in 4 juan; prefaced and printed in 1539 by Zheng Lin (magistrate of Lishui county), under orders by his superior Hu Shenzhai 胡慎齊 (a prefect?). Edition in simplified characters by Wang Mini 王咪咪 in HWHG-5: 1-152, based on an early-seventeenth-century manuscript copy held at the Naikaku bunko.

——. Zhuzheng bianyi lu 諸證辨疑錄 (#04975), in 4 juan; date unknown. Incomplete printed version at CATCM; Ming hand copy at the Sichuan Provincial Library in Chengdu.

Wu Shu 吳恕 (bao Mengzhai 蒙齋). Shanghai huoren zhizhang tu 傷寒活人指掌圖 (a.k.a. Shanghai zhizhang tu) (#01151), in 1 juan; 1337. One Yuan ed. at CATCM. Also two Ming eds. and a 1704 ed. are extant. Rearranged into 5 juan and renamed Shanghai tuge buoren zhizhang 傷寒圖歌活人指掌 [#01150] by Chen Changqing 陳長卿. Surviving eds.: 1600 and 1605 (both in Fujian), 1615, 1643 (in Yiyao jilan 醫藥集覽 [#11605]) late Ming, Ming (2 in Yiyao jilan), early Qing, 1699 (Yiyao jilan), Kangxi (3, all in Yiyao jilan), Qing. In the Zhengtong era (1436-1449), famous Fujian editor Xiong Zongli 熊宗立 (1409-82) merged it with Li Zhixian's 李知先 (zì Yuanxiang 元象; fl. Song period) Shanghai leizheng buoren shukuo 傷寒類證活人書括. Extant eds.: 1564, Jiajing, 1589, Ming.

Wu Tan. See Dai Qing lũi tongkao jiaozhu.

Wu Tang 吳瑭 (zī Peiheng 配珩, bao Jutong 鞠通; 1758-1836; from Shanyang 山陽 county in Huai'an 懷安 prefecture, Jiangsu). Wenbing tiaobian 溫病條辨 (#05689), in 6 juan plus 1 opening juan; compiled from 1798 to 1813. First edition from 1813 by the Wenxin tang 問心堂 (at CATCM).

——. Wu Jutong xiansheng yi'an 吳鞠通先生醫案 [“The medical case files of Wu Jutong xiansheng”] (#10296), in 4 juan; ca. 1833. Originally called Wushi yian 吳氏醫案. Contains cases from 1793 to 1833. First printed in 1916. I cite from WuTYQ.

——. Yi yi bing shu 醫醫病書 [“Book on curing the ills of physicians”] (#10701), in 1

juan. Completed in 1831, first printed in 1836 (?). Photolithographic punctuated 1915

edition made in Shaoxing by Cao Bingzhang 曹炳章 of the Yuxin shuju 育心書局; Cao

rearranged the order of the original entries, added new entries and his own commentary

## PDF第675页

(按語), and separated the original work into two juan and four sections. This version was reedited in simplified characters in Lidai mingyi yihua daguan 歷代名醫醫話大觀, vol. 1 [1996]: 505-53. There is also a 1985 edition at the Jiangsu kexue jishu chubanshe by Shen Suge 沈夙阁 based on the original manuscript (which is not listed in Xue Qinglu 1991). I cite from the WuTYQ ed., which is based on the text of Cao's ed., but restores the original order as found in Shen's 1985 edition.

WuTYQ. Wu Jutong yixue quanshu 吴鞠通醫學全書. Editor in chief Li Liukun 李刘坤. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyi yao chubanshe, 1999.

Wu Youxing 吴有性 (zì Youke 又可). Wenyi lun 溫疫論 [On Warm-Factor Epidemics] (#05886), in 2 juan, author's preface from 1642. Early Qing edition at CATCM.

Wuzhong renwu zhi 吴中人物志, in 13 juan. 1567/1572 (Longqing reign).

XAHL. See Xing'an builan 刑案匪覽.

XAHLXB. See Xing'an builan xubian 刑案匪覽續編.

XBDA. Xingbu dang'an 刑部檔案 [Archives of the Ministry of Punishments]. Held at the First Historical Archives in Beijing. I cite by Bureau (si 司, each named after a province) and file number.

Xiaoer weisheng zongwei lunfang 小兒衛生總微論方 (#07253), in 20 juan; 1156? By unknown author. SKQS ed.

Xiao Shi 蕭奭. Yongxian lu 永憲錄, in 4 juan. Completed in 1752. Beijing, Zhonghua shuju, 1959.

Xie Xinghuan 謝星煥 (zì Yinglu 映盧; from Jinxi 金溪 in Fuzhou 撫州 prefecture, Jiangxi; d. 1857). Dexinji yi'an 得心集醫案 (#10158), in 6 juan; 1861. First printed in 1861 (copy at CATCM). One surviving edition from 1899, as well as two other undated Qing eds. In ZYJ-4: 3-111.

Xinbian jingui yaolüe fanglun. See Zhang Ji, Jingui yaolue fanglun 金匱要略.

Xinfa. See Zhu Zhenheng, Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法.

Xin Tang shu 新唐書. See New Book of Tang.

Xinxiu bencao 新修本草 [Newly revised Materia medica] (#02236), in 10 juan; 659. Reconstituted by Shang Zhijun 尚志钧 (2004).

XinZXAHL. See Xinzeng xing'an builan 新增刑案匪覽.

Xinzeng xing'an huilan 新增刑案彙覽, in 16 juan. 1886 (date of a preface by He Weikai 何維楷). Compiled by Pan Wenfang 潘文舫 and Xu Jianquan 徐諫荃. Contains case from 1842 to 1885. Photoreproduction of the original edition, Taipei: Ch'eng-wen, 1968. See also Xing'an builan, Xing'an builan xubian, and Xuzeng xing'an builan.

Xing'an huilan 刑案彙覽 [Conspectus of Penal Cases], in 60 juan; 1834 (date of a preface by Bao Shuyun 鮑書芸). Compiled by Zhu Qingqi 祝慶祺 and Bao Shuyun. Contains cases from 1736 to 1834. Photoreproduction of the original edition, Taipei:

## PDF第676页

Ch’eng-wen, 1968. See also Xinzeng xing’an builan, Xing’an builan xubian, and Xuzeng xing’an builan.

Xing'an huilan xubian 刑案彙覽續編, in 32 juan. Compiled by Wu Chao 吴潮 and He Xiyan 何錫儼, examined by Xue Yunsheng 薛允升. Contains cases from 1828 to 1870. Photo-reproduction of a 1900 edition, Taipei: Wenhai chubanshe, 1970.

Xingbu bizhao jiajian cheng'an xubian 刑部比照加减成案續編，in 32 juan; 1843. Compiled by Xu Lian 許樉. In Xu xiu Siku quanshu 續修四庫全書，vols. 865-66 (Shanghai: Shanghai guji chubanshe, 1995).

XKTB. Xingke tiben 刑科题本 [“Routine memorials of the Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments”]. All the memorials I cite are from the category “Homicides” (命案), sub-section “Others” (其他). Memorials are classified by year. XKTB is part of the broader Neige tiben 内閣題本 [“Routine memorials of the Grand Secretariat”] collection at the First Historical Archives in Beijing.

Xu Chunfu 徐春甫. Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (#11510), in 50 juan. Author's preface from 1556; first printed in 1557. Copy of original ed. (by Chen Zhangqing 陳長卿 of Suzhou) at CATCM. Two-vol. ed. in trad. characters from Beijing: Renmin weisheng chubanshu, 1991.

___. Neijing yaozhi 内經要旨 (#00040). Now juan 2 of Xu's Gujin yitong daquan.

Xu Dachun 徐大椿 (zì Lingtai 靈胎; 1693-1771). Huixi yi'an 洄溪醫案 (#10074), in 1 juan; 1855 preface by editor Wang Shixiong 王士雄. XuYQ (1999), based on 1857 ed. First printed in 1855 (copy at Guangdong sheng Zhongshan Library), then in 1857, 1864, 1875 (2, including once in Yixue sanshu beke 醫學三書合刻 (#11750), 1876 (2, including once in Xiaoyuan congshu 嘯園叢林 (#11987), 1878 (2), 1883 (in Xiaoyuan congshu), 1889, 1891, 1893 (2, including once in Xu Lingtai yixue quanshu 徐靈胎醫學全書 (#11642)), 1896, 1907 (3, including 2 in Xu Lingtai yixue quanshu), and Qing (10, including 6 in Xu Lingtai shierzhong quanji 十二種全集 (#11641) and 2 in Xu Lingtai yixue quanshu). Also included in Xushi yishu bazhong 徐氏醫書八種 (see below).

——. Lantai guifan 蘭台規範 (#05045), in 8 juan; 1764. Original ed. at CATCM.

___. Nanjing jingshi 難經經釋 (#00236), in 2 juan, 1727. Edition from that year by the Huixi cao tang 洄溪草堂 at CATCM.

——. Shanghai leifang 傷寒類方, in 2 juan. SKQS ed.

Shennong bencao jing baizhong lu 神農本草經百種錄 (#02211), in 1 juan. SKQS ed.

(edited by others). Xushi yishu bazhong 徐氏醫書八種 (#11640). First ed. in 1857. Re-edited and reprinted in 1860, 1878 (2), 1889 (2), 1892, 1893, 1896, 1897, Guangxu, Qing (2+). The publisher was Mr. Jiang 蔣 from Haichang 海昌.

(attributed). Ye'an pimiao 葉案批繆 (#10075), in 1 juan; 1759? In Xu Lingtai yishu sanshier zhong 徐靈胎醫書三十二種 (Republican period). Also appended to some editions of Ye Gui's Linzheng zhinan yian 臨證指南醫案 (1766; q.v.).

. Yixue yuanliu lun 醫學源流論 (#10682), in 2 juan; 1757. SKQS ed., as well as XuYQ (1999, based on 1757 ed.): 113-59. Original at CATCM. Translated by Paul Unschuld (see Unschuld 1990 in Bibl. B).

Xu Kai 徐鍇 (fl. southern Tang, 937-975). Shuowen jiezi xizhuan 說文解字繫傳, in 40 juan;  $ 10^{th} $ century.

Xu Shen 許慎. See Shouwen jiezi 說文解字.

Xu Shuwei 許叔微. Leizheng puji benshi fang 類證普濟本事方 [Fact-Based Recipes for Universal Succor] (#02950), a.k.a. Benshi fang, in 10 juan; 1132. The earliest extant ed. is from the SKQS.

Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (zì Xuanzhi 宣治, bao Xiangcun 椽村). Xiangcun zhiyan 椽村治驗 [Xiangcun's successful treatments] (#10090); 1785. 1785 edition in Xushi youke qizhong 許氏幼科七種 (#07369) at CATCM. One independent edition from 1795.

Xu Yanchun 徐彥純 (d. 1384; zì Yongcheng 用誠). [Yixue zhezhong 醫學折衷.] Completed in 1368. See Liu Chun and Xu Yanchun, Yuji weiyi 玉機微義.

XuYQ. Xu Lingtai yixue quanshu 徐靈胎醫學全書. Editor in chief Liu Yang 刘洋. Ming-Qing mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1999.

Xuzeng xing'an huilan 續增刑案彙覽，in 16 juan, 1840 (date of a preface by Bao Shuyun 鮑書芸). Compiled by Zhu Qingqi 祝慶祺 and Bao Shuyun. Contains mostly cases from the Daoguang reign (1821-1850). Photoreproduction of the original edition, Taipei: Ch'eng-wen, 1968. See also Xing'an huilan, Xinzeng xing'an huilan, and Xing'an huilan xubian.

Xuanzong Cheng huangdi shilu 宣宗成皇帝實錄. The “Veritable Records” of the Daoguang emperor (r. 1821-1850). Volumes 55-56 of Dai Qing shilu (q.v.).

Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559; zì Xinfu 新甫; bao Lizhai 立齋). Baoying cuoyao 保嬰撮要 (#07286), in 20 juan; completed in early 1556 (date announced in a preface from that year). First edition (probably from 1556 too) at CATCM. Was the enhanced version of an earlier compilation by Xue Ji's father Xue Kai 薛鎧. XueYQ ed. based on Xueshi yian 孫詩 (see Xueshi yian below).

Jiaozhu furen liangfang 校注婦人良方 (#06607, entries 3ff), in 24 juan. Preface by Xue from 1547. This is Xue's annotated edition of Chen Ziming's Furen daquan liangfang (see under Chen Ziming). XueYQ ed. based on Xueshi yian ersbisi zhong 薛氏醫

案二十四種 edition (Wanli era).

——. Waike fahui 外科發揮 (#08376), in 8 juan, preface from 1528. One Ming edition available at the library of the Zhongguo yixue kexueyuan.

——. Waike jingyao 外科精要 (aka Waike baojian 外科寶鑑) (#08378), in 3 juan. Xue's annotated edition of a book of the same title by Chen Ziming 陳子明 (mid 13 $ ^{th} $ century). First extant edition from 1548, available at CATCM.

——. Waike xinf $ \underline{\text{a}} $ 外科心法 (#08373), in 7 juan; 1528. Remnants of the 1528 edition are available at CATCM.

——. Xueshi yian 薛氏醫按 (#11573). A compilation from the Wanli era (1573-1620), which is the date of the earliest extant ed. in China. Contains 24 different books, only some of which are by Xue Ji. The latest preface to an individual work is dated 1571. Printed by Wu Guan 吳琯, from Xin'an. A shorter version containing 16 works was printed in 1628 by another publisher. 24-book ed. from Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1997, collated by Zhang Huifang 张慧芳 et al. I also cite the 16-book SKQS ed.

Xueshi yian. See under Xue Ji.

Xue Xue 薛雪 (1681-1770; zì Shengbai 生白, bao Yipiao laoren 一瓢老人). Saoye zhuang yipiao laoren yi'an 掃葉莊一瓢老人醫案 (#10077), in 4 juan; first printed in 1936. In ZYJ-4: 647-94.

Xue Yunsheng 薛允升. Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 [Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes], in 54 juan. Edited for posthumous publication by Xu Shiying 許世英, Beijing Liulichang: Hanmao zhai, 1905. I cite from Duli cunyi chongkan ben: fu zuozhe zhuanliie ji Qingli tiaoli bianbao biaomu 讀例存疑重刊本, 附作者傳略及清律條例編號標目 [A Typeset Edition of the Duli cunyi, With a Biography of the Compiler and Numbering and Titles added to the Sub-statutes], edited by Huang Jingjia 黃靜嘉 (Taipei: Chengwen, 1970).

XuZXAHL. See Xuzeng xing'an builan.

XXZL. Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部现行则例. 1680. In Gujin tushu jicheng 古今圖書集成, "Jingji bian" 經濟編, "Xiangxing dian" 祥刑典, juan 59-61.

Yan Yonghe 嚴用和, Jisheng fang 濟生方 (#02973), in 10 juan. 1253. The earliest extant ed. is a Japanese imprint from 1734 (one copy at CATCM). SKQS ed.

Yang Shangshan. See Grand Basis.

Yang Shiqi. See Yang Yu.

Yang Shiying 楊士瀛 (zì Dengfu 登父; bao Renzhai 仁齋; from Fuzhou 福州, Fujian). Renzhai zhizhi fanglun 仁齋直指方論, in 26 juan; compiler's preface 1264. The earliest extant version is an enlarged edition from 1550. SKQS edition (based on the 1550 ed.).

Ye Gui 葉桂 (1667-1746; zǐ Tianshi 天士, bao Xiangyan 香岩). Linzheng zhinan yian 臨證指南醫案 [Medical Case Files to Guide Therapeutic Practice] (#10065), in 10 juan. Edited by Hua Xiuyun 華岫雲 and others and first published in 1766. I cite the YeYQ ed, which is based on an 1844 ed. by the Jingchu Hall 經鋤堂.

Yifang leiju. See Ui-bang yuch-wi 醫方類聚.

Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸經. See Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels.

[Compilation of Yongzheng-period Chinese-language palace memorials rescripted in vermilion]. In 40 vols. Edited by the First Historical Archives (Beijing). Shanghai: Jiangsu guji chubanshe, 1991.

You Cheng 尤乘. Shoushi qingbian 壽世青編 (#09667), in 2 juan. In Li Zhongzi's Shicai sanshu 士才三書 (ed. by You Cheng; 1667). Other eds.: 1728, 1854, 1891, Qing (2).

You Yi 尤怡 (d. 1749; zì Zaijing 在涇, hao Zhuowu 拙吾; bie hao Sihe shanren 飼鶴山人). Jingui yaolue xindian 金匱要略心典 (#01205), in 3 juan; author's preface 1729, probably printed in 1732, the date of a preface by Xu Dachun 徐大椿.

Jingui yi 金匱翼 (#06221), in 8 juan; earliest dated preface is 1768, by You Yi's uncle (hao Yueyan laoren 岳岩老人); 1813 preface by Xu Jin 徐錦; undated preface by disciple Bo Xuefeng 柏雪峰. First printed in 1813. Original edition at CATCM.

——. Shanghai guanzhu ji 傷寒貫珠集 (#00628), in 8 juan. First printed in 1810, but mentioned in 1768 preface to Jingui yi 金匱翼, though the author of that preface (You Yi's uncle) said he had not seen it.

YouYQ. You Zajing yixue quanshu 尤在涇醫學全書. Editor in chief Sun Zhongtang 孙中堂. Ming-Qing mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1999. Presents the works of You Yi 尤怡.

Yu Bian 俞弁 (zì Zirong 子容). Xu yishuo 續醫說 (#11394), in 10 juan; earliest preface from 1522; more prefaces from 1534 and 1537. Presented as a continuation of Zhang Gao's 張杲 Yishuo 醫說 (1224) (#11389). According to Xue Qinglu 1991, only two surviving manuscript copies have survived (one of which at CATCM). Zhongyi wenxian cidian announces that there is an extant original edition from the Jiajing era (1522-1566), another Ming edition from the Suzhou region, and a 1911 edition, but does not indicate where these prints are held.

Yu Chang 俞昌 (zì Jiayan 嘉言). Yuyi cao 寓意草 [Notes on My Judgment] (#10035), in 1 juan; 1643.

Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517) (𠀤i Tianmin 天民, bao Huaxi hengde laoren 花溪恆德老人). From Yiwu 義烏 in Jinhua 金華, Zhejiang. Yixue zhengchuan 醫學正傳 (#04904), in 8 juan; author's preface dated 1515. I cite from an edition collated and punctuated by Guo Ruihua 郭瑞华, Ma Pai 马湃, Wang Aihua 王愛华, and Sun Deli 孙德立, based on a 1531 edition (Beijing: Zhongyi guji chubanshe, 2002).

Yuxing qiwu zouyi 諭行旗務奏議，in 13 juan; edited by Yinlu 胤祿. I cite from SKQS ed., Shibu”史不，“Zhaoling zouyi lei”招令奏議類，“Zhaoling zhi shu”招令之屬，“Shizong xian huangdi shangyu baqi”世宗憲皇帝上諭八旗. A collection of edicts by the Yongzheng emperor concerning Banner affairs.

Yu Zhen 俞震 (b. 1709; zì Dongfu 東扶, bao Xingzhai 惺齋; from Jiashan 嘉善 county in Jiaxing 嘉興, Zhejiang). Gujin yian an 古今醫案按 [Medical Case Files Ancient and Present, With Commentaries] (#10087), in 10 juan; compiler's preface from 1778. Original ed. by the "Hall of Pondering the Past" (Zhuogu tang 酌古堂) at Junshi yixue kexueyuan, Shanghai Library, and Suzhou yixue yuan. I cite from a 1959 edition in traditional characters by Shanghai kexue jishu chubanshe, which does not indicate the edition on which it was based.

Yuan dianzhang 元典章. Da Yuan shengzheng guochao dianzhang 大元聖政國朝典章. Photo-reproduction of a Yuan edition, Beijing: Guangbo dianshi chubanshe, 1998. Also a photo-reproduction in Xuxiu Siku quanshu 續修四庫全書 (Shanghai: Shanghai guji chubanshe), vol. 787.

Yuandai falü ziliao jicun 元代法律資料輯存. Collated by Huang Shijian 黃時鑑. [Hangzhou?]: Zhejiang guji chubanshe, Zhejiang sheng Xinhua shudian faxing, 1988.

Yuan Haowen 元好問 (1190-1257). Yuan Haowen quanji 元好問全集. Taiyuan: Shanxi renmin chubanshe, 1990. In 2 volumes.

Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1798). Zi bu yu 子不語. Shanghai: Shanghai guji chubanshe, 1998.

ZGZC. Zhongyi guji zhenxi chaoben jingxuan 中醫古籍珍稀抄本精選. 20 vols. Shanghai kexue jishu chubanshe, 2004. Editions in simplified characters of rare medical works that have survived only in manuscript form. Every work is paginated separately.

Zhang Boxing 張伯行. Lian Luo Guan Min xingli jijie 濂洛關閩性理集解, in 4 juan. Compiled and commented by Zhang Boxing 張伯行 (1651-1725). Kangxi reign.

Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (zì Zihe 子和; bao Dairen 戴人; 1156-1228). Rumen shiqin 儒門事親 [The Scholar Serving his Kin] [#04879], in 14 or 15 juan (depending on editions); ca. 1228. A collection of works by Zhang and his disciples centered on a shorter work in 3 juan also called Rumen shiqin. For the individual titles, see below. Juan 13, San xiao lun 三消論, was composed by Liu Wansu (q.v.). The earliest extant ed. in 14 or 15 juan is from 1541. I cite from Zibe yiji (based on the 1541 ed.) and from the SKQS ed., but the latter does not give the titles of the individual works that composed the book.

——. Rumen shiqin (#04879, entry 1); in 3 juan; 1228 or slightly before. Earliest extant edition from 1262 (at Beijing University). Juan 1 to 3 of the received Rumen shiqin. Written with the help of Zhang's friend Ma Jiuchou 麻九疇 (zi Zhiji 知己).

——. Zhibing baifa 治病百法, in 2 juan. Juan 4 and 5 of the received Rumen shiqin.

——. Shixing sanliao 十形三療; in 3 juan. Juan 6 to 8 of the received Rumen shiqin.

——. Sanfa liumen 三法六門. Juan 12 of the received Rumen shiqin.

Zhang Ji. See Treatise on Cold Injury; Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses; and Essentials of the Golden Casket.

Zhang Jiebin 張介賓 (1563-1640; zǐ Huiqing 會卿, bao Jingyue 景岳, biebao Tongyi zi 通一子; from Shanyin 山陰 in Guiji 會稽 county, i.e., Shaoxing, in Zhejiang). Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書 [Jingyue's Complete Books] (#11518), in 64 juan; first printed in 1700. Surviving eds.: Ming, 1710, 1711, Kangxi (6), 1750, 1768, 1783, 1784, Qianlong (2), 1819, 1821, 1853, 1854, 1855, 1858, 1863, 1868, 1872, 1874, 1879, 1883, 1890, 1892, 1894, Qing (14). ——. Leijing 類經 [Canon Arranged by Categories] (#00033), in 32 juan; printed in 1624.

Followed by Leijing tuyí 類經圖翼 (in 11 juan) and Leijing fuyi 類經附翼 (in 4 juan). In Zhang JYQ.

——. Zhi yi lu 質疑錄 (#10643) (first printed in 1688; colophon by Wang Qi 王琦 from 1704). Includes a "Biography of Zhang Jingyue" by Huang Zongxi. In Zhang JYQ 1833-57. 1688 (KX27) ed. at Guotu.

ZhangJYQ. Zhang Jingyue yixue quanshu 張景岳醫學全書. Editor in chief Li Zhiyong 李志庸. Ming-Qing mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1999.

Zhang Lu 張璐 (zì Luyu 路玉; bào Shiwan 石頑, Shiwan laoren 石頑老人). Yitong 醫通 (#05001; a.k.a. Zhangshi yitong 張氏醫通), in 16 juan; author's preface from 1695; other prefaces from 1693 and 1699, and 1709 (on occasion of print ordered by imperial rescript). Extant eds.: 1709 (2), Kangxi (2; one of which in Zhangshi yishu qi zhong 張氏醫書七種), 1801 (3; one of which in Zhangshi yishu qi zhong), 1804 (2), 1894 (2, one of which in Zhangshi yishu qi zhong), 1899, 1907 (2, one of which in Zhangshi yishu qi zhong), Qing (6, four of which in Zhangshi yishu qi zhong).

Zhang Lufeng 張魯峰 (fl. mid 19 $ ^{th} $ century; zǐ Jingtao 景濤; from Guiji 會稽 in Zhejiang). Aitang yihua 鶴塘醫話 [Words on Medicine from the Fragrant Pond] (#10737), in 1 juan, 1851. With a long appendix in 2 juan titled Aitang yihua bubian 補編 added by Cao Bingzhang 曹炳章 (1877-1956; zǐ Chidian 赤電, from Qin county, i.e., Ningbo in Zhejiang), the editor of ZYD. In ZYD-8.

ZhangLYQ. Zhang Lu yixue quanshu 醫學全書. Editors in chief Zhang Minqing 张民庆, Wang Xinghua 王兴华, and Liu Huadong 刘华东. Ming-Qing mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1999.

Zhang Rui 張銳 (zì Zigang 子剛). Jifeng puji fang 雞峰普濟方 (#02954), in 30 juan. 1133. The earliest surviving edition is an 1828 recension (at Guotu and CATCM) based on a southern-Song edition.

Zhang Sanxi 張三錫 (zì Shucheng 叔承; bao Siquan 嗣泉; from Nanjing), Yixue Liuyao 醫學六要 (#11591), in 19 juan; completed in 1609, first printed in 1610. Partial Wanli ed.

## PDF第683页

[1610] supplemented by author's son Zhang Weifan 張維藩 [Xue 1991 gives Zhang Weihan 張維翰] and others and printed in 1644 (CZ17) by the Baixia Qingzhao Hall 白下青照堂; ed. kept at Zhongguo kexueyuan (copy not signaled in Xue 1991) and photoreproduced in SKCM 46: 1-512.

Zhang Siwei 張四維 (zì Guoben 國本, biehao Yunting 筠亭; from Huaining 懷寧 county in Huizhou, Anhui). Yimen mizhi 醫門秘旨, in 15 juan plus one shou juan. Author's preface from 1576; other prefaces from 1577 and 1580; printed 1580/92. In RBXC: 611-814 (photolithographic reproduction of a Wanli edition from the Zhang's Hengde Hall 恆德堂 in Tong'an 同安 [Fujian?] held at the Kunaichō Shoryobu 宮內廳書陵部 [Archives and Mausolea Dept. of the Japanese Imperial Household Agency]), re-edited in simplified characters in HWHG 2: 1-323 (with added subtitles and many changes in characters).

Zhang Wenzhong 張文仲. Suishen beiji fang 隨身備急方. Seventh or eighth century. Lost.

Zhang Yuansu 張元素 (zì Jiegu 潔古). Yixue qiyuan 醫學啟源 (#00293), in 3 juan; date unknown. Yuan ed. at Shanghai library. Preface by Zhang Jifu 張吉甫, written after 1234 (the fall of the Jin, mentioned in the text) and probably before 1251 (Li Gao's death; Zhang Jifu claimed that the preface was commissioned by Li).

——. Zhenzhu nang 珍珠囊 [Sack of Pearls] (#02244), in 1 juan; date unknown.

ZhangYYQ. Zhang Yuansu yixue quanshu 張元素醫學全書. Chief-editor Zheng Hongxin 鄭洪新. Tang-Song-Jin-Yuan mingyi quanshu dacheng. Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 2006.

Zhang Zhongjing. See Treatise on Cold Injury; Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses; and Essentials of the Golden Casket.

Zhao Jizong 趙繼宗 (jinshi 1490; bao Jingzhai 敬齋; retired [because impeached] as Guangdong qianshi 僉事, probably Assistant Surveillance Commissioner [full title: Guangdong ancha qianshi 廣東按察僉事]; from Cixi 慈溪 in Zhejiang). Ruyi jingyao 儒醫精要 [The Essentials of Scholarly Medicine], in 1 juan (33 pian); author's preface from 1528. Photoreproduction of a 1648 Japanese edition kept at the Naikaku bunko (内閣文庫 Diet Library) in RBXC: 247-70. Ed. in simplified char. of that 1648 recension in HWHG 12: 453-91.

Zhao Liangren 趙良仁. See Zhu Zhenheng, Danxi yaoyao buowen 丹溪藥要或問.

Zhao Xianke 趙獻可. Yiguan 醫貫 (#00296), in 6 juan; 1617.

Zhao Zhuquan 趙竹泉. Yimen buyao 醫門補要 [“Supplementing the essentials of medicine”] (#05210), in 3 juan, 1897 preface by Ma Peizhi 馬培之. In ZYJ-4: 1061-1120.

Zhenjiu jiayi jing. See AB Canon of Acumoxa.

Zhenglei bencao 證類本草 (#02238), in 30 juan; 1111/18 (Zhenghe 政和 reign of Song Huizong); enhanced and re-edited several times after that. Compiled by a team led by Tang Shenwei 唐慎微. SKQS ed.

## PDF第684页

Zhongguo yiji kao 中國醫籍考. See Taki Mototane, Iseki kō 醫籍考.

Zhouhou fang. See Ge Hong, Zhouhou beiji fang 肘後備急方.

Zhou Xuehai 周學海 (1856-1906). Pingdian Ye an cun zhen lei bian 評點葉案存真類編 (#10121), in 2 juan; 1893.

Zhou Ying 周應. Jianyao jizhong fang 簡要濟衆方 [Simple and Essential Recipes to Succor the Populace]. First circulated in 1051. Lost.

Zhubing yuanhou lun 諸病源候論 (#00459), in 50 juan; presented to the throne in 610 CE. This is the title listed in the Old Book of Tang (47: 2049), which attributes the book to Wu Jing 吳景. Editions from the Song onwards ascribe it to Chao Yuanfang 巢元方, maybe on the basis of an earlier edition that made the same claim. Wu and Chao were probably both part of the team that compiled the treatise. Re-edited in 1027 as Chaoshi Yuanfang bing yuan bou lun 巢氏元方病源候論 on the basis of several circulating versions. A 19th-century Japanese ed. based on a southern-Song edition, as well as an incomplete Japanese handcopy of the same Song edition, are kept at the Palace Museum in Taibei. They are photo-reproduced in Kosovo Hiroshi 1981. I cite from the SKQS ed.

Zhu Duanzhang 朱端章 (from Changle 長樂 in Fujian). Weishengjia bao chanke beiyao 衛生家寶產科備要 (#06948), in 8 juan; 1184. An anthology of texts. A copy of the original ed. is held at Guotu. SKQS ed. There is also an 1871 ed. at CATCM. Also in Danggui caotang yixue congshu 當歸草堂醫學叢書.

Zhu Gong 朱肱. Shanghai baiwen 傷寒百問 [100 Questions on Cold Injury] (#00814), in 6 juan, 1107.

——. Shanghai leizheng huoren shu 傷寒類證活人書 [Life-Saving Book on Cold Injury, Classified by Symptoms] (#00813), in 22 juan. Prefaces from 1108 and 1111; revised in 1118.

Zhu Shijin 朱時進 (from Nanhui 南匯, south of Shanghai). Yijian nengyi 一見能醫 [Knowing Medicine at One Glance] (#05049), in 1 juan. Compiler's preface 1769. I cite from the ZGZC-2 ed., which is based on a 1942 hand copy kept at the Shanghai Zhongyiyao daxue.

Zhu Zengji 朱增籍 (zī Lantai 蘭台). Yizheng zhili 疫證治例 (#05946), in 1 juan; 1892.

Copies of one edition from that year survive (at CATCM).

Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358; zǐ Yanxiu 彥修, bao Danxi 丹溪, from Yiwu 義烏 in the Yuan-dynasty prefecture of Wuzhou 婺州, later called Jinhua 金華, in Zhejiang). Bencao yanyi buyi 本草衍義補遺 [Supplements to the Elaborations on Materia Medica] (#02248), in 1 juan; before 1358. Designed to correct and supplements Kou Zongshi's Bencao yanyi. Mentioned in Dai Liang's "Danxi weng zhuan" 丹溪翁傳 (q.v.; 1358 or 1359) but earliest known edition is from Yang Xun's Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (1450s; see below). Augmented (from 153 individual entries to 196), annotated, and edited (sometimes heavily) by Fang Guang 方廣 in Danxi xinfa fuyu (see below), the version from which all later editions are based.

## PDF第685页

—. Danxi michuan fangjue 丹溪秘傳方訣, in 10 juan; 1475 postscript by Zhang Yingdiao 張應雕. Contents close to the Jingui gouxuan, but in less polished form. Zhang says that the book was transmitted to the publisher by Zhu Zhenheng's descendant Zhu Sizhen 朱思貞. In HWHG-5: 157-229, based on a Japanese handwritten copy from the early Edo period [probably 1603] kept in the library of the Japanese Imperial Palace (日本宮内庁書陵部).

___ . Danxi shoujing 丹溪手鏡 (#04888), in 3 juan; printed in 1621. Falsely attributed to Zhu Zhenheng. Content very close to Mai yin zheng zhi (see that entry under Zhu Zhenheng).

—. Danxi xiansheng zhifa xinyao 丹溪治法心要 (#04910), in 8 juan; completed in 1543 (preface by Gao Bin 高賓). Edited by Gao Bin's nephew Gao Shuzong 高叔宗 (Zizheng 子正, bao Shishan 石山 [see Liu Shijue 1995: 113]; from Shaoxing in Zhejiang). Translated by Yang Shou-zhong 1993 (see Bibl. B). Earliest extant edition from 1556 (by Zhao Yingchun 趙應春 from Shaoxing [Jiangyin 江陰]) at CATCM.

——. Danxi xinfa [leiji] 丹溪心法〔類集〕，in 4 juan; 1450s. Compiled by Yang Xun 楊珣 (a.k.a. Yang Chuyu 楊楚玉), collated and corrected by Liu Xun 劉勛, published in Xi'an. Simply called Danxi xinfa when it was published. Title lengthened later to distinguish this book from the better-known version of the Danxi xinfa published in Huizhou in 1482. Survives in a 1508 ed. (still titled Danxi xinfa) by Lu Yi 盧翊 (a man from Jiangnan with an official post in Guilin) at Guotu [not in Xue Qinglu 1991; but see Beijing tushuguan guji shanben shumu 北京圖書館古籍善本書目, p. 1263]. Recent edition in simplified characters based on that edition (supplemented by a 19 $ ^{th} $-century Japanese ed.) by Liu Shijue and Xue Yiyan, Danxi yishu 丹溪逸書 (Shanghai: Shanghai Zhongyiyao daxue chubanshe, 2005), pp. 83-319.

——.[Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法]; 1465/81. $ ^{6} $ Compiled in Sichuan by Wang Jihuan 王季瓛 on the basis of Yang Xun's Shaanxi edition (see previous entry). Lost.

——. Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 (#04898), in 5 juan (one ed. in 3 juan); 1482. Compiled in Huizhou by Cheng Chong 程充, who consulted texts held by Zhu Zhenheng's great-grand-son Zhu Xian 朱賢. Based on Yang Xun's 楊珣 earlier Danxi xinfa [leiji] (see two above). Surviving editions from 1482, 1493, 1554, 1601 (in Gujin yitong zhengmai quanshu 古今醫統正脈全書), 1900 (from Danxi quanshu shizhong 丹溪全書十種), Qing.

—. Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附餘 (#04908), in 24 juan; completed in 1536. Compiled in Huizhou by Fang Guang 方廣 (zì Yuezhi 约之, bao Gu'an 古菴; from Xiuning 休寧 in Huizhou, Anhui) to supplement the Danxi xinfa.

——. Danxi yaoyao 丹溪藥要. A later name of Danxi yulu 丹溪語錄 (q.v.).

___ . Danxi yaoyao huowen 丹溪藥要或問 . Danxi yaoyao huowen 丹溪藥要或問 . Zhao Liangben's 趙良本 (zi Yide 以德; bao Yunju 雲居) annotated edition of a

## PDF第686页

manuscript titled Danxi yaoyao 丹溪藥要 that had circulated among Zhu Zhenheng's disciples (see previous entry, as well as 5.5, p. 166). Danxi yaoyao was itself the former title of Danxi yulu 丹溪語錄. In Shi Changyong 1979.

——. Danxi yi'an 丹溪醫按 (#10018), in 1 juan. Compiled at an unknown date, perhaps by Zhu's disciple Dai Sigong. Manuscript with 1377 preface at Suzhou yixueyuan library.

___. Danxi yibo 丹溪醫鉢 [Danxi's Bequest]. Lost.

——. Danxi yulu 丹溪語錄 [Recorded Sayings of Danxi]. A manuscript that circulated among Danxi's disciples after the master's death. Later called Danxi yaoyao and reedited by Zhao Liangben 趙良本 as Danxi yaoyao buowen (see these two titles above).

——. Danxi zuanyao 丹溪纂要 [a.k.a. Danxi xiansheng yishu zuanyao 丹溪先生醫書纂要, a.k.a. Danxi zuanyao and Danxi yiyao 丹溪醫要] (#04899), in 2 juan (some eds. in 4 and 8 juan); 1484. Compiled in Yiwu 義烏 (Jinhua, Zhejiang) by Lu He 盧和 (1440-1515; zhi Lianfu 廉夫; bao Yian 易庵; from Dongyang 東陽 in Jinhua). Said to be based on entries from the Gezhi yulun and fanglun recorded by disciples. Original ed. at CATCM library.

___. Fengshui wenda 風水問答, in 1 juan. Date unknown. Prefaced by Hu Han 胡翰 (1307-1381). In Liu Shijue and Xue Yiyan 2005.

——. Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 [Piecemeal Discussions on the Perfection of Knowledge Through the Examination of Things] (#10629), in 1 juan; 1347. Forty-two short treatises or “discussions” (lun論), some very influential, on various medical topics. Incompletely translated by Yang Shou-zhong and Duan Wu-jin 1994 (see Bibl. B). I cite from the SKQS ed. (vol. 746) and from Danxi yiji.

Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄 [Probing the mysteries of the Golden casket] (#04886), in 3 juan; probably completed after 1360. Based on notes written by Zhu Zhenheng; annotated and edited by Dai Sigong 戴思恭, with six supplementary discussions by the latter. Also called Pingzhi huicui 平治會萃 (as in the Xueshi yian edition; sometimes Huizui 薈蕞). Early surviving editions: 1485 (Shanyang Shen Chun 山陽沉純 ed.; at Shanghai library), Ming ed. by Shenxiu Hall 慎修堂.

——. Jufang fahui 局方發揮 [Elaborations on the Recipes of the (Pharmacy) Service]

(#10630), in 1 juan, 1347. A critique of the Taiping buimin beji jufang 太平惠民和劑局方

(→ Hejju fang). SKQS ed.

——. Mai yin zheng zhi 脈因證治 (#06186), in 2 juan (8 juan according to SKQS tiyao). Falsely attributed to Zhu Zhenheng. Largely based on Huang Jizhi's 黄濟之 (zi Shiren 世仁; from Yuyao 餘姚 in Zhejiang) Bencao quandu 本草權度 (completed in the early Zhengde reign [1506-], first printed in 1535). (See Liu Shijue et al. [2004: 94-96] and Jiang Chunhua 1980b: 8.)

——. Pingzhi huicui 平治會萃 (sometimes Huizui 薈蕞). See Zhu Zhenheng, Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄.

## PDF第687页

Zihe yiji子和醫集. Editor in chief Deng Tietao 鄧鐵濤. Beijing: Renmin weisheng chubanshe, 1994. Contains a 15-juan edition of Zhang Congzheng's Rumen shiqin 儒門事親 (q.v.).

ZPZZ. Zhupi zouzhe lijuan 朱批奏摺立卷 [“Palace memorials with vermilion rescripts”]. Held at the First Historical Archives in Beijing.

Zuanyao. See Zhu Zhenheng, Danxi xiansheng yishu zuanyao 丹溪先生醫書纂要.

ZYC. Zhenben yiji congkan 珍本醫籍叢刊. A collection in simplified characters at the Zhongyi guji chubanshe (Beijing).

ZYD. Zhongguo yixue dacheng 中國醫學大成 (#11912). 1936. Compiled by Cao Bingzhang 曹炳章. 10-volume edition in simplified characters at Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 1997.

ZYJ. Zhenben yishu jicheng 珍本醫書集成 (#11918). 1936. Edited by Qiu Qingyuan 裘慶元 (1873-1947; zǐ Jisheng 吉生; from Shaoxing in Zhejiang). Four-volume edition in simplified characters at Beijing: Zhongguo Zhongyiyao chubanshe, 2001. Vol. 1: Yijing, bencao, maixue, shanghan 醫經·本草·脈學·傷寒. Vol. 2: Tongzhi, neike, waike, fuke, erke lei 通治·内科·外科·婦科·兒科類. Vol. 3: Fangshu lei 方書類. Vol. 4: Yuan, zazhu lei 醫案·雜著類.

APPENDICES

## PDF第725页

# Appendix 1 Glossary of key terms

This is a bilingual glossary of terms I mention in the text and appendices of this dissertation. It identifies the Chinese equivalents of words that I discuss directly in English (e.g., the “Autumn Assizes”) and gives the English equivalents of many Chinese concepts. Most of the longer explanations are found in the English-language entries. Not all the terms explained in this glossary appear in the text; some entries are designed to provide supplementary explanations on legal and medical concepts that I find important. The glosses focus on the significance of these concepts in the history of Chinese approaches to madness.

The symbol “→” refers to other entries in this glossary. “Art. ####” is short for “the statute that is numbered #### in Huang Jingjia’s edition of Xue Yunsheng’s 薛允升 Duli cunyi 讀例存疑” (see 10, p. 411). A list of the legal articles I cite can be found in Appendix 7.

The Chinese glosses marked by an asterisk (*) are the names of medicinal ingredients. The botanical and pharmaceutical identification of these drugs can be found in Appendix 2.

accidental killing (guò shī shā rén 過失殺人): the least serious of the “six killings” (q.v.). From 1669 onwards, the crime of “killing because of madness” (q.v.) was sentenced like accidental killings because both crimes were defined as having been committed fortuitously, without any intention to cause harm. The mandated punishment for both penal denominations (q.v.) was the payment of 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim.

achyranthes: *niú xī牛膝

aconite : *fù zì 附子

Administrative Assistant (pàn 判 or yuàn pàn 院判): in the Ming and Qing dynasties, the second-highest ranking official in the Imperial Academy of Medicine (q.v.) after its Commissioner (q.v.); ranked 6a

affliction by evil (xié suì 邪祟): usually referred to possession by malignant spirits; in late imperial times, a commonly mentioned cause of madness symptoms among both physicians and common people; some physicians who wrote about affliction by evil sometimes believed that ghosts could cause madness; others just used this term to refer to possession-like symptoms that they attributed to regular organic processes (see Chapter 8)

## PDF第726页

“after the Assizes” (jiān bóu 監候, lit. “waiting in prison”): referred to death sentences that had to be reviewed at the annual Autumn Assizes (q.v.) before they could be executed. Because they were often commuted to exile, these postponed death sentences were less severe than those that were to be “executed immediately” (q.v.).

aged tangerine peel: *chén pí陳皮,*jú bóng 橘紅

aloe: *lú buì 蘆薈, lóng buì 龍薈

alum: *bái fán 白礬, míng fán 明礬, Jìn fán 普礬, Guó fán 虢樊

amber: *hũ pò 琥珀

àn 案: → case file

Anemarrhena: *zhī mǔ 知母

angelica: *dāng guī 當歸

Angelica Qi-stimulating decoction (dàngqūi chéngqì tàng 當歸承氣湯): a purgative (q.v.) remedy designed by Liu Wansu 劉完素 (ca. 1120-1200; see 4.1), who drew inspiration from the Great Qi-stimulating decoction (q.v.); composed of angelica root (*dàng guī 當歸), rhubarb (*dà buáng 大黃), licorice (*gān ǎo 甘草), and magnesium sulfate (*máng xiāo 芒硝).

antelope horn: *ling yáng jiǎo 羚羊角

arisaema: *nán xīng 南星, *tiān nán xīng 天南星, *dǎn xīng 瞻星

arsenic ore:  $ ^{*} $pi shí 砒石

asparagus: *tiān dōng 天冬, tiān mén dōng 天門冬

Assizes:  $ \rightarrow $ Autumn Assizes

astragalus root: *buáng qì 黄蓍, 黄耆, or 黄芪

attractylodes: *bái zhú 白术

attack (gōng 攻): a generic reference to aggressive therapies like purgation (q.v.), sweating (q.v.), and vomiting (q.v.), used mostly when patients were replete enough to withstand the treatment and when they suffered from an excess of pathological fluids. In late imperial times, attacking cures were the most common treatment for madness, which was most often attributed to the pathological action of Fire and mucus (qq.vv.).

Autumn Assizes (qiū shēn 秋黍): a conference that was convened annually in the Fall to discuss death sentences that had been postponed until “after the Assizes” (q.v.). These sentences were classified in one of four categories: “effective” (q.v.), “suspended” (q.v.), “worthy of compassion” (q.v.), and “staying at home to take care of one’s parents” (q.v.). People who had killed a senior relative in a fit of madness were usually sentenced to strangulation or decapitation (qq.m.) after the Assizes; their sentence was usually classified as “effective,” but they were often “exempted from the hook” (q.v.) and therefore not executed. Some death sentences postponed until “after the Assizes” were purely nominal insofar as they were always suspended, and eventually commuted to life.

## PDF第727页

exile  $ (q.v.) $. The sentence of mad killers, however, was not commuted but turned into permanent confinement  $ (q.v.) $.

avert-Wind: *fángfēng 防風

bái jīn wán 白金丸: → White-gold pill

Baikal skullcap: *buángqín 蒿芩

bài xuě 败血: → failed Blood

bamboo juice: *zhú lì 竹瀝

bamboo oil: *zhú yóu 竹油

bamboo shavings: *zhú rú 竹茹

bào guān suǒ gù 報官鎖錮: → declaration and confinement

bǎojiǎ 保甲: a sub-administrative system that the government used to supervise local society under the Ming and Qing dynasties. Baojia heads, who were not officially members of the bureaucracy, were supposed to register the local population and report to the local government about suspicious or criminal activities. After 1732, local headmen were made responsible for reporting mad people to the local authorities (→ declaration and confinement). If an undeclared person committed suicide or a homicide, the local headmen were sentenced to a beating with the heavy stick (q.v.).

bastinado:  $ \rightarrow $ heavy stick

bēi yòu 卑幼: → junior relative

bến hũ 本律: → root statute

bing 病: illness; also translated as disorder

bing 并: → piling on

bitter (kǔ 苦): one of the flavors by which medical drugs were classified; bitter drugs were usually classified as “frigorific” (q.v.), and were therefore used for getting rid of Fire, one of the main identified causes of madness in late imperial medicine

black beans: *bēi dòu 黑豆

black henbane: *làng dàng zì 莨菪子

black lead: *bēi qiān 黑鉛

Blood (xué 皿): the red liquid that flows in people’s veins, but also more generally to the Yin aspect of vital fluids (→ Qi). Composed of the character for vessel (mǐn 皿: a bowl used for alimentation) and a dot representing blood, xué originally referred to the blood of sacrificial animals. Physicians almost never attributed madness to Blood disorders. The only exception was the common attribution of postpartum madness (q.v.) to “failed Blood” (q.v.).

## PDF第728页

bonded statement (gān jié 甘結): lit. “willing bond,” a document written by a person involved in a legal matter to make guarantees or ascertain the facts of a case. The signatory was liable to punishments if the statement turned out to be false. Called gān jié because it often ended with the words “if there is any falsehood [in my declaration], I am willing to be punished” (如有捏飾 甘願坐罪) (Chang Wejen 1983, vol. 2: 108n7). The same type of report written by an official was called “stamped bonded statement” (yìn jié 印結, lit. “stamped bond”) because it was stamped with the official’s seal. After 1766, a bonded statement was required from the relatives and neighbors of a mad person to guarantee that this person had indeed been mad at the moment of committing a killing (see Appendix 8, 1766 [3]). After 1803, the same kind of affidavit was required from the relatives of the victim to ascertain that a killer had not faked madness (see 12.3 and Appendix 8, 1803 [2]).

bone-souls ( $ pò $ 魄): the “terrestrial souls” that were thought to reside in the bones of living persons and to stay there when that person died

bǔ補: → replenishing

bugbane: *shēng má升麻

bupleurum: *chái hú 柴胡

bù xǐng rén shì 不省人事／不醒人事 or bù zhì rén shì 不知人事：“unaware of people and things” or “unaware of human affairs;” close in meaning to bùn mí 昏迷。This “unconsciousness” could refer to someone who had fainted, but also to people who were unaware of their surroundings because of madness or febrile delirium。This pexression was more common in medical portrayals of strokes than in descriptions of madness, but it was a central theme in legal accounts of madness, since the key to whether killers would be sentenced as “mad” was whether they had been aware of their homicidal action ( $ \rightarrow $ “killing because of madness”).

bù yìng dǐ 不應抵: said of homicidal acts that did not warrant a death penalty; dǐ 抵 was short for dǐ mìng 抵命

bù zhī rén shì 不知人事: → bù xǐng rén shì

calamus:*chāng pù 菖蒲

calorific (wén 温): said of medicinal drugs that were thought to create a warming effect in the body ( $ \rightarrow $ “calorific replenishing”)

calorific replenishing (wēnbǔ 温補): also called “warm replenishing”; replenishing by means of calorific (or “warming”) drugs (see 6.3). Physicians used this kind of therapy to treat cases of madness they attributed to depletion (q.v.), but the main treatments for madness remained attacking cures (→ attack).

Cardiac Envelope (xin bāo 心包／心胞): in Chinese medical conceptions, referred to the envelope (the pericardium) that surrounded the Heart. Late imperial physicians often attributed madness to the invasion of the Cardiac Envelope by mucus (q.v.; → mucus confusing the openings of the Heart).

## PDF第729页

case file (àn 案): → medical case file

case record (àn 案): → medical case file

cassia bark: *ròu guì 肉桂, guì pí 桂皮

cat-tail: *pú buáng 蒲黄

centipede: *wú gōng 蜈蚣

chăn bòu 產後: → postpartum

chăn bòu diān kuáng 產後癲狂: → postpartum madness

channeling mucus (dǎo tán 導痰): a kind of therapy that consisted in expelling pathological

mucus by means of purgatives (q.v.) ( $ \rightarrow $ transforming mucus, vomiting)

chéng àn 成案: → completed case

chī 凝／痴：“idiocy,” “foolishness,” “folly”; in medical discussions, chī 凝 魔 mostly referred to idiocy, but in literature and common parlance, it usually meant foolishness in the sense of embracing aspirations that were out of proportion with what one could achieve.

chi 答: → light stick

chỉ mài 尺脈: "Foot pulse," proximal pulse ( $ \rightarrow $ pulse)

China-root: *fú shén 茯神, fú lìng 茯苓

Chinese asparagus: *tiān dōng 天冬

Chinese honey locust: *zào jiǎo 皂角

Chinese quinine: *cháng shān 常山

chizi 癡子 / 痴子: "idiot," "fool" ( $ \rightarrow $ chi 癡 / 痴)

chồng jũn 充軍: → military exile

chóng yáng 重陽: → doubling of Yang

chóng yīn 重陰: → doubling of Yin

cinnabar: *zhū shā 朱砂, chén shā 辰砂

citrus fruit: *zhǐ shí 枳實

cloud-souls (bún魂): the ethereal souls that were thought to assure some mental functions. Early medical texts like the Inner Canon claimed that these souls resided in the Liver. Some late-imperial physicians attributed cases of madness to "loss of soul" (shí bún 失魂) or to the "wandering of the souls" (yóu bún 遊魂) (see 8.5).

Cold (bán 寒): one type of external Qi that was thought to cause illness (→ Cold Injury)

Cold injury (shāng bán 傷寒): a wide array of disorders attributed to invasion by external Cold (bán 寒) and often characterized by “heat” (rè 熱) or fever. Also known as Cold Damage,

## PDF第730页

Cold-factor disorders, and febrile disorders. Cold-Injury medicine experienced a renaissance in the eleventh century after the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury was re-edited and printed by the Editorial Bureau for Medical Books (q.v.). Jin and Yuan physicians (see Chapter 4) were fervent admirers of the Treatise. Many remedies from the Treatise were used to treat madness, notably the emetic (q.v.) Melon-pedicle powder (q.v.) and the purgative (q.v.) Great Qi-stimulating decoction (q.v.).

collective implication (yuán zhuò 緣坐): the legal measure dictating that the relatives of people sentenced to death for serious crimes like sedition (q.v.) or rebellion would also be sentenced to death, exile, or slavery.

commissioner (shǐ使): in the Ming and Qing dynasties, the highest post in the Imperial Academy of Medicine (q.v.); ranked 3a in the early Ming, then 5a

## common alum: *bái fán 白樊

community compact (xiàng yuē 鄉约): in the early Qing dynasty the “community compact” (which had been created in the Song dynasty) was turned into a system by which local leaders could lecture to the local people about duty and morality. The tasks of the community compact sometimes included police work. Selected locally, the leader of the community compact was usually an elder; he was among the local headmen who were punished when a madman under their jurisdiction committed suicide or a homicide.

completed case (chéng àn 成案): a case for which a verdict had been approved by the Ministry of Punishments; published collections of such cases served as jurisprudence to guide future legal decisions. The common translation of chéng àn as “leading case” seems inaccurate, insofar as all cases that had been brought to an end—not just model ones—were referred to as chéng àn.

concealment (róng yǐn 容隱): the legal right to conceal offences committed by close relatives (as defined by Art. 32 of the Qing Code); sub-statute 292.14 (see Appendix 7) also used this term to refer to people who concealed a mad relative and refused to declare him or her to the authorities.

contraversion (m'逆): referred to the flow of a fluid in a direction contrary to its regular one, usually upwards; based on a conception of the body as a congery of dynamic flows; contraversion of pathological fluids into the chest or the Heart was a common cause of confusion or madness; also translated as "reversal" (Wiseman and Ye 1998).

## cooling: → frigorific

coroner (wǔ zhuò 仵作): the employees of the local yamen who were responsible for examining the corpse of victims of injuries or killings in order to determine how the victim had died, what weapon or object had been used to hit him or her, and which blow had been fatal; the “inquest” (jiǎn yàn 檢驗) was conducted according to rules strictly defined in the officially sponsored forensic manual, the Xiyuan lu 洗冤錄. Also translated as “ostensor” (Needham, SCC VI.6: 175).

corydalis tuber: *yuán bú 元胡

## PDF第731页

county (xiàn 縣): the lowest level of local administration in the Qing empire; governed by a magistrate (q.v.). Most homicides committed because of madness were first tried at this level, the lowest level at which trials could take place.

critically disabled (dǔ jǐ 篤疾): a category of people who were allowed to redeem certain crimes with monetary payments because of legally defined compassion for “incomplete” (不成) people. In the Tang Code, this category included people who suffered from insanity (diānkuáng 瘧狂 or 癲狂 [q.n.]). The category “critically disabled” still existed in the Qing Code (Art. 22), but I found no trace that it still included mad people.

crossing off with the imperial brush $ ^{1} $ (yù bǐ gǒu chú 御筆勾除; more commonly gǒu jué 勾決, “executing by crossing off”): the procedure at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.) by which the emperor crossed off the name of those who would really be executed on a list of criminals whose death sentence had been classified as “effective” (q.v.). Those who were “exempted from the hook” (q.v.) were not executed ( $ \rightarrow $ tíng zhǐ gǒu jué).

croton: *bă dòu 巴豆

cùn mài 寸脈: "Inch pulse," distal pulse ( $ \rightarrow $ pulse)

dà chéngqì tāng 大承氣湯: → Great Qi-stimulating decoction

dà nì 大逆: → sedition

dăn 丹: → elixir

Dān xī 丹溪: “Cinnabar creek”; Zhu Zhenheng's style name (bào 號), by which later physicians respectfully referred to him (4.4).

Danxi corpus: a body of medical works attributed to fourteenth-century physician Zhu Zhenheng; most of these works contained the word “Danxi” (q.v.) in their title; more broadly, I take this term to refer to all the works—including those written by Zhu’s disciples—that expounded on principles attributed to Zhu Zhenheng (see Chapter 5)

dăng gư (chéngqì) tăng 當歸承氣湯: → Angelica Qi-stimulating decoction

dǎo tán 導痰: → channeling mucus

decapitation ( $ zhǎn $ 軒): one of the two regular types of death sentences defined by the Qing Code. It was considered more severe than strangulation ( $ q.v. $) because it cut the body into two pieces ( $ \rightarrow $ dismemberment).

decapitation after the Assizes (zhǎn jiān bóu 斬監候; zhǎn bóu 斬候): the sentence of death by decapitation (q.v.) postponed until the Autumn Assizes (q.v.).

declaration and confinement (bàoguān suǒgù 報官鎖錮): the policy by which families had to declare their mad members (→ “people with madness illness”) to the local authorities and confine them preventively. Families had been asked to keep preventive guard over their mad members since 1689, but in 1732 family heads and Banner captains were ordered to declare mad people to their local official and to confine them even when they had committed no crime. This policy was made more severe in 1766 (see Appendix

## PDF第732页

8 under the relevant dates). It emerged and became more severe because the Qing state did not punish mad people physically even if when they had committed multiple homicides. This imbalance between serious criminal acts and their lack of punishment made better prevention appear necessary (see 11.4).

Decoction of the General (jiàngjūn tāng 將軍湯): → One-ingredient decoction

department (zhōu 州): a sub-prefectural administrative unit, slightly higher than the county (q.v.), but also governed by a magistrate (q.v.); the next higher administrative unit was the prefecture (q.v.).

depleted:  $ \rightarrow $ depletion

depletion (xū 虚): a pathological imbalance in the body that could lead to many kinds of illnesses (→ inner harm); it was usually treated by “replenishing” (q.v.); → repletion

deposition (gòng 供): the statements that everybody involved in a legal case made to officials during a trial; often called “oral depositions (kǒu gòng □供) even when recorded in writing. Also known as “confessions.”

deviant (xié 邪): in medical language, referred to processes that deviated from the “correct” (zhèng 正) ( $ \rightarrow $ deviant Qi, deviant Wind); later came to mean “evil” in reference to non-human forces ( $ \rightarrow $ affliction by evil).

deviant Qi (xié qì 邪氣, xié 邪): in medical conceptions, referred to Qi that was too abundant in places where it should not; could refer to pathological external Qi or to internal Qi generated by a pathological imbalance

deviant Wind (xié fēng 邪風): in cosmological and medical conceptions, deviant winds were winds that blew unseasonally and could therefore cause illness (3.3).

dĩ 抵: to redeem, requite, or compensate, as in the legal principle of “requital of a life” (dĩ mìng 抵命); short for dĩ cháng 抵償.

diaphoretics (hàn yào 汗藥, hàn yì 汗劑): remedies or single drugs that induce sweating (q.v.)

dī fāng 地方: the head of a dī fāng [ward?]; dī fāng were established in 1757 to relieve and supplement the bǎojiǎ (q.v.) in tax collection and police work (Hsiao 1960: 49); selected from the local population, dī fāng chiefs were theoretically in charge of 100 households (10 jiǔ 甲 of ten households each), but the number of families for which they were responsible varied from region to region; like other local headmen, they were punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick (q.v.) when a mad person in their jurisdiction committed suicide or a homicide.

dĩ mìng 抵命: the principle of “requital of a life” that applied to homicide cases in which a life was demanded in compensation for the life lost ( $ \rightarrow $ nǐ dĩ 擬抵, shí dĩ 實抵); same as cháng mìng 償命.

diān 瘼／顛／癲: an illness that remained paired with kuang (q.v.) for most of the last two millennia despite many changes in meaning. In the Inner Canon (first century BCE) and in medical treatises of the first few centuries CE, dian (or diān jí 巔疾／癲疾／癲疾)

## PDF第733页

referred to an epileptiform illness (3.1, 3.3). Also in the first few centuries CE, but outside medicine, dian 顛 was increasingly used to refer to crazy eccentricity (3.4). This semantic evolution and the continued association of dian with kuang in the compound dian kuang (q.v.) made it easier for the illness name dian to refer to something like madness. In the twelfth century, Liu Wansu 劉完素 (4.1) discussed dian and kuang as two illnesses caused and manifested by excessive emotions, making the two almost indistinguishable. Only in the sixteenth century did physicians attempt again to differentiate dian from kuang as two types of madness (6.5, 6.6). From the late sixteenth century onwards, dian prototypically referred to madness caused by depletion (q.v.). In the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, dian came to refer to pathological apathy or withdrawal (7.6), the meaning it has retained in Chinese medicine until today.

diān kuáng 顛狂／癲狂／癲狂：madness or insanity in a general sense, or the distinct illnesses of diān and kuáng (qq.rv.). Most medical treatises from the fourteenth to the nineteenth century included a section called diankuang, a term that authors usually treated as a compound.

dismemberment (líng chí 凌遲): one form of death penalty meted out for crimes that the Qing Code considered extreme: parricide (q.v.), sedition (q.v.), or rebellion. It was considered the most severe form of execution because it mutilated the body (→ decapitation, strangulation). [Also translated as “death by slow slicing” and “lingering death.”]

dodder: *tù sịz (这里原文本可能存在识别错误，原文本为：*tù sịz) 菟絲子

doubling of Yang (chóngyáng 重陽): the phrase by which the Canon of Problems (20 $ ^{th} $ problem) defined kuang (q.v.); physicians throughout the ages gave the “doubling of Yang” a number of different definitions

doubling of Yin (chóngyīn 重陰): the phrase by which the Canon of Problems (20 $ ^{th} $ problem) defined dian (q.v.); physicians throughout the ages interpreted the “doubling of Yin” in a number of ways

dòu shā 鬪殺: → killing in an affray

dried sick silkworm: *jiāng cán 疆蠶

dǔ jí 篤疾: → critically disabled

Editorial Bureau for Medical Books (jiàozhèng yǐshū jú)校正醫書局): the bureau of the Song government that was put in charge of editing for publication a number of important medical works (3.6), such as the Treatise on Cold Injury and the Basic Questions (see Bibl. A). The books that were so compiled formed the documentary basis of later medical debates (see chapter 4).

education commissioner (xuézhèng 學政, tǔdu xuézhèng 提督學政, tíxué dào 提學道): in Qing times, the education commissioner was the highest educational official in each province, in charge of managing schools, examinations, and examination candidates. Their constant movements and their cultural prestige made them favorite targets for grievance bearers, including men whom their families considered mad (see Chapters 14 and 15). Also translated as “Provincial Education Commissioner” (Hucker 1985: entry 6485, p. 498A) and “provincial director of education” (Brunnert and Hagelstrom).

## PDF第734页

“effective” (qíng shí 情實): “circumstances genuine,” one of the categories in which death sentences could be classified at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.). An “effective” death sentence was supposed to be executed, but it still had to be “crossed off with the imperial brush” (q.v.) before it could be carried out. The Chinese term for “effective” used to be qíng zhēn yīng jué 情真應決 $ ^{2} $ (“circumstances averred, should be executed”) but it was changed to qíng shí 情實 at the beginning of the Yongzheng reign (1723-1735) because of a “taboo” (huì 諱) on the emperor’s personal name (Yinzhēn 胤禛), the second character of which was homophonous with the zhēn of qíng zhēn.

elixir (dān 丹): the name of medical recipes that contained metals or minerals, especially cinnabar (*zhū shā 朱砂)

è lù 惡露: → noxious dew

emetic (tù yào 吐藥, tù jì 吐劑): drugs or remedies designed to induce vomiting; in Chinese medicine, their main purpose was to get rid of mucus (q.v.) blocked in the stomach or chest; they were therefore commonly used to treat madness, an illness that physicians often attributed to mucus blockage (→ “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart”)

èr chén tāng 二陳湯: → Two aged ingredients decoction

etiology: the cause of an illness or the study of illness causes

“executed immediately” (lìjué立決): said of death sentences that were to be executed as soon as they they been approved by the Ministry of Punishments (q.v.); these kinds of death sentence were more severe than those that were postponed until “after the Assizes” (q.v.).

“exempted from the hook” (miǎn gōu 免勾): referred to a situation in which a criminal who had been sentenced to death “after the Assizes” (q.v.) was not executed because the emperor did not cross off his or her name (→ crossing off with the imperial brush) on the list of criminals whose death sentence had been classified as “effective” (q.v.) at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.).

exile (liú 流): → life exile

fā kuáng 發狂: → fit of madness

failed Blood (bài xuě 败血): lifeless Blood; in medical conceptions, a pathological fluid that was thought to be present in a woman's body just after she had given birth (→ noxious dew). Instead of circulating, failed Blood could lodge itself in the viscera (causing dysfunctions) or flush upwards into the Heart (q.n.), causing symptoms similar to those of madness (→ postpartum madness). Physicians who detected the presence of failed Blood usually attempted to expel it.

false hellebore: *lí lú 藜蘆

fán lì 凡例: the general editing principle, usually found at the beginning of a book, where compilers explained the standards they had followed to collate and organize that book

## PDF第735页

fán rén 凡人: → unrelated person

*fáng fēng 防風: avert-Wind

*fáng jǐ 防己: fangji

fàng lùn 方論：“recipes and discussions [thereon]” or “recipes with discussions”; a common form of medical writing

fēng 風：“Wind”; one important cause of illness in medical conceptions until at least the Song; originally referred to moving air that could invade the body from outside (3.3), but was later reinterpreted as being caused by inner processes (see Chapter 4); was once the main identified cause of madness (→ fēng kuáng 風狂); later remained in popular names of madness like fēng diān 風顛, fēng kuáng 風狂, fēng jí 瘋疾, and fēng 瘋. After approximately the twelfth century, the Wind etiology of madness was marginalized by the appearance of new etiologies based on Fire, mucus, depletion, and emotions.

fēng 瘋: a “vernacular character” (su zì 俗字) and a general term for madness or insanity; took its roots in the character fēng 風 (→ Wind), which once referred to the most commonly identified cause of madness (3.3). The sound fēng (usually written 風) was commonly used in terms that meant madness (e.g. expressions like fēng kuàng 風狂 and fēng dian 風顛), to such an extent that fēng came to refer to madness even when used alone. The graphic transition from 風 to 瘋 in terms meaning madness became widespread only in the eighteenth century. The character 瘋 was also part of the compound fēng bìng 瘋病 (→ madness illness) by which Qing law officially referred to insanity.

fēng bìng (1) 風病: → Wind illness; sometimes meant “madness” (→ fēng bìng [2])

fēng bìng (2) 瘋病: → madness illness

fēng bìng shā rén 瘋病殺人: → killing because of madness

fengzi 瘋子: “madman”; a non-technical term used to refer to people who were considered crazy

field mint: *bò bé 薄荷

Fire (huǒ 火): one central aspect of human vitality and an important pathological factor; Zhu Zhenheng (4.4) defined Fire as excessive Qi; Fire was often identified as a cause of madness in medical writings, usually in association with mucus (q.v.). Fire was usually removed with purgatives (q.v.)

fit of madness (fā kuáng 發狂): the name by which physicians referred to madness caused by Cold Injury (q.v.; → shāng hán fā kuáng 傷寒發狂); outside medical circles, fā kuáng meant “going crazy”

Five Agents (wǔ xíng 五行): Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water, which were thought to follow or overcome each other in fixed orders ( $ \rightarrow $ mutual production, mutual conquest). The mapping of the Five Agents on the viscera ( $ \rightarrow $ Yin viscera, Yang viscera) allowed physicians to explain how viscera—and even emotions associated therewith—interacted with one another. Also known as the Five Phases and, mistakenly, the Five Elements.

## PDF第736页

five degrees of mourning (wǔ fú 五服): lit. "the five costumes"; → mourning system

Five Phases:  $ \rightarrow $ Five Agents

five punishments (wǔ xíng 五刑): in ascending order of severity as defined by the Art. 1 of the Qing Code, they were “beating with the light stick” (→ light stick) “beating with the heavy stick” (→ heavy stick), “penal servitude” (q.v.), “life exile” (q.v.), and “death” (→ strangulation, decapitation).

flower madness (buā diān 花癲): the term by which physician Chen Shiduo 陳是鐸 (fl. late 17 $ ^{th} $ century) referred to the inappropriate desires of some women (see 9.2).

foreign aloe: *yáng lú buì 洋芦薈

formulary (fāng shū 方書): a collection of medical remedies. Formularies were the dominant form of medical writing until at least the Song dynasty

forsythia: *lián qiào 連翹

fossil teeth: *lóng chǐ 龍齒

four diagnostic methods (sì zhěn 四診): looking (shì 視), asking (wèn 問), listening or smelling (wén 聞), and palpating (qiè 切), which referred to pulse-taking

Four-gentlemen decoction (sijunżi tàng 四君子湯): a remedy from the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (12 $ ^{th} $ century) composed of ginseng (*rén shén 人參), licorice (*gān cǎo 甘草), China-root (*fú shén 茯神), and atractylodes (*bái zhú 白朮). Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6) claimed that it was Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358; 4.4) remedy of choice for treating Qi depletion.

Four-ingredient decoction (siwü tàng 四物湯): a remedy from the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (12th century) made of angelica (*dàng gui 當歸), Sichuan lovage (*chuān qióng 川芎), peony root (*dān pí 丹皮), and steamed foxglove (*shóu dǐ 熟地). Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6) claimed that it was Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358; 4.4) remedy of choice for treating Blood depletion.

Four-seven decoction (sìqī tāng 四七湯): a remedy composed of pinellia (*bàn xià 半夏), China-root (*fú shén 茯神), purple perilla (*zǐ sū 紫蘇), and magnolia bark (*bòu pú 厚樸), which was supposed to be ingested with seven slices of ginger (*jiāng 薑) and one jujube (*suān zǔ 酸棗).

foxglove (*dì huáng 地黃): “raw foxglove” (*shēng dì 生地) and “steamed foxglove” (*shóu dì 熟地)

frankincense: *rǔ xiāng 乳香

frigorific (bán 寒): referred to drugs that were thought to have a cooling effect on the inner fluids; frigorific drugs were thus seen as good tools for fighting inner Fire (q.v.), a commonly identified cause of madness in late-imperial medicine.

fritillary: *bèi mǔ 貝母

## PDF第737页

fú zhì 服制: → mourning system

Gambir vine: *gōu téng 鉤藤

gān 肝: → Liver

gān jié 甘結:→ bonded statement

gardenia: *zhī zì 栀子 or buáng zhī 黄栀

gastrodia: *tiān má 天麻

gentian root: *lóng dǎn cǎo 龍膽草

ghost (guǐ 鬼): the phantom of a dead person; often blamed for causing madness either among depleted people or among people who had caused harm to others in this life or in a previous one

giant typhonium: *bái fù 白附

ginger juice: *jiāng zhī 薑汁

gold foil: *jīn bó 金箔

ginseng: *rén shēn 人參

gòng 供: → deposition

golden thread: *buáng lián 黃連

gou jué 勾決: → crossing off with the imperial brush

governor (xúnfǔ 巡撫): the highest official in a province (ranked 2b), though he was subordinate to the governor-general (q.v.), who controlled two or more provinces

governor-general (zǒngdū 總督): an official (ranked 2a, sometimes 1b) in charge of military and civil matters in two or more adjacent provinces

Grand coordinator (xúnfǔ 巡撫): from the 1420, high officials who were sent to provinces as delegates of the imperial court to supervise provincial agencies

Grand Council (Jūnji chù 軍機處): a central government institution created in the 1720s by the Yongzheng emperor to manage military campaigns; after that, became the main policy-making body of the Qing government.

Great Qi-stimulating decoction (dà chéngqì tàng 大承氣湯): a purgative (q.r.) recipe from the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury; its ingredients were rhubarb (*dà huáng 大黃), magnolia bark (*hòu pú 厚樸), trifoliate orange (*zhǐ shí 枳實), and magnesium sulfate (*máng xiāo 芒硝) (→ Angelica Qi-stimulating decoction, Peach-kernel Qi-stimulating decoction, Stomach-regulating Qi-stimulating decoction)

gù shā 故殺: → killing intentionally

guā dì sǎn 瓜蒂散: → Melon-pedicle powder

guān mài 關脈: "Pass pulse," medial pulse ( $ \rightarrow $ pulse)

## PDF第738页

gui 鬼: → ghost

gủi sùi 鬼祟: → demonic affliction

guò shī shā rén 過失殺人: → accidental killing

gypsum: *shí gāo 石膏

bàn 汗: → sweating

bào 號: “style name” or “studio name”; a name that literati selected in their adult or old age; usually chosen from the name of a place—a river (xī 溪), a garden, a mountain, a studio (zhài 齋), etc.; it often included a word that meant “old man” (wēng 翁, sǒu 叟, lǎorén 老人)

hare's ear: *chái hú 柴胡

Heart (xin 心): one of the five Yin viscera (q.v.), considered to be the seat of cognitive activities and the residence of the Spirit (q.v.). Madness was often seen as originating in a dysfunction of the Cardiac system, as when “mucus confused the openings of the Heart” or the “Spirit could not hold to its residence” (qq.vv.). The Heart was sometimes described as having seven openings and three “hairs” (máo 毛).

Heart Wind (xīn fēng 心風): a common name for madness, especially in the Song and Yuan dynasties; Xu Chunfu claimed in the sixteenth century that it was a popular equivalent of dianbing 癲病

heavy stick (zhàng 杖): one of the “five punishments” (q.n.) defined by Art. 1 of the Qing Code. Sometimes meted out on its own, it also accompanied sentences of penal servitude (q.n.) and life exile (q.n.). Divided into five degrees from 60 to 100 strokes, which were automatically reduced to 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 strokes. After 1689, when a mad person committed homicide, his or her relatives were sentenced to a beating of 80 strokes of the heavy stick (reduced to 30). After 1732, the sentence became 100 strokes (reduced to 40) for homicide and 80 strokes (reduced to 30) for suicide. (See Appendix 8 under 1732.) Also known as “the heavy bamboo.”

Héji jú 和劑局: → Pharmacy Service

hemp seed: *márén 麻仁

henbane: *làng dàng zì 莨菪子

heteropathic:  $ \rightarrow $ deviant

honey locust: *zào jiǎo 皂角

buà 化: → transformation

huā diān 花癲: → flower madness

huà tán 化痰: → transforming mucus

buăn jué 緩決: → suspended

## PDF第739页

huì shì 會試: → metropolitan examination

human placenta: *bé cbe 河車

bún魂: → cloud-souls

hún pò 魂魄: Cloud-souls and Bone-souls

immature orange: *qīng pí 青皮

immediate decapitation (zhǎn lì jué 斩立决; zhǎn jué 斩决): the sentence of death by decapitation (q.v.), to be “executed immediately” (q.v.)

immediate strangulation (jiǎo lì jué 絞立決; jiǎo jué 絞決): the sentence of death by strangulation (q.v.), to be “executed immediately” (q.v.)

immortal (xiān 仙): a type of being common in Taoist and popular lore; stories often described them as acting like crazy men or women. Also known as "transcendant."

Imperial Academy of Medicine (tùyī yuàn 太醫院): under the Jin (1115-1234), Yuan (1260-1368), Ming (1368-1644), and Qing (1644-1911) dynasties, a central government agency responsible for testing and training physicians for service at court and in regional governments. Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3), an illustrious disciple of Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358; 4.4), served as its Commissioner (q.v.) and trained other physicians there, contributing to the spread of “Danxi” (q.v.) medicine.

Inch pulse (cùn mài 寸脈): → pulse

inner harm (nèi shàng 内傷): a kind of illness caused by exhaustion, insufficiency or excess of food and drink, or emotional imbalances, all grounded in depletion; the pathologies caused by this type of imbalance were theorized by Li Gao 李杲 in the thirteenth century (4.2)

intentional killing (gù shā 故殺): → killing intentionally

jack-in-the-pulpit: *nán xīng 南星, *tiān nán xīng 天南星, *dǎn xīng 瞻星

jiān bóu 監候: → "after the Assizes"

jiāng jūn tăng 將軍湯: Decoction of the General; → One-ingredient decoction

jiǎo 絞: → strangulation

jiǎo bóu 絞候: → strangulation after the Assizes

jiǎo jiān bóu 絞監候: → strangulation after the Assizes

jiǎo jué 絞決: → immediate strangulation

jiǎo lì jué 絞立決: → immediate strangulation

jiào zhèng yī shū jú 校正醫書局: Editorial Bureau for Medical Books

jiǎo zui 絞罪: the sentence of strangulation (q.v.) or a crime carrying the penalty of strangulation

## PDF第740页

jīnsì 進士: → metropolitan graduate

jiǔ qīng 九卿: → Nine Chief Ministers

jujube: *suānzǎo 酸棗, zǎorén 棗仁

junior relative (bèi yòu 卑幼): in the mourning system (q.v.) as defined by the Qing Code, junior relatives were younger or subservient members of the family; their counterparts were senior relatives (q.v.); homicides against junior relatives were punished less severely than the same crime committed against an unrelated person (q.v.). From 1796, the crime of killing a junior relative in a fit of madness became permanent imprisonment (q.v.).

jǔrén 舉人: → provincial graduate

Jūnji chù 軍機處: → Grand Council

kè 客: in Chinese medicine, referred to intrusive Qi that was lodged where it should not. The meaning of ke as “intruder” or “invader” (not “guest,” as it came to mean later) followed similar meanings in the Xici 繫辭 and the Sunzi bingfa 孫子兵法.

kē duàn 科斷: to sentence a crime; similar to kē zui 科罪 and zhi zui 治罪

kě jīn 可矜: → “worthy of compassion”

kē zui 科罪: “to sentence”; similar to zhì zui 治罪 and kē duàn 科斷

killing accidentally:  $ \rightarrow $ accidental killing

killing in an affray (dòu shā 鬥殺): the crime of killing someone during a fight despite not having had the intention to kill ( $ \rightarrow $ killing intentionally); one of the “six killings” (q.v.); defined by Art. 290 of the Qing Code.

killing because of madness (fēng bìng shā rén 瘋病殺人): the crime of killing someone while in a state of madness, without any awareness of what one was doing (→ bù xīng rén shì 不省人事). This penal denomination (q.v.) first appeared in 1667. Starting in 1669, those found guilty of it were sentenced to paying 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of their victim to cover funeral expenses, as in cases of “accidental killing” (q.v.). Until 1786, even multiple homicides committed in a state of madness were judged under this law. Starting in 1803, the punishment for “killing because of madness” became strangulation after the Assizes (q.v.), a nominal death sentence that was automatically converted to permanent imprisonment (q.v.). The monetary payment was eliminated later in the nineteenth century.

killing by design (móu shā 謀殺): the crime of killing someone on purpose and with premeditation; the most serious of the “six killings” (q.v.), defined by Art. 282 of the Qing Code.

killing by mistake (wù shā 誤殺): the crime of killing someone while trying to kill another person; one of the "six killings" (q.v.); defined by Art. 292 of the Qing Code. Also translated as "killing by mischance."

## PDF第741页

killing intentionally (gù shā 故殺): the crime of killing someone on purpose during a fight (→ killing in an affray), but without premeditation (→ killing by design); one of the “six killings” (q.v.); defined by Art. 290 of the Qing Code.

killing in play (xì shā 戲殺): the crime of killing someone while engaging in playful activities that one knew to be dangerous, like martial arts or sword practice; one of the six killings (q.v.); defined by Art. 292 of the Qing Code. Also translated as “killing during roughhousing,” “killing during sport,” and “homicide through horseplay.”

“killing three people from a single family” (殺一家三人): defined by Art. 287 of the Qing Code; one of the Ten abominations (q.v.); punishable by dismemberment (q.v.). Until 1786, people who killed three people from a single family in a state of madness were

Korean ginseng: *Gāoli shēn 高麗參

kǔ 苦: → bitter

kuáng 狂: madness or mania. An illness name, but also a general term for madness outside of medicine. Its symptoms were described in detail in the Inner Canon (first century BCE), borrowing from descriptions of disorders of the Yang-brilliance tract (q.v.) (3.1.). In the first few centuries CE, physicians attributed kuang to Wind (q.v.; 3.3) or to invasion by demonic forces. Following a general shift in medical conceptions of illness, kuang became attributed to Fire and mucus (qq.vm.) from the thirteenth century onwards (Chapter 4). This etiology had become dominant by the fourteenth century (4.5) and has remained central until today in Chinese medicine (Chapters 5 to 9). Throughout its history, kuang was often paired with the illness called dian.

land-tortoise plastron: *gui băn 龜板

leading case:  $ \rightarrow $ completed case

 $ \hat{h} $例: a secondary form of legislation in the Ming and Qing dynasties; originally referred to jurisprudence that was supposed to guide officials in the applications of statutes (q.v.); started to be appended to the relevant statutes in the fifteenth century. Only after 1740, when they could be adduced in judicial judgments on a par with statutes, can  $ \hat{h} $ be strictly translated as “sub-statutes” (this argument is developed in Bourgon 1994). All the legislation concerning madness in the Qing dynasty took place within  $ \hat{h} $.

licentiate (shēng yuán 生員): the generic name of all students who had passed the county-level examination and were thus entitled to receive stipends from the local government; licentiates were unofficially known as xiùcái 秀才. I found several cases of licentiates who became mad because they had failed to pass the provincial examinations (q.v.).

licorice: *gān cǎo 甘草

lite exile (lùi 泒): one of the “five punishments” (q.n.) defined by Art. 1 of the Qing Code; exile could take place at distances of 2000, 2500, or 3000 li depending on the severity of the crime. All sentences of exile were accompanied by 100 strokes of the heavy stick (q.n.). Death sentences postponed until “after the Assizes” and that ended up not being executed were commuted to the highest degree of exile.

lì jué 立決: → “executed immediately”

## PDF第742页

light stick (chī 答): one of the five punishments (q.v.) defined by Art. 1 of the Qing Code.

Divided into five degrees from 10 to 50, automatically reduced to 4, 5, 10, 15, and 20 strokes. A beating with the light stick was less severe than a beating with the heavy stick (q.v.). Also known as light bamboo.

lily turf: *mài dōng 麥冬

limonite: *shé bán shí 蛇含石

ling 令: → ordinance

líng chí 凌遲: → dismemberment

lin yòu 鄰佑：“neighbors”; after 1732, they were among the people sentenced to a beating of the heavy stick (q.v.) when undeclared mad people from a family living in the vicinity committed a homicide or suicide.

liú 流: → life exile

liù bù 六部: → Six Ministries

liù fǔ 六府 or 六腑: the six Yang viscera (→ Yang viscera)

liù jūnzi tăng 六君子湯: → Six-gentlemen decoction

liù shā 六殺: → six killings

liú yǎng 留養: → "staying at home to take care of one's parents"

Liver (gān 肝): one of the five Yin viscera (q.v.), the Liver was associated with Wood among the Five Agents (q.v.). It developed into an important pathological nexus in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries (7.5). The Liver could be associated with the etiology of madness in a number of ways (see p. 296).

loquat: * pípa 枇杷

lovage: *chuān qióng 川芎

hì 律: → statute

“madness illness” (fēng bìng 瘋病): the official term by which Qing legal documents referred to madness ( $ \rightarrow $ fēng 瘋). Not a medical notion.

magistrate (zhī xiàn 知縣; zhī zhōu 知州): the chief administrator in a county (xiàn 縣) or a department (zhōu 州). The magistrate was in charge of maintaining order, collecting taxes, conducting the proper rituals, and administering justice at the local level

magnesium sulfate: *máng xiāo 芒硝

mài 脈: → pulse

mǎn 满 (1): “full” or “full extent”; placed in front of any of the lower four punishments of the Chinese code ( $ \rightarrow $ light stick, heavy stick, penal servitude, life exile) to refer to the

## PDF第743页

highest degree in that category of punishment; e.g. mǎn liú 满流 meant the highest degree of life exile, that is, exile at a distance of 3,000 li, preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy stick.

mǎn 滿 (2): “full”: refers to an uncomfortable sensation of fullness in the chest; close in meaning to mèn 懣 = 悶.

Mandarin orange peel: *jú pí 橘皮, *chén pí 陳皮

medial pulse (guān mài 關脈): → pulse

medical case file (y'an 醫案): the textual form in which therapeutic encounters were recorded and published; the records through which physicians explained how they had treated specific instances of illness. Elite physicians used case files to illustrate the precept that each instance of illness was unique and therefore required a unique treatment. Published case files were sometimes copies of the records that physicians left to the families of their patients. Formerly known as “successful treatment” (q.v.). Commonly translated as “medical case record” or “case history”; Christopher Cullen (2001) has proposed “medical case statement.”

melon pedicle: *guā dì 瓜蒂 or kǔ guā dì 苦瓜蒂

Melon-pedicle powder (guādi sǎn 瓜蒂散): an emetic (q.v.) remedy from the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury; made of melon pedicle (*guā dī 瓜蒂) and rice bean (*chì xiǎo dòu 赤小豆)

memorandum (shuō tiē 說貼): a form of document written by the Ministry of Punishments to provide guidance to the provincial bureaucracy on how to sentence particular crimes.

memorial: → routine memorial, palace memorial

metropolitan examination (huì shì 會試): the third and highest level in the sequence of civil examinations; those who passed it received the title of metropolitan graduate (q.v.).

metropolitan graduate (jìnshì 進士): the title one received upon passing the metropolitan examination (q.v.); also translated as “presented scholar.”

miễn gấu 免勾: → “exempted from the hook”

mica: *yún mǔ 雲母

military exile (chōng jūn 充軍): a punishment that consisted in sending a convict into military service on a distant frontier; was not part of the regular five punishments (q.v.), but was closest to life exile (q.v.) in nature.

Ministerial Fire (xiāng huǒ 相火): one form of Fire that appeared either in nature or in the body, where it was associated with the Heart (q.v.); Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (4.4) gave Ministerial Fire a central role in his explanations of both pathology and vitality

Ministry of Punishments (Xíngbù 刑部): the central government agency that was in charge of administering the legal system of the empire; one of the Six ministries (q.v.); also translated as “Board of Punishments” or “Ministry of Justice.” The Ministry played a

## PDF第744页

central role in the reforms that made homicides committed because of madness be punished more severely in the second half of the eighteenth century.

mint: *bò bé 薄荷

miscellaneous illnesses (zàbìng 雜病): a generic names for all disorders except for seasonal illnesses and skin disorders. This formation of this residual group of illnesses was made possible by the collapse of the category of “Wind illnesses” (q.v.) in the fourteenth century (see 4.7). Madness ( $ \rightarrow $ kuang) was considered part of the miscellaneous illnesses.)

mistaken killing (wù shā 誤殺): → killing by mistake

morning glory: *qiān niúzi 牽牛子

mourning system (fú zhì 服制): the system of kinship relations by which the Code defined the closeness between relatives within a family, and thus the severity of the punishments imposed for crimes like theft, injury, and homicide when they were committed inside the family; literally the “clothing system,” because each of the five degrees of mourning (wǔ fú 五服) was named after one type of cloth that was supposed to be worn in mourning. Crimes committed within the family were punished differently from crimes committed against unrelated persons (q.v.); the penalty was lighter when the victim was a junior relative (q.v.), whereas a crime committed against a senior relative (q.v.) was punished more severely than the same crime against a non-relative.

móu shā 謀殺: → killing by design

moxibustion (jiǔ 灸): the therapeutic cauterization of acupoints with moxa, i.e., mugwort [ài 艾] tinder
