mucus (tán痰): in Chinese medical conceptions, a pathological fluid that could cause illness by blocking regular circulation or invading almost any area of the body; the character 痰 originated from the compound dànyín 淡飲 (“moving fluid”), in which 淡 acted as a modifier. By the seventh century, dànyín referred to one kind of pathological fluid that was eventually written 痰飲. Tàn 痰 and yǐn 飲 came to be understood as two different kinds of fluids, respectively mucus and rheum (→ tàn yǐn 痰飲). Song physicians increasingly likened mucus to saliva and came to consider mucus as an important material cause of Wind strokes (q.v.). Mucus was the most important identified cause of madness in later imperial medicine (→ “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart”), often in combination with Fire (q.v.).

“mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” (tán mí xīn qiào 痰迷心竅): the process by which mucus was thought to cause confusion by blocking the “openings of the Heart” (q.v.). Mí may originally have meant “lost” in the sense of “being where it should not be,” but it came to refer to “confusion” (as in tán mí 痰迷, “mucus confusion”). This phrase became extremely common in late imperial texts. Also translated as “phlegmatic occlusion of the cardiac orifices” (David Hawkes’s translation of Dream of Red Mansions, vol. 3: 97); “phlegm confounding the orifices of the heart” (Wiseman and Feng 1998: 433); “phlegm clogging the orifices of the heart” (Sivin 1987: 349); “mucus blocking the orifices of the heart” (Sivin 1987: 192).

## PDF第745页

mucus-confusion disorder (tán mí bìng zhèng 痰迷病症): one term by which legal reports referred to madness (13.2); not a medical category

mutual conquest: the sequence by which the Five Agents  $ (q.v.) $ were said to overcome each another: Wood, Spleen, Water, Fire, Metal. When this sequence was mapped on the emotions that were associated with the five Yin viscera  $ (q.v.) $, it explained how emotions could curb other emotions.

mutual production: the sequence by which the Five Agents  $ (q.v.) $ were said to produce or follow one another: Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water; also called the cosmological sequence

Nèigé 内閣: Grand Secretariat

m $ \underset{・}{i} $ 擬: to mete out a penal sentence, sometimes said to have been “provisional” because it had to be reviewed and confirmed by higher courts or higher officials before it could be executed

ni 逆: 1) → contraversion; 2) → sedition

nǐ dǐ 擬抵: "to sentence to requital [of life]," i.e., to sentence to death for a killing

nián bào 年號: reign period

Nine Chief Ministers (jiǔ qíng 九卿): a group of high officials who convened to make difficult legislative or judicial decisions; in Qing times, the Nine Ministers were the presidents of the Six Ministries (liù bù 六部), as well as the heads of the Censorate (Dūcháyuàn 都察院), the Court of Judicial Review (Dàlǐsī 大理寺), and the Office of Transmission (Tōngzhèng sī 通政司). See Hucker 1985: 176, entry 1296.

Ningbo figwort: *xuán shēn 玄参

noxious dew (è lù 惡露): the discharges (lochia) that come after parturition; in medicine, a commonly identified cause of “postpartum madness” (q.v.; → failed Blood)

office of the provincial treasurer:  $ \rightarrow $ provincial treasurer

One-ingredient decoction (yī wù tàng 一物湯; also called Decoction of the General [jiāngjūn tàng 將軍湯]): a purgative made entirely of rhubarb (*dábuáng 大黃). It became a popular remedy for treating manic insanity after Wu Kun 吳崑 recommended it in his Yifang kao 醫方考 (1584).

openings of the Heart (xin qiào 心駁): the orifices that Chinese physicians thought existed on the surface of the Heart (4.6). Their blockage by mucus ( $ \rightarrow $ “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart”) was one of the most commonly identified causes of madness in late-imperial China both and in and out of medicine.

optimus (zhuàng yuán 狀元): the candidate who placed first in the triennial metropolitan examinations (q.v.).

ordinance (ling 令): a type of legal text that supplemented statutes (q.v.) in the classical legal tradition until the Ming dynasty. In Qing times, ordinances fell into desuetude and were replaced by sub-statutes (→ h 例) in their role as subsidiaries to statutes. In Tang times,

## PDF第746页

it was an ordinance that specified that mad people were one subset of “critically disabled” (q.v.) people to whom the Code granted the privilege to redeem their sentences with money in some circumstances.

over-replete: → repletion

over-repletion:  $ \rightarrow $ repletion

ox gallstone: *niú buáng 牛黃

palace memorial (zòn zhé 奏摺): a kind of official document created in the late seventeenth century, when provincial officials close to the Kangxi emperor (r. 1661-1722) were authorized to write to him directly instead of forwarding their reports through the Grand Secretariat. After the Grand Council (q.v.) was created in the 1720s, palace memorials were sent to the emperor through the Council. Only the highest provincial officials were authorized to write such memorials, and they could only use them to report on important matters of state. After 1761, parricides (q.v.) committed because of madness had to be reported by palace memorial. The cases of sedition (q.v.) I discussed in Chapters 14 and 15 were also reported through this channel.

parricide: the crime of killing one’s parents or paternal grand-parents, as defined by Art. 284 of the Qing Code. In 1761 (see Appendix 8), the Qianlong emperor order by edict that all men and women who killed a parent should be sentenced to death by dismemberment (q.v.) even if the had committed this homicide in a fit of madness.

peach kernel (*táo rén 桃仁)

Peach-kernel Qi-stimulating decoction (táorén chéngqì tāng 桃仁承氣湯): a recipe for women inspired by the Greater Qi-stimulating decoction, with which it shares two ingredients; composed of peach kernel (*táo rén 桃仁), rhubarb (*dà huáng 大黃), licorice (*gān cǎo 甘草), and cassia bark (*ròu guì 肉桂)

pearls: *zhēn zhuā珍珠

peel of citrus fruit: *zhǐ ké 枳殼

penal denomination (zui ming 罪名): the name of crimes as defined by the Qing Code. These were the legal categories through which officials sentenced criminal acts. Each penal denomination carried a fix sentence. Mad homicides were usually sentenced under the penal denomination “killing because of madness” (q.v.). After 1756, other penal denominations—notably statutes (q.v.) concerning assaults against senior relatives (q.v.) were found relevant to sentence some homicides committed because of madness

penal servitude (tú徒): one of the “five punishments” (q.v.) defined by Art. 1 of the Qing Code. Divided into five degrees, penal servitude lasted from one to three years, each accompanied by a set amount of strokes of the “heavy stick” (q.v.). Penal servitude was less severe than “life exile” (q.v.) but more severe than a beating with the heavy stick (q.v.).

peony root bark: *dānpí丹皮

“people with madness illness” (fēng bìng zhī rén 瘋病之人): the name by which mad people were referred to in the preventive confinement policy of the Qing dynasty ( $ \rightarrow $)

## PDF第747页

declaration and confinement). This appellation was a projection from the penal denomination  $ (q.v.) $ “killing because of madness illness”  $ (q.v.) $.

permanent confinement (yǒngyuǎn jiāngù 永遠監錮): became the official punishment for "killing because of madness" in 1762. People whose sentence of strangulation or decapitation (qq.w.) "after the Assizes" (q.v.) was not executed were also sentenced to permanent confinement.

Pharmacy Service (Héjǐ jǔ 和劑局): the “Bureau for mixing [和 pronounced hǒu] remedies,” an agency of the central government during the Song dynasty. It published the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service sometime between 1107 and 1110 (see Hejǐu fang in Bibl. A); it was renamed “Pharmacy Service to Benefit the People” (Huìmín héjǐu 惠民和劑局) in 1148. Also translated as Pharmaceutical Service (Hucker 1985: entry 2169), Pharmaceutical Bureau (Leung 2003a: 376), Medical Bureau (ibid.: 379), and Office of Pharmacy (Leung 1987: 136).

phellodendron: *buángbái 黄柏

phlegm:  $ \rightarrow $ mucus

piān 篇: one "chapter" in some early texts like the Basic Questions and the Divine Pivot (see Bibl. A)

picrorhiza: *bú buánglián 胡黄連

piling on (bìng 并): the undue dominance of a pathological factor in a given location; used mostly in ancient texts like the Inner Canon to describe the interactions between Yin and Yang inside the body

pinellia: *bàn xià 半夏

placenta:  $ \rightarrow $ human placenta

plotting to kill (móu shā 謀殺): → killing by design

pò 魄: → bone-souls

postpartum (chanhòu 產後): for a woman, the period that followed childbirth; in medical conceptions, the postpartum condition was characterized by depletion (q.v.) and the incomplete expulsion of fluids like “noxious dew” and “failed Blood” (qq.vw.)

postpartum madness (chǎnhòu diǎnkuáng 產後癲狂): the madness that struck some women in the postpartum (q.v.) period (9.3); it was usually explained by the action of “failed Blood” or “noxious dew” (qq.v.) on the Heart (q.v.)

prefect (zhǐfǔ 知府): the official in charge of a prefecture

preventive confinement:  $ \rightarrow $ declaration and confinement

provincial examination (xiàng shì 鄉試): the examination held in provincial capitals every three years; only licentiates (q.v.) and students of licentiate rank were allowed to participate; becoming a provincial graduate (q.v.) made one eligible to participate in the metropolitan examination (q.v.); passing the provincial examination was an important step toward finding employment in the government, but the rising number of

## PDF第748页

candidates and stagnant quota of graduates made the provincial examination an important bottleneck for upward social mobility during the Qing dynasty.

provincial graduate (jǔ rén 舉人): the title that a licentiate (q.v.) received when he passed the provincial examination (q.v.); provincial graduates could participate in the metropolitan examinations (q.v.).

provincial judge (àn chá shí 按察使; full name: tí xíng àn chá shí sī shí 提刑按察使司使;

informal name: niè sī 臬司): the highest legal official in the province under the governor (q.v.). Also translated as "Surveillance Commissioner" (Hucker 1985: entry 12) and "judicial commissioner."

provincial military commander (tí dū 提督): the highest purely military official in provinces (ranked 1b after 1752), in charge of the Green Standards, the regular Chinese troops of the Qing state

provincial treasurer (bù zhèng shǐ 部政使; full name: chéng xuān bù zhèng shǐ sī shǐ 承宣布政使司使; informal name: fàn sī 藩司): the second highest official in a province after the governor. Also translated as “Provincial Administration Commissioner” (Hucker 1985: entries 487 and 4770) and “lieutenant-governor.”

pulse (mài 脈): the main source of diagnostic information for a Chinese physician, who relied on the configuration of the pulse to determine what imbalances were causing a patient's symptoms. The pulse was palpated with three fingers on the radial artery. In order of ascending distance from the wrist, they were the Chi pulse (chǐ mài 尺脈), the Guan pulse (guān mài 關脈), and the Inch pulse (cìn mài 寸脈).

purgation: a medical therapy that consisted in “bringing down” (xià 下) or purging (xiè 瀉) pathological fluids; this was the main way to get rid of Fire (q.v.); purgatives could also be used to remove mucus (→ channeling mucus); purgation was one of the most important therapies for madness in Chinese medicine in the last millennium.

purgative: → purgation

purple perilla: *Ặt sū 紫蘇

purple leadwort: *zǐ xué dān 紫血丹

purple river-chariot (human placenta) (zǐ béchē 紫河車): *hé chē 河車

qíng shí 情實: → “effective”

qíng zhēn 情真: → “effective”

qiū shěn 秋審: → Autumn Assizes

raw foxglove: *shēng dì 生地

red-rooted sage: *dān shēn 丹参

refuse of indigo: *qīng dài 青黛

## PDF第749页

reign period (nián bào 年號): the kind of title under which an emperor reigned (e.g., Qianlong 乾隆); some people whom their families considered mad gave themselves "reign periods" in their fantasies; imperial officials interpreted this act as sedition (q.v.) and usually sentenced these men very severely.

replenishing (bǔ 補): a type of medical therapy designed to get rid of pathological depletion (q.v.); in early times, it meant “filling,” as opposed to xiè 瀉 (“draining,” later purging [→ purgation]). Madness that was attributed to emotional disappointments was often treated by “calorific replenishing” (q.v.).

replete: → repletion

repletion (shí實): referred to the normal state of repletion (i.e., non depletion) of a healthy person, but more often to over-repletion, a pathological condition characterized by over-abundance of deviant Qi. Most physicians attributed madness to over-repletion of Fire or mucus (qq.mv.) and treated it with purgation.

rhinoceros horn: *xī jiǎo 犀角

rhubarb: *dà huáng 大黃

rice bean: *chì xiǎo dòu 赤小豆

river chariot (human placenta): *bé chē 河車

rock sweetflag: *shí chāng pú 石菖蒲

róng yǐn 容隱: → concealment

root statute (běn lǚ 本律): “the appropriate [statutory] provision in the code to be followed in case of a crime” (Sun 1961: 281; square brackets in the original); for example, after 1756, the killing of one’s older brother because of madness was no longer to be judged according to the sub-statute (→ lǚ 例) on “killing because of madness” (q.v.), but according to the “root statute” called “Striking a senior relative of the second degree of mourning” 黜期親尊長 (Art. 318; see 12.2)

routine memorial (tí běn 題本, bù běn 部本): official reports written either by high provincial officials (tí běn 題本) or by central Ministries (bù běn 部本) in response to the latter. Reports from provincial officials were sent to the Grand Secretariat, which forwarded them to the relevant Ministry for assessment. Most homicides committed because of madness during the Qing dynasty were discussed and processed in this type of document.

Rúyí儒醫: scholarly (or “Confucian”) physician; a label for doctors who claimed that their medical practice represented conventional value, especially benevolence (rén仁) toward others and respect for hierarchical relations within the family; these physicians also often tried to integrate precepts from the Confucian Classics (ofte the Yijing 易經, or Book of Transformations) into their medical doctrines. But even these doctors could attribute madness to possession by external entities (8.5).

safflower: *bóng buā 紅花

## PDF第750页

Sagely Duke of Yan (Yǎn shèng gōng 衍聖公): the nobility title of the patrilineal descendant of Confucius, who lived in Qufu 曲阜 (Shandong) and had been the object of an official state cult since the Song dynasty.

saliva: xián 涎; a fluid inside the body; in Song medicine, saliva was imagined to act up during Wind strokes (q.v.); the pathological roles of saliva were soon merged with those of mucus (q.v.)

Saliva-thinning powder (xī xián sǎn 稀涎散): an emetic remedy invented in the eleventh century; it was composed of honey locust (*zào jiǎo 皂角) and alum (*bái fán 白樊); its later variants all retained honey locust as an ingredient

sản fă sĩ 三法司: → Three High Courts

sàng xīn bìng kuáng 喪心病狂: “having lost one's mind and being crazy”; in Qing legal reports, a strong term of abuse applied to rebels who had “lost their heart and mind”; not a medical notion.

Scraps-of-cast-iron beverage (shèng tiě luò yǐn 生鐵洛飲; later written 生鐵落飲): one of the rare medicinal recipes given in the Inner Canon (see Basic Questions 13.2b, in pian 46), it was recommended for the treatment of madness; consisted of mixing scraps of cast iron with water.

script: a scenario according to which a process or a series of events was understood or expected to unfold

scutellaria: *buáng qín 黄芩

sedition (nì 逆; dà nì 大逆): as defined by Art. 254 of the Qing Code, the crime of going against the emperor. Many men whom their relatives considered mad were sentenced to dismemberment (q.r.) for sedition because they had written words that officials interpreted as usurping the symbolic trappings of imperial rule (Chapter 15).

senega: *yuǎn zhì 遠志

senior relative (zūn zhāng 尊長): relatives to whom one was subservient, as defined by the Qing Code; they were divided into five degrees (→ mourning system); homicides against senior relatives were punished more severely than the same killing committed against an unrelated person (q.v.); starting in the late 18 $ ^{th} $ century, the fact that a victim was one's senior became a central factor in determining the punishment of homicides committed because of madness (12.2)

shàng bán 傷寒: → Cold Injury

shāng hán fā kuáng 傷寒發狂：“fit of madness caused by Cold Injury,” “going crazy because of Cold Injury”; a medical category that first appeared in the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury. Qing law considered homicides committed during such fits as equivalent to “killings committed because of madness” (q.v.).

shén bù shǒu shě 神不守舍：“the Spirit does not keep to its lodging”; the failure of the Spirit (q.v.) to remain in the Heart and fulfill its normal functions; this phrase was used in medical explanations of mental dysfunctions. The Spirit could be chased from its

## PDF第751页

lodging by a variety of fluids, including mucus, Fire, and inner Wind. Translators of ancient texts usually render shě as “abode” instead of “lodging.”

shén míng 神明: spiritual clarity

shēngyuán 生員: → licentiate

shí 實: → repletion

shí dī 實抵：“actual requital [of a life],” i.e., an effective death sentence; referred to death sentences that could be actually executed, as opposed to redeemable or nominal death sentences that were routinely commuted; dī was short for dī ming 抵命，“requital with one's life”

sới è 十惡: → Ten abominations

shrubby sophora: *kǔ shēn 苦参

shuō tiē 說貼: → memorandum

Sichuan lovage: *chuān qióng 川芎

sick silkworm: *jiăng cán 疆蠶

sì jūnzi tăng 四君子湯: → Four-gentlemen decoction

silver foil: *yín bó 銀箔

sì wù tăng 四物湯: → Four-ingredient decoction

six fu (liù fǔ 六府 / 六腑): → Yang viscera

Six-gentlemen decoction (liù jūnzi tàng 六君子湯): the Four-gentlemen decoction (q.v.) enhanced with the anti-mucus ingredients pinellia (*bàn xià 半夏) and aged tangerine peel (*chén pí 陳皮); the equivalent of the anti-mucus (*Two-aged-ingredients decoction*) (q.v.) enhanced with ginseng (*rén shēn 人參) and atractylodes (*bái zhú 白术).

six killings (liù shā 六殺): the scheme by which legal commentators usually classified homicides. In descending order of gravity, they were “killing by design,” “killing intentionally,” “killing in an affray,” “killing by mistake,” “killing in play,” and “killing by accident” (see individual entries).

Six Ministries (liù bù 六部): the six government agencies that formed the “administrative core of the central government” (Hucker 1985: entry 3805, p. 318B); they were the Ministries of Revenue (Hùbù 戶部), Personnel (Libù 吏部), Rites (Libù 禮部), War (Bīngbù 兵部), Punishments (Xīngbù 刑部; → Ministry of Punishments), and Works (Gōngbù 工部).

sì zhěn 四診: → four diagnostic methods

small commentary (xiǎo zhù 小注): the commentary in small fonts that was added to the text of the Code in 1646; it was based on semi-official commentaries that had been used in the Ming dynasty (1368-1644).

## PDF第752页

specter (suì 祟): → spectral affliction

spectral affliction (suì 崇): in medical terminology, symptoms that looked as if they are caused by possession; sometimes translated as “curse,” but should be distinguished from illnesses intentionally inflicted by a magical curse. Originally referred to any illness caused by ancestral spirits; in late imperial China, a close equivalent of demonic affliction and affliction by evil (qq.vv.)

Spirit (shén 神): in medical terminology, referred both to mental functions (the “psyche”) and to the refined fluid that was thought to assure mental functions; was believed to reside in the Heart (q.v.); also referred to a person’s general vitality. Outside medicine, shen referred to spirits or “numinous” (Peterson 1982: 103-4) entities.

Spiritual clarity (shén míng 神明): the highest cognitive function; dysfunctions of “Spiritual clarity” led to manifestations of madness. Could also be rendered as “spiritual brilliance” or “numinous clarity”

stasis (yù 鬱): the stagnation of some fluids inside the body. Although it could be associated with depressed affects, stasis referred to the physical substrate of these affects and to other forms of inner stagnation that had no emotional content.

statute ( $ \dot{\hat{u}} $ 律): the fundamental laws in Chinese legal Codes ( $ \rightarrow $ ordinance,  $ \hat{l} $ 例); taken together, the statutes defined a network of crimes and the penalties that went with them. Statutes were where the Six killings (q.v.) were defined. Starting in the 1750s, the killing of senior relatives (q.v.) in a state of madness started to be sentenced according to statutes rather than to the sub-statute ( $ \rightarrow $  $ \hat{l} $ 例) on “killing because of madness” (q.v.). ( $ \rightarrow $ root statue 本律)

“staying at home to take care of one’s parents” (liú yǎng 留養): one of the four categories in which death sentences could be classified at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.). It was short for cún liú yǎng qín 存留養親, a term that was defined in Art. 18 of the Qing Code. This measure applied only to men who were single sons and whose parents or paternal grand-parents were old (qín lǎo dìng dàn 親老丁單). From 1792 onwards, people who had been sentenced to permanent imprisonment for “killing because of madness” (q.v.) could be liberated a few years after they had recovered from madness if they were single sons with aging parents (see Appendix 8, 1792 [1] and 1801).

steamed foxglove: *shóu dì 熟地

Stomach-regulating Qi-stimulating decoction (tiáowèi chéngqì tàng 調胃承氣湯): a purgative recipe from the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury, close to the Greater Qi-stimulating decoction (q.v.), with which it shared three ingredients; composed of rhubarb (*dà huáng 大黃), licorice (*gān cǎo 甘草), and magnesium sulfate (*máng xiāo 芒硝).

strangulation (jiǎo 絞): one of the two regular forms of death sentence defined by the Qing Code. It was considered less severe than decapitation (q.v.) because it left the body in one piece.

strangulation after the Assizes (jiǎo jiān bóu 絞監候; jiǎo bóu 絞候): the sentence of death by strangulation (q.v.) postponed until the Autumn Assizes (q.v.)

## PDF第753页

struck by Wind: → Wind stroke

sub-statute:  $ \rightarrow $ h 例

successful treatment (zhiyán 治驗): the main name by which medical case records were known before the locution “medical case file” (q.v.) became more common in the sixteenth century.

sui 祟: → spectral affliction

summoning the cause: → zhù yóu 祝由

suspended (buǎn jué 缓决): “execution suspended,” one of categories in which death sentences could be classified at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.); a set number of years of suspended execution led to an automatic commutation from death to life exile (q.v.). The sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (q.v.) that was imposed to people found guilty of “killing because of madness” (q.v.) after 1803 was automatically classified as “suspended”, but it was converted to permanent imprisonment (q.v.) for fear mad killers would stir up trouble in their place of exile.

sweating (hàn 汗): 1) emitting sweat; 2) one form of therapy designed to disperse pathogens near the surface (biǎo 表) of the body; one of the “three therapies” (sān fā 三法) from the third-century Treatise on Cold Injury that Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) advocated to treat all illnesses (see 4.2.).

sweetflag: *chāng pù 菖蒲, *shí chāng pú 石菖蒲

tabasheer: *zhú huáng 竺黄, tiān zhú huáng 天竺黄

tài yí 太醫: Imperial Physician

tài yī yuàn 太醫院: → Imperial Academy of Medicine

tán痰:→ mucus

tangerine peel: *júbóng 橘紅 or *chén pí 陳皮

tán mí bìng zhèng 痰迷病症: → “mucus-confusion disorder”

tán mí xin qiào 痰迷心竅: → “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart”

tán sùi 痰祟: “mucus specters,” or possession symptoms caused by mucus; referred to a conception saying that mucus causes possession-like symptoms (8.3); this notion was developed systematically by Zhu Zhenheng (4.4) in his Gezhi yulun 格致餘論 (1347)

tán yǐn 痰飲: 1. originally 淡飲: “moving water”; 2. two different kinds of pathological fluids: mucus (q.v.) and rheum, or thick and serous mucus

Ten Abominations (shí è 十惡): ten crimes defined as extremely evil by Art. 2 of the Qing Code; not all of them were punishable by death; included rebellion, sedition (q.v.), and, under the appellation bù dào 不道, “killing three persons from a single family” (q.v.)

thesium: *băiru căo 百蕊草, xiǎo cǎo 小草

## PDF第754页

Three High Courts (sản fã sĩ 三法司): the collective name of the Ministry of Punishments (q.v.), the Censorate (dụ chá yuàn 都察院), and the Court of Judicial Review (dụ lĩ sĩ 大理司). In the Qing judicial system, a death penalty required the joint approval of all three agencies. The Three Courts jointly confirmed or rejected provincial verdicts in a single “Ministry memorial” (bù běn 部本; → routine memorial).

tí běn 題本: → routine memorial

tiáo wèi chéngqì tăng 調胃承氣湯: → Stomach-regulating Qi-stimulating decoction

tíng jué 停決: → tìng zhǐ gōu jué

ting zhī gōu jué 停止勾决: better known as tìng jué 停决); referred to when the emperor did not cross off (→ crossing off with the imperial brush) the name of a convict from a roster of people whose death sentences had been classified as “effective” (q.v.) at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.); this meant that the convict in question would not be executed that year. If a convict’s name was not crossed off two years in a row, his or her sentence was diminished from “effective” to “suspended” (q.v.).

tóngzi 童子: child medium

tortoise plastron: *gui băn 龜板

traditional Chinese medicine: a modern construct and the quasi-official English translation of Zhōngyī 中醫 [“Chinese medicine”]

tree peony: *dān pí 丹皮

transformation (buà 仳): medically “transforming” something was to make it disappear or to alter it so as to render it assimilable (Grand Ricci, entry 4896, vol. III: 133B) ( $ \rightarrow $ transforming mucus)

transforming mucus (huà tán 化痰): getting rid of mucus (q.v.) by drugs that were supposed to transform (→ transformation) it internally (→ channeling mucus, vomiting).

trichosanthes: *guā lóu 瓜蔞

trifoliate orange *zbi shí 枳實

true yaliēn: *yǎ lián 雅連

tú徒: → penal servitude

tù 吐: 1) vomiting; 2) induced vomiting ( $ \rightarrow $ vomiting)

turmeric: *yù jīn 鬱金

Two aged [ingredients] decoction (ěrchén tàng 二陳湯): a remedy from the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (12 $ ^{th} $ century) made of pinellia (*bàn xià 半夏), red tangerine peel (*jú bóng 橘紅), China-root (*fú shén 茯神), and licorice (*gān cǎo 甘草); Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510) claimed that it was Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) remedy of choice for treating mucus (q.v.).

## PDF第755页

typhonium: *bái fù 白附

unrelated person (fán rén 凡人): in Qing law, a person who was not an official and who had no kinship ties to a criminal; more specifically, someone who fell outside the “five degrees of mourning” (wǔ fú 五服) defined by the Code (→ mourning system). In 1784, the Ministry of Punishments (q.v.) decreed that the crime of “killing because of madness” (q.v.) only applied to people who had killed an unrelated person in a fit of madness, but not to the killing of senior relatives (q.v.).

urine deposit preparation: *qiū shí 秋石

vomiting (tù吐): a kind of therapy that consisted in making the patient vomit accumulated substances with emetic (q.v.) remedies like Melon-pedicle powder (q.v.) or White-gold pill (q.v.). The main target of induced vomiting was mucus (q.v.) that physicians thought was accumulated in the stomach or the chest. Madness was commonly treated with emetics because it was often attributed to the action of mucus (→ mucus confusing the openings of the Heart, channeling mucus, transforming mucus).

vomitive:  $ \rightarrow $ emetic

wēn 温: 1) → calorific; 2) → warm factor disorder

wēnbu 温補: → calorific replenishing

wèn nǐ 問擬: to mete out a [provisional] sentence

white alum:  $ \rightarrow $ alum

White-gold pill (bái jìn wán 白金丸): a Song-dynasty recipe made of white alum (*bái fán 白樊) and turmeric (*yù jìn 鬱金, lit. "gold in stasis"). Designed to induce vomiting (q.v.), this pill was often recommended for the treatment of madness. Also known as the "Alum and turmeric powder" (yù fán sǎn 鬱樊散)

Wind (feng 風): first referred to an external form of Qi that was thought to cause illnesses—including madness—by invading the body's depleted areas. Physicians like Liu Wansu (4.1) equated Wind with inner Heat. In late imperial medicine, Wind usually referred to pathological inner fluids, often associated with the Liver. Identified as the main cause of madness in medical treatises from the seventh and eighth centuries (3.3).

Wind mucus (fēng tán 風痰): mucus either caused by or associated with pathological Wind

Wind stroke (zhòng fēng 中風): being struck by external Wind. Most Song physicians believed that the symptoms of Wind strokes were caused by the sudden movements of saliva and mucus (qq.w.) inside the body. This understanding of illness was then transferred to madness.

winged spindle tree: *guǐ jiàn yǔ 鬼箭羽

Wood-riding-on-Earth (mù chéng tǔ 木乘土): the process by which deviant Qi from the Liver or the Gallbladder was said to encroach on Stomach or Spleen functions (7.5); became identified as an important cause of illness in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, and thus contributed to the construction of the Liver as a dense pathological nexus

## PDF第756页

“worthy of compassion” (kě jīn 可称): one of the four categories in which death sentences could be classified at the Autumn Assizes (q.v.). Led to a reduction of sentence from death to life exile or penal servitude (qq.vm). “This category.... was expressly intended for the young, the aged, and other persons for whom particular extenuating circumstances existed” (Bodde and Morris 1967: 230).

wǔ fú 五服: the five degrees of mourning; → mourning system

Wumei: *wū méi 烏梅

wù shā 誤殺: → killing by mistake

wǔ xíng (1) 五刑: → the five punishments

wǔ xíng (2) 五行: → the Five Agents

wǔ zàng 五藏／五臟:→Yin viscera

wǔ zhuò 仵作: → coroner

xì shā 戲殺: → Killing in play

xī xián sǎn 稀涎散: → Saliva-thinning powder

xiān 仙: → immortal

xián 爛: an intermittent (間) illness characterized by seizures or convulsions; has been translated as “epilepsy” but referred to a broader set of symptoms. In medicine from the third to approximately the twelfth centuries, xian was considered very close to dian 癲 (q.v.) in symptoms and patho-mechanisms. Note that the forms 癲 and 痢, which are used in most secondary publications and re-editions of ancient texts, are incorrect, since they did not appear in a single original text I have consulted

xián 涎: → saliva

xiàn 縣: → county

xiāng huǒ 相火: → Ministerial Fire

xiāng shì 鄉試: → provincial examination

xiāng yuē 鄉均: → community compact

xiǎo cǎo 小草: thesium (*bǎiruǐ cǎo 百蕊草)

xiǎo zhù 小注: → small commentary

xié 邪: 1) → deviant; 2) → deviant Qi

xiě 血: → Blood

xié qì 邪氣: → deviant Qì

xié suì 邪祟: → affliction by evil

xin 心: → Heart

## PDF第757页

xin bāo 心包 or 心胞: → Cardiac Envelope

xīn bāo luò 心包絡: → Cardiac Envelope

xīn fēng 心風: → Heart Wind

Xíngbù 刑部: → Ministry of Punishments

Xingke 刑科: “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments”; a bureau charged with monitoring the activities of the Ministry of Punishments (q.v.); attached to the Censorate (Ducháyuàn 都察院) after 1723

xīn qiào 心竅: → openings of the Heart

xiù cái 秀才: → licentiate

xū 虚: → depletion

xué 血: → Blood

xuézhèng 學政: → education commissioner

xúnfǔ 巡撫: 1) → Grand coordinator; 2) → governor

yámén 衙門: the general name for the official compounds where official matters were conducted at all levels of administration

Yang-brilliance tract (yángmíng jīng 陽明經): according to Chinese medical conceptions, one of the eleven or twelve circulatory tracts in the human body; medical texts even earlier than the Inner Canon (first century BCE) attributed some symptoms of madness to the stirring (dòng 動) of the Yang-brilliance tract; these symptoms were eventually integrated into most medical descriptions of kuang (3.1)

yáng kuáng 陽狂: → Yang madness

Yang madness (yáng kuáng 陽狂): the name that was sometimes given to madness when it was caused by excess or over-repletion ( $ \rightarrow $ repletion)

Yang viscera (fú 府 or 腑; also liù fú 六府 or 六腑): six viscera that were thought to be relatively less deep inside the body than the Yin viscera (q.v.); the six Yang viscera were the Gallbladder (dān 膽), the Stomach (wèi 胃), the Large Intestine (dà cháng 大腸), the Small Intestine (xiǎo cháng 小腸), the Bladder (páng guāng 膀胱), the Triple jiao (sān jiāo 三焦), and the Gate of Life (mìng mén 命門)

Yăn shèng gōng 衍聖公: → Sagely Duke of Yan

yī àn 醫案: → medical case file

yi guan 醫官: “medical official”; in broad usage, yiguan was a respectful term of address for doctors; in restricted usage, it referred to physicians who worked at the county or prefectural yamen (q.v.); among other tasks, they had to testify that a killer who appeared mad was not faking and treated prisoners who had not recovered from madness after killing someone (see 12.4)

## PDF第758页

yī zhì 醫治: medical treatment; this term did not imply a successful cure, nor even that treatment had been medical

yìn jié 印結:→ bonded statement

yín kuáng 陰狂: → Yin madness

Yin madness (yin kuáng 陰狂): in medical terminology, referred to madness caused by an underlying depletion

Yin viscera (zàng 藏 or 臟, or wǔ zàng 五藏 or 五臟): the viscera that were thought to lie deeper in the body than the Yang viscera (q.v.); the five viscera were the Heart (q.v.), the Liver (q.v.), the Spleen (pí 脾), the Lungs (fèi 肺), and the Kidneys (shèn 腎)

young melon stalk: *guā dì 瓜蒂

yù 鬱: → stasis

yù 愈: "to become better," almost always used intransitively

yù bǐ gōu chú 御筆勾除: → crossing off with the imperial brush

yú gōu 予勾: "to give a hook" or "to cross off" (→crossing off with the imperial brush)

yuánzuò 緣坐: → collective implication

zá bìng 雜病: → miscellaneous illnesses

zàng fǔ 藏府 / 臟腑: the Yin viscera (zàng, which originally meant "storehouse") and the

Yang viscera (fǔ, “depots”); → Yin viscera, Yang viscera

zhǎn 斬: decapitation

zhǎn jiān bóu 斬監候: → decapitation after the Assizes

zhǎn jué 軒決: → immediate decapitation

zhǎn lịjué 斬立決: → immediate decapitation

zhăn bòu 斬候: → decapitation after the Assizes

zhàng 杖: → heavy stick

[zhèng 證 [sometimes 証]: “illness,” “symptom,” “manifestation of illness”; until at least the Ming, the translation “manifestation type” is anachronistic. For most of imperial history, zhèng 證 was used in the same sense as the homophonous zhèng 症 to refer to symptoms or to illnesses as a whole, as in kuang zhèng 狂證.

zhèng 症: depending on context, “symptom,” “illness,” or even “cluster of symptoms”; in modern usage, usually means “symptom”

zhī fú 知府: prefect, in charge of a prefecture and all the counties and departments it included; was thus the immediate superior of the magistrate (q.v.), whose judicial decisions the prefect had to review

zhī xiàn 知縣: → magistrate

## PDF第759页

zhi yan 治驗: → successful treatment

zhī zhōu 知州: → magistrate

zhì zài 治罪: to mete out a sentence; more specifically, to determine the penal denomination (q.v.) under which a criminal act would be sentenced

zhōu 州: → department

zhuàng yuán 狀元: → optimus

zhù yóu 祝由：“summoning the cause”; usually referred to exorcistic methods that consisted in identifying the agent of possession before expelling it; also the name of one of the thirteen departments or specialties of the Imperial Academy of Medicine (q.v.) specializing in this kind of therapy.

zi 字: courtesy name, the name by which educated men were addressed socially; this name was usually chosen when they were about 20 sui

zòu zhé 奏摺: palace memorial

zù罪: a criminal act as defined by the Code, or the sentence that this act deserved

zùi mìng 罪名: → penal denomination

zūn zhǎng 尊長: → senior relative

## PDF第760页

## Appendix 2

## Information on materia medica

Although some scholars choose to refer to Chinese medicinal plants by their botanical names in order to facilitate identification, I prefer to use the common English names of Chinese materia medica to facilitate reading. One more reason for avoiding Latin binomials is that I did not want to overlay the native Chinese categories with a strong classificatory system like Linnaean taxonomy. I was tempted to refer to each drug by the literal translation of its Chinese name (“black pods” for honey locust fruits, “dragon-gallbladder herb” for gentian), but some drug names have no obvious translation, and such direct renderings would probably be more confusing than helpful. The second column of the table below nonetheless indicates the literal meaning of each Chinese drug name.

The column called “English name” borrows from the second edition of Hu Hsiu-ying’s An Enumeration of Chinese Materia Medica, which was published in 1999 by the Chinese University of Hong Kong Press. Following Hu, English names that are entirely capitalized (like CROTON) are the names of plants that have no common English names. The name adopted under such a circumstance is either the Chinese name or part of the botanical name. The pharmaceutical name is derived from the botanical name and indicates what part of the plant was used as a drug (Radix = root, Cortex = bark, etc.). Minerals and animal or human parts have, of course, no botanical name.

## PDF第769页

## Appendix 3

# The madness section of Lou Ying's Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396)

In section 5.2, I cited Lou Ying's 櫻英 (1320-1400) treatise as an example of intertextual composition and as good representatives of the zhèzhong (5.1) approach to the textual past that consisted in selectively borrowing approaches from previous books and authors. Here is a list of the works cited in the madness section of Lou Ying's Yixue gangmu. Lou referred to each book or author by one or two distinctive characters, which I cite in square brackets at the end of each entry.

1. The Suwen 素問 (Basic Questions): short passages from pian 28, 46, 74, and 79, the latter along with Wang Bing's 王冰 eighth-century annotations [cited as Su 素]

2. The Lingshu 靈樞 (Divine Pivot): pian 22 and 78 [Ling 靈]

3. The Nanjing 難經 (Canon of Problems): problems 20 and 59 [Nan 難]

4. The Zhenjiu jiayi jing 針灸甲乙經 (AB Canon of Acumoxa) [Jia 甲], compiled by Huangfu Mi in the third century CE.

5. Zhang Ji 張機: “Fangji and foxglove decoction” 防已地黃湯 from an entry called “Fangji and foxglove decoction” (防已地黃湯方) in Jingui yaolüe 金匱要略, juan 1, section 5 [Zhong 仲, from Zhang Ji’s zi Zhongjing 仲景]

## PDF第770页

6. Shen Kuo's Lingyuan fang 靈苑方 (1048/77): “Cinnabar powder” 辰砂散 $ ^{1} $

7. A case record by Sun Zhao 孫兆 (late 11 $ ^{th} $ century; see 3.7, pp. 65-65) [Sun 孫] $ ^{2} $

8. Xu Shuwei's 許叔微 Puji benshi fang 普濟本事方 (1132): recipes for "Will-appeasing paste" (寧志膏) and "Fright-Qi pill" (驚氣丸), as well as three case files that Xu appended to his entry on the latter [Ben 本, from the title of Xu's book] $ ^{3} $

9. Chen Yan's 陳言 Sanyin fang 三因方 (1174): three recipes, "Guo-alum elixir" (號礬丹), $ ^{4} $ the "Heart-pacifying elixir" (鎮心丹), and a modified version of the "Lesser life-prolonging decoction" (小續命湯) [Wu 無, from Chen's  $ \ddot{\gamma} $ Wuze 無擇] $ ^{5} $

10. Liu Wansu's 劉完素 Suwen bingji qiyi baoming ji 素問病機氣宜保命集 (1186): for the “Angelica Qi-supporting decoction” (當歸承氣湯) [Bao 保] $ ^{6} $

11. Zhang Congzheng’s 張從正 Rumen shiqin 儒門事親 (ca. 1228): four case records [Zihe 子和, Zhang’s  $ z_{i} $]

12. Wang Haogu's 王好古 Yinzheng lieli 陰證略例 (author's prefaces from 1232 and 1243): one case record and a commentary on some passages from the Inner Canon concerning madness [Hai 海, short for Wang's bao Haicang 海藏] $ ^{8} $

## PDF第771页

13. Shatumusu's 沙圖穆蘇 Ruizhu tang jingyan fang 瑞竹堂經驗方 (1323): one recipe, "Coming-to paste" (Laisu gao 來蘇膏), designed to induce vomiting in cases of Wind strokes [Zhu'竹] $ ^{9} $

14. Wei Yilin's 危亦林 Shiyi dexiao fang 世醫得效方 (1337; first printed in 1345): three recipes (“Instant-drunkenness paste” 一醉膏,  $ ^{10} $ “Turmeric and alum powder” 鬱謦散,  $ ^{11} $ and “Shrubby sophora decoction” 苦參湯  $ ^{12} $ and one case  $ ^{13} $ [Shi 世]

## PDF第772页

## Appendix 4

## Ming editions of books by Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412)

Liu Chun's 劉純 medical treatises were widely read in the fifteenth century and thus contributed to the diffusion of Danxi medicine, the doctrines attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) that became so central to medical thinking in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. $ ^{1} $ This appendix lists most known editions of four works by Liu Chun: Yijing xiaoxue 醫經小學 [“Elementary Learning on the Medical Canons”; first ed.: 1388], Yuji weiyi 玉機微義 [“Subtle Meanings of the Secrets Carved in Jade”; compiled on the basis of a shorter manuscript by Xu Yanchun 徐彥純 (d. 1384); first ed.: 1396], Zabing zhili 雜病治例 [“Examples of Treatments for Miscellaneous Illnesses”; first ed.: 1408?], and Shanghai zhili 傷寒治例 [“Examples of Treatments for Cold Injury”; first ed.: 1479].

The table is based on information found in the Quanguo Zhongyi tushu lianbe mulu 全國中醫圖書聯合目錄 (Xue Qinglu 1991), a catalog of Chinese medical books that have survived in Chinese libraries, as well as on data from prefaces to Liu's books. $ ^{2} $ In addition to four editions of the Yijing xiaoxue (all listed in the table below), Xue 1991 mentions copies of fifteen extant Ming editions of the Yuji weiyi. Twelve of them are identified below. I do not list the other three because they are undated. Two of these three undated editions, however, indicate the name of the publisher, which should make them identifiable. The third undated

## PDF第773页

edition is just called a “Ming edition” (明刻本). Liu Chun, who was originally from Jiangsu, lived most of his adult life in the northwestern city of Xi'an, hence the inordinate number of editions of his books that were made there.

To facilitate consultation, Yijing xiaoxue is underlined (Yijing xiaoxue), Yuji weiyi written in bold (Yuji weiyi), and the other titles simply italicized. CATCM refers to the China Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine (in Beijing).

## PDF第775页

# Appendix 5 Zhang Jiebin on kuang in Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書

I discussed Zhang Jiebin's 張介賓 approach to madness in section 6.8 (pp. 249-54).

The section I translate below is from juan 34 of Zhang' Jingyue quanshu 景岳全書.¹

All the remedies Zhang Jiebin recommended for the treatment of kuang were listed in the last 14 juan of Zhang's treatise. Zhang divided all remedies between “new” (新; those he had designed himself) and “ancient” (古; all other recipes), and classified them between “eight military formations” (八陣), following the common notion that using drugs was like deploying troops. Zhang's “new” remedies were found in juan 51. The eight formations were “replenishing” (補; in juan 53), “harmonizing” (和; juan 54), “attacking” (攻; juan 55), dispersing (散; juan 56), “cooling” (寒; juan 57), “warming” (熱; juan 58), “solidifying” (固; juan 59), and “conforming” (?) (因; juan 60). $ ^{2} $ Juan 61 contained recipes for women, juan 62 remedies for children, juan 63 pills, powders, and decoctions to treat “poxes” (痘疹), and juan 64 recipes for external use. I indicate in the footnotes how Zhang classified the remedies he recommended for treating kuang.

Kuang illness is mostly caused by Fire. It is [occasioned] either by pondering that

leads to loss of mind, by ratiocination that causes stasis, or by vexations with

nowhere to extend themselves or anger that cannot be vented that cause

## PDF第776页

contraversion of Liver and Gallbaldder Qi and the deviant [i.e., illness-causing] union of Wood and Fire: this is an Eastern repletion disorder. When this deviant Qi [inner Fire] rides on the Heart, it renders the Spirit unable to hold on to its lodging; when it rides on the Stomach, it makes people violent and strong. When treating such [an illness], one should therefore treat Fire as a priority, and then examine whether either mucus or Qi is dominant and treat them in conjunction.

凡狂病多因於火 此或以謀為失志 或以思慮鬱結 屈無所伸 怒無所泄 以致肝膽氣逆 木火合邪 是誠東方實證也 此其邪乘於心 則為神魂不守 邪乘於胃 則為暴橫剛強 故治此者 當以治火為先 而或痰或氣 察其甚而兼治之

If [kuang] is caused only by the deviant [Qi] of Fire and there is neither abdominal swelling, nor blockage, nor Heat knots, clearing Fire will suffice, mainly with [recipes] such as Chouxin beverage, Foxglove poison-resolving decoction, or Triple-replenishing pill. $ ^{3} $

若止因火邪 而無脹閉熱結者 但當清火 宜抽薪飲 黃連解毒湯 三補丸之類主之

If Water cannot curb Fire, and the Heart or the Kidneys are slightly depleted,

Cinnabar Spirit-appeasing pill, Subdue-the-barbarians decoction, and Double-Yin

decoction are indicated. $ ^{4} $

若水不制火而兼心腎微虛者 宜朱砂安神丸 或服蠻煎 二陰煎主之

If Yang-brilliance Fire is dominant, White-tiger decoction and Jade-fount powder should be used. $ ^{5} $

若陽明火盛者 宜白虎湯 玉泉散之類主之

## PDF第777页

If the Heart and the Gallbladder are affected by Heat [and the patient] screams and scolds, acts abnormally, and there is also slight constipation, [one] should [use] remedies such as Heart-clearing decoction, Diaphragm-cooling powder, Three-huang pill, and Angelica-and-aloe pill. $ ^{6} $

若心脾受熱 叫罵失常 而微兼閉結者 宜清心湯 涼膈散 三黃丸 當歸

龍薈丸之類主之

If Fire leads to the formation of mucus, use Diaphragm-clearing beverage, Dragon-embracing pill, or Scraps-of-wrought-iron beverage, and in serious cases Mucus-roiling pill. $ ^{7} $

若因火致痰者 宜清膈飲 抱龍丸 生鐵落飲主之 甚者宜滾痰丸

If deviant Qi is replete and Heat is strong in the triple-jiao, one should purge with Greater Qi-supporting decoction. $ ^{8} $

若三焦邪實熱甚者 宜大承氣湯下之

If mucus and rheum cause blockage and the Qi ways [the respiratory tracts] are blocked, the vomiting method needs to be used first in order to clear drink and food.

若痰饮壅闭 氣道不通者 必須先用吐法 並當清其飲食

These are the essentials of the treatment of kuang.

此治狂之要也

## PDF第778页

## Appendix 6

# The dian-xian section of Linzheng zhinan yian 臨證指南醫案 (1766)

I discussed Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746) medicine and the Medical Case Files to Guide Clinical Practice in section 7.4 (pp. 288-92). The text translated below was written by Gong Shangnian 龔商年 and appended to eleven case files on dian 癲 or xian 癲 that purported to be by Ye Gui. $ ^{1} $

Heaven-and-Earth is one Yin-and-Yang. When Yin and Yang are in harmony, Heaven is clear and Earth peaceful, but as soon as there is an imbalance, aberrant transformations take place. The human body is also one Yin-and-Yang. When Yin and Yang are harmonious, the Spirit is clear and Qi settled, but as soon as there is imbalance, unpredictable ailments will occur. This is why the Inner Canon says "heavy on Yang is kuang, heavy on Yin is dian."

天地 一陰陽也 陰陽和則天清地寧 一有偏勝 遂有非常之變 人身亦一陰陽也 陰陽和則神清氣定 一有偏勝 自致不測之疴 故內經曰 重陽者狂 重陰者癲

As for xian and dian, their principle is the same. The ancients compiled distinctions between dian, xian, and kuang, interpreting them as Yang piling on Yin or Yin piling on Yang. $ ^{2} $ Sincerely, these views are not fit for print.

## PDF第779页

痼與癲 其原則同也 古人集癲癇狂辨 以為陽并於陰 陰并於陽 此誠不刊之論

Let us speak of the manifest symptoms [of these illnesses]. Kuang: one rests little,
does not feel hunger, and speaks and laughs rashly. In serious cases, one climbs on
rooftops and jumps walls. The symptoms are mostly restless and usually conscious.

言乎现症 狂则少臥不飢 妄言妄笑 甚則上屋逾垣 其候多躁而常醒

Dian: one either sings or cries, is as if drunk or stultified; when serious, it goes so far as not distinguishing filth from cleanliness. The symptoms are mostly calm and usually unconscious.

癲則或歌或哭 如醉如痴 甚至不知穢潔 其候多靜而常昏

Xian flares up irregularly; one suddenly faints and falls to the ground, the tendons and the vessels convulse, the mouth emits sounds. Because of what these sounds resemble, later people have divided xian into five names: horse xian, ox xian, pig xian, sheep xian, and chicken xian. The symptoms always cease with time.

癎則發作無時 卒然昏仆 筋脈瘛瘲 口中作聲 後人因其聲似 分馬癎

牛癎 豬癎 羊癎 雞癎五名 其候經時而必止

Let us infer the cause of these illnesses. Kuang comes from great fright or great anger. $ ^{3} $ The illness is located in the Liver, Gallbladder, and Stomach tracts: the [Qi of these] three Yang [tracts] join together and rise. $ ^{4} $ As a result, Fire burns ardently; mucus therefore gushes, and the openings of the Heart are obstructed by it.

推其病因 狂由大驚大怒 病在肝膽胃經 三陽并而上升 故火熾則痰涌 心竅為之閉塞

## PDF第780页

Dian comes from accumulated despondency and stasis. The illness is in the Heart, Spleen, and Heart envelope [tracts]: these three Yin [tracts] are obstructed and do not express themselves. As a result, Qi is in stasis; one is therefore confused by mucus, and the Spirit and Will are bewildered by it.

癲由積憂積鬱 病在心脾胞絡 三陰蔽而不宣 故氣鬱則痰迷 神志為之潛

淆

Xian illness can come from fright and fear, irregularity of eating and drinking, or one's having been startled in one's mother's womb, causing the internal organs to become unbalanced and the tracts to lose regularity enduringly. As soon as one encounters accumulated mucus, reverse Qi and inner Wind suddenly and brutally move in reverse. Nothing can bring this to a halt. [The fit] stops after the Qi returns [where it belonged].

瘤病或由驚恐 或由飲食不節 或由母腹中受驚 以致內臟不平 經久失調

一觸積痰 厥氣內風捽焉暴逆 莫能禁止 待其氣反然後已

Concerning the main treatments. Examine the visible bodily symptoms and the pulse symptoms to distinguish between depletion and repletion. In cases of repletion kuang, use recipes such as the Qi-stimulating or White-Tiger [decoctions], which directly cut Yang-brilliance Fire, or Scraps-of-cast-iron beverage, which strongly curbs Liver and Gallbladder heteropathies. For depletion [kuang], one ought to fortify Water to curb Fire. Use mainly “Two-Yin decoction” and the like.

至於主治 察形證 診脈候 以辨虛實 狂之實者 以乘氣 白虎直折陽明之火 生鐵落飲重制肝膽之邪 虛者當壯水以制火 二陰煎之類主之

In cases of repletion dian, open mucus blockage with the Mucus-roiling pill and purge Fire stasis with the Heart-clearing pill. For depletion [dian], one ought to

## PDF第781页

nourish the Spirit and open up the Will. Use mainly the likes of Return-to-the-Spleen
[decoction] and Inside-the-pillow [recipe]. $ ^{5} $

癲之實者 以滾痰丸開痰壅閉 清心丸泄火鬱勃 虛者當養神而通志 歸脾枕中之類主之

In cases of repletion xian, use the Five-xian pill to attack Wind, the Saliva-controlling pill to despoil mucus, and the Aloe pill to purge Fire. For depletion [xian], one ought to replenish and help Qi and Blood and regularize Yin and Yang, mainly with the likes of Constructive-Qi-nourishing decoction or River-chariot [i.e., human placenta] pill.

痼之實者 用五痼丸以攻風 控涎丸以劫痰 龍薈丸以瀉火 虛者當補助氣

血 調攝陰陽 養營湯 河車丸之類主之

The essentials of the treatment methods for the three illnesses kuang, dian, and xian do not go beyond this. Now if there is Liver Wind and mucus-Fire, use bitter and acrid drugs to open and purge. When the Spirit is depleted and Fire is flaming, clear [Fire] and replenish at the same time. When the Liver-Gallbladder Attenuated-Yang [tract] becomes pathological and produces Wind that swirls in reverse [i.e., upwards], cut it with extremely bitter drugs. When the Spirit and the Will are both depleted, use the method of connecting the Heart and the Kidneys. When one is mentally exhausted, concentrate on creating calmness in order to generate Yin (?) ; restrain and

狂癲癇三症治法 大旨不越於此 今如肝風痰火者 苦辛以開泄 神虛火炎者 則清補并施 肝膽厥陽化風旋逆者 以極苦之藥折之 神志兩虛者 用交心腎法 勞神太過者 宗靜以生陰意 為斂補鎮攝

## PDF第782页

Although [Ye Gui's] recipes and case files are not detailed and are dispersed and disparate, they are still a treasure for the world. $ ^{6} $ All that physicians have to do is to regulate Yin and Yang without letting either become imbalanced or dominant. Stasis and reverse flow will then disappear of their own and Spiritual Qi will get to return to normal.

方案雖未詳備 而零珠碎玉 不悉堪為世寶哉 醫者惟調理其陰陽 不使有所偏勝 則鬱逆自消 而神氣得反其常焉矣

# Appendix 7 List of Qing laws cited in the text

This is a list of the Qing legal articles that I cite in the text and appendices of this dissertation. Each article is conventionally numbered according to Huang Jingjia's 黄静嘉 1970 edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 (1905; hereafter DLCY). Qing sub-statutes had neither numbers nor names. The titles of the sub-statutes translated here are from Huang Jingjia's edition of DLCY. Articles whose numbers are integers (e.g., 256) are statutes; those followed by a decimal point (e.g., 292.14) are sub-statutes. "DLCY 25: 567" means "Duli cunyi, juan 25, p. 567 of Huang Jingjia's 1970 edition." Jones 1994 refers to William Jones's 1994 translation of the Qing Code, which does not include the sub-statutes. The sub-statutes are arranged according to the order in which they appeared in the Qing Code at the very end of the dynasty, when DLCY was compiled. Some laws that had disappeared by the late Qing are not listed here, but they are discussed under the relevant dates in Appendix 8.

Laws on denominations and general principles 名例律

Art. 1: “The five punishments” 五刑 [DLCY 1: 1-2; Jones 1994: 33-34]

Art. 2: “The ten abominations” 十惡 [DLCY 1: 16-19; Jones 1994: 34-36]

Art. 18: “Committing an offense and being permitted to stay at home to take care of one’s parents” 犯罪存留養親 [DLCY 3: 61; Jones 1994: 49]

Art. 22: “Allowing monetary redemption for the old, the young, and the disabled” 老小廢疾收贖 [DLCY 4: 91; Jones 1994: 52-53]

## PDF第784页

Art. 32: “Relatives who [may] conceal each other” 親屬相為容隱 [DLCY 5: 131; Jones 1994: 66-67]

Art. 35: “When an article has a different penal denomination [from the one specified in the section of the Code on ‘Denominations and general principles’]” 本條別有罪名 [DLCY 5: 134; Jones 1994: 68]

Art. 36: “Rules for increasing or decreasing punishments” 加减罪例 [DLCY 5: 134; Jones 1994: 68-70]

Art. 39: “On [sentencing to] ‘the same penalty’” 稱與同罪 [DLCY 5: 136-37; Jones 1994: 71-72]

Art. 43: “Meting out a sentence according to newly promulgated statutes” 斷罪依新頒律 [DLCY 5: 137-38; Jones 1994: 74]

Art. 44: “Meting out a sentence when there is no directly relevant article” 斷罪無正條 [DLCY 5: 138; Jones 1994: 74]

## Laws of [the Ministry of] Personnel 吏律

Art. 65: “Matters on which one should memorialize but does not” 事應奏而不奏 [DLCY 8:311-12; Jones 1994: 92-93]

Laws of [the Ministry of] Revenue 戶律

None

Art. 162: “Prohibiting sorcerers and deviant arts” 禁止師巫邪術 [DLCY 18: 421; Jones 1994: 174]

Art. 165: “Keeping prohibited books” 收藏禁書 [DLCY 19: 430; Jones 1994: 176]

Art. 171: “Memorials and statements [to the throne]” 上書陳言 [DLCY 19: 432; Jones 1994: 178-79]

Laws of [the Ministry of] War 兵律

Art. 195: Breaching the imperial procession 衝突儀仗 [DLCY 20: 455; Jones 1994: 194-95]

Laws of [the Ministry of] Punishments 刑律

Art. 254: “Plotting rebellion and grand sedition” 謀反大逆 [DLCY 25: 555; Jones 1994: 237-38]

## PDF第785页

Art. 256: “Writing Books on Sorcery or Speaking About Sorcery” 造妖書妖言 [DLCY 25: 567; Jones 1994: 239-40]

256.01: “Blithely circulating devious words, writing placards, and rousing and confusing people’s hearts” 妄布邪言書寫張貼煽惑人心 [DLCY 25: 567]. This penal denomination was based on an imperial edict proclaimed in 1636; this edict was imperially approved during the Kangxi reign (1662-1722), when it was made into a li 例. Applied only to the imperial capital originally, but was broadened to the entire empire in 1740. Was merged with another text of law in 1801 (see next entry) to form sub-statute 256.01.

256.01: “Writing apocryphal auguries and books about sorcery in order to circulate them and confuse people, [cases in which the materials] did not reach the multitudes” 造纖緯妖書傳用惑人不及衆者 [DLCY 25: 567]: the legal text that contained this formulation was turned into a li例 in 1677 after the Ministry of Punishments had “approved of a proposal found in a routine memorial” (題准); it was inserted as a sub-statute into the Yongzheng-era edition of the Code (1725). Merged with another li in 1801 (see previous entry) to form sub-statute 256.01.

Art. 282: “Killing by design” 謀殺 [DLCY 32: 775; Jones 1994: 268-69]

and mete out a sentence”瘋人報官鎖禁其曾殺人之犯分別鎖錮及取供定擬[DLCY 34: 859]

292.17: “Individual cases of killing because of madness in which [the convict may be allowed] to stay at home to take care of his parents and continue the ancestral sacrifices” 瘋病殺人分別留養承祀 [DLCY 34: 861]

292.18: “By sub-statute, when criminals who have been sentenced to death for killing because of madness but have been exempted from the hook and have been permanently confined recover from illness, they may be released if they encounter an edict of grace” 瘋病殺人問擬死罪免勾永遠監禁之犯病愈遇有恩旨例得查辦釋放者 [DLCY 34: 861]

292.19: “When criminals who have killed because of madness can provide a clear deposition upon re-trial, it is authorized that they be sentenced as for killing in an affray” 瘋病殺人之犯覆審供吐明晰准擬闘殺 [DLCY 34: 862]

## PDF第789页

# Appendix 8 Chronological evolution of Qing laws on madness

This chronology explains how laws on madness-induced homicides developed during the Qing dynasty. Texts of law are presented in bold type. This Appendix may be read along with Chapters 11 and 12, which explain the same developments. As in other parts of this dissertation, Qing legal texts are cited as they are numbered in Huang Jingjia's edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 (1905).

Here is a list of themes that these Qing madness laws discussed, and the years in which they were discussed. Each year corresponds with an entry in the chronology below.

Accidental killing: 1647, 1669, 1713, 1731, 1753, 1756 [1], 1763, 1786 [1]

Autumn Assizes: 1756 [2], 1776, 1784, 1809 [2], 1824

- Confinement: → Permanent confinement, Preventive confinement

- Killing junior relatives: 1792 [2], 1796 [1], 1804 [1]

- Killing parents: 1654, 1713, 1761, 1763

Killing senior relatives (see also killing parents): 1697, 1754 [1], 1756 [2], 1766 [1], 1784, 1786 [1], 1792 [1], 1809 [1], 1809 [2]

Measures for liberation (see also “Staying at home to take care of one’s parents”): 1753, 1762, 1796, 1811, etc.

- Multiple killings: 1731, 1753, 1766 [2], 1776, 1784, 1809 [2], 1824

- Permanent confinement (and shorter punitive confinement): 1740, 1754 [1], 1754 [2], 1762, 1766 [3], 1792 [2], 1796 [2], 1803 [1], 1804 [1], 1809 [1] ( $ \rightarrow $ Measures for liberation)

- Preventive confinement: 1689, 1732, 1766 [3], 1767

- Punitive confinement :  $ \rightarrow $ permanent confinement

- Single homicides: 1654, 1667, 1669, 1697, 1754 [2], 1762, 1803 [1], 1803 [2], 1804 [2]

- “Staying at home to take care of one’s parents”: 1792, 1797, 1801, 1809 [2]

The abbreviations cited in this Appendix refer to the following works (see Bibliography A for complete references):

BAXB Bo'an xinbian 駁案新編 (1780s)

DLCY          Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Duli cunyi 讀例存疑 (1905), a study of the Code's sub-statutes as of the late nineteenth century

DQLL Dai Qing lili 大清律例, the official "Code of the Great Qing" as it was named after 1740

DQLLAY Dai Qing lili anyu 大清律例按語, a collection of official commentaries on Qing legislation

DLTJ Dai Qing liuli tongkao jiaozhu 大清律例通考校注, the annotated nineteenth-century edition of Wu Tan's late eighteenth-century study of the Code as it stood in the late Qianlong reign (1736-1796)

HDSL Dai Qing buidian shili 大清會典事例 (Guangxu edition, 1899)

QSG Qingshi gao 清史稿 (1927)
