XAHL Xing'an builan 刑案匪覽 (1834)

XXZL [Xingbu] xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例, a book of Kangxi-era legislation (1680)

## Chronology

1647 The first edition of the Qing Code (largely based on the Ming Code) was printed under the title Dai Qing li jijie fuli 大清律集解附例 [“Code of the Great Qing with Appended Commentaries and Precedents”]. $ ^{1} $

In continuation with Ming law, an article mandated that those who committed “accidental killing” (過失殺人) against an unrelated person (凡人) should pay 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of their victim to cover burial costs. $ ^{2} $

1654 Someone who had committed parricide “because of madness” was exempted from any crime (免罪). $ ^{3} $ This is the earliest Qing legal document I have found that used the term “madness illness” (瘋病), which may have been the translation of a Manchu term (see 10, p. 417).

1667 Following a recommendation from a high provincial official from Shandong, the Ministry of Punishments approved of the following guideline for sentencing:

## PDF第791页

“Whenever [a person with] madness illness kills or injures someone, exempt from punishment” (凡瘋病殺傷人者 免議). $ ^{4} $

This guideline was the first explicit Qing rule concerning the proper way to sentence homicides committed during a fit of madness. Mian yi 免議 literally meant “exempt from deliberation,” in which “deliberation” meant determining the proper punishment for a criminal act. Note that “madness illness” was the object of no definition.

Because it proposed to handle “killing because of madness” in a way that differed from the way the other “six killings” (六殺) were sentenced, the 1667 guideline did not recognizably belong under any statute in the Code. $ ^{5} $ And because it was soon superseded by a new regulation ( $ \rightarrow $ 1669), the 1667 rule did not appear in the 1680 Currently Operative Regulations of the Ministry of Punishments (Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例), a collection of the sentencing guidelines that had been promulgated since the beginning of the new dynasty in 1644.

Upon recommendation from a high provincial official, a new regulation approved by the Ministry of Punishments mandated that “When [a person with] madness illness kills someone, collect 12.42 ounces of silver from the criminal for funeral expenses, to be given to the family of the dead person” (瘋病殺人者從犯人名下追取埋葬銀十二兩四錢二分 給與死者之家). $ ^{6} $

This sub-statute likened the crime of “Killing because of madness” to that of “Accidental killing” (過失殺人) for which a funeral compensation of 12.42 ounces of silver was also obtained from the culprit. $ ^{7} $ Unlike the 1667 guideline, the 1669 rule did not explain what to do in cases of injuring because of madness ( $ \rightarrow $ 1680).

## PDF第792页

“The Xianxing zeli were an amalgamation of three sources: the previous Shengjing regulations [Shengjing dingli 盛京定例], imperial edicts, and precedents reported from below and approved by the throne” (Zheng Qin 1995b: 326). Since the 1669 rule appeared as a “supplementary li” (增例) in the 1725 Yongzheng Code, we have to assume that it was the outcome of a regular judicial decision approved by the Ministry of Punishments. $ ^{8} $

This rule was integrated into the Code in 1725, but eventually deleted in 1852 when nominal death sentences and permanent imprisonment for mad killers had made the collection of fines obsolete.

Two article concerning the infliction of injuries with blades were created from previous legislation during the compilation of the Kangxi-era Xianxing zeli 现行则例 (see previous entry). One of the new proclaimed that one who injured another because of madness would not be sentenced, but that a monetary compensation would be paid to the family of the victim in proportion to the victim's injuries. $ ^{9} $

“If it is averred that one was unawares because of madness, exempt from sentencing, but still examine the degree of the injuries and collect silver from the criminal, to be given to the family of the injured person.”

果係瘋疾不知 是實免坐 仍驗傷痕等第 於犯名下追銀給與被傷之人

This law on injuring someone because of madness was modified in 1725 ( $ \rightarrow $ 1725 [2]).

A new sub-statute was introduced, requiring that all families keep watch over their mad members:

As for people with madness illness: their father or paternal grandfather, maternal and paternal uncles, elder or younger brothers, sons or nephews, as well as the sons of their relatives should be ordered to keep precautionary watch. When there are no such relatives, order the neighbors, the head of the community compact, and the difang to keep precautionary watch. When negligence or carelessness leads [the person with madness illness] to kill someone, [sentence the persons in charge to] 80 strokes of the heavy stick according to the statute on ‘what should not be done, heavy case’. $ ^{10} $

瘋病之人 應令父祖叔伯兄弟或子侄親屬之嫡者防守 如無此等親屬 令鄰佑鄉約地方防守 如有疏縱以致殺人者 照不應重律杖八十

This was the first measure of preventive confinement imposed against mad people in the Qing dynasty. Only if the person under guard committed homicide were the people in charge punished. This rule was made more severe in 1732.

## PDF第793页

A woman who had killed her husband had been sentenced to death according to the law on “A Wife of Concubine Striking her Husband” (妻妾歐夫), $ ^{11} $ but the Ministry of Punishments reversed the sentence and ordered the official in charge to sentence the woman under “killing because of madness” ( $ \rightarrow $ 1669; see 11.2, p. 433). This judgment shows that madness could be seen as the only circumstance relevant to sentencing a homicide even when a senior relative was the victim (but see 1756 [2]).

1713 The culprit in a case of parenticide because of madness was sentenced to death by “immediate decapitation” (斬立決), lower than the penalty of dismemberment that was usually mandated for the killing of one parent. This was nonetheless the first case I know in which a killing legally recognized as having been committed because of madness was not sentenced according to the 1669 law that likened “killing because of madness” to “accidental killing.”

1725 [1] The 1669 rule on “killing because of madness” is incorporated into the Code as a “supplementary sub-statute” (增例) placed under the statute on “Killing in an affray or assault, and intentional killing” 鬪毆及故殺人. $ ^{12} $

1725 [2] The Office of Codification modified a piece of Kangxi legislation on injuries inflicted with weapons (→ 1680), including during a fit of madness. In replacement of an earlier phrase that was found proportionally more severe than the way in which the law sentenced “killing because of madness” (→ 1669), the Ministry of Punishments introduced the following passage into the modified sub-statute:

If there is madness illness, grant [the privilege of] monetary redemption according to the statute on “Injuring accidentally” $ ^{13} $ and give the redemption silver to the person who has been injured. $ ^{14} $

若有瘋疾 依過失傷人律收贖 將贖銀給付被傷之人

The comment by the Office of Codification 律例館 (DLTJ 27: 818) that the prior law ( $ \rightarrow $ 1680) had been too severe implies that the fine in proportion of injuries was heavier than the amount of money necessary to redeem the crime of “injuring accidentally.”

1731 One case of killing four persons of the same family—which under ordinary circumstances would have owed the killer a sentence of death by dismemberment—was judged according to the law on “killing because of madness” (→ 1669), which requires only monetary compensation. $ ^{15} $ The contrast between the gravity of

## PDF第794页

the crime and the lightness of the punishment made Sichuan Governor Huang Tinggui 黄廷桂 memorialize to the emperor to propose stricter preventive measures against mad people ( $ \rightarrow $ 1732).

Following a proposal from the Yongzheng emperor, who had been advised by Sichuan Governor Huang Tinggui ( $ \rightarrow $ 1731), the Ministry of Punishments “deliberated and approved” (議準) the implementation of stricter measures of preventive confinement against mad people throughout the empire, including among Banner troops. Unlike the previous preventive law ( $ \rightarrow $ 1689), which had only instructed families to keep guard over their mad members, this one ordered households to declare mad people to the local authorities. $ ^{17} $

In all provinces and in the Eight Banners, whenever there is a person with madness illness, his [close] relatives or neighbors should report his name to the local official or the Banner captain, who will order relatives to lock up [the mad person] and guard him or her. If [the mad person] has no relatives, then people such as the neighbors, the head of the community compact, the difang, or the local clan head will strictly implement guard. If they harbor and hide [the mad person] and do not report him [to the authorities], and fail to implement guard so that the person with madness illness commits suicide, sentence to 80 strokes of the heavy stick according to the statute on ‘what should not be done, heavy case’. $ ^{18} $ If [the person with madness illness] kills someone, sentence [the persons in charge] to 100 strokes of the heavy stick according to the statute on ‘knowing that someone is plotting to harm another, but not trying to stop him or not reporting him [to the authorities after the crime has been committed]’. $ ^{19} $ If relatives or the neighbors had already reported the name [of the mad person to the authorities], but the local official or Banner captain did not strictly order for him to be guarded, leading to his committing suicide or killing another person, report [these officials] to the Ministry for deliberation of an administrative sanction.

各省及八旗 凡有瘋病之人 其親屬鄰佑人等 即報名地方官該佐領處令伊親屬鎖錮看守 如無親屬 即令鄰佑鄉約地方族長人等嚴行看守 倘容隱不報 不行看守 以致瘋病之人自殺者 照不應重律杖八十 致殺他人者 照知人謀害他人不即阻當首報律杖一百 如親屬鄰佑人等已經報名而該地方佐領各官 不嚴飭看守 以致自殺及致殺他人者 俱交部議處

The new measure was more severe than the previous preventive rule ( $ \rightarrow $ 1689), because it increased penalties on the guardians of mad people and for the first time it imposed penalties on negligent officials. The punishment for “killing because of

## PDF第795页

"madness" ( $ \rightarrow $ 1669) remained monetary compensation. This preventive confinement rule was modified again in 1766 ( $ \rightarrow $ 1766 [3]).

1740 [1] The law a passage from which had likened “injuring because of madness” to “accidental injuring” (→ 1680, 1725 [2]) was merged into a tiaoli 條例 that had been attached to the statute on “Affrays and blows” 鬭毆 (Art. 302; DLCY 35: 890) since the Ming dynasty. $ ^{20} $ The resulting law was not modified again until the end of the Qing.

1740 [2] A new guideline specified that after a killing committed because of madness had been redeemed according to the relevant sub-statute (→ 1669), the “criminal will be handed to his relatives for confinement” (交犯屬鎖錮) instead of being simply released, as had previously been the case. $ ^{21} $

1740 [3] The 1732 law on preventive confinement was integrated into the law books. $ ^{22} $

1753 A mad man from Guangxi who had killed four members of a single family was allowed to redeem his crime with a monetary payment according to the 1669 substitute. $ ^{23} $ Guangxi governor Dingchang 定長 (a Manchu of the Plain Yellow Banner) proposed stricter measures of preventive confinement against mad killers.

1754 [1] Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣州 sent a memorial $ ^{24} $ proposing to grade punishments in cases of “killing because of madness” according to the degree of closeness between mad killers and their victim. Jiang also requested that mad killers who had not been registered as mad at the county office should be punished more severely than those who had been declared. Most of Jiang’s proposals were not heeded, but the concerns Jiang had raised—namely the fear of fakers and the importance of family relations in determining punishment—would soon become central to the Qing legal treatment of mad homicides (→ 1756, 1803 [2]). Jiang also suggested the use of procedures that would later be adopted, namely the sentencing of mad killers to permanent imprisonment (→ 1762), death sentences to deter fakers (→ 1803 [1]), and the extraction of “bonded statements” from a madman’s relatives before a homicide could be considered to have been committed “because of madness” (1803 [2]).

1754 [2] $ ^{25} $In response to memorials by Dingchang ( $ \rightarrow $ 1753) and Jiang Zhou ( $ \rightarrow $ 1754 [1]), the Ministry of Punishments decided that people guilty of “killing because of

## PDF第796页

"madness" would be kept in the county prison for one year after recovery before being turned over to their relatives ( $ \rightarrow $ 1740):

Criminals [guilty of] “Killing because of madness” are allowed to redeem [this crime] in accordance with the [1669] sub-statute, [but they] will still be confined to prison. If after they have completely recovered, their illness has not recurred for an entire year, [the official in charge will] order them to be handed to their relatives, who will take them back [home] and take precautions [against their stirring up further trouble]. $ ^{26} $

瘋病殺人之犯　照例收贖　仍行監禁　俟痊愈後　以期年為斷　如不舉發

飭交親屬領回防範

This rule replaced the 1740 guideline saying that mad people who had killed someone should be handed to their relatives for confinement at home, but was itself soon replaced by a measure of permanent imprisonment for mad people who had committed a crime ( $ \rightarrow $ 1762).

The measure of waiting one year before releasing someone who had killed in a fit of madness had first been proposed by Jiang Zhou ( $ \rightarrow $ 1754 [1]).

1756 [1] Sometime in January 1756 (QL20.12), the Ministry of Punishments instructed provincial officials to report on all cases of “killing because of madness” and “accidental killing” in one annual “routine memorial” because both crimes could be redeemed monetarily and therefore be processed locally. In 1735 (YZ13.4), the government had already instructed officials to report in bulk about cases of accidental killing.

Soon after this way of reporting appeared, the treatment of “killing because of madness” started to diverge from that of “accidental killing” (1756 [2], 1761, 1762) Joint memorializing on both kinds of cases nonetheless survived until the end of the Qing. $ ^{27} $

1756 [2] After a lengthy review by the Ministry of Punishments and the Nine Chief Ministers, the sentence of a man who had killed his older brother in a fit of madness was reduced from “immediate decapitation” to “decapitation after the Assizes.” $ ^{28} $ This sentence for the madness-induced homicide of second-degree senior relatives remained in place until the end of the Qing dynasty. The 1756 ruling was the first act of legislation that ordered officials to take family relations into account in cases of homicides committed because of madness (see also 1761).

1761 An edict from the Qianlong emperor (who was following a verdict proposed by the Jiangxi governor in a case of parricide) mandated that people who killed their parents or paternal grand-parents in a fit of madness would now be executed by dismemberment. $ ^{29} $ Parenticides committed “because of madness” were henceforth

## PDF第797页

discussed in palace memorials sent directly to the Grand Council instead of to the Ministry of Punishments.

The 1761 edict was the second piece of Qing legislation (→ 1756 [2]) that breached the similarity between “Killing because of madness” and “Killing accidentally” that had prevailed since 1669. When the 1761 edict was promulgated, the penalty for “Accidentally killing one’s father or mother” was still life exile at 3,000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy stick, a strong contrast with death by dismemberment (→ 1763). $ ^{30} $

The Ministry of Punishments decided to impose a penalty of permanent confinement to people found guilty of “Killing because of madness” (→ 1669, 1740, 1754 [2]). $ ^{31} $ Because this law was quickly enhanced by more detailed measures (→ 1766 [3]), I am not certain what the original text was. It may have been as follows: $ ^{32} $

This Ministry [the Ministry of Punishments] authorizes monetary redemption for [the crime of] killing because of madness. However, madness illness alternately flares up and heals. [Because] it is difficult to guarantee that [madness] will not flare up again [even] after more than one year of imprisonment, [the Ministry] memorializes to ask that [the sentence for those found guilty of “Killing because of madness”] be changed to permanent imprisonment, and that liberation not be authorized even if [madness] has healed completely.

本部以瘋病殺人例准收贖然瘋病原係時發時愈霸禁逾年難保其不復再發奏請改為永遠鎖錮雖或痊愈不准釋放

This law was soon integrated into a more detailed sub-statute on preventive and punitive confinement ( $ \rightarrow $ 1766 [3]). Jiang Zhou had recommended permanent imprisonment for the more violent among mad killers ( $ \rightarrow $ 1754 [1]), but his proposal had not been heeded. More sub-statutes mandating permanent imprisonment for crimes other than “killing because of madness” were published in the Qianlong reign (1736-1796). $ ^{33} $

1763 In response to an imperial edict, the Ministry of Punishments promulgated a new sub-statute that made the crime of “Killing one’s parents accidentally” punishable

## PDF第798页

by “immediate strangulation” (絞決). $ ^{34} $ The creation of this sub-statute was

triggered not by a case of accidental killing, but by one of mistakenly injuring one’s

parents that had led to the death of the injured parents (誤傷身死). $ ^{35} $

1766 [1] Following a suggestion by Jiangsu provincial judge Li Yongshu 李永書, the Ministry of Punishments adopted a new sub-statute mandating that the crime of “Killing one’s husband accidentally” be punished by “immediate strangulation” (絞決). $ ^{36} $ This sentence aimed to re-establish balance with the new way of sentencing the crime of “Killing one’s parents accidentally” ( $ \rightarrow $ 1763), since both parents and husbands were considered to be first-degree senior relatives.

1766 [2] Sichuan provincial judge Shi Lijia 石禮嘉 (from the Han-martial Plain White Banner) memorialized to request that cases of killing either three or more people, or three people from a single family because of madness be sentenced according to the relevant statutes on multiple homicides (因瘋殺死三人以上及一家三命者各按律問擬) instead of allowing monetary redemption, as had previously been the case (→ 1731). The Ministry of Punishments rejected this proposal, claiming that the requested sentence of immediate decapitation was too severe for people who committed crimes without awareness of their own acts. $ ^{37} $ Shi Lijia’s suggestions also included part of what became DLCY 292.15 (→ 1766 [3]).

1766 [3] The Ministry authorized a change to the 1732 rule on preventive confinement. $ ^{38} $ Local officials would now have to provide the chains and locks to families of mad people, and to imprison mad men in county prisons when these men had no families or when families had no secure room in which to imprisoned their mad relatives. Mad women would still be confined at home. The new law also specified that private confinement without declaration would bring punishment to the guardians of a mad person if that person committed suicide or homicide. Some of these measures were based on Shi Lijia's recent proposals (→ 1766 [2]). The new

## PDF第799页

law was merged with the 1762 rule (q.v.) on permanent imprisonment for mad killers. The resulting sub-statute was integrated into the law books in 1768. $ ^{39} $

If the family of someone with madness illness has a strict and safe room where to lock up [the mad person], it is acceptable for these relatives to manage and restrain [the mad person]. [Even if] a married woman or a girl suffers from [this] illness, she should still be reported to officials, who will hand her over to relatives so that they can guard her. The local official is ordered to issue locks and chains in person [to the family of a mad person] so that confinement and chaining up can be strictly implemented. If locking up and confinement by relatives is not strict, so that a homicide takes place, the relatives should be strictly sentenced according to the sub-statute.

If the [mad person] recovers completely and [the illness] no longer flares up, report to the authorities, who will verify [the recovery] by examination. Only after [the local official] has collected “bonded statements” $ ^{40} $ from the clan elder, the local headmen, and the neighbors, can [the recovered mad person] be liberated. If no report is made to the authorities and [relatives] open the seal of the locks on their own, sentence [them] according to the sub-statute. $ ^{41} $

If [a person with madness illness] has not relatives or if there is no [secure] room [in which the mad person can be confined], on the very day [the mad person] is reported [to the authorities], the responsible official is ordered to examine and verify [the claim to madness], to strictly chain up and imprison the person with madness illness, and to write a report to establish a case file. If after imprisonment madness illness no longer flares up, [it is necessary to] wait several years before the situation can be observed and given new consideration, and a report be written to request liberation. [The liberated person should be] returned [to relatives] for precautionary surveillance.

瘋病之人 如家有嚴密房屋可以鎖錮 的當親屬可以管束 及婦女患病者

俱報官交與親屬看守 令地方官親發鎖銬 嚴行封錮 如親屬鎖禁不嚴

致有殺人者 將親屬照例嚴加治罪

如果痊愈不發報官驗明取具族長地鄰甘結始准開放如不行報官

及私啟鎖封者照例治罪

若並無親屬 又無房屋者 即於報明之日 令該管官驗訊明確 將瘋病之人嚴加鎖錮監禁 具詳立案 如果監禁之後 瘋病並不舉發 俟數年後診驗情形 再行酌量詳請開釋 領回防範

The second part of the law was about mad people who had been imprisoned after committing homicide.

## PDF第800页

If [the mad person] has killed someone, apart from granting monetary redemption according to the sub-statute, [the mad person in question] will be ordered to be permanently confined. Liberation will not be allowed even if [he or she] recovers completely. $ ^{42} $ If [the mad person] is not strictly chained up and confined and causes trouble to other prisoners, the official in charge of the prison $ ^{43} $ will be cashiered and submitted to administrative sanctions, and the prison guard will be strictly sentenced according to the [administrative] guidelines.

When local officials encounter a case of “Killing because of madness,” when a report is presented to the official, he needs to collect accurate depositions from the party of the victim, as well as accurate depositions and bonded statements from neighbors, difang[, and other local headmen]. The official in charge will proceed to a detailed and strict investigation. If madness is faked and there is blithe reporting [of madness to the authorities], in addition to sentencing the criminal according to the [relevant] statutes, sentence the difang, neighbors, and relatives who knew the circumstances but made a false report to the same sentence as the offender, diminished by one degree according to the statute on “knowing the circumstances and hiding the offender.” $ ^{44} $

若曾經殺人者 除照例收贖外 即令永遠鎖錮 雖或痊愈 不准釋放 如鎖禁不嚴 以致瘋犯在監擾累獄囚者 將管獄有獄官加參處 獄卒照例嚴加治罪

地方官遇有瘋病殺人之案 呈報到官 務取被殺之事主切實供詞 並取鄰佑地方確實供結 該管官詳加嚴訊 如有假瘋妄報 除兇犯即行按律治罪外 將知情捏報之地方鄰佑親屬人等 照隱匿罪人知情者減罪人一等律問擬

The 1732 sub-statute on preventive confinement, which had been integrated into the law books in 1740, was reformulated as follows: $ ^{45} $

If the relatives, neighbors, etc., of a person with madness illness harbor and hide [that person] and do no implement surveillance, so that the person with madness illness commits suicide, [sentence to] 80 strokes of the heavy stick according to the statute on “doing what should not be done, heavy case.” If [the person with madness illness] kills another person, [sentence relatives, neighbors, etc., to] 100 strokes of the heavy stick in accordance with the statute on “knowing that someone is plotting to harm another, but not trying to stop him or not reporting him [to the authorities after the crime has been committed].” If the relatives, neighbors, etc. have already reported the name [of the person with madness illness to the authorities] but the official in charge has not strictly ordered guard, so that [the person with madness illness] is not the person who has been committed to the act.

## PDF第801页

madness illness] commits suicide or kills another person, report [the official] to the Ministry for deliberation of an administrative sanction.

瘋病之人 其親屬鄰佑人等 容隱不報 不行看守 以致瘋病之人自殺者 照不應重律杖八十 致殺他人者 照知人謀害他人不即阻當首報律杖一百 如親屬鄰佑人等 已經報名 而該管官不嚴飭看守 以致自殺及致殺他人者 俱交部議處

I am not sure whether this law was reformulated when the 1766 rule (→ 1766 [3]) was promulgated—in which case that “1766” rule should be considered to have been made in 1767 (see note 38 above)—or whether it was adjusted a year after the 1766 law to erase contradictions between the 1766 law and the 1732 sub-statute on preventive confinement.

“Censor-in-chief of the Left” (左都御史) Cui Yingjie 崔應階 submitted a proposal that the Ministry of Punishments and the Ministry of Rites decided to accept. From then on, “When [people with] madness illness successively kill two ordinary people, sentence to strangulation after the Assizes” (瘋病連殺平人二命以上者 擬以絞候). $ ^{46} $

This was the first time in the Qing period that multiple homicides committed because of madness were sentenced according to another law than the 1669 sub-statute concerning “killing because of madness” ( $ \rightarrow $ 1731, 1753, 1766 [2]).

When he submitted this proposal, Cui Yingjie had recently been promoted from president of the Ministry of Punishments (where he had served for four years) to president of the Censorate (“Censor-in-chief of the Left”), one of the most powerful posts in the central government.

1784 A new regulation explained how to classify mad homicides at the Autumn Assizes:

In cases of killing because of madness, if two or more unrelated persons are killed or even if there are fewer than two victims but the [case] involves mourning relations [i.e., the victim was a senior relative], the sentence should be [classified as] ‘effective.’ Otherwise, even if two lives [have been taken] but one is a son or nephew [i.e., a junior relative], or if the single victim is one’s mother’s second husband and the ties of mutual affection are not deep, it is acceptable [to classify the sentence] as ‘suspended. $ ^{347} $]

瘋病殺人之案 如有殺死凡人二命以上及雖非二命而有關服制者 應擬情實 其餘死雖二命而一係子姪或一命而死係繼父等類 恩義未深者 可以緩決

## PDF第802页

This clarification was made necessary by the 1756 and 1776 rules that instructed officials to sentence certain kinds of homicides committed because of madness to strangulation or decapitation “after the Assizes.” This is the first guideline I have found about how to process crimes committed because of madness at the Autumn Assizes, an institution in which formal punishments could be modulated to reflect official concern for imposing just and balanced punishments.

1786 [1] 'The Ministry of Punishments reversed a decision by the Shandong governor to sentence to monetary redemption and permanent imprisonment (1669, 1762, 1766 [3]) someone who had killed a fifth-degree (緦麻) senior relative—an uncle—in a fit of madness. The corrected sentence of decapitation after the Assizes was decided according to the statute on "Junior member of a family striking to death a fifth-degree senior relative of the main paternal branch" (卑幼毆死本宗緦麻尊屬; Art. 317). $ ^{48} $

The Ministry also remarked that there was no explicit law (明文) concerning the killing of one's elder relatives in a fit of madness, and decided that the current sub-statute on "killing because of madness" applies only to the killing of one unrelated person (凡人). This was a new distinction. This way of sentencing shows that the analogy between accidents and unconscious acts committed because of madness ( $ \rightarrow $ 1669) was no longer strong. From then on, the degree of kinship between killer and victim would be considered more relevant than the madness of the killer in the sentencing of mad homicides.

1786 [2] A decision of the Ministry of Punishments affirmed the similarity between acts committed in a state of insanity and acts committed during fits of madness occasioned by fevers. $ ^{49} $

1792 [1] A provincial governor sentenced to monetary redemption and permanent imprisonment someone who had killed his wife’s mother in a fit of madness. $ ^{50} $ The Ministry required that this case be sentenced according to the degrees of mourning ( $ \rightarrow $ 1786 [1]).

1792 [2] A man from Shandong who had killed his wife in a fit of madness and been sentenced to permanent imprisonment was liberated because his illness had healed and he was a single son whose parents were old (親老丁單). $ ^{51} $ This is the first known measure for the liberation of permanently confined mad people (→ 1796 [2], 1797). This way of releasing convicted mad killers to perpetuate the family line was confirmed by a sub-statute in 1801 (q.v.).

1796 [1] A memorandum of the Ministry of Punishments claimed that it was mistaken to sentence the madness-induced killing of a junior relative according to the law ( $ \rightarrow $

## PDF第803页

(1669) that equated mad killings with accidental killing, because the killer would then receive no punishment at all. $ ^{52} $ Permanent imprisonment (1766 [3]) became the official punishment for this kind of crime.

1796 [2] The Ministry decided that mad killers sentenced to permanent confinement could not be released unless the they had recovered for twenty years and were older than 70 years old (cf. 1792 [2]). $ ^{53} $ If the mad killer has not healed, had recovered for less than 20 years, or had recovered for 20 years but was younger than 70 sui, liberation should not be permitted. This decision was triggered by a Shandong governor who had asked if mad killers who had been in prison for 10 or 20 years, or old men over 70 sui could benefit from the general amnesty (called either 恩詔 or 大赦 in the memorandum) that had been decreed in 1796 when the Qianlong emperor had yielded the throne to his son.

1797 A man from Zhili who had been sentenced to permanent imprisonment, had been in prison for more than ten years, whose illness had healed, and who was a single son whose parents were old (親老丁單) was allowed to “remain [at home] to take care [of his parents]” (留養). $ ^{54} $ The Ministry of Punishments judged this case on the basis of a similar case from 1792 (→ 1792 [2]). Instead of reviewing each similar case individually, the Ministry eventually decided to turn this measure into a sub-statute (應於例內增入以便引用) (→ 1801).

On the occasion of a clearing up of prisons (清理監獄), an imperial edict of clemency (恩旨) requested that all criminals who were permanently imprisoned should be processed in bulk (永遠監禁各犯一體查辦), presumably to be liberated. The Ministry put forth the following suggestion, which was approved: "Henceforth, when there is an edict of grace concerning the clearing up of prisons, those who have been imprisoned because of madness for less than five years shall not be processed" (嗣後遇有清理刑獄恩旨 其因瘋病監禁未及五年者俱毋庸查辦). These instructions became the official guideline (zhangcheng 章程) by which such cases would be treated. $ ^{55} $ From then on, mad killers who had been sentenced to permanent imprisonment could be liberated if an edict of grace was promulgated, but only if they had already been in prison for more than five years. This measure was made more specific in 1809 ( $ \rightarrow $ 1809 [1]).

1801 A sub-statute was promulgated to allow recovered mad killers who were also single sons to return home to take care of their parents (→ 1792 [2], 1797). $ ^{56} $

## PDF第804页

If mad criminals who have been permanently imprisoned for killing someone have old parents and are single sons, and [therefore] qualify to remain [at home] to take care [of his parents] and perpetuate the sacrifices according to the sub-statute; if [such criminals] have recovered from illness, let the local official examine them to ascertain [that this is true]. [The local official] will then produce a bonded statement $ ^{57} $ and memorialize to assess liberation. The local official will still be responsible for handing the criminal over to his relatives, who will take [the recovered mad man] back and take stringent precautionary measures. If [the criminal's] illness flares up again and he stirs up trouble, his relatives will be sentenced according to the sub-statute, $ ^{58} $ and the criminal will be permanently confined without permission to be released [ever again]. The local official who has produced the bonded statement will be subject to administrative sanctions according to sub-statutes.

凡瘋犯殺人永遠鎖錮 若親老丁單例應留養承祀者 如病果痊癒 令地方官診驗明確 加結具題核釋 仍責成地方官飭交犯屬領回嚴加防範 倘病復發滋事 親屬照例治罪 本犯永遠監禁不准釋放 出結之地方官照例議處

This law differed from the recent guidelines for liberation ( $ \rightarrow $ 1800) in that no time after recovery was specified before liberation could be allowed, and the convicts could be released even without an edict of mercy. Measures for the liberation of single sons were made more specific in 1809 ( $ \rightarrow $ 1809 [2]).

1803 [1] $ ^{59} $The Ministry of Punishments issued new measures saying that mad killers who recovered from insanity should be sentenced to death according to the regular statutes on homicide. $ ^{60} $ Only those who remained permanently mad would still be sentenced to permanent imprisonment (1766 [3]).

Henceforth, for criminals who have killed because of madness, besides the cases in which the criminal has been examined and found to be permanently mad and incoherent, in which case he will still be permanently confined according to the sub-statute, when one has killed because of a sudden fit of madness and can give a clear deposition upon arraignment, or when at first one has been ascertained to be mad upon arraignment but can

## PDF第805页

give a clear deposition upon retrial, one will be sentenced to death according to the relevant root statute or sub-statute. $ ^{61} $

嗣後瘋病殺人之犯 除珍驗該犯始終瘋病語無倫次者 仍照定例永遠鎖錮外 若因一時瘋發殺人到案供吐明晰 或初到案時雖驗係瘋迷 迨覆審時供吐明晰者 即仍按各本律例擬抵

The explicit goal of this measure was to deter people from killing someone and then trying to avoid the death penalty by pretending to have been mad (恐其裝瘋捏飾翼圖免抵). $ ^{62} $ First sent to the provinces by circular (通行), these measures were integrated into a sub-statute in 1804 (q.v.).

1803 [2] In April or May 1803 (JQ8.3), in response to a memorial by the Governor-general of Zhili concerning a recent case, the Ministry of Punishments resolved that when someone recovered immediately after having killed someone else in a fit of madness, the main way to ascertain the killer’s madness would be prior declaration of this person as mad to the local authorities, or a “bonded statement” from the victim’s relatives certifying that the killer had indeed been mad at the moment of committing his crime. $ ^{63} $

[In cases of killing because of madness in which the killer recovers immediately,] one should always take as evidence [of madness] a prior declaration to officials resulting in a file. If this was really [a case of] abrupt madness with no time to make a declaration, so that [the mad person] kills someone and immediately recovers, the department or county magistrate will elucidate [the case] by interrogating the relatives of the deceased and obtaining from them a truthful bonded statement that will be taken as evidence [of madness]. [He should then] write a report and forward it, and only then will the meting out of [a sentence of] killing in an affray be allowed. If there is neither a file [from a prior] declaration, nor a truthful bonded statement from the relatives of the deceased, the real circumstances should be investigated, and the sentence will be meted out according to the root statute on “plotting [to kill]” or “[killing] intentionally.” $ ^{64} $

總以先經報官有案為據 如實係陡患瘋病猝不及報以致殺人旋經痊癒者

該州縣官審明 即應訊取屍親切實甘結為據 敘詳咨方准擬以鬥殺 如無

報案 又無屍親切結 即確究實情 仍按謀故各本律定擬

This measure was integrated into a sub-statute in 1804 ( $ \rightarrow $ 1804 [2]).

## PDF第806页

1804 [1] In response to a verdict imposed by the governor of Sichuan in late 1803 or early 1804, $ ^{65} $ the Ministry of Punishments issued a new circular specifying that the recent circular concerning recovery for trials ( $ \rightarrow $ 1803 [1]) only applied to cases in which the regular statutes demanded the death penalty for the killer. $ ^{66} $ Those who recovered after having killed a junior relative (as in the Sichuan case) should still be sentenced to permanent imprisonment (1796 [1]).

1804 [2] Recent legislation and judicial decisions (→ 1803 [1], 1803 [2], 1804 [1]) were edited into a new sub-statute that would be easier to cite (一併纂輯為例以便引用). $ ^{67} $

In cases of killing because of madness, one should always take as evidence [of madness] a prior declaration to officials that has resulted in a file. If the criminal has been examined and found to be permanently mad and incoherent, confine [him] permanently according to the sub-statute. If a killing takes place because of abrupt madness which there is no time to declare, but [the killer] recovers immediately, or if upon arraignment [the killer] has been ascertained to be mad but can give a clear deposition upon retrial, the department or county magistrate will investigate, and interrogate the relatives of the deceased to obtain from them a truthful bonded statement. Only after he writes a report and forwards it to the Ministry $ ^{68} $ will the meting out of [a sentence of] “killing in an affray” be allowed. If there is neither a file [from a prior] declaration, nor a truthful bonded statement from the relatives of the deceased, the real circumstances should be investigated, and the sentence will be meted out according to the root statute on “plotting [to kill]” or “[killing] intentionally.”

As for when the person who has been killed is a junior relative within the mourning degrees whose [killing] does not warrant the death penalty, it is not permitted to implement exile [simply] because the killer has recovered from illness. [Such criminals should] still be permanently confined in accordance with the sub-statute on “killing because of madness.”

凡瘋病殺人之案 總已先經報官有案為據 如診驗該犯始終瘋病語無倫次者 仍照定例永遠鎖錮 若因一時陡患瘋病 猝不及報 以致殺人 旋經痊愈 或到案時雖驗係瘋迷 迨覆審時供吐明晰者 該州縣官審明 即訊

取屍親切實甘結 敘詳咨部 方准擬以闘殺 如無報案 又無屍親切結

即確究實情 仍按謀故各本律定擬

至所殺係有服卑幼罪不至死者 不得以病已痊癒即行發配 仍依瘋病殺人例 永遠鎖錮

This law guided the sentencing of the madness-induced single homicide of an unrelated person until the end of the Qing. The deterrent sentence of “hanging after the Assizes” imposed to killers who had recovered from madness was classified as “suspended” at the Autumn Assizes, and then commuted to permanent imprisonment—the same as for mad killers who never recovered.

1809 [1] A circular (通行) sent to provincial governments stipulated that those who had killed a senior relative in a fit of madness could not be liberated from permanent confinement even if they encountered an edict of mercy ( $ \rightarrow $ 1800).

Whenever a criminal who has killed because of madness has been sentenced to death but has been “exempted from the hook” $ ^{69} $ and is thus permanently imprisoned; [when such a criminal] recovers from illness and encounters an edict of mercy for which, by sub-statute, he or she is to be processed for release, besides [cases] in which the person who was killed was an ordinary person, which should be processed as usual, in cases having to do with the mourning system, as when a junior relative has killed a senior relative or a wife her husband, [the convict] should still be permanently imprisoned and not authorized to be released. $ ^{70} $

凡瘋病殺人問擬死罪免勾永遠監禁之犯 病愈後遇有 恩旨例得查辦

釋放者 除所殺係平人仍照辦理外 若卑幼致死尊長及妻致死夫關係服制

者 仍永遠監禁不准釋放

1809 [2] A circular (通行) was sent to provincial governments to clarify the way in which people who had killed someone in a fit of madness should be processed at the Autumn Assizes, and how some mad killers who had been permanently imprisoned but had recovered from madness could be released under certain circumstances.

For criminals who have been sentenced to strangulation or decapitation after the Assizes for killing because of madness, besides cases in which the person who died was a senior relative of the second or third degree and cases in which two ordinary person were killed in succession, in which cases [the death sentence] should be classified as “effective” and the case not processed [according to the current rule], the rest [of criminals] should enter the category of criminals [whose death sentence has been] “suspended.” If after the case has been processed, [madness] illness has already healed completely and has not flared up for five years or more, when

## PDF第808页

this is a case in which the parents are old and [the convict] is a single son, or a case in which the parents are already dead and there is no [other] male heir in the family [than the criminal], the official in charge must extract stamped and bonded statements $ ^{71} $ and memorialize to request that [the criminal be allowed to] stay at home to take care of his parents and perpetuate the ancestral sacrifices. If after release [the criminal] incites trouble again, impose punishments and administrative sanctions on the local official and the neighbors and relatives who signed the bonded statements, and confine the said criminal permanently, so that his release will no longer be authorized even if he recovers. $ ^{72} $

凡瘋病殺人問擬斬絞監候之犯　除死係期功尊長尊屬　及連殺平人二命應入情實　各犯毋庸查辦外　其餘應入緩決人犯　如果到案後病已痊癒　監禁至五年以後不復舉發　於遇有親老丁單或父母已故　家無次丁　該管官飭取印甘各結　題請留養承祀　倘釋放後復行滋事　將出結之地方官並鄰族人人等　分別議處惩治　本犯仍永遠監禁　雖或痊癒不准再予釋放

The Autumn Assizes rules dating to 1784 (q.v.) concerning how to process cases of mad homicides were modified and clarified as follows.

When, because of madness, one kills two or more ordinary persons who are not from a single family or two persons from a single family, or when one kills three or more persons from the same family, classify [their death sentence] as “effective” at the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{73} $

瘋病連殺平人非一家二命以上及殺死一家二命者 殺死一家三命以上者秋審俱入於情實.

The sentence for killing three or more people of the same family because of madness was decapitation after the Assizes. The sentence for the other homicides mentioned was strangulation after the Assizes. Classification as “effective” at the Autumn Assizes usually meant that the death sentence was effectively executed, but there was always a possibility that a convict could be “exempted from the hook” (q.v. in Appendix 1) and therefore not executed.

## PDF第809页

# Appendix 9 Ding Wenbin's introductory letter and his confession (1753)

I analyzed Ding Wenbin's 丁文彬 case in section 15.4, pp. 591-600. Here I first translate the letter of introduction he wrote on 29 June 1753 (QL18.5.28) to Kong Zhaohuan 孔昭焕, the patrilineal descendant of Confucius. I then translate the official record of Ding's interrogation by Shandong governor Yang Yingju 楊應居 a few days later.

I this small man Ding Wenbin $ ^{1} $ am originally from Shangyu county in Shaoxing prefecture, Zhejiang. My father Ding Shan and my grand-father were farmers from generation to generation. $ ^{2} $ I have an uncle, Ding Zhitian, who taught in the northern Circuit [?] and once made an alliance with my defunct father-in-law the former Sagely Duke. After staying several days at [your] residence, $ ^{3} $ I this small man returned to Songjiang, where I was privileged to receive my father-in-law's orders in person.

Presently, I am cultivating the Way in Songjiang. In 1749, I presented two volumes of the Records of the Literary and the Martial and a one-fascicle Biography of Taigong wang $ ^{4} $ to Songjiang Education commissioner Zhuang Yougong. $ ^{5} $ It has been five years, and

## PDF第810页

he has not yet reported back. $ ^{6} $ Today, I have continued and completed the Hongfan Chunqiu in five volumes $ ^{7} $ and thus fulfilled the orders of the Lord. $ ^{8} $ These are all Heavenly-ordained texts and [examples of] Xingming learning, $ ^{9} $ they are means by which the Way of Sagelihood can be protected. I dare to extend them for your respectful perusal. As for the rest, I will tell you when we meet face-to-face.

予小子丁文彬原係浙江紹興府上虞縣人 予父丁善至祖公世居務農 有叔祖丁芝田在北路教習 曾與先岳老聖公為盟 予小子帶府留住數日後 予子回松荷蒙岳父面命 今在松修道 於已巳年曾有文武記二本 太公望傳一冊申付松江學政莊有恭 至今五載未有復命 今續成洪範春秋五本 已終帝命皆天命之文 性命之學 所以衛聖門之道 敢申達尊覽 餘面談不暨

Ding's Wenbin's confession appears in Qingdai wenziyu dang 清代文字狱檔, vol. 1: 16-23 (1.3b-7a of Ding's case in the 1930 edition).

Question. Quoting Ding Wenbin's confession: "My (小子) $ ^{10} $ name is Ding Wenbin. I am 38 sui this year. I am registered at Shangyu County in Zhejiang, but I was born and I grew up in Hangzhou. Since sometime in the Yongzheng era [1723–1735], I have lived in Songjiang, where I have been relying on my elder brother and his wife for more than ten years. My

## PDF第811页

father and mother are both deceased. I only have one older brother, and no [other] male siblings. I have never been married, and have no son or daughter."

問 據丁文彬供 小子叫丁文彬 今年三十八歲了 原籍浙江上虞縣人 生長杭州今從雍正年間依傍兄嫂住在松江十餘年了 父母俱亡 止有一個哥子 並無兄弟未曾婚娶 没有兒女的

“In the past, I once followed my paternal great-uncle $ ^{11} $ Ding Zhitian to Qufu, where I saw the old Sagely Duke of Yan lecture on the way of Yao and Shun and refute the deviant teachings of Buddhism and Taoism. $ ^{12} $ [The Duke] granted me his two daughters as brides. In the fifth month of the present year, I left home after I had arranged to gather 1.1 ounces of silver. Upon arriving at Taizhuang [on the Grand Canal as it enters Shandong], I hired a man named Tian, who was traveling on the same boat as me, to carry my luggage. I then traveled on foot for several days. I arrived in Qufu on the 28 $ ^{th} $ day of the fifth month [29 June 1753], but the gatekeeper inside the Duke’s residence did not convey [my demand for audience]. Only after I wrote a message and delivered it inside did [the gatekeeper] take my book and go see. Who could have expected that because he disliked my being poor and destitute, not only was he unwilling to convoke me for an audience, he even enjoined the county magistrate to capture me.”

小子從前跟了族叔祖丁芝田到過曲阜 見過老衍聖公講堯舜之道 闢佛老邪教 曾把兩個女兒許配小子的 今年五月裏在家設湊了一兩一錢銀子出門 到臺庄顧了同船性田的人挑着行李 小子步行走了好幾天 於五月二十八日到曲阜 公府裏門役不傳 小子寫了說帖送進纔取書去看 誰知他嫌小子窮苦 不肯傳見 反囑縣官拿住了

“My grandfather and father were peasants. Neither ever became an official.”

祖父是庄農人 俱不曾出仕的

## PDF第812页

Other confession: "I live in Songjiang [prefecture], within the West gate of the Huating county seat, [at a place named] "Under-the-flowery-willow." My neighbor to the south is a man surnamed Wang who runs a needle store. I forget his name. My northern neighbor is sheng guan Zhou Yong.

又供 小子住松江華亭縣西門內花柳下 南隣是開針舖的姓王 不記名了 北隣是

周永生官

Other question: "Not only were you born in a small household, but now you look like a beggar. How could the Duke of Yan have accepted to become your relative through marriage that year? Not to mention that, in becoming relatives through marriage, there has never been anything like granting one's two daughters to a single man. Besides, there is no matchmaker [to testify that the marriage took place]. This is clearly reckless fudging.

又問　你既是小家出身　現在看你形如乞丐　當年衍聖公怎肯與你結親　況結親從無兩女同許一人之理　又並無媒妁　顯屬妄賴了　供來

Confession: “My becoming his relative through marriage was in obedience to orders from the Lord Above.” $ ^{13} $ why would there need to be a matchmaker? As for two wives for one man, this is nothing but the way of Yao and Shun. Shun took Yao’s two daughters as wives: this is clear and verifiable. For his protecting and preserving the way of the ancient kings that year, the Sagely Duke of Yan should actually be called Emperor. He saw me lecture on the Way and discuss Virtue. I was no different from Shun. Therefore he transmitted his title $ ^{14} $ to me / and [granted] me his two daughters as wives. This was all accomplished on Shangdi’s instructions. When I heard that the Duke of Yan had passed away, I succeeded to his title. It has now been eight years. In fact, because nobody has helped, I don’t even have anything to

## PDF第813页

eat. $ ^{15} $ The year before last, while residing at home, I made a living [telling fortune by] analyzing characters [in people's names]. In the first month of last year, I went to Suzhou and wrote parallel couplets, which I would give to various stores, asking for a few copper coins. I also begged for money for a few days in Qingpu. Those people from the South don't understand the Great Way; they ignored me. It is because I had no other way that I brought this completed book to the Kong residence to hand it to the Sagely Duke and to transmit the title [of Emperor back] to him.

供 小子結親是奉上帝命 何須媒妁呢 那一夫二婦 乃堯舜之道 堯妻舜之二女班班可考 當年老衍聖公守先王之道 實應稱帝 看小子講道論得 與舜無異 故傳位與小子以二女妻之 這都蒙上帝啟迪所成 小子聞得衍聖公歿了 就接了位如今已八年了 實因沒有人幫助 連飯也沒得吃 前年住在家裏 測字糊口 去年正月到蘇州寫對子 送各店家討幾文錢 又在青浦討了幾天錢 那南方的人都不明大道 不理小子 没奈何纔要把做成這部書送到孔府來交與聖公 傳位與他的

Counter question: "What kind of person are you to dare confusedly utter words about being on the throne because of the Heavenly Mandate? Only for this you deserve ten thousand deaths! In what year exactly did you form this idea? With whom did you plot? [As for] this seditious book, where and in what year did you forge it? A lot of the words uttered in this book are highly seditious. It was certainly not composed by a single person. Immediately provide the name of each person with whom you conferred to create [this book], and the names of those who have read it.

駁問　你是何等樣人輒敢湣稱天命在位的話　這就該萬死了　到底你是何年起意何人同謀　這逆書是你幾年上在何處偽造　看這書內許多說話都是大逆不道之言必不止係一人著作　快把同何人商酌做造　何人看過一一供來

Confession: "When I was young and reached [the stage of my education where I read] the Analects, I still did not know the meaning of the text. Afterwards, I painstakingly studied on my own. Only in Songjiang in the thirteenth year of Qianlong [1748], when I was living and

## PDF第814页

teaching at my older brother's place, did I start to write this book. This was also on Shangdi's instruction. I finished writing it in the fourteenth year [1749]. It was I alone who composed and copied it all. By no means were there any co-plotters or people with whom I conferred. Only once, on the third day of the third month of the fourteenth year [19 April 1749], I presented a copy of the book's first two leaflets, the Biography of Taigong wang to His Excellency Zhuang Yougong. $ ^{16} $ At the time, he was working on academies in Jiangnan, and had come to Songjiang to investigate. I gave him [the text] inside the West gate's semi-circular barbican. He took it but gave me no response and did not return the book to me. This is the truth."

供 小子幼年讀到論語 尚不知文義 後來自己苦學 到乾隆十三年住在松江哥子家裏教書纔著起這書的 也是上帝啟迪 十四年上著完了 都是小子一人著作抄寫的 並沒有同謀商酌的人 只有十四年三月初三日曾把這書上兩冊太公望傳一本獻過莊大人名有恭 那時他在江南做學院 到松江來考 小子在西門月城內送的 他接去沒有回報 也沒有把書還我 是實

“In the fifteenth year of Qianlong [1750], on another order from Shangdi, I modified [the title of my book] to Hongfan Chunqiu, improved it a little by augmenting it, and changed ‘Hou Dingzi [the Later Master Ding] said’ in all 60 sections to ‘The Son of Heaven said,’ ‘the Kingly Emperor said,’ and ‘Wangruo [?] said.’ Only after Shangdi ordered me to make these changes did I do this cutting and mending and change some of the contents by pasting slips [on the lines that needed to be modified]. In total, this book represents ten volumes and there are two copies of it. Originally, I wrote one copy to give to the Kong household, and one for Governor Zhuang. $ ^{17} $ He has some understanding of textual studies. Who would have

## PDF第815页

known that Governor Zhuang was no longer in Suzhou? Therefore, I didn't give [him that copy] and have brought both to Qufu."

到乾隆十五年上帝又命我改作洪範春秋 把這書又增添了好些 把書內六十章之後丁子曰都改作天子曰王帝曰王若曰 是上帝命改纔挖補抽換粘聯的 那書共是十本兩部 我原寫一部送孔府 一部送庄撫臺 他還懂得文學 誰知庄撫臺不在蘇州了故此沒有送 都帶到曲阜來了

“Last year, while threshing rice at the Wang family rice shop, near Fengle Bridge at the West gate of Songjiang, I made the acquaintance of the shop owner’s father Mr. Wang. In the first month of this year, I gave him the book. He read it for a while and then I reclaimed it. Previously, when I had not finished writing it, I also gave it to Xu Xuchu, from near Fengle Bridge in Songjiang, and to Mr. Cai from outside the West gate. They both read it and returned it after they did. I never showed it to anyone else. I don’t know the personal names of either Mr. Wang or Mr. Cai. As for [my great-uncle] Ding Zhitian, he died of illness eight years ago. This is the truth.”

去年在松江西門豐樂橋王家米舖內舂米 認識店主父親王先生 今年正月送給他看了一回就取回了 從前未著完時還曾送與松江豐樂橋徐旭初 都看過 他們看了就還了 從沒有給別人看過 那王先生蔡先生 都不知道他名字 那丁芝田已病故八年了 是實

Confession: "I originally was not willing to do such a thing. It was really Shangdi's orders; there was nothing I could do about it. Since there is no such thing as a 'leading plotter,' how could there be a faction of followers? Right now, I can barely feed and clothe myself, and I

am totally poor and destitute. How could there still be anyone helping me? Tell me from where to confess."

# Further question: “You said that Shangdi ordered you. Where on earth is he? And how did he order you? Is it that there was another person inciting you from the shadows?”

Confession: “The Lord Above is the Heavens Above. How could there be a ‘person’

[inciting me]? I really receive frequent instructions from Shangdi. He is often by my side, speaking words that people cannot hear."

供 上帝是上天 如何有人 小子實是蒙上帝時時啟迪 常在身傍說話 人不能聽聞的

Confession under torture: “Since there was no leading plotter or faction of followers, I cannot confess to them. It was truly [on] Shangdi’s orders. Right now, my being tortured is also an affliction brought [upon me] by Shangdi. I can confess no more. Even if you further interrogate [me] under torture, [I] will not change my confession.”

Further question: "Why do you call the Duke of Yan the 'Sagely Emperor'? [In your texts,] there is also a draft letter that you mailed to married women of the Kong household. [And] whose daughter is this 'Woman Dong'? Answer these questions one by one."

Confession: “According to Shangdi’s orders, as long as one has virtue, one has an official post. For his having protected and preserved the Way of Yao and Shun, the old Duke of Yan should occupy the post of Son of Heaven. When he transmitted the Way of Yao and Shun to me, it was as though he transmitted [this] post to me [as well]. Therefore, in the book, I venerate him as the Sagely Emperor. I also call him the Virtuous ancestor [i.e., the founder of the dynasty]. In those times, when Shun received the post from Yao, he was also called the Virtuous ancestor. He himself was not originally referred to as King or Emperor. This is clearly recorded in the book.” $ ^{18} $

據供　上帝之命總以有德即有位　老衍聖公能守堯舜之道　應居天子之位　小子蒙聖公傳堯舜之道　就如傳位　故書內尊崇他為聖帝　又稱他是藝祖　當日舜受位於堯也稱藝祖的　他本人原沒有稱王稱帝之事　那書內載明

“[Concerning] the letter to [my] mother-in-law and [my] wife [in the Kong household], this is something I wrote vacuously. I did not send it. And I don’t remember clearly in what year this matter took place. As for what [I] say in the draft message, it was all explained to me by Shangdi; this was not something I would have dared to fabricate. In that book, [I] also say that [I] bestowed the title of imperial concubine to Ms. Dong. This Ms. Dong is from Songjiang. Her father’s name is Dong Hengshan; he runs a teahouse. I had nothing to eat, [so] I helped him blow bellows for half a year. $ ^{19} $ I saw that daughter of his, barely fifteen sui

and fair-looking. I thought I would name her imperial concubine and I recorded this in my book."

Further question: “We also found in your book that you conferred on Kong Dajiu [‘elder uncle Kong’] $ ^{20} $ the titles of Minister of State and Prisoner-Quelling General, named Kong Shujiu [‘middle uncle Kong’] Minister of Rites, and designated Kong Bojiu [‘younger uncle Kong’] as Grand Minister of Education. $ ^{21} $ What are all these names? You also granted your older brother the title of Duke Wen of Xia, designated your father and mother as King and Queen, and your uncle Ding Zuobai as Great Steward. Where did they receive their titles? Did you ever send them written directives or other items [of official correspondence] $ ^{22} $

Confession: “Kong Dajiu’ is an official for the Sagely Duke of Yan. Originally, this [post] was probably occupied by someone from the Kong household. For this reason, I jotted down that I wanted to enfeoff [the holder of that post]. There are lots of people in that Kong household: there was no room to record their personal names.”

供 孔大舅就是衍聖公官員 原該都是孔家人做的 故此隨手記着要封的 那孔家人多的狠 沒處去記着名字的

“My father died long ago. Both my father and my mother died at my older brother’s house

seven years ago. My brother's name is Ding Wenhui. He is 49 sui this year. So is my sister-in-

## PDF第819页

law, Mrs. Qin. In Songjiang he used to make and sell pancakes. This year, he is helping at someone's noodle shop. He is an unreasonable person; $ ^{22} $ he often calls me a fool. Therefore in my book, I say that my brother is stubborn, my sister-in-law haughty, and my nephew arrogant. But after I ascended the throne, according to ritual, I had to confer a title on him. This is why I wrote this down in the book. Actually, there was no such thing as their receiving a title, and I have never given written directives."

小子父親早死了 父母死在哥子家七年了 哥子名丁文輝 今年四十九歲 與嫂子秦氏是同歲的 他從前在松江打燒餅賣 今年在人家面館幫工 他是不明道理的人常罵我癡子 故此我書內說兄頑嫂囂侄傲了 因小子即了位 禮應封他 故此寫在書上 他们都實沒有受封的事 也都沒有給過箚付的

“There is also one Kong Guanggai, who was once Adjutant of the Left Brigade in Songjiang. I knew his personal name, so I also wrote it down. I’ve always thought that all members of the Kong family should be officials, [but] actually I have not enfeoffed them yet. Now, I hear that Kong Guanggai has died in Qufu.”

“That Ding Zuobai is a paternal uncle. He lives within the walls of the Shaoxing prefectural city. Now he is about 55 smi. I also wanted to give him a title, so I wrote him into my book. How is there [anything like] written directives?”

那丁左白是族中叔子 住在紹興府城內 現有無五十五歲了 也是想要封他 就寫在書上 那有箚付呢

## PDF第820页

Confession: “I do not know him in daily life. I gave him [the book] because he came to Songjiang to supervise the examinations.” $ ^{23} $ When Zhang Yingchen from Hangzhou returned home to mourn his parents, [I] wrote [an essay on] “Tracing the Dao to its Origins” $ ^{24} $ and sent it to [him] to ask for advice. [But] he would neither accept the text nor receive me. All I could do was return [home]. Apart from this, I have never asked an official for advice.”

供 小子平日並不認識 因他來松江考試 送他看的 後來杭州張映辰丁憂回家也寫了一篇元道送去請教 他文也不收 人亦不見 小子只得回來了 餘外並沒有請教過做官的人了

# Further question: “In your book are drawings of models for making coins. What kind of person are you to dare want to mint coins for circulation and use! You really should die ten thousand times! Did you actually ever mint [coins]?”

又問 你書上畫有制錢式樣 你是何等樣人敢要鑄錢行用 真該萬死了 你果曾鑄過沒有呢

Confession: “These coin models that I drew are coins from Taigong’s Nine Fiscal Agencies;” $ ^{25} $ they are drawn from standard models. I am orphaned and miserable, with nothing to rely on. Who could have been willing to provide funds? How could I have accomplished such a feat? I never minted [the coins I drew].”

供 小子畫這錢式 是太公九府錢 有出典的 小子孤苦無依 誰人肯出資本 那裏做得成這件事 並不曾鑄過的

Further question: “In your book, you recklessly designed a system of ceremonies and music for capping, wedding, mourning, and ancestor’s worship. This is certainly not [something that] can be achieved by a single person’s vision. You must undoubtedly have conferred with other people. You are still not telling the truth!”

## PDF第821页

又問 你書上妄作冠婚喪祭禮樂制度 必非一己之見可成 一定同人商酌 還不實說麼

Confession: “The system of rites and music that I established was all compiled according to the way of Yao and Shun. By no means did [I] fabricate [them]. I was only acting in obedience to Shangdi’s orders [so that I would] be able to protect and preserve the Sagely Way. I am not a fool, damn it! At home, everyone says I am a fool. [So] how could there still be anyone [willing] to come and confer with me? There was indeed nobody.”

小子所定禮樂制度皆是按照堯舜之道纂輯　並非杜撰　小子不過遵上帝之命克守聖道而行　並不是癡子　可恨　在家時人人道是小子是癡的　那裏還有人來同小子商酌呢　實是沒有的

Further question: “What kind of person is this Zhang the Seventh that you record in your book? Why are you so angry at him?”

又問你書中記張七是什麼樣人為何這樣惱他呢

Confession: “Zhang the Seventh is Zhang Bugeng. He is the son of Zhang signan of Songjiang. When I inherited the task of the sages, the Sagely Duke’s daughter should have been wedded to me, but she was unexpectedly snatched away and taken as bride by him. That year, Zhang the Seventh’s wedding was extremely lavish. Not only did I not get a wife; [I was] also worried that [my] master’s younger sister did not get a [proper] place. For this reason, I was irritated and disturbed, and [so] I wrote down this sentence.”

供　張七即張不賡　他是松江張司冠之子　小子承聖人之緒　聖公之女應配與小子不料被他奪娶了去　那年張七娶親豪華已極　不特小子不得妻　且念師妹不得所故此惱不過記這一句的

Further question: “In your book, you record that your mother was wrong to listen to Muslim Li’s wife and to go to Jiangxi for three years to do what wicked thing?

又問你書中記載汝母誤聽李回回妻子往江西三載做什麼歹事呢

## PDF第822页

Confession: “Mother went to Jiangxi because she was hired by a family to cook meals. If these were wicked things, would I still be willing to record them in my book?”

供 母子往江西是僱與人家做飯 若是歹事 小子還肯記在書內麼

Further question: "In your book, you record that you and Xu Tiaowu went to Huguang for one year. There is also something about an edict to Zhang Yingchen [on] his not knowing that you were the Son of Heaven. This shows that Xu Tiaowu already knew you as the Son of Heaven. Below, [the book] also recklessly carries [a] draft edict to Zhang Yingchen. In what manner did Zhang Yingchen report back to you? What kinds of person are these Xu Tiaowu and Zhang Yingchen? Where is their native place? Confess."

又問　你書中記載你與徐調五往湖廣一歲　又有諭張映辰不知你為天子的事　可見徐調五是知道你為天子的了　後又妄載張映辰的諭稿　張映辰如何回覆你的　那徐調五張映辰都是什麼樣人　何處籍貫　供來

Confession: “Zhang Yingchen and Xu Tiaowu are both from Hangzhou. That year, Zhang Yingchen was in Huguang working on academies, and invited Xu Tiaowu to go [with him] to read texts. I was so poor and destitute I could barely survive, so I followed Xu Tiaowu, making food on the way there. I served in the yamen for more than a year, and then came back. One or two years after I arrived home, [since I was] earnestly implementing the Way of Yao and Shun, the Lord Above ordained [me] as the Son of Heaven. I wanted to inform Zhang Yingchen by edict, and so I did. As for this draft, because [Zhang] was in post as an official, Shangdi told me not to dispatch it, so I suspended [my actions]. When I was following Xu Tiaowu, [my] work on the Way [of Yao and Shun] was not complete, and I had not received the Mandate of Heaven yet. At the time, I myself was not expecting to reach this position. This Xu Tiaowu also does not understand the Way. How could he have got to know that I was going to be the Son of Heaven?”

## PDF第823页

供 張映辰徐調五都是杭州人 那年張映辰在湖廣做學院 請徐調五去看文章 小子窮苦不過 跟徐調五去 路上做飯 在衙門裏伺候一年多回來的 小子到家一二年後 力行堯舜之道 上帝命為天子了 想要諭知張映辰 就做就了這個稿 因他是個現任官 也是上帝說不要寄去 就中止了 小子跟徐調五之時 道業未成 尚未舉有天命 小子當日自己也不料到此地位 那徐調五也是不明道的 如何知到小子要做天子的呢

Further question: “You also have six copies (?) of a forged calendar book. How did you dare to appropriate [the right to] write the characters qinding [‘published by imperial orders’]? You recklessly say [that you have been] on the throne for 8 years. But why is it that in each of the two copies [of your book], only three years—six, seven, and eight—[are recorded]? Why did you conceal the previous ones? Whose reign name and dynastic name are ‘Tianyuan of the Great Xia’?” Concerning this ‘Eighth year of Tianyuan’: why is it that on the cover of this forged book, [you] also annotate it in the margin as [meaning] ‘First year of Zhaowu’? There is undoubtedly another person [involved].” Why did you also write the characters ‘Great Xia’ and ‘Great Ming’ on these seditious books?”

又問　你還有偽造的時憲書陸本　怎敢擅寫欽定字樣　你既妄稱在位八年為何　又是每樣兩本　只有六七八三年的　以前的為何又藏匿呢　那大夏天元都是誰的國號年號　這天元八年　這一本偽書面頁上　為何又旁註昭武元年　必定另有一人了那幾本逆書上為何又寫大夏大明的字呢

Confession: “I am the only one who composed and copied [these books]. Because Shangdi ordered me to rush to edit this Hongfan Chunqin, I did not have time to write this new book. Only in the sixth year after I ascended the throne did I start to compose [it]. I have only composed three years of it. By no means did I hide [other parts] somewhere else. This ‘Great Xia’ is my dynastic name.” $ ^{28} $ ‘Tianyuan’ is the reign period. Because I did [all this] to absolutely no avail, last year I asked Shangdi for a mandate to change ‘Tianyuan’ [‘Heavenly

## PDF第824页

Primordiality’] into ‘Zhaowu’ [‘Manifest Martiality’] and to pass the title to the younger Sagely Duke. $ ^{29} $ I wrote qinding [‘published by imperial order’] because [I] have [an imperial] reign name. As for ‘Great Xia’ and ‘Great Ming’ on the cover of the book: Great Ming draws its meaning from ‘illuminating the illustrious virtue,’ Great Xia from ‘implementing the time of the Xia’.” $ ^{30} $

供 小子只有一個人著書抄寫 因上帝命我赶修這洪範春秋 故此不能再有功夫造這新書了 直到即位六年上纔造起的 只造得三年 並沒有隱藏別處 那大夏是小子國號 天元是年號 小子因做得一無好處 去年請命了上帝 把天元改作昭武傳位與小聖公的 既有年號就寫欽定了 至於書面上寫大夏大明 那是取明明德的意思 大夏是取行夏之時的意思
